The Lawfare Podcast 6 episode(s) · 151 insights

Rational Security: The “Hip to Be a Square State” Edition85 min35 insights2026-07-16
▶ Watch on YouTube
Lawfare's national-security panel dissects the collapse of the US-Iran ceasefire and blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, the frictions and modest wins of NATO's Ankara summit ('NATO 3.0'), and a US-Israel relationship under growing strain from diverging strategic priorities and shifting domestic politics in both parties.
Iran Ceasefire Collapse and the Strait of Hormuz Standoff · 9NATO 3.0 and the Fraying Transatlantic Alliance · 12US-Israel Rift: Diverging Interests and Political Realignment · 9Second-Order Fallout: Gulf Leverage, Asian Side-Deals, and the China Precedent · 5
Iran Ceasefire Collapse and the Strait of Hormuz Standoff 9
The US-Iran ceasefire collapsed after Iran struck several commercial vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz; the US responded with strikes on more than 300 targets across Iran, and Iran retaliated (or attempted to) against US bases in Bahrain, Kuwait, and Jordan.
data_point🗣 Scott (host)conviction: highIranUnited StatesStrait of HormuzBahrainKuwaitJordan
So what: Confirms the conflict has re-escalated beyond the narrow self-defense strikes of the ceasefire period, raising renewed risk to Gulf shipping and energy flows.
Trump declared the US would patrol the Strait of Hormuz as a self-styled 'guardian,' instituting a blockade on Iranian ships and initially charging a 20% fee on all cargo transiting the strait, before walking back the fee requirement.
data_point🗣 Scott (host)conviction: highDonald TrumpIranStrait of Hormuz
So what: Signals an improvised, rapidly-reversed US policy toward strait traffic that Iran has flatly rejected, adding uncertainty for shippers and insurers relying on the strait.
The original US-Iran ceasefire memorandum was structurally doomed because it was rushed, said nothing about Lebanon (one of Iran's core issues), and let Israel act as a co-belligerent in the fighting without being a party bound by the deal's terms.
causal🗣 Julia (guest)conviction: highIranIsraelLebanonUnited States
So what: Explains why the ceasefire failed within weeks and implies any future truce needs Israel as a signatory and must address Lebanon to hold.
“this was not a piece that failed. Uh this was a pause that was written by a real estate developer and a son-in-law that was always going to fail.” ▶ 8:11
The current US-Iran dynamic should be understood as a 'violent competitive bargaining phase' rather than a war moving toward decisive resolution, in which strikes, pauses, and shuttle diplomacy function as one continuous negotiation rather than separate military and diplomatic tracks.
framework🗣 Julia (guest)conviction: highIranUnited States
So what: A mental model for interpreting future strikes/pauses as leverage moves, not as signs of imminent full-scale war or full resolution.
“Haggling over the terms with a strike, pause, shuttle, diplomacy, and repeat where the shooting and the talking are one negotiation.” ▶ 9:11
Iran holds the upper hand in the current standoff because it can use the Strait of Hormuz to throttle traffic on its own timetable, and because Iran is fighting what it sees as a war of survival while the US is fighting an unpopular war of choice with less domestic tolerance for sustained costs.
contrarian🗣 Julia (guest)conviction: highIranUnited StatesStrait of Hormuz
So what: Implies the US is more likely to make further concessions over time rather than Iran backing down, a bearish signal for a durable near-term resolution and bullish for continued shipping/insurance risk premia in the strait.
Unconfirmed reports that early strikes on shipping were carried out by a 'rogue' IRGC faction acting without Tehran's sign-off are a recurring, convenient Iranian narrative that is unlikely to be accurate, especially now that the IRGC has consolidated more political influence following the recent funeral of the Ayatollah.
contrarian🗣 Ari (guest)conviction: mediumIranIRGC
So what: Investors/analysts should discount Iranian claims of internal disunity as a negotiating tactic rather than genuine loss of central control.
The panel expects a 'new steady state' of episodic Iran-US escalation with no comprehensive deal, because Iran already won major concessions early on (language on US regional withdrawal and rollback of sanctions including human-rights sanctions), Trump wants to pivot attention elsewhere (e.g. Cuba), and looming US midterms make the conflict's economic fallout (fertilizer, oil, gas prices) more politically salient than the war itself.
prediction🗣 Ari (guest)conviction: highIranDonald Trumpsanctionsoil
So what: Investors should expect a prolonged low-grade conflict pattern rather than either full resolution or full-scale war, with periodic shocks to oil and shipping costs tied to the election calendar.
Actually securing freedom of navigation through the Strait of Hormuz would require either a large, continuous US Navy deployment (straining a force already stretched by Pacific commitments and maintenance backlogs) or ground troops, both of which carry a real risk of American casualties that Trump is especially sensitive to, and there is little enthusiasm for either option within the military or the administration.
framework🗣 Dan (guest)conviction: highUnited StatesIranStrait of HormuzUS Navy
So what: Suggests the US is unlikely to commit the forces needed to actually reopen the strait to normal traffic, implying persistent shipping disruption risk.
Trump's decision-making is characterized by short-term problem-solving ('get it off the front pages this week'), which produced the rushed, poorly drafted ceasefire memorandum, and the same instinct makes it plausible he will offer Iran further concessions to get talks back on track as oil and gas prices rise and anger among US Gulf allies grows.
prediction🗣 Dan (guest)conviction: mediumDonald TrumpIranGulf States
So what: A framework for anticipating US policy swings toward Iran: watch oil/gas prices and Gulf-ally pressure as leading indicators of renewed US concessions.
“It seems to be how do I get through the problem I'm facing right now and, you know, just have it off the front pages this week and we'll move on.” ▶ 20:22
NATO 3.0 and the Fraying Transatlantic Alliance 12
NATO's Ankara summit produced more than $50 billion in newly announced defense procurement and at least 70 billion euros in fresh military assistance for Ukraine, framed as progress toward the alliance's prior pledge to spend 5% of GDP on defense.
data_point🗣 Scott (host)conviction: highNATOUkraine
So what: Concrete evidence of accelerating European defense procurement spend, relevant for defense-sector demand forecasting.
Europe and Canada were reported to have made defense investment commitments of up to $139 billion over the past year, with another $50 billion announced separately, plus a distinct $40 billion 'Drone Edge' program dedicated to uncrewed systems over the next five years.
data_point🗣 Julia (guest)conviction: highNATOEuropeCanada
So what: A specific, sizable multi-year European drone/uncrewed-systems procurement pipeline ($40B) is a concrete sector-level data point for defense-tech investors.
Iran ended up overshadowing the Ankara summit agenda even though it isn't formally a NATO topic, because European allies were not consulted on US Iran strategy and spent much of the summit trying to understand how they fit into the plan going forward.
data_point🗣 Ari (guest)conviction: mediumNATOIranUnited States
So what: Signals continued erosion of US-European coordination on Middle East policy, a recurring source of transatlantic friction to watch.
Trump raised acquiring Greenland with President Erdogan within hours of landing in Ankara, prompting the Danish prime minister to publicly state Denmark would defend NATO territory including its own from 'the dominant ally' -- language the panel treats as an extraordinary signal that Denmark takes the threat as serious, not merely rhetorical.
data_point🗣 Ari (guest)conviction: highDonald TrumpGreenlandDenmarkNATO
So what: A NATO ally publicly preparing to defend itself against the US, rather than an adversary, is a structural warning sign for alliance cohesion and defense planning.
“we will defend every inch of NATO territory including our own from the dominant ally” ▶ 39:40
Denmark reportedly dispatched soldiers with live ammunition on short emergency orders to secure its Greenland airports in the months after Trump's Greenland comments, which the panel cites as evidence Europeans believe Trump's territorial threats are operationally real rather than posturing.
anecdote🗣 Dan (guest)conviction: mediumDenmarkGreenlandDonald Trump
So what: A concrete, verifiable signal (military deployment) that a NATO member is treating an intra-alliance territorial threat as credible -- a genuine geopolitical tail risk, not just rhetoric.
“Denmark started sending soldiers to blow up their airports and with blood and live ammunition explosives on a, you know, short emergency order.” ▶ 58:36
Ankara produced two concrete positives: a short, strong NATO declaration naming Russia as the threat and reaffirming Article 5, and the alliance's first-ever defense industry forum aimed at moving the conversation from raw defense-spending targets toward building actual, integrated capabilities.
data_point🗣 Ari (guest)conviction: mediumNATORussia
So what: The new defense industry forum is an institutional mechanism worth tracking for how European defense procurement gets coordinated (or fragmented) going forward.
Trump signaled willingness to issue licenses allowing Ukraine to co-produce Patriot missile systems domestically, a step beyond the US's only existing overseas Patriot co-production agreements (Japan and Germany); experts are split on whether Ukraine can realistically stand up such complex production mid-war, versus it being mainly a symbolic signal of continued US support.
data_point🗣 Ari (guest)conviction: mediumUkrainePatriot missile systemDonald Trump
So what: If real, domestic Patriot co-production in Ukraine would be a major and unprecedented expansion of Western defense-industrial cooperation with a country at war; watch whether licenses are actually issued.
“these are very complicated systems. It is going to take a long time for the Ukrainians to be able to actually produce them domestically. And at that point, does it really matter?” ▶ 56:54
No commitment to a 2027 NATO summit was secured at Ankara, and the Pentagon is already conducting a six-month posture review that could lead to further US troop withdrawals from Europe before European militaries are ready to fill the gap.
prediction🗣 Dan (guest)conviction: mediumNATOUnited StatesPentagon
So what: A leading indicator that US force presence in Europe could shrink faster than European capability build-out, creating a near-term security gap to monitor.
European governments have shifted from treating Trump-era friction as tolerable 'bumps in the road' to seriously planning for 'Europe without the United States,' driving both increased defense spending and a push to convert that spending into real capabilities and interoperability rather than just budget totals.
thesis🗣 Julia (guest)conviction: highEuropeNATOUnited States
So what: A structural, multi-year shift toward European strategic autonomy is underway and likely to persist regardless of short-term summit outcomes, relevant for long-horizon European defense-sector investment.
Historically, US arms sales create dependency and leverage over buyers (a deliberate feature of US security-assistance strategy); the current push toward European strategic autonomy is deliberately unwinding that leverage, which could be net-positive for the US by freeing capacity for the Pacific theater if pursued as a partnership, but risks duplicated, redundant capability spending and lost synergies if pursued adversarially.
framework🗣 Dan (guest)conviction: mediumUnited StatesEuropeChina
So what: European defense self-sufficiency could reduce US arms-export leverage over allies long-term while allowing US strategic focus to shift toward China/Asia -- a trade-off worth tracking in US foreign-military-sales trends.
Because the US political system now swings between an administration threatening to annex allied territory (Greenland) and one emphasizing alliance centrality roughly every four years, with a largely inactive Congress providing no offsetting predictability, Europeans concluded during the Biden years that 'waiting out Trump' does not restore reliability -- making continued pursuit of strategic autonomy likely regardless of who wins the next US election.
thesis🗣 Ari (guest)conviction: highUnited StatesEuropeCongress
So what: European defense-autonomy investment should be treated as a durable structural trend rather than a Trump-specific reaction likely to reverse after the next US election.
There are meaningful regional splits within Europe on strategic autonomy: France is the most forward-leaning on independent European defense capacity (and could become more so if Marine Le Pen wins the French presidency), while the Baltic states, facing an immediate Russian threat, remain more inclined to stay close to the United States.
data_point🗣 Ari (guest)conviction: mediumFranceBaltic statesRussiaNATO
So what: European defense policy is not monolithic; France and the Baltics are likely to diverge in pace and posture, affecting where defense-industrial investment concentrates.
US-Israel Rift: Diverging Interests and Political Realignment 9
The US-Israel relationship is fraying in sentiment and rhetoric even though operational security and intelligence cooperation remains deep, a split Dan compares directly to the current state of the US-NATO relationship.
thesis🗣 Dan (guest)conviction: highUnited StatesIsraelNATO
So what: Suggests headline political tension shouldn't be read as an imminent break in actual military/intelligence cooperation, but the rhetorical damage is real and could compound over time.
US and Israeli Iran priorities diverge structurally: a nuclear Iran is existential for Israel but merely a negative foreign-policy outcome for the US; Israel is far more focused on Lebanon and Iranian medium-range ballistic missiles, while the US is far more focused on freedom of navigation in the Strait of Hormuz -- explaining why the two governments are now pursuing different, sometimes conflicting policies.
framework🗣 Dan (guest)conviction: highUnited StatesIsraelIranLebanonStrait of Hormuz
So what: A concrete mental model for predicting where US and Israeli policy will align versus clash going forward: nuclear issues align, but Lebanon and shipping-lane security do not.
“But for Israel, it's existential. While for the United States, it's much more simply of a negative foreign policy outcome.” ▶ 1:06:44
Netanyahu's political brand -- built on the claim that he alone could extract favorable outcomes from Washington and Moscow, destroy Hezbollah, and bring security to northern Israel -- has been damaged because the current US-brokered deal path is seen by Israelis as too forgiving of Iran, undermining his signature promises to voters ahead of Israeli elections.
thesis🗣 Dan (guest)conviction: mediumBenjamin NetanyahuIsraelIran
So what: Netanyahu's domestic political standing is more vulnerable heading into Israeli elections than it was during the peak of US-Israel Iran cooperation, a factor for anyone tracking Israeli political risk.
Israel's traditional strategy of relying on bipartisan US political support has shifted toward near-total reliance on the Republican Party, but Trump has repeatedly shown he can bring the bulk of Republicans with him on any position -- meaning that bet on unconditional GOP support is now more precarious than in the past, not more secure.
contrarian🗣 Dan (guest)conviction: mediumIsraelRepublican PartyDonald Trump
So what: Israel's political insurance policy in Washington is now concentrated in a single, personality-driven party coalition rather than diversified bipartisan support, raising tail risk if Trump's priorities shift.
“They put all their chits on the Republican party.” ▶ 1:08:34
A US congressman was detained for over an hour by armed Israeli settlers and then by the IDF during a West Bank visit, and a CNN crew was separately attacked by armed settlers days later -- incidents the panel says will further erode establishment/bipartisan appetite in Washington for business-as-usual Israel policy.
data_point🗣 Scott (host)conviction: mediumIsraelWest BankCongress
So what: Concrete recent incidents likely to accelerate the political shift already underway in both US parties toward more critical Israel policy.
A live, unresolved debate is underway within Democratic national-security circles -- highlighted by former NSC strategy director Tom Wright's recent piece in The Atlantic -- over whether the US should have conditioned military assistance to Israel during the Gaza war, and whether future policy should resemble a more normal security alliance with a stronger tie to a reformed Palestinian Authority.
data_point🗣 Julia (guest)conviction: mediumIsraelDemocratic PartyPalestinian AuthorityTom Wright
So what: A specific, named internal Democratic policy debate to track as a leading indicator of how a future Democratic administration might reshape US-Israel security assistance.
A future Democratic administration is likely to impose materially more conditionality on US military and intelligence assistance to Israel than has historically been the case, reflecting the shift already visible within the party's national-security establishment.
prediction🗣 Julia (guest)conviction: mediumIsraelDemocratic PartyUnited States
So what: Signals a plausible future US policy shift toward conditioned aid to Israel that could affect the durability of current US-Israel security arrangements after this administration.
Skepticism of the US-Israel relationship is no longer confined to the Democratic left; it now spans into parts of the Trump coalition aligned with figures like JD Vance, producing an unusual convergence where progressives and segments of the MAGA right land on similar Israel skepticism from very different underlying motivations.
contrarian🗣 Ari (guest)conviction: mediumIsraelJD VanceRepublican PartyDemocratic Party
So what: A cross-ideological erosion of pro-Israel consensus in US politics is a structural, not merely partisan, risk factor for the durability of US-Israel policy continuity.
The US-Israel security assistance memorandum of understanding is due to be renegotiated starting next year, during Trump's term, representing a concrete near-term flashpoint for the relationship.
data_point🗣 Ari (guest)conviction: mediumIsraelUnited StatesDonald Trump
So what: A specific date-bound event (MOU renegotiation) to watch as a test of whether current tensions translate into materially different aid terms.
Second-Order Fallout: Gulf Leverage, Asian Side-Deals, and the China Precedent 5
If the current Strait of Hormuz disruption becomes the 'new normal,' Gulf states become structurally dependent on Iran as gatekeeper of a major export route, giving Iran persistent leverage over them even under a negotiated US solution, and increasing the incentive for Gulf states to pursue their own side deals with Iran rather than rely on Washington.
causal🗣 Scott (host)conviction: mediumGulf StatesIranStrait of Hormuz
So what: A durable Hormuz disruption would restructure Gulf-Iran relations around direct bilateral accommodation rather than US-mediated security, altering the region's alignment.
Countries most exposed to reduced Gulf energy exports and refining disruption -- specifically Singapore and India -- are likely to pursue direct deals with Iran to secure energy flows and imports, giving Iran an added revenue source and leverage over strategically significant Asian economies.
prediction🗣 Scott (host)conviction: mediumSingaporeIndiaIran
So what: Investors tracking energy security in South/Southeast Asia should watch for direct India-Iran or Singapore-Iran energy arrangements as a hedge against continued strait disruption.
If the US effectively concedes to Iran's practice of tolling or closing the Strait of Hormuz, it undermines the US's historic 'freedom of the seas' position and could embolden China to impose its own toll or control over traffic in the Taiwan Strait.
contrarian🗣 Julia (guest)conviction: mediumChinaTaiwan StraitIranUnited States
So what: A direct, specific precedent-risk channel linking Iran/Hormuz policy outcomes to future Chinese assertiveness over Taiwan Strait shipping -- a key watch item for Asia-Pacific security and trade-route risk.
“if we adopt the Iranian theory of the strait, I don't know what's to prevent, for example, Beijing from imposing its own toll on traffic in the Taiwan Strait.” ▶ 33:09
US adversaries are likely drawing the broader lesson that the American pattern in this conflict -- escalate, then run out of domestic political endurance, look for an exit, and be unable to hold even a face-saving position -- is a poor deterrence playbook, weakening the credibility of US threats in future confrontations.
thesis🗣 Julia (guest)conviction: mediumUnited StatesChinaRussiaIran
So what: A generalized erosion of US deterrence credibility with adversaries beyond Iran is a macro geopolitical risk factor extending to Russia-Ukraine and China-Taiwan dynamics.
European farmers reportedly could not afford fuel to plant spring crops this year due to the economic fallout of the Iran conflict, and European media and governments publicly blamed the United States for starting the war without consultation, a grievance the panel expects to leave a lasting negative impression on European public opinion of the US.
anecdote🗣 Dan (guest)conviction: mediumEuropeUnited StatesIran
So what: A tangible, ground-level economic grievance (fuel costs hitting European agriculture) tied directly to the Iran conflict is likely to harden anti-US sentiment in Europe beyond elite policy circles.
Lawfare Daily: Consent in the Age of AI56 min24 insights2026-07-17
▶ Watch on YouTube
A Georgetown computer scientist (Alyssa Redmiles) and two Lawfare editors argue that AI-generated non-consensual intimate imagery (NCII) is fundamentally a consent and likeness problem, not a detection/authenticity problem, walking through concrete platform failures (Meta's tag-a-friend generation feature, Grok's 'donut glaze' filter-bypass exploit), why training-data filtering and input/output filters barely slow down bad actors, and an emerging legal fork between authenticity-based and harm-based regulation of generative AI outputs.
Detection vs. consent: reframing the AI-nudification problem · 3Platform failures in practice: Meta, Grok, and the donut-glaze exploit · 5Why model-level technical fixes don't work yet · 4Inside the creator communities · 4The legal fork: authenticity rights vs. harm-based regulation · 4What actually works: deterrence messaging and corporate governance gaps · 4
Detection vs. consent: reframing the AI-nudification problem 3
The central technical/policy question for AI-generated non-consensual intimate imagery (NCII) is not "is this media fake" but "is this media of me" — existing report-and-attestation systems solve neither the search problem (discovering you've been depicted) nor prevention of the content being created in the first place.
framework🗣 Alyssa Redmiles, guest, Georgetown University computer science professorconviction: highAlyssa Redmiles
So what: Reframes the regulatory and product target from detection/authenticity tooling toward likeness-recognition and prevention infrastructure; deepfake-detection startups should note detection alone doesn't solve NCII.
“Is this media fake is different than is this media of me?” ▶ 0:02
Mathematical authenticity distinctions are legally and sociologically irrelevant to NCII harm — a generated image can be pixel-by-pixel completely different from any real photo of a person yet still produce identical reputational and psychological harm because it plays the same social role.
framework🗣 Kate Klonick, senior editor, Lawfareconviction: highKate Klonick
So what: Signals that legal liability frameworks built around 'is this authentic/real' (favoring generative-AI First Amendment defenses) may lose out to harm-based, dignitary-privacy frameworks — a key swing factor for AI-model legal risk.
“it is pixel by pixel a completely different image than a photo of Renee” ▶ 26:25
There is no agreed mathematical definition of "likeness," unlike membership inference (recovering exact training-data images), which can be formally defined — this gap is why technical fixes for NCII lag technical fixes for other AI privacy harms.
framework🗣 Alyssa Redmilesconviction: mediumAlyssa Redmiles
So what: Explains why NCII-specific safety tooling remains immature relative to other AI-privacy research (e.g., data extraction attacks) — a technical white-space for further research or startups.
Platform failures in practice: Meta, Grok, and the donut-glaze exploit 5
Meta AI shipped a feature that sent users push notifications encouraging them to generate AI content of other people by tagging their Instagram handle, using that person's public Instagram photos as the generation base layer, without the depicted person's consent or knowledge; the feature was reportedly pulled after protest from creator guilds.
data_point🗣 Renee DiResta, senior editor, Lawfareconviction: highMetaInstagram
So what: Illustrates concrete platform-level product risk in 'tag a person to generate' AI features and shows reputational/regulatory tail risk for similar consumer AI features.
On X/Grok, users discovered that requesting "donut glaze" be applied to a person functioned as a filter-bypass that routed around content moderation to generate extremely explicit sexualized imagery of real, non-consenting individuals directly beneath their posts, and some resulting images received millions of views.
data_point🗣 Renee DiRestaconviction: highGrok (xAI)Elon Musk
So what: A concrete, named jailbreak pattern (euphemism substitution) that safety teams and competitors should test for; also shows viral-scale distribution risk once an exploit is discovered.
“you could ask for donut glaze to be all over someone” ▶ 36:25
The Grok 'donut glaze' exploit triggered EU regulators to request specific data from X, making it one of the few AI-NCII incidents to draw formal regulatory action, even though it received little US mainstream media coverage.
causal🗣 Renee DiRestaconviction: mediumGrok (xAI)European Union
So what: EU regulators appear more responsive to concrete abuse-vector evidence than US press coverage — foreign regulatory action can be a leading indicator of platform policy change even absent domestic pressure.
Ashley St. Clair, an influencer, is suing Elon Musk over Grok-generated sexualized imagery of her; as of this recording the case had seen little docket movement and was reportedly stuck on a venue dispute.
data_point🗣 Renee DiRestaconviction: mediumAshley St. ClairElon MuskGrok (xAI)
So what: An active, named piece of litigation to track as a potential precedent-setting test of platform liability for AI-generated NCII.
In a forthcoming Center for Democracy and Technology (CDT) report, Stability AI (maker of Stable Diffusion, heavily used on Civitai) reportedly filed zero reports to NCMEC during a one-year study period, while OpenAI filed far more; the researchers argue report volume reflects monitoring intensity rather than actual abuse prevalence.
data_point🗣 Alyssa Redmilesconviction: mediumStability AICivitaiOpenAINCMECCenter for Democracy and Technology
So what: A concrete, comparable transparency metric for benchmarking model providers' trust-and-safety investment — near-zero NCMEC reporting from an open-weight leader is a red flag for governance diligence.
Why model-level technical fixes don't work yet 4
Stripping certain content categories (e.g., images of children) from training data does not reliably prevent a model from generating that content — in Redmiles's research it only raised generation difficulty from roughly three prompts to about twelve, and even that partial mitigation applies only to closed-weight models since open-weight models can have the concept reintroduced via fine-tuning.
data_point🗣 Alyssa Redmilesconviction: highAlyssa Redmiles
So what: Quantifies how weak training-data filtering is as a safety control — a concrete metric ('3 to 12 prompts') that undercuts marketing claims by model providers about training-data safeguards.
“it kind of makes it go from three prompts worth of difficult to 12 prompts worth of difficult” ▶ 42:44
The dominant safeguard foundation model providers actually deploy is input/output filtering (checking prompts and outputs for policy violations) rather than training-data interventions, but these filters are circumventable and are themselves often powered by unreliable AI classifiers.
causal🗣 Alyssa Redmilesconviction: highAlyssa Redmiles
So what: 'AI safety' marketing claims about generation prevention often rest on the weakest, most gameable layer of the stack — relevant for diligence on any 'safe AI image generation' vendor claims.
Anti-tampering and anti-fine-tuning research exists to make it harder to strip safety controls out of open-weight models, but none of it is mature enough for deployment and no platform currently uses it.
data_point🗣 Alyssa Redmilesconviction: mediumAlyssa Redmiles
So what: Identifies a genuine technical white-space — potential opportunity for AI-safety infrastructure research or tooling.
Open-weight model providers (e.g., those distributing via Hugging Face) do essentially no monitoring of downstream use of their models, and dedicated online hobbyist communities function as informal technical 'upskilling centers' that teach even non-technical users to compress, fine-tune, and run these models locally to create sexual content.
causal🗣 Alyssa Redmilesconviction: highHugging Face
So what: The technical barrier to producing harmful synthetic content has collapsed from 'requires ML expertise' to 'learnable via community tutorials,' expanding the population of potential bad actors — relevant to any risk model for open-weight model proliferation.
Inside the creator communities 4
Of 28 people interviewed across AI sexual-content-creation communities, 3 admitted to making non-consensual intimate imagery and several others said they had been asked to make it but declined, indicating NCII creation is a real but minority practice within larger, mostly consensual content-creation communities.
data_point🗣 Alyssa Redmilesconviction: exploratoryAlyssa Redmiles
So what: Provides a rare quantified base rate for how common NCII requests/creation are within adjacent legitimate communities, useful for calibrating the scale of the problem versus media narratives.
Moderators of AI-content-creation communities found that citing a platform's Terms of Service prohibition on NCII, and the legal risk posed by the U.S. TAKE IT DOWN Act, were the two most effective justifications for enforcing anti-NCII rules — more effective than ethical appeals, which often drew pushback from members framing moderation as censorship of sexual expression.
causal🗣 Alyssa Redmilesconviction: mediumTAKE IT DOWN Act
So what: Suggests platform-hosting ToS clauses plus clear federal criminal liability are the two most practically effective levers for community self-policing — a template other platforms or regulators could replicate.
“Excuse me, you're not supposed to kind of yuck my yum. Don't tell me what to do.” ▶ 21:04
Facial recognition technology's accuracy on AI-generated images is essentially unstudied — the underlying pixel distribution of a generated image differs statistically from a photograph, and there has been 'pretty much no research' on how well standard facial recognition algorithms (like TSA's 1:1 matching) perform on synthetic images.
data_point🗣 Alyssa Redmilesconviction: mediumAlyssa Redmiles
So what: A concrete research/product gap: any startup claiming facial-recognition-based detection of a person in AI-generated content is operating on an unvalidated assumption.
Because recognizing someone's 'likeness' is inherently a human-perception question rather than a pure biometric-matching one, Redmiles's team borrows psychology methodology — the 'familiar facial recognition task' used to diagnose prosopagnosia, plus vignette surveys — to measure whether a depiction counts as harmful, instead of relying on facial-recognition confidence scores.
framework🗣 Alyssa Redmilesconviction: mediumAlyssa Redmiles
So what: A reusable methodological approach for any company or regulator trying to operationalize 'likeness' as a legal/product threshold — psychology-based perception testing rather than pure computer-vision confidence.
The legal fork: authenticity rights vs. harm-based regulation 4
The unsettled legal question of whether generative AI outputs receive First Amendment protection (playing out in cases like Garcia v. Character Technologies) will likely determine NCII regulation's future: an authenticity-based framing (AI outputs as genuinely new, protected expression) would block much regulation/litigation targeting model outputs, while a harm-based dignitary-privacy framing would open a much broader set of legal remedies regardless of technical origin.
prediction🗣 Kate Klonickconviction: highKate KlonickGarcia v. Character Technologies
So what: A binary legal fork that materially changes AI model providers' liability exposure for generated-content harms — worth tracking as a leading indicator for regulatory/litigation risk across the sector.
Property/IP-based legal claims are historically more economically enforceable in court than privacy-based claims, which is why organized interest groups mobilize around IP theories first; Klonick draws a direct parallel to the Napster/Kazaa era, when the RIAA and MPAA sued over clear-cut copyright theft rather than dignitary harm, and predicts NCII/likeness disputes will similarly gravitate toward property-based legal theories.
thesis🗣 Kate Klonickconviction: mediumKate Klonick
So what: Predicts which legal theory (property/IP-style 'likeness theft' vs. privacy/dignitary harm) will actually dominate NCII litigation and case-law development going forward — relevant for legal-tech and media-rights investors.
A proposed update to the EU AI Act would reportedly bar deployment of models capable of generating NCII-type content unless 'sufficient safeguards' are implemented, but Redmiles's research found foundation model providers offer little transparency on how those safeguards are evaluated and remain vague about which threat model they're addressing (direct image generation, code generation, instructions, or API misuse).
data_point🗣 Alyssa Redmilesconviction: mediumEuropean UnionEU AI Act
So what: A pending EU rule change that could force disclosure/compliance costs on foundation model providers; watch how 'sufficient safeguard' gets defined, since current vagueness favors incumbents with more legal/compliance resources.
There is currently no clear legal framework defining who is authorized to red-team AI systems for NCII-generation vulnerabilities or what liability protection they have — simply claiming 'I was red-teaming' is not a legal defense, creating ambiguity for both in-house safety teams and outside researchers.
framework🗣 Renee DiRestaconviction: mediumRenee DiResta
So what: A concrete legal/policy gap: absent a safe-harbor framework for authorized AI safety testing, independent researchers face legal exposure that likely suppresses external red-teaming coverage of these harms.
What actually works: deterrence messaging and corporate governance gaps 4
Deterrence messaging — warnings shown to a user when their prompt appears to request illegal content (e.g., under the TAKE IT DOWN Act) — has shown strong promise in prior research (previously used for CSAM) and in early NCII studies, yet as of this recording zero of seven foundation model providers studied were deploying it for NCII, with only two having it on their roadmap.
prediction🗣 Alyssa Redmilesconviction: highAlyssa RedmilesTAKE IT DOWN Act
So what: Flags a specific, evidence-backed, currently-unimplemented intervention; any foundation model provider that ships deterrence messaging for NCII would be visibly ahead of all seven peers studied.
“none of the seven foundation model providers we looked at and none of the ones we interview interviewed were doing deterrence messaging for NCI. Two had it on the road map, but nobody else um did.” ▶ 52:50
Redmiles argues NCII policy should target the funding/monetization pipeline and 'purpose-built' tools (websites or fine-tuned models specifically built for nudification, identifiable by keywords, functionality, or training signatures) rather than broadly restricting sexual content generation, because overly broad prohibitions tend to push activity to less-controllable corners of the internet like 4chan or offshore sites.
thesis🗣 Alyssa Redmilesconviction: highAlyssa Redmiles
So what: A specific regulatory design principle — narrow, purpose-built-tool targeting over blanket content bans — that policymakers and platform trust-and-safety teams could use to avoid counterproductive enforcement.
Mainstream platforms (e.g., Google, Facebook) acting as single sign-on providers for nudification/NCII websites confer an implicit 'acceptability stamp' on those sites even without intending to, and ad networks and app-store listings for such apps similarly normalize them — a corporate-governance blind spot DiResta says companies have paid 'lip service' to fixing but continue to fail on.
causal🗣 Renee DiRestaconviction: mediumGoogleFacebook
So what: An auditable, concrete corporate-governance/ESG diligence item: check whether a platform's SSO, ad network, or app store is inadvertently distributing or legitimizing NCII-generation services.
“it gives this kind of like acceptability stamp to the platform even if it's not the intent of the single signon providers” ▶ 32:09
Testing whether an AI system will generate NCII/CSAM-adjacent content requires actually running realistic test prompts (e.g., a face-covered generated image checked for identifiability) rather than relying on self-reported compliance forms, because Redmiles found that in practice compliance forms are often filled out without real testing behind them.
causal🗣 Alyssa Redmilesconviction: mediumAlyssa Redmiles
So what: A due-diligence red flag for anyone evaluating a platform's safety claims: request evidence of actual red-team testing, not just a completed compliance questionnaire.
Lawfare Daily: Taking Stock of the Ukraine-Russia Talks54 min24 insights2026-07-14
▶ Watch on YouTube
Two Ukraine-Russia negotiation experts, Eric Ciaramella (Carnegie Endowment) and Samuel Charap (RAND), dissect why the Trump administration's US-mediated talks stalled after early 2026, arguing the US made two central errors — elevating the Donbas territorial question too early and floating a non-credible Article 5-style security guarantee — while debating more credible deterrence and negotiation-structure alternatives going forward.
Russia's War Optimism and Putin's Opaque Calculus · 5Negotiation Structure and the US as Mediator · 5How the Donbas Became the Central Sticking Point · 5The Credibility Problem With an Article 5-Style Guarantee · 5Deterrence Infrastructure and Snapback Mechanisms · 4
Russia's War Optimism and Putin's Opaque Calculus 5
Ukraine's deep drone strikes inside Russia and 'middle strikes' against the logistics corridor connecting Crimea to Russia have forced the Russian military and economic managers into trade-offs they did not previously have to make, marking a distinct new phase of pressure in the war.
causal🗣 Eric Ciaramella, Carnegie Endowmentconviction: mediumUkraineRussiaCrimea
So what: Sustained Ukrainian strike pressure on Russian logistics is a leading indicator to watch for shifts in Russian negotiating posture, though it has not yet visibly changed Kremlin strategy.
Despite mounting battlefield pressure on Russia, there is no evidence Putin has personally concluded he needs to change strategic course, since the war's trajectory ultimately hinges on one man's largely unknowable internal calculation.
thesis🗣 Eric Ciaramella, Carnegie Endowmentconviction: highVladimir PutinRussia
So what: Investors and policymakers should discount signals like oligarch essays or partial admissions of problems as insufficient to predict a Russian pivot until top-level decision-making visibly shifts.
“he himself is a black box and he may very well not have any idea himself what course he wants to take over the next 6 to 12 months.” ▶ 4:04
Putin has proven willing to subject the Russian population and economy to a much higher level of sustained pain than Western policymakers originally expected, suggesting economic pressure alone has a high threshold before it forces concessions.
data_point🗣 Eric Ciaramella, Carnegie Endowmentconviction: highVladimir PutinRussia
So what: Sanctions and economic-pressure-based strategies should be evaluated with a longer time horizon and higher pain tolerance assumption for the Kremlin than initially modeled.
“he's been willing to uh sustain a much higher level of pain than Western policy makers, I think, expected.” ▶ 6:19
Even if Putin privately recognizes Russia's battlefield and economic problems, he has strong incentives not to show diminished optimism publicly given upcoming Russian legislative elections and his reportedly sinking domestic popularity.
framework🗣 Samuel Charap, RAND Corporationconviction: mediumVladimir PutinRussia
So what: Public Kremlin bravado should not be read as a reliable signal of Putin's actual private assessment of the war's trajectory.
“He's very little incentive to let that on in public, right?” ▶ 8:16
Because Russia currently lacks any credible alternative to continued fighting, it is likely to keep prosecuting the war even if its underlying battlefield optimism has diminished, a pattern consistent with historical war-termination dynamics.
framework🗣 Samuel Charap, RAND Corporationconviction: highRussiaUkraineVladimir Putin
So what: A durable ceasefire is unlikely until negotiators construct a genuinely credible off-ramp for Russia, not just battlefield pressure alone.
“I don't think there is really a credible alternative that Putin is looking at for even to explore a process that could be there to explore what a credible alternative to continued fighting might look like.” ▶ 9:31
Negotiation Structure and the US as Mediator 5
A non-neutral US serving as mediator between Ukraine and Russia can theoretically work, drawing on the historical precedent of US mediation of the Yom Kippur War aftermath and Egypt-Israel/Syria-Israel agreements despite the US openly backing Israel militarily at the time.
framework🗣 Samuel Charap, RAND Corporationconviction: mediumUnited StatesIsraelEgypt
So what: Arguments that the US is disqualified from mediating because it backs Ukraine are historically weak; the real requirement is that both parties trust the mediator's objectives, not its neutrality.
The two lead US negotiators on the Ukraine-Russia talks have no dedicated negotiating team of their own and are simultaneously responsible for separate negotiations with Iran, a structural resourcing gap Eric Ciaramella views as a core reason the talks have underperformed.
data_point🗣 Eric Ciaramella, Carnegie Endowmentconviction: highSteve WitkoffJared KushnerIranUnited States
So what: Watch whether the US expands or professionalizes its negotiating team as a leading indicator of whether the next round of talks will be taken more seriously.
Jared Kushner's approach to the talks reflects a freewheeling, business-style negotiating philosophy carried over from his Abraham Accords experience, reinforced by his belief that traditional conflict-diplomacy structures (UN involvement, career experts) have failed.
framework🗣 Samuel Charap, RAND Corporationconviction: mediumJared KushnerAbraham Accords
So what: Expect continued avoidance of traditional multilateral diplomatic structures and expert staffing in future rounds unless this philosophy shifts.
The negotiations have proceeded episodically, with meetings roughly every six weeks rather than continuously, which Samuel Charap argues is inadequate for resolving complex, multi-issue international disputes that historically require regular, sustained engagement to build trade space across issues.
causal🗣 Samuel Charap, RAND Corporationconviction: highUnited StatesRussiaUkraine
So what: A shift to continuous, confidential, party-owned negotiation sessions would be a concrete signal that the process is being taken more seriously and could produce a settlement faster.
“you need to actually get people in a room like regularly, not every few months” ▶ 15:43
Negotiations have been conducted in disconnected silos (a separate US-Ukraine security track, a Europe-Ukraine track, and a US-Russia track), which prevents cross-issue trade-offs and leaves open the critical unknown of whether Russia has actually signed off on the US-Ukraine security guarantee that Zelensky has described as nearly fully agreed.
causal🗣 Samuel Charap, RAND Corporationconviction: highUnited StatesUkraineRussiaVolodymyr Zelensky
So what: Any 'nearly finalized' US-Ukraine agreement should be treated skeptically as a signal of overall deal progress until Russian buy-in to the broader package is confirmed.
“we have no idea if the Russians have signed off on this US-Ukraine security guarantee that apparently is 100% or 90% or 95% agreed” ▶ 41:00
How the Donbas Became the Central Sticking Point 5
The territorial question became the central issue in the talks largely because Steve Witkoff put it on the table early, in the Moscow meeting preceding the Anchorage summit, rather than because it was objectively Russia's top priority from the start.
causal🗣 Samuel Charap, RAND Corporationconviction: highSteve WitkoffRussiaDonbasAnchorage
So what: The prominence of the territorial issue in current talks is partly an artifact of US negotiating sequencing, not a fixed Russian redline, meaning renewed sequencing could de-emphasize it again.
Both analysts agree that elevating the Donbas territorial question to the center of negotiations at Anchorage was a major US negotiating error, because Russia came away believing the territory had effectively already been conceded and had incentive to 'pocket' that perceived concession and move the goalposts rather than compromise further.
contrarian🗣 Samuel Charap, RAND Corporationconviction: highSteve WitkoffRussiaDonbas
So what: Future negotiators should avoid granting apparent early concessions on high-value issues without securing reciprocal, binding commitments in the same exchange.
“a combination of Whitcoff's blunder and Russian craftiness uh about trying to get an early major concession and then pocket it and move the goalposts” ▶ 22:51
The two guests disagree on how locked-in Putin is to fully seizing Donbas: Samuel Charap argues Putin's repeated public commitments since the 2022 annexation announcement have boxed him into treating full territorial capture as near-mandatory, while Eric Ciaramella believes Putin remains firmly enough in control of Russia's information environment to redefine 'victory' and retreat from that framing whenever it suits him.
thesis🗣 Eric Ciaramella, Carnegie Endowmentconviction: mediumVladimir PutinDonbas
So what: Do not assume a Russian territorial climbdown is politically impossible for Putin; watch for propaganda narrative shifts as the real tell rather than treating stated Donbas demands as immovable.
“there's a lot of bravado in public up until the moment that they make a pivot, and then they make you think that the pivot was the plan all along.” ▶ 26:01
Russian pro-war commentators and 'patriotic camp' Telegram accounts are already circulating narratives that Russia's original 'special military operation' objectives were achieved and that a long-term pause or armistice would itself constitute a Russian victory, providing Putin a ready-made off-ramp narrative independent of actual territorial gains.
data_point🗣 Eric Ciaramella, Carnegie Endowmentconviction: mediumVladimir PutinRussia
So what: A Russian ceasefire acceptance could arrive with little additional territorial concession, since domestic narrative management gives Putin flexibility to declare victory on existing terms.
A proposed negotiating tactic is to shelve the zero-sum territorial question first, work through other political and security issues, and then retest whether the territorial dispute becomes easier to resolve once mutual understandings exist elsewhere.
framework🗣 Samuel Charap, RAND Corporationconviction: mediumDonbasRussiaUkraine
So what: Negotiators (and observers) should watch for a sequencing shift that defers territory to gauge whether this untested approach gains traction in the next round.
The Credibility Problem With an Article 5-Style Guarantee 5
The Trump administration's proposed Article 5-like bilateral US-Ukraine security guarantee, which Zelensky says is 100% agreed in text and under which the US would determine future measures including armed force if Russia attacks again, is fundamentally not credible because it promises future US direct military intervention that the US is currently unwilling to undertake.
thesis🗣 Samuel Charap, RAND Corporationconviction: highUnited StatesUkraineVolodymyr ZelenskyRussia
So what: A headline 'security guarantee' announcement should not be treated as materially reducing invasion risk unless backed by demonstrated present-day commitments, not just paper promises of future action.
“the the basic problem with it is it's not credible.” ▶ 32:08
If an Article 5-style US-Ukraine guarantee were tested by Russia and the US failed to act, it would call into question all of the US's other global security commitments and could embroil the US in a war with Russia that risks escalation to the nuclear level.
causal🗣 Samuel Charap, RAND Corporationconviction: highUnited StatesRussiaNATO
So what: The downside tail risk of an untested, overextended security guarantee is not just a failed Ukraine policy but a credibility shock to the broader US alliance system.
Genuine NATO Article 5 deterrence rests on decades of joint political and military commitments, force stationing, and contingency planning; a standalone Ukraine guarantee would lack all of that supporting infrastructure, making it, in Eric Ciaramella's words, 'a check written without the money in the account.'
contrarian🗣 Eric Ciaramella, Carnegie Endowmentconviction: highNATOSteve WitkoffRussia
So what: Markets and policymakers should not price a Ukraine security guarantee as equivalent to actual NATO membership-level deterrence.
“a check that would be written without the money in the account” ▶ 35:16
Rather than a broad Article 5-style pledge, Eric Ciaramella argues a more credible approach would codify already-demonstrated forms of US support (long-term Ukrainian military capacity building, allied-resourced weapons stockpiles, and defense production commitments) under a dedicated governance mechanism resembling a 'North Atlantic Council' for Ukraine.
framework🗣 Eric Ciaramella, Carnegie Endowmentconviction: mediumUnited StatesUkraineNATO
So what: Watch for institutional proposals (a Ukraine-specific governance body, pre-positioned stockpiles) as a more credible and durable deterrence signal than headline guarantee language.
The two analysts disagree on whether a US-Ukraine security guarantee needs Russian sign-off to be effective: Samuel Charap sees Russian buy-in as important (unlike the 2022 Istanbul framework where Russia would have been a formal party), while Eric Ciaramella argues the guarantee can be built purely through US-Ukraine channels and then simply presented to Russia as an established fact on the ground.
thesis🗣 Samuel Charap, RAND Corporationconviction: mediumRussiaUnited StatesUkraine
So what: The structural design choice of whether to negotiate a guarantee with or without Russia at the table will shape whether it functions as a genuine deterrent or an unverified unilateral pledge.
Deterrence Infrastructure and Snapback Mechanisms 4
A bipartisan provision in this year's Senate Intelligence Authorization Act, backed by Senator Cotton and Senator Bennet, lays out a three-phase framework for US intelligence support to Ukraine: continued real-time targeting intelligence until a ceasefire, a transition to longer-term capacity-building support during an armistice, and reversion to full real-time targeting support if Russia re-invades.
data_point🗣 Eric Ciaramella, Carnegie Endowmentconviction: highUnited StatesUkraineSenator CottonSenator Bennet
So what: This legislative provision is a concrete, trackable proxy for how credible future US deterrence commitments to Ukraine will be structured, and is worth monitoring as it moves through Congress.
“To me, that is eminently reasonable, and it does codify a future commitment that I think is credible in this case.” ▶ 48:53
Any future 'snapback' of US support to Ukraine would start from a much higher baseline than February 2022, when the US had only provided Javelins and Stingers, since Ukraine has since received F-16s, tanks, HIMARS, and ATACMS — meaning pre-positioned stockpiles and resourced defense production plans could make the threat of renewed support more credible to Russia.
data_point🗣 Eric Ciaramella, Carnegie Endowmentconviction: highUkraineUnited States
So what: Assess future deterrence proposals against this higher capability baseline rather than the pre-invasion 2022 starting point, since the credible 'threat' of resumed aid is now materially larger.
An effective snapback mechanism should be 'total,' conditioning all Russian benefits from any agreement — including political and military restraint commitments, not just resumed Western aid to Ukraine — on continued Russian ceasefire compliance.
framework🗣 Samuel Charap, RAND Corporationconviction: mediumRussiaUkraine
So what: Deal terms should be evaluated for whether Russian-side benefits (not just Ukrainian-side aid pauses) are also reversible on violation, which is a stronger deterrent design than one-sided snapback.
No single deterrence mechanism (Article 5-style guarantees, intelligence snapback, stockpiles) should be treated as a silver bullet against a determined Russia; a capable, well-armed Ukrainian military remains the primary deterrent, with other measures serving as supplements.
thesis🗣 Samuel Charap, RAND Corporationconviction: highUkraineRussia
So what: Long-term investment in Ukraine's own military capacity should be weighted as the core deterrence variable, with security guarantees and snapback clauses as secondary reinforcement rather than the primary safeguard.
“we're not talking about silver bullets of deterrence that if Russia is determined to go to war, these are not going to like be insurmountable roadblocks.” ▶ 51:53
Lawfare Daily: AI Targeting Systems Are Coming—But Not as Fast as You Think47 min22 insights2026-07-15
▶ Watch on YouTube
Carnegie's Steve Feldstein argues that despite the Pentagon's Maven-driven strike tempo in the Iran war, full AI targeting systems require decades of deep infrastructure (ISR, compute, trained algorithms) and won't diffuse to other militaries quickly like software does; instead, capital-rich, threat-exposed states like the UAE are the most likely near-term adopters, while chip and normative pressure -- not model export controls -- are the only realistic levers to slow proliferation.
What building an AI targeting system actually requires · 5Compute and cloud infrastructure as the hidden bottleneck · 3Accountability is diffused across a 'stack,' not a breakable chain · 3Diffusion economics: nuclear-weapon pace, not chatbot pace · 3Drones vs. targeting systems: two very different diffusion curves · 2Policy levers: chips and norms, not model export controls · 3Human oversight is becoming a legal fiction · 3
What building an AI targeting system actually requires 5
During the 38-day Iran war, the Pentagon's use of Palantir's Maven surged over 4,000% to roughly 20 billion tokens a day, with 13,000 targets struck, but this reflects a uniquely mature US infrastructure rather than a capability any military can now replicate.
data_point🗣 guest, Steve Feldstein (Carnegie Endowment)conviction: highPalantirMavenPentagonIran
So what: Headline strike-tempo numbers from the Iran war overstate how close other militaries are to matching US AI-targeting capability -- useful counterweight to "AI warfare is proliferating" narratives.
“the Pentagon's use of Maven surged to something like what 20 billion tokens a day and it was like over 4,000% increase and 13,000 targets struck” ▶ 3:10
Palantir's Maven is a user-interface layer that aggregates disparate intelligence streams into a click-through workflow from target identification to strike authorization, rather than a standalone AI model comparable to a chatbot like Claude.
framework🗣 guest, Steve Feldsteinconviction: highPalantirMaven
So what: Investors evaluating Palantir's defense-software moat should understand Maven's value is in orchestration/UX over existing data pipelines, not in a proprietary foundation model.
“Palunteer's Maven is essentially a user interface software packaging that helps to bring together different streams of data to facilitate decision-m” ▶ 5:44
A working AI targeting system requires a deep, multi-decade intelligence-surveillance-reconnaissance (ISR) apparatus combining real-time satellite imagery, signals intelligence, phone intercepts, and social media (e.g., Telegram in the Ukraine war) before any algorithm can be usefully trained on it.
framework🗣 guest, Steve Feldsteinconviction: highISRTelegramUkraine
So what: This is the real barrier to entry for AI-targeting diffusion -- it favors incumbents with pre-existing surveillance infrastructure over countries simply buying software off the shelf.
Little vetting occurs on the raw intelligence fed into targeting algorithms -- the operating premise, dating back to Palantir's founding in 2003 for post-9/11 counterterrorism, is that humans cannot sift the data volume so the system is trusted to filter noise from signal.
causal🗣 guest, Steve Feldsteinconviction: mediumPalantir
So what: Raises an accuracy/liability risk investors and policymakers should weight when assessing targeting-software vendors: the system's core value proposition (handling unvettable data volume) is also its core error source.
“essentially you're put inputting a bunch of noise and then you're hoping the system will be able to kind of pierce through the noise and derive insights” ▶ 9:52
US military computer-vision targeting models that achieved roughly 70% success rates in dusty, desert-like theaters such as Afghanistan fell to under 30% when applied to dense-rainforest terrain like the Philippines, showing target-recognition accuracy is highly terrain- and context-dependent.
data_point🗣 guest, Steve Feldsteinconviction: highMavenAfghanistanPhilippines
So what: A concrete, quotable data point on why AI targeting can't simply be exported turnkey to a new theater -- retraining for local terrain is a real cost and time constraint on diffusion speed.
Compute and cloud infrastructure as the hidden bottleneck 3
Israel's Unit 8200 built a surveillance capacity intercepting roughly a million phone calls a day from Palestinians in the West Bank and Gaza but ran out of internal server capacity, forcing it to contract Microsoft to store and process the data on Azure servers based in the Netherlands and Ireland.
data_point🗣 guest, Steve Feldsteinconviction: highUnit 8200IsraelMicrosoftMicrosoft Azure
So what: Even a top-tier military/intelligence service is compute-constrained and dependent on US hyperscaler capacity, underscoring how much of the modern kill chain runs through commercial cloud infrastructure rather than sovereign systems.
“it soon ran out of space on his servers uh internally. So right so it didn't have the data.” ▶ 14:15
Project Nimbus, the 2021 cloud-computing contract between Google, AWS, and the Israeli Defense Forces, is worth $1.2 billion and supplies machine-learning tools and storage/infrastructure capacity that most countries lack the resources to match.
data_point🗣 guest, Steve Feldsteinconviction: highGoogleAmazon Web ServicesIsraeli Defense ForcesProject Nimbus
So what: A concrete revenue data point on defense-cloud contracts and evidence that the compute/storage layer -- not the AI model -- is the scarce, capital-intensive chokepoint in military AI, favoring the largest hyperscalers.
“I think project Nimbus like this cloud computing contract that was signed between Google, a AWS and the IDF, the Israeli uh defense forces in 2021 is a $1.2 billion contract.” ▶ 15:01
Microsoft withdrew from an Azure-related contract tied to Israeli military targeting operations after reputational pressure from a Guardian investigation, suggesting cloud providers view themselves as bearing some culpability in the kill chain despite not directly authorizing strikes.
causal🗣 guest, Steve Feldsteinconviction: mediumMicrosoftIsrael
So what: Signals reputational/ESG risk for hyperscalers supplying military AI infrastructure -- a factor investors in Microsoft, Google, or Amazon should track as press scrutiny of defense-cloud contracts grows.
“when called out upon it reputationally by a guardian investigation they have pulled out from the contract” ▶ 20:15
Accountability is diffused across a 'stack,' not a breakable chain 3
The modern military kill chain is better understood as a diffuse web or stack of interdependent private and state actors (cloud providers, software interface makers, drone/munitions manufacturers) rather than a linear chain with a single choke point that can be broken to stop a strike.
contrarian🗣 guest, Steve Feldsteinconviction: highPalantirMicrosoftGoogleAmazonOpenAIAnthropic
So what: Policy or activist efforts aimed at a single vendor (e.g., pressuring one cloud provider to exit a contract) are unlikely to meaningfully disrupt AI-enabled targeting because redundant commercial suppliers exist across the stack.
“there really isn't a chain. It's kind of a web or it's kind of a stack and the stack is diffuse and has many options on how it can go.” ▶ 22:41
Legal and military accountability for AI-assisted strikes ultimately rests with the Pentagon as the 'owner' of the kill chain, even though private vendors like Palantir, Microsoft, and Google supply the software and infrastructure that shape decision-making.
thesis🗣 guest, Steve Feldsteinconviction: highPentagonPalantirMicrosoftGoogle
So what: For regulatory or litigation risk assessments, the accountable party in AI-targeting harms is the commissioning military, not the software/cloud vendor -- relevant to how liability risk should be priced for defense-tech suppliers.
“there is an owner of the kill chain, that's the Pentagon.” ▶ 23:45
Palantir's Maven interface, which compresses the targeting process into roughly four clicks, sits closer to the 'end product' of the kill chain than the underlying data infrastructure, making it a more direct point of decision-bias and accountability than cloud or surveillance providers further upstream.
framework🗣 guest, Steve Feldsteinconviction: mediumPalantirMaven
So what: Differentiates Palantir's exposure/accountability profile from that of pure infrastructure providers like Microsoft or Google in any future regulatory or reputational scrutiny of military AI.
“that interface that puts it all together, that compresses the killchain, that gives you four clicks towards uh destroying a target, that's probably closer in line ... to the end product” ▶ 21:07
Diffusion economics: nuclear-weapon pace, not chatbot pace 3
AI targeting systems are more analogous to nuclear weapons diffusion -- slow, capital-intensive, closed, with costs not commensurate with most countries' benefit -- than to consumer AI diffusion, where an innovation like ChatGPT went from a handful of developers to global mass adoption within a couple of years.
framework🗣 guest, Steve Feldsteinconviction: highChatGPTOpenAI
So what: Investors and policymakers should not extrapolate the rapid global diffusion curve of consumer AI onto military AI targeting; the relevant comparison for pacing expectations is nuclear proliferation, which argues against near-term widespread military AI adoption.
“the converse of that is the spread of something like open AI's you know uh like chatbot like chat GPT ... that's an example of rapid democratized diffusion across the globe” ▶ 26:33
The UAE is the most likely next adopter of full AI targeting capability because it combines political incentive (a volatile neighborhood and unpredictable adversaries), pre-existing surveillance infrastructure, extensive AI data-center investment, and a domestic military-industrial push (its EDGE conglomerate) to build homegrown drones and an arms industry.
prediction🗣 guest, Steve Feldsteinconviction: mediumUAEEDGE
So what: A specific, actionable forecast for defense-tech and geopolitical-risk watchers on which state is next in line for AI-targeting capability -- relevant to Gulf defense-spending and AI-infrastructure themes.
Saudi Arabia, India, and Brazil are named as the next tier of 'rising powers' with sufficient capacity to plausibly adopt AI targeting systems in the coming years, behind the UAE.
prediction🗣 guest, Steve Feldsteinconviction: lowSaudi ArabiaIndiaBrazil
So what: Extends the watchlist of states for defense-AI-adoption tracking beyond the primary UAE call, useful for scenario planning around future defense-AI export/contract flows.
Drones vs. targeting systems: two very different diffusion curves 2
Combat drones diffused far faster than AI targeting systems will -- from $30 million exquisite US Reaper drones used for signature strikes, to Turkey's low-cost TB2 medium-range drones, to Ukraine's 2022-era mass adoption of $500 consumer quadcopters retrofitted with munitions -- because they required comparatively little specialized infrastructure to replicate at scale.
causal🗣 guest, Steve Feldsteinconviction: highTurkeyTB2Ukraine
So what: Explains why cheap hardware-centric military technologies (drones) proliferate on a completely different timeline than data/infrastructure-heavy AI targeting systems -- a useful mental model for forecasting which defense-AI segments commoditize fastest.
“a very simple quadcopter that you could buy for $500 off of Amazon, you could outfit it with a basic munition and create tremendous damage” ▶ 33:33
Full comprehensive AI targeting systems like Maven will likely remain limited to a small group of high-capacity militaries for the near future, while the nearer-term proliferation risk is 'last-mile' autonomous weapons -- e.g., drones already deployed in Ukraine that use onboard computer vision to lock onto targets after losing contact with their human operator.
prediction🗣 guest, Steve Feldsteinconviction: mediumMavenUkraine
So what: Redirects near-term proliferation/regulatory attention away from Maven-style comprehensive systems and toward edge-device autonomous targeting, where oversight gaps are emerging first.
Policy levers: chips and norms, not model export controls 3
Export controls on AI software and models are not a realistic chokepoint for slowing military AI diffusion because 'the cat's out of the bag' -- weights, distilled models, and APIs are effectively globally accessible already.
contrarian🗣 guest, Steve Feldsteinconviction: high
So what: Pushes back on a policy consensus favoring model-level export controls; investors and policymakers evaluating AI-safety/export-control proposals should weight chip and infrastructure controls far more heavily than software restrictions.
“I think that at least on software and model I think the cat's out of the bat. I don't really think there's much we can do on that front.” ▶ 36:55
Restricting the flow of cutting-edge chips and data-center/surveillance infrastructure can't stop major militaries from acquiring AI targeting capability but can meaningfully slow second- and third-tier state actors who lack the capital to route around such controls.
framework🗣 guest, Steve Feldsteinconviction: medium
So what: Suggests chip export-control policy (e.g., US restrictions relevant to Nvidia and similar suppliers) has real teeth against smaller/poorer state actors even if ineffective against top-tier militaries -- a nuance for assessing chip-export-policy efficacy.
“you won't stop major militaries, but you can stop second and third tier actors maybe from acquiring as easily as they would like these types of uh these types of systems” ▶ 38:07
Normative and public political pressure can raise the effective cost of AI-targeting acquisition for states -- turning it into a resource trade-off where leaders weigh, e.g., spending $150 million on a system against the political price of pursuing it -- even absent binding treaties.
thesis🗣 guest, Steve Feldsteinconviction: medium
So what: Identifies reputational/political pressure campaigns (media investigations, activism) as a genuine, if soft, lever on AI-targeting proliferation -- relevant to how quickly demand for these systems might grow among mid-tier states.
“the more you can start to to create a political cost for countries that are thinking about getting them, the more that uh it becomes a resource trade-off question” ▶ 38:32
Human oversight is becoming a legal fiction 3
Meaningful human oversight of AI-assisted targeting is already eroding for the most sophisticated militaries -- reviews compressed to as little as seven seconds do not constitute genuine human control compared to the hours-long, multi-step vetting process (analyst, lawyer, commander sign-off) that used to precede a strike.
contrarian🗣 guest, Steve Feldsteinconviction: highMaven
So what: Undercuts the policy assumption that 'human in the loop' requirements provide real safety guardrails as targeting cycles compress from hours to minutes -- relevant to how much weight regulators/investors should give HITL compliance claims.
“I don't know if 7-second reviews count as meaningful humanight when it comes to the targeting cycle that we've described with with Maven.” ▶ 44:03
International humanitarian law frameworks have not kept pace with AI-enabled targeting speed, leaving a growing accountability gap as systems like Lavender generate kill lists near-automatically and Maven compresses detection-to-strike into minutes.
thesis🗣 guest, Steve Feldsteinconviction: mediumLavenderMaven
So what: Flags an unresolved legal/regulatory risk area that could eventually produce new binding rules or litigation exposure for both militaries and the private vendors supplying targeting software.
A prevailing but contestable theory driving investment in ever-faster AI targeting is that speed and scale determine who wins modern wars, such that sacrificing pace for greater human accountability is framed as a path to losing -- a framing Feldstein calls a partly false choice.
contrarian🗣 guest, Steve Feldsteinconviction: medium
So what: Identifies the core strategic justification driving defense-AI capex (speed-wins-wars doctrine) that investors should recognize as a debated assumption, not settled fact, when evaluating demand durability for targeting software.
“I think that's a little bit of a false choice, but I I don't think you can dismiss those those arguments either.” ▶ 43:36
Scaling Laws: New Paths to Social Mobility with Joe Seddon47 min24 insights2026-07-14
▶ Watch on YouTube
UK edtech founder Joe Seddon describes how Zero Gravity's non-invasive targeting algorithm has scaled from a 150-student pilot to placing over 23,000 low-income UK students into elite universities and now into jobs at firms like McKinsey and Rolls-Royce. He warns that AI-driven entry-level job cuts (~8%/year, 140 applicants per UK graduate role) could sever the very career ladder his students need, framing the problem as existential for US/UK competitiveness against rivals like China.
Measuring and Comparing Social Mobility · 3Zero Gravity's Non-Invasive Talent-Identification Algorithm · 5Zero Gravity's Growth and Expansion into Employment · 3AI's Squeeze on the Entry-Level Job Ladder · 6AI as a Potential Equalizer in Education and Hiring · 4Civilizational Stakes and Policy Responses · 3
Measuring and Comparing Social Mobility 3
Goldman Sachs research on the intergenerational elasticity of earnings shows you can predict about 45% of a UK person's earnings from what their parents earned, versus only about 30% in the Netherlands.
data_point🗣 Joe Seddonconviction: highGoldman SachsUKNetherlands
So what: Benchmarks how mobile the UK economy is relative to Western European peers, useful context for social-policy and human-capital investment theses.
“in the UK, you can essentially across the population predict around 45% of people's earnings based on knowing what their parents earned” ▶ 6:33
Despite the American Dream narrative of mobility, the US actually has more intergenerational income stickiness than the UK on the same Goldman Sachs elasticity-of-earnings measure.
contrarian🗣 Joe Seddonconviction: mediumUSUKGoldman Sachs
So what: Challenges the assumption that the US is more socially mobile than Europe, relevant for any thesis built on US meritocracy narratives.
“the US is actually higher than the UK. So that in the US there is kind of more of a intergenerational stickiness of of income” ▶ 6:44
Socioeconomic background, not gender or race, is the single strongest predictor of who gets into elite universities such as Oxford, Cambridge, or the Ivy League.
framework🗣 Joe Seddonconviction: highOxford UniversityCambridge UniversityIvy League
So what: Suggests admissions or diversity interventions targeting only race or gender will under-address the real access gap.
“the the the biggest marker is is not gender, it's not even race, it's socio-economic background.” ▶ 8:20
Zero Gravity's Non-Invasive Talent-Identification Algorithm 5
Zero Gravity runs an algorithm connected to roughly one-third of all UK state-funded schools that each year flags disadvantaged students with elite-university potential, using indirect signals like home-postcode socioeconomic and crime data and local university-progression rates rather than direct financial disclosures.
framework🗣 Joe Seddonconviction: highZero GravityUK
So what: A scalable, privacy-light sourcing model for identifying underrepresented talent that other markets or HR-tech platforms could replicate.
Zero Gravity benchmarks a student's academic results against their own school's peer average rather than absolute scores, so a top performer at a weak school (two standard deviations above their school's mean) gets flagged even though a bare resume would make them look worse than students from stronger schools.
framework🗣 Joe Seddonconviction: highZero Gravity
So what: A methodology any talent-sourcing model could borrow to correct for school-quality bias hidden in raw credentials.
Joe Seddon argues conventional resume-based hiring is itself more biased than algorithmic targeting because it unconsciously favors candidates whose families had the resources to "game" the education system.
contrarian🗣 Joe Seddonconviction: highZero Gravity
So what: Reframes debates over AI-driven candidate screening by pointing out the status-quo baseline is not neutral either.
“if you just look at a CV or just look at a resume, then you are going to naturally skew towards people who are able to sort of game the education system through through resources.”
UK corporate and university diversity programs built around race or ethnicity alone have often ended up recruiting affluent members of underrepresented ethnic groups, raising diversity numbers without addressing underlying socioeconomic disadvantage.
causal🗣 Joe Seddonconviction: mediumUK
So what: A cautionary data point for any DEI or talent program that measures success by demographic representation rather than socioeconomic mix.
Roughly three to four years before this conversation, China's government banned its entire private tutoring industry overnight, reportedly out of concern that tutoring access was distorting equality of opportunity.
data_point🗣 Joe Seddonconviction: mediumChina
So what: Signals a geopolitical dimension to human-capital policy that Western policymakers may be underweighting relative to a strategic rival.
“three or four years ago they essentially overnight banned their entire private tutoring industry in their country.” ▶ 44:22
Zero Gravity's Growth and Expansion into Employment 3
Zero Gravity launched in 2018 with a first cohort of about 150 low-income UK students, of whom 50 were admitted to Oxford or Cambridge.
data_point🗣 Joe Seddonconviction: highZero GravityOxford UniversityCambridge University
So what: Demonstrates a strong early proof-of-concept conversion rate (roughly one-third to Oxbridge) that underpinned the organization's later scale-up.
Zero Gravity has since scaled to support more than 23,000 low-income students in accessing leading UK universities.
data_point🗣 Joe Seddonconviction: highZero Gravity
So what: Indicates meaningful scale for an education-access organization, relevant to anyone assessing its growth trajectory or funding case.
“we've supported over 23,000 students from low-income backgrounds to access leading universities in the UK.” ▶ 25:46
Zero Gravity has expanded beyond university admissions into job placement, partnering with employers including McKinsey, Rolls-Royce, Snapchat, and Roche to widen access to graduate schemes and internships.
data_point🗣 Joe Seddonconviction: highZero GravityMcKinseyRolls-RoyceSnapchatRoche
So what: Shows the organization moving from admissions into employment placement, a natural expansion of an education-to-career platform's addressable market.
AI's Squeeze on the Entry-Level Job Ladder 6
Across the large UK enterprise employers Zero Gravity works with, entry-level job postings have fallen for two consecutive years at roughly 8% year-on-year.
data_point🗣 Joe Seddonconviction: mediumUK
So what: A concrete, employer-sourced data point on AI-linked entry-level hiring contraction worth tracking for staffing, HR-tech, or education-sector exposure.
“there's been two consecutive years now of a reduction in the amount of entry-level jobs roughly around the rate of 8% reduction year-on-year.” ▶ 32:07
The average UK graduate scheme now receives about 140 applications per role, giving applicants under a 1% chance of success, a lower success rate than getting into university.
data_point🗣 Joe Seddonconviction: mediumUK
So what: Quantifies how much more competitive entry-level hiring has become than higher-education admissions, a leading indicator for graduate underemployment.
“In the UK on average, there's 140 applications for every graduate role.” ▶ 32:20
Companies face a prisoner's-dilemma incentive to cut entry-level headcount and instead poach already-trained mid-level talent from competitors, which benefits any single firm but collectively destroys the talent pipeline every firm will need in a decade.
framework🗣 Joe Seddonconviction: high
So what: A mental model for why AI-driven entry-level cuts could be a coordination failure requiring policy or industry-level intervention rather than something any single employer can fix alone.
“it is a prisoner's dilemma in many ways where every company kind of has an individual incentive to cut back an entry-level workforce.” ▶ 37:44
