Substituting entry-level headcount with AI can lift a public company's operating margin by roughly 3-4%, creating a strong quarterly-earnings incentive to shrink graduate hiring even where it damages long-term talent pipelines.
causal🗣 Joe Seddonconviction: medium
So what: Gives a rough magnitude for the margin incentive driving corporate entry-level cuts, relevant to modeling near-term earnings tailwinds from AI-driven headcount reduction.
“drive another, no, 3 or 4% in your operating margins” ▶ 37:03
Meta's program to retrain laid-off software engineers for data-center construction work illustrates that AI-driven job transitions are not seamless, since moving from software engineering to an electrician- or plumber-type role is a large skills leap.
contrarian🗣 Joe Seddonconviction: highMeta
So what: Cautions against assuming corporate reskilling announcements will smoothly absorb AI-displaced workers, relevant to labor-market and consumer-spending forecasts.
“it is not a seamless transition from being a software engineer to suddenly turning into a electrician or a or a plumber.” ▶ 40:06
Historically, when the demands of an economy shift, retraining and reskilling rarely fully absorb displaced workers, and current UK/US infrastructure is not built to convert AI-driven job losses in one sector into new hires in another.
thesis🗣 Joe Seddonconviction: highUKUS
So what: Implies structural-unemployment risk from AI job displacement should be weighted more heavily than optimistic "AI creates as many jobs as it destroys" narratives.
AI as a Potential Equalizer in Education and Hiring 4
An Anthropic study of Claude Code usage found the users getting the most value are not the most technically skilled but those with the deepest domain expertise in their field, suggesting domain expertise is currently more AI-resistant than raw technical skill.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: mediumAnthropicClaude
So what: Implies workforce-development priority should shift toward deepening domain expertise rather than only teaching coding/AI tool use, since expertise appears to compound AI leverage.
“Instead, it's the folks who have the deepest domain expertise for whatever it is that they're actually working on.” ▶ 2:16
AI-driven labor- and university-matching platforms could reduce hiring bias against candidates who lack elite social signals such as a "received pronunciation" accent, which today can eliminate UK candidates the moment they speak in an interview.
thesis🗣 Joe Seddonconviction: mediumZero GravityUK
So what: A potential HR-tech use case: stripping non-meritocratic signals like accent from candidate evaluation.
“under a traditional interview context, the second you walk into a room and let's say you don't have the right accent, game over.” ▶ 30:18
The fundamental structure of how people get educated and hired has not changed in roughly 50 years; recruitment is now digital but otherwise remains a manual, in-person, 1980s-style process, which Joe Seddon says explains stagnant social mobility across the Western world for over a generation.
causal🗣 Joe Seddonconviction: high
So what: Frames education/hiring systems as ripe for AI-driven structural disruption rather than incremental digitization, relevant to edtech and HR-tech investment theses.
“the fundamental structure of how people get these opportunities is is remarkably similar to how it was in the 1980s.” ▶ 31:33
Texas-based Alpha Schools, an AI-driven private schooling model, is producing extraordinary early academic results, but so far predominantly for students from very affluent backgrounds, raising the risk that AI-in-education gains stay confined to the private sector unless government adopts similar approaches in state-funded schools.
anecdote🗣 Joe Seddonconviction: mediumAlpha Schools
So what: A live case study to track for evidence of AI-native schooling results and equity outcomes as it scales beyond affluent early adopters.
Civilizational Stakes and Policy Responses 3
Joe Seddon frames failure to preserve intergenerational opportunity as existential for the US and UK, drawing a parallel to how past civilizations from Egypt to Rome to European empires declined once opportunity became concentrated at the top.
thesis🗣 Joe Seddonconviction: highUKUS
So what: Elevates social-mobility policy from a welfare issue to a geopolitical/civilizational-competitiveness issue, relevant to long-horizon macro and policy-risk framing.
“civilizations have kind of risen and fallen over time based on their ability to kind of distribute opportunity across the populace.” ▶ 43:01
Income-sharing agreements, where an employer funds a graduate's training in exchange for a share of that person's future earnings, are proposed as one structural mechanism to give companies "skin in the game" in a worker's long-term development, alongside new apprenticeship and national-service-style programs.
framework🗣 hostconviction: exploratory
So what: A concrete policy/financing mechanism worth tracking as a potential new workforce-development model tied to AI-driven labor disruption.
Anthropic's "Claude Corps" program, which trains young people to teach AI skills to others, and Meta's pipeline training laid-off workers for data-center construction jobs are cited as early private-sector prototypes for AI-era workforce transition programs that the speakers argue need broader public coordination to scale.
anecdote🗣 hostconviction: exploratoryAnthropicMeta
So what: Early private-sector experiments worth monitoring as templates for scalable AI-transition workforce policy and as a signal of where large AI/tech firms are directing investment.
Lawfare Daily: What Do the Russians Actually Think About the War?44 min22 insights2026-07-16
▶ Watch on YouTube
CSIS's Maria Snegovaya argues Russian war-support polling is broadly reliable and splits supporters into a hardcore 'hawk' minority (15-30%) and a larger, status-quo 'loyalist' majority (35-50%) whose passive support hinges on personal economic well-being rather than ideology; Ukraine's refinery strikes and the resulting gasoline shortages are eroding loyalist support faster than battlefield casualties ever did, but a real breaking point likely requires a full-blown economic crisis.
Reliability of Russian war-support polling · 3Hawks vs. loyalists framework · 5Economic shocks outweigh military shocks · 3Refinery strikes and the gasoline shortage as leverage · 4Casualty tolerance and the elusive breaking point · 3Regime response and the limits of loyalist discontent · 4
Reliability of Russian war-support polling 3
Methodological studies using techniques like list experiments (which let respondents mask sensitive views) have found no major systematic bias in Russian war-support polling since 2022; discrepancies top out around 10 percentage points in the most skeptical studies and zero in others.
data_point🗣 Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellowconviction: highRussiaLevada Center
So what: Investors and analysts tracking Russian sentiment as a war-termination signal can treat headline polling numbers as directionally trustworthy rather than dismissing them as propaganda artifacts.
“There are no major biases in the Russian polling.” ▶ 4:32
Passive alignment with Russia's war has fluctuated between roughly 70-80% earlier in the conflict and has eroded into the mid-60s in recent years, even after adjusting for an estimated 10 percentage points of respondents who may misrepresent their true views.
data_point🗣 Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellowconviction: highRussia
So what: A multi-year downward trend in even the most conservative reading of war support is a slow-moving but trackable indicator of Kremlin political capacity.
Extreme Scan, an independent (non-state-funded) Russian pollster run by sociologist Elena Koneva who has since been designated a 'foreign agent' by the Russian state, focuses on borderline regions like Belgorod and Kursk near the front line, making it the best available proxy for how ordinary Russians react to direct war exposure such as drone strikes on refineries.
data_point🗣 Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellowconviction: highExtreme ScanElena KonevaBelgorodKurskRussia
So what: Extreme Scan's border-region data is the closest available real-time gauge of Russian reaction to Ukraine's deep-strike campaign and is worth monitoring as a leading indicator.
Hawks vs. loyalists framework 5
Russian war supporters split into two structurally different groups: ideological 'hawks' (roughly 15-30% of the population, depending on how national identity strength is measured) and status-quo 'loyalists' (roughly 35-50%), and this heterogeneity explains why the same population can simultaneously show majority war support and majority desire to end the war.
framework🗣 Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellowconviction: highRussia
So what: Because loyalists are the larger group and their support is conditional on personal well-being rather than ideology, they are the segment most likely to swing quickly if economic conditions worsen — a key variable for forecasting Kremlin political durability.
Hawks skew male, from smaller towns and villages, consume state TV propaganda heavily, hold revanchist/neo-imperialist views, and historically believed Putin was too weak internationally — until he starts a war (this is his fifth), at which point they rally behind him as having 'finally' acted correctly.
framework🗣 Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellowconviction: highVladimir PutinRussia
So what: This group ('Z-niks') will not be moved by economic pressure or negotiated settlements that don't include territorial gains, making them a persistent hard-line constituency regardless of war outcome.
Loyalists skew female, are politically disengaged, consume more diverse media, and support the war passively only insofar as it doesn't disrupt their personal lives — they delegate all judgment about the war's justification to Putin ('Putin does the right thing... I delegate these decisions').
framework🗣 Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellowconviction: highVladimir PutinRussia
So what: This delegation-of-agency dynamic means loyalist 'support' is really a green light contingent on personal comfort, so any shock that disrupts daily life (economic decline, fuel shortages) has outsized power to flip this majority segment.
“Putin does the right thing. If he decided to do it, he knows what he's done.” ▶ 15:14
Moscow has shifted from a relative opposition stronghold (site of the 2012 Bolotnaya protests) to a pro-Putin, pro-war city, driven partly by administrative redistricting that annexed new provincial territory into Moscow and partly because many Muscovites have economically benefited from the war and Western sanctions.
causal🗣 Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellowconviction: mediumMoscowRussia
So what: Moscow's political character can no longer be assumed to be liberal or anti-war, which matters for any analysis using regional variation to gauge Kremlin vulnerability.
“Moscow, unfortunately, has become very pro-Putin and pro-war.” ▶ 12:20
Since 2011-12 the Kremlin has deliberately replaced independent entrepreneurial and business jobs with state and public-sector positions, engineering an electorate that is economically dependent on — and therefore more politically loyal to — the state.
causal🗣 Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellowconviction: highKremlinRussia
So what: This is a structural, multi-decade policy that makes the loyalist bloc more durable than a simple wartime propaganda effect would suggest — economic dependency, not just messaging, underpins Kremlin support.
Economic shocks outweigh military shocks 3
Economic shocks are far more corrosive to loyalist war support than military or battlefield shocks; hawks rally around the flag regardless of cost, while loyalists' support is conditioned almost entirely on their perceived personal economic well-being.
causal🗣 Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellowconviction: highRussia
So what: For anyone modeling Kremlin political risk, economic indicators (real incomes, fuel availability, inflation) are more predictive of eroding war support than casualty counts or territorial setbacks.
In the war's first two years, Kremlin 'military Keynesianism' — large fiscal stimulus into the military-industrial complex — raised working-class incomes and reduced inequality, which paradoxically increased war support; that dynamic has now reversed as the Russian economy stagnates and some analysts flag recession risk since early 2026.
causal🗣 Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellowconviction: highRussiaKremlin
So what: The economic tailwind that initially bought the Kremlin political support from war spending has expired, and the same mechanism is now working in reverse against Putin.
Since early 2026, Russia's economy has been stagnating with some analysts suggesting outright recession, coinciding with measurable erosion of war support and growing public demand for peace talks.
data_point🗣 Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellowconviction: mediumRussia
So what: A stagnating-to-recessionary Russian economy is a concrete macro signal correlated with declining Kremlin political capacity to sustain the war.
Refinery strikes and the gasoline shortage as leverage 4
Ukrainian drone strikes on Russian refineries have produced gasoline shortages and lines reminiscent of the late Soviet period, a shared, universally felt experience that is proving uniquely effective at eroding loyalist war support — unlike battlefield casualties, which nearly everyone across the political spectrum has tolerated.
causal🗣 Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellowconviction: highUkraineRussia
So what: Refinery strikes function as an economic-pressure lever specifically targeted at the loyalist majority's decision-relevant variable (personal well-being), making them potentially more effective at shifting Russian opinion than frontline attrition.
“Everybody experiences this.” ▶ 26:29
For three consecutive months starting in May, Russians have reported Ukrainian drone strikes as the most significant event of the month, a trend that directly coincides with an ongoing decline in war approval.
data_point🗣 Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellowconviction: highUkraineRussia
So what: A sustained, self-reported salience of drone strikes in Russian polling is a trackable leading indicator for continued erosion in Kremlin war support.
A poll by the Institute of Conflictology and Analysis of Russia (ECAR) found a record 81% of respondents want the war to end — the first time such a high figure has been recorded — though this is not a clean anti-war signal since most of those same respondents still want Russia to keep the Ukrainian territory it currently occupies.
data_point🗣 Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellowconviction: highRussiaUkraine
So what: Headline 'want the war to end' numbers should not be read as support for a fair or unconditional settlement; any peace-talk framing needs to account for entrenched territorial demands even among war-weary Russians.
“a record 81% of respondents say that they want the war to end” ▶ 28:06
Reduced refined-fuel output from strikes hits two channels at once: it lowers loyalist public morale via civilian gasoline shortages, and it directly constrains the Russian military's own fuel supply, giving Ukraine's refinery campaign a dual civilian-opinion and battlefield-logistics payoff.
causal🗣 Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellowconviction: highUkraineRussiaVladimir Putin
So what: Ukraine's refinery-strike strategy should be evaluated as a combined economic-warfare and public-opinion tool, not just a battlefield-logistics play — it compounds pressure on the Kremlin from two directions simultaneously.
Casualty tolerance and the elusive breaking point 3
Russian tolerance for battlefield casualties has proven far higher than analysts expected; a RAND Corporation study found that what matters to Russians is believing the war is morally justified and winnable, not minimizing human losses — respondents didn't especially value even a hypothetical replacement of soldiers with robots to reduce casualties.
contrarian🗣 Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellowconviction: highRAND CorporationRussia
So what: Strategies premised on casualty attrition alone breaking Russian political will are likely to underdeliver; economic and normalization-of-suffering dynamics matter more than body counts.
“We have discovered that the price of human life in Russia is extremely low.” ▶ 38:25
A genuine breaking point for Russian war support would likely require a full-blown economic crisis on the scale of 2008 or the late-Soviet collapse, and any such shift is expected to be gradual rather than sudden, given the Kremlin's decades of experience managing public sentiment.
prediction🗣 Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellowconviction: exploratoryRussiaKremlin
So what: Investors and policymakers betting on a near-term Russian capitulation should recalibrate toward a longer, grinding timeline pegged to macro deterioration rather than a single triggering event.
“I think the real breaking point and then again that's pure speculation.” ▶ 36:04
In a separate study with Graham Robertson and Peter Pomerantsev, priming Russians on national identity ('Russianness') measurably reduced their willingness to support immediate peace negotiations, indicating a durable revanchist constituency will persist even if Putin himself pushes for peace talks.
causal🗣 Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellowconviction: highRussiaVladimir Putin
So what: Any negotiated settlement risks facing a domestic identity-driven backlash constituency in Russia regardless of what Putin agrees to, complicating assumptions that a Kremlin deal automatically translates into durable Russian public buy-in.
Regime response and the limits of loyalist discontent 4
The Kremlin actively polls and manages public perception — for example, running pro-Kremlin ad campaigns during the 2022 partial mobilization to downplay how many people were being called up — because it knows public sentiment constrains its options even without competitive elections as a formal check.
framework🗣 Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellowconviction: highKremlinVladimir PutinRussia
So what: Public opinion still functions as an informal resource constraint on Kremlin war-fighting capacity, meaning opinion erosion has real operational consequences even absent electoral accountability.
Political science research shows public dissatisfaction in autocratic regimes is more likely to manifest as elite splits than as street protests, which is the more probable channel through which eroding Russian loyalist sentiment could eventually threaten Kremlin stability.
framework🗣 Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellowconviction: mediumKremlinRussia
So what: Analysts should watch for signs of elite defection or infighting rather than mass demonstrations as the leading indicator of regime stress from declining war support.
“public dissatisfaction is more likely in autocratic regimes to lead to elite splits” ▶ 40:50
The 2022 partial mobilization triggered a mass exodus of Russians fleeing conscription rather than organized protest, illustrating that avoidance and exit — not confrontation — is the characteristic Russian response to unpopular state action, echoing late-Soviet-era sabotage and disengagement.
anecdote🗣 Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellowconviction: highRussiaVladimir Putin
So what: Expect continued passive resistance (draft evasion, emigration, quiet non-compliance) rather than mass protest even as loyalist discontent grows, which still degrades Kremlin war capacity without producing visible political crisis.
No single official justification for the war — protecting Russian speakers in Ukraine or stopping NATO expansion among them — has ever gathered majority support among Russians, showing that even 4.5 years in, there is no consensus domestic rationale for the war.
data_point🗣 Maria Snegovaya, CSIS senior fellowconviction: highRussiaUkraine
So what: The absence of a unifying domestic justification is a latent vulnerability the Kremlin has to keep managing through propaganda and normalization rather than genuine persuasion, and it underpins why economic disruption (not ideology) is the more effective lever on loyalists.

Adam Taggart | Thoughtful Money 3 episode(s) · 123 insights

Get Ready For A.I.-Mageddon | Fred Hickey77 min48 insights2026-07-14
▶ Watch on YouTube
Veteran tech newsletter writer Fred Hickey argues US markets are in the greatest bubble in history, driven by an 'earnings bubble' in which hyperscaler capex and depreciation timing inflate reported AI profits while cheap Chinese models, token-cost pushback, physical buildout constraints, and political backlash threaten the underlying AI-capex growth story. He is holding the most cash of his 47-year career, waiting for a 2000-style crash to buy hyperscaler survivors (Microsoft, Google, Meta) and rotate into undervalued gold and commodities.
AI Earnings Bubble & Stretched Valuations · 4Hyperscaler Capex-Depreciation Mismatch · 6Token Economics Break: Cheap Chinese Models Undercut Frontier AI · 9Physical, Political and Regulatory Limits on Data Center Buildout · 6AI Is Carrying the Entire US Economy · 2Late-Cycle Exhaustion Signals · 6Post-Crash Playbook: Hickey's Positioning · 5Commodities and Value Rotation · 3Gold and Precious Metals Outlook · 7
AI Earnings Bubble & Stretched Valuations 4
Fred Hickey argues US equities are in the greatest stock market bubble in history, and unlike prior bubbles, this one is compounded by a genuine 'earnings bubble' where reported profit growth is artificially inflated by AI-infrastructure-related accounting effects rather than organic demand.
thesis🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: highS&P 500Jeremy Grantham
So what: Investors relying on headline S&P 500 earnings growth to justify current valuations may be underestimating downside risk once the inflation in reported earnings reverses.
“I'm in complete agreement with Jeremy Grantham that we're in the greatest bubble in stock bubble in US history.” ▶ 3:42
Standard valuation ratios show the current market more extended than the 2000 dot-com peak: the Buffett Indicator (market cap to GDP) sits at 241% versus 160% in 2000, and the price-to-sales ratio is 3.7x versus 2.3x in 2000.
data_point🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: highS&P 500
So what: These broader ratios, which aren't distorted by the earnings bubble, suggest valuations are already more stretched than at the last historic top, arguing for caution regardless of the earnings debate.
The S&P 500's headline P/E of about 25x looks moderate, but Hickey estimates that adjusting the Shiller CAPE ratio (currently 40, matching the 2000 peak) for AI-related earnings inflation would put the 'true' CAPE near 67 -- roughly 50% above the 2000 record.
data_point🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: mediumS&P 500
So what: An investor comparing today's CAPE to the 2000 top as if they're similar is likely mispricing risk; the adjusted figure implies materially more downside if earnings normalize.
Running Q1 S&P 500 earnings through an AI model (Gemini) to strip out one-time gains, Hickey found reported 28% year-over-year earnings growth falls to about 16% once mark-to-market gains on hyperscalers' stakes in OpenAI, Anthropic and SpaceX are excluded, and to single digits once gains tied to AI-supplier revenue (NVIDIA, Micron, Western Digital, Seagate) are also excluded.
data_point🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: highNVIDIAMicronWestern DigitalOpenAIAnthropicSpaceX
So what: The 'real' non-AI earnings growth rate for the market is closer to flat-to-mid-single-digits, a much weaker fundamental backdrop than the headline 28% growth number implies.
“you strip out the 40% and it's from the one-time markup gains and it's 16%” ▶ 8:48
Hyperscaler Capex-Depreciation Mismatch 6
AI component makers such as NVIDIA, Micron and Western Digital recognize revenue and earnings from data-center orders almost immediately, while hyperscalers depreciate those same GPU and DRAM purchases over five to six years, creating a multi-year lag where supplier profits look great long before the true cost burden shows up on hyperscaler income statements.
framework🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: highNVIDIAMicronWestern Digital
So what: Investors should expect hyperscaler earnings to look artificially strong now and progressively worse over the next several years purely from depreciation timing, independent of whether AI generates real returns.
Micron went from negative gross margins in 2023 (a historically cyclical low point) to roughly 85% gross margins currently, and its stock is up about 700% this year, illustrating how much of the current 'AI earnings' story is really a supplier order-cycle effect rather than durable end-demand profitability.
data_point🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: highMicron
So what: A component supplier's margin and stock performance this extreme is itself a signal of a capex bubble rather than sustainable end-market growth.
The five largest US hyperscalers are on pace to spend about $750 billion on AI infrastructure this year (plus roughly $100 billion more from other players), up 75% year-over-year, yet only about 5% of the data-center capacity they plan to build has actually been turned on so far.
data_point🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: highMicrosoftAmazonAlphabet/GoogleMetaOracle
So what: With 95% of planned capacity still to come online, any weakness in AI demand or pricing will show up as overcapacity for years, not quarters -- a long overhang risk for chip and infrastructure names.
Google's depreciation expense as a share of revenue is projected to roughly double, from about 17% to 35% by 2028, directly compressing reported earnings if data-center revenue growth doesn't keep pace.
data_point🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: mediumAlphabet/Google
So what: This is a concrete, modelable headwind to Alphabet's forward earnings that isn't yet reflected in consensus optimism about hyperscaler margins.
Oracle is currently spending roughly 100% of its revenue on data-center capex, and Amazon and Meta are spending close to 100% of their operating cash flow on the same buildout, leaving essentially no margin for error if AI returns disappoint.
data_point🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: highOracleAmazonMeta
So what: These companies have no financial slack to absorb a demand shortfall without cutting capex, taking on more debt, or diluting shareholders -- any of which would pressure the stocks.
OpenAI and Anthropic together represent roughly half of hyperscalers' cloud/compute order backlogs; both need large amounts of external cash (part of why they're pursuing IPOs), and Hickey believes some AI labs could ultimately go bankrupt, which would flow straight through to hyperscaler backlog and revenue assumptions. Oracle's own debt is about 2.5x its trailing annual sales.
causal🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: highOpenAIAnthropicOracle
So what: Backlog quality, not just headline backlog size, matters -- half of it depends on the financial survival of two cash-burning private labs, a hidden concentration risk in hyperscaler revenue guidance.
Token Economics Break: Cheap Chinese Models Undercut Frontier AI 9
Chamath Palihapitiya said on the All-In podcast that his company's AI token spending costs are doubling roughly every 45 days while the measured return on that spend is only about 5% at most, prompting him to reconsider the spending.
anecdote🗣 Fred Hickey, citing Chamath Palihapitiyaconviction: mediumChamath Palihapitiya
So what: If a sophisticated tech investor's own company is questioning AI ROI at these growth rates, it signals broader enterprise pushback on AI spending is coming, threatening revenue assumptions behind hyperscaler and AI-lab valuations.
“the token costs are doubling every 45 days” ▶ 11:43
Enterprise AI spending is already being reined in: Amazon reportedly ran up a $500 million one-month internal token bill, Microsoft cut back Cloud Code licenses, Tesla imposed $200-per-week token spending limits on employees, and Coinbase cut its AI spending in half.
data_point🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: highAmazonMicrosoftTeslaCoinbase
So what: These are concrete, near-term data points that enterprise AI token demand growth, a key revenue driver assumed by AI-lab and hyperscaler bulls, is decelerating faster than models assume.
Usage is shifting rapidly toward cheaper Chinese open models: on OpenRouter, Chinese models' share of the top-20 model usage hit 46% recently, versus an 11% trailing 12-month average.
data_point🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: highDeepSeekAlibaba
So what: If token demand migrates to cheap, open-weight Chinese models, the pricing power (and therefore revenue-per-token) assumed for OpenAI, Anthropic and the chips underpinning them is overstated.
Zhipu's latest model now ranks ahead of Google's Gemini 3.5 and 3.1 on Artificial Analysis capability benchmarks, showing the Chinese open-source cohort is now competing on quality, not just price.
data_point🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: mediumZhipuAlphabet/Google
So what: The bull case that Western frontier labs retain a durable capability moat is weakening, removing one justification for premium pricing on US frontier models.
DeepSeek reportedly completes about 90% of tasks at roughly 1.5% of the cost of frontier US models -- the core economic threat to the current AI capex thesis, since if enterprises standardize on cheap alternatives, compute demand growth falls well short of what's priced into hyperscaler and chip valuations.
contrarian🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: highDeepSeek
So what: This is the single biggest disconfirming data point against bullish AI-infrastructure spending forecasts -- watch DeepSeek/Chinese-model adoption rates as a leading indicator of chip and data-center demand disappointment.
“you can get 90% of your tasks done at 1.5% of the cost which is uh you know which is insane” ▶ 16:12
Microsoft is reportedly seeking US government permission to use DeepSeek internally and is separately building its own low-cost models using techniques similar to a startup called Reflection, suggesting pricing pressure from cheap AI will persist even if Chinese models themselves were banned outright.
data_point🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: mediumMicrosoftDeepSeekReflection
So what: A national-security ban on Chinese models wouldn't rescue frontier-model pricing power, since domestic low-cost alternatives are emerging anyway -- the pricing compression thesis holds either way.
A startup Hickey referred to (heard on the recording as 'Vinned Colossus') claims to have shrunk Alibaba's Qwen 3.6 model to run directly on an iPhone 17 Pro, illustrating a tail-risk scenario where advanced AI inference needs no data center or cloud GPU at all.
contrarian🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: exploratoryAlibabaNVIDIA
So what: On-device inference at frontier-adjacent quality would be a direct long-term demand threat to NVIDIA and cloud GPU capex if it scales beyond a proof of concept.
“They shrunk it to run on an iPhone 17 Pro.” ▶ 41:45
Meta cut its AI model/API pricing by 75% in the week before this interview, which Hickey cites as live evidence that the pricing collapse he describes is already happening, not theoretical.
data_point🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: highMeta
So what: Realized price cuts of this magnitude directly undercut revenue-per-token assumptions embedded in AI infrastructure spending justifications -- worth tracking as a recurring pattern across labs.
“they're going to cut prices, you know, slash prices 75%” ▶ 43:16
Even during the 'token maxing' surge in agentic AI usage that began earlier this year, OpenAI was still losing money in Q1, underscoring how far current spending levels are from profitability even at peak usage growth.
data_point🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: highOpenAI
So what: If OpenAI can't reach profitability during a period of accelerating usage, the path to profitability once usage growth normalizes or shifts to cheaper competitors looks even less certain.
Physical, Political and Regulatory Limits on Data Center Buildout 6
About $300 billion of announced US data center projects have already been cancelled due to local and state backlash over land, water and power use.
data_point🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: highdata centers
So what: Regulatory and community pushback is already a binding constraint on buildout, not a hypothetical future risk, and should be tracked alongside capex guidance as a leading indicator of delays.
Reno, Nevada, previously a data-center-friendly market where Hickey lives, just passed a moratorium on new data center construction, an early sign of a broader shift in local government posture.
anecdote🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: mediumdata centers
So what: Watch for similar moratoria spreading to other previously receptive markets, which would compress the pipeline of buildable sites faster than capex forecasts assume.
Public opinion has turned negative on AI: a cited survey found 55% of US adults expect AI to do more personal harm than good, and 75% believe it will cost jobs, a sharp contrast to the broadly welcomed 2000 internet buildout.
data_point🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: mediumAI
So what: Unlike the dot-com era, the AI buildout lacks public goodwill, which raises the odds of political intervention that could slow capex.
Political candidates running explicitly anti-AI campaigns are winning elections, adding a real electoral and policy tail risk to continued hyperscaler capex growth.
data_point🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: mediumAI
So what: Investors modeling multi-year capex growth should factor in rising regulatory and political risk to permitting and subsidies, not just financing availability.
Adam Taggart argued, and Hickey agreed, that physical constraints -- available land, permits, water, copper, capacitors and memory chips, plus public support -- may cap the pace of data-center buildout even if capital funding itself is unconstrained.
framework🗣 Adam Taggart (host), affirmed by Fred Hickeyconviction: highdata centerscopper
So what: Analysts modeling AI capex purely as a function of available capital are missing a binding real-world constraint; commodity and permitting bottlenecks could throttle growth independent of the AI-ROI debate.
McKinsey's forecast of roughly $6.7 trillion in AI data-center capex (about $2 trillion per year) is unlikely to be reached, in Hickey's view, because current unit economics don't support spending at that scale.
contrarian🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: mediumdata centers
So what: If consensus capex forecasts feeding chip, memory and power-infrastructure revenue models are too high, current valuations for those suppliers likely overstate multi-year growth.
AI Is Carrying the Entire US Economy 2
Citing an estimate from David Sacks, the Trump administration's AI czar, Hickey said that of the roughly 2% recent US GDP growth (3.1% annualized last quarter), AI-related activity contributed about 3.1 percentage points while the rest of the economy grew only about 0.1% -- meaning US economic growth is now almost entirely dependent on AI capex continuing.
data_point🗣 Fred Hickey, citing David Sacksconviction: highDavid Sacks
So what: This makes AI capex a de facto macro variable: any slowdown in hyperscaler spending would show up directly in headline US GDP, not just in tech-sector earnings, raising the stakes of the AI trade for the whole economy.
“3.1% is the AI is driving 3.1% and everything else is 0.1%” ▶ 44:55
Because AI buildout is effectively the only source of US growth right now, a slowdown in AI capex would compound with an already-weak underlying economy (negative real wage growth, depleted consumer savings) rather than being offset by strength elsewhere.
causal🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: mediumAI
So what: Investors should not assume a non-AI 'soft landing' cushion exists if the AI capex cycle turns down -- the downside case is a broader growth shock, not a sector-contained one.
Late-Cycle Exhaustion Signals 6
Margin debt has surged past $1.4 trillion, up 55% year-over-year, and now represents about 4.5% of GDP versus roughly 3% at the 2000 peak -- a chart pattern Hickey says looks steeper and larger than prior bubble tops in 2000, 2008 and 2021.
data_point🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: highmargin debt
So what: Elevated margin debt amplifies downside once a selloff starts, since forced deleveraging accelerates the decline -- a key mechanism behind Hickey's expectation of a sharp, not gradual, correction.
A wave of large low- or no-earnings capital raises -- SpaceX's roughly $85 billion equity round (at a valuation Hickey describes as over $2 trillion), SK Hynix's $27 billion equity raise, and nearly $200 billion of hyperscaler debt issuance -- mirrors the 'get out while the getting's good' financing pattern Hickey associates with late-cycle tops.
data_point🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: mediumSpaceXSK Hynix
So what: A surge in large capital raises by AI-adjacent companies, especially those without current earnings, is a classic exhaustion signal worth tracking alongside price action.
Amazon's recent $25 billion bond offering reportedly moved the 10-year Treasury yield by about 8 basis points, and Amazon has had to borrow in euros, yen, Swiss francs and British pounds because it has saturated the US dollar bond market -- a sign hyperscaler debt issuance is now large enough to move broader interest-rate markets.
data_point🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: mediumAmazon
So what: When a single company's bond issuance moves the Treasury market, it signals capital markets may be approaching capacity to absorb further hyperscaler debt without pushing borrowing costs higher for everyone.
Treasury yields have stayed elevated (30-year near 5%, 10-year near 4.6%, 2-year near 4.2%) even as oil fell from around $120 to the $70s per barrel, which Hickey attributes to an oversupply of new issuance and speculative demand across markets rather than typical macro drivers.
data_point🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: mediumTreasury yieldsoil
So what: Persistently high real yields despite falling energy costs raise financing costs for the very AI capex buildout that needs continuous debt issuance, a potential negative feedback loop.
Hyperscaler stock buybacks, previously a major source of price support for MAG7 shares, have effectively stopped as capex consumes available cash flow, removing a structural bid that had been propping up index-level returns.
causal🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: mediumMicrosoftAmazonAlphabet/GoogleMeta
So what: The loss of buyback-driven demand for MAG7 shares removes a price support mechanism just as valuations are most stretched, increasing vulnerability to a demand-driven pullback.
New forms of speculation absent from prior bubble cycles -- zero-days-to-expiration options and leveraged ETFs -- are now widespread, adding a source of fragility Hickey says he hasn't seen in the 2000, 2008, or 2021 cycles.
data_point🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: medium0DTE optionsleveraged ETFs
So what: These instruments can amplify intraday volatility and forced selling in a downturn beyond what historical bubble-unwind analogues would predict.
Post-Crash Playbook: Hickey's Positioning 5
Hickey expects a NASDAQ-style collapse similar to 2000-2002 (when the NASDAQ fell 83% and even the era's best performer, Microsoft, fell 60%), because AI-related MAG7 stocks now represent roughly half the market and heavy ETF ownership means forced selling would drag 'everything' down together in a downturn.
prediction🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: highMicrosoftNASDAQ
So what: Diversification within US large-cap indices may offer less protection than expected in an AI-driven correction, since AI exposure is now so concentrated and ETF-linked across the market.
Hickey says he is currently holding the most cash, mostly in Treasury bills, as a percentage of his portfolio that he can recall in his 47-year investing career, deliberately sitting out the AI rally rather than chasing it.
data_point🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: highFred Hickey
So what: A veteran value and contrarian investor holding record cash is itself a signal worth weighing alongside his specific thesis -- he is positioning for opportunity rather than making a precise market-timing call on the exact top.
Hickey's post-crash buy list centers on hyperscaler 'survivors': Microsoft, because of its enterprise installed base and willingness to offer lower-cost model options including DeepSeek if needed, and Google and Meta, because of their large non-AI cash flows from search, social media and YouTube.
thesis🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: highMicrosoftAlphabet/GoogleMeta
So what: Investors who share Hickey's bearish near-term view but want long-term AI exposure should watch these three names specifically as candidates to buy after a correction rather than avoid AI exposure entirely.
Meta's 2022 stock rally after it halted heavy 'metaverse' cash burn (about $85 billion) is Hickey's template for a possible hyperscaler recovery trigger: a public capex retrenchment announcement once AI ROI disappoints enough could mark the bottom for a given stock.
framework🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: mediumMeta
So what: Watch for the first major hyperscaler to publicly announce an AI capex cut as a potential contrarian buy signal for that specific stock, rather than waiting for the whole sector to bottom simultaneously.
Hickey's timing discipline comes from having bought market bottoms in October 2002 and October 2008 (five months early in 2008); he expects the AI-bubble unwind 'sooner than later' but declines to name a specific year, saying he is watching for exhaustion signals rather than trying to call a top in advance.
framework🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: mediumFred Hickey
So what: His approach argues against trying to precisely time the top -- instead, monitor the exhaustion indicators he cites (margin debt, IPO and debt issuance, buyback cessation) and be ready to act once they clearly reverse.
“keep the bat under your shoulder and wait for the fat pitch. I'm waiting for the fat pitch.” ▶ 1:13:51
Commodities and Value Rotation 3
Hedge fund positioning in energy is near its lowest level in a long time, and commodity producers broadly are severely undervalued relative to the rest of the market, setting up a potential value-stock rotation Hickey compares to 2002-2004, one of the best periods for value investing after the dot-com bust.
thesis🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: highenergygold miners
So what: Underowned, cash-generative, dividend-paying commodity producers could be a primary beneficiary of capital rotating out of AI-related growth stocks once the AI trade turns.
Despite his structural bullishness on commodities, Hickey is not adding to positions now, citing demand risks from negative real wage growth, depleted consumer savings and high debt levels; he holds some energy positions but is simply holding them, preferring to wait for the AI-driven market decline to begin before deploying more capital.
framework🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: mediumenergy
So what: Even a bullish long-term commodities thesis can have poor near-term entry timing if demand is weak -- Hickey's approach is to wait for the catalyst rather than pre-position broadly.
Hickey is skeptical that commodity demand such as copper will simply scale one-for-one with the AI buildout narrative, drawing a parallel to the internet-era fiber-optic and equipment overbuild, and pointing to looming memory-chip oversupply risk from Micron's own $250 billion capex plan alongside new Chinese entrants CXMT (about a $5 billion IPO) and YMTC.
contrarian🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: mediumMicroncopperCXMTYMTC
So what: Not every commodity tied to AI infrastructure is a clean buy -- some inputs like DRAM and memory face their own overcapacity risk from aggressive new supply, complicating a blanket 'AI needs commodities' trade.
Gold and Precious Metals Outlook 7
After more than tripling from about $1,800 to $5,600 over roughly 2.25 years (with mining stocks up about 4.5x), gold has been correcting for four months, and Hickey sees signs the correction is reaching capitulation: the Gold Miners Bullish Percent Index (BPGDM) fell from 100% in January to as low as 2%, and now sits in the mid-20s.
data_point🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: highGoldgold miners
So what: A bullish sentiment indicator collapsing from 100% to near-zero and starting to stabilize is a classic capitulation signal Hickey uses to time entries into gold miners.
Gold mining equities are trading at roughly 10x P/E on average, which Scotia Bank has called the cheapest valuation for the sector in over four decades.
data_point🗣 Fred Hickey, citing Scotia Bankconviction: highgold miners
So what: Historically cheap valuations combined with a sentiment washout make gold miners a candidate for outsized returns once the correction ends, independent of the gold price itself moving much.
Unlike the 2011-2012 gold top, retail investors never meaningfully piled into this gold bull run: GLD and global gold ETFs have seen net outflows through most of the rally (about 110 tons out of GLD alone in the past six months), and speculative futures open interest has fallen to roughly a 13-year low (around 328,000-378,000 contracts versus about 800,000 at the 2020 top), evidence the move was central-bank driven, not retail, and now looks washed out.
data_point🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: highGold
So what: Because retail was never heavily positioned, there's substantial pent-up buying capacity still on the sidelines if sentiment turns, unlike prior tops where retail exhaustion capped further upside.
Central banks, led by China, which has cut its US Treasury holdings from $1.3 trillion to about $630 billion while continuing to buy gold, remain the primary structural buyer of gold; World Gold Council survey data show the share of central banks planning to keep increasing gold holdings has risen from 29% in 2024 to 43% last year to 45% now, and central banks collectively now hold more of their reserves in gold than in US Treasuries.
causal🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: highGoldChina
So what: The dedollarization and gold-accumulation trend among central banks is structural and ongoing, providing a persistent bid under gold independent of retail or speculative positioning.
Gold has held support around $4,000 even through a Middle East-driven oil price spike that prompted some sovereign holders such as Turkey to sell gold reserves to help fund energy purchases, a short-term headwind Hickey expects to fade.
data_point🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: mediumGoldoil
So what: Gold's resilience at $4,000 despite this specific selling pressure suggests underlying demand from central bank buying is currently strong enough to absorb episodic sovereign selling.
Hickey thinks the traditional inverse oil/gold correlation could break down, because persistent above-target inflation (PCE and CPI near 4% for about five years) may become gold's dominant driver instead, similar to the 1970s, when both oil and gold rose together during stagflation.
contrarian🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: mediumGoldoilinflation
So what: If this correlation break plays out, gold could rally even alongside rising oil prices, changing how investors should hedge inflation risk relative to the historical playbook.
Hickey does not expect incoming Fed figure Kevin Warsh to meaningfully raise rates despite hawkish rhetoric, because record annual interest expense on federal debt (about $1.35 trillion) plus a roughly $2 trillion annual deficit make aggressive hikes fiscally and politically untenable; he believes Warsh will instead focus more on shrinking the Fed's $6.7 trillion balance sheet than on raising rates.
prediction🗣 Fred Hickeyconviction: mediumKevin WarshFederal Reserve
So what: If rate hikes are effectively off the table for fiscal reasons even as inflation runs hot, that supports Hickey's gold-friendly, negative-real-rate backdrop rather than a Volcker-style tightening scenario.
“I don't think he's going to be tough and raise rates.” ▶ 1:11:36
A Market Reversal Is Inevitable | Chance Finucane @OxbowAdvisors70 min36 insights2026-07-16
▶ Watch on YouTube
Oxbow Advisors CIO Chance Finucane tells Adam Taggart the AI/semiconductor trade shows bubble-level extremes (record momentum, concentration, and margin debt) and that a severe multi-year reversal is inevitable though untimeable; Oxbow keeps AI-beneficiary exposure near 5% of its growth portfolio, is trimming into strength and buying prior pullbacks (precious metals, energy, defensive 'halo trade' stocks), shortening bond duration toward a 30/30/30/10 framework, and counsels clients to preserve capital for the eventual buying opportunity.
AI/Semiconductor Speculative Extremes · 5Historical Bubble Precedent & Bear-Market Playbook · 4AI/Hyperscaler Economics Under Strain · 7Oxbow's Tactical Positioning · 7Household & Systemic Risk Signals · 2IPO Mania · 3Fixed Income & Rate Positioning · 4Wealth Management Practice for High-Net-Worth Clients · 4
AI/Semiconductor Speculative Extremes 5
The semiconductor index rose more than 230% over the trailing 14 months as of mid-2026, a magnitude matched only once before in the chart's history: the final 14 months of the dot-com bubble into March 2000.
data_point🗣 Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisorsconviction: highsemiconductor sectordot-com bubble
So what: Momentum this extreme has historically preceded severe, multi-year sector bear markets, flagging semiconductor exposure as a high-risk area to trim rather than chase.
“In the last 14 months, the semiconductor index has appreciated by more than 230%, which has only happened one other time, which was the last 14 months of the dot-com bubble.” ▶ 3:09
Momentum stocks have outperformed minimum-volatility stocks by the widest margin in 30 years of data, and the current overshoot versus trend is roughly a five-standard-deviation extreme, the most severe reading since 2000, surpassing even the 2007-08 housing bubble period.
data_point🗣 Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisorsconviction: highmomentum stocksminimum volatility stocks
So what: A five-sigma overshoot implies an unusually large mean-reversion risk; investors overweight momentum names should expect an outsized retracement, not just a mild pullback.
“Momentum stocks have outperformed minimum volatility stocks by more than any time in on record in the last 30 years.” ▶ 3:19
AI-related stocks make up roughly 45% of S&P 500 market capitalization and about 70% of the Nasdaq 100, meaning a reversal in the AI trade would drag the broad indexes down along with many ETFs that hold these mega-cap tech names, including funds not typically thought of as tech-heavy.
data_point🗣 Adam Taggartconviction: highS&P 500Nasdaq 100AI stocksETFs
So what: Passive index and even non-tech ETF investors carry far more AI-trade concentration risk than they may realize, warranting a look under the hood of holdings.
≈ “45% of the S&P market cap is made up of AI related stocks.” paraphrase
In June 2026, the semiconductor index moved more than 5% in a single day on roughly half of all trading days, a level of daily volatility Chance Finucane says cannot reflect genuine changes in underlying business value and instead signals extreme speculation.
data_point🗣 Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisorsconviction: highsemiconductor sector
So what: Volatility of this magnitude in supposedly fundamentals-driven mega-cap names is itself a late-cycle speculation indicator worth monitoring for further deterioration.
“essentially half the trading days in the month of June, the semiconductor index went up or down by more than 5%.” ▶ 17:52
IBM, a roughly quarter-trillion-dollar market-cap 'blue chip,' dropped about 25% in a single trading day, which Adam Taggart cites alongside semiconductor moves as evidence of manic, late-stage-bubble-like volatility spreading beyond purely speculative names.
anecdote🗣 Adam Taggartconviction: mediumIBM
So what: Large single-day drops in traditionally stable blue-chip names suggest fragility is broadening beyond the most speculative corners of the market.
“IBM stock opened today and was down basically 25%.” ▶ 15:31
Historical Bubble Precedent & Bear-Market Playbook 4
When a sector goes through a bubble-type move of hundreds of percent in a short period, Oxbow's research (using Cisco Systems and Nvidia during the dot-com era as examples) finds it typically takes about two and a half years after the peak to reach a new bottom.
framework🗣 Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisorsconviction: highCisco SystemsNvidiadot-com bubble
So what: If the semiconductor/AI cycle peaks, investors should plan for a prolonged, multi-year drawdown rather than a quick V-shaped recovery before re-entering.
“it tends to be about two and a half years that you go down until you make a new bottom.” ▶ 8:26
A 40-50% overall market decline would imply the prior cycle's biggest winners fall by two-thirds or more from their peaks, given how concentrated recent gains have been.
framework🗣 Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisorsconviction: highsemiconductor sector
So what: Investors overweight the current cycle's leaders should size position risk assuming downside far steeper than the headline index decline.
“if you have a a 40% or 50% market decline, that means the winners of the last cycle are probably going down by 2/3 or more.”
During the 2000-2003 dot-com bear market, tech stocks and the broad index fell sharply, but investors could still make money because a number of previously sold-off sectors from the late 1990s outperformed; Oxbow is trying to identify today's equivalent unloved sectors ahead of a similar rotation.
framework🗣 Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisorsconviction: mediumdot-com bubble
So what: A broad AI-trade unwind need not mean a total portfolio loss; capital rotation into unloved, undervalued sectors historically offers a place to hide and even profit.
The current secular bull market since the 2009 financial-crisis low is about 17 years old, and historical secular bull runs tend to last roughly 20 years, implying limited further runway before a regime shift comparable to the stagnant 1968-1982 stock market.
prediction🗣 Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisorsconviction: mediumS&P 500
So what: Investors, especially those near retirement who have benefited from 17 years of gains, should weigh the historical odds that the secular bull is in its final stretch rather than assume indefinite continuation.
AI/Hyperscaler Economics Under Strain 7
Tech analyst Fred Hickey argues hyperscaler earnings are artificially inflated by one-time investment gains tied to stakes in frontier-model companies like OpenAI and Anthropic, which are not recurring operating profits.
contrarian🗣 Fred Hickey (relayed by Adam Taggart)conviction: highOpenAIAnthropichyperscalers
So what: If reported hyperscaler earnings are overstated by non-operating gains, headline valuation multiples understate true overvaluation, making the sector riskier than it appears.
“these earnings are getting boosted by a lot of one-time gains in investments that these companies have made like in a lot of the frontier models like Open AI or Anthropic.” ▶ 21:58
On a cyclically-adjusted (CAPE-style) basis, Fred Hickey puts the AI/hyperscaler sector's valuation near 67 times earnings, far above the seemingly reasonable multiples implied by headline current-year P/E figures.
contrarian🗣 Fred Hickey (relayed by Adam Taggart)conviction: highhyperscalers
So what: Investors relying on headline PE ratios to justify AI valuations may be missing a much richer adjusted multiple that signals significant overvaluation.
“he says it's more like 67 times.” ▶ 23:11
Chance Finucane describes an accounting dynamic where hyperscaler AI capex is recognized as 100% revenue immediately by the semiconductor and capital-equipment companies selling the gear, while on the hyperscalers' own books that same spending is only expensed at roughly 20% per year over a five-year depreciation schedule, meaning today's reported hyperscaler profitability outpaces the true economics once depreciation catches up.
causal🗣 Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisorsconviction: mediumhyperscalerssemiconductor sector
So what: As depreciation expense compounds in future years, hyperscaler margins could compress meaningfully even without revenue declining, a headwind not yet priced into current earnings estimates.
“it's it's 100% of the revenue being recognized today, but then on the expense line, uh since you can depreciate it, let's just say over five years, only 20% of that is being expensed right now.”
Semiconductor and capital-equipment companies have captured nearly all of the hyperscalers' free cash flow over the past couple of years, a dynamic Chance Finucane doubts hyperscaler management teams -- among the smartest in the world -- will tolerate indefinitely, making a pushback on AI spending a plausible catalyst for a reversion in the trade.
contrarian🗣 Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisorsconviction: mediumsemiconductor sectorhyperscalers
So what: Watch for hyperscalers slowing capex growth or seeking efficiency gains as an early signal that the semiconductor trade's free-cash-flow capture is ending.
“the semiconductor businesses have basically stolen all of the free cash flow from the hyperscalers uh in the last couple of years.” ▶ 24:34
Chamath Palihapitiya's CTO reported that the cost of AI tokens for his company was doubling roughly every 45 days while measured productivity gains from that spend were only about 5%, prompting Chamath and, per Fred Hickey, other companies to start ratcheting down token and compute usage.
anecdote🗣 Chamath Palihapitiya (anecdote relayed by Fred Hickey via Adam Taggart)conviction: mediumChamath Palihapitiya
So what: Deteriorating unit economics of AI compute spend among sophisticated AI-bullish operators is an early warning sign for slowing enterprise AI capex and token-consumption growth.
“the cost of the our tokens is doubling like every 45 days.” ▶ 27:03
Some large hyperscalers, including Microsoft, are reportedly shifting workloads to cheaper Chinese AI models that deliver roughly 90% of needed task performance at a fraction of what OpenAI or Anthropic charge, raising national-security and corporate-security concerns that strengthen the case for cybersecurity investment.
data_point🗣 Fred Hickey (relayed by Adam Taggart)conviction: mediumMicrosoftOpenAIAnthropiccybersecurity
So what: A shift toward cheaper Chinese AI models both pressures the pricing power of US frontier-model providers and creates a durable tailwind for cybersecurity spending.
“you can still get about 90% of the the tasks you want done but just at a fraction of what Anthropic or OpenAI might charge you.” ▶ 30:42
A bullish sell-side analyst's own model shows Micron's earnings per share surging to $250 by 2028 (with gross margin doubling to roughly 90% from a normal ~45%) before collapsing 80% to about $50 by 2030 as the memory chip cycle turns, implying a base-case share price near $400 -- about two-thirds below its recent $1,200 peak -- once Micron reverts to its normal ~8x earnings multiple on normalized profits.
data_point🗣 Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisorsconviction: highMicron
So what: Even the bull case for a hot AI-supply-chain name like Micron embeds a severe subsequent earnings collapse, illustrating why Oxbow avoids the most cyclical AI beneficiaries despite currently strong reported profits.
“by 2030, that same bullish analyst, just knowing the cycles of memory chips, has the earnings per share dropping from $250 in 2028 to 50. An 80% decline in earnings in 2 years.” ▶ 19:50
Oxbow's Tactical Positioning 7
Oxbow's overall split between risky assets and short-term Treasuries has stayed roughly stable through 2026; the active management has instead come from trimming positions after they become 'too exciting' and valuations can't be justified, then buying back in after a pullback of 30-40-50%.
framework🗣 Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisorsconviction: highOxbow Advisors
So what: A disciplined trim-into-strength, buy-the-drawdown process is a repeatable framework retail investors can apply instead of trying to time absolute market tops and bottoms.
“We're usually trimming, taking some money out of those areas just because we think it's you know, it's gotten too exciting in the market.” ▶ 12:56
Oxbow trimmed precious metals positions when gold traded above $5,000/oz and silver was over $100-120/oz in January 2026, and has spent the last month rebuilding those positions now that gold is back around $4,000 and silver around $60, levels the firm had targeted in advance.
data_point🗣 Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisorsconviction: highgoldsilver
So what: Illustrates a concrete, price-target-based rebalancing discipline in precious metals that investors could mirror rather than chasing or panic-selling gold and silver.
“gold is back around 4,000, silver is back around 60, those were always kind of our targets” ▶ 13:40
Oxbow trimmed energy exposure when oil prices spiked unexpectedly amid the war in Iran in March 2026, then added back after oil subsequently fell about 40% and energy stocks pulled back.
data_point🗣 Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisorsconviction: mediumenergy sectoroil
So what: Shows the same trim-on-spike, buy-on-drawdown discipline applied to energy, a sector Oxbow is using as a diversifier away from the AI trade.
In January-February 2026, a 'halo trade' into heavy-asset, low-obsolescence-risk stocks (industrials, consumer staples, healthcare, utilities) bid those names up as an escape from AI-disruption risk; when the Iran war caused a March selloff in those same defensive sectors, Oxbow bought the dip, and those positions have rallied for months, accelerating as capital rotates out of the AI trade over the last three weeks.
thesis🗣 Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisorsconviction: highindustrialsconsumer stapleshealthcareutilities
So what: Defensive, low-AI-disruption-risk sectors are already showing early rotation flows out of AI and into value, potentially the leading edge of the broader unwind Oxbow expects.
“the whole first 2 months of this year was that halo trade, the heavy asset, low obsolescence uh stock trade to get away from disrupted AI stocks” ▶ 14:22
AI-beneficiary positions make up only about 5% of Oxbow's long-term growth portfolio; the firm bought cybersecurity company Fortinet in January 2026 at roughly 20 times free cash flow when cyber stocks were mistakenly grouped with AI-disruption risks, the stock has since doubled to about 40 times free cash flow (near a 6-7 year high multiple), and Oxbow cut the position in half to bank gains and remove its cost basis.
anecdote🗣 Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisorsconviction: highFortinetcybersecurity
So what: A real, dated example of Oxbow's buy-cheap/trim-rich discipline: entering an unloved beneficiary sector at a low multiple and systematically taking profits once valuation re-rates to a multi-year high.
“we had bought Fortinet at a little over 20 times free cash flow. And it's now doubled in 6 months” ▶ 29:06
Oxbow's recent long-term growth additions -- IDEXX (niche industrial products with high customer switching costs and pricing power), McKesson (drug distributor benefiting from GLP-1 weight-loss drug distribution), and US Bancorp (a top-quality super-regional bank) -- reflect rotation into industrials, healthcare, and financials rather than chasing the AI trade.
data_point🗣 Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisorsconviction: mediumIDEXXMcKessonUS Bancorp
So what: Provides investors specific, named alternatives to AI mega-caps that a high-net-worth advisory firm is currently buying as 'unloved' quality names.
In its high-income strategy, Oxbow has been adding Northern Oil and Gas and Kimbell Royalty as oil prices fell back below roughly $70-80, and rebuilding precious metals, gold/silver miners, and royalty positions back to target base allocations.
data_point🗣 Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisorsconviction: mediumNorthern Oil and GasKimbell Royaltygoldsilver
So what: Gives income-focused investors concrete names being accumulated as a diversification away from AI/semiconductor concentration.
Household & Systemic Risk Signals 2
Among US households that own financial assets, roughly 73% of those assets are held in stocks versus just 7% in bonds -- a record equity concentration -- and stock exposure is highest among households approaching or in retirement, the group with the least time to recover from a severe drawdown.
data_point🗣 Adam Taggartconviction: highUS households
So what: A severe AI-trade unwind would disproportionately harm near-retirement and retired households who are both the most equity-exposed and least able to wait out a multi-year recovery.
“they're 73% in stocks and 7% uh in bonds” ▶ 35:59
Margin debt relative to money supply is at its highest ratio on record; per data from David Rosenberg, two-thirds of all outstanding margin debt has been added in just the last six years (roughly tripling), and margin debt is up more than 50% year-over-year, a surge that has occurred only three other times in 30 years -- early 2000, mid-2007, and early 2021 -- each preceding a bear market.
data_point🗣 Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisorsconviction: highmargin debt
So what: Record margin debt growth is a recurring precursor to major bear markets (dot-com, GFC, 2022), making current leverage levels a systemic risk gauge worth tracking for a forced-deleveraging trigger.
“2/3 of all margin debt uh has come on in the last 6 years, uh which is pretty staggering that it's essentially tripled uh in a 6-year period” ▶ 41:41
IPO Mania 3
SpaceX's opening-day IPO price valued it at roughly 79 times sales, or about 58 times next year's projected revenue -- among the most excited IPO demand Oxbow founder Ted Oakley has seen from clients calling in during his career -- compared to Google's 2004 IPO, which priced at about 8.5 times revenue.
data_point🗣 Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisorsconviction: highSpaceXGoogle
So what: Today's 'reasonable premium' for a hot tech IPO is many multiples richer than historical comparables like Google's IPO, signaling extreme investor demand that has historically not persisted.
“that opening price you saw on the first day it was trading at 58 times next year's revenue.” ▶ 32:08
Looking at the last decade of hyped IPOs, every one has at some point traded below its first-day closing price, typically cutting in half within the first year; SpaceX is already down about 12% from its first-day close.
data_point🗣 Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisorsconviction: highSpaceX
So what: Investors chasing newly public 'hot' names at IPO pricing have a historically poor entry-point track record; patience through the first year has repeatedly offered better entry prices.
“the average usually is it cuts in half at some point during the first year.” ▶ 33:35
Oxbow waited roughly four to five years after Airbnb's 2020 IPO (around $145/share) before buying the stock at a little above $120/share once its valuation became more reasonable.
anecdote🗣 Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisorsconviction: mediumAirbnb
So what: A concrete, multi-year patience example showing that waiting out post-IPO hype can produce a better entry price even in a genuinely high-quality company.
“we watched it for four or five years uh, before we ended up taking a position at a little above $120 a share uh, when the valuation got more reasonable” ▶ 33:58
Fixed Income & Rate Positioning 4
Oxbow has moved its target portfolio framework away from the traditional 60/40 stocks/bonds split toward roughly 30/30/30/10 across short-duration high-quality fixed income, commodities (energy, precious metals, agriculture), high-quality stocks, and an opportunistic/special-situations sleeve that can include cash.
framework🗣 Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisorsconviction: highOxbow Advisors
So what: Offers a concrete alternative allocation framework for investors questioning whether the classic 60/40 model still fits a higher-inflation, more volatile regime.
Oxbow keeps fixed-income duration generally at 3 years or less and has locked in about 15% of its income-strategy portfolios in 2-3 year Treasuries yielding just above 4% (around 4.15%), betting that Fed Chair Kevin Warsh's inflation-fighting stance combined with favorable year-over-year base effects (from March's Iran-war oil spike rolling off) could bring inflation back toward 2% and trigger rate cuts within about a year.
prediction🗣 Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisorsconviction: mediumKevin WarshUS Treasuries
So what: Locking in short-duration yields near 4% ahead of anticipated Fed cuts is a specific, actionable fixed-income trade being deployed today.
“It's now about 15% of those portfolios is locked in at rates of 4% or a little above a 4% yield.” ▶ 46:26
Oxbow avoids long-duration Treasuries, viewing the post-2020 regime of higher and more volatile inflation combined with a widening US fiscal deficit, rising debt levels, and rising mandatory spending and interest expense as structural forces likely to keep long-term yields elevated.
thesis🗣 Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisorsconviction: highUS TreasuriesUS fiscal deficit
So what: Structural fiscal pressures argue against a long-duration Treasury bet even if short rates fall, a relevant caution for bond investors positioning for rate cuts.
As an example of Oxbow's opportunistic sleeve, the firm bought Boeing convertible preferred stock a couple of years ago for its attractive yield while the common share price had fallen significantly, planning to eventually shift into the common stock at an appreciated price.
anecdote🗣 Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisorsconviction: mediumBoeing
So what: Illustrates a specific special-situations technique -- buying convertible preferred shares of a good business in temporary distress -- for generating yield with upside optionality.
“Boeing issued a convertible preferred stock that had a good yield and the common share price had really gone down a lot” ▶ 44:09
Wealth Management Practice for High-Net-Worth Clients 4
For clients experiencing a sudden liquidity event (business sale, concentrated stock windfall, inheritance), Oxbow's default advice is that there is no rush to deploy the capital -- park it in T-bills near a risk-free ~4% yield and take up to a year to adjust to the life change before committing to a portfolio.
framework🗣 Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisorsconviction: highOxbow Advisors
So what: Counters the pressure many new advisory clients feel to deploy capital immediately; parking in T-bills preserves optionality without sacrificing meaningful yield while decisions are made.
“there's no rush to take action with this liquidity event, this cash flow that you just received.” ▶ 55:03
Oxbow's own historical data showed that slowly dollar-cost-averaging new client money into stocks underperformed versus immediately sizing an appropriately risk-calibrated equity sleeve (currently about 60% stocks/40% Treasuries in the long-term growth strategy, using reasonably-valued rather than overvalued names) and deploying it within the first few months.
data_point🗣 Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisorsconviction: highOxbow Advisors
So what: Challenges the common assumption that slow-drip investing is always the safer onboarding approach; Oxbow's data favors calibrated but prompt deployment over gradual dollar-cost averaging.
“we used to buy in, for example, in the stock strategy, really slowly. And we actually have uh enough data to suggest that um that actually was not helping our clients as much as it could have” ▶ 59:46
For clients transitioning large, highly appreciated concentrated positions into Oxbow's strategy, the firm caps realized taxable gains at roughly 10% of portfolio value per year (about 2% of the portfolio paid in long-term capital gains tax), migrating the account over two to three years rather than triggering a large one-time tax bill.
framework🗣 Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisorsconviction: highOxbow Advisors
So what: Gives a concrete, replicable rule of thumb (about 10% of portfolio value in gains, ~2% in tax per year) for tax-efficiently unwinding concentrated stock positions.
“if you had a a portfolio that was doubling every 5 years, um and you decided you needed to sell uh 1/5 of the portfolio per year, you would essentially be recognizing 10% of the portfolio uh in gains and paying about 2% of the portfolio in taxes.” ▶ 1:04:54
Oxbow Advisors' minimum account threshold to become a client is $2 million in investable assets.
data_point🗣 Chance Finucane, CIO Oxbow Advisorsconviction: highOxbow Advisors
So what: Sets clear expectations for which investors can directly engage Oxbow versus needing to self-manage using the firm's publicly shared philosophy and trades.
“the minimum we would set would be 2 million.” ▶ 1:09:22
The Equity Markets Are Insane Right Now | Jan van Eck67 min39 insights2026-07-12
▶ Watch on YouTube
VanEck CEO Jan Van Eck argues the historically extreme equity valuations (price-to-sales at an 80-year high) are justified by matching mega-cap tech profit growth (Nvidia +129%, Google +80%), lays out four specific headwinds threatening Anthropic (cost-conscious clients, Chinese open-source competition, trust/ethics attacks, corporate infrastructure control), and reaffirms VanEck's three 10-year themes — AI, the rise of India, and gold/Bitcoin as a hedge against US fiscal overspending — while flagging that Social Security's insolvency date has moved up to 2032.
AI-Driven Equity Valuations & Profit Growth · 8Mapping AI 2.0 Winners, Vulnerable Players, and Moats · 8Anthropic's Four Headwinds · 6Tactical Trades: BDCs, Alt Managers, and Semis · 4VanEck's Three 10-Year Structural Trades: AI, India, Gold/Bitcoin · 6Macro Backdrop: Fed, Deficit, Labor, and Social Security · 6Market Dispersion: The SaaS-vs-AI Quality Rotation · 1
AI-Driven Equity Valuations & Profit Growth 8
US equity market price-to-sales ratios (data back to 1946) are more expensive by an order of magnitude than at any point in the last 60-80 years, a distortion Van Eck attributes almost entirely to technology company multiples.
data_point🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: highS&P 500price-to-sales
So what: Extreme P/S is a valuation red flag on its face, but Van Eck argues it's explained by margin structure rather than mania — investors should judge it against margins/earnings, not in isolation.
“today's market um is more expensive by an order of magnitude than any other market, you know, over whatever for the last 60 years, 70 years. So, 80 years.” ▶ 10:54
Mega-cap tech profit growth in the most recent quarter was extraordinary: Nvidia's profits rose 129% year-over-year, Google's 80%, and Meta's 62%.
data_point🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: highNvidiaGoogleMeta
So what: These growth rates set the bar for what markets are pricing in; a deceleration toward more normal 20-30% growth would be a meaningful negative surprise relative to current multiples.
“Nvidia's profits going up 129%, right? Google's profits going up 80%, Meta 62.” ▶ 12:20
Across the group of large tech companies Van Eck tracks, average implied forward earnings growth is running at 95% with a median of 40% — an unprecedented pace of profit expansion.
data_point🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: hightech sector
So what: Investors should track realized growth against this 95%/median-40% bar; missing it is a much larger risk to current multiples than a normal growth miss would be.
“the average earnings growth of these tech companies, these are tech companies, is 95% and the median is 40%.” ▶ 18:28
AI and AI-adjacent companies now represent roughly 45% of total S&P 500 market capitalization, meaning the index's near-term direction is effectively tied to that cohort's continued earnings growth.
data_point🗣 Adam Taggart, host, framing confirmed by Jan Van Eckconviction: highS&P 500AI sector
So what: A broad market downturn is unlikely while AI earnings momentum holds, but the index's concentration means any AI earnings deceleration would hit the whole market disproportionately.
Unlike the dot-com bubble, the top-10 US stocks' valuations have compressed back toward their historical average (about 20.8x forward earnings on average versus roughly 21.6x currently) because profit growth has caught up with price appreciation.
causal🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: highS&P 500 top 10
So what: This convergence of earnings and price is Van Eck's core justification for staying fully invested in mega-cap tech rather than trimming on valuation grounds alone.
“the average is about 20.8 times forward earnings, and they're about 21.6 times.” ▶ 25:23
Two years ago the top-10 US stocks traded at genuinely elevated multiples of roughly 24x to 34x forward earnings, which Van Eck says correctly warranted worry at the time; that valuation risk has since normalized as earnings caught up.
data_point🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: mediumS&P 500 top 10
So what: Shows Van Eck isn't a perma-bull on valuation — he flagged real stretch before, lending more credibility to his current 'valuations are fine' call.
“well above 24 times forward, right? Up to up to 34 uh ish times.” ▶ 28:23
The trailing 10-year S&P 500 total return through June was 15.5%, which is strong but not abnormal compared to four prior technology-driven bull markets, including the 1990s.
data_point🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: mediumS&P 500
So what: Provides a historical anchor pushing back on the idea that the current AI-driven return profile is unprecedented or necessarily bubble-like.
≈ “the trailing 10-year returns for the S&P through June is 15.5%.” paraphrase
Price-to-sales is a comparatively weak valuation metric for today's mega-cap tech because their net margins (around 50%) are far higher than the roughly 5% margins typical of dot-com-era companies, which is why Scott McNealy's famous dot-com-era critique of paying 10x sales doesn't translate directly to today's market.
contrarian🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: mediumScott McNealy
So what: Investors screening purely on P/S risk misreading high-margin AI-era tech giants as dot-com-style bubbles; margin trends and PE are the more reliable lens now.
“instead of making 5% margins, they're making 50% margins.” ▶ 33:21
Mapping AI 2.0 Winners, Vulnerable Players, and Moats 8
Van Eck segments AI-exposed companies into four categories: vertically-integrated players owning compute, models and customer relationships (Alphabet, Amazon, SpaceX); partial-stack hardware players (Intel, Cerebras); memory chipmakers (SK Hynix, Samsung); and software companies split between single-LLM vendors, AI middleware, and 'AI inside' incumbents like Microsoft and Apple.
framework🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: highAlphabetAmazonSpaceXIntelCerebrasSK HynixSamsungMicrosoftApple
So what: This is Van Eck's explicit tool for judging which AI-exposed companies will still have a durable moat in 10 years versus which get disrupted by the next wave.
Van Eck believes memory chipmakers SK Hynix and Samsung lack a durable moat because most of their current profit surge comes from price hikes rather than volume growth, leaving them vulnerable once supply catches up with AI-driven demand.
contrarian🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: mediumSK HynixSamsung
So what: Flags memory stocks — up sharply over the past year — as a segment to be cautious on despite currently sitting at a critical AI supply-chain bottleneck.
“Most of their profits, Adam, uh to your to your point, actually just come because they jacked up prices. They're not even increased volumes that much.” ▶ 15:48
The South Korean stock market, driven by SK Hynix and Samsung, went essentially sideways for 17 years before exploding higher recently — a price pattern Van Eck calls distorting and extreme for markets.
data_point🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: mediumSK HynixSamsung
