So what: A parabolic move after a 17-year flat base is a warning sign of a narrow, momentum-driven rally rather than a broad-based repricing of the market.
“17 years basically of no performance whatsoever, right? And then it explodes” ▶ 11:18
Companies that embed AI without owning the underlying models or compute — Van Eck's 'AI inside' category, like Microsoft and Apple — are structurally capped at 10-20% earnings growth, both historically and looking forward, because they depend on other vendors for the core technology.
framework🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: highMicrosoftApple
So what: Investors expecting Microsoft or Apple to re-rate like pure AI infrastructure plays may be disappointed; they're better framed as a lower-growth, lower-risk way to hold AI exposure.
“they're really relying on other people to provide that the AI models and compute, right? They're just kind of wrapping it.” ▶ 16:55
Nvidia's compute demand is growing roughly five to six times per year while supply is not keeping pace, and Van Eck doesn't expect that imbalance to close soon — the core reason he says investors should stay fully invested in AI infrastructure.
thesis🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: highNvidia
So what: As long as the demand/supply gap persists, pricing power and margins for compute suppliers should stay elevated; the moment supply finally catches up is the key inflection risk to watch.
Google has transformed its business over the past 18 months by answering queries directly through Gemini instead of serving 'blue link' search results, yet advertiser spend (including VanEck's own) hasn't declined because placement, analytics fees, and ecosystem stickiness still generate revenue even as click-throughs fall.
causal🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: highGoogleGemini
So what: Explains why Alphabet's ad-adjacent revenue and 80% profit growth have held up despite a widely-feared bear case that AI answers would cannibalize search-click monetization.
“we're not VanEck's not spending less money on Google, right? We still want to get placement through I'll call it the old the old-fashioned way.” ▶ 21:20
A meaningful but sub-50% portion of Big Tech's reported profit growth (e.g., Google's 80%) comes from venture-investment gains — such as Google's stake in SpaceX and Nvidia's venture investments across the AI ecosystem — rather than purely organic AI-services revenue.
data_point🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: mediumGoogleSpaceXNvidia
So what: Investors modeling forward mega-cap tech earnings growth should separate organic AI monetization from one-off venture-investment gains to avoid over-extrapolating durability.
Van Eck frames Nvidia as a long-duration compounder akin to IBM — paying a dividend, buying back significant stock, and generating strong free cash flow — expecting it to still be rewarding shareholders in 10 years.
thesis🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: mediumNvidiaIBM
So what: Reframes Nvidia from a cyclical semiconductor trade into a long-term core holding comparable to a dividend-compounding blue chip.
Anthropic's Four Headwinds 6
Van Eck lays out four specific risks to Anthropic: cost-conscious corporate clients cutting AI spend, intensifying model competition, public trust/ethics attacks from competitors and government, and large corporate clients moving to control their own AI infrastructure to protect data and costs.
thesis🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: highAnthropic
So what: A multi-pronged, specific bear case on one of the most hyped private AI names — relevant for anyone with exposure via Anthropic funding rounds or AI-infrastructure proxies.
Anthropic's annualized run-rate revenue was around 30 (billion) a quarter ago per VanEck's chart and Van Eck estimates it's now approaching 50, but he still expects a negative revenue surprise for Anthropic within the next 12 months given mounting competitive and trust headwinds.
prediction🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: mediumAnthropic
So what: A specific, time-bound prediction of an Anthropic revenue disappointment that investors in AI-exposed private or public vehicles should track over the next year.
“I would I would be surprised if there weren't a revenue surprise to Anthropic over the next 12 months.”
Chinese open-source AI models caught up to Anthropic's Claude in effectiveness within about three months, and since much of business AI usage isn't latency- or cutting-edge-dependent, cheaper alternative models are increasingly viable substitutes.
data_point🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: highAnthropicClaude
So what: Commoditization of 'good enough' AI models threatens premium LLM vendors' pricing power; investors should separate genuine cutting-edge AI demand from commodity/bulk AI demand.
“the Chinese models have caught up with Claude.” ▶ 43:21
VanEck's own internal token usage on Anthropic's models doubled from about 10 million to 20 million tokens over the three months since Van Eck's prior quarterly appearance, while dollar spend has begun to level off, evidencing active cost-optimization and multi-model use rather than single-vendor loyalty.
data_point🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: highVanEckAnthropic
So what: A real-world case study of margin-compression risk for premium LLM vendors as corporate buyers multi-home across models to control costs.
“our VanEck's use of Anthropic is is growing has doubled in 3 months if you want to be extreme. But what you can see here is that what we're spending um is leveling out a little bit.” ▶ 42:02
Coinbase is a case study Van Eck cites where AI token usage keeps rising while AI spend falls, achieved by routing queries to cheaper models — including free or open-source options — rather than defaulting to a single premium model like Claude.
data_point🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: mediumCoinbaseAnthropicClaude
So what: A likely template other enterprises will follow, structurally pressuring premium LLM providers' revenue-per-token even as overall AI usage keeps growing.
Palantir CEO Alex Karp publicly accused Anthropic, in a CNBC interview echoed on the All-In podcast, of learning how client businesses operate and then building competing products, citing Figma — whose stock fell about 73% — as a company allegedly harmed after cooperating with an AI vendor that later competed with it.
anecdote🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck, recounting Alex Karp's commentsconviction: mediumAlex KarpAnthropicFigma
So what: A live 'AI vendor eats client's business' case study — a due-diligence red flag for any company sharing proprietary workflows or data with foundation-model vendors.
“Anthropic is stealing, you know, is learning how those businesses run. And in some cases, uh you know, competing with them and then competing with them.” ▶ 44:37
Tactical Trades: BDCs, Alt Managers, and Semis 4
Van Eck reaffirms his roughly one-year tactical bullish calls from last quarter on BDCs, alternative asset managers (Blue Owl, Ares), and semiconductors including Nvidia, saying the Q1 selloff in BDCs and alt managers created a buying opportunity that has already started paying off.
thesis🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: mediumBlue OwlAresNvidia
So what: A tactical, roughly one-year buy call on beaten-down BDCs and alternative-asset managers, distinct from Van Eck's longer 10-year theme picks.
Blue Owl currently yields about 9%, and Van Eck says he's satisfied holding it even if the stock price goes nowhere, because the dividend alone delivers an acceptable return, with fears around the common stock already priced in as of Q2.
thesis🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: mediumBlue Owl
So what: A high-yield income thesis independent of price appreciation, relevant for income-focused investors weighing private-credit-adjacent equities.
“Their their dividend yield is like 9% Adam. So, even if the stock is is goes nowhere, I'm I've made 9% over the course of a year.” ▶ 50:33
Van Eck argues private-credit default concerns are overblown, noting high-yield debt defaults remain low and BDCs like Ares are roughly flat-to-up since Q2 despite elevated redemption activity.
contrarian🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: mediumAres
So what: Pushes back on a widely-discussed private-credit-blowup narrative — a useful counterpoint for investors worried about systemic private-credit risk.
Despite staying structurally bullish on AI, Van Eck now advises trimming (not eliminating) profits on outsized semiconductor and AI trade gains, framing this quarter's move as profit-taking within a maintained overweight.
thesis🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: mediumNvidiasemiconductors
So what: A practical portfolio-management signal: rebalance out of outsized AI winners rather than chase further, while remaining structurally long the theme.
“It's totally reasonable to be taking profits um, on some of your your extra gains in semiconductors or what have you, your AI trades.” ▶ 1:01:23
VanEck's Three 10-Year Structural Trades: AI, India, Gold/Bitcoin 6
Van Eck frames AI, the rise of India, and hedging US government overspending via gold and Bitcoin as VanEck's three core 10-year portfolio themes, explicitly distinguishing them from shorter tactical trades like BDCs.
framework🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: highIndiaGoldBitcoin
So what: Gives investors a top-down structural allocation framework distinct from Van Eck's quarter-to-quarter tactical calls, useful for long-horizon portfolio construction.
Indian equities have gotten cheaper even as earnings continue to grow, which Van Eck views as an attractive entry point into a 'lighter tech,' pro-growth-policy market that he treats as a 10-year structural holding rather than a near-term trade.
thesis🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: mediumIndia
So what: A valuation-driven case to add India exposure now, explicitly framed as a decade-long hold rather than a tactical trade investors should expect quick payoff from.
VanEck has internal debate about Bitcoin entry timing but currently leans toward believing now, before October, is a reasonable entry point after Bitcoin fell from roughly the 70s to the 60s (thousands, USD) during Q2.
thesis🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: mediumBitcoin
So what: A specific, time-bound tactical entry signal on Bitcoin from a major asset manager, nested within a longer 10-year bullish structural thesis.
“It went down from I think like 70-ish to 60-ish over Q2.” ▶ 48:29
Gold's recent selloff was driven by Asian investors deleveraging after the shock of the Iran war and Strait of Hormuz closure, not by US inflation trends — gold hit all-time highs last year even as US inflation was moderating, reinforcing Van Eck's view that gold is primarily an Asian wealth story.
causal🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: highGold
So what: Reframes gold's price driver away from the conventional US-inflation-hedge narrative toward Asian wealth/demand dynamics — useful for forecasting gold moves around non-US macro shocks.
“it's more of an Asian wealth story. So it's no surprise when Asia gets a gut punch from the uh Strait of Hormuz closure and the Iran war that gold sell sells off.” ▶ 53:10
Van Eck is bullish on hard-asset and commodity exposure (citing copper as one example) as a necessary 'AI 2.0' complement to the AI software/compute trade, since building data centers and AI infrastructure requires physical materials and power, though not every related sub-trade has worked out.
thesis🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: mediumcopperdata centers
So what: Positions commodities and power infrastructure as a second-order, still-early way to play the AI capex boom beyond mega-cap tech and chips.
Van Eck cites a bipartisan policy shift favoring nuclear power as a beneficiary of AI-driven electricity demand, though some data-center and independent-power-producer stocks have paused after appreciating sharply.
data_point🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: mediumnuclear power
So what: Nuclear and power-infrastructure names are a volatile but structurally-supported way to play AI-driven electricity demand growth.
Macro Backdrop: Fed, Deficit, Labor, and Social Security 6
The Federal Reserve's policy stance remains a 'less interventionist Fed,' consistent with Treasury Secretary Bessent's Q4-last-year messaging, with Fed leadership's internal task forces expected to issue year-end conclusions that Van Eck predicts will surprise no one.
prediction🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: highFederal ReserveKevin Warsh
So what: Signals no near-term monetary policy surprises; Van Eck expects any mildly contractionary balance-sheet moves to be delayed until after the midterms.
“Their their conclusions will surprise absolutely no one.” ▶ 56:06
Through May of the current fiscal year, the US federal budget deficit is running at 5.8% (down from a 6.5% peak), with tax receipts up 5% year-over-year and spending up 3%, but interest expense up 10% year-over-year and now larger than defense spending.
data_point🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: highUS federal budget deficit
So what: Rising interest costs outpacing revenue and spending growth is the specific mechanism behind Van Eck's long-term US fiscal-risk thesis and the rationale for his gold/Bitcoin hedge.
“we are running at a 5.8% budget deficit. Receipts tax receipts are up 5% compared to last year. Spending is up 3%.” ▶ 54:57
Despite widespread fears at the start of the year about AI-driven job losses, Van Eck sees no evidence of net job losses in labor market data — instead there is significant sectoral displacement, with healthcare and education (driven by an aging population) consistently the strongest job-creating sectors.
data_point🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: highUS labor markethealthcare
So what: Counters an early-2026 bear narrative that AI would quickly show up as aggregate labor-market damage; the actual signal so far is sectoral rotation, not aggregate contraction.
“there's a ton of displacement, but not overall job loss.” ▶ 57:48
The Social Security trust fund is now projected to run out of money in 2032, a year earlier than the prior 2033 estimate.
data_point🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: highSocial Security
So what: A concrete, dated fiscal cliff investors should factor into long-horizon planning around entitlement reform, tax policy, and the durability of Van Eck's fiscal-overspending hedge thesis.
“We're going to run out of money, Adam, not in 2033, but in 2032.” ▶ 58:01
Absent legislative action, the projected 2032 Social Security shortfall would trigger an automatic cut of about 20% to promised benefit payments.
data_point🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: highSocial Security
So what: Quantifies the real benefit-cut risk facing retirees under current law, a concrete data point for retirement and fixed-income planning conversations.
“In 2032, they will cut payments by about 20%.” ▶ 1:00:36
A bipartisan proposal to eliminate the income cap on Social Security payroll contributions is circulating as one fix, alongside newly effective 'Trump accounts' that have the government contributing $1,000 per child born 2026-2028 toward a tax-advantaged investment account, though Van Eck notes the non-investing half of Americans are unlikely to proactively enroll.
data_point🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEckconviction: mediumSocial Security
So what: Both a policy signal (potential payroll-tax cap changes affecting higher earners) and a reminder that participation gaps limit how much new retail-investor demand these programs actually generate for equities.
Market Dispersion: The SaaS-vs-AI Quality Rotation 1
Stock-level dispersion within the market is unusually high, roughly a once-a-decade level, and the 'quality' investment factor has underperformed this year specifically because many quality-index constituents are legacy SaaS software companies now being disrupted by AI.
causal🗣 Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck (citing colleague Angus)conviction: highSaaSquality factor
So what: Standard factor-investing assumptions that 'quality' equals safety are breaking down where quality holdings are themselves AI disruption targets; investors need to underwrite each holding's AI vulnerability individually, not just its factor label.
“AI is coming to eat everybody's lunch.” ▶ 35:21

All-In Podcast 3 episode(s) · 107 insights

Can the AI Industry Regulate Itself? Stripe Wants PayPal, China Catches Up, NY Bans Datacenters90 min45 insights2026-07-18
▶ Watch on YouTube
The All-In hosts debate Demis Hassabis's FINRA-style AI self-regulation proposal and David Sacks's conditions for backing it, dig into the $53B+ Stripe/Advent/Block bid to take PayPal private as a potential Visa-Mastercard challenger, and cover Apple's trade-secret suit against OpenAI, xAI's Grok codebase data leak, wild cross-provider token pricing, and New York's hyperscale data-center moratorium. It closes with a Calico/Revel Pharma study using AlphaFold-designed enzymes to reverse skin aging.
AI Self-Regulation (SRO) Fight · 8Stripe-PayPal-Block Mega-Deal · 8Apple vs. OpenAI Legal Battle · 4AI Model Trust and Token Economics · 6Energy and Data-Center Buildout · 5NY Data-Center Moratorium and the AI Moral Panic · 8Apple's Local AI Compute Bull Case · 3Longevity Science: AI-Designed Anti-Aging Enzyme · 3
AI Self-Regulation (SRO) Fight 8
DeepMind CEO Demis Hassabis proposed a US-led international AI standards body modeled on FINRA (a self-regulatory organization): federally overseen but industry-funded and run by independent experts, with frontier labs submitting models 30 days before release, starting voluntary and becoming mandatory, and benchmarks assessing cyber, national security, and biological risk updated quarterly.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: mediumDemis HassabisDeepMindFINRA
So what: Watch whether this SRO framework becomes the actual US AI regulatory model, which would materially reduce compliance uncertainty for frontier labs versus a new federal agency.
David Sacks said he could support Demis Hassabis's SRO proposal only if it meets five conditions: broad industry representation including startups and open source (to avoid regulatory capture), jurisdiction limited to true frontier models, scope restricted to catastrophic risks only (cyber and CBRN, not disinformation), a voluntary-first rollout before any mandatory phase, and it must substitute for rather than add to future regulation.
framework🗣 David Sacksconviction: highDavid SacksDemis HassabisFINRA
So what: These five conditions are a concrete checklist for tracking whether the AI SRO becomes genuine light-touch self-regulation or a vehicle for regulatory capture as it's implemented.
David Sacks argued that Dario Amodei's alternative proposal, an 'FAA for AI,' would be catastrophic for the industry because FAA aircraft type-certification for an entirely new design takes 5 to 9 years (the Boeing 737 Max took about 5 years), which is incompatible with an industry releasing new model versions every few months.
contrarian🗣 David Sacksconviction: highDario AmodeiAnthropicFAA
So what: If Anthropic succeeds in pushing FAA-style licensing for AI, expect frontier release cadence to slow from months to years, ceding pace advantage to China.
≈ “for an entirely new aircraft design, it takes 5 to 9 years to get the certification” paraphrase
David Sacks characterized Anthropic's public support for the SRO proposal as merely 'an opening bid,' warning that unless AI companies hold a hard line, Anthropic will keep pushing toward its preferred FAA-for-AI model, which he sees as much more restrictive.
contrarian🗣 David Sacksconviction: highAnthropicDario Amodei
So what: Monitor whether Anthropic pushes past the SRO toward mandatory FAA-style licensing after the SRO is adopted — a signal that would be bearish for smaller and open-source AI labs.
“this is just an opening bid for anthropic” ▶ 11:18
Chamath Palihapitiya said he tweeted in October that Anthropic was 'running a sophisticated regulatory capture strategy based on fear-mongering,' and cited a Politico report describing Anthropic's strategy of pushing individual states (starting with California's SB53) to adopt progressively stricter AI rules rather than settling on one national framework.
contrarian🗣 Chamath Palihapitiyaconviction: highAnthropicSB53CaliforniaPolitico
So what: A deliberate state-by-state regulatory ratchet raises compliance costs disproportionately for smaller AI labs relative to well-capitalized incumbents like Anthropic, a headwind for open-source/startup AI valuations in stricter states.
“Anthropic is running a sophisticated regulatory capture strategy based on fear-mongering” ▶ 16:12
Chamath Palihapitiya noted Anthropic already carries a trillion-dollar valuation, with Gavin Baker projecting it could reach $3 trillion post-IPO, undercutting the idea that its regulatory advocacy comes from a scrappy startup rather than a company now on par with Big Tech incumbents.
data_point🗣 Chamath Palihapitiyaconviction: mediumAnthropicGavin Baker
So what: Recalibrates the framing of Anthropic's regulatory lobbying: it should be evaluated as incumbent-scale corporate influence, not underdog policy advocacy.
“Gavin Baker thinks it'll be at three trillion after they IPO” ▶ 16:33
David Sacks argued that once tech companies ask government for oversight power, officials rarely decline, so voluntary regulatory concessions tend to invite escalating demands rather than resolve the underlying pressure.
framework🗣 David Sacksconviction: highAnthropic
So what: A general playbook warning for how AI labs should negotiate the SRO: partial concessions historically ratchet rather than settle regulatory pressure.
“The government will be happy to take this and then come back for more and more and more until it's fully under government control.”
A co-host stressed that any AI SRO must not be allowed to restrict open-source development, arguing that regulatory-capture dynamics typically favor whoever can afford compliance costs, which structurally disadvantages startups and open-source projects relative to well-capitalized incumbents.
framework🗣 co-host (All-In panelist)conviction: mediumopen sourceAnthropic
So what: Whether the eventual SRO rules impose costs that disproportionately burden open-source and startup labs is a key tell for whether the body becomes captured.
“all of these other efforts require money that you have to spend which is always where regulatory capture happens” ▶ 13:24
Stripe-PayPal-Block Mega-Deal 8
Stripe and private-equity firm Advent are jointly bidding roughly $53 billion (about $60/share, a modest premium) to acquire PayPal, with Block (Jack Dorsey's company) also joining the bid by contributing $17 billion in equity; market consensus expects the final price to land closer to $70/share.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: mediumStripePayPalAdventBlockJack Dorsey
So what: A bidding war could push PayPal's takeout price meaningfully above the initial $60/share offer, benefiting existing PayPal shareholders.
Chamath Palihapitiya framed the combined Stripe/PayPal/Block/Advent entity as an effort to build a direct competitor to Visa and Mastercard, combining PayPal's 400M+ consumer accounts, Stripe's merchant risk infrastructure and stablecoin rails (Bridge), and Block's point-of-sale network and Cash App to enable low-cost 'on-us' transactions that bypass card networks.
thesis🗣 Chamath Palihapitiyaconviction: highStripePayPalBlockVisaMastercardBridgeBraintreeCash App
So what: A combined Stripe-PayPal-Block stack could pressure Visa/Mastercard's take rate over time — a structural risk worth tracking for card-network investors.
“it's a shot across the bow for Visa and Mastercard” ▶ 35:30
David Sacks said PayPal has stagnated despite its scale, growing only about 7% a year on a 25-year-old legacy product, and attributed the decline to eBay purging PayPal's founding team after the 2002 acquisition and installing 'consulting types,' which he argues created the 'PayPal diaspora' (his preferred term over 'PayPal mafia').
causal🗣 David Sacksconviction: highPayPaleBayMeg Whitman
So what: A repeatable pattern: acquirers who purge founder DNA from acquired platforms risk long-term stagnation, a relevant lens for judging post-close management of the new deal.
“It's a misnomer to call it the PayPal mafia. It's really the PayPal diaspora.” ▶ 32:01
David Sacks predicted the current $60/share PayPal offer is not the final clearing price and will likely rise another 10-15%, and speculated Elon Musk — a PayPal co-founder with roughly $4-5 trillion in resources and a recent $60B acquisition on his record — could plausibly submit a competing bid.
prediction🗣 David Sacksconviction: mediumPayPalElon Musk
So what: A potential competing bid from Musk could trigger a bidding war, a scenario worth monitoring for PayPal shareholders and arbitrage positioning.
A co-host argued the Stripe-PayPal-Block deal is part of an emerging wave in which AI-native operators and capital acquire mature, founder-less digital businesses that have stalled and under-utilized AI, citing Ryan Cohen's eBay bid, Josh Kushner's accounting-firm roll-up, and General Catalyst's similar strategy as parallel examples.
thesis🗣 co-host (All-In panelist)conviction: highRyan CoheneBayJosh KushnerGeneral Catalyst
So what: Signals a broader M&A thesis: look for other stagnant, founder-absent digital incumbents as targets for AI-driven buyout and operational-revival plays.
A co-host cited Bending Spoons — which acquired AOL, Vimeo, WeTransfer, Eventbrite, and Evernote and staffs them with young AI-first executives to cut costs and revitalize operations — as the existing template for this wave of mega-deals that 'AI-ify' stagnant legacy internet assets, predicting more such deals are coming.
thesis🗣 co-host (All-In panelist)conviction: highBending SpoonsAOLVimeoWeTransferEventbriteEvernote
So what: Bending Spoons is a template worth studying for investors looking to identify or back similar AI-driven roll-up plays on legacy internet assets.
Panelists noted the M&A wave extends beyond PayPal: Uber's acquisition of Delivery Hero is expected to lift Uber's revenue roughly 24%, part of a broader resurgence in dealmaking since Trump's election that they credited with ending the caution of the Lina Khan-era antitrust environment.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: mediumUberDelivery HeroLina KhanDonald Trump
So what: A more permissive antitrust environment is unlocking consolidation across sectors, a tailwind for M&A-adjacent equities and private equity.
≈ “Thank you my President Donald J. Trump for putting M&A back on the menu.” paraphrase
David Sacks argued the Stripe/PayPal deal's antitrust risk hinges entirely on market definition: narrowly defined as merchant payment APIs (Stripe vs. Braintree) it looks anti-competitive, but defined as competing with the Visa/Mastercard duopoly, adding PayPal as a challenger is pro-competitive — and this deal likely would have been blocked two years ago under stricter enforcement.
framework🗣 David Sacksconviction: highStripeBraintreeVisaMastercard
So what: Illustrates how antitrust market-definition arguments can flip a deal from illegal to pro-competitive depending on regulatory posture — relevant to gauging the deal's closing risk.
“how you define the market determines whether it's anti-competitive or prompetitive” ▶ 37:42
Apple vs. OpenAI Legal Battle 4
Apple filed a 41-page lawsuit against OpenAI on July 10 alleging OpenAI stole Apple trade secrets to build its consumer hardware device, alleging Apple's former VP of iPhone design Tang Tan (now OpenAI's chief hardware officer) directed job candidates to bring 'actual parts' to interviews, and that OpenAI poached over 400 Apple employees over the past year or two.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: mediumAppleOpenAITang TanTim Cook
So what: The legal outcome could affect the IP ownership and launch timeline of OpenAI's rumored consumer hardware device.
“lol, I found out I can access the network storage. So funny.” ▶ 38:56
Chamath Palihapitiya said Apple's willingness to sue is itself a signal, noting the company has rarely litigated in 25 years in Silicon Valley, implying something significant provoked the suit.
contrarian🗣 Chamath Palihapitiyaconviction: mediumAppleOpenAI
So what: Apple's atypical litigiousness raises the probability the trade-secret claims have real substance, adding legal overhang to OpenAI's hardware roadmap.
“I haven't really seen Apple very latigiously in 25 years in Silicon Valley. So that's obviously a concerning data point for OpenAI.” ▶ 39:50
David Sacks offered a simple rule for employee-mobility disputes: departing employees can take only 'what's in your head' to a new employer — no files, thumb drives, or documents — regardless of California's lack of non-competes.
framework🗣 David Sacksconviction: highAppleOpenAI
So what: A general risk-management rule for any company hiring from competitors amid the ongoing AI talent war.
“The only thing you can bring to your new job is what's in your head.” ▶ 42:09
David Friedberg noted OpenAI's mounting legal troubles form a pattern rather than bad luck, following prior lawsuits, suggesting a broader governance or culture risk at the company beyond this single case.
contrarian🗣 David Friedbergconviction: lowOpenAI
So what: Repeated litigation exposure across multiple fronts could raise legal and reputational risk for OpenAI ahead of any future IPO.
“Couple dots make a line, I guess.” ▶ 42:41
AI Model Trust and Token Economics 6
xAI's Grok build coding tool (powered by Grok 4.5) was found to be transmitting developers' entire codebases -- including passwords, API keys, and change logs, not just task-relevant files -- to xAI's cloud servers during sessions, despite xAI's explicit promise that nothing from a user's codebase would be transmitted; xAI disabled the upload on July 13 and Musk said all previously uploaded data has been deleted.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: mediumxAIGrokGrok buildElon Musk
So what: A concrete case study in AI-coding-tool privacy risk, and a due-diligence red flag for enterprises evaluating any AI coding assistant's data-retention claims.
“nothing from your codebase is transmitted to XAI servers during a session” ▶ 43:06
Chamath Palihapitiya argued 'zero data retention' (ZDR) promises from AI model providers can't be fully trusted because non-obvious data leak vectors exist that even well-intentioned companies don't discover until it's too late, reinforcing his push for an independent third-party layer between enterprises and frontier model providers.
contrarian🗣 Chamath Palihapitiyaconviction: highxAIAnthropicOpenAI
So what: Enterprises relying on vendor ZDR assurances should assume leak risk and architect independent data-isolation layers rather than trusting model-provider promises alone.
“there are all kinds of non-obvious data leak vectors lurking in AI” ▶ 44:44
Chamath Palihapitiya cited data showing wide per-million-input-token pricing disparities across AI model providers, ranging as high as roughly $26-56 for premium closed models versus about $1.50 for Grok and roughly $0.50 for Chinese open-source models, arguing users are paying 50-100x more for comparable output on most tasks.
data_point🗣 Chamath Palihapitiyaconviction: highGrokxAIGLM
So what: Token-cost arbitrage is a real competitive lever -- the price gap alone could let a much cheaper, slightly weaker competitor out-compete premium-priced frontier labs on unit economics.
“People are paying between 26 and 56 bucks. They should be paying 50 cents.” ▶ 51:50
RAMP CEO Eric Glyman said the company is launching a token-spend-management product because AI token spend among RAMP customers has grown 21x over the past year, and CFOs are increasingly surprised by ballooning, hard-to-forecast AI usage bills.
data_point🗣 host (citing RAMP CEO Eric Glyman)conviction: highRAMPEric Glyman
So what: A leading indicator that AI inference cost is becoming a material, under-controlled opex line item for enterprises.
“token spend among RAMP customers has grown by 21 times” ▶ 49:49
Chamath Palihapitiya predicted that unmanaged, rapidly compounding AI token spend will eventually cause a public company to miss earnings due to opex surprises, because engineers default to the most expensive frontier model regardless of cost while CFOs lack visibility into the spend.
prediction🗣 Chamath Palihapitiyaconviction: mediumRAMP
So what: Watch for AI-driven opex misses as an emerging earnings-season risk factor across software and tech companies.
“somebody's going to miss a quarter. I don't know who, but somebody.” ▶ 51:00
Panelists noted a growing multi-provider AI infrastructure ecosystem forming as an alternative to closed, vertically-integrated model stacks: Bittensor subnets, GLM hosting Grok 4.5, and new fine-tuning platforms positioning themselves as cheap layers on open models rather than frontier-intelligence providers.
thesis🗣 co-host (All-In panelist)conviction: mediumBittensorGLMGrok
So what: The rise of a cheap, open multi-model ecosystem structurally threatens the premium pricing power of closed frontier labs like Anthropic and OpenAI.
Energy and Data-Center Buildout 5
Chamath Palihapitiya said the US will face an energy deficit by 2050 equivalent to about 2.5 times all of California's current energy consumption, underscoring a structural power shortage as the binding constraint on AI infrastructure growth.
data_point🗣 Chamath Palihapitiyaconviction: highUnited States
So what: Power availability, not chip supply, is emerging as the primary bottleneck for AI scaling, favoring investment in power generation, grid, and behind-the-meter solutions over pure compute plays.
“By 2050, the United States of America will be 2 and 12 California's worth of energy in deficit.” ▶ 56:43
Chamath Palihapitiya cited a recent PJM (the utility serving Pennsylvania, New Jersey, Maryland and 13 states) forward-power auction where the utility sought roughly 7-8 gigawatts of committed future capacity but bidders offered only about 156 megawatts, illustrating a severe electricity supply shortfall relative to forecast demand.
data_point🗣 Chamath Palihapitiyaconviction: highPJM
So what: Extreme undersupply in forward power auctions signals persistent electricity price inflation in PJM territory, relevant for utility and data-center-adjacent investments.
“They needed like seven or eight gawatt. They had 156 megawatts or something show up.” ▶ 57:29
Chamath Palihapitiya explained Elon Musk's power strategy at his Memphis 'Colossus' site: rather than wait for grid interconnection, Musk deployed mobile generator turbines classified as 'personal use' equipment to sidestep clean-air permitting rules, and is now adopting technologies like Bloom Energy fuel cells that qualify under the same personal-use exemption for future capacity.
causal🗣 Chamath Palihapitiyaconviction: highElon MuskxAIBloom Energy
So what: Behind-the-meter generation plus creative regulatory structuring is becoming the standard playbook for hyperscale AI data centers to bypass grid bottlenecks -- worth tracking as a template (and permitting risk) for other builders.
Panelists reported that despite geographic advantages, Middle East data-center buildout has stalled relative to expectations, while Australia and other parts of Asia have seen an unexpected surge in hyperscale deals, and the UAE has recently gained approval to import best-in-class leading-edge chips.
data_point🗣 co-host (All-In panelist)conviction: mediumMiddle EastAustraliaUAE
So what: UAE chip-import approval is a leading indicator for a fresh wave of regional data-center investment that could relieve some US capacity constraints.
Chamath Palihapitiya said pricing data from his own data-center asset portfolio shows the market paying an extreme premium for verifiable, energizable power available today versus future capacity, because roughly 40% of planned data-center power projects are getting mothballed or stalled.
data_point🗣 Chamath Palihapitiyaconviction: highdata centers
So what: Scarcity pricing for near-term energizable power favors owners of already-permitted, already-energized data-center and power assets, a distinct investable niche from raw compute.
“about 40% of all these projects are getting mothballled and stopped” ▶ 1:07:50
NY Data-Center Moratorium and the AI Moral Panic 8
New York Governor Kathy Hochul signed the nation's first statewide moratorium on hyperscale data centers, citing concerns about fossil-fuel power use, land displacement, higher utility bills, water depletion, and noise pollution.
data_point🗣 Kathy Hochul (via clip)conviction: highKathy HochulNew York
So what: Sets a precedent other blue states may follow, potentially pushing data-center investment toward more permissive states like Texas and other countries.
“I'll be signing the nation's first ever statewide moratorum on hyperscale data centers.” ▶ 1:00:29
David Sacks rebutted Hochul's justifications point by point: behind-the-meter data centers bring their own power rather than straining residential rates; land use is efficient relative to economic output; modern data centers use closed-loop water recirculation (one study put typical usage at roughly the water equivalent of two-and-a-half In-N-Out burgers); and natural gas, the primary power source, is a relatively clean-burning fuel.
contrarian🗣 David Sacksconviction: highKathy HochulNew York
So what: Undercuts the stated policy rationale for state-level data-center moratoriums, suggesting the drivers are political rather than factual -- relevant for assessing regulatory risk state by state.
“a typical data center uses the same amount of water as two and a half in-n-out burgers” ▶ 1:01:55
Jason Calacanis noted New York State is roughly 70-80% undeveloped land outside New York City, arguing the moratorium's land-use rationale doesn't hold given how much rural and upstate capacity exists.
data_point🗣 Jason Calacanisconviction: highNew York
So what: Undercuts one of the stated justifications for the moratorium, suggesting upstate NY is a viable but politically blocked data-center location.
“70 to 80% of the land in New York State is undeveloped” ▶ 1:03:39
David Sacks speculated, relaying a source in politics, that the Democratic data-center moratorium strategy is a negotiating tactic rather than a permanent block -- pausing construction until Democrats gain enough leverage to dictate reopening terms, likely including a new federal AI regulatory agency and social-media-style speech controls -- and warned any reversal implies an effective pause of five years or more given project ramp-up time.
prediction🗣 David Sacksconviction: mediumNew YorkDemocratic Party
So what: Data-center investors should model an extended, multi-year effective shutdown in moratorium states rather than a short-term pause when making site-selection decisions.
“when you start talking about a moratorium on data centers, it's not like a a few month pause” ▶ 1:06:57
Jason Calacanis relayed comments from Energy Secretary Chris Wright at a defense summit suggesting that funding behind the anti-data-center protest movement traces back to the same networks that historically funded anti-fracking protests, implying a recurring 'professional protester' funding pattern rather than organic grassroots opposition.
anecdote🗣 host (relaying Chris Wright)conviction: lowChris Wright
So what: Suggests anti-data-center activism may be coordinated campaign funding rather than spontaneous public sentiment, worth scrutinizing the funding disclosures of groups opposing local data-center projects.
Chamath Palihapitiya drew a historical parallel between today's anti-data-center sentiment and the anti-GMO movement, presenting Google Trends-style evidence that US anti-GMO sentiment emerged only after Russian state broadcaster Russia Today launched in the US in 2010 (despite GMOs existing commercially since 1996) and declined after RT was removed from US platforms in 2022, implying foreign influence operations may similarly be driving anti-data-center activism.
causal🗣 Chamath Palihapitiyaconviction: mediumRussia TodayGMO
So what: If anti-data-center sentiment is substantially foreign-influence-driven, current moratoriums like New York's may reflect an informational vulnerability rather than genuine policy consensus, and could reverse as influence campaigns are exposed.
Chamath Palihapitiya cited an OpenAI blog post explicitly identifying PRC-linked influence operations targeting the US AI debate, and argued China has a direct strategic incentive to slow US data-center construction to help it win the broader AI race.
data_point🗣 Chamath Palihapitiyaconviction: mediumOpenAIChina
So what: Adds a national-security dimension to state-level AI infrastructure policy debates -- watch for congressional investigation findings referenced on the show.
“China is behind a lot of these influence campaigns to shape US attitudes on AI data centers” ▶ 1:15:23
David Sacks argued the AI 'moral panic' is premature because none of the predicted catastrophic harms (cyber risk, mass job loss) have materialized, noting every current frontier model has far exceeded the 10^25 FLOPs threshold once floated as a regulatory trigger for existential risk without producing anything resembling the feared outcome, while panelists separately mocked Dario Amodei's forecast that 50% of entry-level knowledge-worker jobs would vanish within 1-5 years.
contrarian🗣 David Sacksconviction: highDario AmodeiAnthropic
So what: Argues against precautionary regulation calibrated to hypothetical future risk, relevant to how aggressively investors should price in near-term AI regulatory catalysts.
“they thought that 10 to the 25th flops would be enough compute to create, you know, the Terminator, you know, to create Skynet” ▶ 1:20:38
Apple's Local AI Compute Bull Case 3
Jason Calacanis predicted Apple's upcoming M7 Ultra chip will support up to 1.5 terabytes of unified memory, double the prior generation's ceiling, enough to run an Opus-level frontier model locally on a $4,000-5,000 Mac Studio.
prediction🗣 Jason Calacanisconviction: mediumAppleMac StudioM7 Ultra
So what: If realized, local high-memory Apple Silicon could materially cannibalize cloud inference revenue for OpenAI and Anthropic by letting enterprises run near-frontier models locally at a fixed hardware cost instead of per-token API pricing.
“M7 Ultra is going to support as much as 1.5 terabytes” ▶ 54:38
Jason Calacanis called Apple 'a screaming buy right now,' arguing that because Apple monetizes through hardware rather than API tokens, it can put sustained downward pricing pressure on Claude and OpenAI by shipping capable local models with enough memory that token limits become irrelevant to end users.
thesis🗣 Jason Calacanisconviction: mediumAppleOpenAI
So what: A bullish structural thesis on Apple as an AI-compute disintermediator, relevant for modeling the long-term API pricing power of cloud model providers.
“Apple is a screaming buy right now” ▶ 55:11
Panelists connected the local-compute trend to broader edge-compute fragmentation, citing a solar company building distributed data-center blocks for homes and a company called SPAN partnering with Nvidia on residential compute/energy integration.
thesis🗣 co-host (All-In panelist)conviction: lowNvidiaSPAN
So what: An emerging theme worth tracking: residential compute-plus-energy bundles as a category adjacent to both AI infrastructure and home-energy investing.
Longevity Science: AI-Designed Anti-Aging Enzyme 3
David Friedberg described a new study from Calico (Google's anti-aging research unit) and Revel Pharma that used AlphaFold to design a novel enzyme capable of degrading CML, an advanced glycation end-product that accumulates in the extracellular matrix and drives skin wrinkling, joint stiffness, and inflammation with age; the enzyme degraded 52-97% of CML on target proteins (casein, collagen, retinal proteins, hemoglobin) in testing, with some sites showing over 90% degradation.
data_point🗣 David Friedbergconviction: highCalicoRevel PharmaAlphaFoldCML
So what: A concrete AI-drug-discovery proof point worth tracking for Calico/Revel Pharma and follow-on longevity biotech investment or licensing opportunities as delivery mechanisms are developed.
David Friedberg said that when the enzyme was applied directly to donated human skin from patients over 70, it eliminated 55% of the skin's CML and reversed the skin's biological age markers down to those of a 31-year-old, though the delivery mechanism -- cream, injection, or an RNA-encoded in-body production -- remains unresolved.
data_point🗣 David Friedbergconviction: highCalicoRevel Pharma
So what: If a topical delivery format proves viable, this could create a large near-term cosmetic/skincare commercial application well ahead of any systemic anti-aging therapy.
“they were able to eliminate 55% of the CML on the skin which basically reversed the skin's age down to the age of a 31y old” ▶ 1:26:58
A co-host predicted the first commercial application for this CML-degrading enzyme will be cosmetic skincare rather than medical or joint therapeutics, sizing a viable topical anti-aging cream product at as much as $2 trillion in market potential.
prediction🗣 co-host (All-In panelist)conviction: mediumCalicoRevel Pharma
So what: Flags a potential multi-trillion-dollar cosmetics/consumer-health category forming at the intersection of AI-driven protein design and skincare, worth watching for licensing deals or spinouts.
“that alone is $2 trillion” ▶ 1:28:23
The Trillion-Dollar Industries AI Is Disrupting: Voice, Law & the End of the Billable Hour52 min34 insights2026-07-14
▶ Watch on YouTube
Two founder interviews back to back: ElevenLabs' Mati Staniszewski details the company's hypergrowth (to ~$600M revenue, 600 employees, zero founding-team attrition) and its bet on staying model-agnostic rather than building a general model, while Legora's Max explains how AI is dismantling the billable-hour law-firm model and threatening legacy data incumbents LexisNexis and Westlaw, backed by Legora's own 50%-QoQ growth and M&A-driven expansion.
ElevenLabs' Hypergrowth Revenue Ramp & Org Design · 4Voice AI Is Changing How Humans Interact With Machines · 3Voice Cloning IP, Celebrity Licensing & Deepfake Safeguards · 4ElevenLabs vs. the Frontier Labs: Coopetition & Model Strategy · 4The Trillion-Dollar Legal Market's Software Gap & Legora's Growth · 9Legal Data Moats: Legora vs. LexisNexis and Westlaw · 4Legora's Narrow-Model Strategy & Compliance Moat · 6
ElevenLabs' Hypergrowth Revenue Ramp & Org Design 4
ElevenLabs' ARR milestones accelerated sharply: after founding in 2022 and launching its first human-sounding text-to-speech model in 2023, it took ~20 months to reach $100M ARR, ~10 more months to reach $200M, ~5 more months to reach $300M by the end of last year, and it now stands at roughly $600M in revenue.
data_point🗣 Mati, ElevenLabs co-founder/CEOconviction: highElevenLabs
So what: The compressing time-to-milestone curve signals unusually strong and still-accelerating product-market fit, a key data point for benchmarking growth-multiple valuations against other AI application companies.
“It took us roughly 20 months to get to the first 100 million in ARR. Roughly 10 months to get to 200, 5 months to get to 300.” ▶ 1:14
ElevenLabs has never hired a product manager, instead favoring people who are expert in at least one of coding, customer understanding, or design while competent in the others, arguing AI now lets such generalists 'step change' to an advanced level across functions without needing a dedicated PM to coordinate them.
framework🗣 Mati, ElevenLabs co-founder/CEOconviction: highElevenLabs
So what: A replicable AI-native org-design pattern (no PM layer) that could mean leaner headcount and higher operating leverage for comparable AI-native startups versus legacy software orgs.
“We don't have any PMs.” ▶ 7:13
ElevenLabs embeds engineers inside non-engineering functions like talent/HR and legal, whose job is both to automate that team's work and to police AI-adoption quality, because employees using AI coding tools 'can create but not necessarily review' whether their output is actually secure.
framework🗣 Mati, ElevenLabs co-founder/CEOconviction: highElevenLabs
So what: Flags an emerging governance risk (shadow security debt from non-engineers using AI coding tools) and a hiring/role category — embedded reviewer-engineers — that other AI-adopting companies may need to build out.
ElevenLabs now has 600 employees and reports zero attrition among its original ten core research and engineering hires from 2022, which its CEO attributes to deliberately optimizing the hiring and onboarding cycle to preserve a research-and-product-driven culture through hypergrowth.
data_point🗣 Mati, ElevenLabs co-founder/CEOconviction: mediumElevenLabs
So what: Founding-team retention through hypergrowth is a useful proxy for cultural/execution risk investors should track when comparing fast-scaling AI startups.
“from the first 10 people we had zero attrition” ▶ 3:11
Voice AI Is Changing How Humans Interact With Machines 3
ElevenLabs' CEO says voice-agent interaction quality crossed a threshold in roughly the last six to twelve months, to the point that consumers now find AI voice agents faster and less socially burdensome to interrupt than human agents, which he expects to drive a broad consumer shift toward preferring AI-first phone interactions.
thesis🗣 Mati, ElevenLabs co-founder/CEOconviction: highElevenLabs
So what: If consumer preference is genuinely flipping toward AI-first phone support, it expands the addressable market for conversational voice platforms well beyond cost-cutting IVR replacement into a true UX upgrade — worth tracking via call-center and customer-support software adoption metrics.
In ElevenLabs' financial-services deployments (cited: Revolut and Clara) for use cases like payment reminders and debt collection, customers are more willing to honestly disclose their real financial situation to an AI voice agent than to a human, because the shame people feel confessing to another person doesn't apply to AI, and customers also engage AI agents more tersely and quickly since interrupting them carries no social cost.
causal🗣 Mati, ElevenLabs co-founder/CEOconviction: mediumElevenLabsRevolutClara
So what: This emotional-disinhibition effect suggests AI voice agents could be structurally better than humans at sensitive fintech workflows like collections and compliance calls — a specific vertical to watch for AI voice adoption.
“people would naturally feel ashamed of telling the real situation with AI people are much more open to share what actually happened” ▶ 14:53
A push-to-talk pedal paired with the dictation app Whisper Flow (which uses ElevenLabs' models among others on its back end) lets users give one-to-two-minute 'stream of consciousness' spoken prompts instead of typed text, which the host says has fundamentally changed how he prompts LLMs because the models handle long rambling spoken input better than expected.
anecdote🗣 Jason Calacanis (host)conviction: mediumWhisper FlowElevenLabs
So what: Signals a growing market for voice-first AI-workflow accessories (dictation apps, foot pedals) as an adjacent product category to core LLM usage.
“when I press the pedal down, I just give a stream of consciousness now.” ▶ 13:30
Voice Cloning IP, Celebrity Licensing & Deepfake Safeguards 4
ElevenLabs has paid out over $22 million to date to a community of voice talent through its marketplace, where users can create, authenticate, and license their own AI voice clone and set or default their own price — turning voice licensing into a recurring income stream for former hourly voiceover actors rather than a one-time gig fee.
data_point🗣 Mati, ElevenLabs co-founder/CEOconviction: highElevenLabs
So what: Establishes a royalty-style monetization model for voice IP that other AI content platforms (video, music) may replicate, and creates a proprietary data flywheel for ElevenLabs.
≈ “we paid back over $22 million back to the community of talent” paraphrase
Before he passed, James Earl Jones licensed his Darth Vader voice to Disney in perpetuity using ElevenLabs' technology, and Epic Games subsequently used it to let Fortnite players interact live with an AI-voiced Darth Vader after reaching certain in-game stages — an early example of interactive, licensed celebrity-likeness content at consumer scale.
anecdote🗣 Mati, ElevenLabs co-founder/CEOconviction: highElevenLabsDisneyEpic GamesFortniteJames Earl Jones
So what: Opens a new 'interactive likeness rights' licensing category relevant to media, gaming, and estates looking to monetize legacy IP beyond static content.
ElevenLabs' CEO frames the company's most important work as restoring speech for people who lost their voice to ALS or throat cancer, citing U.S. Congresswoman Jennifer Wexton, who used a restored AI voice to deliver a speech in Congress.
anecdote🗣 Mati, ElevenLabs co-founder/CEOconviction: highElevenLabsJennifer Wexton
So what: Points to a durable assistive-healthcare vertical for voice-cloning technology with strong goodwill and potential reimbursement dynamics beyond entertainment and enterprise use cases.
To manage impersonation and deepfake risk, ElevenLabs runs three safeguards: tracing everything generated so misuse can be actioned, moderating both voice and text prompts to block commercial or scam-oriented generation, and offering a free public detection tool that checks whether any audio sample — including from other open-source models — is AI-generated.
framework🗣 Mati, ElevenLabs co-founder/CEOconviction: highElevenLabs
So what: This traceability-moderation-detection stack is becoming a template that regulators and enterprise buyers may come to require of any generative-voice vendor, relevant for assessing compliance moats in the sector.
“We do three things. One, trace everything that's generated so we can take action when needed.” ▶ 19:02
ElevenLabs vs. the Frontier Labs: Coopetition & Model Strategy 4
Even though Anthropic and OpenAI explicitly want ElevenLabs' business, its CEO keeps the platform model-agnostic across Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, and open-source models, arguing ElevenLabs' real moat is the interaction layer (speech-to-text, text-to-speech, turn-taking, music) rather than the underlying LLM, and that architecture, not scale, is what has let ElevenLabs out-compete frontier labs on voice models.
contrarian🗣 Mati, ElevenLabs co-founder/CEOconviction: highElevenLabsAnthropicOpenAIGoogle
So what: For investors weighing 'wrapper risk' in AI application companies, ElevenLabs is betting that a defensible interaction layer plus vertical workflow plus ecosystem can survive being commoditized by frontier labs — a thesis worth testing against churn/retention data.
“It's the architecture that matters, not the scale. You really need to change how the model operates.” ▶ 27:06
ElevenLabs' research edge is fueled by an internal team of over 1,000 contractors who label unstructured audio data, which its CEO frames as a bigger differentiator than raw model scale.
data_point🗣 Mati, ElevenLabs co-founder/CEOconviction: highElevenLabs
So what: Highlights labeled proprietary data — not compute — as the scarce input in voice AI, a useful lens for evaluating the durability of vertical AI vendors' moats versus foundation labs.
ElevenLabs' CEO acknowledges frontier labs are 'continuously trying to figure out how to distill and use' its data despite claiming not to, says the company has mechanisms only to 'slow it down, not stop it,' and that ElevenLabs itself ships 'tens of millions of dollars' a year to those same labs in token spend even as they compete for its business.
contrarian🗣 Mati, ElevenLabs co-founder/CEOconviction: mediumElevenLabsAnthropicOpenAI
So what: Quantifies a real data-leakage and dependency risk that vertical AI application companies face when built on frontier-model APIs, and shows the scale of token spend flowing from the app layer up to the labs.
ElevenLabs' CEO believes decades-old AI/AGI benchmarks have already been passed and that the industry needs new tests, proposing the bar should be whether a system is 'more intelligent than every single person on the planet times 10.'
thesis🗣 Mati, ElevenLabs co-founder/CEOconviction: mediumElevenLabs
So what: Reflects how AI-native founders are quietly redefining 'AGI' upward as older benchmarks are cleared — a narrative shift worth tracking when parsing lab and founder claims about AGI timelines.
“the new test is like can this be more intelligent than every single person on the planet times 10” ▶ 30:45
The Trillion-Dollar Legal Market's Software Gap & Legora's Growth 9
The global legal services market is roughly $1 trillion a year, but only about $40 billion of that is legal-technology software spend — about 4% software versus 96% manual service — which Legora's executive says is set to invert as software eats into the service revenue.
data_point🗣 Max, Legora co-founderconviction: highLegora
So what: Quantifies a large software-penetration gap in legal services comparable to the early-stage disruption other professional-services markets underwent — a sizeable greenfield TAM for legal AI.
“the software spend into legal technology is about 40 billion. So it means there's 4% software 96% service which is bananas.” ▶ 34:30
Legora has sustained 50% quarter-over-quarter revenue growth for seven consecutive quarters and, as of the week before this interview, became one of the fastest enterprise companies with a direct sales motion to go from $1M to roughly $100-150M in ARR, beating Sierra to that milestone by one quarter.
data_point🗣 Max, Legora co-founderconviction: highLegoraSierra
So what: A best-in-class enterprise SaaS growth benchmark useful for valuing Legora and comparable vertical legal-AI startups against top-tier B2B AI growth stories like Sierra.
“a 50 sustained 50% quarter over quarter for the last seven quarters” ▶ 31:51
Law-firm billable-hour economics systematically overcharge junior associates (roughly $800/hour, up to $4,000/hour at top firms) relative to their actual value while undercharging senior partners, because hourly pricing cannot capture that thirty minutes of a top partner's judgment — for example, averting a bet-the-company mistake — can be worth far more than the billed rate.
framework🗣 Max, Legora co-founderconviction: highKirkland & Ellis
So what: Explains why AI-driven automation is targeting commoditizable junior-associate work first while partner-level judgment remains harder to automate or price — a lens for identifying which law-firm revenue is most at risk from AI.
“the way that that business model works is you overcharge for the associates and you actually undercharge for the partners” ▶ 35:52
Kirkland & Ellis generates about $10 billion a year in revenue with 4,000-5,000 lawyers, and its partners earn between $5 million and $10 million each in annual profit — illustrating the scale of incumbent law-firm economics that AI-native legal challengers are targeting.
data_point🗣 Max, Legora co-founderconviction: highKirkland & Ellis
So what: Sizes the profit pool at stake for legal AI disruptors and explains why incumbent firms simultaneously see AI as an existential threat and an existential opportunity.
“Kirkland Ellis turns around $10 billion a year.” ▶ 38:10
A firm referred to in the conversation as 'Kulie' (likely the law firm Cooley, known for serving startups) built a self-serve software platform where founders upload their materials and get the firm's own precedent-driven contract-review workflows, an example of an incumbent law firm productizing its services with AI rather than just staffing them.
anecdote🗣 Max, Legora co-founderconviction: mediumKulie
So what: Shows an incumbent law firm using its proprietary precedent data plus AI to defend and expand share, a pattern relevant to handicapping winners versus losers among legacy legal players facing AI disruption.
Enterprises are moving legal work in-house because their incentive (closing deals fast) is opposed to outside law firms' incentive (maximizing billable hours, which drags deals out); Legora itself has acquired four companies this year and closed its fastest acquisition in 12 days from LOI to closing by running diligence in-house with its own AI tool.
causal🗣 Max, Legora co-founderconviction: highLegora
So what: A concrete proof point that AI-native legal tooling can compress M&A timelines dramatically, useful precedent for corporate-development teams evaluating AI-driven diligence.
“the fastest transaction we did was 12 days from LOI to closing” ▶ 36:55
Law firms are experimenting with pricing models beyond the billable hour, including fixed fees per transaction or fundraise and success fees tied to litigation outcomes, as AI compresses the time required to do the underlying legal work.
data_point🗣 Max, Legora co-founderconviction: mediumLegora
So what: Signals a structural repricing of legal services from time-based to outcome/value-based billing, relevant to modeling future legal-services revenue per matter.
“a fixed fee for a transaction or for a fund raise” ▶ 37:26
Legora created a 'legal engineer' role explicitly modeled on Palantir's forward-deployed engineer, embedding people directly with law-firm clients — including Kirkland & Ellis partners — to help transform their workflows and business model from pre-AI to post-AI.
framework🗣 Max, Legora co-founderconviction: highLegoraPalantirKirkland & Ellis
So what: A go-to-market pattern borrowed from Palantir that other vertical-AI enterprise vendors may replicate to drive adoption in change-resistant, high-value industries.
“we have forward deployed lawyers and their job is to sit down with the Kirkland partners and help them transform their business” ▶ 38:50
The junior-lawyer career path is being restructured, not eliminated: instead of manually reviewing every document in a data room, junior lawyers increasingly orchestrate AI agents that do that review, though firms still need a training pipeline to develop future senior partners.
causal🗣 Max, Legora co-founderconviction: mediumLegora
So what: Implies near-term legal-sector headcount impact is more about task-mix change than outright job elimination, relevant to sizing the AI labor-market effect on legal employment.
“It's orchestrating the agent that will be doing that work.” ▶ 40:21
Legal Data Moats: Legora vs. LexisNexis and Westlaw 4
Legora's data moat combines a client's own proprietary precedent and organizational data with Legora's own aggregation of case law, legislation, and regulatory updates across every global jurisdiction, letting a California-based general counsel with a new South African customer get an '80% accurate' localized legal answer immediately instead of routing through a chain of local-counsel referrals.
causal🗣 Max, Legora co-founderconviction: highLegora
So what: Positions cross-border legal-data aggregation as a structural moat and a concrete product capability — instant jurisdictional localization — that could compress the traditional local-counsel referral business.
“they can get an 80% accurate response immediately that they can start working off out of” ▶ 42:03
Legal research inverts the usual AI power-law logic: a litigator preparing a major case needs the entire corpus of case law, not just the most common precedents, because a single missed case can be fatal to a filing, so a legal research product cannot be built on partial data coverage.
contrarian🗣 Max, Legora co-founderconviction: highLegora
So what: Explains why complete data coverage — not just breadth in the top percentile — is the real moat in legal research, raising the bar for any new entrant trying to compete with LexisNexis or Westlaw on data completeness.
“you cannot build a legal research solution that doesn't have all of the data” ▶ 45:43
LexisNexis generates only 'a couple billion dollars a year' despite its historical data moat, while Legora and Harvey combined are already generating hundreds of millions of dollars in revenue, and Westlaw holds a government-granted monopoly on official U.S. case reporting despite being a privately owned company.
data_point🗣 guest/host discussionconviction: highLexisNexisWestlawHarveyLegora
So what: Quantifies incumbent legal-research revenue against fast-growing AI-native challengers, a useful comparator when valuing legal-tech disruption plays, and flags the unusual public-utility-like monopoly Westlaw holds over official case reporting.
The publicly traded parent companies behind LexisNexis and Westlaw are described in the conversation as having stocks that are 'getting crushed,' which the speakers attribute to the market pricing in AI-driven disruption risk to their legacy data-moat business models.
data_point🗣 host and Max, Legora co-founderconviction: mediumLexisNexisWestlaw
So what: A public-market signal that AI disruption of legal-research incumbents is already being priced by investors — relevant to anyone tracking the parent companies behind LexisNexis or Westlaw.
“they're getting crushed” ▶ 44:37
Legora's Narrow-Model Strategy & Compliance Moat 6
Legora's executive explicitly rejects building or fine-tuning a general legal-intelligence model, calling it a total waste of time and money, in direct contrast to some competitors who are attempting exactly that.
contrarian🗣 Max, Legora co-founderconviction: highLegora
So what: A direct strategic bet against vertical foundation-model-building that investors can use to differentiate Legora's capital efficiency from rivals pursuing proprietary general legal models.
“I don't believe in fine-tuning or building any general intelligence models. I think that's uh total waste of time and money.” ▶ 48:48
Legora does build narrow, task-specific fine-tuned models for high-volume repetitive workloads — for example, its 'tabular review' feature runs documents times prompts (100 documents by 100 prompts equals 10,000 API calls) through a fine-tuned contract-data-extraction model to cut cost and latency rather than routing every call through a general frontier model.
framework🗣 Max, Legora co-founderconviction: highLegora
So what: A concrete cost-optimization pattern — narrow fine-tuned models for bulk repetitive tasks — that other AI application companies can copy to reduce frontier-model API spend at scale.
“100 documents, 100 prompts, 10,000 API calls.” ▶ 49:16
Legora's executive characterizes Anthropic's built-in Claude legal offering as shallow — 'a bundling of markdown skills files and a couple of integrations' — arguing it functions as a pipeline generator for Legora because users hit its ceiling and then become Legora customers.
contrarian🗣 Max, Legora co-founderconviction: highLegoraAnthropicClaude
So what: Suggests frontier labs' vertical add-on features may inadvertently seed demand for specialized application-layer competitors rather than displacing them — a nuance for the 'does the model layer eat the app layer' debate.
“a bundling of markdown skills files and a couple of integrations” ▶ 47:54
Following the release of Anthropic's Opus 4.5 and 4.6 models, Legora's executive says agents can now perform genuinely intelligent end-to-end case strategy by combining witness statements and case law, shifting the lawyer's role from AI merely augmenting work to orchestrating and managing agents that actually do the work.
thesis🗣 Max, Legora co-founderconviction: highLegoraAnthropic
So what: Marks a claimed capability inflection point in legal-AI agents directly tied to a specific model upgrade, worth validating against Legora's actual case-strategy product output.
“they can now start to do really intelligent case strategy and they can actually start to combine the witness statements, the cases” ▶ 46:54
Legora treats regulatory compliance as its core competitive currency because it serves highly regulated customers — including governments and, per the executive, national-security and weapons-manufacturer clients — where any leakage of case data into a language model would be disastrous; this compliance bar makes legal AI hard to sell initially but creates high switching costs and easy expansion once a customer is onboarded.
causal🗣 Max, Legora co-founderconviction: highLegora
So what: Explains both the slow initial sales cycle and the durable retention/expansion economics of compliance-driven enterprise legal AI, relevant to modeling net revenue retention for this category.
“compliance is our currency” ▶ 50:04
Legora does not offer on-premise deployment and avoids VPC deployment because it is 'very time consuming' and creates dependencies that slow the product roadmap, a deliberate tradeoff even with highly sensitive government and national-security customers.
data_point🗣 Max, Legora co-founderconviction: highLegora
So what: Signals Legora is willing to forgo some enterprise deals requiring on-prem or VPC deployment in order to preserve product velocity — a specific risk/tradeoff for investors to weigh against its compliance-sensitive customer base.
“deploying in a VPC is very time consuming and it creates a lot of dependencies which slow down your road map and the execution forward” ▶ 50:55
Former Intel CEO on What Went Wrong, What's Next + Lovable CEO on the Real Promise of Vibe Coding50 min28 insights2026-07-15
▶ Watch on YouTube
Former Intel CEO Pat Gelsinger dissects how Intel's shift from technical to finance-driven leadership caused it to miss Apple Silicon, Nvidia's CUDA rise, and TSMC's foundry model, then warns Taiwan's thin energy reserves make it a single point of failure for global chip supply; Lovable CEO Anton Osika details the vibe-coding startup's hypergrowth (roughly $500M revenue, a million new projects weekly) and its strategy of routing across multiple AI models while expanding from app-building into hosting and an 'AI co-founder' product.
Intel's Leadership Failure: Technologists vs Bean Counters · 3Apple Silicon and Nvidia's CUDA Ascent · 4TSMC's Foundry Dominance and Taiwan Risk · 5AI Buildout: Bubble Risk and the Energy Ceiling · 4Quantum Computing's Coming Decade · 2Lovable's Vibe-Coding Hypergrowth · 4Lovable's Multi-Model Strategy and Enterprise Disruption · 6
Intel's Leadership Failure: Technologists vs Bean Counters 3
Pat Gelsinger attributes Intel's decline to a shift from technical, founder-like leadership toward finance-driven management, noting he was the company's first technical CEO in roughly 15 years when he became chief executive, and argues that billion-dollar technology bets can't be made through a spreadsheet.
causal🗣 Pat Gelsinger (guest, former Intel CEO)conviction: highIntelPat Gelsinger
So what: Leadership composition (technical vs. financial background) is a signal for execution risk at capital-intensive hardware companies; worth screening other semiconductor/hardware incumbents for the same pattern.
“you don't do that through a spreadsheet. That's a lousy investment, right?” ▶ 3:15
Intel distributed about $100 billion to shareholders via dividends and stock buybacks in the five to six years before Gelsinger returned as CEO, capital he argues should instead have funded new fabs and EUV lithography equipment.
data_point🗣 Pat Gelsingerconviction: highIntel
So what: Illustrates the opportunity cost of prioritizing capital returns over capex in a capital-intensive industry; a lens for evaluating other buyback-heavy hardware/tech companies against their reinvestment needs.
“Intel gave $100 billion to shareholders” ▶ 3:31
Intel had not built a new factory in a decade by the time Gelsinger returned and had failed to acquire EUV lithography machines needed for leading-edge manufacturing, a gap he says only a technologist-led company would have avoided.
data_point🗣 Pat Gelsingerconviction: highIntel
So what: Underinvestment in leading-edge capex during the finance-led era is a direct, traceable cause of Intel's subsequent loss of process leadership to TSMC.
“it hadn't built a new factory in a decade when I got there” ▶ 3:53
Apple Silicon and Nvidia's CUDA Ascent 4
Steve Jobs was quietly building Apple's internal chip competency for years before publicly ending the Intel partnership; during an earlier conversation about porting macOS to x86, Jobs told Gelsinger he had already been working on it for four prior releases, showing Apple was hedging against Intel dependency long in advance.
anecdote🗣 Pat Gelsingerconviction: highAppleSteve JobsIntel
So what: Vertically-integrated tech leaders often build strategic optionality (in-house silicon) quietly for years before executing a supplier switch — worth watching for similar quiet hedges among today's major Nvidia/cloud customers.
“I've been working on that for the last four releases.” ▶ 7:00
Nvidia's dominance grew from continuously improving its CUDA software stack and SIMT programming model on top of graphics hardware that Intel dismissed early on as a niche 'gamer' product, until Japanese HPC researchers realized GPUs could be repurposed for high-performance computing workloads.
causal🗣 Pat Gelsingerconviction: highNvidiaIntel
So what: A software moat compounding over many generations, not a single breakthrough, is what turned graphics chips into the AI-compute standard — a repeatable pattern to look for in other emerging hardware categories.
“these aren't just graphics cards anymore. You know, these are generalpurpose computing devices that can start applying to these other uh workloads.” ▶ 9:36
Intel had its own GPU-like project, Larrabee, built on x86 to compete in general-purpose parallel computing, but it was cancelled a week after Gelsinger's first departure from Intel — a decision he suggests could have changed the trajectory of the GPU/AI compute race had it continued.
contrarian🗣 Pat Gelsingerconviction: mediumIntelNvidia
So what: A near-miss case study in how internal politics and short-term capital discipline can foreclose an incumbent's shot at an emerging compute paradigm — a cautionary comp for judging which of today's incumbents might be one cancelled project away from missing the next platform shift.
“the project was killed a week after I left” ▶ 10:16
Gelsinger notes Nvidia did not foresee that its GPUs would become the preferred hardware for Bitcoin mining or, later, AI training — those use cases emerged organically from the hacker and developer community finding new applications for already-improving hardware.
contrarian🗣 Pat Gelsingerconviction: mediumNvidiaBitcoin
So what: Platform winners often benefit from emergent, unplanned demand rather than top-down foresight — a reason to stay open to unanticipated future uses (or stranding risk) for today's AI infrastructure buildout.
“Nvidia did not predict that this Bitcoin project would take over and that this would be the best way to do those computations” ▶ 10:43
TSMC's Foundry Dominance and Taiwan Risk 5
TSMC won the semiconductor industry by committing early to a pure-foundry model — manufacturing any company's chip design regardless of origin — while Intel's integrated design-and-manufacture (IDM) approach kept its fabs closed to outside customers, a gap Intel dismissed as immaterial at the time.
causal🗣 Pat Gelsingerconviction: highTSMCIntel
So what: A textbook case of a segment an incumbent treats as trivial becoming the dominant business model — a prompt to check which 'trivial' adjacent markets today's leaders are currently ignoring.
“I don't care whose chip it is. I don't care what you're designing, I'll be your manufacturing partner.” ▶ 12:45
By the time Gelsinger returned to lead Intel, TSMC was producing roughly five times the wafer volume of Intel, and that gap has since widened to roughly seven times.
data_point🗣 Pat Gelsingerconviction: highTSMCIntel
So what: Quantifies how far behind Intel Foundry remains in manufacturing scale, a key input for gauging how much re-shored capacity would actually be needed to rebalance the industry.
“TSMC was producing 5x the wafers of Intel” ▶ 13:13
The U.S. share of global leading-edge chip manufacturing has risen from about 12% to about 18% since the CHIPS Act era began, which Gelsinger calls real progress but still far short of a resilient domestic supply chain.
data_point🗣 Pat Gelsingerconviction: highCHIPS ActIntelTSMC
So what: A concrete benchmark for tracking U.S. semiconductor re-shoring progress against CHIPS Act goals over time.
“the US was building about 12% of leading edge. Today, that number is more like 18%.” ▶ 15:22
Gelsinger warns Taiwan has less than three weeks of energy reserves, so a Chinese blockade could brown out the island without a shot being fired, and once a fab loses power it takes about 90 days to restart — an economic shock he says would exceed the Great Depression.
thesis🗣 Pat Gelsingerconviction: highTaiwanTSMCChina
So what: Investors in semiconductor supply chains should treat Taiwan's energy dependency, not just direct military invasion, as a standalone tail-risk trigger for a prolonged fab outage.
“less than 3 weeks of energy reserves” ▶ 15:58
China has blockaded the Taiwan Strait seven times over the past four years, according to Gelsinger, which he frames as an established pattern of coercive signaling rather than a hypothetical risk.
data_point🗣 Pat Gelsingerconviction: highChinaTaiwan
So what: A concrete frequency metric for calibrating geopolitical risk premia on Taiwan-exposed semiconductor equities.
“China has blockaded the Taiwan Straits seven times over the last four years” ▶ 16:56
AI Buildout: Bubble Risk and the Energy Ceiling 4
Gelsinger says current AI company valuations have become 'quite extraordinary,' and that if these firms keep spending far more than they earn, investor sentiment could sour quickly, similar to prior overbuilding cycles.
contrarian🗣 Pat Gelsingerconviction: mediumAI
So what: A caution flag on current AI infrastructure valuations from a veteran chip operator turned investor, worth weighing against more bullish buildout narratives.
“the valuations of these companies has gotten quite extraordinary” ▶ 18:04
Gelsinger argues global energy capacity — growing about 4-5% annually versus roughly 1% U.S. grid growth over the prior decade — acts as a natural ceiling on the AI buildout, since GPUs and data centers cannot be deployed without sufficient power.
framework🗣 Pat Gelsingerconviction: highAI
So what: Energy capacity growth is a leading indicator/governor for AI capex pacing; tracking grid buildout data alongside GPU shipment data can help gauge how far the current cycle can run before hitting a physical constraint.
“nobody's going to build and buy GPUs and build data centers if they don't have energy” ▶ 18:57
Gelsinger states a core objective (through his investing activity) is to cut AI's cost per token by five orders of magnitude, roughly 10,000x, which he expects to trigger Jevons-paradox-style demand growth as AI becomes radically cheaper to run.
prediction🗣 Pat Gelsingerconviction: highAI
So what: A quantified target for AI cost-efficiency gains that can be used to model long-run token pricing and margin trajectories across AI infrastructure and model providers.
“I have to make AI 10,000x better” ▶ 19:55
Gelsinger predicts the AI buildout will span multiple decades rather than a couple of years, punctuated by periodic corrections he likens to a past 'SaaS apocalypse,' which he views as healthy for keeping valuation multiples in check.
prediction🗣 Pat Gelsingerconviction: highAI
So what: Sets expectations for cyclical drawdowns inside a structurally bullish long-term AI capex thesis, useful for position-sizing through volatility rather than treating every correction as thesis-breaking.
