“I'm predicting two decades of goodness and there's going to be lots of disruptions along the way” ▶ 22:03
Quantum Computing's Coming Decade 2
Gelsinger predicts quantum computing will deliver meaningful, industry-relevant results within this decade (by 2030), with harder problems such as breaking modern encryption ('Q-day') arriving around 2032-2033.
prediction🗣 Pat Gelsingerconviction: highquantum computing
So what: Gives a rough commercial-inflection timeline for quantum-exposed portfolio companies, and a rough deadline for encryption-dependent security infrastructure to prepare for Q-day.
“we will see quantum supremacy uh results across multiple industries” ▶ 23:30
Gelsinger, who discloses SI Quantum as one of his portfolio companies, notes quantum computing now has four to six viable hardware modalities (trapped ions, photonic, spin-based, etc.) all showing credible error-correction progress, meaning the remaining challenge is engineering scale rather than picking the winning modality.
thesis🗣 Pat Gelsingerconviction: mediumSI Quantumquantum computing
So what: Suggests diversified exposure across quantum hardware modalities may be more prudent than betting on a single architecture, since error correction has been de-risked across multiple approaches.
“you now have like four, five, six modalities of quantum that are demonstrating pretty good results” ▶ 23:53
Lovable's Vibe-Coding Hypergrowth 4
Lovable users are creating roughly one million new software projects per week on the platform, with more than 700 million monthly visits to applications built on it and over 50 million apps created to date, after 20 months in market.
data_point🗣 Anton Osika (Lovable CEO)conviction: highLovable
So what: These usage metrics are a leading indicator of revenue trajectory and of vibe-coding's mainstream adoption curve, useful for comping against other AI app-builder platforms.
“We're seeing a million new projects built every single week on the” ▶ 25:43
Lovable's annualized revenue reached $500 million as of May 2026, having grown roughly $100 million every six months, a pace the host jokes has made the company 'die' and get 'reborn' every six months as skeptics keep declaring it obsolete against each new foundation model release.
data_point🗣 Anton Osikaconviction: highLovable
So what: A hard revenue growth benchmark for one of the fastest-scaling AI-native software companies, useful for valuing the vibe-coding category against incumbents like GitHub Copilot or Cursor.
“We we we reached 500 in May.”
About 60% of Lovable's customers on its lowest subscription tier exceed their usage caps and pay overage fees, which Osika frames as evidence customers get outsized value relative to price on a roughly $25-50/month product that can replace software that used to cost hundreds of thousands of dollars to build.
data_point🗣 Anton Osikaconviction: mediumLovable
So what: A high overage attach rate signals strong willingness-to-pay and pricing-power headroom, a bullish signal for future ARPU expansion via usage-based pricing.
“it's something like 60% of our customers” ▶ 44:10
About 20% of Lovable's users are technical (engineers or similar) while roughly 80% are non-technical, and Osika says enterprise adoption is currently the platform's fastest-growing segment, positioning Lovable as a tool for the broad non-developer population rather than a developer-only product.
data_point🗣 Anton Osikaconviction: highLovable
So what: Confirms Lovable's addressable market extends well beyond developer tooling into general business software creation, relevant for market-sizing versus traditional low-code/no-code vendors.
“four out of five are are nontechnical” ▶ 28:37
Lovable's Multi-Model Strategy and Enterprise Disruption 6
Lovable dynamically routes each user prompt to whichever AI model performs best for that task — spanning multiple commercial frontier labs plus its own open-weight models — rather than committing exclusively to a single vendor or to open source.
framework🗣 Anton Osikaconviction: highLovableAnthropic
So what: Multi-model routing reduces single-vendor dependency risk and lets Lovable arbitrage cost and quality across providers, a structural advantage over app builders locked to one foundation model.
“if you ask Lovable now, it's actually routed to the model that's most suitable to whatever you want to do” ▶ 40:35
Lovable has a research team based in Stockholm running reinforcement learning and post-training focused on the specific mistakes frontier models make on Lovable's workloads (e.g., tool-calling and integration errors), building proprietary training data from its large token volume, partly to build strategic AI capability in Europe.
data_point🗣 Anton Osikaconviction: mediumLovable
So what: Proprietary post-training on real usage data is a moat-building mechanism that could let Lovable's in-house models outperform generic frontier models on its specific task distribution over time.
“we have a really really strong research team up in Stockholm who is working on what's called post training” ▶ 41:34
Osika confirms Lovable uses Anthropic's newer model, referred to in the conversation as 'Fable,' as one of its routed options, noting it produces very sophisticated first-attempt outputs (e.g., 3D games) but that human strategic planning about what to build remains the bottleneck rather than raw model capability.
framework🗣 Anton Osikaconviction: mediumAnthropicLovable
So what: Signals that further AI coding model gains are shifting the bottleneck from code generation to human product/strategy judgment, a consideration for how AI-native dev tools should evolve their UX.
“it can in the first attempt create very sophisticated things that look really good” ▶ 47:35
A Lovable enterprise customer at a company referred to as Nursa used the platform to build a nurse-certification and scheduling product, then took the same approach internally and replaced more than 10 bespoke internal tools, saving over $1 million per year.
anecdote🗣 Anton Osikaconviction: mediumLovable
So what: Concrete proof that vibe-coding tools are starting to displace internal enterprise software spend, not just accelerate greenfield app creation, relevant for assessing downside risk to internal-tools and no-code incumbents.
“they've now replaced more than 10 tools that they had bespoke applications” ▶ 38:29
Lovable is expanding beyond app-building into a hosting product line (functioning as an AWS competitor) that Osika says is growing faster than the core build product, and is piloting an 'AI co-founder' feature that proactively surfaces business strategy recommendations from data across a customer's connected tools.
thesis🗣 Anton Osikaconviction: mediumLovable
So what: Signals Lovable's expansion into adjacent higher-margin categories (hosting, business advisory), broadening its addressable market and stickiness beyond one-time app generation.
“we're working with some of our customers in pre-release to give them access to a co-founder that works for you even when you're sleeping” ▶ 35:56
Osika describes a 'co-opetition' practice borrowed from his time at CERN, where multiple teams independently build competing versions of the same internal tool on Lovable and later cross-pollinate the best elements, arguing this is newly viable because AI has removed engineering effort as the bottleneck to experimentation.
framework🗣 Anton Osikaconviction: mediumLovable
So what: A new organizational pattern enabled by cheap AI-generated software — parallel redundant builds as deliberate experimentation rather than wasted duplication — worth watching as a broader signal of how AI changes engineering resource allocation.
“they have two actually quite isolated teams working on the same um particle accelerator but different places on it and then they don't share the results until they publish” ▶ 46:07

Anthony Pompliano 3 episode(s) · 107 insights

Jake Paul's Business Partner on Bitcoin, AI & the Next Trillion-Dollar Trade89 min35 insights2026-07-13
▶ Watch on YouTube
Jeff Woo, managing partner at Antifund (Jake Paul and Logan Paul's investment firm) and co-founder of Ketone IQ, lays out a 'choke-point' investing framework spanning AI compute/memory, defense tech, energy commodities like tungsten, and the human-data economy, revealing that Sam Altman told him at a dinner that OpenAI bought roughly 40% of the world's memory supply after a trip to Korea. He also discloses he is now trimming his long-held Bitcoin position in favor of AI exposure and Hyperliquid.
Choke-Point Investing Framework · 4AI Value Chain: Foundation Labs vs. Vertical Specialists · 4The Human & Synthetic Data Economy · 5The Memory & Compute Trade · 5Choke-Point Commodities, Power, and Capex · 7Defense Tech and Neo-Primes · 3Bitcoin and Crypto Portfolio Positioning · 3Culture, Risk, and Power Dynamics · 4
Choke-Point Investing Framework 4
Jeff Woo traces the downstream capital chain of an AGI investment thesis: capital first bids up OpenAI/Anthropic, then flows down to Nvidia and semiconductor chips, then further downstream into power infrastructure and rare-earth minerals.
framework🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: highOpenAIAnthropicNvidiasemiconductorspower infrastructurerare earth minerals
So what: Investors can systematically follow an AGI-belief chain downstream from model labs to chips to power to raw materials to find underpriced links.
“obviously people are bidding up open anthropic and then you go down to Nvidia and semiconductor chips and I think it's increasingly valuable and clear that you go down to power you go go down to rare earth minerals”
Jeff Woo uses a 'three strikes and I'm in' heuristic: when he hears the same investment idea independently from three unconnected people, he treats that convergence itself as a strong buy signal rather than requiring further diligence.
framework🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: highAntifund
So what: Retail or smaller investors can replicate this by tracking when a niche thesis starts surfacing from multiple independent, credible sources.
≈ “I do have a three strikes and I'm in. Uh if I hear the same thing from three people who don't know each other, I just learned like don't ignore it.” paraphrase
Woo builds his investment universe bottoms-up via 'mosaic theory' — talking directly to founders working on robotics, mining, and fusion research to identify what is underpriced, rather than relying on public information or sell-side research.
framework🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: mediumroboticsfusion researchmining
So what: Signals that the most differentiated alpha in AI/hard-tech investing currently comes from direct founder access rather than public data.
“talk to all the smart people in the world doing all the interesting things on robotics, mining stuff, doing fusion research, making robotics actually happen and just build up a bottoms up universe” ▶ 2:23
According to Woo, hedge fund managers who became crossover investors into private markets did so primarily to harvest information advantage, not direct private-market returns, because private-market developments often led public-market moves before quarterly disclosures caught up.
causal🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: mediumhedge fundsprivate marketspublic markets
So what: Public-market investors without private access are structurally behind information that later shows up in earnings; crossover/hybrid strategies are a way to close that gap.
AI Value Chain: Foundation Labs vs. Vertical Specialists 4
Woo expects foundation model labs like OpenAI and Anthropic to increasingly verticalize into application-layer businesses (e.g., legal tech, medical tech, finance, drug discovery spinouts) rather than remaining purely horizontal model providers, citing that they are already hiring PMs and domain experts like investment bankers to pursue specific verticals.
prediction🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: highOpenAIAnthropiclegal techmedical techfinance tech
So what: Startups building thin wrappers on foundation models in large verticals face direct competitive entry risk from the labs themselves.
“I think the foundation labs are going to verticalize and do application layer stuff.” ▶ 5:36
Woo estimates the stable long-term equilibrium is roughly a 50/50 split of AI applications between foundation labs and the startup ecosystem, a moderation from an earlier fear that big labs would capture around 80% of the most useful applications and leave startups only 20%.
prediction🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: mediumOpenAIAnthropicstartups
So what: Investors betting on vertical AI startups should size positions assuming labs will contest roughly half of the application layer, not assume startups get a clear field.
“I think there is a universe where the big boys control like 80% of the most useful applications and they'll let some startups play on the platform for like 20% of the applications.” ▶ 6:58
Woo argues vertical AI startups' real defensibility comes from an 'unfair distribution advantage' — proprietary personal expertise, data, and an existing audience (e.g., a well-known financial personality's following baked into a product like Sylvia) — not from model quality, since generic frontier models are becoming commoditized.
framework🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: highSylviaOpenAIAnthropic
So what: When evaluating vertical AI startups, investors should weight distribution/data moats over raw model capability, since the latter is quickly replicated by foundation labs.
≈ “you're a financial expert. You're like a guru in this space. You have a big following. So I think you have a lot of credibility to say that my personal insights, the data, the network that I have is being baked into Sylvia.” paraphrase
Both startups and foundation labs are racing to accumulate proprietary data fast enough to reach flywheel 'escape velocity' before the other side, a race intensified by big labs now spending billions of dollars directly on data acquisition.
causal🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: mediumOpenAIAnthropicGemini
So what: Data-moat speed, not just model quality, is becoming a key competitive KPI to track for AI startups versus incumbents.
“you got a race like basically open anthropic or Gemini of like can you acrue so much proprietary data that you get the flywheel to escape velocity before the big boys do it” ▶ 9:56
The Human & Synthetic Data Economy 5
Woo says he has heard that the entire public internet is roughly 30 trillion tokens, while leading labs like OpenAI and Anthropic reportedly used on the order of 200 trillion tokens of data for pretraining, implying most of their training data now comes from non-public, synthetic, or human-generated sources rather than the open web.
data_point🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: mediumOpenAIAnthropicpretraining data
So what: The scarcity of public web data relative to what labs are actually consuming supports a structural bull case for human-data and synthetic-data suppliers as a picks-and-shovels play.
Frontier AI labs are reportedly paying $2,000 to $10,000 per task for human-generated agent traces and labeled data, with individual contractors sometimes paid around $1,000 an hour, pulling professionals like investment bankers and financial advisors out of their jobs to generate training data at roughly double their prior salary.
data_point🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: highhuman data labelingAI labs
So what: Human-data labor markets tied to AI training are becoming a real wage-competitive labor pool, a leading indicator of lab spend intensity worth tracking.
“people are they're just spending like $2,000 to $10,000 a task” ▶ 11:25
Woo notes that companies helping other companies shut down (he cites a portfolio company called Sunset) have discovered an emerging market for selling the intangible operational data of businesses that are winding down, treating a defunct company's historical operating data as a sellable AI-training asset.
anecdote🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: mediumSunsetMicro One
So what: Company-shutdown and data-brokerage services are an overlooked adjacent beneficiary of the AI training-data boom.
Woo argues the next frontier of training-data value is 'intent data' — not just an artifact like a lawyer's redlined document, but the human's stated reasoning for the edit — because models need to be trained on the 'why,' which cannot be scraped and must be purpose-generated.
framework🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: highhuman data companieslegal tech
So what: Businesses that can capture reasoning/intent alongside raw work-product have a data moat that generic document scraping cannot replicate.
“that intent data almost has to be specifically created for model training” ▶ 13:10
Because well-funded model labs can simply 'throw money' at data problems, resource-constrained AI startups are forced into synthetic-data innovation earlier out of necessity, turning a funding constraint into a competitive advantage.
framework🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: mediumSylviasynthetic data
So what: Capital-constrained AI startups may develop more efficient synthetic-data pipelines than well-capitalized labs, a potential technical moat worth diligencing.
“the constraint should breed innovation if you do it right” ▶ 12:14
The Memory & Compute Trade 5
Woo says Sam Altman told him at a private dinner that OpenAI had bought roughly 40% of the world's supply of memory following a trip to Korea, a data point Woo received before it became public knowledge and which confirmed his early conviction in the 'memory trade.'
anecdote🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: highSam AltmanOpenAImemoryKorea
So what: Direct founder/executive access surfaced the memory-supply-crunch thesis roughly a year before it became consensus and priced into Micron/SK Hynix.
“I had a dinner with Sam Alman at OpenAI and he just mentioned that like he came back from Korea. He like bought like 40% of the world's supply of memory.” ▶ 0:00
Woo believes the memory trade (Micron, SK Hynix) is now fairly well understood and priced in the near term, but the open question is whether AI-driven memory demand is structural or cyclical; he leans toward Micron being undervalued since it trades cheaper on a multiple basis now than earlier in the year despite the stock's run.
thesis🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: mediumMicronSK HynixDRAM
So what: A cheaper forward multiple despite a large stock price run suggests the memory trade may not be as crowded as headline returns imply.
“Micron is cheaper now than it was at the beginning of the year if I if I remember correctly.” ▶ 28:32
Woo speculates that if humanoid robot deployment scales toward billions of units, memory demand could grow up to 100x current levels, potentially making Micron and SK Hynix into multi-trillion-dollar companies, though he is uncertain whether the market is early or late to this structural shift.
prediction🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: exploratoryMicronSK Hynixhumanoid robotsmemory
So what: A humanoid-driven memory demand supercycle is a multi-year thesis worth monitoring as robot production volumes scale.
“if we really see like more humanoids than humans, I think you still have 100x more memory to go, right?” ▶ 28:00
Woo observes a shift in AI inference infrastructure from single-agent, GPU-heavy workloads (roughly an 8:1 GPU-to-CPU ratio) toward multi-agent orchestration where each agent runs on its own container/cluster, requiring proportionally more CPUs — a dynamic he says is already showing up in Intel and AMD trading, with portfolio company Cognition's cloud coding agents as an example of the orchestration pattern.
causal🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: exploratoryIntelAMDCognitionGPUCPU
So what: A shift toward multi-agent orchestration could rebalance AI infrastructure spend from pure GPU demand toward CPU-heavy compute, a potential tailwind for Intel/AMD relative to Nvidia.
“maybe it's instead of like 8:1 GPU to CPU, maybe it gets one to one CPU to GPU.” ▶ 27:46
Woo expects new entrants like Etched (founded by Gavin and Rob, targeting Nvidia inference dominance) to emerge and capture a share of the outsized memory/compute profit pool, predicting one to three credible new technical teams will challenge the choke point alongside opportunistic 'grift' attempts by others without real technology.
prediction🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: mediumEtchedNvidiamemory chips
So what: The scale of profits sitting with Micron/SK Hynix/Nvidia is large enough to attract credible new competitors, a dynamic worth tracking for both upside challengers and public-market hype/grift plays.
“we are credibly think we can go, you know, relieve this choke point. And if we do, we can make billions and billions of dollars.” ▶ 29:55
Choke-Point Commodities, Power, and Capex 7
Woo argues that as AI commoditizes software development (via coding agents), competitive advantage is shifting from Silicon-Valley-style capex-light software models toward capex-heavy 'atoms' businesses like data centers, chips, and power, reversing decades of preference for asset-light business models.
contrarian🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: mediumdata centerspowercapex
So what: Investors historically biased toward asset-light software should reconsider capital-intensive infrastructure plays as a new source of durable moat in an AI-commoditized software world.
“everyone's buying data centers, everyone's buying ships, everyone's buying power” ▶ 31:12
Woo frames electricity availability and cost ('watts') as the ultimate AI bottleneck once chips, models, and applications are assumed to be good, which he says explains rising interest in unconventional power sources like space-based clusters, ocean-based clusters, and nuclear clusters to feed AI data centers.
thesis🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: highpowernucleardata centers
So what: Power generation and grid capacity are becoming the binding constraint on AI scaling, making energy infrastructure a higher-leverage investment than another layer of chips or models.
“everything is just like the denomination of watts now, right?” ▶ 31:24
Tungsten is an underappreciated choke-point commodity: it is used for thermal management in high-end semiconductor/GPU packaging (to prevent melting under high compute density) and in fusion reactor components, and China holds the highest-purity tungsten concentrate and has begun restricting exports.
data_point🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: hightungstenChinaGPUsfusion
So what: China's tungsten export restrictions are a supply-chain risk for both current AI chip production and future fusion energy buildout, a commodity worth watching for price spikes.
“China has like the highest purity concentrate of tungsten. They're starting to like shut down exports of that because they know that's a choke point.” ▶ 15:06
Woo is a direct investor in fusion startup Helion and, based on visiting its reactor prototype, believes the company is close to breakthroughs that could soon be published, reinforcing his view that fusion energy is now an investable near-term reality rather than a purely speculative long-horizon bet.
anecdote🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: mediumHelionfusion energy
So what: Direct diligence access to fusion prototypes suggests fusion timelines may be compressing faster than public consensus expects, relevant for long-duration energy and power-hungry AI infrastructure plays.
≈ “I think they're close to some breakthroughs and hopefully will be published and all of that stuff.” paraphrase
Woo highlights that top investors like Peter Thiel/Founders Fund succeed by holding multiple interlocking bets on the same underlying choke point (data center capacity spanning ocean-based compute and SpaceX's orbital data ambitions), capturing information asymmetry and first-look access across an entire theme rather than picking a single company.
framework🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: highPeter ThielFounders FundSpaceXdata centers
So what: Rather than picking a single winner in a sector, investors can replicate top-fund strategy by building a portfolio of complementary bets across one choke-point theme.
“whoever solves the data center problem, Peter and founders fund is going to make a lot of money.” ▶ 33:35
Woo cites portfolio company Efronence (purpose-built robot cameras) as a second-order choke point: current camera suppliers spun out of Intel's RealSense can only produce a few hundred units a month, forcing most robotics and data-collection teams to rely on repurposed consumer hardware like Meta glasses, GoPros, and iPhones.
anecdote🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: highEfronenceIntel RealSenseMeta glassesrobotics
So what: Purpose-built sensor/camera supply for robotics data collection is a manufacturing bottleneck distinct from the chip/memory choke points, and a candidate picks-and-shovels investment in the humanoid robotics buildout.
“the leading camera providers are like real sensors spun out of Intel they can only make like a few hundred cameras a month. So there's like a huge choke point there.” ▶ 22:36
A robotics founder in Woo's network was, roughly 6-12 months before this conversation, personally sourcing recycled DRAM chips scavenged from old computer boards from Chinese suppliers due to memory scarcity — an early, ground-level signal of the memory shortage that later became a consensus 'memory trade.'
anecdote🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: highDRAMChinamemory
So what: Founder-level operational pain points (needing to buy scavenged components) can precede consensus macro trades by 6-12 months, making founder conversations a leading indicator.
“there's like Chinese suppliers who like rip old DRAM chips off of like old computer boards and like resell them.” ▶ 23:26
Defense Tech and Neo-Primes 3
Woo's defense-investing thesis (Antifund holds stakes in Anduril and other defense startups) rests on the view that legacy defense primes are culturally slow — citing multi-month planning cycles and quarterly board-meeting cadences at legacy contractors — causing top engineering talent to migrate to venture-backed 'neo-primes,' similar to how SpaceX pulled the best talent out of legacy aerospace.
thesis🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: highAndurilSpaceXAntifunddefense primes
So what: Talent migration from legacy defense primes to newer venture-backed contractors is a leading indicator of where defense-tech market share will shift over the next decade.
“they basically just become these magnets of anyone who's worth anything in the industry and wants to go work for them anyways.” ▶ 37:57
Woo predicts a market will emerge for weaponized humanoid robots for military use, even though major public humanoid makers (Tesla, Figure, 1X, Agility Robotics, and Chinese makers like Unitree) have publicly committed not to arm their robots, because he says at least one unnamed humanoid company is deliberately pursuing weaponization as a core use case.
contrarian🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: highTeslaFigure1XAgility Roboticshumanoid robots
So what: A humanoid company willing to build weaponized robots for the military could capture an entire underserved defense-tech niche that publicly-committed competitors are avoiding.
“I know of one humanoid company I'm not going to say their name who is taking the exact opposite approach” ▶ 43:01
Woo argues autonomous targeting of human targets by drones is likely already occurring in active conflicts (referencing Ukraine/Russia), framing drone-on-drone and drone-on-human autonomous warfare as an inevitable near-term reality rather than a speculative future risk.
contrarian🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: highdronesUkraineRussiaautonomous weapons
So what: Autonomous-weapons capability is moving faster than public policy debate suggests, relevant to defense-tech investment timing and regulatory risk.
“there's going to be autonomous targeting of human targets by robots.” ▶ 44:34
Bitcoin and Crypto Portfolio Positioning 3
Despite holding Bitcoin since 2013, Woo says he is now considering trimming his position because he cannot clearly articulate what tail-risk scenario Bitcoin actually hedges — arguing that if the US dollar or government were to fail, he would rather hold bullets, water, and land than Bitcoin.
contrarian🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: mediumBitcoin
So what: A long-time OG Bitcoin holder publicly questioning Bitcoin's hedging logic and trimming exposure is a notable sentiment data point among crypto-native investors.
≈ “if all the US stuff is bad then I probably want bullets, water, land, like just actual like survivable stuff.” paraphrase
Woo has rotated a portion of his crypto allocation from Bitcoin toward Hyperliquid, citing its clearer cash-flow-generating business model (fee capture from trading/leverage activity plus token buybacks) versus Bitcoin's harder-to-underwrite 'digital gold' store-of-value narrative.
thesis🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: mediumHyperliquidBitcoin
So what: Investors comparing crypto assets on cash-flow fundamentals rather than narrative may find protocols like Hyperliquid more analytically tractable than Bitcoin.
“I've rotated a lot towards hyperlquid cuz I understand the use case.” ▶ 1:24:18
Woo states that market-wide risk capital has rotated out of Bitcoin into AI over the past year because AI assets currently offer more yield, productivity narrative, and better realized returns, explicitly noting that buying Bitcoin a year ago produced very different (worse) outcomes than buying AI exposure.
data_point🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: highBitcoinAI
So what: Capital rotation from Bitcoin to AI narratives has been a measurable performance driver over the trailing year and may continue until AI returns cool off.
“if you bought Bitcoin a year ago versus bought AI, like there are very different outcomes.” ▶ 1:23:13
Culture, Risk, and Power Dynamics 4
Woo argues GLP-1 drugs are becoming a significant, underappreciated force reducing gambling, drinking, and partying behavior at a societal level, citing that multiple hedge funds have banned traders from taking them because the drugs reduce risk-taking and aggression, though he doesn't yet see the effect as large enough to be a standalone tradeable thesis.
contrarian🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: highGLP-1hedge funds
So what: Widespread GLP-1 adoption could be a slow-moving macro tailwind against consumer discretionary categories tied to vice spending (alcohol, gambling, fast food) worth monitoring as a long-term thesis.
“I think everyone will be micro doing GLP-1s in the future.” ▶ 1:02:27
Woo predicts compulsive gambling driven by betting and prediction-market apps will become a 'huge national epidemic,' framing it as a symptom of broader economic hopelessness among people who feel they cannot escape low-wage work, channeling that desperation into meme-stock and prediction-market speculation.
prediction🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: highgambling appsprediction marketsmeme stocks
So what: Rising gambling-app and prediction-market penetration is a social/regulatory risk that could eventually draw policy intervention, similar to past crackdowns on other addictive consumer products.
“I am very convinced that the gambling in particular is going to be this like huge national epidemic. It may already be there, but like it it's going to get worse.” ▶ 1:06:52
Woo argues respect, not money, is the ultimate currency of power — observing that wealthy people without social standing or respect get little deference in rooms that matter, and that the ideal position is being highly respected within an influential inner circle while remaining relatively unknown to the broader public.
framework🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: high
So what: A useful heuristic for evaluating founders and operators: track who commands respect among insiders, which is often a better signal of durable influence than public fame or net worth alone.
“if you don't have respect but you have money, the money is no good in the rooms that you want to be in.” ▶ 1:15:41
Woo describes observing Donald Trump at the White House deliberately pause and stand still upon entering a room to visibly command attention before proceeding, framing it as a masterclass in interpersonal power dynamics independent of his politics.
anecdote🗣 Jeff Woo, guest, managing partner at Antifundconviction: mediumDonald TrumpWhite House
So what: Illustrates how Woo evaluates high-status individuals on tactical mastery of influence and attention, a transferable skill he suggests founders and operators can study regardless of political alignment.
≈ “He walks in a room and he stops. He just stands in the door and he just looks and everyone looks at him, right?” paraphrase
Has Bitcoin Hit The Bottom?52 min36 insights2026-07-11
▶ Watch on YouTube
Jordy Visser tells Anthony Pompliano the 'AI midcycle slowdown' is over and that the memory/semiconductor selloff was a leverage flush rather than a bubble bursting, while separately calling a Bitcoin bottom on a fresh RSI divergence and expecting crypto to become 'the memory trade of next year' as fundamental managers rotate in.
AI Model Commoditization & Price Wars · 5AI Midcycle Slowdown Ending, Consumer Agents Repricing Apple & Meta · 5Memory Trade: Leverage Flush, Not a Bubble · 5Bitcoin Bottom Thesis · 11Beyond AI & Crypto: Healthcare, Insurance, Regional Banks · 3Commodities: Gold, Silver, and Range-Bound Oil · 4Robotics & the AI Compute Supply-Demand Mismatch · 3
AI Model Commoditization & Price Wars 5
Meta and XAI's aggressive AI pricing/affordability announcements are an attempt to keep enterprise customers from migrating to open-source alternatives, not evidence that they can catch up to OpenAI and Anthropic on frontier model capability.
contrarian🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: highMetaXAIGrokOpenAIAnthropic
So what: Don't read Meta/XAI price cuts as sector-wide AI margin compression; OpenAI and Anthropic's enterprise business remains largely insulated from this specific price war.
“I don't think anyone anymore can catch up to anthropic and open AAI and what their plans are.” ▶ 1:21
Meta, Apple, Google, and Amazon are pursuing a different business model from OpenAI/Anthropic centered on consumer agents, advertising, and personal assistants, while Amazon, Google, and Microsoft additionally run cloud/compute-rental businesses (e.g., renting compute to SpaceX).
framework🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: highMetaAppleGoogleAmazonMicrosoftSpaceX
So what: Value these companies on advertising/commerce ROIC from consumer agents rather than on frontier-model quality, since they've effectively ceded the model race to OpenAI and Anthropic.
Enterprises are restructuring how they call AI models by routing simple queries to cheap models and complex ones to expensive frontier models, which should cut aggregate AI compute spend even as total usage keeps rising and could accelerate open-source adoption.
thesis🗣 Anthony Pompliano, hostconviction: mediumopen-source AI modelsSylvia
So what: A drop in cost-per-query doesn't signal falling AI capex demand; track token volume growth outpacing dollar spend as the real signal of a healthy market.
“how do you actually start to route queries to different models based on the complexity or the compute need of that query.” ▶ 3:52
Semianalysis founder Dylan Patel's firm increased AI spend from roughly $100,000 a year in December to an $11 million annualized run-rate, and argues any company cutting AI budget out of cost concern is signaling competitive decline.
data_point🗣 Jordy Visser, guest (relaying Dylan Patel)conviction: highDylan PatelSemianalysis
So what: Rising, not falling, AI opex is becoming a competitive necessity — a useful screen for identifying which companies are under- vs. over-investing in AI.
“if you're not spending more money every year, you're going out of business.” ▶ 9:14
Startups have a structural AI-adoption advantage over large incumbents like Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley because they aren't constrained by legacy payroll and can build AI-native workflows from scratch.
thesis🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: mediumGoldman SachsMorgan Stanley
So what: Expect AI-native smaller companies to out-execute large incumbents on margin expansion from AI adoption over the next few years.
AI Midcycle Slowdown Ending, Consumer Agents Repricing Apple & Meta 5
The market has become too negative on the AI buildout, and the 'AI midcycle slowdown' is now over, with the next leg driven by consumer agents repricing companies like Apple and Meta and by agentic commerce flowing into crypto.
prediction🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: highAppleMetaBitcoin
So what: Investors who cut AI infrastructure/hyperscaler exposure on slowdown fears may be underweighting a re-acceleration; watch for Apple and Meta re-rating as confirmation.
“I'm calling the the AI midcycle slowdown is over” ▶ 13:58
Apple's stock hit all-time highs on its Siri/personal-intelligence announcement before selling off, then fell over 10% off highs on memory-driven price-increase news, then fully recovered on no news — a pattern read as a bullish reaction to bad news.
data_point🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: highApple
So what: The 'sells off on bad news but doesn't follow through lower' pattern is a technical tell that positioning is already shifting bullish ahead of a fundamental re-rating.
“This is not a cheap stock. This tells me this is a bullish reaction to bad news.” ▶ 13:29
Visser expects Apple to be repriced higher on consumer-agent ROIC over the coming months and predicts the market narrative a year from now will be 'how did we miss Apple,' but he is not yet convinced on Meta because its technical picture is weaker.
prediction🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: mediumAppleMeta
So what: Differentiate within mega-cap AI laggards: Apple looks like the higher-conviction re-rating candidate versus Meta near-term.
A Goldman Sachs report estimates consumer AI agents will consume roughly 30 times the compute of coding agents, implying there is no near-term glut in AI compute capacity.
data_point🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: highGoldman Sachsconsumer agents
So what: This directly counters the 'AI compute overcapacity' bear case; expect a fresh demand leg for compute-adjacent infrastructure as consumer agents scale.
“consumer agents will consume about 30 times 30 times what coding agents will consume from a compute basis.” ▶ 15:42
Voice assistants like Siri and Alexa underperform because they require the user to actively open an app, whereas always-on, low-latency contexts like Grok inside a Tesla show much better conversational performance, suggesting hardware-embedded, always-listening devices are the stronger consumer-agent form factor.
framework🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: mediumGrokAppleAmazon
So what: Watch which companies pair AI assistants with always-on hardware (cars, wearables) as a leading indicator of consumer-agent product-market fit.
Memory Trade: Leverage Flush, Not a Bubble 5
Visser sold his Micron position near its highs around $1,250-1,300 specifically because leverage had become visibly obvious across Korean investors, macro strategies, and fundamental long-short funds, even while remaining structurally bullish on the memory trade.
thesis🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: highMicron
So what: Rising, visible leverage/positioning crowding is a sell signal independent of the underlying fundamental thesis; re-entry may require buying back above the exit price.
Visser expects Micron to reach $4,000-5,000 before the memory cycle plays out, but believes the climb from current levels near $900-950 will be harder than the prior run from $100 to $500.
prediction🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: mediumMicron
So what: The long-term bullish memory thesis is intact, but expect 30-40% drawdowns and choppier trading rather than a smooth compounding move.
Samsung is projected to earn roughly $217 billion this year alone, more than its combined earnings over the prior 40 years, which Visser cites as evidence the memory rally reflects real earnings power rather than a bubble.
data_point🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: highSamsung
So what: Judge whether AI-memory names are overextended using realized/forward earnings power rather than price action alone.
Samsung's roughly 20% drawdown over a 10-day period has happened only twice before in 25 years — during the COVID crash and the post-Lehman crisis — yet the stock remains near all-time highs, indicating the selloff was a leverage/positioning flush rather than a fundamental repricing.
data_point🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: highSamsung
So what: A rare-magnitude drawdown alongside record earnings and a price still near highs argues for buying the memory-sector dip rather than treating it as a top.
A leveraged 2x semiconductor ETF in Hong Kong fell 62%, and SK Hynix's oversubscribed ADR issuance was dominated by long-only managers who had missed the earlier rally — both signs that speculative leverage has been meaningfully washed out of the memory trade.
data_point🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: mediumSK Hynix
So what: Capitulation-style buying from previously underexposed long-only funds after a leverage flush often marks a more durable entry point than the earlier leveraged rally.
Bitcoin Bottom Thesis 11
Visser identifies the first bullish RSI divergence on Bitcoin's 4-hour chart since the cycle peak — price made a new low breaking below $60,000 while RSI printed higher than at the prior low — and is using it as a trigger to buy above $60k with a stop below the recent lows.
data_point🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: highBitcoin
So what: A concrete, replicable technical trigger for tactical Bitcoin entries; $60k is the pivot level to watch.
Visser believes Bitcoin is near the bottom of its range for the next year — a dip to $45,000-50,000 wouldn't surprise him — but expects it to trade above $100,000 within a year, making near-term entry price a secondary concern relative to the longer-term move.
prediction🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: highBitcoin
So what: On a 12-month horizon, exact entry timing is less important than positioning for a large asymmetric move higher.
“Do I think we'll be over 100 a year from now? Yeah.” ▶ 25:08
Capital rotating into AI-linked equities like Micron (a roughly 20-bagger) pulled money out of Bitcoin and crypto broadly, since investors chase whatever asset class is moving fastest rather than sticking to a single theme.
causal🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: highBitcoinMicron
So what: Bitcoin's underperformance reflected a capital-rotation effect, not crypto-specific weakness; a cooling in AI-stock momentum could rotate capital back into Bitcoin.
Bitcoin's decline coincided with the release of Anthropic's Opus 4.5 around October and a shift in Fed rate expectations, as roughly 150 basis points of priced-in rate cuts as of late September evaporated and were partly replaced with priced-in hike risk.
causal🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: mediumBitcoinAnthropicFederal Reserve
So what: Bitcoin's recent price action has been more macro-rate-driven than crypto-native; track Fed pricing as a leading indicator for Bitcoin direction.
Visser assigns a 35-40% probability the Fed raises rates at the July 29 FOMC meeting; if it does not hike, he expects Bitcoin to trade above $70,000 as markets price out further hikes ahead of the midterms.
prediction🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: mediumBitcoinFederal Reserve
So what: The July 29 FOMC decision is a concrete near-term catalyst and level to watch for a potential Bitcoin breakout above $70k.
“There's a possibility, a good possibility, it's like 35 to 40%, that they will. If they don't, then I think Bitcoin will be above 70,000 when that time comes.” ▶ 0:02
A Fed official Visser referred to in conversation as 'Walsh' has signaled AI could be a productivity boom that's briefly inflationary before turning deflationary, and Visser argues the Fed's last preference is a tightening cycle heading into the midterms given its political origin.
framework🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: mediumFederal Reserve
So what: A dovish reaction function around AI-driven disinflation reduces near-term hike risk, which is supportive for Bitcoin and other risk assets.
Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent's speech at the New York Economic Club explicitly named digital assets, tokenization, and stablecoins as central to the administration's strategy to keep the US in control of global financial rails.
thesis🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: highBitcoin
So what: Policy support for stablecoins and tokenization is a structural tailwind investors should weight into crypto allocations, separate from short-term price timing.
“digital assets, tokenization, stable coins are all part of the administration's focus on this new economic order.” ▶ 28:38
Bitcoin has traded as a high-beta hedge within the software-momentum trade, grouped alongside Salesforce, Adobe, and Workday as vehicles funds used to hedge semiconductor exposure, meaning its recent weakness reflected a momentum-unwind effect rather than crypto-specific rejection.
causal🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: mediumBitcoin
So what: As momentum/quant deleveraging completes, the same correlation mechanism that dragged Bitcoin down should support a rebound.
“All rallies that last for a year start with short covering.” ▶ 31:37
Visser's Bitcoin bull thesis plays out in stages: first short covering, then a durable momentum shift once price reclaims the 200-day moving average, currently around $76,000-77,000 and roughly 20% above spot.
framework🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: highBitcoin
So what: The 200-day moving average (~$76-77k) is the technical level to watch for confirmation the rally has shifted from short-covering to a durable uptrend.
Michael Saylor's two large Bitcoin sales drew a muted market reaction, with Bitcoin trading higher after his largest-ever sale than before it, which Visser reads as a bullish signal rather than a warning sign, and expects the Saylor narrative to become irrelevant within a year.
contrarian🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: mediumBitcoin
So what: Don't treat Saylor-related headline risk as a structural threat to Bitcoin; the market's failure to sell off on his largest sale is itself a bullish tell.
Fundamental and generalist fund managers have not yet rotated into Bitcoin or stablecoins because they remain focused on AI positioning, but Visser expects that once Fed clarity emerges, crypto will become 'the memory trade' of the next year.
prediction🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: highBitcoin
So what: Positioning ahead of generalist-fund rotation into crypto could capture the next multi-month momentum leg, similar to how the AI-memory trade played out this past year.
“crypto will be the memory for next year.” ▶ 52:14
Beyond AI & Crypto: Healthcare, Insurance, Regional Banks 3
Visser holds Eli Lilly as a high-conviction, essentially permanent position, citing its 'sovereign AI' compute buildout (he references roughly a thousand Nvidia Blackwell GPUs on its campus) applied directly to biotech drug discovery.
thesis🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: highEli LillyNvidia
So what: Owning in-house AI compute is becoming a competitive moat in biotech/pharma, not just at hyperscalers — a screen for picking AI-adjacent healthcare names.
“I don't think even if it were to go up, even if it were a threebagger from here, I wouldn't get out of it.” ▶ 38:26
Regional and midsize banks are likely acquisition targets for consolidation because they are hard to take private under banking regulation, and acquirers can make deals immediately accretive by infusing AI and cutting headcount post-merger.
prediction🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: mediumregional banks
So what: Watch for M&A premiums building in midsize regional bank names as AI-driven cost synergies make roll-ups newly attractive.
Insurance and healthcare companies that have already invested in AI/agentic workflows are positioned to show visible earnings benefits over the next year, alongside banks broadly, several of which have already made new all-time highs.
thesis🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: mediuminsurance sectorregional banks
So what: A sector-rotation opportunity into AI-adoption laggards-turned-early-adopters outside the mega-cap tech complex.
Commodities: Gold, Silver, and Range-Bound Oil 4
Visser expects gold and silver to rally alongside his Bitcoin thesis, driven by AI capital having pulled dollars out of these assets more than realized, rather than a resurgence of the classic dollar-debasement trade.
thesis🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: mediumgoldsilverBitcoin
So what: Precious metals could be a correlated proxy trade on the same 'AI capital suck reversing' thesis driving the Bitcoin call.
If the Fed hikes rates in July, Visser expects it to be a one-and-done 'credibility hike' that pushes long-end yields down and ultimately acts as a positive catalyst for risk assets including gold, silver, and Bitcoin.
framework🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: mediumFederal ReservegoldsilverBitcoin
So what: A near-term hike need not be bearish if read as a credibility move capping future tightening; watch the long end of the yield curve for confirmation.
Despite the on-again/off-again Iran conflict, crude oil hasn't sustained a move higher and trades as if there's a supply glut; Visser expects it to fall back toward $50 once the conflict resolves and sees it range-bound between $65-80 barring a Strait of Hormuz disruption.
contrarian🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: highoil
So what: Fade geopolitical oil-spike fears absent an actual Hormuz shipping disruption or a break above $80; the investment community has largely stopped pricing in Iran-war risk.
“unless we get above 80 and the Iran situation is worsening where there's no ships going through Hormuz, I just don't think it matters for the markets.” ▶ 44:50
Crack spreads — the price differential between gasoline and crude — remain wide even as crude is flat, indicating the bottleneck is refining/conversion capacity for gasoline rather than crude supply itself.
data_point🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: mediumoil
So what: Refining-capacity-exposed names may retain pricing power even if crude oil itself stays range-bound.
Robotics & the AI Compute Supply-Demand Mismatch 3
1X's robotic hand demo, built on a synthetic tendon-based mechanism rather than traditional robotic joints, demonstrated fine dexterity such as screwing in a lightbulb and picking a cherry without crushing it, which both speakers view as a major leap likely developed with AI assistance.
data_point🗣 Anthony Pompliano, hostconviction: high1X
So what: The historically hardest unsolved problem in robotics, dexterous hands, may be inflecting now, a leading indicator for humanoid-robot investment timelines.
The ROIC risk for AI hyperscalers is not comparable to the fracking bust because oil demand grows only linearly with GDP and is physically capped, whereas AI/intelligence usage grows exponentially as token prices fall, with the only real constraint being physical buildout like data centers and gas turbines.
contrarian🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: highoil
So what: Directly rebuts the 'AI capex is the next fracking-style bust' bear case circulating among hedge fund investors; Visser sees a persistent supply/demand mismatch, not overbuild.
“Oil is oil. Intelligence is everything.” ▶ 48:44
Visser believes AI-driven recursive self-improvement across model generations will eventually help solve fundamental science and physics problems, including energy generation via fission/fusion, to the point that he expects crude oil's economic value to eventually go to zero.
prediction🗣 Jordy Visser, guestconviction: lowoil
So what: A long-horizon framework for why energy-transition and fusion-adjacent bets could benefit from the same AI-compounding thesis driving equity markets today.
“eventually crude will go to zero. It will go to zero because we will solve energy at the fision fusion.” ▶ 49:28
Chief Economist: Inflation Has Peaked — Here's What Happens To Bitcoin Next46 min36 insights2026-07-15
▶ Watch on YouTube
Wolf Research chief economist Stephanie Roth tells Anthony Pompliano that the latest CPI print came in flat versus a much hotter consensus, evidence that tariff-, AI/chip-shortage-, and Iran-war-driven inflation are all starting to peak and roll over. Despite the video title's Bitcoin framing, Bitcoin barely features — the substantive content is on Fed chair Kevin Warsh's communication overhaul, a resilient-but-unhappy consumer, shifting housing/work preferences, and why this year's mega-cap IPO wave (SpaceX, and reference to OpenAI/Anthropic staying private) doesn't look like a 1990s-style bubble; the 'debasement trade' into gold/Bitcoin is described as having already faded.
Inflation Peaking: Tariff, AI-Chip, and Energy Drivers Rolling Over · 6The Warsh Fed: Communication Overhaul and Policy Path · 7Consumer Sentiment vs. Actual Spending: A Widening Gap · 5Housing and Work Preference Shifts · 6AI's Real-World Labor Market Impact · 3IPO Wave and Liquidity: Not the 1990s Bubble · 5Bitcoin/Gold Debasement Trade and Key Risks · 4
Inflation Peaking: Tariff, AI-Chip, and Energy Drivers Rolling Over 6
The latest CPI print came in flat month-over-month, far softer than consensus forecasts that had been calling for roughly 0.2% or higher, which took a potential July Fed hike nearly off the table.
data_point🗣 Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economistconviction: highCPIFederal Reserve
So what: A much-softer-than-expected CPI print removes near-term hike risk and supports a 'peak inflation' narrative that argues for lower-for-longer rate expectations.
“Today's CPI data came in much softer than anyone expected. It came in at flat.” ▶ 1:35
Tariff-related inflation added an estimated 50-70 basis points to core goods prices (core goods ran above 2% year-over-year versus their typical disinflationary trend), but this effect is now peaking now that the economy is past the 'Liberation Day' tariff shock.
data_point🗣 Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economistconviction: highCPItariffs
So what: Tariff inflation was real but contained (not a feared inflationary spiral into services), and is a fading tailwind to headline CPI going forward.
Short-term AI-related inflation is showing up specifically through a chip shortage, with computer software and accessories components in the CPI basket running about 15% year-over-year, but this is expected to fade out of the data by the middle of the year rather than persist.
data_point🗣 Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economistconviction: highCPIAI
So what: Investors should not extrapolate the current chip-shortage-driven inflation spike; it is a one-off base effect set to roll off mid-year, adding to the disinflation case.
Over the long run AI is expected to be disinflationary as productivity gains lower unit labor costs and let companies pass savings through to prices, even though near-term chip-shortage effects are temporarily inflationary.
thesis🗣 Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economistconviction: highAI
So what: Frames AI as a structural disinflationary force investors can weigh against near-term noisy inflation prints when thinking about the multi-year rate path.
Oil/energy inflation from the Iran war is expected to stay short-term and contained, with WTI likely settling in the $80s per barrel for a period; this mainly flows through into core inflation via airfares rather than broadly across the economy.
causal🗣 Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economistconviction: mediumWTI crude oilIran
So what: Even elevated oil prices in the $80s are framed as manageable for the consumer and not a broad-based inflation driver, limiting the case for a larger inflation resurgence.
Consumers are upset about the price LEVEL of goods (a step-change from post-pandemic stimulus and supply-chain shocks that companies rarely reverse), not the current rate of inflation, even though middle-to-upper income consumers can broadly still afford things because wage growth has largely kept pace outside of housing and autos.
framework🗣 Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economistconviction: high
So what: Separates the psychological 'everything is expensive' narrative from actual purchasing power, explaining why consumer sentiment can stay depressed while spending data stays resilient.
The Warsh Fed: Communication Overhaul and Policy Path 7
New Fed chair Kevin Warsh has changed his communication style dramatically (moving away from forward guidance) even though actual policy has not changed much yet after only one meeting, making every incoming data print more market-moving because investors can no longer rely on the Fed telegraphing its path.
causal🗣 Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economistconviction: highKevin WarshFederal Reserve
So what: Expect higher volatility in rates and equities around every CPI/payrolls release since the market must now interpret data itself rather than lean on Fed guidance.
Stephanie Roth believes a rate cut is 'very far' away and unlikely this year; if anything, the more live near-term risk was a possible hike (e.g., at a September meeting) had recent payrolls and inflation data not moved in a favorable direction.
prediction🗣 Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economistconviction: highFederal ReserveKevin Warsh
So what: Pushes back against market narratives of imminent 2026 cuts; positions the realistic base case as the Fed holding rates steady through year-end absent labor-market weakness.
“A cut is very far from now.” ▶ 24:05
Anthony Pompliano floated a contrarian theory that the Warsh Fed and administration may believe AI-driven productivity gains and mass deportations are both structurally disinflationary forces, and could use that logic to justify cutting rates preemptively rather than waiting for hard labor-market weakness.
contrarian🗣 host (Anthony Pompliano)conviction: exploratoryFederal ReserveKevin WarshAI
So what: If true, this would be a distinctly non-formulaic, forward-looking Fed reaction function investors should watch for versus the traditional 'data-dependent, react to weakness' Fed playbook.
Stephanie Roth argues that if the Fed does cut rates in 2026, it would signal real labor-market weakness rather than a policy victory, so the White House should not actually want a 2026 cut since current inflation (core running near 3%, ~100bps above target) doesn't justify one on its own.
contrarian🗣 Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economistconviction: highFederal Reserve
So what: Reframes a 2026 rate cut as a bearish signal (labor deterioration) rather than a bullish one, useful for interpreting market reaction if a cut does happen this year.
“If there's a scenario where the Fed is cutting this year, I don't think the White House is going to be very happy with where the economy is.” ▶ 25:58
The Fed will not officially change its 2% inflation target, but Roth and Pompliano debate whether it could unofficially tolerate a higher range without announcing it — Pompliano guesses 2.5-3%, Roth guesses closer to 2-2.5% — which would still be a meaningful shift from historical Fed behavior.
framework🗣 Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economistconviction: mediumFederal Reserve
So what: Watch whether the Fed cuts in late 2027 even with inflation still above 2% — that would be the tell for an unofficial, unannounced target shift.
A politically-motivated rate cut aimed at boosting markets ahead of the midterm election is seen by both speakers as an unlikely and self-defeating outcome, since it would be read as the Fed losing independence and raise the risk of a renewed inflation spiral, which would be negative for risk assets.
thesis🗣 Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economistconviction: highFederal ReserveKevin Warsh
So what: Investors shouldn't price in 'juice the market' cuts as a bullish catalyst — an unexpected, unjustified cut would more likely spook markets than rally them.
“I think that would be very negative for markets.” ▶ 31:36
Warsh's strategy is reportedly to establish Fed credibility and independence by leaning hawkish early, which brings down long-end yields (the 10-year) that actually anchor mortgage rates and business borrowing costs — a bigger lever than the federal funds rate itself.
framework🗣 Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economistconviction: highKevin WarshFederal Reserve
So what: Explains why a 'no cut, credible and hawkish' Fed posture can still be net-easing for the real economy via lower long-end rates, without an actual rate cut.
“Nobody pays the federal funds rate, right? People pay the 10-year rate realistically.” ▶ 32:20
Consumer Sentiment vs. Actual Spending: A Widening Gap 5
The stock market sits at all-time highs while consumer sentiment sits at all-time lows, an unusual divergence that both speakers flag as one of the more confusing signals in the current economy.
data_point🗣 host (Anthony Pompliano)conviction: medium
So what: A stock-market/sentiment divergence this wide suggests sentiment surveys may be overstating underlying economic distress relative to what asset prices and spending data show.
“The stock market's at all time highs but consumer sentiment is at all-time lows.” ▶ 10:11
Citing a Tom Lee report, Pompliano notes the Michigan Consumer Sentiment survey's political composition has shifted from roughly 50% Democrat/50% Republican historically to about two-thirds Democrat, one-third Republican, which mechanically skews the headline reading lower given the partisan gap in economic perception.
data_point🗣 host (Anthony Pompliano)conviction: medium
So what: Investors treating Michigan sentiment as a clean macro signal should adjust for this sampling shift before reading deep pessimism into the headline number.
“They now are surveying 2/3 Democrats, one third Republican.” ▶ 10:33
The headline consumer sentiment survey asks five questions, three of which relate to perceived affordability (e.g., is now a good time to buy durable goods, how do you feel about your finances) rather than actual purchase intent, so a negative reading doesn't necessarily predict reduced spending.
framework🗣 Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economistconviction: high
So what: Sentiment surveys should be read as a gauge of how people feel about affordability, not as a leading indicator of actual consumption — credit card and bank data are the better spending signal.
Credit card spending data and bank earnings reports (as of the taping) show resilient, optimistic consumer spending trends that contradict the negative headline sentiment readings.
data_point🗣 Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economistconviction: high
So what: Hard spending data currently outweighs soft sentiment surveys as the more reliable read on consumer health for positioning purposes.
Roth believes U.S. government economic data (CPI, employment) is largely trustworthy and not politicized, but seasonal-adjustment models built on 5-8 year historical patterns struggle when underlying patterns shift (as they have post-pandemic), producing larger revisions and month-to-month volatility that make the data look less reliable than it is on a smoothed, year-over-year basis.
framework🗣 Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economistconviction: highCPI
So what: Investors should discount single-month data surprises and focus on smoothed/year-over-year trends rather than reading political motive into data volatility.
Housing and Work Preference Shifts 6
New York City rent hit a new all-time high in June, driven by concentrated demand for a limited supply of desirable housing rather than a broad national affordability collapse.
data_point🗣 host (Anthony Pompliano)conviction: medium
So what: Highlights that 'unaffordability' complaints are often concentrated in specific high-demand metros rather than reflecting the whole housing market.
A large share of perceived housing unaffordability is a mismatch between what people can actually afford (e.g., a home in a mid-tier suburb) and what they want (a specific big-city location or larger home), rather than true inability to buy any home.
thesis🗣 host (Anthony Pompliano)conviction: medium
So what: Suggests housing affordability narratives should distinguish genuine affordability crises from location/lifestyle preference, which matters for how policy or markets should respond.
“There's actually a mismatch between what you can afford and what you want.” ▶ 14:46
Elevated mortgage rates are keeping many current homeowners on the sidelines because they don't want to trade a lower locked-in rate for a materially higher one, a lock-in effect Roth expects to ease only gradually as rates come down modestly and incomes grow into current price levels.
causal🗣 Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economistconviction: medium
So what: The housing market's supply constraint from rate lock-in is expected to unwind slowly, implying gradual rather than sharp improvement in transaction volumes and affordability.
A growing cohort, especially younger consumers and the FIRE community, increasingly prefers renting over owning because they've concluded homeownership is not as strong a financial investment as previously assumed, alongside a preference for job/location mobility.
contrarian🗣 host (Anthony Pompliano)conviction: medium
So what: A structural shift in preference away from homeownership (not just affordability-driven) could keep rental demand and prices elevated even if mortgage rates ease.
Consumer spending preferences have shifted toward experiences and travel over physical goods in recent years, a trend both speakers expect to persist as younger generations place less value on traditional markers like homeownership.
data_point🗣 Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economistconviction: medium
So what: Sector allocators should expect continued relative strength in travel/services spending versus goods retail as a generational trend, not just a temporary post-pandemic blip.
Employers are increasingly using workplace flexibility (remote/hybrid work) as a non-cash benefit to attract talent without raising pay, reflecting an evolving preference among younger workers for time efficiency and work-life balance over hours worked, even as large banks push for full return-to-office.
thesis🗣 Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economistconviction: medium
So what: Companies offering flexibility can potentially manage labor costs and retention without raising wages, a relevant input for evaluating labor-cost trends and margins.
AI's Real-World Labor Market Impact 3
Earlier fears that AI would cause a sharp rise in unemployment have not materialized; instead the more likely path is gradual productivity gains, some unfilled job openings as AI absorbs certain tasks, and new job creation, ultimately lowering unit labor costs and reinforcing disinflation.
thesis🗣 Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economistconviction: highAI
So what: Investors worried about AI-driven labor market shocks should instead model a gradual productivity/disinflation story rather than a sudden unemployment spike.
Anthony Pompliano says he has completely reversed his prior view that AI would eliminate jobs, now believing it will create substantial new hiring demand for workers who know how to use the technology because it makes them more productive.
contrarian🗣 host (Anthony Pompliano)conviction: highAI
So what: A notable public reversal from a prominent commentator on AI-and-jobs, signaling the 'AI destroys jobs' narrative may be losing traction among market observers.
“I have 100% changed my mind. I used to think it was going to take all the jobs. Now I think that it's going to create a ton of jobs.” ▶ 21:27
AI productivity benefits have not yet shown up meaningfully in broad economic data because industry adoption rates remain low, but Roth expects this to change as adoption accelerates.
prediction🗣 Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economistconviction: mediumAI
So what: Productivity/margin gains from AI adoption are a forward-looking catalyst not yet priced into current macro data — worth tracking adoption-rate metrics by industry.
IPO Wave and Liquidity: Not the 1990s Bubble 5
Wolf Research's liquidity analysis finds that expected corporate buybacks this year should notably exceed the combined supply from new IPO issuance and expiring lockups, putting net demand in positive territory and suggesting the market can absorb the current IPO wave without a liquidity problem.
data_point🗣 Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economistconviction: high
So what: Counters the bearish 'too much IPO supply will overwhelm demand' narrative — buyback-driven demand is estimated to provide cushion for this year's issuance calendar.
“This year corporate buyback should be pretty good.” ▶ 36:46
Unlike past IPO waves that involved a broad breadth of companies going public, this year's wave is concentrated in a small handful of mega-cap IPOs (larger dollar issuance per deal), reflecting companies choosing to stay private longer and go public only when conditions were optimal, rather than a 1990s-style speculative frenzy.
contrarian🗣 Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economistconviction: high
So what: Structurally different IPO wave composition (few mega-deals vs. broad speculative issuance) argues against directly analogizing this period to the dot-com bubble.
Current AI-related investment is only about 2% of GDP, well below the roughly 4% of GDP level historically associated with past major investment bubbles, indicating the AI investment boom has room to grow before reaching bubble-like scale, though malinvestment risk could emerge over the next couple of years.
data_point🗣 Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economistconviction: highAI
So what: Provides a concrete quantitative benchmark (2% vs. ~4% of GDP) for gauging how much further an AI capex boom could run before it resembles historical bubble scale.
“In the past you've had big investment bubbles when said investment bubble was about 4% of GDP.” ▶ 38:29
Companies like SpaceX are going public partly to access a different, larger quantum of capital than private markets offer and to gain broader investor engagement, with SpaceX's share price rising post-IPO and the company moving quickly into an acquisition, while other recent private-to-public valuation resets (Stripe's valuation coming down from its peak, Ramp raising at $6B after being valued near $10B before rising sharply since) show the public/private valuation gap being tested.
anecdote🗣 host (Anthony Pompliano)conviction: mediumSpaceXStripeRamp
So what: Illustrates that going public is partly a price-discovery mechanism forcing formerly high-flying private valuations (like Stripe's) to reset against real market demand.
This year's favorable conditions for IPOs — high CEO confidence, lower volatility, higher valuations, and strong AI-driven growth narratives — mark a clear improvement from last year, when tariff-driven volatility discouraged companies from going public, but this does not by itself mean markets are at a bubble peak.
thesis🗣 Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economistconviction: highAI
So what: Frames the current IPO window as a cyclical opportunity created by favorable macro conditions rather than evidence of speculative excess, useful context for evaluating new listings.
Bitcoin/Gold Debasement Trade and Key Risks 4
The Fed-independence-risk 'debasement trade' into Bitcoin and gold, which was prominent roughly 9-12 months ago on fears of a non-independent Fed, has substantially cooled since Trump nominated Kevin Warsh, a candidate viewed as credible with a long, historically hawkish Fed track record, reducing fears of political capture.
thesis🗣 Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economistconviction: mediumBitcoinGoldKevin WarshFederal Reserve
So what: The macro narrative that drove flows into Bitcoin/gold as a Fed-independence hedge has largely unwound, meaning that catalyst is less likely to be a near-term driver of BTC/gold price action.
After briefly reigniting around Warsh's first press conference on fears the Fed would be more hawkish than expected, the debasement trade has now settled down, with markets refocusing on fundamentals, demand, and standard inflation dynamics rather than the non-independent-Fed narrative.
thesis🗣 Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economistconviction: mediumBitcoinGoldKevin Warsh
So what: Suggests Bitcoin and gold price action going forward should be analyzed more through traditional supply/demand and inflation fundamentals rather than a Fed-debasement political narrative.
“Now I think we're kind of settling in the middle where we're back to sort of trading on other things.” ▶ 42:33
The top near-term risk keeping Roth up at night is the on-again, off-again uncertainty around the Iran war, which makes it hard for businesses and consumers to plan, echoing how last year's tariff-policy uncertainty (more than the tariffs' actual economic impact) was the bigger drag on sentiment and growth.
thesis🗣 Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economistconviction: highIran
So what: Positions geopolitical/policy uncertainty itself (not the underlying economic fundamentals) as the swing factor most likely to slow the economy from here — worth monitoring headline flow on the conflict as a sentiment driver.
“Nobody even knows if the war is on or off.” ▶ 4:12
Aside from the Iran war uncertainty, Roth sees no other obvious domestic economic risks right now — a state of affairs she finds mildly concerning in itself, since an absence of visible risks typically means the eventual surprise will come from an unforeseen, likely non-domestic-economic source.
thesis🗣 Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economistconviction: medium
So what: A 'no obvious risks' environment is itself a signal to stay alert for tail risk from an unexpected direction (geopolitical or otherwise) rather than complacency.

Sourcery with Molly O'Shea 4 episode(s) · 107 insights

Senra Systems' $65M Series B: Fixing Aerospace & Defense's Biggest Bottleneck71 min30 insights2026-07-15
▶ Watch on YouTube
Senra Systems CEO Jordan Black and newly hired CIO Ken Venner (ex-SpaceX, ex-Broadcom) detail the company's $65M Series B (led by Lower Carbon and Interlogos, with Sequoia, Founders Fund, General Catalyst and Andreessen Horowitz participating) to automate wire-harness manufacturing, arguing the largely manual, Cold-War-era harness industry is the hidden bottleneck behind the US hard-tech and defense re-industrialization boom.
Wire Harnessing as Hardware's Hidden Bottleneck · 4Re-industrialization & Defense Supply Chain Fragility · 4Senra's Capital Raise & Investor Syndicate · 3Vertical Integration, Software & the Path to Automation · 5Talent, Training & the Ken Venner Scaling Playbook · 7Founding Story & Company Culture · 3Commercial Traction & Facility Scale-Up · 4
Wire Harnessing as Hardware's Hidden Bottleneck 4
Wire harnesses can account for up to 25% of a vehicle's total cost, yet the industry remains a largely manual, custom, hand-built ('arts and crafts') process, making it a disproportionately expensive and fragile link in hardware supply chains.
data_point🗣 Jordan Black, Senra Systems CEOconviction: highSenra Systemswire harnessing
So what: Investors in auto, aerospace and defense hardware should treat harness-supply risk as a material cost and schedule variable, not an afterthought.
“It could be 25% of the cost of your vehicle at any given moment, too.” ▶ 2:25
SpaceX outsourced 95% of its wire harness production for the Falcon and Dragon programs because in-house harnessing was so operationally painful, showing that even the most vertically integrated hardware company in the industry chose to avoid building harnesses itself.
anecdote🗣 Jordan Black, Senra Systems CEOconviction: highSpaceXFalconDragon
So what: Validates a standalone harness-manufacturing category as a real outsourcing wedge rather than a function hardware primes will simply insource.
“We outsourced 95% of it for Falcon and Dragon back in the day.” ▶ 3:24
The wire harness manufacturing process has not fundamentally changed since the Cold War era, leaving it as one of the last fully analog, non-digitized steps in otherwise high-tech hardware production.
thesis🗣 Jordan Black, Senra Systems CEOconviction: highwire harnessing
So what: A multi-decade technology gap in a critical-path manufacturing step is exactly the setup for a venture-scale automation/software disruption play.
“Everyone hasn't changed the process since the cold war era.” ▶ 3:32
The average wire harness technician is between 48 and 55 years old, and because the skill is passed on almost entirely through decades of unstructured, tribal, on-the-job apprenticeship, the loss of even a couple of veteran workers can stop production of an entire product line.
causal🗣 Jordan Black, Senra Systems CEOconviction: highwire harnessingaerospace and defense supply chain
So what: An aging, non-scalable labor base is a structural supply constraint that will keep worsening absent new training infrastructure -- a durable tailwind for automated/trained-workforce competitors like Senra.
“The average age of a wire technician is between like 48 to 55 years old.” ▶ 18:48
Re-industrialization & Defense Supply Chain Fragility 4
Over 800 small, independent 'mom and pop' wire harness shops exist across the US, and rather than growing with demand they are shrinking -- being bought by private equity or absorbed -- creating single points of failure where one shop's closure can halt production of a specific fighter jet or missile program.
data_point🗣 Jordan Black, Senra Systems CEOconviction: highUS industrial basedefense supply chain
So what: Defense-program delivery risk is concentrated in an invisible, consolidating tier of small suppliers -- a due-diligence red flag for anyone underwriting defense-prime execution risk, and a roll-up/consolidation opportunity for well-capitalized entrants.
“The 800 plus mom and pop shops that exist all over the US... not only are they not growing, they're actually going away.” ▶ 8:32
Jordan Black expects defense-system component demand to grow three to five times current levels, and argues the current US industrial base cannot organically scale skilled harness labor fast enough to meet it, which is why Senra is building excess supply capacity ahead of realized demand rather than reactively.
prediction🗣 Jordan Black, Senra Systems CEOconviction: highSenra SystemsUS defense industrial base
So what: A 3-5x demand forecast for defense components implies harness capacity (and companies serving it) could be a multi-year structural growth bet independent of any single defense program's fate.
“We know we're going to have to three to 5x extra demand in all these defense systems.” ▶ 8:07
