A meaningful share of Senra's growth is being pulled by fast-scaling defense and space primes such as Anduril and SpaceX moving rapidly into high-volume production and needing agile, responsive suppliers, rather than by legacy incumbent harness shops.
causal🗣 Jordan Black, Senra Systems CEOconviction: highAndurilSpaceXSenra Systems
So what: Senra's growth is directly levered to the production ramp of the new wave of defense-tech primes -- their capex and output cadence is a leading indicator for Senra's own order book.
“We're seeing a lot of the demand coming from these scaling companies like the Anderals of the world and the SpaceX of the world that are moving very rapidly into their production phase.” ▶ 8:18
Senra is proactively engaging with government policy staff ('folks on the hill') on both policy improvements and government relations, positioning itself as a supply-chain-critical vendor to the government and to the primes and neoprimes it buys from, ahead of most of its revenue currently coming from the defense segment.
framework🗣 Jordan Black, Senra Systems CEOconviction: mediumSenra Systemsdefense primes
So what: Direct policy engagement suggests Senra is positioning for procurement/regulatory tailwinds (e.g. onshoring mandates) rather than just organic commercial demand.
Senra's Capital Raise & Investor Syndicate 3
Senra Systems closed a $65 million Series B led by Lower Carbon and Interlogos, with existing investors Sequoia, Founders Fund, General Catalyst, HBC, and Andreessen Horowitz all participating roughly three years after the company's founding.
data_point🗣 Jordan Black, Senra Systems CEOconviction: highSenra SystemsLower CarbonInterlogosSequoiaFounders FundGeneral CatalystAndreessen Horowitz
So what: A full re-up from nearly every major existing investor in a Series B signals strong internal conviction in Senra's trajectory, not just new-money enthusiasm for the hard-tech theme.
≈ “Senra Systems officially closed our series B fundraising for $65 million.” paraphrase
Figma CEO Dylan Field personally co-led Senra's Series A after spending time with founder Jordan Black on-site discussing a 'Figma for wire harness design' vision, illustrating how prominent software-company operators are now writing early checks directly into physical hard-tech manufacturing startups.
data_point🗣 Jordan Black, Senra Systems CEOconviction: highDylan FieldFigmaSenra Systems
So what: Crossover conviction from top consumer/software operators into industrial hard-tech is a signal worth tracking as a leading indicator of capital rotating into the sector.
According to the host, Senra has raised over $100 million in total capital across its funding history, spanning a cap table that includes Sequoia's Shawn Maguire, General Catalyst's Paul Kwan, Founders Fund's Delian Asparouhov, plus Lower Carbon and Interlogos.
data_point🗣 host, Molly O'Sheaconviction: mediumSenra SystemsSequoiaGeneral CatalystFounders Fund
So what: A concentrated, name-brand syndicate across multiple rounds suggests Senra has priority access to follow-on capital, reducing financing risk relative to less-connected hard-tech peers.
Vertical Integration, Software & the Path to Automation 5
Jordan Black's vertical-integration rule is purely economic: build in-house only where it makes the company structurally more profitable or where no external partner can match the required expertise (e.g. harness-specific work-instruction software and training), and otherwise partner -- such as with outside robotics companies for dexterous robotic arms rather than building that capability internally.
framework🗣 Jordan Black, Senra Systems CEOconviction: highSenra Systems
So what: A disciplined build-vs-partner heuristic (own the differentiated core, buy the commoditizable periphery) is a useful lens for evaluating capital efficiency at other vertically-ambitious hard-tech startups.
≈ “I think vertical integration is like incredibly important. We do a ton of it here... but other times... we don't plan to be this AI modeling company.” paraphrase ▶ 15:47
Senra built its own end-to-end manufacturing software system -- explicitly modeled on SpaceX's internal 'Warp Drive' platform -- covering everything from customer quoting to shipping, arguing that owning this software layer is what allows a bespoke, historically unscalable manual process to actually scale into a factory model.
framework🗣 Jordan Black, Senra Systems CEOconviction: highSenra SystemsWarp DriveSpaceX
So what: The company's moat is framed as a proprietary operating system, not just factory floor space -- relevant for how investors should value Senra versus a pure-labor manufacturing roll-up.
≈ “Similar to this software system that SpaceX had, Warp Drive, to power the entire ecosystem of manufacturing, we essentially are doing the same thing.” paraphrase
Senra is deploying 2D machine-vision inspection at the individual wire-crimp level to replace manual visual quality inspection performed by veteran inspectors, aiming to cut human error out of a process where the company argues even a small nick on a cable jacket can cause catastrophic system failure, citing the Apollo 1 mission as an example.
causal🗣 Jordan Black, Senra Systems CEOconviction: highSenra SystemsApollo 1
So what: Automated, data-driven quality inspection both cuts labor cost and reduces catastrophic-failure tail risk on mission-critical defense and aerospace programs -- a differentiator worth diligence versus manual-inspection competitors.
“The reason why the astronaut side in the Apollo 1 mission was like a small nick on a cable jacket of a wire harness.” ▶ 59:08
Senra believes current robotic dexterity and AI models are not yet capable of fully automating harness assembly, but is deliberately capturing labeled video and process data now via factory-wide vision systems so it can be first to automate once the underlying technology matures, drawing an explicit analogy to the collapsing cost curve of solar photovoltaic cells in the early 2000s.
prediction🗣 Jordan Black, Senra Systems CEOconviction: mediumSenra Systems
So what: Senra is positioning its proprietary manufacturing dataset as the long-term automation moat, similar to how data advantages compound in other AI-adjacent categories -- a thesis worth revisiting as robotic dexterity models improve.
“I look at it as solar back in early 2000s... converting a photon to electron wasn't fiscally responsible for anyone to go move into that direction but now it's incredibly cheap.” ▶ 20:35
Jordan Black is explicitly undecided on which robotics form factor (humanoid robots, robotic arms, or custom-built machines) Senra will ultimately deploy on the factory floor, treating hardware-automation choice as an open technology-selection problem to be resolved by whichever partner delivers the best cost-effective dexterity rather than committing early to one bet.
framework🗣 Jordan Black, Senra Systems CEOconviction: exploratorySenra Systems
So what: Investors should not assume Senra is a humanoid-robotics play; its automation roadmap is technology-agnostic and will likely track whichever robotics vendor wins on cost and dexterity, not brand hype.
≈ “Senra will always adopt the best technology to make sure we are building a high quality product at a very high throughput as well... whether it's humanoid robots, robotic arms, custom machines, the answer is still I think a little bit unknown.” paraphrase
Talent, Training & the Ken Venner Scaling Playbook 7
Senra cut wire-harness technician onboarding from the industry-standard two-year shadowing apprenticeship down to four weeks, via a proprietary training program that is the only US Department of Labor-certified training program for wire harnessing, combined with its manufacturing operating system that removes the need for tribal-knowledge transfer.
data_point🗣 Jordan Black, Senra Systems CEOconviction: highSenra Systems
So what: A 24x reduction in time-to-productivity for a scarce skilled trade is a concrete, defensible unit-economics advantage over legacy harness shops that still rely on multi-year apprenticeships.
“We're actually the only US Department of Labor certified training program for wire harnessing.” ▶ 18:05
Senra hires production technicians based on attitude and aptitude rather than prior harnessing experience, deliberately building a younger workforce that skews away from the aging incumbent talent pool (48-55 years old on average).
framework🗣 Jordan Black, Senra Systems CEOconviction: highSenra Systems
So what: Recruiting for trainability rather than tenure sidesteps the industry's core labor-supply bottleneck and widens Senra's addressable hiring pool relative to competitors chasing the same shrinking pool of veteran technicians.
“We hire based on attitude and aptitude of like, can you use your hands? Can you read instructions?” ▶ 19:03
Newly hired Senra CIO Ken Venner previously scaled Broadcom from roughly 1,000 employees and $400 million in revenue to 10,000 employees and $8.6 billion in revenue over 11 years, including 52 mergers and acquisitions, and separately built SpaceX's internal 'Warp Drive' platform that helped scale rocket production from about one booster per year to 40 boosters per year across 9,000 employees.
data_point🗣 Ken Venner, Senra Systems CIOconviction: highKen VennerBroadcomSpaceX
So what: Venner's track record of two separate 10x-plus organizational scale-ups via software/operating systems is the direct credibility basis for betting Senra can replicate that scaling curve in wire harnessing.
“In the 11 years I was there, grew to 10,000 employees and 8.6 billion in revenue, did 52 mergers and acquisitions.” ▶ 40:44
Ken Venner's organizational scaling framework is 'smart people, stupid systems': apply minimal process at each headcount tier (roughly 10, 100, 1,000, and 5,000+ people) so the company isn't constrained by premature bureaucracy, while still tracking individual accountability, and only add heavier information-aggregation layers once headcount exceeds about 5,000.
framework🗣 Ken Venner, Senra Systems CIOconviction: highKen VennerBroadcomSpaceX
So what: A reusable staged-scaling framework investors can apply when assessing whether any fast-growing hard-tech company is over- or under-processed for its current headcount.
“You always have to assume smart people, stupid systems, let the right people do the right thing in the moment.” ▶ 42:27
Ken Venner attributes much of Elon Musk's operating effectiveness at SpaceX to first-principles thinking paired with a strict 'responsible engineer' accountability model, where one person owned a given part end-to-end from initial requirement through post-flight with no handoffs or finger-pointing.
framework🗣 Ken Venner, Senra Systems CIOconviction: highKen VennerElonSpaceX
So what: Single-owner, no-handoff accountability is a transferable organizational design principle Senra is explicitly importing from SpaceX into wire-harness manufacturing.
“That person owned that part from first requirement all the way through to post-flight. There were no handoffs.” ▶ 46:32
Because of AI leverage, Ken Venner expects to run Senra's core platform/IT organization with a team of roughly six people, versus the approximately 175-person platform team he built for a comparable scope at SpaceX, implying AI-era manufacturing software teams can now be built at a fraction of historical headcount.
prediction🗣 Ken Venner, Senra Systems CIOconviction: highKen VennerSpaceXSenra Systems
So what: A roughly 25-30x reduction in required platform-engineering headcount for equivalent scope is a concrete data point on how AI is compressing the capital and time needed to build internal manufacturing software, relevant to underwriting opex for other hard-tech operators.
≈ “I got a team of six here... I think I'll be able to do exactly what I did at SpaceX with only six because AI drives most of what we're doing.” paraphrase ▶ 52:25
Ken Venner and Jordan Black both invoke a 'rule of three' -- echoing SpaceX Merlin engine designer Tom Mueller's claim that it takes three engine iterations to get it right -- expecting Senra's third factory build to be the one that proves a truly repeatable, copy-paste scaling model, after Torrance (brute-force first attempt) and Rondo (standardization) preceded the current Cypress facility.
framework🗣 Ken Venner, Senra Systems CIOconviction: mediumKen VennerTom MuellerSenra Systems
So what: If the 'rule of three' holds, Senra's planned third facility -- not the current one -- is the milestone that should validate (or falsify) the company's replicable-factory thesis and its long-term unit economics.
“It only takes three versions of an engine to get it right.” ▶ 50:56
Founding Story & Company Culture 3
Jordan Black and co-founder Ben Shanahan, both ex-SpaceX, started Senra by hand-building wire harnesses on their apartment carpet and landed their first paying customer -- pizza-automation startup Stellar Pizza -- just two weeks after quitting SpaceX.
anecdote🗣 Jordan Black, Senra Systems CEOconviction: highJordan BlackBen ShanahanStellar PizzaSpaceX
So what: Illustrates a bootstrapped, customer-development-first origin rather than a purely capital-led start, which shaped Senra's early word-of-mouth, no-sales-team growth model.
“Two weeks later, we started building harnesses for a company called Stellar Pizza.” ▶ 11:45
The company name 'Senra' is the word 'harness' spelled backward with the H and S removed, reflecting founder Jordan Black's operating philosophy of stripping unnecessary steps out of manufacturing processes ('delete the horseshit').
anecdote🗣 Jordan Black, Senra Systems CEOconviction: highSenra SystemsJordan Black
So what: A minor but telling data point on founder identity and operating philosophy -- process-simplification obsession as the stated core cultural driver behind product decisions.
“We're deleting the horseshit of harnessing.” ▶ 34:34
In Senra's early fundraising, Jordan Black carried a portable wire-stripping machine to investor meetings across the country and physically contrasted a 600-page manual harness-building manual against a one-button automated alternative to make the automation opportunity visceral, reflecting how much harder it was to sell hard-tech conviction to investors before the current hard-tech funding boom.
anecdote🗣 Jordan Black, Senra Systems CEOconviction: highJordan BlackSenra Systems
So what: A reminder that today's hard-tech capital abundance is recent -- investors evaluating current hard-tech valuations should account for how quickly investor appetite shifted in just a few years.
≈ “Here's the old process, manual tools, a lot of documentation. Here's the new process, configured machines, and one button that you press.” paraphrase
Commercial Traction & Facility Scale-Up 4
Senra runs a dedicated production line for one defense customer building more than 4,000 units per week, and separately built a 120-foot-long harness for a defense system in about two months after being called in at the last minute because the missing harness was blocking a multi-billion-dollar program.
data_point🗣 Jordan Black, Senra Systems CEOconviction: highSenra Systems
So what: Concrete evidence Senra is already embedded as a critical-path supplier on multi-billion-dollar defense programs, not just a prototyping shop -- relevant for assessing revenue durability and switching costs.
≈ “We got called in the 11th hour to go build this thing in two months because they are blocking a multi-billion dollar program.” paraphrase
Senra operates two Southern California facilities with distinct roles: Rondo for rapid prototyping and new product introduction, and the newer Cypress facility (the subject of this tour) for scaling proven programs into high-volume, cost-effective production.
framework🗣 Jordan Black, Senra Systems CEOconviction: highSenra Systems
So what: A prototype-to-production facility split lets Senra serve customers across their full program lifecycle, capturing revenue earlier (napkin-sketch stage) and retaining it through scale-up, increasing switching costs for customers.
Senra did not have a dedicated sales team until roughly four months before this interview, having grown primarily through word of mouth on the premise that a good product generates inbound demand on its own.
data_point🗣 Jordan Black, Senra Systems CEOconviction: highSenra Systems
So what: Strong organic, sales-team-free growth in a fragmented industrial category is a signal of genuine product-market fit and pricing power rather than growth manufactured by aggressive go-to-market spend.
“I didn't have a sales team until 4 months ago.” ▶ 1:03:28
Senra's Cypress facility is currently only about 25% built out and is targeted to reach near-100% capacity by the end of the year, with the company planning to open a third factory as it continues to run out of space at each successive site (apartment, then Torrance, then Rondo, then Cypress).
data_point🗣 Jordan Black, Senra Systems CEOconviction: highSenra Systems
So what: A clear, near-term capacity utilization ramp (25% to ~100% within the year) gives investors a concrete milestone to track Senra's revenue scaling against, independent of the funding headline.
≈ “This is only 25% built up, so everything around us will start having more tables, more machines, more automation over the next few months to really fill out the space.” paraphrase
Inference 101: SambaNova CEO Rodrigo Liang60 min33 insights2026-07-17
▶ Watch on YouTube
SambaNova CEO Rodrigo Liang discusses the company's $1B first-close raise at an $11B valuation (total funding now $2.5B) and lays out an inference-first hardware thesis: ultra power-efficient racks that undercut Nvidia's footprint, a bet that agentic AI's compounding latency requirements will drive distributed and edge compute, and a strategy of partnering rather than building its own cloud while helping enterprises and nations move toward sovereign, on-prem AI deployments.
SambaNova's $11B Fundraise and Scaling Momentum · 4SN40/SN50 Rack Efficiency vs Nvidia · 6Agentic AI Latency Economics · 3The Premium Inference Thesis · 4Distributed and Edge Infrastructure · 5Chip Market Structure and Inference Routing Economics · 5Sovereignty, On-Prem Repatriation, and Enterprise Differentiation · 6
SambaNova's $11B Fundraise and Scaling Momentum 4
SambaNova closed a first tranche of $1 billion in a funding round at an $11 billion valuation, led by General Atlantic with investors including Capital Group and Seligman Ventures.
data_point🗣 Rodrigo Liang, SambaNova CEOconviction: highSambaNovaGeneral AtlanticCapital Group
So what: Signals strong institutional confidence in SambaNova's inference chip thesis at a moment of peak semiconductor investor interest, a bullish read-through for AI infrastructure valuations broadly.
“We just did a first close of a billion dollar fund raise at an 11 billion valuation.” ▶ 0:00
SambaNova has raised $2.5 billion in total funding since founding, a cumulative scale reached by very few semiconductor startups historically.
data_point🗣 Rodrigo Liang, SambaNova CEOconviction: highSambaNova
So what: A rare multi-billion-dollar cumulative raise signals investors see a durable, long-term platform rather than a speculative bet.
After 32 years building high-performance chips, Rodrigo Liang says he has never seen investor interest in semiconductors as high as it is now, driven by the realization that chips are the bottleneck constraint on AI data center buildouts.
thesis🗣 Rodrigo Liang, SambaNova CEOconviction: highSambaNova
So what: A 32-year veteran's read that current semiconductor investment enthusiasm is unprecedented suggests the AI infra capex supercycle is not yet near its peak.
“I've been in this industry for 32 years, building high performance chips for a long time. I've never seen the interest in semiconductors higher, right?” ▶ 2:16
Large capital raises into the multi-billion-dollar range function as a market signal that sophisticated investors believe a company will be a durable long-term player, since investors are highly selective about which companies they think will still exist years from now.
framework🗣 Rodrigo Liang, SambaNova CEOconviction: mediumSambaNova
So what: Investors can use the size/quality of a startup's cap raise as a proxy for institutional conviction on long-term survival, useful for screening among many AI infra 'land grab' companies raising money.
SN40/SN50 Rack Efficiency vs Nvidia 6
SambaNova's SN40 chip delivers state-of-the-art inference performance from a 10-kilowatt, air-cooled rack that outperforms a 130-140 kilowatt Nvidia GPU rack requiring liquid cooling.
data_point🗣 Rodrigo Liang, SambaNova CEOconviction: highSambaNovaNvidiaSN40
So what: A roughly 13-14x power-density advantage lets any standard data center retrofit for state-of-the-art inference without capex-heavy cooling and power upgrades, undercutting Nvidia-based deployment economics.
“instead of a 130 140 kilowatt rack of Nvidia GPU, we were outperforming them with a 10 kilowatt SM40 rack.” ▶ 0:22
A single SambaNova SN40 rack can run a trillion-parameter model that would otherwise require dozens of racks of competing hardware.
data_point🗣 Rodrigo Liang, SambaNova CEOconviction: highSambaNovaSN40
So what: Massive footprint collapse (dozens of racks to one) is a direct capex/opex argument for switching inference workloads to SambaNova hardware.
“You could take a trillion parameter model and run it in a single rack where it would take dozens of racks of other people's equipment to run the same model.” ▶ 0:30
SambaNova's minimum deployment unit for inference is a single rack, versus 10-20 racks needed by other providers to serve large models such as DeepSeek's 1.5-trillion-parameter model.
data_point🗣 Rodrigo Liang, SambaNova CEOconviction: highSambaNovaDeepSeek
So what: A lower minimum viable cluster size dramatically lowers the capital barrier to becoming an inference provider, opening the market to smaller regional or edge operators.
SambaNova built its rack architecture on standard 19-inch form factors, standard air cooling, Kubernetes, Red Hat Linux, and standard Ethernet networking rather than proprietary high-performance interconnects, so operators can swap out old gear and go live quickly instead of the 9-18 months typically needed to build new liquid-cooled, high-power data center capacity.
framework🗣 Rodrigo Liang, SambaNova CEOconviction: highSambaNova
So what: Standardized infrastructure removes the biggest time-to-deploy bottleneck in AI infra buildouts, a structural advantage versus large gigawatt data center projects.
SambaNova's newest chip, the SN50, launched this year and enables aggregated inference that blends SambaNova hardware with existing Nvidia and Xeon racks, targeting both hyperscale clusters and highly energy-efficient edge deployments.
data_point🗣 Rodrigo Liang, SambaNova CEOconviction: highSambaNovaNvidiaSN50
So what: Interoperability with incumbent Nvidia/Xeon infrastructure lowers switching costs for data center operators, widening SambaNova's addressable market beyond greenfield deployments.
SambaNova has taped out six chip generations in seven years since founding in 2017, with a seventh generation planned for next year.
data_point🗣 Rodrigo Liang, SambaNova CEOconviction: highSambaNova
So what: A roughly annual new-chip-generation cadence suggests SambaNova can keep pace with the fast-moving model-size and architecture curve, a key competitive requirement in inference chips.
Agentic AI Latency Economics 3
In multi-agent systems, per-agent response latency compounds across orchestration steps, so a chain of 20 agents each taking 2 seconds produces a 40-second total response time that exceeds users' tolerance, meaning individual agent latency budgets must shrink to roughly 0.1 seconds each to keep end-to-end response times near 1-2 seconds.
framework🗣 Rodrigo Liang, SambaNova CEOconviction: highSambaNova
So what: As agentic workflows proliferate, ultra-low per-inference latency becomes a hard requirement rather than a nice-to-have, a structural tailwind for inference providers optimized for speed over single-model chat use cases.
“if each of those 20 agents took two seconds that's 40 seconds you've already given up on that prompt” ▶ 12:52
Because gigawatt-scale data centers cannot be physically sited in dense urban centers like Manhattan or downtown Paris, ultra-low-latency use cases such as banking and healthcare will require smaller, distributed compute deployments located directly in metro areas.
causal🗣 Rodrigo Liang, SambaNova CEOconviction: highSambaNova
So what: Real-estate and power constraints in cities create a durable niche for smaller-footprint inference hardware near end users, favoring vendors like SambaNova whose racks fit into existing urban data centers.
SambaNova is deliberately deploying hardware in large metropolitan areas because that is where latency-sensitive business such as banking, healthcare, and agentic workflows is concentrated, rather than relying solely on remote gigawatt campuses.
thesis🗣 Rodrigo Liang, SambaNova CEOconviction: highSambaNova
So what: Positions SambaNova against the 'bigger is better' mega-data-center narrative, betting on distributed metro-area inference as a differentiated go-to-market.
The Premium Inference Thesis 4
Liang defines 'premium' inference along two axes, model size (a proxy for accuracy) and speed, arguing model size keeps growing because labs are chasing incremental accuracy gains: flagship closed models are heading toward roughly 10 trillion parameters while leading open-source models are already at 1-2 trillion parameters.
framework🗣 Rodrigo Liang, SambaNova CEOconviction: highSambaNova
So what: Investors should expect continued growth in flagship model parameter counts rather than a plateau, sustaining demand for high-memory, high-compute inference hardware.
Anthropic's Claude became the most popular code-generation model because it produces output developers can trust without extensive manual double-checking, illustrating that output accuracy, not just price, drives model adoption for high-value use cases.
causal🗣 Rodrigo Liang, SambaNova CEOconviction: mediumAnthropicClaude
So what: Accuracy-driven stickiness in code-generation use cases supports premium pricing power for top-tier models, relevant for anyone valuing Anthropic or code-gen-focused AI products.
SambaNova runs large models at original, full precision without quantization, claiming to be faster than competitors who typically must shrink models to hit speed targets, breaking the usual speed-versus-size trade-off.
thesis🗣 Rodrigo Liang, SambaNova CEOconviction: highSambaNova
So what: Avoiding the standard speed/size trade-off is framed as SambaNova's core technical moat; worth monitoring whether that edge holds as rival chips improve quantization-free performance.
“we don't chop the model down we just run original precision full precision we're faster than anybody else” ▶ 17:21
Liang predicts that fast inference will stop being a paid premium tier and instead become the baseline expectation for all users, drawing a direct analogy to how consumers universally moved to 5G speeds with no one voluntarily staying on 2G.
prediction🗣 Rodrigo Liang, SambaNova CEOconviction: mediumSambaNova
So what: Implies a future compression of speed-based pricing premiums industry-wide once fast inference becomes cheap enough to be ubiquitous, a headwind for providers relying on speed-based price differentiation long-term but a tailwind for volume adoption.
“when 5G showed up nobody's signing up for 2G” ▶ 19:04
Distributed and Edge Infrastructure 5
Liang pushes back on the narrative that future AI data centers must cost $50-100 billion each, arguing the market will be heterogeneous, with large hyperscale campuses alongside a growing wave of midsize distributed data centers that matter more as agentic AI raises the importance of low latency.
contrarian🗣 Rodrigo Liang, SambaNova CEOconviction: highSambaNova
So what: Challenges the mega-data-center capex thesis dominating AI infrastructure headlines, suggesting capital may increasingly flow to smaller distributed facilities rather than only the largest campuses.
Because SambaNova racks run at roughly 10 kilowatts, they can be packed into standard shipping containers to create edge data center clusters of 10-20 racks, deployable in locations that cannot support gigawatt facilities or their own power plants, often paired with Starlink connectivity and solar power.
data_point🗣 Rodrigo Liang, SambaNova CEOconviction: highSambaNovaStarlink
So what: Opens a distinct market for AI compute in regions and economies that cannot afford mega data centers, a geography most large-model providers are not directly targeting.
SambaNova is a multi-year hardware partner of Armada, which deploys modular edge data centers for critical industries such as oil and gas, mining, and defense that need real-time, on-site AI processing.
data_point🗣 Rodrigo Liang, SambaNova CEOconviction: highSambaNovaArmada
So what: A concrete commercial proof point for SambaNova's edge-computing thesis and validation of Armada's modular data center model as a channel for AI hardware deployment into remote and critical-industry sites.
Liang cites the spread of Starlink connectivity, including on airplanes, as a macro driver expanding data usage and enabling more edge AI workloads in previously disconnected or remote locations.
causal🗣 Rodrigo Liang, SambaNova CEOconviction: mediumStarlink
So what: Satellite connectivity expansion is a second-order tailwind for edge AI compute demand, worth tracking alongside chip and data center capex trends.
SambaNova has deliberately chosen not to build its own competing cloud service, unlike some other chip companies, instead partnering with infrastructure operators such as a new Neocloud venture called VC2 announced last month with Vista Equity Partners and Cambium, so it can focus capital and engineering purely on shipping chips, racks, and software.
thesis🗣 Rodrigo Liang, SambaNova CEOconviction: highSambaNovaVista EquityCambium
So what: A capital-efficiency choice that avoids competing with AWS-style hyperscalers directly, letting partners bear data-center capex while SambaNova captures hardware and software margin, a distinct business model versus vertically-integrated chip/cloud competitors.
Chip Market Structure and Inference Routing Economics 5
Liang predicts the AI infrastructure chip market will not fragment into many competitors but stabilize around roughly two to four dominant chip architectures, because further variety produces diminishing returns for data center operators.
prediction🗣 Rodrigo Liang, SambaNova CEOconviction: mediumSambaNovaNvidiaAMD
So what: Investors should expect consolidation rather than proliferation among AI chip vendors, favoring already-scaled players like Nvidia, AMD, and SambaNova over long-tail challengers.
“It's not going to be thousands of different versions because there's diminishing returns” ▶ 36:31
Liang argues Nvidia hardware has effectively become a commodity, since providers using it offer largely undifferentiated service and can only compete on modest cost advantages such as hardware discounts or government data-center subsidies.
contrarian🗣 Rodrigo Liang, SambaNova CEOconviction: highNvidia
So what: A notable claim from a rival chipmaker CEO that undercuts the idea that Nvidia GPU access alone is a durable moat for inference providers, implying service differentiation, not raw GPU access, determines winners.
“People forget as much as Nvidia costs is commodity.” ▶ 33:45
At many inference cloud providers, 70-80% of their existing, often Nvidia-based, rack fleet is now running inference workloads; SambaNova's pitch is to reroute that inference traffic onto SambaNova racks at a fraction of the cost and higher performance, freeing the Nvidia racks for other workloads like training or HPC and lifting the provider's overall margin without new hardware purchases.
thesis🗣 Rodrigo Liang, SambaNova CEOconviction: highSambaNovaNvidia
So what: This is SambaNova's core commercial wedge, selling margin uplift to existing Nvidia-heavy cloud and inference providers rather than requiring greenfield builds, a faster sales cycle than displacing incumbent infrastructure.
“the inference has arrived and you look at the percentage, 70 80% of those racks are running inference” ▶ 26:20
Inference providers commonly measure unit economics on a per-rack basis, where revenue equals tokens generated per second times price per token times uptime, compared against the operating cost of that rack, and SambaNova's value proposition is maximizing tokens-per-dollar on that calculation regardless of which model is deployed on the rack.
framework🗣 Rodrigo Liang, SambaNova CEOconviction: highSambaNova
So what: Gives investors a concrete KPI, revenue-per-rack via tokens/second times price/token, to evaluate inference infrastructure economics and compare providers' margin profiles.
Many inference service providers today generate substantial revenue but insufficient margin to be sustainable long-term, which is a central concern among the investors currently funding AI infrastructure companies.
data_point🗣 Rodrigo Liang, SambaNova CEOconviction: mediumSambaNova
So what: Signals a looming profitability and sustainability risk across the inference-cloud layer of the AI stack that investors should scrutinize before assuming current revenue growth translates into durable businesses.
Sovereignty, On-Prem Repatriation, and Enterprise Differentiation 6
Multiple countries, including Japan and Korea, are investing significant capital to train their own national AI models from scratch rather than relying on U.S.-based models, partly with SambaNova's help on training and inference infrastructure, driven by data-sovereignty and privacy concerns.
data_point🗣 Rodrigo Liang, SambaNova CEOconviction: highSambaNova
So what: National AI sovereignty is becoming a distinct, government-funded demand category for AI infrastructure vendors, a growth vector separate from hyperscaler or enterprise demand.
Enterprises are increasingly wary of sending proprietary data to third-party model providers after cases like the widely discussed Anthropic-Figma incident, where an internal actor was seen as having used access to closely replicate Figma's product, raising fears about competitive data leakage through AI vendors.
anecdote🗣 host (Molly O'Shea)conviction: mediumAnthropicFigma
So what: Concrete competitive-leakage fears are pushing enterprises toward private and on-prem inference deployments, a direct demand driver for sovereign and on-prem-capable hardware vendors like SambaNova.
On-premises infrastructure deployment is experiencing a resurgence after roughly a 20-year cycle dominated by public cloud migration, driven by enterprise demand for data privacy and security.
thesis🗣 Rodrigo Liang, SambaNova CEOconviction: highSambaNova
So what: Reinforces a cyclical 'on-prem is back' trade thesis relevant to enterprise infrastructure and hardware vendors that can serve on-prem AI deployments, not just cloud-native ones.
Liang argues that if most companies rely on the same commodity AI models, their competitive differentiation and pricing power will erode within about two years, since proprietary customers, IP, and data, not model access, are what previously let companies charge premium prices.
prediction🗣 Rodrigo Liang, SambaNova CEOconviction: highSambaNova
So what: A structural threat to enterprises treating AI purely as a cost-cutting tool; those that don't build proprietary or fine-tuned models risk margin compression as commodity-model usage becomes ubiquitous, favoring vendors enabling custom or private model training.
Liang advises enterprises that the goal of AI spending should not be to maximize token usage or throw money at AI, but to redirect existing costs toward AI to find efficiency gains and to use AI to open new revenue-generating services.
framework🗣 Rodrigo Liang, SambaNova CEOconviction: highSambaNova
So what: A practical operating framework investors can use to judge whether portfolio companies' AI spending is disciplined (efficiency and new-revenue focused) versus wasteful, undirected token consumption.
“throwing money at AI isn't really the goal” ▶ 48:27
Liang believes companies that fail to adopt and differentiate with AI risk losing competitive ground the way Blockbuster and Circuit City were displaced by companies like Netflix that embraced new technology-enabled business models.
framework🗣 Rodrigo Liang, SambaNova CEOconviction: mediumSambaNova
So what: Frames AI adoption speed as an existential competitive-positioning issue for incumbents across industries, not just a cost-optimization decision, a useful lens for assessing disruption risk in portfolio holdings.
What AI Leaders Really Think | Dylan Field, OpenAI, Boston Dynamics, MIT, IVP49 min22 insights2026-07-12
▶ Watch on YouTube
A rapid-fire series of hallway interviews at Figma's Config 2026 conference with Figma CEO Dylan Field, Boston Dynamics, OpenAI, MIT Media Lab, artist Holly Herndon, and IVP partner Shreyas surfaces a recurring theme: AI is making raw creative/coding output cheap, so hardware reliability, frontier-model intelligence, and human creative judgment are becoming the scarcer, more valuable differentiators.
AI Sycophancy and the Erosion of Independent Thinking · 3Boston Dynamics: Hardware's Durable Value Amid the AI Robotics Boom · 5IVP's Foundation-Model and Frontier-Intelligence Investment Thesis · 5OpenAI Product Design and the Future of Interfaces · 3Design Tooling Shift: Code as Material, Chat as Source of Truth · 3Generative AI Democratizing Music and Art · 3
AI Sycophancy and the Erosion of Independent Thinking 3
Figma CEO Dylan Field warns that AI models are sycophantic, praising a user's prompt as genius while actually feeding back the AI's own suggestion, which tricks users into adopting an idea they mistake for their own.
contrarian🗣 Dylan Field, Figma CEOconviction: highDylan FieldFigma
So what: As AI copilots proliferate across creative and coding tools, product teams should watch for user complacency/quality decay as a hidden risk of sycophantic model tuning.
≈ “it's telling you you're so great and such a genius but then kind of giving you what it wants to do and you're like oh yeah that's kind of good. I'll follow that.” paraphrase
Dylan Field argues AI should be used as a tool to learn and to offload non-critical work faster, but users must keep 'actually thinking' rather than letting the AI think for them, since giving up ownership of intent means the resulting output is no longer genuinely the user's idea.
framework🗣 Dylan Field, Figma CEOconviction: highDylan FieldFigma
So what: Signals Figma's product philosophy is likely to keep humans in the loop rather than fully autonomizing design, relevant for how AI-native design tools will be built and marketed.
“it is not enough to wear a thinking cap. You have to actually think.” ▶ 1:10
Zach Liverman, who runs the Future Sketches Group at MIT Media Lab, says writing, drawing, and coding are all forms of thinking, and if AI automates that thinking away, students reach outcomes faster but lose the learning that comes from creative friction, so educators need new frameworks for thoughtful AI use in the classroom.
framework🗣 Zach Liverman, MIT Media Lab (Future Sketches Group)conviction: highZach LivermanMIT Media Lab
So what: An emerging concern among both a major design-tool CEO (Dylan Field) and an academic institution (MIT Media Lab) about AI-driven skill atrophy could shape demand for 'process-preserving' AI education and design tools, a potential product category.
“Writing is thinking, drawing is thinking, coding is thinking.” ▶ 38:08
Boston Dynamics: Hardware's Durable Value Amid the AI Robotics Boom 5
Boston Dynamics interaction designer Brian Ringley directly rebuts the popular thesis that a single dominant AI 'brain' will commoditize robot hardware; he argues the opposite is happening because any AI intelligence still needs a durable, repeatable physical body, making hardware more valuable, not less, as the software layer explodes.
contrarian🗣 Brian Ringley, Boston Dynamicsconviction: highBoston DynamicsBrian Ringley
So what: Investors weighing robotics exposure between AI/software plays versus hardware/actuation plays should note that a senior Boston Dynamics designer sees hardware reliability as the durable moat, not a commodity layer.
“I think hardware is more important than ever by virtue of this software explosion.” ▶ 10:27
Boston Dynamics is already running commercial deployments of its robots with Hyundai for automotive part sequencing on production lines and is working toward the harder goal of general assembly.
data_point🗣 Brian Ringley, Boston Dynamicsconviction: highBoston DynamicsHyundai
So what: Concrete near-term commercialization proof point for humanoid/industrial robotics revenue in automotive manufacturing, a sector to watch for Boston Dynamics/Hyundai robotics scaling.
Boston Dynamics deliberately designs its industrial robots to visually read as 'a power tool' rather than mimicking a human, avoiding any implied agency, so that factory workers instantly understand it as a useful machine rather than something uncanny.
framework🗣 Brian Ringley, Boston Dynamicsconviction: highBoston Dynamics
So what: Illustrates a deliberate anti-anthropomorphism design strategy for industrial robot adoption, relevant to how robotics companies manage worker trust and regulatory/PR risk versus consumer-facing humanoid competitors.
“We're not trying to trick you into thinking it's a human.” ▶ 6:34
Boston Dynamics gave its Atlas humanoid robot a face and two degrees of freedom in the head specifically for human-robot interaction safety: if the robot looks at an approaching person it signals awareness and safe behavior, and if it looks at a part it signals predictable next movement.
framework🗣 Brian Ringley, Boston Dynamicsconviction: highBoston DynamicsAtlas
So what: A concrete design mechanism for building human trust/safety around humanoid robots operating alongside people, relevant to how regulators and factory operators will evaluate cohabitation risk.
Ringley expects humanoid robots to reach real-world deployment 'faster than you think,' drawing an analogy to Boston Dynamics' Spot quadruped, which went from being seen as pure exotic R&D to an unremarkable sight on the street within two to three years, though full-scale adoption could still take 10 to 20 years.
prediction🗣 Brian Ringley, Boston Dynamicsconviction: mediumBoston DynamicsSpotAtlas
So what: Provides an insider's non-hype adoption-curve timeframe (potentially a decade or two) for humanoid robots reaching everyday ubiquity, useful for calibrating robotics investment time horizons.
IVP's Foundation-Model and Frontier-Intelligence Investment Thesis 5
IVP partner Shreyas argues many VCs made a strategic error by missing the first wave of foundation-model winners (OpenAI, Anthropic) and are now compounding the mistake by chasing 'local maximum' copycats of that same category instead of identifying the genuinely next category of company to back.
contrarian🗣 Shreyas, partner at IVPconviction: highIVPOpenAIAnthropic
So what: A cautionary framework for allocators: chasing the last winning pattern (more foundation-model bets) may be lower-value than searching for the next distinct category, informs how to read the current wave of AI-adjacent VC deployment.
“a lot of investors missed out on the first wave of foundation models namely openthropic” ▶ 46:47
Despite a market shift toward cheaper open-source and specialized models driven by rising 'token maxing' bills, Shreyas of IVP believes frontier-model intelligence will retain a durable premium because certain high-stakes tasks, such as drug discovery, require the best available intelligence rather than a cheaper open-source substitute.
thesis🗣 Shreyas, partner at IVPconviction: highAnthropicIVP
So what: Suggests a bifurcated AI market where commodity open-source models serve low-stakes tasks while frontier labs like Anthropic keep pricing power on mission-critical, high-value applications — relevant to how investors value frontier-model companies versus open-source challengers.
“I think frontier intelligence will always be quite quite valuable.” ▶ 47:48
IVP has invested in Suno, a generative-music company that lets users with no musical training create music in multiple languages including Hindi and Spanish, and in Anthropic, the foundation-model company.
data_point🗣 Shreyas, partner at IVPconviction: highIVPSunoAnthropic
So what: Direct portfolio disclosure naming Suno and Anthropic as IVP's most recent AI bets, useful for tracking IVP's actual capital allocation versus stated thesis.
Shreyas is also invested in a company building AI protein models to help pharmaceutical companies optimize and develop drugs, which he cites as the clearest example of a task where only frontier-level intelligence, not cheaper open-source models, is acceptable given the stakes.
data_point🗣 Shreyas, partner at IVPconviction: highIVP
So what: Names a specific vertical (AI-driven drug discovery/protein modeling) IVP is backing as a proof point for the 'frontier intelligence commands a premium' thesis.
Shreyas observes that in board meetings, portfolio-company engineers now bring Figma prototypes rather than code demos to show the board what's coming next, implying code is becoming a purely functional back-end layer while design tooling like Figma has become the medium companies use to communicate and sell their product vision.
causal🗣 Shreyas, partner at IVPconviction: mediumFigmaIVP
So what: Suggests design/prototyping tools are gaining strategic mindshare in how companies plan and pitch product roadmaps, a dynamic relevant to Figma's competitive positioning versus pure code-generation tools.
OpenAI Product Design and the Future of Interfaces 3
Ian Silver, head of product design at OpenAI, says the team is investing in voice interaction and 'generative UI' as the next interface paradigm beyond chat, describing the space as still in its early phases but a significant future direction for how people interact with AI models.
prediction🗣 Ian Silver, Head of Product Design, OpenAIconviction: mediumOpenAIIan Silver
So what: A named OpenAI product leader flags generative UI and voice as the next battleground for AI interfaces beyond the chat box, worth tracking for what OpenAI ships next in ChatGPT.
This year's Apple Design Award-winning app was built by two designers with no engineers using OpenAI's Codex, which Ian Silver cites as evidence that AI coding tools are letting small, non-engineering teams ship award-caliber finished software products.
anecdote🗣 Ian Silver, Head of Product Design, OpenAIconviction: highOpenAICodexApple Design Award
So what: Concrete evidence of AI-coding tools compressing the team size needed to build award-winning consumer software, relevant to how startup team composition and hiring may shift.
“it wasn't like we just prompted codeex to do it, but it empowered us to like build our idea.” ▶ 20:43
Ian Silver argues the 'design is dead' narrative is wrong because as AI lets more people generate mediocre products cheaply, the bar for differentiated, human-crafted design actually rises, giving skilled designers a bigger competitive advantage, not a smaller one.
contrarian🗣 Ian Silver, Head of Product Design, OpenAIconviction: highOpenAI
So what: Counter-narrative to fears that AI eliminates design jobs: as AI raises the floor of average output, human design craft becomes the differentiator, relevant to how design-tool and creative-labor markets could evolve.
Design Tooling Shift: Code as Material, Chat as Source of Truth 3
Figma product manager Nico frames code inside Figma as raw exploratory 'material' rather than a production artifact, saying the goal of the tool is to help users generate hundreds of divergent ideas quickly so the best ones can be found, not to converge immediately on one polished implementation.
framework🗣 Nico, Product Manager, Figmaconviction: highFigmaNico
So what: Clarifies Figma's strategic bet on code-in-canvas as an ideation multiplier rather than a dev-handoff replacement, relevant to how Figma will be positioned against pure AI code generators.
“Figma's in the business of getting the bad ideas out so you can find the great ones.” ▶ 21:42
Nico predicts that design and engineering work will shift toward organizing around 'clusters of representations' of an idea rather than discrete single-source files, implying future design tools will manage many interconnected AI-generated variants instead of one canonical document.
prediction🗣 Nico, Product Manager, Figmaconviction: exploratoryFigma
So what: A forward-looking signal on how Figma's own roadmap may evolve away from the single-file paradigm toward multi-variant, AI-generated representations.
“I think that experts will work in clusters of representations, not files.” ▶ 28:44
Zach Liverman of MIT Media Lab noticed that during this year's Config keynote, presenters referenced going back to 'the chat' rather than 'the source code' when editing a design, suggesting the conversational LLM chat log may be functionally becoming the new source of truth for a product, replacing raw code as the canonical artifact.
causal🗣 Zach Liverman, MIT Media Labconviction: mediumMIT Media LabFigma
So what: If chat transcripts become the de facto source of truth over source code, that reshapes what version control, IP, and product documentation look like for AI-built software.
“maybe the source code is the chat now” ▶ 34:53
Generative AI Democratizing Music and Art 3
IVP partner Shreyas says Suno lets users with no musical training generate original, multi-language music (he specifically cites Hindi and Spanish output) that they genuinely enjoy, evidencing that generative-audio tools have moved beyond novelty into mainstream personal use.
data_point🗣 Shreyas, partner at IVPconviction: highSuno
So what: Firsthand user testimony from a VC backer supports Suno's product-market fit claim of enabling non-musicians to create usable, enjoyable original music across languages.
Artist Holly Herndon says she is restructuring her creative studio around AI agents and plans to keep scaling the number of agents she uses over the next 12 months, reflecting a broader shift of professional creative practices toward agentic AI workflows rather than traditional single-tool production.
thesis🗣 Holly Herndon, artistconviction: exploratoryHolly Herndon
So what: An artist-operator signal that creative production workflows are moving toward multi-agent orchestration, a trend relevant to tooling built for creative professionals rather than just developers.
Holly Herndon argues that building custom AI training datasets, such as recording and combining multiple vocalists into her own collectively-trained models, is an underexplored creative practice in its own right, not just a technical prerequisite to making AI art.
framework🗣 Holly Herndon, artistconviction: mediumHolly Herndon
So what: Reframes dataset curation itself as a monetizable creative craft/service, a niche worth watching as more artists seek differentiated, human-sourced training data versus generic scraped datasets.
Andrew Feldman on Building a Chip 58x Larger Than Nvidia's33 min22 insights2026-07-13
▶ Watch on YouTube
Cerebras CEO Andrew Feldman, speaking about two months post-IPO, argues AI compute demand has structurally outpaced data center buildout and defends Cerebras' $20B+ OpenAI hardware deal and 20x-faster wafer-scale inference architecture. He also warns that Nvidia is using its balance sheet to entrench chip lock-in via investments in neoclouds and model builders, and lays out a 'multiple choices at every stack layer' alternative to full-stack sovereign AI.
Data center buildout lagging AI compute demand · 3Cerebras' wafer-scale bet: OpenAI deal, fast inference, co-design · 5Nvidia's market power and chip dependency risk · 2Deal-making behind the AI compute headlines · 2Sovereign AI and full-stack strategy · 1AI's positive externalities: health, safety, education · 4Founder mindset and building enduring companies · 5
Data center buildout lagging AI compute demand 3
Data center construction has historically moved at 'the speed of real estate' (multi-year permitting and building), but AI compute demand exploded so quickly over the last two years that buildout is now structurally behind demand for compute and memory, making data center capacity a binding constraint on AI deployment.
causal🗣 Andrew Feldman (CEO, Cerebras)conviction: highdata centersAI compute
So what: Physical data center capacity, not just chip supply, may be the near-term bottleneck for deploying AI — permitting/construction timelines and power availability are leading indicators for effective compute supply.
“data centers had historically moved at sort of the speed of real estate” ▶ 12:04
Core data center backup infrastructure such as generators, batteries, chillers, and CDUs had gone essentially unchanged for about 20 years, but surging AI power demand is now driving rapid innovation, including use of fuel cells and jet-engine-derived turbines to generate on-site power for data centers.
data_point🗣 Andrew Feldman (CEO, Cerebras)conviction: highdata centers
So what: Power generation technology for data centers (fuel cells, turbines) is an emerging investable sub-theme within the AI infrastructure buildout.
“the generators that we use for backup have been unchanged for about 20 years” ▶ 13:19
Despite industry talk of putting data centers in space, Feldman believes that is more than five years away, though he notes Cerebras' single large wafer-scale chip sidesteps one of the hardest problems for space compute — getting many small chips to communicate with each other.
prediction🗣 Andrew Feldman (CEO, Cerebras)conviction: mediumCerebras
So what: Space-based data centers are not a near-term (sub-5-year) investable theme; architectures that minimize chip-to-chip interconnect could have an edge if/when it happens.
“I don't think we're in danger in the near term of having a data center in space.” ▶ 15:08
Cerebras' wafer-scale bet: OpenAI deal, fast inference, co-design 5
Cerebras signed a hardware supply deal with OpenAI announced in January, worth more than $20 billion over several years, which Feldman calls one of the biggest deals in Silicon Valley history.
data_point🗣 Andrew Feldman (CEO, Cerebras)conviction: highCerebrasOpenAI
So what: A multi-year, $20B+ compute supply commitment from OpenAI is a major revenue/backlog data point for Cerebras and validates demand for non-Nvidia inference hardware at scale.
≈ “we'll be doing more than 20 billion dollars of hardware for them over the next several years.” paraphrase
Cerebras positions its edge as inference speed roughly 20x faster than rivals, arguing that as AI models become broadly useful the bottleneck shifts from training compute to fast inference at deployment time.
thesis🗣 Andrew Feldman (CEO, Cerebras)conviction: highCerebras
So what: Inference speed/latency is a differentiated competitive axis versus raw training FLOPs — Cerebras is explicitly betting its business on being materially faster, not just cheaper or larger.
“the fast is not by a little bit but by 20x” ▶ 4:01
Cerebras' core product is a single wafer-scale chip described as 58x larger than competitors' chips, but it is packaged into a fridge-sized server enclosure with only a few units per standard data-center rack, so its physical deployment footprint is similar to competitors despite the radically different chip architecture.
data_point🗣 Andrew Feldman (CEO, Cerebras)conviction: highCerebrasNvidia
So what: Physical data-center integration is not a real barrier to adoption for Cerebras' unconventional architecture, removing a common objection to alternative chip designs.
≈ “It goes in a metal enclosure that's about the size of a fridge for a dorm room” paraphrase
Chip and software design are increasingly being co-designed simultaneously rather than sequentially through an operating-system abstraction layer; AI's scale and speed requirements are forcing hardware and software teams to negotiate trade-offs together, similar to how Google co-designs its TPU with its Gemini/DeepMind model teams and Cerebras leverages its OpenAI relationship to see where the model frontier is heading.
framework🗣 Andrew Feldman (CEO, Cerebras)conviction: highCerebrasGoogleDeepMindOpenAI
So what: Hardware-software co-design is becoming a competitive moat; assess whether chip vendors have deep design-partner relationships with frontier model labs versus selling into an arm's-length market.
“what changes could we make in software that would advantage the hardware or as we're designing the hardware what changes could we make that would make the software easier to run” ▶ 16:40
The biggest misconception about hardware/software co-design is that it's easy; in practice it requires painful trade-offs where making software easier to run makes hardware harder to build (and vice versa), creating real schedule and resourcing conflicts between hardware and software teams.
contrarian🗣 Andrew Feldman (CEO, Cerebras)conviction: highCerebras
So what: Co-design claims from chip vendors should be scrutinized — genuine co-design shows up as organizational friction and schedule trade-offs, not marketing slides.
≈ “anything you do to make it easier to write the software makes it harder to do the hardware” paraphrase
Nvidia's market power and chip dependency risk 2
Feldman is concerned Nvidia can use its balance sheet to limit competition by investing directly in neoclouds and AI model builders, which creates pressure on those companies not to adopt non-Nvidia chips.
contrarian🗣 Andrew Feldman (CEO, Cerebras)conviction: highNvidia
So what: Nvidia's equity investments in downstream AI infrastructure and model companies could function as an anticompetitive lever — watch for antitrust scrutiny of Nvidia's investment strategy.
≈ “there are ways frequently for Nvidia to exercise market strength” paraphrase
Feldman compares free or subsidized AI token/compute credits offered to startups to drug dealers giving free samples to build dependency, and advises startups to accept the free capacity but deliberately diversify across multiple chip suppliers (e.g., Cerebras and AMD) to avoid lock-in.
contrarian🗣 Andrew Feldman (CEO, Cerebras)conviction: highCerebrasAMDNvidia
So what: Startups should treat free compute/token offers as a dependency risk, not a free lunch — multi-vendor sourcing is a defensive signal investors should look for in portfolio companies' infrastructure choices.
“these are drug pushers. Here, little girl, try a little bit. Just a little bit.” ▶ 21:04
Deal-making behind the AI compute headlines 2
Feldman says headline multi-billion-dollar AI compute deals — citing SpaceX's announced deals with Google and Reflection as an example — often lack real substance, and that outsiders and even industry insiders frequently cannot tell what is actually being transacted.
contrarian🗣 Andrew Feldman (CEO, Cerebras)conviction: mediumSpaceXGoogle
So what: Investors should discount headline AI infrastructure deal announcements until firm commitments/terms are disclosed; not every announced partnership represents binding revenue.
“there've been announced some sort of deals that didn't have teeth” ▶ 18:58
xAI reportedly began leasing out excess GPU capacity, originally provisioned for its Grok model which saw lower-than-expected usage, to Anthropic and others, discovering that leasing scarce GPUs into a supply-constrained market was a lucrative business in itself.
anecdote🗣 Andrew Feldman (CEO, Cerebras)conviction: mediumGrokAnthropic
So what: Underutilized GPU capacity at AI labs is being monetized via compute resale/leasing — a secondary-market dynamic worth tracking as a source of compute supply for constrained buyers.
“they had available capacity because the Grock model wasn't used very much” ▶ 19:21
Sovereign AI and full-stack strategy 1
Responding to a comment from Alex Karp about companies owning the full AI stack, Feldman argues companies and nations don't need to own every layer but should maintain multiple supplier choices at each layer (chips, models) to avoid single-vendor dependence, while being careful never to give away proprietary data that would help a third party's model improve.
framework🗣 Andrew Feldman (CEO, Cerebras)conviction: highAlex KarpNvidia
So what: A practical alternative to 'own the full stack' is 'maintain choice at every layer plus protect proprietary data' — a useful lens for evaluating enterprise AI vendor strategy and moat durability.
“be sure you have multiple choices in different parts of the stack, but you get the credit for your data.” ▶ 24:04
AI's positive externalities: health, safety, education 4
Feldman states that eliminating cancer as a top cause of death — to the point that the next generation won't personally know anyone who died from it — is an achievable goal within roughly 25 years, citing a recent pancreatic cancer research breakthrough as a sign of accelerating progress.
prediction🗣 Andrew Feldman (CEO, Cerebras)conviction: mediumAI
So what: Frames AI-accelerated biomedical research (oncology specifically) on a decades-not-centuries timeline — relevant for long-horizon healthcare/biotech AI investment theses.
“we have a chance for our children or the next generation not only to not die from cancer but to not know anybody who died from cancer.” ▶ 0:30
Feldman argues self-driving vehicles will eliminate one of the largest causes of death for people roughly aged 15-40 (car accidents caused by human inattention or impairment), since machines already drive better than humans do today.
causal🗣 Andrew Feldman (CEO, Cerebras)conviction: highself-driving
So what: Reinforces autonomous driving as a major, near-achievable public-health-scale externality of AI, supporting long-term AV investment theses beyond convenience or labor-savings framing.
“machines can drive better than we can today” ▶ 26:29
Feldman frames the AI 'doomer' critique as one-sided because it weighs power consumption and risk without crediting concrete upside such as AI-enabled personalized tutoring, which could finally deliver individualized education at scale after millennia of one-size-fits-all classroom teaching.
contrarian🗣 Andrew Feldman (CEO, Cerebras)conviction: highAI
So what: Provides a 'weigh both sides of the ledger' framework useful for evaluating AI-in-education investment opportunities and countering purely risk-focused AI narratives.
“We've known for 2,000 years the right way to educate children. And we never do it ever.” ▶ 28:38
Feldman describes himself as bullish on peptides and longevity biology broadly rather than any single peptide, expecting big strides from GLP-1-class drugs and similar breakthroughs, and cites Brian Armstrong's company New Limit as an example of a startup aiming to eradicate disease.
thesis🗣 Andrew Feldman (CEO, Cerebras)conviction: mediumNew LimitBrian Armstrong
So what: Signals investor-adjacent enthusiasm for AI-accelerated longevity/biotech as an adjacent theme to core AI infrastructure investing.
Founder mindset and building enduring companies 5
Feldman attributes Cerebras' resilience to a 'chase the work, not the money' philosophy — people who kept building hardware even when it was unfashionable and unrewarded stay emotionally even-keeled through both downturns and hype cycles.
framework🗣 Andrew Feldman (CEO, Cerebras)conviction: highCerebras
So what: A qualitative signal for evaluating founder/team durability in cyclical deep-tech investing: teams motivated by the work itself tend to outlast those chasing valuation momentum.
“chasing money is not the path to money” ▶ 9:10
As of Cerebras' IPO, roughly 1,000 current and former employees became millionaires, and Feldman says the most noticeable post-IPO change has been a sharp increase in inbound requests for his time, meetings, and donations.
data_point🗣 Andrew Feldman (CEO, Cerebras)conviction: highCerebras
So what: Quantifies wealth creation at Cerebras' IPO (~1,000 employee millionaires) as a scale reference point for the current AI hardware IPO wave.
“At IPO is more. It was about a thousand.” ▶ 6:48
Feldman frames Cerebras' IPO as 'a plateau, not an endpoint' that provides more capital, credibility, and reach to pursue an ambitious roadmap of building more chips and data centers, rather than a terminal liquidity event.
thesis🗣 Andrew Feldman (CEO, Cerebras)conviction: highCerebras
So what: Signals continued aggressive capex and roadmap execution post-IPO rather than a slowdown — relevant for assessing Cerebras' forward growth trajectory.
≈ “getting to an IPO is not the end of a journey. It's sort of a plateau. It's sort of the arrival at corporate adulthood.” paraphrase
Cerebras was founded with five co-founders carried over from Feldman's prior company, and Feldman notes that 'five is almost every case too many founders,' while crediting them for shaping him as a leader.
anecdote🗣 Andrew Feldman (CEO, Cerebras)conviction: mediumCerebras
So what: Minor color on founder-team structure at Cerebras, useful context for evaluating founding-team dynamics at deep-tech startups.
“there are five of us. Five is in almost every case too many founders.” ▶ 31:15
Feldman credits two mentors for shaping his leadership: veteran venture capitalist Pierre Lamond, who invested in Cerebras and joined its board at age 84, and Mark Leslie, former CEO of Veritas, both of whom set extraordinarily high standards for him and others.
anecdote🗣 Andrew Feldman (CEO, Cerebras)conviction: mediumCerebrasPierre LamondMark LeslieVeritas
So what: Illustrates the role long-tenured, high-standards board members can play in guiding hardware startups through scale-up and IPO.

Odd Lots · Bloomberg 4 episode(s) · 98 insights

Why Fed Independence Is Hanging by a Thread | Odd Lots65 min23 insights2026-07-17
▶ Watch on YouTube
Columbia law professor Lev Menand and Fed-watcher Nathan Pincus join Odd Lots to dissect the Supreme Court's Trump v. Slaughter and Cook v. Trump rulings, which stripped removal protections from agencies like the FTC while carving out a Fed-specific exception based on a novel 'history and tradition' analogy to Alexander Hamilton and Second Amendment jurisprudence. They argue the carve-out is legally incoherent, an unstable 5-4 decision resting on just two justices, and vulnerable to future challenges that could separate Fed bank-regulatory powers from protected monetary policy, with broader implications for rule-of-law enforcement and unilateral executive fiscal action.
The Fed's New History-and-Tradition Carve-Out · 5A Fragile 5-4 Equilibrium · 4Bank Regulation and Monetary Policy Are Legally Inseparable · 3The Fed's Independence From Congress's Power of the Purse · 4Rule-of-Law Erosion Beyond the Fed · 3Why This Is Happening Now · 4
The Fed's New History-and-Tradition Carve-Out 5
In the Trump v. Slaughter decision, the Supreme Court eliminated the 1935 Humphrey's Executor precedent, ruling that the president can remove heads of independent multi-member agencies like the FTC without cause, reversing nearly a century of protection for officials with quasi-judicial or quasi-legislative functions.
data_point🗣 Lev Menand, guest (Columbia law professor)conviction: highSupreme CourtFTCHumphrey's Executor
So what: Removes a major legal check on presidential control over financial/economic regulators (SEC, FTC, CFTC), raising political-cycle policy volatility risk for regulated industries.
In a companion case protecting Fed governor Lisa Cook from removal, the Court carved out a Fed-specific exception by importing the 'history and tradition' test used in Second Amendment cases, asking whether a founding-era historical analog supports insulating the institution from at-will presidential removal.
framework🗣 Lev Menand, guest (Columbia law professor)conviction: highSupreme CourtLisa CookFederal ReserveSecond AmendmentRahimi
So what: Fed independence is now legally grounded in a novel, thinly-precedented doctrinal transplant rather than settled administrative law, making it more vulnerable to future reversal.
“Is there a history and tradition of this?” ▶ 0:13
The Court's historical justification for Fed independence rests on Alexander Hamilton's advocacy for 'independent monetary policy,' but the First Bank of the United States that Hamilton championed was a privately-owned commercial bank (the government held only about 20% of shares, later sold) with no executive-branch officers, not a government regulatory body, making it a weak legal analog for today's Federal Reserve Board.
contrarian🗣 Lev Menand, guest (Columbia law professor)conviction: highAlexander HamiltonFederal Reserve
So what: If courts later scrutinize this analog more closely, the entire constitutional basis for the current Fed carve-out could unravel.
“Alexander Hamilton didn't want a government bank.” ▶ 29:49
No dedicated government banking regulator existed at the founding, and general incorporation for state-chartered banks didn't appear until the late 1830s, so there is no genuine founding-era 'history and tradition' of an independent government bank regulator for the Court's Second-Amendment-style analogy to rest on.
contrarian🗣 Lev Menand, guest (Columbia law professor)conviction: highFederal ReserveSecond Amendment
So what: Strengthens the case that the Fed carve-out is doctrinally improvised rather than historically grounded, a vulnerability future litigants could exploit.
Before these rulings, the Federal Reserve legally had the weakest removal protections of any independent federal agency because its enabling act never defined 'cause' for removal; after Slaughter stripped protections from every other agency, the Fed now has the strongest protections of any agency by default, not by original design.
data_point🗣 Lev Menand, guest (Columbia law professor)conviction: highFederal ReserveFTC
So what: The Fed's newly outsized independence relative to peer agencies is an accident of litigation sequencing, undermining the coherence and durability of the rationale for protecting it.
“They've gone from the agency with the independent agency with the least removal protections to the most just by mowing down everyone else.” ▶ 50:08
A Fragile 5-4 Equilibrium 4
The Cook decision preserving Fed independence passed 5-4, but only two of nine justices (Roberts and Kavanaugh) actually endorse the substantive view that the Fed deserves different treatment than the FTC; the three liberal justices in the majority (Kagan, Sotomayor, Jackson) joined only because they believe the FTC itself remains constitutionally protected too, meaning they reject the Slaughter/Cook distinction altogether.
thesis🗣 Lev Menand, guest (Columbia law professor)conviction: highSupreme CourtJohn RobertsBrett KavanaughLisa Cook
So what: A doctrine backed by only 2 of 9 justices is a weak foundation for markets to price in permanent Fed independence.
“There are only two out of nine justices that, um, think the outcome makes sense.” ▶ 46:07
Because the Fed carve-out is substantively supported only by Roberts and Kavanaugh, both relatively young for the Court, the arrangement is a fragile, court-composition-dependent equilibrium unlikely to survive their eventual retirement or replacement, creating multi-decade legal tail risk to Fed independence.
prediction🗣 Lev Menand, guest (Columbia law professor)conviction: mediumJohn RobertsBrett KavanaughFederal ReserveSupreme Court
So what: Investors treating Fed independence as structurally permanent should instead treat it as a personnel-contingent bet tied to two justices' tenure.
Justice Clarence Thomas has publicly stated the Fed's removal protections should be eliminated entirely, signaling at least one existing vote against the current carve-out if the Court's composition or a future case revisits the question.
data_point🗣 Lev Menand, guest (Columbia law professor)conviction: highClarence ThomasFederal ReserveSupreme Court
So what: A concrete, named swing risk for anyone modeling the durability of Fed independence.
“Thomas has gone out and said, I think the fed has to go.” ▶ 46:57
Footnote 6 of the Cook opinion limits the Fed exception to the Fed 'as currently constituted and with its existing enforcement authorities,' deliberately avoiding a power-by-power analysis of whether each individual Fed function (bank supervision, capital rules, stress tests) is truly analogous to Hamilton's monetary-policy rationale, leaving room for future litigation to strip protection from specific Fed regulatory powers even while preserving core rate-setting.
framework🗣 Lev Menand, guest (Columbia law professor)conviction: mediumFederal ReserveSupreme CourtJohn RobertsClarence Thomas
So what: Bank regulatory independence (capital rules, stress tests) may be legally more exposed than headline rate-setting independence, worth tracking as a distinct risk factor for bank regulation stability.
“As the Federal Reserve is currently constituted and with its existing enforcement authorities.” ▶ 33:52
Bank Regulation and Monetary Policy Are Legally Inseparable 3
The Federal Reserve Board of Governors is itself a pure regulatory agency with no balance sheet or lending authority; actual money creation and lending happen at the regional Federal Reserve Banks (e.g., the New York Fed), which are membership cooperatives nominally owned by commercial banks such as JPMorgan Chase and sit formally outside the federal government.
framework🗣 Nathan Pincus, guest (Notes on the Crisis)conviction: highFederal ReserveJPMorgan Chase
So what: Clarifies that 'the Fed' investors discuss as one entity is legally split between a government regulator (the Board) and quasi-private operating banks (the Reserve Banks), a distinction that matters for future litigation targeting specific parts of the institution.
The Fed Board pursues its interest-rate mandate largely through bank regulation (capital requirements, reserve rules) rather than direct market transactions, meaning bank regulatory policy and monetary policy are functionally the same lever, undermining any future legal argument that only narrowly-defined 'monetary policy' deserves independence while bank supervision does not.
causal🗣 Lev Menand, guest (Columbia law professor)conviction: highFederal Reserve
So what: Bank capital/supervisory independence and rate-setting independence rise or fall together legally, so a threat to one is effectively a threat to both.
The Trump administration is currently relaxing bank equity capital requirements, which functions as de facto monetary stimulus even though it isn't officially framed that way, a live example of how the Fed's regulatory actions have immediate, monetary-policy-equivalent economic effects.
data_point🗣 Lev Menand, guest (Columbia law professor)conviction: mediumFederal ReserveTrump administration
So what: Watch bank capital-rule changes as a real-time, less-scrutinized channel of monetary easing that runs alongside the more visible policy-rate decision.
“This is stimulative. This is monetary stimulus. That's not why they're doing it, but it is stimulating the economy right now.” ▶ 33:29
The Fed's Independence From Congress's Power of the Purse 4
The Federal Reserve Board of Governors funds its own operations not through congressional appropriations but by levying assessments on the regional Federal Reserve Banks; because those Banks create money, an assessment effectively directs them to create new credit and deposit it into the Board's own account, giving the Board de facto unlimited operating-budget autonomy from Congress, OMB, and the White House.
data_point🗣 Nathan Pincus, guest (Notes on the Crisis)conviction: highFederal Reserve
So what: This funding mechanism is a structural pillar of Fed independence separate from the removal-protection fight; a unitary-executive-controlled Fed with this funding autonomy plus at-will removal power over other agencies would concentrate unusual fiscal-adjacent authority in the presidency.
“They're essentially directing the Federal Reserve banks to create money and credit the Federal Reserve Board's bank account.” ▶ 35:47
Menand argues the strongest constitutional case for Fed independence isn't the Court's Hamiltonian 'history and tradition' theory but that Fed operations (rate-setting, sector-targeted lending) implicate Congress's exclusive constitutional power of the purse, since instruments like non-recourse loans (used during Covid and the Silicon Valley Bank rescue) can function as disguised fiscal allocations that only Congress should control.
thesis🗣 Lev Menand, guest (Columbia law professor)conviction: mediumFederal ReserveAlexander HamiltonSilicon Valley Bank
So what: A power-of-the-purse framing, if ever adopted, would ground Fed independence in a sturdier constitutional principle than the current Hamilton-bank analogy, worth watching for in future litigation strategy.
“The sword is in one hand and the purse is in the other.” ▶ 38:56
The Court likely avoided adopting a power-of-the-purse rationale for Fed independence because it would logically extend removal protections to other budget-related offices too, citing Bowsher v. Synar (1986), where the Court struck down removal protection for a Comptroller General role created to enforce deficit-reduction spending caps, as the controlling precedent against that approach.
causal🗣 Lev Menand, guest (Columbia law professor)conviction: mediumSupreme CourtBowsher v. Synar
So what: Explains why the Court chose the narrower, more doctrinally awkward Hamilton-history rationale over a broader and arguably more defensible purse-power argument, a sign the current doctrine was chosen for its narrowness, not its coherence.
Non-recourse lending, where a loan is issued far above the current market value of pledged collateral and functions effectively as a purchase, gives the Fed (via the New York Fed) an extraordinarily powerful, largely unchecked tool to direct fiscal-like support to specific assets or sectors without congressional appropriation; Menand flags this as under-scrutinized and warns it could, under a unitary-executive-controlled Fed, be used to accept unconventional collateral.
contrarian🗣 Nathan Pincus, guest (Notes on the Crisis)conviction: mediumFederal Reserve
So what: Non-recourse lending facilities are a stealth channel of fiscal policy that could become a flashpoint if Fed independence weakens further, a mechanism worth monitoring in future crisis-response facilities.
“There's nothing on the books that could prevent a unitary executive fed from making non-recourse loans or where they're putatively accepting it as collateral for Trump coins.” ▶ 39:49
Rule-of-Law Erosion Beyond the Fed 3
As removal protections erode across agencies, courts' practical ability to enforce compliance with their rulings is also eroding: a series of unexplained 'shadow docket' Supreme Court rulings have redirected plaintiffs challenging allegedly illegal executive actions away from injunctive relief, which could stop the conduct in real time, toward the Court of Federal Claims, where they can only recover money damages after the fact.
causal🗣 Nathan Pincus, guest (Notes on the Crisis)conviction: highSupreme CourtTrump administration
So what: Illegal executive action can proceed largely unchecked in real time even when courts eventually rule against it, since injunctive relief is being systematically foreclosed, a broader governance risk beyond just the Fed.
