If the president can fire any official who tries to comply with a court order, then effectively 'the law is what Trump says,' a functional breakdown of the Marbury v. Madison (1803) principle that courts can compel the executive branch to follow the law.
contrarian🗣 Nathan Pincus, guest (Notes on the Crisis)conviction: highMarbury v. MadisonTrump administration
So what: Signals a structural shift in how enforceable U.S. regulatory and legal commitments are, relevant to any investment thesis that assumes courts can reliably check executive overreach.
“The law is what Trump says.” ▶ 58:08
As a hypothetical illustrating unilateral fiscal-policy risk, the panel notes a president could theoretically fire all IRS enforcement staff to effectively halt tax collection, an unconstitutional stimulus by executive fiat, and concludes the debt ceiling is effectively the only remaining legal backstop against this kind of maneuver, though its practical effectiveness is itself uncertain absent removal protections for IRS officials.
exploratory🗣 Joe Weisenthal, hostconviction: lowIRSTrump administrationCongress
So what: Frames the debt ceiling as an underappreciated structural check on executive fiscal overreach, relevant to debates about eliminating or reforming it.
Why This Is Happening Now 4
The 'unitary executive' theory driving these rulings originates from decades of Federalist Society legal theorizing explicitly aimed at dismantling the administrative state (the 'fourth branch of government'), and has gained new political momentum by merging with a newly radicalized right-wing tech constituency angered by SEC enforcement under Gary Gensler and FTC enforcement under Lina Khan against crypto and tech industries.
thesis🗣 Nathan Pincus, guest (Notes on the Crisis)conviction: highFederalist SocietyGary GenslerLina KhanTrump administration
So what: Explains the political coalition (legal theorists plus tech/crypto interests) sustaining momentum for further deregulatory litigation against agencies like the SEC and FTC, a trend investors in crypto and fintech should track.
Post-Volcker-shock academic economics developed a consensus that central bank independence is essential for monetary credibility, and the US government and IMF spent decades persuading other countries to legally insulate their central banks from finance ministries, creating an awkward dissonance now that US courts are treating the domestic legal basis for that same independence as constitutionally fragile.
causal🗣 Lev Menand, guest (Columbia law professor)conviction: highFederal ReserveIMF
So what: A credibility gap between what the US preaches abroad about central bank independence and what its own courts are willing to legally guarantee at home could affect how global markets price US institutional risk relative to peer central banks.
In 2020-2021, CDC independence from White House political pressure arguably mattered as much or more for public welfare than Federal Reserve independence does for markets, involving 'hundreds of thousands, potentially millions' of lives, yet the CDC never received comparable legal removal protections, evidence that which agencies get insulated from presidential control is a matter of historical happenstance rather than principled design.
contrarian🗣 Nathan Pincus, guest (Notes on the Crisis)conviction: mediumCDCFederal Reserve
So what: Undercuts the idea that Fed independence reflects a coherent constitutional philosophy about which functions deserve insulation, reinforcing that its current protection is legally arbitrary and thus more contestable long-term.
“It's not at all clear that the independence of the Federal Reserve was more important than the independence of the CDC.” ▶ 19:52
The Federal Reserve Board's structure traces back to the Interstate Commerce Commission (created 1887), which Congress designed as a specialized quasi-judicial body to regulate railroads because generalist federal judges couldn't handle complex technical economic disputes; the Fed (created 1913) was explicitly modeled on this ICC template for banking, not derived from any distinct monetary-policy theory of central bank independence.
data_point🗣 Lev Menand, guest (Columbia law professor)conviction: highFederal ReserveInterstate Commerce Commission
So what: Reframes the Fed's institutional design as an accident of 19th-century administrative-law history, like railroad regulation, rather than a purpose-built monetary institution, relevant context for evaluating how 'special' the Fed's legal status really should be.
Why AI Might Actually Create More Work for Lawyers | Odd Lots56 min26 insights2026-07-13
▶ Watch on YouTube
Gary Wingens, chair of law firm Lowenstein Sandler, argues AI is cutting the cost of individual units of legal work (e.g., a due-diligence project fell 70% in cost) while a Jevons-paradox dynamic expands total legal work volume, keeps hourly rates rising, and leaves firms still deciding on tech stack, training, and pricing as AI vendors shift from flat to token-based pricing.
Billable hours vs. project pricing · 4AI as efficiency tool vs. thought partner · 6Jevons paradox in legal services · 4Legal AI tech stack: Harvey, security, and build-vs-buy · 5Talent, training, and firm culture · 4In-house vs. outside counsel · 1Malpractice risk and regulatory posture reversal · 2
Billable hours vs. project pricing 4
Billable-hour pricing in law has persisted for decades despite universal complaints because clients don't fully trust project/value-based quotes and prefer hourly billing so they can see exactly what work was done, according to Lowenstein Sandler chair Gary Wingens.
causal🗣 Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandlerconviction: highLowenstein Sandlerbillable hours
So what: Explains why alternative fee arrangements have stalled industry-wide for 15+ years despite constant client and lawyer complaints — a structural friction investors in legal-tech should account for.
“I have never met a client who has come to me asking to buy billable hours.” ▶ 5:43
Average hourly rates at the largest U.S. law firms rose 10.1% in 2025 per data published by The American Lawyer, with the top 20 firms by profitability rising even faster, far outpacing roughly 3% CPI inflation that year.
data_point🗣 Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandlerconviction: highAmerican LawyerLowenstein Sandler
So what: A concrete, sourced data point showing elite law firm pricing power is accelerating even as AI compresses hours worked — a signal that top-tier legal services are not being commoditized by AI, unlike lower-value legal work.
“average hourly rates in 2025 went up by 10.1% at the largest firms in United States, and at the top, the top 20 firms by profitability, they went up even more” ▶ 46:47
Gary Wingens expects hourly billing rates at top-tier law firms to keep rising faster than in the past because AI tools make each billable hour more productive and valuable, even as the total price clients pay for a given deal or matter falls due to fewer hours being needed.
prediction🗣 Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandlerconviction: mediumLowenstein Sandlerbillable hours
So what: Implies a bifurcation investors should track: per-hour law firm pricing power rising even as total client legal spend per matter falls — margin dynamics differ from a simple 'AI destroys legal fees' narrative.
Wingens believes AI-driven cost transparency and reduced outcome uncertainty could finally push the legal industry toward project- or outcome-based pricing, a shift that has failed to take hold for the past 15 years of 'alternative fee arrangement' pushes.
prediction🗣 Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandlerconviction: lowLowenstein Sandlerbillable hours
So what: A potential structural shift in how legal services are priced and sold, relevant to anyone evaluating legal-tech vendors selling outcome- or workflow-based pricing models.
AI as efficiency tool vs. thought partner 6
AI is changing corporate legal work in two distinct ways: as an efficiency tool (comparable to black-lining software or Microsoft Word) that lowers the cost of producing a given work product, and, more novel, as a 'thought partner' or copilot that improves the substantive quality of the legal work itself from the outset — a function prior technology waves never provided.
framework🗣 Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandlerconviction: highLowenstein Sandler
So what: A reusable framework for evaluating any legal-AI product: does it just cut costs (a commodity play) or does it upgrade output quality (a defensible, harder-to-replicate value proposition)?
A due-diligence project reviewing thousands of trust agreements had its cost quoted down by roughly 70% using AI for the first review pass (with lawyers doing QC), turning a project the client had rejected as too expensive (roughly $10 million in the firm's illustrative figures) into one worth doing (roughly $3 million), generating revenue that otherwise would have been zero.
data_point🗣 Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandlerconviction: highLowenstein Sandler
So what: A concrete, quantified case of AI unlocking previously uneconomical legal work rather than just shrinking existing revenue — direct evidence for the Jevons-paradox thesis in legal services.
“when the price came down by 70%, they said, oh yeah, at that price, we'll do it” ▶ 30:42
One Lowenstein Sandler tax partner uses Claude to stress-test and generate new international tax-structuring ideas for family-office clients, in an iterative loop where the AI's output is then handed to an associate to research and validate — producing structures Wingens says neither the AI nor the human would have arrived at alone.
anecdote🗣 Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandlerconviction: mediumClaudeLowenstein Sandler
So what: Illustrates a human-in-the-loop workflow pattern (AI ideation -> human validation) that specialized professional-services firms are using in practice, relevant to how AI augments (not replaces) senior expert judgment.
Lowenstein Sandler patent lawyers using an AI tool for over six months report the primary benefit is a better patent application, because the AI brings cross-disciplinary scientific and engineering knowledge beyond an individual patent attorney's own specialty (e.g., a biologist's perspective added to a software patent).
anecdote🗣 Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandlerconviction: mediumLowenstein Sandler
So what: Suggests AI's edge in specialized professional work may come less from speed and more from synthesizing cross-domain knowledge no single human expert holds — a differentiator that's harder to commoditize.
Some of Lowenstein Sandler's most sophisticated clients now send the firm AI-generated first drafts of contracts, complaints, or answers for the firm to refine, while certain patent clients use their own AI tools to review the firm's AI-assisted patent work — meaning client-side and firm-side AI agents are effectively interacting with each other.
anecdote🗣 Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandlerconviction: mediumLowenstein Sandler
So what: Points to an emerging workflow where legal work is increasingly AI-to-AI mediated before human lawyers touch it, with implications for how legal-tech vendors should design interoperability and review layers.
Wingens does not believe current AI models can invent an entirely new structured-finance product (e.g., a novel ABS or CMBS structure) from scratch, though they can meaningfully assist expert lawyers in testing and refining structuring theories they already have in mind.
contrarian🗣 Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandlerconviction: mediumLowenstein Sandler
So what: A useful boundary condition for AI hype in structured finance: treat current models as validators/co-pilots for expert-generated ideas, not as autonomous financial-engineering inventors.
“I don't, I don't I don't believe that you can come up with a new structure.” ▶ 14:33
Jevons paradox in legal services 4
Gary Wingens confirms a Jevons-paradox dynamic in legal services: cutting the cost of a specific legal task (such as reviewing trust agreements) can turn previously uneconomical work economical, expanding the total volume of legal work performed rather than simply shrinking law-firm revenue per matter.
thesis🗣 Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandlerconviction: highLowenstein SandlerJevons paradox
So what: Core thesis for evaluating legal-tech and law-firm economics: falling per-unit AI-driven costs may grow the addressable market for legal services rather than compress law firm revenue.
If AI-driven efficiency brings electronic-discovery costs down from roughly $2 million to $200,000 per matter, it would make sophisticated litigation dramatically cheaper to file — a change Wingens frames as good for access to justice (e.g., civil-rights plaintiffs) but also likely to increase overall litigation volume, including from large corporates that previously abandoned claims as uneconomical.
prediction🗣 Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandlerconviction: mediumLowenstein SandlerJevons paradox
So what: A specific, tradeable-adjacent forecast: falling e-discovery costs could expand litigation volume industry-wide, a tailwind for litigation-focused law firms and legal-tech e-discovery vendors alike.
“If we can bring, say, 2 million of discovery costs down to 200,000, it does change the calculus for, both. The good news lawsuits are a lot more economical to file.” ▶ 0:23
A Lowenstein Sandler patent client whose engineers now use AI internally has seen the number of inventions and internal patent requests quadruple, which Wingens expects to offset the falling per-application cost of patent prosecution with much higher total application volume.
data_point🗣 Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandlerconviction: mediumLowenstein Sandler
So what: Concrete evidence that AI-driven R&D productivity gains at clients can flow through as higher demand for downstream legal/IP services, a second-order beneficiary effect of enterprise AI adoption.
“they're seeing a quadrupling in the number of inventions that they're that their engineers and scientists are coming up with, and the number of patent requests that they're getting internally from their engineering team has quadrupled” ▶ 32:57
Host Joe Weisenthal argues the Jevons paradox could apply broadly to bureaucracy: AI-armed insurers already push back against claims while AI-armed claimants push back against insurers, risking an escalating volume of automated back-and-forth that consumes rather than saves aggregate effort.
contrarian🗣 Joe Weisenthal, hostconviction: mediumJevons paradox
So what: Warns that AI-driven productivity gains in adversarial, high-friction domains (insurance, litigation, HR) may not translate into net productivity at the economy level, a caution against assuming AI = pure efficiency gain.
“the future could just be a bunch of bots, like talking to each other and filing different claims” ▶ 3:11
Legal AI tech stack: Harvey, security, and build-vs-buy 5
Lowenstein Sandler is primarily a 'Harvey shop,' with almost all of its lawyers using the Harvey legal-AI platform regularly, alongside Microsoft Copilot and the Office/Outlook suite, rather than lawyers interacting directly with frontier models like Claude, ChatGPT, or Gemini.
data_point🗣 Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandlerconviction: highHarveyMicrosoft CopilotClaudeChatGPTGeminiLowenstein Sandler
So what: Direct evidence of enterprise legal-AI vendor adoption patterns at a mid-size big-law firm, useful for tracking Harvey's competitive position against direct frontier-model access and rivals like CoCounsel/Westlaw.
The core value application layers like Harvey add over calling a frontier model directly is a more robust security and ethics layer — controlling where client data goes (including offline sessions), preventing loss of attorney-client privilege that can occur when lawyers use consumer-grade Claude or ChatGPT, plus shareable internal 'playbooks' and a legal-tuned retrieval-augmented-generation layer for tasks like finding supporting quotes across deposition transcripts.
framework🗣 Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandlerconviction: highHarveyClaudeChatGPT
So what: Clarifies the actual moat for vertical legal-AI application layers versus raw frontier-model access: security/privilege protection and shared institutional knowledge, not just model quality.
Wingens does not believe Lowenstein Sandler, or even a much larger firm like Kirkland & Ellis, will train its own foundation model from scratch, citing Kirkland's reported plan to spend roughly $500 million over five years on AI infrastructure and estimating training a frontier model costs on the order of $1.5 billion — instead the firm plans to build customized playbooks and fine-tuned layers on top of existing models.
data_point🗣 Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandlerconviction: highKirkland & EllisLowenstein Sandler
So what: Sets a cost benchmark showing why even large law firms will remain customers of frontier-model providers and application layers rather than competitors, supporting continued demand for platforms like Harvey.
“Does a Kirkland announce they're spending $500 million over five years, right. It costs something like a billion and a half dollars to train a model.” ▶ 49:24
Enterprise legal-AI pricing has so far been offered as unlimited 'all you can eat' plans, but vendors are shifting to token-based pricing — Wingens notes Claude is doing this first — and law firms don't yet know the true, unsubsidized cost of their AI usage, creating risk of a 'sticker shock' repricing once outside-investor-subsidized pricing normalizes.
contrarian🗣 Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandlerconviction: mediumClaudeLowenstein Sandler
So what: A direct warning for anyone modeling legal-tech or AI-vendor margins: current enterprise AI pricing may be subsidized and due for a repricing event that could reverse some of the cost savings firms are currently booking.
“we don't know the true cost, especially if we, you know, kind of hit this, energy grid wall” ▶ 51:19
Wingens' own Claude usage cost $36 across the first three business days of July, which he characterizes as trivial relative to the value of billable time, illustrating how small current marginal AI usage costs look under today's (possibly subsidized) pricing.
anecdote🗣 Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandlerconviction: mediumClaude
So what: A real, if small, individual data point on current AI usage costs at a law firm, useful as an anchor before comparing against future token-based pricing once subsidies fade.
“so far this month I've my use of Claude has used up $36” ▶ 51:47
Talent, training, and firm culture 4
Lowenstein Sandler has not reduced hiring of summer associates or first-year lawyers and has already filled its summer-associate class for 2027, though the firm says it is shifting the skill set it screens candidates for.
data_point🗣 Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandlerconviction: highLowenstein Sandler
So what: Direct counter-evidence, from inside a big-law firm, against the narrative that AI is already shrinking junior legal hiring — relevant to assessing law-school employment and junior-associate labor market fears.
Junior-lawyer training at Lowenstein Sandler is shifting away from tedious hands-on tasks like manual black-lining toward AI-assisted, simulator-style training where trainees use AI tools live during sessions and gain access to the firm's full historical database of negotiated deal terms instead of relying on informal hallway knowledge-sharing.
framework🗣 Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandlerconviction: highLowenstein Sandler
So what: A concrete model of how AI is restructuring professional apprenticeship in law, relevant to any professional-services firm rethinking how it trains junior staff once routine work is automated.
Wingens is genuinely unsure whether removing repetitive 'grunt work' (the legal equivalent of a musician practicing scales) will impair young lawyers' development of the attention to detail and judgment that the old apprenticeship model built, calling it an unresolved behavioral-psychology problem for the profession.
exploratory🗣 Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandlerconviction: lowLowenstein Sandler
So what: Flags a genuine, unresolved talent-development risk in AI-augmented professional services that could affect the long-term quality pipeline of expert judgment across knowledge industries, not just law.
Wingens suggests that rising 'superstar' lawyer compensation (comparable to sports-style signing bonuses) may partly reflect firms paying to capture a top lawyer's proprietary deal knowledge into firm-wide AI systems and playbooks, since AI tools only add value if partners are willing to contribute their expertise — creating a cultural tension at highly autonomous law firms between individual partner independence and firm-wide AI value creation.
thesis🗣 Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandlerconviction: mediumLowenstein Sandler
So what: Identifies a non-obvious driver of rising elite professional-services compensation and a real alignment risk for any firm trying to build AI knowledge systems on top of autonomous, highly compensated experts.
In-house vs. outside counsel 1
Wingens expects large clients with dedicated in-house legal teams to keep more routine, high-volume legal work in-house as AI automates it — citing how most banks already brought ISDA and derivatives documentation in-house — while outside firms retain value where broad, cross-client market knowledge and independent perspective matter most.
prediction🗣 Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandlerconviction: mediumLowenstein Sandler
So what: A structural forecast for how AI could reshape the outside-counsel business model, shifting commoditized routine work toward in-house teams while premium, market-breadth advisory work stays with law firms.
Malpractice risk and regulatory posture reversal 2
Malpractice insurers (Lowenstein Sandler works with underwriters out of Lloyd's of London) have flipped their posture on AI in roughly two years — from asking whether lawyers were being allowed to use AI at all (a risk concern) to now asking whether lawyers are being required to use AI properly, mirroring how corporate clients went from banning AI use 18 months ago to now demanding it to cut costs.
causal🗣 Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandlerconviction: highLloyd's of LondonLowenstein Sandler
So what: Signals that AI use in legal work has crossed from optional/risky to an expected baseline in under two years, which should accelerate legal-AI vendor adoption curves and shift malpractice liability norms toward non-use of AI being the riskier choice.
Despite widespread AI adoption, fabricated ('hallucinated') case citations slipping into court filings remain a real and embarrassing risk that Wingens says has no excuse given the verification tools now available, underscoring that AI legal output still requires mandatory human double-checking.
causal🗣 Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandlerconviction: highLowenstein Sandler
So what: A persistent operational risk for any firm or legal-tech vendor deploying generative AI in filings — verification/QC layers remain a mandatory cost center, limiting how far headcount reduction can go.
“there was no excuse for filing a brief or pleading in a court that cites, you know, made up hallucinated cases. They're just embarrassing.” ▶ 42:03
Why Soccer Analytics Works Like Volatility Arbitrage Trading | Odd Lots56 min21 insights2026-07-16
▶ Watch on YouTube
Odd Lots hosts Joe Weisenthal and Tracy Alloway talk with Austin FC's Mike Treacy (ex-PEAK6 volatility trader, now head of risk at Apex Fintech Solutions) and soccer analytics consultant Joris Bekkers about how soccer analytics has evolved from on-ball event data to petabyte-scale tracking and skeletal body-pose data, and why the discipline structurally resembles volatility trading, portfolio allocation, and derivatives risk management more than baseball-style Moneyball statistics.
Volatility trading maps onto soccer analytics · 3Data stack evolution: on-ball to tracking to skeletal · 4Handling noise: randomness, referees, and data censoring · 4The model-to-coach translation bottleneck · 2Quantifying player value: Bellingham and win-probability models · 2Data ownership and the big-club vs small-club edge · 2Optimization risk and Goodhart's Law in soccer · 2Sell-side World Cup forecasts don't hold up · 2
Volatility trading maps onto soccer analytics 3
Mike Treacy argues soccer analytics resembles volatility trading more than traditional sports statistics because every layer of the sport — individual player performance, team seasonal outcomes, and promotion/relegation finances — is distributional and highly levered rather than cleanly predictive like baseball.
framework🗣 Mike Treacyconviction: highMike TreacyApex Fintech SolutionsPEAK6soccer analyticsvolatility trading
So what: Frames soccer player and club valuation as an options/variance-pricing problem rather than a point-estimate forecasting problem, implying valuation models should price distributions and tail outcomes, not just expected performance.
“Soccer is unique because I'd say every aspect of the game is distribution.” ▶ 8:18
Mike Treacy applies a derivatives-trading framework of 'expected outcome versus realized outcome' to in-game soccer analysis: teams form a pre-match expected outcome for how each side will play, then reconcile that against the realized in-game data feed to make halftime adjustments.
framework🗣 Mike Treacyconviction: highMike Treacy
So what: A reusable mental model for any domain with a pre-event forecast and a live data feed — the value is in the gap between expected and realized, not either number alone.
“In the derivatives world, I always think of expected outcome versus realized outcome.” ▶ 25:09
Mike Treacy is applying an explicit portfolio-allocation framework to Austin FC's roster construction in MLS: because the league's salary-cap 'charge' for a player can diverge sharply from actual salary (a Designated Player like Lionel Messi can earn over $20 million while carrying a cap charge as low as roughly $750,000), building a roster becomes an asset-allocation problem across differently-priced 'slots' rather than simple spending.
thesis🗣 Mike Treacyconviction: exploratoryMike TreacyAustin FCMLSLionel Messi
So what: MLS's cap-charge mechanics create relative-value mispricings between a player's cap cost and market talent, similar to structuring a portfolio around risk budgets rather than notional dollar amounts — Austin FC is a live test case since Treacy admits the club is only nine months into proving the thesis works in the standings.
“I'm going to give you $10 million. You can spend $2 million on Nvidia, $7 million on Walmart and $1 million on a speculative biotech stock.” ▶ 35:34
Data stack evolution: on-ball to tracking to skeletal 4
The 2026 FIFA World Cup is projected to generate more than 90 petabytes of match-based data across its 104 matches, a roughly 45-fold increase over the data volume produced at the 2022 World Cup, according to a stat host Tracy Alloway cited from law firm Morgan Lewis.
data_point🗣 Tracy Allowayconviction: medium2026 FIFA World CupMorgan Lewis
So what: Illustrates the scale of the sports-data infrastructure buildout (storage, compute, and AI tooling) now required to process a single major tournament, relevant to vendors and infrastructure providers serving the sports-data market.
“104 matches generating more than 90PB of data... a 45 fold increase over the volume produced in the last World Cup in 2022.” ▶ 4:01
Soccer analytics data has evolved through three increasingly dense layers: on-ball event data (a spreadsheet-style log of tagged actions like pass, dribble, shot and goal with x/y coordinates and timestamps), positional tracking data (x/y coordinates for every player and the ball at 10-25 frames per second), and skeletal body-pose data (roughly 27 body keypoints per player, such as shoulders, ears, feet and ankles), with each layer an order of magnitude richer than the last.
framework🗣 Joris Bekkersconviction: highJoris Bekkers
So what: Explains where the sport's petabyte-scale data volumes actually come from and signals that body-pose data is the current frontier where the largest analytical edge is likely to emerge next.
Expected goals (xG) is a flawed and incomplete metric because it only registers value at the moment of a shot, ignoring possession value, the threat generated by build-up play, match momentum, and changing tactical styles — which is why analysts have moved to models measuring scoring probability across an entire possession rather than just at the shot.
contrarian🗣 Joris Bekkersconviction: highJoris Bekkersexpected goals
So what: Investors or bettors relying on published xG figures as the primary soccer analytics signal are working with an outdated, incomplete metric; possession-value and threat models capture more of the true picture.
“There is much more to the game than simply which shots occur.” ▶ 14:01
Modern soccer analytics traces to two catalysts in the 2010s rather than a single Moneyball-style moment: the 2013 book 'The Numbers Game' by Chris Anderson and David Sally, which reframed soccer as a 'weakest link' game, and a Twitter-driven expected-goals charting movement led by analyst Michael Caley, who posted xG charts for every Premier League match each Saturday.
data_point🗣 Joris Bekkersconviction: mediumJoris BekkersChris AndersonDavid SallyMichael CaleyPremier League
So what: Shows the discipline's public credibility was built bottom-up through open online discourse rather than top-down club adoption, a pattern relevant to how any niche analytics community can gain mainstream traction.
Handling noise: randomness, referees, and data censoring 4
Analysts deliberately strip anomalous game states out of training data before computing per-90-minute player statistics; the example given is Chelsea forward Nicolas Jackson scoring three goals in a single match after Tottenham went down to nine men and pushed a high defensive line — a game worth roughly 20% of his season goal total that is excluded so it doesn't distort his true per-90 output.
anecdote🗣 Mike Treacyconviction: highMike TreacyChelseaTottenhamNicholas Jackson
So what: Anyone valuing a player off per-90 statistics (for transfer pricing or fantasy/betting markets) should check for game-state outliers, since a single freak match can meaningfully inflate a seasonal average used to price a player.
“This three goals a game for them, I tell you, it was a it was a rich it was a rich fountain of bad data.” ▶ 20:16
Analysts use a discrete 'game state' variable (scored from -3 to +3 based on goal differential) to isolate genuine effort and positioning quality by comparing how a player performs at a near-certain win probability (e.g. roughly 98%) versus a close game state, functioning as a quantitative proxy for old-school scouting concepts like 'hustle' or 'grit'.
framework🗣 Mike Treacyconviction: mediumMike Treacy
So what: Offers a template for converting a vague qualitative trait ('this guy has grit') into a measurable, comparable statistic conditioned on situational context — applicable beyond sports to any performance evaluation problem.
Predictive win models for soccer generally treat referee errors and other in-game randomness (like a marginal VAR decision) as an uncontrollable, unmodelable input, and base models often simply exclude events like red cards from the dataset entirely rather than trying to isolate their effect.
framework🗣 Mike Treacyconviction: mediumMike Treacy
So what: A reminder that even sophisticated models explicitly wall off irreducible uncertainty rather than pretending to explain it — a discipline worth importing into any forecasting exercise with human-judgment noise (e.g. regulatory or referee-style decisions in markets).
“I think it's control, the controllables.” ▶ 16:41
VAR (Video Assistant Referee) reviews create new usable data even when they overturn a goal for a marginal offside, because the buildup play, positioning, and shot-creation that preceded the disallowed goal remain analytically valid signal about a player's or team's attacking quality.
causal🗣 Mike Treacyconviction: mediumMike TreacyVAR
So what: Analysts and data vendors should treat VAR-overturned events as a data-enrichment opportunity rather than simply discarding the outcome, since the underlying process data still carries signal.
The model-to-coach translation bottleneck 2
Raw outputs from soccer analytics neural nets — such as classification of a team's buildup structure over a 10-20 second window, or an expected-possession-value model estimating the probability of scoring or conceding in the next roughly 30 seconds — are not directly usable by coaches; teams instead depend on dedicated data analysts who translate model signals into specific video clips a coach can act on.
framework🗣 Joris Bekkersconviction: highJoris Bekkers
So what: The bottleneck to monetizing advanced machine learning in sports (as in many domains) isn't model quality but the 'last mile' translation layer — implying the durable competitive moat and business opportunity is in analyst talent and interpretability tooling, not raw model access.
In-game analytics-driven tactical adjustments are rarely made live within seconds of a model output; most model-informed changes happen pre-match, post-match, or specifically at halftime, since the communication chain from live data to analyst to bench limits real-time responsiveness even though enough live data exists to support faster decisions.
data_point🗣 Mike Treacyconviction: mediumMike TreacyJoris Bekkers
So what: Signals that the near-term commercial opportunity in soccer analytics is concentrated in pre/post-match decision support and halftime tooling rather than milliseconds-latency live optimization.
Quantifying player value: Bellingham and win-probability models 2
Mike Treacy describes valuing a versatile player like Jude Bellingham through two decomposed value streams — 'in possession' value (progressing the ball into threatening areas and creating high-probability chances) and 'out of possession' value (denying the opponent's ball progression, including modeling off-ball movement that closes passing lanes) — enabling a quantitative comparison of positional versatility across different phases of a match.
framework🗣 Mike Treacyconviction: highMike TreacyJude Bellingham
So what: Provides a template for quantifying the value of actions that 'don't happen' (like closing a passing lane) — a hard measurement problem analogous to valuing optionality or hedges in markets where the payoff is an avoided bad outcome.
Soccer win-probability models are anchored to a pre-game team-strength rating (Elo-style) and update only modestly on small in-game samples, so an underdog dominating the first five minutes should shift a bettor's beliefs only slightly rather than dramatically, because the strong prior outweighs a few minutes of new information.
framework🗣 Joris Bekkersconviction: mediumJoris Bekkers
So what: Live in-play odds swinging sharply on an early flurry from the underdog may represent an overreaction relative to what a properly Bayesian-updated Elo-anchored model would imply, a potential edge for in-play bettors.
Data ownership and the big-club vs small-club edge 2
Ownership and licensing of soccer tracking and event data remains a gray area with no consistent industry standard; Joris Bekkers sourced his first professional dataset for AFC Bournemouth in 2016 informally through an Upwork-hired developer based in Ukraine, rather than through an official licensed vendor.
anecdote🗣 Joris Bekkersconviction: mediumJoris BekkersAFC Bournemouth
So what: Highlights that in the early buildout of a data-driven niche, informal or gray-market data sourcing can be a real competitive edge for early movers, similar to alternative-data arbitrage in finance before markets formalize.
“It's, it's a big question mark.” ▶ 41:58
Joris Bekkers builds open-source soccer analytics software specifically intended to help smaller, resource-constrained clubs build tracking-data models and close the technology gap with wealthier clubs, but he is uncertain it is actually leveling the playing field, since the scarce resource has shifted from raw tooling access to the expertise needed to translate data into decisions.
contrarian🗣 Joris Bekkersconviction: exploratoryJoris Bekkers
So what: Suggests that as with open-source ML broadly, democratizing access to tools doesn't automatically democratize outcomes if the bottleneck has moved to a scarcer human-capital layer (interpretation and translation), a caution for anyone betting that open-sourcing a technology alone will level a competitive field.
Optimization risk and Goodhart's Law in soccer 2
Host Joe Weisenthal raises a Goodhart's Law-style risk in analytics-driven soccer: once a metric becomes an explicit optimization target, players and coaches over-fit to it, and the resulting play can become formally 'better' by the model's own measure while becoming less entertaining for fans — drawing a direct parallel to basketball's shift toward three-point-heavy offense.
contrarian🗣 Joe Weisenthalconviction: mediumJoe Weisenthal
So what: A generalizable warning for any domain where a quantitative proxy becomes the optimization target (trading strategies, corporate KPIs): the proxy metric can decouple from the true underlying goal it was originally meant to represent.
Teams and organizations optimize for genuinely different objective functions — maximizing wins, maximizing profit, and avoiding relegation are distinct problems with different correct answers — so a strategy that looks tactically inferior, such as a weaker side playing ultra-defensively to force penalty kicks (as Paraguay was criticized for), can still be the game-theoretically rational choice given that team's actual objective.
framework🗣 Mike Treacyconviction: highMike TreacyParaguay
So what: Before judging any strategy as 'suboptimal,' identify the actual objective function being optimized — a directly transferable discipline for evaluating trading strategies or corporate decisions that look irrational only if you assume the wrong goal.
“Solving for winning is very different from solving for profit is very different from solving for a gambler.” ▶ 54:56
Sell-side World Cup forecasts don't hold up 2
Host Joe Weisenthal notes that sell-side bank analysts' published World Cup prediction notes are frequently wrong, citing an example of Nomura strategists predicting Japan, despite investment banks' quantitative resources and modeling capability.
contrarian🗣 Joe Weisenthalconviction: mediumJoe WeisenthalNomura
So what: A reminder that publicly published sell-side forecasts (in sports or markets) are marketing/content products, not necessarily the bank's best genuine edge.
Mike Treacy's explanation for weak sell-side World Cup forecasting is a classic trading-desk maxim: a firm with a genuinely good predictive model would use it to beat the bookmakers directly rather than publish it as free research content, so published sell-side sports predictions shouldn't be read as differentiated proprietary edge.
contrarian🗣 Mike Treacyconviction: highMike Treacy
So what: Mirrors the standard skepticism warranted toward public sell-side equity research in financial markets — if the model were truly alpha-generating, it would be monetized directly rather than published.
“If you have a good model, you wouldn't polish it. You just beat the bookies, right?” ▶ 52:16
NY Governor Kathy Hochul on Her One Year Data Center Moratorium | Odd Lots42 min28 insights2026-07-15
▶ Watch on YouTube
New York Governor Kathy Hochul explains the state's new one-year data center moratorium as a negotiating pause requiring developers to bring their own power or pay a grid premium, details an 'all of the above' energy buildout (nuclear, Quebec hydro, offshore wind, gas) aimed at winning jobs-heavy investments like Micron's $100 billion fab, and discusses managing AI-driven labor disruption via a paused Waymo trial, an AI-assisted regulatory purge, and housing-cost politics behind New York's leftward electoral shift.
New York's Data Center Moratorium · 5Energy Strategy and Nuclear Buildout · 7Micron and the Semiconductor Bet · 3AI and Labor-Market Disruption Policy · 3Waymo and the Politics of Concentrated Losers · 3Using AI to Deregulate New York's Rulebook · 3Finance Competition and Housing Affordability Politics · 4
New York's Data Center Moratorium 5
New York became the first U.S. state to impose a formal one-year moratorium on new large-scale data centers, which Governor Kathy Hochul frames as a pause to set clear rules rather than an outright ban, triggered by a sudden surge of siting proposals.
thesis🗣 Kathy Hochul, NY Governorconviction: highKathy HochulNew York StateData centers
So what: Sets a template other states may copy; data center developers and their suppliers (power, cooling, chips) face at least a year of regulatory uncertainty in the largest East Coast market.
“It is not a ban. It is simply saying, step back, take a breath and get it right.” ▶ 6:08
A single data center project of just 50 megawatts draws power equivalent to roughly 50,000 homes, illustrating why even mid-sized AI data centers strain New York's grid capacity and residential affordability.
data_point🗣 Kathy Hochul, NY Governorconviction: highData centersNew York State
So what: Investors sizing data center capex should factor in that even 'smaller' 50MW projects compete directly with residential load, inviting local political and regulatory friction.
“even on the somewhat smaller side, 50MW of power required, that's 50,000 homes” ▶ 4:49
Under New York's forthcoming data center framework, developers will have to either bring their own power generation source or pay a premium to draw from the state grid, with the Public Service Commission finalizing the rules within the one-year moratorium.
framework🗣 Kathy Hochul, NY Governorconviction: highData centersNew York State
So what: A bring-your-own-power-or-pay-premium model raises the effective cost of siting in New York, favoring developers who can co-locate generation (e.g., gas, SMRs) over pure grid-draw projects.
“you come, you're the bring your own power source or you have to pay a premium to use our grid.” ▶ 5:57
Hochul says data center companies have been willing to pay rural host communities on the order of $1 million per megawatt, but many small upstate localities lacked the planning staff and sophistication to negotiate such terms, a gap the state's new community investment framework and grid resiliency fund are meant to close.
data_point🗣 Kathy Hochul, NY Governorconviction: mediumData centersNew York State
So what: Community payment benchmarks (~$1M/MW) are becoming a de facto price of entry for data center siting deals, raising project costs and local-approval complexity nationally.
“we want you to give, you know, $1 million per megawatt” ▶ 5:25
Even amid national anti-data-center political backlash, New York continues to offer tax breaks to attract data center investment, showing that 'compute scarcity' narratives coexist with active interstate competition for these facilities.
contrarian🗣 Joe Weisenthal, hostconviction: mediumData centersNew York State
So what: States are simultaneously restricting and subsidizing data centers, so investors should track state-by-state policy divergence rather than assume uniform national headwinds.
Energy Strategy and Nuclear Buildout 7
Facing federal opposition to renewable energy and surging AI-driven power demand, Governor Hochul is pursuing an 'all of the above' energy strategy for New York combining Quebec hydro imports, offshore wind, a new Pennsylvania-to-Long Island natural gas pipeline, and nuclear expansion.
thesis🗣 Kathy Hochul, NY Governorconviction: highNew York StateNuclear power
So what: New York's multi-source buildout creates investment opportunities across hydro transmission, offshore wind, gas pipeline, and nuclear supply chains simultaneously rather than a single-technology bet.
Hochul says she wants to build more nuclear power capacity in New York than the entire United States has built in the past 30 years, and is negotiating with the U.S. Department of Energy for long-term, low-interest loans to fund new reactors at existing upstate sites.
prediction🗣 Kathy Hochul, NY Governorconviction: highNew York StateNuclear powerTrump administration
So what: A state-level nuclear buildout of this scale, if realized, would be a major catalyst for nuclear equipment, fuel, and construction-trade suppliers, contingent on federal DOE loan approval.
“I want to build more nuclear in New York than the entire country has in 30 years.” ▶ 14:16
Hochul calls the 2021 shutdown of the Indian Point nuclear plant, which once supplied 20-25% of New York City's power, one of the biggest mistakes in state history, and says restarting it is now complicated because the plant has already been decommissioned and had its radioactive materials removed, despite the owner's claim a restart is 'technically doable.'
contrarian🗣 Kathy Hochul, NY Governorconviction: highIndian PointNew York State
So what: A quick Indian Point restart is likely off the table as a near-term power source for NYC-area AI/data-center demand, despite periodic headlines suggesting otherwise.
“one of the biggest mistakes in the state's history was shutting down Indian Point, which powered 20 to 25% of New York City's power without a plan B” ▶ 22:30
To partially offset Indian Point's lost capacity, Hochul authorized construction of a hydroelectric transmission line importing power from Quebec within weeks of taking office, which she describes as the longest underground/underwater transmission line in the country.
data_point🗣 Kathy Hochul, NY Governorconviction: mediumNew York State
So what: Cross-border hydro transmission infrastructure is a multi-year, capital-intensive bet; investors in grid/transmission construction and Canadian hydro exporters should track New York-specific opportunity here.
New York is also developing an offshore wind facility Hochul says will power roughly half a million homes in Brooklyn, which she is fighting to keep alive against federal opposition she calls a 'war on offshore wind.'
data_point🗣 Kathy Hochul, NY Governorconviction: mediumNew York StateTrump administration
So what: Offshore wind project completion in New York remains politically contested at the federal level, adding permitting and timeline risk for offshore wind developers and suppliers.
“a facility that'll power half a million homes in Brooklyn” ▶ 23:33
Hochul is offering a direct incentive for power-hungry AI and semiconductor projects: any company that brings its own small modular reactor (SMR) capacity will be granted site access, signaling the state wants to become a hub for SMR deployment.
framework🗣 Kathy Hochul, NY Governorconviction: highSmall modular reactorsNew York StateMicron
So what: SMR developers and manufacturers have a concrete state-level customer pathway in New York, tied directly to large industrial and data-center siting decisions.
“Data centers or the next micron show up with your own small modular reactor capability, and I'll find the space for you.” ▶ 25:06
Hochul says nuclear plant approvals typically take about ten years, seven of them at the federal level, and that she has asked the Trump administration to streamline federal review while she speeds up state approvals, one of the few areas where she describes alignment with Washington, in contrast to friction over offshore wind, Canadian-material tariffs, and ICE enforcement.
causal🗣 Kathy Hochul, NY Governorconviction: mediumTrump administrationNuclear powerNew York State
So what: Federal permitting reform is the binding constraint on New York's nuclear timeline; DOE/administration signals on streamlining are a leading indicator for how fast new capacity can come online.
“It takes ten years for approvals. So seven of those years are at the federal level.” ▶ 24:42
Micron and the Semiconductor Bet 3
New York secured a roughly $100 billion Micron semiconductor investment, which Hochul calls the largest private-sector investment in American history, expected to create 10,000 direct and 40,000 indirect jobs, with the state committing to secure the plant's power supply through the New York Power Authority.
data_point🗣 Kathy Hochul, NY Governorconviction: highMicronNew York State
So what: The Micron buildout is a multi-decade capex and job-creation anchor for upstate New York, with state-guaranteed power supply reducing project execution risk versus data centers stuck in the moratorium queue.
“Largest private sector investment in American history.” ▶ 13:51
Given a choice, Hochul says she would prioritize allocating scarce grid power to a jobs-heavy manufacturer like Micron over a 'largely vacant' data center consuming the same amount of electricity, revealing the implicit hierarchy of who gets power access under the moratorium regime.
causal🗣 Kathy Hochul, NY Governorconviction: highMicronData centersNew York State
So what: Data center developers should expect to be second in line for New York grid capacity behind job-creating manufacturing investments like chip fabs, affecting siting and interconnection timelines.
“if I had to choose between powering a largely vacant data center with the same amount of power, I can have a micron with thousand jobs” ▶ 14:24
To meet Micron's stated need for 9,000 engineers and tens of thousands of construction-trade workers, New York has already rewritten school curricula in the nine counties surrounding the Micron site to teach relevant skills starting in kindergarten, a roughly 30-year workforce pipeline.
data_point🗣 Kathy Hochul, NY Governorconviction: mediumMicronNew York State
So what: Long-lead workforce investment signals the state sees Micron as a multi-decade anchor tenant, relevant for semiconductor supply-chain investors evaluating the project's durability.
“Micron, when I was trying to recruit them, said they needed 9000 engineers and tens of thousands of people in the construction trades.” ▶ 20:22
AI and Labor-Market Disruption Policy 3
Hochul launched a 'Future Works Commission' bringing together business, labor, and academic leaders to study AI's impact on jobs and design reskilling programs, explicitly citing her memory of automation and offshoring hollowing out Bethlehem Steel-era Buffalo as the reason for acting pre-emptively rather than reactively.
framework🗣 Kathy Hochul, NY Governorconviction: highNew York State
So what: State-level AI labor policy is moving from rhetoric to institution-building; companies operating in New York should expect emerging reskilling/consortium expectations rather than new direct taxes.
“We are now called the Future Works Commission.” ▶ 11:56
Hochul says she gathered ten New Yorkers in her office who had recently lost jobs to AI, including people who had studied computer science and coding, and notes that women are disproportionately concentrated in the roles she considers most vulnerable to AI displacement.
anecdote🗣 Kathy Hochul, NY Governorconviction: highNew York State
So what: Even technically-skilled workers with CS/coding backgrounds are reporting AI-driven job loss, undercutting the assumption that STEM training alone insulates workers from AI disruption.
“I gathered ten of them in my office the other day. I invited people who had lost their jobs.” ▶ 16:42
Rather than directly taxing tech companies to fund worker retraining, Hochul is pushing a 'consortium' model where AI firms contribute expertise and technology, such as helping data centers cut energy, water, and noise footprints, in exchange for New York's continued low-tax stance toward high earners and businesses.
thesis🗣 Kathy Hochul, NY Governorconviction: mediumNew York StateData centers
So what: AI and data center companies operating in New York face soft-power pressure (in-kind contributions, technical partnerships) rather than new tax liabilities, differing from labor-disruption funding models proposed elsewhere.
Waymo and the Politics of Concentrated Losers 3
New York paused Waymo's driverless-car trial because Hochul wants a concrete transition plan ('Plan B') for professional drivers before allowing full rollout, drawing an explicit parallel to the lack of worker-transition support during the Industrial Revolution and prior automation waves.
causal🗣 Kathy Hochul, NY Governorconviction: highWaymoNew York State
So what: Autonomous vehicle operators like Waymo face state-level political risk in New York tied not to safety data but to displaced-worker transition planning, a distinct regulatory hurdle for AV rollout timelines.
“What is the alternative for the people who now are the ones we're counting on to drive us in person? You have to have a plan B.” ▶ 15:42
Odd Lots hosts push back that the number of people who would benefit as passengers from driverless cars is far larger than the number of professional drivers who would be displaced, arguing New York's Waymo pause prioritizes a smaller, concentrated group over larger diffuse public benefits like safety and reduced congestion.
contrarian🗣 Joe Weisenthal, hostconviction: mediumWaymo
So what: The cost-benefit framing used against AV rollouts (concentrated losers vs. diffuse winners) is a recurring political pattern that could apply to other AI-driven automation debates, useful for handicapping regulatory risk.
“like the number of people who would theoretically be a passenger in any car is far larger than the number of people who drive cars professionally.” ▶ 17:17
Odd Lots hosts frame New York's driverless-car caution as a 'Jones Act phenomenon,' where a small, identifiable group with concentrated losses (professional drivers) can delay or block policies with large diffuse benefits, and suggest this dynamic is likely to recur across many future AI labor disruptions, making Waymo's New York trajectory an early test case worth watching.
framework🗣 Joe Weisenthal, hostconviction: highWaymoNew York State
So what: Investors and operators in AI-exposed industries should watch for 'Jones Act'-style political blocking by concentrated loser groups as a template risk factor, independent of the underlying technology's actual safety or efficiency case.
“This is the Jones act phenomenon which is like how many people have Jones act jobs” ▶ 40:21
Using AI to Deregulate New York's Rulebook 3
Hochul used AI to scan New York's entire regulatory and legal code for outdated or antiquated rules, generating more than 4,000 public recommendations for repeal in a couple of months, a review she says would have taken five years using state staff alone, and has directed all state agencies to act on the flagged list.
data_point🗣 Kathy Hochul, NY Governorconviction: highNew York State
So what: AI-driven regulatory review is a concrete, replicable government-efficiency use case with a measurable speed-up (5 years to ~2 months), relevant to govtech vendors and other jurisdictions considering similar deregulation sprints.
“I would have tried taking five years at the staff level in a couple of months, and I have over 4000 recommendations from the public who want us to look into things.” ▶ 9:50
Examples of antiquated New York rules flagged by the AI regulatory review include a requirement that pregnant women obtain a special permit to work after midnight and a $25 fee to bring a dog hunting in the state.
anecdote🗣 Kathy Hochul, NY Governorconviction: mediumNew York State
So what: Illustrates the kind of low-value, low-risk compliance burden AI-assisted review can identify and clear quickly, a template for cost-of-doing-business improvements in other jurisdictions.
“You have to pay $25 to bring your dog hunting in the state” ▶ 0:52
Odd Lots hosts frame the AI regulatory scan as a template AI use case, reducing an unreviewable corpus of millions of pages of code into a manageable shortlist of flagged items for human judgment, rather than replacing human review outright.
framework🗣 Tracy Alloway, hostconviction: mediumNew York State
So what: The 'AI narrows, humans decide' pattern demonstrated here is a reusable model for applying AI to large unstructured compliance and legal corpora across government and regulated industries.
“find the dumb stuff” ▶ 38:53
Finance Competition and Housing Affordability Politics 4
Despite JPMorgan expanding in Texas and Citadel expanding in Florida, Hochul says New York City commercial office leasing has hit extraordinary levels over the past three to four months, citing American Express's new World Trade Center headquarters as a 'statement of confidence' that finance and tech firms are expanding rather than relocating away from New York.
data_point🗣 Kathy Hochul, NY Governorconviction: mediumJPMorganCitadelAmerican ExpressNew York State
So what: New York office and commercial real estate leasing strength contradicts the narrative of wholesale finance-sector flight to Texas and Florida, relevant for NYC office REIT and commercial real estate positioning.
“There's only one New York.” ▶ 29:08
Hochul links the rise of Democratic Socialist politics in New York City, including Zoran Mamdani's recent electoral wins, directly to housing unaffordability, citing that the average age of a first-time homebuyer rose from 30 to 40 within just a few years.
causal🗣 Kathy Hochul, NY Governorconviction: highZoran MamdaniNew York State
So what: Housing affordability is the proximate driver of NYC's leftward political shift per the sitting governor's own diagnosis, suggesting housing policy is the key variable to watch for the city's political trajectory.
“the average age of a first time home buyer, if you're lucky enough to have a home at all, went from 30 to 40” ▶ 30:33
To unblock housing supply, Hochul pushed through a reform of New York's 1975 SEQR environmental review law this year despite it being called 'dead on arrival' in the legislature, and describes ongoing NIMBY resistance, including from self-identified progressive city council members, to office-to-residential conversions and a low-income homeownership project for 900 people.
data_point🗣 Kathy Hochul, NY Governorconviction: highNew York State
So what: SEQR reform is a concrete, already-enacted deregulatory lever for New York housing supply this year; investors in NYC residential development and office-conversion plays should track its implementation and continued local-level resistance.
“it was called dead on arrival” ▶ 32:51
Hochul is funding a $1 billion energy rebate program to offset New Yorkers' utility bills and says she took on trial lawyers to reduce auto insurance costs, positioning cost-of-living relief as her top political priority alongside energy policy.
data_point🗣 Kathy Hochul, NY Governorconviction: mediumNew York State
So what: A $1 billion rebate program is a direct fiscal offset to rising electricity costs partly driven by AI and data center demand, relevant to utilities and ratepayer-cost analysis in New York.
“I'm going to do $1 billion energy rebate to help pay utility bills.” ▶ 31:47

Bankless 3 episode(s) · 94 insights

Crypto Is Running Out of Reasons to Go Lower67 min38 insights2026-07-17
▶ Watch on YouTube
Bankless hosts David and Ryan debate whether crypto has bottomed using a four-cycle Bitcoin chart, cover cooling CPI against an escalating Iran war and oil-price dynamics, and dig into the Robin Hood chain vs. Base L2 rivalry (including Jesse Pollak's admission Base's creator-coin bet was wrong) and the resulting ETH tokenomics debate over whether Ethereum should optimize for fee revenue or pure store-of-value status.
Bitcoin Cycle Bottom Timing · 4Macro Backdrop: Inflation and the Iran War · 5Crypto Treasury Companies: Saylor and Tom Lee · 3Robin Hood Chain vs. Base: The L2 War · 6ETH Tokenomics: Store of Value vs. Revenue Debate · 8L2-L1 Rent and Governance Proposals · 4Ethereum Foundation Spinoffs and DeFi Risk · 5Buy-and-Burn Token Value Capture · 3
Bitcoin Cycle Bottom Timing 4
A chart overlaying Bitcoin's four historical bear-to-bull cycles (2014, 2018, 2022, 2026) shows a consistent bear/pre-bull/first-bull/second-bull pattern; if it holds, the market is close to but has not yet hit bottom, with a final capitulation likely in the next two to three months followed by a sideways grind into 2027 and a stronger 2028.
framework🗣 Ryanconviction: highBitcoincrypto cycle
So what: Watch for a possible final capitulation low around August-October 2026 under this pattern before positioning for the next bull leg.
“Never fade the cycle.” ▶ 3:13
A Brian Armstrong Twitter/X poll of roughly 31,000 votes on whether Bitcoin has bottomed came back nearly split, with 44% saying yes and 55% saying no.
data_point🗣 Bankless hostconviction: mediumBrian ArmstrongBitcoin
So what: Retail sentiment on the bottom call is genuinely divided, so it shouldn't be treated as a decisive signal in either direction.
“44% said yes, the bottom is in. 55% said no, the bottom is not in.” ▶ 5:34
A genuine capitulation bottom would likely require a broader equity shock, such as a 10-20% NASDAQ selloff that could pull Bitcoin down toward the high-$20,000s from its then-current level near $29,000, rather than the modest pullback occurring at time of recording.
prediction🗣 Ryanconviction: mediumBitcoinNASDAQ
So what: A NASDAQ drawdown of that magnitude is a leading trigger to watch for a true capitulation low, rather than crypto-specific weakness alone.
“If we saw a 10 to 20% um NASDAQ selloff from here, things could still get uglier. I mean, that could be a final capitulation bottom.” ▶ 6:32
Crypto commentator Good Alexander argued the market has run out of bearish catalysts because Bitcoin didn't break down despite the Iran war and Michael Saylor selling MSTR shares, Zcash didn't crash despite an AI-discovered privacy bug, and ETH rallied even after Vitalik Buterin disavowed gambling as a use case -- framing this as a sign of seller exhaustion.
contrarian🗣 Good Alexander (X commentator, quoted)conviction: mediumBitcoinZcashEthereumVitalik Buterin
So what: If negative catalysts stop moving price lower, it can signal seller exhaustion near a cycle bottom, though the hosts caution real downside risk still exists via a broader equity selloff.
“Bitcoin can't go down with a war and Michael Sailor selling. Zcash can't go down with a day zero privacy bug discovered by AI. ETH books Robin Hood after Vitalic disavows gambling as a use case. The night is darkest before dawn. Bitches.” ▶ 5:47
Macro Backdrop: Inflation and the Iran War 5
June US CPI inflation came in at 3.5% versus 3.8% expected, with core CPI at 2.6% versus 2.8% expected and the largest month-over-month inflation drop since May 2020, easing fears of near-term rate hikes that had spooked markets the prior month.
data_point🗣 Bankless hostconviction: highCPIFederal ReserveBitcoin
So what: Cooler-than-expected inflation removed a key overhang for risk assets and coincided with this week's crypto strength.
“June CPI inflation falls to 3.5% below expectations of 3.8%.” ▶ 7:47
Despite the cool CPI print, oil prices rose about 20% in July amid the intensifying Iran conflict, which the hosts flag as a lagging risk since higher energy costs typically feed into future CPI readings with a delay.
causal🗣 Bankless hostconviction: mediumoilCPIIran
So what: The current disinflation trend could reverse if elevated energy prices persist, a risk worth monitoring before assuming the CPI relief is durable.
As of the fifth straight day of US strikes on Iran, the campaign is targeting Iranian command centers, air defense, missile/drone capabilities and coastal surveillance to force open the Strait of Hormuz; Trump has framed this as a new, second conflict distinct from the earlier one, resetting a fresh 60-day War Powers Act reporting window.
data_point🗣 Bankless hostconviction: mediumIranTrumpStrait of Hormuz
So what: An extended or escalating conflict raises tail risk for an oil-price shock that could spill into risk-asset sentiment, including crypto.
Despite intensifying conflict, oil at $78 remains the cheapest level of the entire Iran war period (which had ranged near $95 before falling to $66), because global buyers and US/Gulf producers have had time to reroute supply around the Strait of Hormuz, suggesting oil is unlikely to spike back to prior highs.
prediction🗣 Bankless hostconviction: mediumoilStrait of HormuzUnited States
So what: Structural rerouting of global oil supply caps the upside energy-price risk to risk-asset narratives, supportive of continued risk-on positioning.
“$78 oil is still the cheapest oil during this Iran war conflict in total.” ▶ 13:53
Small domestic economic protests are re-emerging inside Iran as its economy decays under the reinstated naval blockade, suggesting Iran's negotiating leverage (mainly threatening the Strait of Hormuz) is eroding even as the conflict shows no clear end in sight, raising 'forever war' risk.
thesis🗣 Bankless hostconviction: mediumIranStrait of Hormuz
So what: A prolonged, open-ended conflict is a tail risk for energy markets and broader risk sentiment that investors should keep on the radar rather than assume resolves quickly.
Crypto Treasury Companies: Saylor and Tom Lee 3
Michael Saylor's Strategy (MSTR) sold zero Bitcoin this week -- a reversal from selling several thousand BTC worth roughly $300-400 million the prior week -- and instead raised $466 million via at-the-market MSTR share issuance, bringing cash reserves to about $3 billion, enough for roughly 20 months of dividend coverage.
data_point🗣 Bankless hostconviction: highMichael SaylorMSTR/StrategyBitcoin
So what: A roughly 20-month cash runway would carry Strategy through the projected bear-market tail per the cycle framework, reducing near-term forced-Bitcoin-selling risk.
“He sold zero Bitcoin this week.” ▶ 17:52
Peter Schiff argued Michael Saylor 'needlessly destroyed shareholder value' by issuing MSTR shares at a discount to Bitcoin net asset value instead of selling Bitcoin itself, a critique the hosts say is actually a bear case for MSTR equity specifically, not for Bitcoin -- and arguably bullish for Bitcoin since it avoids adding BTC sell pressure.
contrarian🗣 Peter Schiff (bear commentator)conviction: mediumPeter SchiffMSTR/StrategyBitcoin
So what: Investors should separate the MSTR-equity dilution risk from the Bitcoin-price implication -- share dilution to avoid BTC sales is bearish for MSTR holders but not necessarily for BTC.
“By selling MSTR at a huge discount to Bitcoin value per share, you needlessly destroyed shareholder value just to avoid selling Bitcoin.” ▶ 19:12
Tom Lee's Bitmine has accumulated 4.8% of total ETH supply (5.77 million ETH), reaching 96% of his publicly stated 5% target within about a year, including through this bear market, prompting the hosts to argue he should raise the target further rather than stop.
data_point🗣 Bankless hostconviction: highTom LeeBitmineEthereum
So what: Continued large-scale ETH accumulation by a visible institutional buyer is a persistent demand-side tailwind for ETH; watch whether Lee raises his target as a sentiment signal.
≈ “He now has 4.8% of all ETH supply. That's 5.77 million ETH supply.” paraphrase
Robin Hood Chain vs. Base: The L2 War 6
Robin Hood's L2 chain overtook Coinbase's Base in daily activity on July 10th, reaching 117 user-operations-per-second versus Base's 93, despite having launched only a few weeks earlier -- an ascent the hosts attribute largely to Robin Hood's existing retail-app distribution.
data_point🗣 Bankless hostconviction: highRobin Hood chainBaseCoinbase
So what: Distribution from an established retail app can bootstrap L2 usage faster than pure technical or DeFi merit -- a template other brokerages/exchanges may copy.
“Robin Hood chain usage flipped base on July 10th, so six days ago from the time of recording.” ▶ 24:52
On Robin Hood chain, the top protocol by TVL is Morpho at $152 million (powering the in-app 7% USDG yield feature), followed by Athena at $110 million, Uniswap at $40 million and Maple Finance at $30 million, while the dominant activity by transaction volume is memecoin trading through competing token launchpads after early leader Noxa shut down amid internal team disputes.
data_point🗣 Bankless hostconviction: highRobin Hood chainMorphoAthenaUniswapMaple Finance
So what: Real DeFi TVL is building but memecoin speculation remains the primary usage driver on the new chain, typical of early-stage L2 bootstrapping.
Base creator Jesse Pollak publicly admitted the social/creator-coin direction he pushed for Base through 2025 (the Farcaster/Zora-adjacent strategy) was the wrong bet, calling Q1 2026 a low point, and is stepping back from running the Base app while a colleague, Kobe, takes over app-layer strategy, pivoting toward DeFi and trading ('internet of finance') instead.
anecdote🗣 Jesse Pollak (Base creator)conviction: highJesse PollakBaseFarcasterZoraCoinbase
So what: Base's pivot toward trading/DeFi is a direct competitive response to Robin Hood chain's early success and signals where Coinbase will direct resources going forward.
“The collateral damage talking about this year how things have gone has been an exercise in eating [ __ ].” ▶ 29:58
Austin Campbell argued Base has been 'an outright failure' and a costly distraction that kept Coinbase from fixing its core business, a critique the hosts view as overstated since Base remains a top-tier L2 by usage (recently overtaken to roughly fourth place) and its underlying infrastructure still works even as the application-layer strategy resets.
contrarian🗣 Austin Campbellconviction: mediumAustin CampbellBaseCoinbase
So what: Investors shouldn't read Base's app-layer stumble as terminal -- infrastructure value and chain ranking remain intact even amid a strategic pivot.
The hosts frame the real competitive battle as being between the Coinbase.com and Robin Hood apps for retail distribution, not the underlying chains -- Base was originally hyped for giving an L2 unprecedented centralized-exchange distribution (echoing Binance/BNB Chain), but Robin Hood replicated that advantage quickly while Coinbase lagged in adding tradfi (stock trading) features that Robin Hood already offered.
framework🗣 Bankless hostconviction: mediumCoinbaseRobin Hood chainBaseBinance
So what: L2 competitive advantage increasingly hinges on parent-app distribution and cross-asset feature velocity, not just technical differentiation.
Robin Hood chain's leading memecoin Cash Cat (roughly $104 million fully diluted valuation) and a comparably sized Solana memecoin from Anom (roughly $190 million FDV) launched around the same time near the market bottom, and the hosts are using their relative performance as a dual barometer of risk appetite and which ecosystem is winning the memecoin battle.
framework🗣 Bankless hostconviction: exploratoryCash CatAnomSolanaRobin Hood chain
So what: Relative performance of Cash Cat versus Anom's coin can be watched as a real-time gauge of speculative risk appetite and Robin Hood-vs-Solana memecoin dominance.
ETH Tokenomics: Store of Value vs. Revenue Debate 8
An Ark Invest analyst's breakdown of Robin Hood chain's revenue split shows Robin Hood keeps roughly 90% margin ($816,000 in revenue), Arbitrum as tech provider takes about 10% ($80,000), and Ethereum L1 has captured only $1,538 (about 0.15%) in blob-space settlement fees since inception.
data_point🗣 Lorenzo (Ark Invest analyst, cited)conviction: highEthereumArbitrumRobin Hood chain
So what: This is the core bear case for ETH as a revenue-generating asset: L2 growth does not proportionally translate into L1 fee capture, pressuring fundamentals-based ETH valuation.
“Robin Hood gets 90% margins, arbitum gets 10% and Ethereum gets 0.15% for data availability.” ▶ 37:07
The same analyst argued a 'healthier split' would have Ethereum L1 capture at least 15% of L2-generated margin rather than roughly 0.15%, framing the current arrangement as ETH being underpaid for the settlement security it provides to L2s like Robin Hood chain.
thesis🗣 Lorenzo (Ark Invest analyst, cited)conviction: mediumEthereumArbitrumRobin Hood chain
So what: If a rent renegotiation between L2s and Ethereum L1 gains traction, it could materially shift ETH's fee-revenue economics -- a catalyst worth monitoring.
“He said a healthier split would be at least Ethereum captures 15% of this margin.” ▶ 38:06
Longtime Ethereum L2 bull Plenny returned after a long silence to admit his 2021-2023 expectations for application demand growth were wrong, arguing blockspace supply exploded while demand for applications proved highly inelastic and grew negligibly, undermining the original fee-driven ETH-as-money thesis.
contrarian🗣 Plenny / Plenia (L2 commentator, cited)conviction: highPlennyEthereum
So what: A prominent L2 bull's public reassessment signals the fee-driven ETH valuation thesis has structurally weakened, reinforcing a pivot toward store-of-value framing.
“My expectations for growth in application demand from 2021 and 2023 post have proven to be wrong.” ▶ 39:55
Plenny frames Ethereum's choice as binary: either subsidize fees and compete for the multi-trillion-dollar store-of-value market (accepting near-zero L2 fee revenue), or shrink blockspace capacity to hike transaction fees and compete in a shrinking billion-dollar fee market, which he pegs as consistent with roughly a $100 ETH endgame.
framework🗣 Plenny / Plenia (L2 commentator, cited)conviction: mediumEthereum
So what: This framing crystallizes the core debate investors must resolve before valuing ETH: fee maximization implies a far lower terminal price than a pure store-of-value framing.
“ETH is money implies a market cap far higher than revenue could support.” ▶ 42:05
David argued Ethereum no longer needs to prioritize L2 fee revenue -- after ten years of 'bootstrapping,' ETH should optimize purely as a store-of-value and scaling asset, similar to how Bitcoin and gold don't optimize for fee revenue, making cheap blockspace and scaling the priority over fee capture.
thesis🗣 Davidconviction: highEthereumBitcoin
So what: If this view gains consensus among Ethereum's core community, expect continued deprioritization of L1 fee revenue in protocol roadmap decisions, reinforcing a low-fee, high-throughput scaling strategy.
“We're 10 years into the project now. ETH can afford to not care about fee revenue.” ▶ 43:12
David argued that mixing a 'store of value' narrative with a 'valued on fee revenue' narrative is self-defeating for Ethereum -- noting the market already prices a dollar of Ethereum fee revenue far higher than a dollar of Microsoft or Amazon revenue -- and that Ethereum should stop discussing fees entirely, mirroring how Bitcoin scrubbed non-monetary use cases like timestamping from its narrative.
framework🗣 Davidconviction: highEthereumBitcoinMicrosoftAmazon
So what: A narrative shift away from 'fee revenue' toward pure store-of-value framing could itself be a re-rating catalyst for ETH's valuation multiple, independent of fundamentals changing.
“Fees are not the point of ether.” ▶ 46:01
Joseph Lubin has publicly argued Ethereum L1 fees should stay low to foster ecosystem growth and market-share capture, with ETH's value ultimately accruing through its store-of-value status rather than fee revenue.
thesis🗣 Joseph Lubin (Ethereum co-founder, cited)conviction: mediumJoseph LubinEthereum
So what: A prominent Ethereum figure publicly backing the low-fee, store-of-value camp adds weight to that side of the ongoing tokenomics debate.
“Ethereum L1 revenue fees should stay low to foster growth.” ▶ 47:12
David estimated more than 50% probability that ETH's roughly 8-9% weekly rally versus Bitcoin's 2.5% (pushing the ETH/BTC ratio up 16% since June) reflects positive 'ETH is money' sentiment from Robin Hood chain's success rather than any actual L1 fee capture, while noting Tom Lee's continued buying is a confounding factor and one week of data is noisy.
exploratory🗣 Davidconviction: mediumEthereumBitcoinRobin Hood chainTom Lee
So what: If the ETH-is-money narrative genuinely drives price independent of fee capture, it validates the store-of-value re-rating thesis, but more weeks of data are needed to confirm the signal isn't noise.
L2-L1 Rent and Governance Proposals 4
Arbitrum founder Steven Goldfeder proposed that Ethereum should 'adopt' its largest rollups (Arbitrum, Base, Robin Hood chain) by treating a critical bug in any of them as an Ethereum-level vulnerability that would trigger an L1 fork, with those L2s paying Ethereum a much higher rent (roughly 10-20% versus today's ~0.15%) and dropping their independent security councils in exchange.
thesis🗣 Steven Goldfeder (Arbitrum founder)conviction: mediumSteven GoldfederArbitrumBaseRobin Hood chainEthereum
So what: If adopted, this could materially increase Ethereum L1's fee capture from major L2s -- a structural catalyst worth tracking for ETH's revenue-based valuation case.
“Ethereum should adopt its largest roll-ups in the sense that a critical bug in arbitum base or Robin Hood chain should be treated as an Ethereum vulnerability and trigger an L1 fork just like an L1 bug would.” ▶ 47:57
David countered that Goldfeder's proposal risks overloading Ethereum's consensus and dependency surface, echoing Vitalik Buterin's earlier warning not to overload Ethereum consensus, and noted a simpler competing path already exists in native rollups, which inherently fork with L1 and start with no separate state or pre-existing distribution.
contrarian🗣 Davidconviction: mediumVitalik ButerinEthereumArbitrum
So what: Native rollups offer an alternative way to tighten L2-L1 alignment without the governance entanglement Goldfeder's proposal would introduce.
The hosts frame Goldfeder's proposal as effectively monetizing Ethereum's unique decentralized-governance and coordination capability, which no other blockchain ecosystem currently offers, while questioning whether Ethereum's core client teams would actually want to take on governance responsibility over L2 state changes just to increase ETH monetization.
framework🗣 Bankless hostconviction: exploratoryEthereumArbitrum
So what: Whether Ethereum's client teams are willing to expand consensus scope for monetization purposes is a key governance risk to watch before this proposal could advance.
The hosts estimated that if Ethereum L1 had captured a much larger share of Robin Hood chain's revenue (e.g., $100,000 instead of $1,538 in the first week), it would be bullish for ETH price in the near term, though David argued it would ultimately cap ETH's long-term valuation at a few hundred billion dollars rather than supporting a multi-trillion-dollar store-of-value valuation.
causal🗣 Davidconviction: mediumEthereumRobin Hood chain
So what: Investors betting on higher L2 rent capture for ETH should weigh the near-term price boost against a potentially lower long-run valuation ceiling if ETH becomes fee-revenue-priced.
“bearish as hell. You're talking about a $100 ETH price. Fees don't matter.” ▶ 53:47
Ethereum Foundation Spinoffs and DeFi Risk 5
The Ethereum Foundation's institutional privacy task force has spun off into ETH Systems, a for-profit venture financed by the same ETH treasury companies (Bitmine, Sharplink, Joseph Lubin) that fund earlier spinoffs ETH Labs and Ethereum Institutional, continuing a pattern of EF-adjacent entities being separately capitalized.
data_point🗣 Bankless hostconviction: highEthereum FoundationBitmineSharplinkJoseph Lubin
