So what: The proliferation of EF-adjacent for-profit spinoffs funded by ETH treasury companies signals where institutional Ethereum capital and talent are concentrating.
A five-year Ethereum Foundation researcher, Francesco, departed for ETH Labs, which the hosts describe as becoming a landing spot for EF talent whose mission no longer fits inside the Foundation -- a trend they view as healthy for the broader ecosystem.
anecdote🗣 Bankless hostconviction: mediumEthereum FoundationEthereum Labs
So what: The emergence of alternative outlets for EF talent could shape where core protocol development and research momentum shifts over time.
Despite broad crypto criticism that 'DAT' (digital asset treasury) companies are poor innovation, David argued they deserve credit specifically for financing alternative EF-adjacent entities ('Alt EFs'), calling that outcome phenomenal for Ethereum.
contrarian🗣 Davidconviction: highEthereum FoundationBitmine
So what: Even skeptics of the DAT trend broadly should note their capital has meaningfully funded Ethereum's institutional research and development ecosystem.
“A lot of people are talking a lot of [ __ ] about DATs, Ryan. Like DATs are just terrible. Terrible innovation. Fake innovation. At least for one thing with Ethereum. They financed alt EFS which I think is phenomenal.” ▶ 58:25
Contrary to the earlier 2026 narrative that AI-assisted hacking was driving a DeFi 'hack apocalypse,' data cited on the show shows April 2026 was the peak month for losses, and both the number and especially the dollar size of hacks have declined since, with attackers now picking off smaller 'abandonware' protocols as defenders also gain AI-driven hardening capability.
contrarian🗣 Hib (crypto analyst, cited)conviction: mediumDeFi
So what: The AI-hacking tail risk that had been weighing on DeFi/ETH sentiment appears overstated for now, removing one bearish overhang, though the risk could resurface with the next major AI capability jump.
“the DeFi hack apocalypse as a false alarm.” ▶ 59:23
A separate, underappreciated DeFi risk is 'vault permutation risk' -- yield vaults depositing into other vaults that deposit into further vaults, sometimes terminating in externally-owned accounts, creating opaque, layered counterparty chains behind seemingly simple yield products.
framework🗣 Bankless host (David)conviction: mediumDeFi
So what: Investors chasing vault yields should map underlying vault-of-vaults exposure rather than trusting the headline yield or protocol alone -- a growing, underpriced risk distinct from smart-contract exploits.
Buy-and-Burn Token Value Capture 3
David argued the buy-and-burn token model is 2026's dominant winning design, citing Hyperliquid (HYPE), Venice (VVV), Lit Protocol, and Jito (JTO, via its new JTX trading interface that burns 80% of fees) as leading examples, arguing the model works because it requires genuine fee revenue and demonstrates fiduciary commitment to token holders.
thesis🗣 Davidconviction: highHyperliquidVeniceLit ProtocolJito
So what: Tokens with credible buy-and-burn mechanics tied to real revenue may command valuation premiums; watch for more projects adopting the model as a bullish signal for token-holder alignment.
“The buy and burn token model is undefeated.” ▶ 1:01:52
Crypto commentator Anom argued buybacks and burns alone don't explain valuation: Hyperliquid generates $800 million in annualized revenue and trades at a $65 billion valuation, while Pump.fun generates $440 million in annualized revenue (roughly half of Hyperliquid's) but trades at only $1.4 billion, attributing the gap to a 'trust premium' since Hyperliquid launched with no VC allocation while Pump.fun raised a $1 billion ICO and never delivered a promised airdrop.
contrarian🗣 Anom (crypto commentator, cited)conviction: mediumHyperliquidPump.fun
So what: Token valuation premiums increasingly reflect a legitimacy/trust factor around team conduct and holder alignment, not just revenue or burn mechanics -- a qualitative factor investors should weight alongside financials.
“Hyperlid makes 800 million in annualized revenue. Pump Fund makes 440 annualized revenue, but Hype trades at 65 billion, whereas Pump trades at 1.4 billion.” ▶ 1:04:17
The hosts connect the trust-premium thesis to revenue durability: memecoin-launchpad trading fees (Pump.fun's core revenue) are viewed as less durable than perpetual-futures trading fees (Hyperliquid's core revenue), suggesting the market prices both business-model durability and social legitimacy when valuing buy-and-burn tokens.
framework🗣 Bankless hostconviction: mediumPump.funHyperliquid
So what: Revenue quality and durability, not just headline revenue figures, should factor into how investors value fee-generating, buy-and-burn tokens.
Is Bitcoin Going According to Plan? Gold, Saylor, Satoshi58 min30 insights2026-07-13
▶ Watch on YouTube
OG Bitcoiner Dan Held tells Bankless that Bitcoin's core code has stayed uncaptured even as its culture institutionalized around Saylor and ETFs, but argues the community broke its post-block-size-war promise to build real Bitcoin L2s, ceding DeFi to Ethereum and Solana, while flagging quantum signatures, Strategy's supply concentration, and Trump-linked narrative risk as the open threats ahead.
Institutionalization vs. Code Immutability · 4Michael Saylor, Strategy, and Supply Concentration · 6Geopolitical Narrative Risk: US, Trump, Russia, China · 3The Missed Bitcoin L2 / Scaling Opportunity · 3Privacy as an Application Layer, Not a Protocol Commitment · 4Protocol Roadmap: Quantum Threat and BIP Politics · 3Satoshi's Identity and Original Design Mistakes · 4Bitcoin vs. Gold: The Long Flip · 3
Institutionalization vs. Code Immutability 4
Bitcoin's underlying protocol code has not been altered or captured by institutions despite 15 years of mainstream adoption; institutions adapted to Bitcoin's rules rather than Bitcoin changing to accommodate them.
thesis🗣 Dan Heldconviction: highBitcoin
So what: Supports the thesis that Bitcoin's fixed monetary policy remains credible even as ETFs, banks, and governments pile in, reinforcing the digital-gold investment case.
“From Bitcoin's code perspective, it didn't bend the knee. The institutions bent the knee to bring Bitcoin into it.” ▶ 3:42
Bitcoin's price is the single best KPI for the network's success because it functions as an aggregate, real-time compression of belief, adoption, liquidity, resilience, and narrative into one number.
framework🗣 Dan Heldconviction: highBitcoin
So what: Investors tracking Bitcoin's health can treat price itself as the primary composite signal rather than chasing dozens of secondary on-chain metrics.
“So the price is a one-way hash function of all of the collective belief in it and that's what the price represents.” ▶ 7:20
US Bitcoin ownership penetration is roughly 5-10% of the population, implying around 40 million Americans have bought in, which Dan Held considers a major marketing success for a 15-year-old asset once associated with drugs and money laundering.
data_point🗣 Dan Heldconviction: mediumBitcoin
So what: A single-digit-to-low-double-digit penetration rate implies substantial remaining headroom for adoption-driven price appreciation if the trend continues.
“40 million people in the US alone... woke up and decided to go down the rabbit hole of questioning the nature of the reality so core to them like what is money? I think that's success.” ▶ 14:44
The share of Bitcoin holders who self-custody via private keys remains low and has not scaled proportionally with overall adoption, which Dan Held sees as an unresolved weak point relative to his ideal of widespread self-custody.
data_point🗣 Dan Heldconviction: mediumBitcoin
So what: Persistently low self-custody rates mean a growing share of Bitcoin's float sits with custodians (exchanges, ETFs), a structural counterparty-risk factor for long-term holders to weigh.
Michael Saylor, Strategy, and Supply Concentration 6
Michael Saylor has been a net positive for Bitcoin as an advocate and through Strategy's roughly $60 billion in Bitcoin purchases, even though Dan Held views his energy-related narratives as too esoteric.
thesis🗣 Dan Heldconviction: mediumMichael SaylorStrategyBitcoin
So what: Large corporate treasury buyers like Strategy have been a structural demand source that's hard to ignore when assessing Bitcoin's price drivers, though their narrative influence carries reputational risk for the asset.
Dan Held never invested in Bitcoin treasury companies (DATs) like Strategy or Stretch, and would not recommend Bitcoiners buy them instead of Bitcoin itself, despite being offered the CEO role at multiple such vehicles.
contrarian🗣 Dan Heldconviction: highStrategyMichael SaylorBitcoin
So what: A prominent Bitcoin insider explicitly favors direct Bitcoin exposure over leveraged corporate-treasury proxies, a signal for investors weighing MSTR/DAT premiums against holding BTC outright.
“You call me oldfashioned, but if you want to buy Bitcoin, just buy Bitcoin.” ▶ 18:32
Strategy's roughly 4% ownership of total Bitcoin supply is not concerning today, and even 10% would still sound low to most people, but concentration in the 40%+ range would represent a real centralization problem.
framework🗣 Dan Heldconviction: mediumStrategyMichael SaylorBitcoin
So what: Gives investors a rough mental threshold for when single-entity Bitcoin accumulation would start to threaten the network's decentralization narrative and, potentially, price stability.
Because Bitcoin is proof-of-work rather than proof-of-stake, a large holder attempting to manipulate the network (e.g., via miner concentration and a 51% attack) would have to destroy the value of their own upfront capital-intensive mining hardware, making concentrated attacks economically self-defeating in a way proof-of-stake systems are not.
causal🗣 Dan Heldconviction: highBitcoin
So what: Explains why large single-entity Bitcoin holdings (like Strategy's) are structurally less dangerous to network security than equivalent concentration would be under proof-of-stake, tempering centralization fears.
“Proof of work is provably burning capital to purchase machines up up front.” ▶ 21:23
Strategy's pace of Bitcoin accumulation has slowed to a near-halt, coinciding with the stock trading below its roughly $100 target/par reference price, which Dan Held reads as a bad sign.
data_point🗣 Dan Heldconviction: mediumStrategyMichael Saylor
So what: A pause in Strategy's buying combined with a depressed share price is a leading indicator to watch for reduced institutional treasury demand for Bitcoin.
≈ “It doesn't seem like he's going to be buying a lot of Bitcoin in the short term... trading below the $100 sort of target price, I don't think is a good omen.” paraphrase ▶ 25:55
The host raised a specific worry that Strategy's growing Bitcoin holdings create attention and branding centralization risk even if the underlying protocol is unaffected, using a hypothetical of one entity owning 50% of supply as the point where it would clearly become bad.
thesis🗣 hostconviction: exploratoryStrategyBitcoin
So what: Frames a scenario investors should monitor: even without technical risk, narrative capture by a single large holder could affect how markets and regulators perceive Bitcoin's neutrality.
Geopolitical Narrative Risk: US, Trump, Russia, China 3
Dan Held is surprised that Russia and China have not visibly accumulated large Bitcoin reserves as a hedge, especially since Russia has had significant traditional assets frozen due to the Ukraine war and could have used Bitcoin's fluidity to pay partners.
contrarian🗣 Dan Heldconviction: mediumBitcoinRussiaChina
So what: If sanctioned states began visibly accumulating Bitcoin as a reserve hedge, it would be a significant bullish demand catalyst; its apparent absence so far is a data point against that thesis materializing quickly.
Bitcoin's association with the United States and specifically with Donald Trump could turn into a negative narrative within roughly two years, as Trump's crypto-linked pump-and-dump-style ventures likely draw investigations after he leaves office.
prediction🗣 Dan Heldconviction: mediumDonald TrumpBitcoin
So what: Investors should watch for political/regulatory blowback tied to Trump-era crypto promotions as a potential source of reputational or narrative risk to Bitcoin, distinct from the underlying protocol's fundamentals.
The United States holding the world's largest gold reserves has not diminished gold's value, which Dan Held argues suggests the US government's growing Bitcoin reserve position should similarly not be seen as devaluing or compromising the asset.
causal🗣 Dan Heldconviction: mediumBitcoingold
So what: Counters the narrative that state-level accumulation is inherently bad for a scarce asset's credibility, using gold as a precedent case investors can reference.
The Missed Bitcoin L2 / Scaling Opportunity 3
The Bitcoin community broke the promise it made during the Bitcoin Cash block-size-war split to build robust, trustless layer-2s anchored to Bitcoin's base layer -- basic scripting upgrades like OP_CAT that would have enabled that were never implemented.
contrarian🗣 Dan Heldconviction: highBitcoinBitcoin Cash
So what: Explains why Bitcoin has structurally weaker DeFi/L2 infrastructure than Ethereum or Solana, a gap investors should not expect to close quickly given the community's track record on this issue.
“I do think Bitcoin, the Bitcoin community has failed in that regard.” ▶ 27:05
Because most current Bitcoin L2s carry weaker security assumptions than Bitcoin's L1 rather than true trustless bridging, they never captured the DeFi and smart-contract demand that instead flowed to Ethereum and Solana, whose market caps represent that missed value transfer.
thesis🗣 Dan Heldconviction: mediumBitcoinEthereumSolana
So what: Frames Ethereum's and Solana's valuations partly as a proxy for the DeFi opportunity Bitcoin failed to capture, a lens for assessing relative upside if Bitcoin ever does ship trustless L2s.
Had Bitcoin built genuinely trustless L2s, it could have captured lending, borrowing, and other basic DeFi demand against Bitcoin as collateral, which Dan Held believes would have been additive to (not distracting from) the digital-gold and store-of-value narrative.
framework🗣 Dan Heldconviction: mediumBitcoin
So what: Suggests that future Bitcoin protocol upgrades enabling trustless borrowing/lending against BTC could be a real re-rating catalyst by making the asset more productive without diluting its core narrative.
Privacy as an Application Layer, Not a Protocol Commitment 4
The word 'cash' in the Bitcoin whitepaper was cipherpunk jargon referring to an irreversible, private transaction between two parties, not a claim that Bitcoin was designed for everyday retail payments -- a widely misunderstood point about Satoshi's original intent.
data_point🗣 Dan Heldconviction: mediumBitcoinSatoshi Nakamoto
So what: Reframes debates about Bitcoin's 'failure' as everyday payment cash; the original design goal was closer to irreversible settlement finality with privacy, which Bitcoin largely achieves.
“The cipher punks don't use the word cash to mean everyday transactions. That's a huge misconception.” ▶ 31:50
Perfect on-chain privacy is fundamentally in tension with the auditability that makes Bitcoin's 21 million hard cap credible, so Bitcoin deliberately keeps privacy off the base layer to protect its core monetary-policy value proposition.
framework🗣 Dan Heldconviction: highBitcoin
So what: Explains why Bitcoin is unlikely to add strong native L1 privacy despite cipherpunk roots, meaning investors should not expect a Zcash-style privacy pivot and should evaluate privacy needs via separate application-layer tools.
“You cannot have perfect privacy on an L1 and auditability and Bitcoin's core value prop is around the 21 million hardcap being a credible monetary policy.” ▶ 36:29
Zcash's 2025 inflation-related bug inside its privacy pool (not an actual unit-supply inflation) validated Bitcoin's long-standing reluctance to build deep, unauditable privacy features directly into its base layer.
causal🗣 Dan Heldconviction: mediumZcashBitcoin
So what: A concrete recent case investors can point to when evaluating the security trade-offs of privacy-coin architectures versus Bitcoin's more conservative, auditable design.
The comparatively tiny market capitalizations of privacy-focused coins like Zcash relative to Bitcoin show that real-world user demand for strong on-chain transaction privacy is much lower than cipherpunk ideology would suggest.
data_point🗣 Dan Heldconviction: mediumZcashBitcoin
So what: Tempers expectations that privacy features alone would be a major value driver for a crypto asset; market pricing suggests it's a secondary consideration for most holders.
Protocol Roadmap: Quantum Threat and BIP Politics 3
BIP 360 proposes upgrading Bitcoin's transaction signatures to post-quantum-secure cryptography to prevent private keys from being exposed and stolen; because post-quantum signatures are larger in byte size, adopting it will raise transaction costs and could reopen the block-size debate.
data_point🗣 Dan Heldconviction: mediumBitcoin
So what: Signals a concrete future protocol change (BIP 360) worth tracking, along with a likely secondary effect of higher on-chain fees and renewed block-size-limit debate.
Dan Held estimates a cryptographically-relevant quantum computer capable of breaking Bitcoin signatures ('Q-day') is at minimum about five years away, though AI-accelerated progress in quantum computing research could pull that timeline forward, and he wants community consensus on a migration path within the next few years.
prediction🗣 Dan Heldconviction: mediumBitcoin
So what: Gives investors a rough timeline (roughly 5+ years, possibly sooner given AI) for when quantum risk to Bitcoin's cryptography becomes acute, useful for assessing urgency of protocol-level fixes.
“I think it's still five years away probably at a at a minimum but even then it still might take us quite a while to agree upon how we upgrade and the process of migration.” ▶ 44:42
BIP 110, a fringe proposal to censor arbitrary data from Bitcoin blocks championed largely by one controversial core developer, has been rejected by miners and businesses and could trigger a minority chain fork if its backers push it through, which Dan Held would welcome as a way to remove them from the ecosystem.
anecdote🗣 Dan Heldconviction: mediumBitcoin
So what: A low-probability but watchable governance flashpoint; a BIP 110-driven fork would be a non-event for Bitcoin's core value proposition according to Held, useful context if headlines about a Bitcoin 'civil war' resurface.
Satoshi's Identity and Original Design Mistakes 4
Dan Held finds the Finding Satoshi documentary's conclusion that Hal Finney was the primary author credible ('Hal Finney fits the bill perfectly'), but is skeptical that Finney needed Len Sassaman's help specifically to write the whitepaper, viewing that co-author claim as the documentary's weakest link.
exploratory🗣 Dan Heldconviction: lowSatoshi NakamotoHal FinneyLen Sassaman
So what: Offers a credible insider's partial endorsement of the Finding Satoshi documentary's theory while flagging exactly where its evidence is weakest, useful for readers weighing the documentary's claims.
≈ “Halfy, it definitely feels like Hellf wrote the code... the idea that how Finny couldn't write a white paper though, that seems a little weak.” paraphrase ▶ 48:22
Dan Held believes it is becoming less, not more, likely that Satoshi Nakamoto will ever be conclusively identified, because emails and written communication logs that could prove identity are steadily decaying or being erased even as forensic/AI analysis tools improve.
contrarian🗣 Dan Heldconviction: mediumSatoshi Nakamoto
So what: Pushes back on the assumption that better technology will eventually crack Satoshi's identity; the decaying evidence base may be the binding constraint instead.
“I think it's more likely we would have found him sooner. And I think the more time that progresses there, it's less likely that we find him.” ▶ 47:28
Dan Held argues it would be net negative for Bitcoin if Satoshi Nakamoto were definitively identified, because turning the founder from a pseudonymous myth into a concrete real person would undermine the leaderless, no-one-to-look-to-for-direction ethos Satoshi intentionally built into the project.
thesis🗣 Dan Heldconviction: mediumSatoshi NakamotoBitcoin
So what: Explains why continued anonymity is arguably a feature supporting Bitcoin's decentralized governance narrative, relevant to how investors should interpret any future Satoshi-identification claims.
Dan Held views Satoshi's choice of a 21 million (rather than 21 billion or trillion) unit supply as an overly bearish design mistake that created a lasting 'unit bias' problem making Bitcoin feel psychologically too expensive to new buyers, alongside an overly aggressive early issuance schedule that front-loaded coin supply instead of stretching out the network's long-term security budget.
framework🗣 Dan Heldconviction: mediumSatoshi NakamotoBitcoin
So what: Highlights unit bias as a real, still-relevant psychological headwind to retail adoption, and flags the security-budget trade-off from early aggressive issuance as a long-term miner-incentive risk worth monitoring.
Bitcoin vs. Gold: The Long Flip 3
Dan Held predicts Bitcoin will eventually surpass gold's total market capitalization, but not for another 10 to 15 years, driven by a generational wealth and belief transfer as boomer gold-holders die off and millennials and Gen Xers who favor Bitcoin gain control of institutional and policy-setting positions.
prediction🗣 Dan Heldconviction: mediumBitcoingold
So what: Sets a long-horizon (10-15 year) framework and generational-adoption mechanism for the 'Bitcoin flips gold' thesis that investors can use to calibrate position sizing and time horizon rather than expecting a near-term flip.
Dan Held argues that as SpaceX's Starship program matures and dramatically lowers the cost of space travel, economically viable asteroid mining could become tangible within his lifetime, and once markets start pricing in that future supply threat, gold could drop as much as 25% in a single day as its scarcity narrative breaks.
prediction🗣 Dan Heldconviction: lowSpaceXgoldBitcoin
So what: A speculative but specific tail-risk scenario for gold investors: credible progress on asteroid mining (via SpaceX Starship) is a catalyst worth monitoring as a potential sharp negative repricing event for gold, and a relative tailwind for Bitcoin's 'digital gold' narrative.
“I bet gold drops 25% in a day, you know, like that's that's going to be a death sentence for gold.” ▶ 55:18
Even non-self-custodied Bitcoin exposure, such as buying spot Bitcoin ETFs, still counts as a genuine win for Bitcoin's adoption and network effect, though Dan Held still prefers self-custody as the ideal.
framework🗣 Dan Heldconviction: mediumBitcoin
So what: Validates ETF-driven demand as real, durable adoption for Bitcoin's network effect rather than a lesser or illegitimate form of ownership, relevant to assessing the durability of ETF-era inflows.
How JTX Plans to Move Price Discovery Onchain52 min26 insights2026-07-15
▶ Watch on YouTube
Jito founder Lucas Bruder explains JTX, a CEX-grade 'prosumer' trading terminal Jito is launching on Solana that leans on Jito's BAM block-building infrastructure and Solana's new generation of proprietary market-maker AMMs ('prop AMMs') to deliver sub-basis-point spreads and route 80% of trading fees into JTO buybacks. He argues Solana's prop-AMM architecture is finally solving on-chain price discovery for major assets and tokenized equities, and that SOL is undervalued given accelerating protocol iteration and pending deflationary tokenomics changes.
Solana's Tokenized-Stock Momentum · 2JTX: A Prosumer Trading Terminal for Solana · 6Jito's Infrastructure Edge Feeds JTX Execution · 4Prop AMMs as the New On-Chain Price-Discovery Engine · 4Toxic Prop AMM Behavior and Aggregator Safeguards · 2JTO Tokenomics and Value Accrual · 2Solana Tokenomics Reform and the SOL Investment Thesis · 4JTX Rollout Mechanics and Roadmap · 2
Solana's Tokenized-Stock Momentum 2
Tokenized stocks on Solana have on some days generated higher trading volume than meme coins, marking a shift from Solana's reputation as primarily a meme-coin chain toward legitimate tokenized-equity issuance.
data_point🗣 Lucas Bruder, Jito founder/CEOconviction: highSolanatokenized stocksmeme coins
So what: A volume mix shifting from meme coins to tokenized equities is a concrete, trackable signal of Solana's transition toward more durable, institutional-adjacent asset flow.
“There's a few days where tokenized stock volume is higher than meme coins, and it's cool to see that flip.” ▶ 2:03
Backpack currently leads Solana's tokenized-equity venues by trading volume despite listing only around five assets versus competitors offering tens or hundreds, suggesting the market-share picture in tokenized stocks is still unsettled and likely to shift as rivals expand asset coverage.
data_point🗣 Lucas Bruder, Jito founder/CEOconviction: mediumBackpackHXROOndotokenized stocksSolana
So what: Early leadership in tokenized-equity volume could flip quickly as competitors like HXRO and Ondo scale up listed-asset counts.
JTX: A Prosumer Trading Terminal for Solana 6
Jito is building JTX, a 'prosumer' trading terminal that deliberately hides token-level metadata (holder distribution, freeze status) that meme-coin traders obsess over, targeting traders of roughly $1M+ market-cap tokens, tokenized equities, and major assets rather than brand-new meme-coin launches.
thesis🗣 Lucas Bruder, Jito founder/CEOconviction: highJTXJitoSolanatokenized equities
So what: JTX is a deliberate segment bet away from degen meme trading toward institutional-adjacent flow, which could pull volume from centralized exchanges rather than from Solana's existing meme platforms.
“We're not building for the token that launched 5 seconds ago where you need to know the distribution.” ▶ 9:53
Average hold times on existing Solana meme-coin trading platforms are under a minute, and rarely exceed 10 minutes even at the upper end, which Jito cites as evidence that current Solana retail UX is built for ultra-short-term speculation rather than the investor-grade experience JTX targets.
data_point🗣 Lucas Bruder, Jito founder/CEOconviction: highSolanameme coinsJTX
So what: Quantifies how speculative current Solana retail flow is today, giving investors a baseline to judge whether JTX actually shifts trading behavior toward longer holds.
“the average hold time is like less than a minute. And like at the upper end of it, it's like less than 10 minutes.” ▶ 9:45
JTX uses Privy for key management, letting users sign in via email or set up passkeys instead of managing seed phrases, aiming for onboarding friction closer to a centralized exchange than a typical Solana DEX.
data_point🗣 Lucas Bruder, Jito founder/CEOconviction: highJTXPrivy
So what: Lower onboarding friction is a testable adoption lever; watch JTX signup conversion versus wallet-based Solana DEX competitors.
“We're using privy for key pair management so you can sign in through email.” ▶ 6:58
JTX will not launch with perpetuals but plans to integrate Phoenix Perps shortly after spot launch, followed by prediction markets, once the core spot trading experience is in a good state.
prediction🗣 Lucas Bruder, Jito founder/CEOconviction: mediumJTXPhoenix Perpsprediction markets
So what: A Phoenix Perps integration announcement would be the next expansion catalyst to watch for Jito's consumer product suite.
“It won't be a launch, but shortly after launch, we are looking at Phoenix perps and we'll be integrating that.” ▶ 16:08
JTX is positioned to eventually compete directly with centralized exchanges and traditional venues like Nasdaq and the New York Stock Exchange over a multi-year horizon, not just with other Solana apps such as Jupiter or pump.fun, even though the near-term go-to-market is focused narrowly on the existing Solana-native, crypto-Twitter audience.
thesis🗣 Lucas Bruder, Jito founder/CEOconviction: highJTXNasdaqNew York Stock ExchangeJupiterpump.fun
So what: Signals Jito's total addressable market ambition extends far beyond crypto-native trading, which is relevant context for long-term JTO valuation.
“I would say the centralized exchanges are our main competition.” ▶ 16:53
Jito is considering building an in-house custodial fiat on/off-ramp (debit card, credit card, bank account) for JTX to avoid the high fees third-party on-ramps charge non-custodial platforms to cover fraud risk, though this idea was raised by the host and Lucas characterized it as still exploratory.
exploratory🗣 Lucas Bruder, Jito founder/CEOconviction: exploratoryJTX
So what: A custodial on-ramp would be a significant strategic pivot for a non-custodial-focused team; worth watching for a formal product announcement.
“rolling it in-house could be interesting. There's definitely that would be something completely new for us.” ▶ 19:13
Jito's Infrastructure Edge Feeds JTX Execution 4
Jito has been the number one validator client and block builder on Solana since 2022 and holds the deepest liquidity in its liquid staking token JitoSOL, which the company frames as the credibility base for verticalizing from infrastructure into the consumer-facing JTX trading app.
data_point🗣 Lucas Bruder, Jito founder/CEOconviction: highJitoJitoSOLSolana
So what: Infrastructure dominance being leveraged into a new consumer revenue line is a template other infra-first crypto companies may replicate; JTX's success is a proxy for whether that leverage works.
“we're the number one validator client block builder on Solana since like 2022.” ▶ 4:37
Inside Jito's BAM block-building system, a 'maker priority plugin' places proprietary market makers (prop AMMs) at the top of the block across Solana assets, letting them quote tighter spreads without fear of being sandwiched or picked off.
framework🗣 Lucas Bruder, Jito founder/CEOconviction: highBAMJitoprop AMMs
So what: Explains the structural mechanism behind Solana's tightening on-chain spreads — a block-building-level moat that competing chains would need to replicate to match execution quality.
“all the uh makers for these prop AMMs across all these different assets on Solana, they basically get put at the top of the block on Solana in inside the BAM blocks.” ▶ 13:01
JTX includes a 'Good Trade' feature that, on every swap, simulates the same trade on top centralized exchanges in real time and shows the user how much cheaper their Solana execution was, turning Solana's execution-quality claim into a per-trade, user-visible metric rather than a Twitter talking point.
data_point🗣 Lucas Bruder, Jito founder/CEOconviction: highJTXJitoSolana
So what: If the savings shown are consistently positive, this becomes a powerful retention and marketing tool; worth tracking published Good Trade savings data after launch.
“we will look at the output amount and we will run that same trade on the best centralized exchanges at the same time and show you how much money you saved on chain compared to Solana.” ▶ 13:57
Jito selectively avoids routing JTX transactions to specific Solana validators known for exploitative behavior such as sandwiching, an opinionated infrastructure choice learned from operating BAM rather than treating all validators as interchangeable.
causal🗣 Lucas Bruder, Jito founder/CEOconviction: highJitoBAMJTX
So what: Reduces MEV-related slippage for JTX users, a differentiator versus routing-agnostic wallets or DEX front-ends.
Prop AMMs as the New On-Chain Price-Discovery Engine 4
A new generation of single-market-maker 'prop AMMs' (SolFi from the Ellipsis team, BisonFi run by Jump Crypto, Humidifi, Scorch, Tesera, succeeding the earlier Lifinity) put market-maker curve logic directly on-chain, and can reprice for roughly sub-100 compute units versus about 40,000 compute units needed to requote a position on an on-chain order book.
data_point🗣 Lucas Bruder, Jito founder/CEOconviction: highSolFiEllipsisBisonFiJump CryptoHumidifiScorchTeseraLifinitySolana
So what: This ~400x compute-cost advantage is the structural technical reason prop AMMs beat order books on Solana — a durable architectural edge, not a temporary liquidity phenomenon.
“I think the cheapest prop AMM oracle update is like sub 100 cost units. Where if you want to make a trade on an order book, it's like 40,000.” ▶ 27:30
Unlike traditional constant-product AMMs (e.g., Uniswap-style pools), which rely on external arbitrageurs to correct mispricing after the fact, prop AMMs have an active market maker adjusting the curve directly, making them structurally capable of genuine price discovery rather than merely tracking an external reference price.
contrarian🗣 Lucas Bruder, Jito founder/CEOconviction: highprop AMMsUniswapSolana
So what: Bearish read-through for vanilla constant-product AMMs as venues for new-asset price discovery going forward; capital and innovation should keep migrating to prop-AMM designs.
“I think this is uh super bearish on just like traditional AMMs because those those can never do price discovery because they rely on people arbitraging the price to bring it in line.”
Aggregated prop-AMM liquidity on the SOL/USDC pair (viewable on sandwich.me) currently shows sub-one-basis-point spreads for 100 SOL of depth, and Lucas expects similarly tight spreads to extend to Bitcoin, Zcash, Hyperliquid's token, and newly listed tokenized equities like SpaceX.
data_point🗣 Lucas Bruder, Jito founder/CEOconviction: highSOLUSDCBitcoinZcashSpaceXsandwich.me
So what: Sandwich.me provides a concrete, publicly checkable metric to monitor whether Solana's execution-quality claims hold up as more assets list.
“you can see here that for 100 SOL depth on Solana right now, you can get less than a bip spread. That's just like insane.” ▶ 32:39
The prop-AMM top-of-block 'maker priority' pattern pioneered on Solana via BAM is now spreading to Ethereum L2s like Base and to Ethereum itself, where block builders such as Titan give prop AMMs priority to produce spreads tighter than the tightest centralized exchange, and similar dynamics are emerging on Robinhood's chain.
causal🗣 Lucas Bruder, Jito founder/CEOconviction: mediumEthereumBaseTitanRobinhood chainSolanaBAM
So what: If the pattern spreads to EVM chains, Solana's current execution-quality edge could narrow over time — a competitive dynamic worth monitoring rather than assuming permanent.
“that's been something really cool to see that like that's born on Solana and then now we're seeing it on L2s and um Ethereum” ▶ 28:53
Toxic Prop AMM Behavior and Aggregator Safeguards 2
Some Solana prop AMMs previously engaged in 'bait transaction' tactics — posting an attractive quote and then pulling it before a retail trade could execute against it — a toxic pattern publicly called out by 0x/Matcha's Will Warren.
anecdote🗣 hostconviction: medium0xMatchaWill Warrenprop AMMsSolana
So what: Illustrates a real adversarial-design risk within prop-AMM market structure that retail traders on Solana faced a few months prior to this conversation.
“they would set the price with a bait transaction, but then they would yoink the bait transaction” ▶ 43:20
The bait-transaction problem was mitigated primarily through public social shaming of offending market makers plus distribution-layer aggregators like Jupiter and DFlow throttling or disconnecting flow to prop AMMs exhibiting toxic behavior, and increasingly by aggregators moving their routing logic fully on-chain so quotes reference live chain state instead of a stale off-chain snapshot during the one-to-two-second settlement window.
causal🗣 Lucas Bruder, Jito founder/CEOconviction: highJupiterDFlowprop AMMsSolana
So what: Aggregator gatekeeping and on-chain routing function as an emerging self-policing mechanism in Solana market structure, reducing toxicity risk for retail flow without requiring new regulation.
“we're seeing more uh, AMMs or more of these uh, aggregators put their actual routing logic onto Solana.” ▶ 45:48
JTO Tokenomics and Value Accrual 2
80% of all JTX trading revenue will be used to buy JTO tokens on the open market and send them to the Jito DAO treasury rather than being collected as USDC or volatile fee tokens, with the remaining 20% reinvested into the business.
data_point🗣 Lucas Bruder, Jito founder/CEOconviction: highJTOJTXJito DAO
So what: JTX trading volume becomes a directly quantifiable driver of JTO buy pressure — a metric investors can track against JTO's price and FDV over time.
“80% of the revenue from JTX will go to the DAO.” ▶ 19:47
JTO's fully diluted valuation was cited at roughly $600-700 million during the conversation, versus SOL's market cap discussed in the tens of billions, a gap the host frames as a potential asymmetric setup if JTX successfully routes meaningful trading-fee flow into JTO buy pressure.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: mediumJTOSOL
So what: The JTO-to-SOL valuation gap is a concrete number for investors evaluating whether JTX's fee-to-buyback mechanism could re-rate JTO relative to SOL.
“it's the FDV right now is probably like 600 or 700 million.” ▶ 21:27
Solana Tokenomics Reform and the SOL Investment Thesis 4
Solana is weighing SIMD governance proposals that would roughly double the rate at which staking inflation decreases and introduce a resource-based transaction burn tied to network usage, rather than today's flat per-transaction burn, changes Lucas expects to reduce sell pressure and make SOL more deflationary as prop-AMM-driven transaction volume grows.
prediction🗣 Lucas Bruder, Jito founder/CEOconviction: highSolanaSOLSIMD
So what: A concrete governance catalyst to track — passage of these SIMDs would directly tighten SOL's token supply dynamics and reduce emissions-driven sell pressure.
“there's one that will double disinflation.” ▶ 22:37
Solana's current transaction/priority-fee burn is a flat fee driven purely by transaction count rather than computational intensity, meaning heavy and light transactions burn the same amount today — a design gap the pending resource-based-fee proposal aims to fix.
data_point🗣 Lucas Bruder, Jito founder/CEOconviction: highSolanaSOL
So what: Quantifies a current inefficiency in SOL's fee/burn design; resolving it would let compute-heavy prop-AMM activity contribute proportionally more to burn.
“Yes straight transaction volume.” ▶ 24:32
Jito's CEO argues Solana is uniquely undervalued relative to the rest of crypto because it is iterating faster than any other ecosystem, citing that core client teams are set to roughly double average block size from 60 million to 100 million within about a month, alongside compounding improvements in tokenomics, prop-AMM execution, and tokenized-equity issuance.
thesis🗣 Lucas Bruder, Jito founder/CEOconviction: highSolanaSOLAnzaFiredancerJito
So what: The block-size doubling within roughly a month is a concrete near-term technical catalyst to watch as a leading indicator for the broader Solana bull thesis.
“we're going to go from 60 million to 100 million block size within the next month or so.” ▶ 50:45
Roughly around the time of the Jito airdrop, Solana's transaction pipeline was unreliable enough that most users had to land transactions through Jito's infrastructure to succeed, illustrating how much core-protocol reliability has improved since then via client teams Anza and Firedancer alongside Jito.
anecdote🗣 Lucas Bruder, Jito founder/CEOconviction: highSolanaJitoAnzaFiredancer
So what: Provides historical context for how recent and rapid Solana's reliability turnaround has been, useful for judging how durable the current execution-quality improvements are.
“If you used the chain back then, it was just like a nightmare to use. Like transactions wouldn't land.” ▶ 25:54
JTX Rollout Mechanics and Roadmap 2
JTX access is being gated by referral count during rollout starting the week of July 14-15, 2026 — users in roughly the top 10% by referrals get earlier access — with the initial release explicitly described as an MVP ahead of planned data features, equities, perps, and prediction-market expansions.
data_point🗣 Lucas Bruder, Jito founder/CEOconviction: highJTXJito
So what: The referral-driven waitlist is itself a growth-loop distribution mechanic worth watching for early adoption and viral-growth metrics.
“we're going by the number of referrals that you have. So if you're in the top like 10% or something, you'll get access earlier.” ▶ 47:16
Lucas Bruder expects that within roughly 6 to 9 months, any stock from any country in the world will be tradable on-chain, contingent on regulatory clarity and a hoped-for SEC exemption.
prediction🗣 Lucas Bruder, Jito founder/CEOconviction: mediumSECtokenized equitiesJTX
So what: Sets a specific, checkable forward timeline for the global tokenized-equities buildout that investors can monitor for progress or slippage.
“I think within the next probably 6 to 9 months, you will be able to trade any stock in any country in the world on chain.” ▶ 48:46

Unchained · Laura Shin 3 episode(s) · 91 insights

Why Taylor Monahan Thinks a $23M Crypto Hack Was by North Korea: Uneasy Money90 min28 insights2026-07-17
▶ Watch on YouTube
A casual three-host crypto roundtable (Uneasy Money) covering CAP's stable-drop payout controversy, Robin Hood chain's meme-coin-dominated early activity and the revived Ethereum L2 settlement-fee debate, an Ethereum Foundation spinout (ETH systems), Coinbase's Base app handoff from Jesse to Kobe, a Barnbridge DAO governance attack draining stale token approvals, and — the episode's namesake story — a $23M derivatives-protocol hack that security researcher Taylor Monahan says bears North Korea's behavioral signature based on eight hours of non-stop, untraceable fund movement.
CAP Stable Drop Controversy & Incentive Design · 5Robin Hood Chain & the Ethereum Settlement-Fee Debate · 6Ethereum Foundation Spinouts & Execution Culture · 3Coinbase's Base Leadership Handoff · 2Barnbridge Governance Attack & Approval Hygiene · 3Ostium/Alium $23M Hack & North Korea Attribution · 7Crypto Twitter Sentiment & Market-Cycle Psychology · 2
CAP Stable Drop Controversy & Incentive Design 5
CAP cut its promised "stable drop" reward from an implied $11-12M (5% of token value priced in February) down to a $4M budget after the broader market downturn crushed its token's implied valuation, but chose to make early users whole rather than pay out a shrunken pro-rata reward.
anecdote🗣 guest (CAP co-founder)conviction: highCAP
So what: Shows how quickly published token-based reward commitments can be repriced once a bear market hits — treat any protocol's stated airdrop/reward figures as provisional until distribution actually happens.
“let's just make everybody whole so at least nobody lost any money” ▶ 5:28
CAP reports that roughly 98% of its current TVL now comes from institutional counterparties with multi-month commercial deals rather than points-farming retail depositors, following the wind-down of its points program.
data_point🗣 guest (CAP co-founder)conviction: highCAP
So what: Institutional-sourced TVL is stickier through downturns than mercenary farmed liquidity — a useful diligence check when comparing DeFi protocols' TVL quality, not just size.
“today 98% of our TVL comes from people that are not farming points” ▶ 12:29
CAP's team argues crypto points programs are structurally flawed because the marketing-expense budget they represent is effectively uncapped — projects can attract far more TVL than they can productively deploy, and that liquidity evaporates once the points period ends.
framework🗣 guest (CAP co-founder)conviction: mediumCAP
So what: When evaluating a protocol's TVL growth, check whether it's driven by an open-ended points program — that liquidity is temporary and can vanish the moment incentives stop.
“this marketing expense is now uncapped” ▶ 8:41
CAP's team now advises that in the current low-interest-rate crypto environment, new projects are better off skipping points/incentive programs entirely and instead striking direct TVL deals with liquid funds.
contrarian🗣 guest (CAP co-founder)conviction: mediumCAP
So what: Signals a shift away from airdrop-farming bootstrapping toward institutional-first liquidity deals as the preferred launch playbook this cycle — relevant for handicapping which new protocols will have durable vs. mercenary TVL.
“it is much cheaper for a new project to skip on all the incentives” ▶ 20:08
The host frames protocol reward design as an inescapable trade-off: fixed-dollar commitments reduce user uncertainty but create default risk if funding falls short (as happened to CAP), while token-denominated rewards can always be paid but carry unknown value — the real lesson is communicating that risk clearly, not eliminating it.
framework🗣 hostconviction: highCAP
So what: A reusable lens for evaluating any protocol's incentive/reward structure before committing capital or time to farm it.
Robin Hood Chain & the Ethereum Settlement-Fee Debate 6
In its first two weeks live, Robin Hood chain's dominant on-chain activity has been meme-coin trading rather than the tokenized-equities or DeFi use cases its launch was framed around.
data_point🗣 guest (CAP co-founder)conviction: mediumRobin Hood chain
So what: Watch whether Robin Hood diversifies volume into its stated goals (on-chain yield, tokenized stocks) or the chain's activity fades the way other short-lived "hot L2" launches have.
“the biggest I guess actually activity that we've seen just like exponentially go up over the last well two weeks has been the mostly the meme coins” ▶ 36:52
Robin Hood chain's integration of Morpho for on-chain lending, following Morpho's earlier Coinbase integration, is viewed by the panel as a strong signal that Morpho is becoming the default lending rail for retail-facing consumer chains.
thesis🗣 hostconviction: mediumMorphoRobin Hood chainCoinbase
So what: Morpho's recurring wins as the embedded lending layer for major retail platforms support a thesis that it's consolidating distribution advantage in consumer DeFi lending.
“let's talk about morpho they've been getting some very good integrations you coinbase and now what they did with Robin Hood that's that's amazing”
One panelist argues Ethereum "unequivocally" fails to capture enough of the value that L2s like Arbitrum-based Robin Hood chain generate for themselves while paying minimal settlement fees back to Ethereum mainnet, and that Ethereum should try to charge L2s more.
contrarian🗣 co-hostconviction: mediumEthereumRobin Hood chainArbitrum
So what: If Ethereum moves to raise L1 settlement fees on L2s, it would directly compress L2 economics — a potential catalyst/risk to monitor for ETH value-accrual bulls.
“it does not capture enough of that value. Like unequivocally it should capture more.” ▶ 48:32
A counter-argument raised is that forcing L2s to pay more for Ethereum settlement could push weaker L2s to become fully independent L1s, which would be healthy because most current L2s are not meaningfully decentralized and effectively lean on Ethereum's security brand.
contrarian🗣 co-hostconviction: mediumEthereum
So what: A rise in Ethereum settlement fees could trigger consolidation/shakeout among low-quality L2s — worth tracking L2 decentralization claims as a leading indicator of which chains would survive that scenario.
“I promise you like the 85th L2 is not viable as no one if they had to actually like produce their own blocks and have validators” ▶ 49:07
A panelist argues Ethereum shouldn't rush to monetize L2 settlement, comparing it to Amazon's long unprofitable growth phase, and proposes Ethereum's real KPI should be the number of companies building/settling on it — since on any non-Ethereum chain, accessing real capital still requires paying counterparties in that chain's native token.
framework🗣 guest (CAP co-founder)conviction: mediumEthereum
So what: Reframes ETH's investment case away from near-term fee capture toward platform adoption breadth — a bullish counter-narrative to the "Ethereum is undercharging" critique.
“I think our KPI for Ethereums should be how many companies are posting to Ethereum because the reality is if you're not building on Ethereum, you're paying in tokens.” ▶ 45:48
Panelists specifically praised Robin Hood for building on Ethereum/Arbitrum rather than launching a fully proprietary chain the way Stripe did with Tempo, framing that choice as better for long-term liquidity and interoperability even if it means Ethereum captures less direct fee revenue.
thesis🗣 guest (CAP co-founder)conviction: mediumRobin Hood chainEthereumStripe
So what: Positions "staying within the Ethereum ecosystem" as a bullish differentiator for evaluating new fintech-launched chains, versus fully sovereign chains that fragment liquidity.
“at least at least Robin Hood is is building on on Ethereum and they're not making their own chain like Stripe” ▶ 46:08
Ethereum Foundation Spinouts & Execution Culture 3
A new entity, ETH systems, is spinning out of Ethereum-Foundation-adjacent institutional and privacy work, backed by Joe Lubin, Sharp(Link), and BitMine, making it the third such EF-linked spinout alongside ETH Labs and an earlier institutional team.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: mediumETH systemsEthereum FoundationJoe LubinBitMine
So what: Track whether privatized, better-funded EF spinouts ship institutional/privacy infrastructure faster than the Foundation itself did — a read on whether Ethereum's execution bottleneck is organizational rather than technical.
“it's actually a pretty stellar lineup of folks. Once again, backed by all sorts all sorts of the the ETH dieards.” ▶ 25:19
Panelists argue Ethereum's culture over-indexes on research relative to shipping, describing the ecosystem as a community of researchers rather than shippers, and say regaining confidence in ETH requires visibly executing on already-researched ideas.
contrarian🗣 co-hostconviction: highEthereumEthereum Foundation
So what: A recurring bear-case critique worth weighing against any thesis that more EF spinouts alone will fix Ethereum's shipping velocity.
“we're a we're a community of researchers it seems at FL and not a community of shippers” ▶ 29:33
A panelist argues that current infighting around the Ethereum Foundation is driven primarily by ETH's depressed price rather than by the substance of any organizational dispute, asserting the same disagreements would generate no drama if ETH traded at $10k.
causal🗣 co-hostconviction: mediumETHEthereum Foundation
So what: Implies EF-related governance drama is a lagging indicator of ETH price sentiment rather than a leading fundamental signal — discount headline-driven "Ethereum Foundation in chaos" narratives accordingly.
“if the same thing was happening at Eve, but Eve was 10k” ▶ 28:20
Coinbase's Base Leadership Handoff 2
Coinbase's Jesse handed day-to-day control of the Base consumer app (the wallet product, distinct from the broader Base network) to Kobe, a move the panel reads as bullish given Kobe's reputation for hands-on community engagement, including personally answering user complaints on social media.
thesis🗣 hostconviction: mediumCoinbaseBaseKobe
So what: Worth watching for whether Base's consumer app shifts toward faster, more community-responsive product decisions under Kobe versus Coinbase's broader corporate product culture.
Panelists noted that Jesse's heavy promotion of Coinbase's "creator coins" feature was unpopular both internally and among users, and read the handoff of app-specific responsibilities to Kobe as a tacit acknowledgment that push hadn't landed.
anecdote🗣 co-hostconviction: lowCoinbaseBase
So what: Suggests Coinbase may deprioritize creator-coin-style features going forward under new Base app leadership.
≈ “I wasn't a really big fan of the whole creator coins thing that he kept pushing over and over again” paraphrase
Barnbridge Governance Attack & Approval Hygiene 3
Barnbridge, a five-to-six-year-old DAO-governed DeFi protocol previously targeted by SEC enforcement, was drained in multiple waves this week (roughly $600,000-$800,000 per wave) after an attacker acquired enough governance tokens to pass proposals updating the protocol's proxy contracts, then swept funds from wallets that still held old, unrevoked token approvals — some dating back five years.
data_point🗣 co-hostconviction: highBarnbridge
So what: Audit and revoke stale token approvals on any long-dormant DeFi protocol you've interacted with — governance-controlled upgradability combined with old infinite approvals remains an active, low-cost attack vector even on protocols long presumed dead.
“they're able to take end users money via the anyone that has an open approval” ▶ 1:02:28
The panel questions whether routing contract upgradability through on-chain DAO governance still makes sense given how few DAOs are actively monitored, noting Barnbridge's attack succeeded specifically because no one was watching its governance for years.
contrarian🗣 co-hostconviction: mediumBarnbridge
So what: DAO governance that nobody actively monitors is itself an attack surface — a red flag to check for in any protocol whose upgrade path still runs through token-weighted voting.
“very similar to last week, nobody was watching the governance” ▶ 1:01:42
New MetaMask smart-account "delegator" permissions (built on account-abstraction-style delegation) now let users pre-authorize Revoke.cash to automatically revoke their open token approvals to a compromised protocol, without giving Revoke.cash any ability to move funds itself — described as one of the first practical consumer use cases for delegated smart-account permissions.
framework🗣 co-hostconviction: highMetaMaskRevoke.cash
So what: Users exposed to old approvals (as in the Barnbridge case) can now pre-delegate automatic revocation as a standing defense rather than reacting incident-by-incident.
“Via Revocach you can give them permission to revoke your open approvals.” ▶ 1:08:47
Ostium/Alium $23M Hack & North Korea Attribution 7
A DeFi derivatives protocol (referred to interchangeably as "Alium" and "Ostium" in the discussion) lost roughly $23 million after private keys controlling a price/oracle forwarder contract — not the treasury hot wallet itself — were compromised, while a separate wallet holding roughly $7-9 million was untouched.
data_point🗣 co-hostconviction: highOstium
So what: Peripheral admin/oracle keys are now a common attack path distinct from treasury-wallet compromise — any privileged key touching contract state should get treasury-grade protection (multisig), not just funds-holding wallets.
“my account was like 23 mil and I think there was like one other wallet that somehow couldn't be hit or whatever that had 7 mil in it or 9 mil” ▶ 1:13:22
The security co-host says she has not formally attributed the Ostium/Alium hack to a specific threat actor, but views the attacker's ability to move funds continuously for at least eight straight hours without pausing as a strong behavioral signature she associates specifically with North Korean state-linked hacking teams.
contrarian🗣 Taylor Monahan (co-host, security researcher)conviction: mediumNorth KoreaOstium
So what: A practitioner attribution heuristic for future incident analysis — sustained, non-stop fund movement over many hours is treated as a DPRK-linked signature, independent of formal on-chain forensic attribution.
“since I spent eight hours tracing this [__] straight yesterday, non-stop and they did not stop and I I could not keep up, that is a pretty strong indicator that it's probably someone in North Korea” ▶ 0:05
The same speaker argues nearly every recent major hack now follows an identical pattern — credential/private-key theft via social engineering (e.g., fake job interviews requiring victims to install malicious software) rather than smart-contract code exploits — and that builders still wrongly assume their admin/hot wallets (EOAs) are secure.
causal🗣 Taylor Monahan (co-host, security researcher)conviction: highNorth Korea
So what: Security diligence on any protocol should weight private-key/operational-security practices (multisig on all admin functions, zero implicit trust in any EOA) at least as heavily as smart-contract audits.
“you don't ever trust any EOA ever” ▶ 1:13:58
A panelist observes that smart-contract code quality has measurably improved industry-wide, with far fewer logic-bug exploits, while the dominant loss vector in 2026 so far has shifted almost entirely to private-key and operational-security failures — a problem the industry knows how to fix but keeps failing to execute on.
causal🗣 guest (CAP co-founder)conviction: high
So what: When assessing protocol risk, code-audit quality is becoming a smaller differentiator; operational key-management practices are now the dominant loss driver to diligence.
“We know the illness. We know the medicine. It's just kind of we refuse to take it.” ▶ 1:21:13
Comparing hack-recovery outcomes, the panel notes Euler ("Oiler") fully recovered its stolen funds through the founder's relentless personal pursuit of the attacker — a rare outcome — while most hacked protocols lack that kind of advantage and typically take years, if they recover at all.
causal🗣 hostconviction: mediumEuler
So what: Sets a base-rate expectation for hacked-protocol recovery timelines and TVL/trust rebuild — useful when deciding whether to hold a position in a protocol that has just suffered a comparable exploit.
“they managed to get the money back, which is super rare” ▶ 1:26:19
The host recounts personally advising the eventual founders of the Ostium derivatives protocol in 2021 that building in DeFi wasn't worth the pain and suggesting they not launch; they proceeded anyway, launched in 2022, operated successfully for several years, and then lost roughly $23M in the hack described above — illustrating how a single operational-security failure can erase years of otherwise-successful protocol operation.
anecdote🗣 hostconviction: mediumOstium
So what: A cautionary data point on tail risk in DeFi: multi-year successful operating track records don't protect against a single catastrophic key-management failure.
“Honestly, my advice to you is like don't.” ▶ 1:23:52
The panel frames recurring hack root causes through complex-systems "normal accident" theory (analogized to Three Mile Island): temporary security workarounds accumulate over years until an overlooked gap becomes the failure point, compounded in crypto by active adversarial probing rather than random entropy.
framework🗣 co-hostconviction: high
So what: A mental model for why even well-resourced, security-conscious teams keep getting hacked — useful for setting realistic expectations about any protocol's residual security risk regardless of its audit history.
“there's a concept in like complex systems like a normal accident” ▶ 1:15:18
Crypto Twitter Sentiment & Market-Cycle Psychology 2
A panelist argues that structural or economic changes (e.g., fee models, protocol rules) are far easier to push through during bear markets than bull markets, because participants have less incentive to defend the status quo when prices are down, and whatever equilibrium emerges from a bear market tends to persist stably through the following bull run.
framework🗣 co-hostconviction: high
So what: A timing framework for advocating or expecting protocol-level changes (e.g., the Ethereum fee changes discussed earlier): push for structural changes now, in the bear market, rather than waiting for a bull run when resistance is highest.
“whatever comes out of the bare market is like the thing that is the stable equilibrium now” ▶ 52:58
Panelists note that X/Twitter's algorithm had apparently suppressed crypto users' visibility of their mutual "crypto Twitter" network for roughly a year, only restoring it days before this recording, and that the change visibly shifted the tone and sentiment observed on the platform.
causal🗣 hostconviction: medium
So what: Illustrates that observed "crypto Twitter sentiment" can be driven by platform algorithm changes rather than underlying market fundamentals — a reminder to discount social-sentiment signals that coincide with unexplained engagement/visibility shifts.
“it's insane to me that they could construct the algo in this way” ▶ 22:06
Is DAO Governance Broken? How Voter Apathy and Power Struggles Should Be Fixed65 min36 insights2026-07-15
▶ Watch on YouTube
Laura Shin hosts Nick (DAO governance lead who works with Gito DAO) and Prophet (Meta DAO co-founder) to dissect the ENS DAO crisis, where founder Nick Johnson used his own tokens to block a Security Council renewal and shift treasury control to a foundation, plus the Bonq DAO $20M governance exploit and the Dragonfly/Venice VVV tokens-vs-equity backlash. Both guests argue DAO governance is near a cyclical bottom, with Meta DAO's futarchy/decision-market model and curated-delegate structures (as used by Gito DAO) offered as candidate fixes to voter apathy and treasury capture.
ENS DAO's Security Council Capture · 6Voter Apathy and Delegate Curation · 6Meta DAO's Futarchy Alternative · 6Bonq DAO Exploit and Governance Attacks · 5Tokens vs Equity: The Venice/VVV Debate · 6Centralization vs Decentralization Trade-offs · 4Outlook: Has DAO Governance Bottomed? · 3
ENS DAO's Security Council Capture 6
ENS co-founder Nick Johnson used his own ~3% ENS token holding to single-handedly block a Security Council renewal vote (after abstaining in the earlier non-binding Snapshot signal vote), clearing the way for the DAO's treasury to be transferred to a foundation; Nick (guest) calls the move a deliberate capture attempt.
contrarian🗣 Nick (guest)conviction: highENS DAONick JohnsonSecurity Council
So what: Shows how a founder can weaponize low voter turnout to override a DAO's own check-and-balance mechanism, a governance risk investors should price into any token they treat as decentralized.
“I think that was absolutely intentional and it was done as a effectively, yeah, it's as a capture attempt, if you like.” ▶ 38:11
ENS Labs has spent roughly $20 million over a couple of years (on the order of $1M+/month) developing ENS, a burn rate Prophet (Meta DAO co-founder) says he would flag as a red flag if evaluating ENS as a private-company investor.
data_point🗣 Prophet (guest)conviction: mediumENS LabsENS DAO
So what: High labs spend relative to shipped output is a warning sign for anyone assessing whether ENS treasury funds are being converted into protocol value.
“if I were an investor, and ENS is a private company, that would be a red flag, for sure.” ▶ 6:31
ENS's planned V2 protocol upgrade and a proposed ENS Layer-2 chain ("Name Chain") were both abandoned after roughly two years of work, illustrating that heavy treasury and labs spend on both sides of ENS did not convert into shipped protocol outcomes.
data_point🗣 Nick (guest)conviction: mediumENS DAOENS Labs
So what: A multi-year, unshipped L2 effort is concrete evidence for investors questioning whether ENS treasury capital has been well allocated.
Because ENS DAO voter turnout has been in long, steady decline, Nick Johnson's roughly 3% token stake ended up constituting about half of all votes cast on the Security Council election, turning a minority holder into the deciding vote.
causal🗣 hostconviction: highENS DAONick Johnson
So what: Illustrates a general DAO vulnerability: as turnout falls, the effective capture threshold falls with it, so even modest token holders can become decisive.
ENS DAO ultimately ceded management of a treasury reportedly over $100 million to a foundation rather than reforming its on-chain governor contract, a move Nick (guest) calls disappointing given ENS was considered one of Ethereum's flagship DAOs "still holding the dream alive."
thesis🗣 Nick (guest)conviction: mediumENS DAOENS Labs
So what: A high-profile DAO abandoning on-chain treasury control for centralized custody is a bearish signal for the broader on-chain-governance thesis that other protocols may follow.
“which is a bit of a shame, I think.” ▶ 26:55
A longtime ENS contributor, Jeff Lau, argued that routing the ENS treasury into an on-chain governor contract was "idealism that created a honeypot with zero accountability," reflecting internal skepticism that decentralized treasury custody actually protects funds rather than inviting extraction.
contrarian🗣 host (relaying Jeff Lau, ENS)conviction: mediumJeff LauENS DAO
So what: A large permissionless treasury is itself an attack surface; investors should weight treasury-custody design as a distinct risk factor from protocol-level decentralization.
“sending the treasury to the governor contract was idealism that created a honeypot with zero accountability.” ▶ 24:33
Voter Apathy and Delegate Curation 6
Voter apathy is structural across DAOs: most average token holders have no interest in spending time on governance, so participation coalesces around a small, cross-DAO cluster of professional delegates, producing a slow capture by the most engaged rather than the most representative participants.
framework🗣 Nick (guest)conviction: highDAO governance
So what: Explains why DAO voting outcomes across many protocols tend to reflect a small recurring insider group rather than broad token-holder preference — relevant to assessing any DAO's true governance risk.
Jupiter DAO on Solana shows the opposite failure mode from apathy: unusually high retail ("degen") turnout staking tokens purely to vote produced chaotic decision-making, and the DAO ultimately wound down despite (or partly because of) that high participation.
anecdote🗣 Nick (guest)conviction: mediumJupiter DAO
So what: High turnout alone doesn't fix DAO governance — participant quality/alignment matters as much as quantity when designing token distribution and voting mechanisms.
Gito DAO solved Solana's lack of a pre-existing delegate culture by having the foundation hand-curate a set of Solana-aligned delegates and assign them tokens directly, rather than relying on the typical EVM approach of randomly distributing voting power via airdrop.
framework🗣 Nick (guest)conviction: highGito DAOSolana
So what: Curated-delegate design is a replicable alternative to random airdrop-based governance power distribution that other new-chain DAOs could adopt to avoid ENS-style apathy problems.
“which made a huge difference in the kind of quality of the decision-making.” ▶ 12:41
Solana recently upgraded its protocol-level governance so validators vote on network proposals by default, but individual token holders can override their assigned delegate's vote — a "delegation with revocation" hybrid Nick (guest) calls a genuinely useful structure.
framework🗣 Nick (guest)conviction: mediumSolanaGito DAO
So what: A delegation-with-override model is a concrete mechanism worth watching as a template for balancing efficient default decision-making with individual holder sovereignty.
“which I think is a a really useful structure.” ▶ 22:45
A proposal by ENS co-founder Alex Van de Sand to delegate 5 million DAO-held tokens to new delegates (enough to reach roughly 60% of the vote and check the largest stakeholder) was seen by Nick (guest) as directionally good, though its viability is now uncertain given the Security Council's takeover of treasury control.
thesis🗣 Nick (guest)conviction: mediumENS DAOAlex Van de SandNick Johnson
So what: A pending token-redistribution proposal to rebalance ENS governance power away from its founder is a concrete catalyst to watch for signs ENS DAO governance is being restored versus permanently centralized.
Nick (guest) expects DAOs to evolve toward specialized, expertise-weighted substructures with defined treasury mandates that automatically sunset (e.g., after 6-12 months) specifically to prevent permanent capture by career governance participants who show up mainly to get paid.
prediction🗣 Nick (guest)conviction: mediumGito DAODAO governance
So what: Self-terminating, mandate-bound governance committees are a design pattern to watch for in next-generation DAO structures as an antidote to entrenched delegate capture.
“You have a mandate to spend this treasury over 6 months, 1 year, and then it all disappear and it automatically disbands.” ▶ 21:56
Meta DAO's Futarchy Alternative 6
Prophet argues voting is the wrong governance primitive because turnout is a cost, not a goal — the ideal governance system makes the correct decision with minimal participant effort, and turnout only matters as a defense against capture by concentrated holders.
contrarian🗣 Prophet (guest)conviction: highMeta DAO
So what: Reframes the DAO-voter-turnout problem investors worry about: the real design target is decision quality, not participation rate, which changes how to evaluate a DAO's governance health.
“voter turnout is not intrinsically an issue, right? The The main thing that we want from a governance system is making good decisions.” ▶ 13:21
Prophet illustrates DAO capture risk mathematically: a holder with 51% of a $10 million treasury's token supply could vote the entire treasury to themselves and still profit even after destroying the DAO, since their ~$5 million stake is smaller than the $10 million prize.
framework🗣 Prophet (guest)conviction: highMeta DAO
So what: A concrete threshold (just above 50% supply concentration) for when a DAO's treasury becomes a rational self-dealing target — useful for underwriting treasury-heavy DAO tokens.
Meta DAO replaces token voting with decision markets (futarchy), where participants trade on outcomes tied to token value instead of casting votes, giving direct financial incentive to participate accurately since you profit from being right and lose from being wrong.
thesis🗣 Prophet (guest)conviction: highMeta DAO
So what: A live, functioning futarchy implementation is a real-world test case for whether market-based governance can outperform apathy-prone token voting.
“instead of people voting, they they trade on uh essentially what would be good for the value of the token” ▶ 14:47
Meta DAO's stated philosophy is "not pro-governance, we're pro-oversight": the goal is not deep community involvement in day-to-day operations but a check-and-trust mechanism that makes the token investable.
thesis🗣 Prophet (guest)conviction: highMeta DAO
So what: Signals that Meta DAO-style structures are pitched as a lighter-weight trust layer for founders/operators rather than a replacement for operational control — relevant when comparing it against full-DAO governance models.
“we're not pro governance, we're pro oversight.” ▶ 58:50
Meta DAO has facilitated roughly $40 million+ raised for entrepreneurs across 13 companies via ICO-style token sales on its platform, with one portfolio company (a crypto credit card product) seeing 10x usage growth after roughly 10,000 token buyers converted into users and marketers.
data_point🗣 Prophet (guest)conviction: highMeta DAO
So what: Concrete evidence that community token sales can double as a distribution/growth channel, not just fundraising — a reusable thesis for evaluating ICO-adjacent launch platforms.
Meta DAO's current fundraising round for a new company was oversubscribed, with roughly $20 million+ committed as of the recording, which Prophet cites as evidence of continued ICO-style demand even in what he calls a "tough ICO market."
data_point🗣 Prophet (guest)conviction: lowMeta DAO
So what: A live oversubscription figure is a real-time gauge of retail/community appetite for token-based fundraising despite the broader ICO market slowdown.
Bonq DAO Exploit and Governance Attacks 5
On July 6th, an attacker drained $20 million from Bonq DAO's treasury via a governance proposal that publicly promised to rebuild the DAO but secretly included an instruction to send 4.4 trillion Bonq tokens to the proposer; the attacker spent only about $4.4 million (882 billion tokens) to reach quorum, with just 7 of 18,000 possible wallets voting, passing a one-day time lock before executing.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: highBonq DAO
So what: A quantified case study of how cheap it can be to capture a low-turnout DAO treasury — useful benchmark for assessing quorum/turnout risk on other token treasuries.
“an attacker drained $20 million from Bonq DAO's treasury” ▶ 41:44
The Bonq DAO exploit succeeded largely because no monitoring system alerted the core team that the malicious proposal was live; Prophet notes Meta DAO runs a Telegram and Slack bot that flags every live proposal specifically to prevent this kind of "apathy attack."
causal🗣 Prophet (guest)conviction: highMeta DAOBonq DAO
So what: Basic on-chain proposal-monitoring tooling is a cheap, concrete mitigation any DAO treasury holder should demand exists before trusting a protocol's governance security.
“we at Meta DAO we have a Telegram bot and a Slack bot that notifies us whenever a proposal goes live” ▶ 43:18
The Bonq DAO attack mirrors the earlier Compound "Humpty" exploit, where an attacker bought governance tokens and pushed a proposal while Compound's core active voter base — university blockchain clubs — were on holiday, exploiting a known low-attention window rather than any code bug.
causal🗣 Nick (guest)conviction: highCompoundBonq DAO
So what: Timing-based apathy attacks are a recurring, predictable DAO exploit pattern (not one-off bugs), so recurrence should be expected wherever monitoring is weak.
Bonq DAO's proposal did have a one-day time lock, but Nick (guest) questions whether any time-lock length matters without active human monitoring, since it's unclear whether a longer lock would have caught an attack nobody was watching for.
contrarian🗣 Nick (guest)conviction: mediumBonq DAO
So what: Time locks alone are not sufficient governance security; investors evaluating DAO treasury safety should look for active monitoring, not just time-lock length.
Because the Bonq DAO attacker technically followed all on-chain governance rules (five-day voting period, one-day time lock, quorum reached), Nick (guest) is unsure the theft is even legally actionable, drawing a parallel to the Avi Eisenberg/Mango Markets case where a judge ruled a smart contract cannot be defrauded.
data_point🗣 Nick (guest)conviction: lowBonq DAOMango MarketsAvi Eisenberg
So what: Legal ambiguity around rule-following exploits means token holders currently have limited recourse against "legitimate" governance-process attacks, a real tail risk for any DAO treasury.
“you can't defraud a smart contract.” ▶ 42:47
Tokens vs Equity: The Venice/VVV Debate 6
Dragonfly's investment in Venice and the associated VVV token sparked a "tokens vs equity" controversy because many buyers mistakenly treated VVV as a governance/ownership token when it is actually a utility token: staking VVV grants perpetual access to $1/day of AI compute via a secondary DM token.
thesis🗣 hostconviction: highVeniceVVVDragonfly
So what: A recurring source of crypto controversy is buyer misunderstanding of token mechanics rather than team misconduct — investors should verify a token's actual claim structure before assuming equity-like rights.
Nick (guest) calls VVV one of the only viable utility tokens seen in years, structurally selling "compute perpetuities," and notes the token rose roughly 8x during the bear market, though he flags uncertainty over how much of that gain reflects genuine compute demand versus speculation.
data_point🗣 Nick (guest)conviction: mediumVVVVenice
So what: VVV is being cited as a rare working example of non-equity utility-token design — a reference case for evaluating whether other "tokenized compute" projects can replicate its mechanism.
“the token did go up some something like 8X in the in the bear market.” ▶ 49:17
Prophet argues the Venice/VVV backlash was overstated because the team clearly communicated from launch that VVV was not equity; he attributes the negative reaction to lingering distrust ("PTSD") from prior crypto rug pulls rather than any actual Venice misconduct.
contrarian🗣 Prophet (guest)conviction: mediumVeniceVVV
So what: Suggests market overreaction risk around any new token launch is elevated industry-wide due to prior scams, independent of a given project's actual conduct — worth discounting sentiment-driven selloffs accordingly.
“I do think the backlash was a little was definitely overstated.” ▶ 50:36
Prophet expects equity to keep capturing more value than parallel utility tokens because equity holders retain fiduciary control and financial incentive to direct value there: Venice's equity investors bought in at a $1 billion valuation while the VVV token was trading at a $1.4 billion valuation at the same time.
prediction🗣 Prophet (guest)conviction: mediumVeniceVVVDragonfly
So what: A tradeable signal: when a utility token's implied valuation exceeds the equity round backing the same business, that premium may be structurally unsustainable as fiduciary-controlled equity captures future upside instead.
“the investors had just gotten into Venice at a billion dollars like on the equity and then the token was trading at 1.4 at the time.” ▶ 53:06
Prophet analogizes utility tokens to commodities futures versus equities, estimating the equities market is roughly an order of magnitude (~10x) larger than the commodities futures market, implying utility-token valuations may be structurally capped relative to equity in comparable categories.
framework🗣 Prophet (guest)conviction: lowVVV
So what: Offers a rough valuation ceiling heuristic for utility tokens versus their equity counterparts that investors can use when sizing positions in token-vs-equity structured projects.
“the equities market is about one order of magnitude bigger than the commodities futures market” ▶ 53:41
A growing number of projects — Meta DAO, Morfo (Morpho), Grass, and Gito DAO — are making explicit public declarations that their token, not any equity, is the sole value-accrual instrument, a trend both guests expect to accelerate as investors reward fully token-centric commitments over hybrid equity/token structures.
prediction🗣 Prophet (guest)conviction: mediumMorfoGrassGito DAOMeta DAO
So what: Watch for more projects publicly renouncing equity in favor of pure token value-accrual; this positioning is becoming a marketed differentiator investors can screen for.
“Morfo has like publicly stated Morfo is the only token that we care about.” ▶ 54:08
Centralization vs Decentralization Trade-offs 4
Host Laura Shin frames the current period as a temporary centralization phase for crypto driven by its early adoption stage, expecting the pendulum to swing back toward decentralization once the technology matures; she cites ENS's foundation takeover, Aave's centralizing moves, and ENS DAO sunsetting its public-goods funding as evidence, partly linking the trend to a post-election "we can be centralized now" sentiment.
thesis🗣 hostconviction: lowAaveENS DAO
So what: If correct, today's wave of DAO-to-foundation centralization is cyclical rather than terminal, meaning decentralized governance structures could see renewed relevance later in the adoption curve.
“after Trump got elected everyone's like, "We can be centralized now."” ▶ 30:25
Nick (guest) frames decentralized governance as justified mainly when third parties need long-term assurance to build integrations or investment theses on a protocol that must resist a founder's unilateral pivot — otherwise founder-led centralization is more efficient.
framework🗣 Nick (guest)conviction: highENS DAO
