So what: Gives investors a concrete test for whether a project's decentralization claims are substantive: does the protocol need to be un-pivotable for others to safely build on it?
“if it can change at the drop of a hat, then you you can't really do it, right?” ▶ 32:34
Nick (guest) argues many crypto projects adopted decentralized governance not out of genuine decentralization intent but as regulatory arbitrage ("reg arb") to get a token to market, a distinction he sees as underlying much of the current backlash against DAOs.
contrarian🗣 Nick (guest)conviction: mediumDAO governance
So what: Investors should distinguish between projects that decentralized for genuine product reasons versus those that did so mainly to avoid securities scrutiny, since the latter are more likely to recentralize opportunistically.
“a lot of the time we pick decentralized governance as a kind of reg arb rather than a we wanted to decentralize the protocol” ▶ 33:15
Both guests converge on a governance-scope rule: major tokenomics decisions (minting or issuing new tokens, large treasury draws) should require DAO/token-holder approval, while day-to-day operational spending should sit with a capitalized, accountable operating entity (labs or foundation) rather than going through governance proposals.
framework🗣 Prophet (guest)conviction: highMeta DAOGito DAO
So what: A practical governance-scoping heuristic investors can use to judge whether a given DAO's division of labor between community and operating team is sound or overreaching in either direction.
Outlook: Has DAO Governance Bottomed? 3
Both guests believe DAO governance sentiment is near a cyclical bottom after a wave of failures (ENS, Bonq DAO, and earlier Compound/Aave incidents), and expect a coming wave of governance experimentation to finally replace the largely unchanged Compound Governor V2 template that has dominated DAOs since roughly 2020-2021.
prediction🗣 Nick (guest)conviction: mediumCompoundENS DAOBonq DAO
So what: A contrarian buy-the-dip-on-sentiment signal for governance-token-heavy DAOs, contingent on real mechanism innovation (e.g., futarchy, curated delegates) actually emerging rather than more copy-pasted Governor V2 forks.
“we've hit close to DAO bottom.” ▶ 1:01:15
Prophet expects securities regulation to remain lightly enforced for the next few years, which he sees as complicating rather than helping the bull case for DAOs, since avoiding securities classification was historically a key rationale for decentralized token structures.
contrarian🗣 Prophet (guest)conviction: lowDAO governance
So what: If regulatory pressure to decentralize eases, some projects may have less incentive to maintain genuine DAO structures, potentially accelerating the recentralization trend discussed elsewhere in the episode.
Nick (guest) predicts the next generation of DAOs will increasingly manage on-chain value-accrual mechanisms — buybacks and dividend-like protocol-revenue distributions — arguing centralized companies structurally cannot perform these functions, positioning DAOs as necessary infrastructure specifically for tokenized revenue-sharing.
prediction🗣 Nick (guest)conviction: mediumGito DAOMeta DAO
So what: Frames a specific forward use case (on-chain buybacks/revenue distribution) where DAO structures may retain durable advantage over centralized entities, useful for identifying which token projects still need real decentralized governance.
Why the Supreme Court's FTC Ruling Could Rewire Crypto Regulation: DEX in the City56 min27 insights2026-07-15
▶ Watch on YouTube
The Dex in the City hosts (KK, Jesse, and V) walk through a wave of new UK FCA and EU MiCA crypto rulemaking, then dig into the Supreme Court's FTC v. Slaughter ruling stripping independent agencies of removal protections and what it means for SEC/CFTC regulatory risk, before covering a Cambridge report on Boko Haram's operational use of AI chatbots for weapons and attack planning and a TRM Labs case tracing wildlife-trafficking crypto flows.
UK FCA's New Crypto Rulebook · 5MiCA's Stablecoin Expansion and the Genius Act Conflict · 4Europe's Broader Institutional Crypto Push · 3Supreme Court's FTC Ruling Rewires Agency Independence · 8AI Weaponization: The Boko Haram Case Study · 3AI/Crypto Regulatory Parallels and the Section 230 Gap · 3Illicit Finance: Blockchain Analytics vs. Wildlife Trafficking · 1
UK FCA's New Crypto Rulebook 5
On June 30, the UK's Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) published four final policy statements plus guidance forming a comprehensive crypto rulebook covering stablecoin issuance, market abuse, prudential capital requirements, disclosures and DeFi, marking the final piece of the UK's crypto regulatory roadmap.
data_point🗣 host (KK)conviction: highFCAUK
So what: Institutional and TradFi players now have a defined UK compliance roadmap to plan around; watch for firms weighing UK market entry or exit once the full scope of obligations is priced in.
“on June 30th, the UK's Financial Conduct Authority, the FCA, their primary financial regulator, published a pretty massive package of final policy statements and guidance for crypto in the UK.” ▶ 3:18
The FCA cut capital requirements for stablecoin issuers in direct response to industry feedback, but the new UK crypto regime does not take effect until fall 2027, giving firms roughly a year-plus to prepare and lobby further.
data_point🗣 host (KK)conviction: highFCAUK
So what: Fall 2027 is a concrete forward catalyst date for UK stablecoin compliance costs; issuers have a defined window to structure operations before the regime binds.
Two FCA consultation papers covering market making and DeFi remain unfinished, with the FCA saying it will further define 'objective indicators of decentralization' and financial-crime expectations before finalizing DeFi rules, rather than force DeFi protocols into TradFi-style regulation outright.
data_point🗣 host (KK)conviction: mediumFCADeFiUK
So what: DeFi protocols with UK exposure should track these consultations; a workable UK decentralization test could become a reference model other jurisdictions copy.
Despite positive elements, the FCA's framework fundamentally applies TradFi-style regulation to crypto, and the host expects this to be a genuinely difficult compliance burden for smaller UK crypto firms, forcing many to weigh whether continuing to serve UK customers is worth the cost.
thesis🗣 host (KK)conviction: mediumFCAUK
So what: Expect potential consolidation or UK market exit among smaller crypto firms as compliance costs rise, favoring larger, better-capitalized institutional players.
“but this regime is no joke.” ▶ 5:55
Rather than hard-coding detailed rules into legislation the way the EU did with MiCA, the UK's approach lays out only a general legislative framework and pushes most substantive rulemaking into ongoing FCA handbook consultations, a design explicitly intended to give regulators more flexibility to adapt as stablecoins and tokenization evolve faster than lawmakers can legislate.
framework🗣 Vconviction: highFCAMiCAUK
So what: UK crypto rules should iterate faster and lag technology less than the EU's MiCA, reducing regulatory-lag risk for fast-moving products like stablecoins and tokenized assets.
“it's a lot more flexible, right? So like the legislation sort of lays out the general framework, but it looks like most of the actual rules are going to come through like consultations and the FCA handbook” ▶ 11:24
MiCA's Stablecoin Expansion and the Genius Act Conflict 4
MiCA's first provisions entered force in 2023, and the transition period for grandfathered legacy crypto firms only fully ended around July 1, meaning it took roughly three years for the EU's flagship crypto framework to become fully binding across the industry.
data_point🗣 host (KK)conviction: highMiCAEU
So what: Long implementation timelines are the norm for major EU crypto regulation; investors should expect multi-year lags between announced EU rules and full enforcement.
The European Commission is seeking comment on expanding MiCA to cover tokenized assets more broadly and stablecoins issued by non-EU issuers, driven by explosive growth in tokenization (especially tokenized equities) since 2023 that MiCA's original drafters did not anticipate; the comment period runs to roughly September, with actual proposed revisions unlikely for at least a year.
data_point🗣 host (KK)conviction: mediumMiCAEU
So what: Non-EU stablecoin issuers may eventually need MiCA-comparable standards to retain EU market access; the ~September comment deadline and 1-year-plus runway to actual rules gives issuers time to engage before binding changes land.
There is a direct regulatory conflict between US stablecoin rules under the Genius Act and EU requirements under MiCA, which the host calls 'nonsensical' given stablecoins' inherently cross-border design; this is pushing issuers toward different structural models, such as Circle's single-issuance-entity approach versus a multi-entity, multi-jurisdiction issuance model used by other issuers to satisfy divergent regimes simultaneously.
causal🗣 host (KK)conviction: mediumGenius ActMiCACircleMoon
So what: Stablecoin issuers face real structuring costs from US/EU regulatory divergence; investors should watch which issuance-model approach (single-entity vs. multi-entity) proves more resilient as MiCA's non-EU-issuer rules develop.
“the stable coin requirements under Genius conflict with the stable coin requirements under Mika” ▶ 16:03
Circle is reportedly working on policy efforts in Europe aimed at changing or accommodating its single-issuance stablecoin structure in light of MiCA's requirements, though the host flagged this detail as uncertain.
data_point🗣 host (KK)conviction: lowCircleMiCA
So what: If confirmed, a shift in Circle's issuance structure would be a signal worth tracking for how major stablecoin issuers adapt to EU rules.
Europe's Broader Institutional Crypto Push 3
A group of blue-chip crypto and TradFi firms have joined the UK government's tokenization task force, signaling growing institutional convergence around UK-based tokenization initiatives.
data_point🗣 host (KK)conviction: mediumUK
So what: UK tokenization infrastructure is attracting institutional participation ahead of the FCA regime taking effect, a leading indicator for where institutional tokenization activity may concentrate.
The EU Parliament voted in favor of creating a digital euro and separately adopted a crypto policy position calling for further review of DeFi.
data_point🗣 host (KK)conviction: highEUdigital euroDeFi
So what: A parliamentary vote in favor of a digital euro is a concrete step toward CBDC infrastructure that could compete with or complement private stablecoins in the EU.
The European Central Bank selected 36 banks and payment firms to join a digital euro pilot program, while the European Securities and Markets Authority (ESMA) is separately reviewing crypto custody security standards, showing parallel EU momentum on both CBDC infrastructure and crypto custody oversight.
data_point🗣 host (KK)conviction: highECBdigital euroESMA
So what: Custody providers serving EU crypto clients should anticipate incoming ESMA security standards; the 36-institution digital euro pilot is a concrete near-term CBDC rollout to monitor.
Supreme Court's FTC Ruling Rewires Agency Independence 8
The Supreme Court overturned the 91-year-old Humphrey's Executor precedent (1935), holding that because FTC commissioners exercise a form of executive power, the president generally must have authority to remove them at will, ending nearly a century of 'for cause only' removal protection for commissioners at independent federal agencies.
data_point🗣 Vconviction: highSupreme CourtFTCHumphrey's Executor
So what: This directly extends to SEC and CFTC commissioners, fundamentally changing the risk model for how quickly crypto-relevant regulatory leadership can turn over.
“they held that because FTC commissioners exercise a form of executive power, the president generally must have the authority to remove them.” ▶ 27:55
Even before this ruling, independent agencies were never fully insulated from politics: the president nominates commissioners, the Senate confirms them, the president selects the agency chair, and Congress controls agency budgets and can conduct oversight hearings — the new ruling removes what had been the core remaining protection, for-cause-only removal.
framework🗣 Vconviction: mediumSECCFTCFTC
So what: Useful mental model for gauging how much genuine independence any US regulator retains going forward when evaluating regulatory-risk premiums.
The case arose after the president removed two Democratic FTC commissioners without cause last year; one of them, Rebecca Slaughter, challenged her removal by relying on Humphrey's Executor, and the Supreme Court ruled against her, overturning the precedent she relied on.
anecdote🗣 Vconviction: highFTCRebecca Slaughter
So what: Establishes the direct factual chain from a single removal dispute to a structural change affecting all independent financial regulators, including SEC and CFTC.
Because commissioners at independent agencies (SEC, CFTC, FTC, FCC) can now be removed at will, presidential elections will have a much more immediate effect on crypto and financial regulation: a new administration can replace commissioners and quickly reshape rulemaking, enforcement priorities, and exemptive relief, rather than waiting years for staggered commissioner terms to naturally expire.
prediction🗣 Vconviction: highSECCFTCFTC
So what: Crypto regulatory risk becomes more binary and election-driven; investors should price in sharper, faster regulatory swings around SEC/CFTC leadership changes rather than gradual multi-year shifts.
“presidential elections will now have like a much more immediate effect on crypto regulation and just like regulation in general” ▶ 30:00
The practical effect of weakened commissioner independence is already visible: SEC and CFTC commissioner seats have sat empty for a while without being filled, giving the president additional de facto leverage over how those agencies operate even without formal removals.
data_point🗣 Vconviction: mediumSECCFTC
So what: Unfilled SEC/CFTC seats are a live signal of executive influence over crypto rulemaking pace; watch nomination and confirmation activity as a regulatory-risk indicator.
The removal-power change cuts both ways: the same authority that lets a pro-crypto administration reshape the SEC and CFTC favorably would give exactly the same power to a future administration with very different, potentially anti-crypto, policy priorities.
contrarian🗣 Vconviction: highSECCFTC
So what: Crypto industry optimism about current deregulatory momentum should be tempered by the same mechanism's downside risk under a future administration; regulatory tailwinds now are not structurally durable.
“A decision that gives a pro- crypto administration more influence and power over the SEC and the CFTC would give exactly the same authority to a future administration with very different policy priorities.” ▶ 38:02
In a companion ruling, Trump v. Cook, issued around the same time, the Supreme Court held that the Federal Reserve board is constitutionally protected from at-will removal, carving the Fed out as an exception to the new removal-power rule that now applies to the SEC, CFTC, FTC and FCC.
data_point🗣 Vconviction: highFederal ReserveTrump v. CookSupreme Court
So what: Monetary policy independence remains structurally protected even as financial and crypto regulators lose that protection, an important distinction for macro versus crypto-specific regulatory risk.
The host argues that despite the crypto industry's current excitement about favorable SEC/CFTC posturing, there is a real scenario in a few years where regulatory leadership flips and enforcement 'rains down,' and she frames scenario-mapping across bull market, bear market, and enforcement outcomes as a core part of a crypto general counsel's job.
prediction🗣 host (KK)conviction: mediumSECCFTC
So what: Legal and compliance teams should actively scenario-plan for abrupt SEC/CFTC enforcement reversals rather than assuming the current friendly posture is a permanent structural state.
AI Weaponization: The Boko Haram Case Study 3
A new Cambridge report based on interviews with former Boko Haram members found the terrorist group has used large language models extensively for weapons troubleshooting, explosive device design, attack planning, and post-attack review to improve future operations.
data_point🗣 Jesseconviction: highBoko HaramCambridge reportLLMs
So what: Concrete documented evidence of terrorist groups operationalizing consumer LLMs for lethal ends raises the stakes for AI safety-guardrail policy and content-moderation liability debates.
The report contrasts 2017, when Boko Haram fighters could not get captured military-grade rifles to work despite using Google searches and YouTube manuals, with the present day, when fighters type a weapon's identifying number into a chatbot that walks them through loading, servicing, and using it, illustrating how AI collapsed the gap between having dangerous information and being able to act on it.
causal🗣 Jesseconviction: highBoko HaramLLMs
So what: Frames the core AI-safety risk as reduced friction between knowledge and capability, a mechanism relevant beyond terrorism to any dual-use technology policy debate, including crypto.
Boko Haram has built what amounts to an industrial AI operation with dedicated specialist units, including fighters stationed near the front line using AI to plan next moves, and jailbreak or workaround techniques for LLM safety guardrails (e.g., posing requests as being for a movie) are being shared and taught through jihadist networks.
data_point🗣 Jesseconviction: highBoko HaramLLMs
So what: Existing LLM safety guardrails are being systematically defeated and the workarounds are propagating through organized networks, undermining the assumption that basic refusal prompts are sufficient protection.
AI/Crypto Regulatory Parallels and the Section 230 Gap 3
Jesse draws a direct parallel between AI and crypto as neutral technologies exploited by bad actors, arguing crypto's response over the past decade — blockchain analytics, screening, auditing, and bug bounties rather than outright bans — offers a governance model AI could learn from instead of assigning blame to the underlying technology itself.
framework🗣 Jesseconviction: mediumBoko HaramLLMs
So what: Crypto's compliance-tooling playbook (chain analytics, screening, bounties) is being positioned as a transferable template for AI governance debates, relevant to any crypto/AI convergence investment thesis.
Neither crypto nor AI platforms have a Section 230-style liability shield (the 1990s law protecting internet platforms from liability for third-party content), and V argues this gap means the practical rails around AI accountability will likely be shaped by civil liability litigation, including products-liability suits already being filed against AI platforms alleging they facilitated crimes or gave harmful advice.
thesis🗣 Vconviction: highSection 230
So what: Civil litigation outcomes against AI platforms, not new legislation, may become the near-term de facto regulatory framework for AI liability — a legal-risk vector worth tracking for AI companies and their insurers/investors.
“a lot of I think what's going to happen with the rails around this are going to be informed by good oldfashioned suing.” ▶ 50:55
The UK and EU have recently proposed regulating AI's financial-services touchpoints (framed as a potential 'backdoor' route into broader AI oversight, reaching infrastructure providers like AWS and Cloudflare) given the absence of a direct, comprehensive AI-specific regulatory framework.
data_point🗣 Vconviction: mediumUKEUAWSCloudflare
So what: Cloud and infrastructure providers with financial-services exposure could face indirect AI regulation via financial rules before any dedicated AI statute exists, a channel worth monitoring for compliance risk.
Illicit Finance: Blockchain Analytics vs. Wildlife Trafficking 1
Blockchain analytics firm TRM Labs traced $15 million in crypto moving through a single Southeast Asia-based network trafficking tiger parts, ivory, and rhino horn, demonstrating how blockchain forensics can target the financial rails of wildlife trafficking, a trade whose financial side has historically drawn comparatively little enforcement attention despite the trafficking itself being well documented (e.g., via pangolin trafficking, the most trafficked mammal on the planet).
data_point🗣 host (KK)conviction: highTRM Labs
So what: Illustrates blockchain analytics' expanding use case beyond crypto-native crime into traditional illicit trades, a positive data point for the analytics/compliance sector's societal value proposition and potential enforcement partnerships.
“traced 15 million in crypto through a single Southeast Asia based network moving tiger parts, ivory, rhin rhino horn across Asia” ▶ 53:53

This Week in Startups · Jason Calacanis 3 episode(s) · 88 insights

Jason turned $11 and one tweet into 1.1M views | E231189 min26 insights2026-07-13
▶ Watch on YouTube
Jason Calacanis and Lon Harris host serial founder Tibo Lucas on the 'build in public' audience-compounding playbook, then dig into the Apple v. OpenAI trade-secrets lawsuit, the economics and policy fights around autonomous-vehicle rollout in the US and China, an economist-signed warning on AI job displacement, and Flock Safety's surveillance-camera PR crisis.
Build-in-Public Audience Growth Playbook · 6Apple v. OpenAI Trade-Secrets Lawsuit · 6Autonomous Vehicle Economics and Rollout Timeline · 7AI Job Displacement and Transition Policy · 3Crisis PR and Surveillance-Tech Backlash · 4
Build-in-Public Audience Growth Playbook 6
Tibo Lucas built Tweet Hunter as his 11th public product launch within four months, with each earlier public launch compounding his Twitter following until Tweet Hunter itself surpassed $1M ARR within a year and was acquired by Lemlist.
anecdote🗣 Tibo Lucas (guest, serial founder)conviction: highTibo LucasTweet HunterLemlist
So what: Serial public shipping compounds audience/distribution faster than betting everything on one launch — a repeatable, low-cost customer-acquisition strategy other founders can copy.
“the 11th product was Tweet Hunter. That was a hit like at the very very beginning. In less than a year, it reached a million per year and got eventually sold to to List.” ▶ 0:12
Tibo Lucas's growth strategy was to target one narrow audience (creator-economy Twitter/LinkedIn users) and have every new product solve a different problem for that same audience, so the following built by an earlier product carried over to the next launch.
framework🗣 Tibo Lucas (guest)conviction: highTibo LucasTweet HunterTaplio
So what: A repeatable 'same audience, new product' framework for multi-product founders — avoid resetting distribution with every launch.
Tibo Lucas shifted influencer marketing from paying a handful of large flat sponsorship fees toward seeding hundreds of micro-influencers a small base fee (around $200) plus a success bonus (around $500) once their content breaks 50,000 views, then re-investing in whichever partnerships convert.
framework🗣 Tibo Lucas (guest)conviction: highTibo Lucas
So what: A capital-efficient influencer playbook — pay for outcomes across many small bets rather than for guaranteed reach, since algorithmic distribution now matters more than follower count.
“suggest a base fee of something like 200 USD. Uh, and then if they break something like a 50k views on their content, talking talking about my product, uh, I would offer a bonus of about 500.” ▶ 19:50
Jason Calacanis argues that platform 'for you' algorithms have replaced follower count as the real distribution currency — even accounts with over a million followers get no reach if the algorithm decides a given post isn't engaging.
contrarian🗣 Jason Calacanis (host)conviction: highJason CalacanisTwitter/X
So what: Reframes influencer-marketing ROI: pay for algorithmic breakout potential across many small creator bets instead of buying guaranteed reach from big accounts.
“now even if you've got a million followers if grock is like people aren't going to be interested in this particular tweet, you don't get shared on the for you and people don't see it.” ▶ 18:57
Jason Calacanis built a personal podcast-highlights tool ('Pod Me') for about $11 using Perplexity Comet as the agent harness and Grok 4.5 to organize deep-linked segments across up to 75 podcasts, and a tweet about it drew 1.1 million views.
anecdote🗣 Jason Calacanis (host)conviction: highJason CalacanisPerplexityGrok
So what: Shows how cheap AI agent tooling has become for building niche utilities — sub-$15 builds can generate outsized organic reach when shared publicly, reinforcing the build-in-public thesis.
“It cost me $11. It has now 1.1 million views this tweet.” ▶ 13:04
LinkedIn actively blocks AI browser agents such as Perplexity's Comet or Claude's browser from searching or contacting people on its platform, pushing power users toward third-party scraping tools built in countries with weaker data-scraping regulation such as Israel, the Philippines, and India.
data_point🗣 Tibo Lucas / Jason Calacanisconviction: mediumLinkedInPerplexityClaude
So what: A regulatory/enforcement arbitrage is forming around AI-agent data access — offshore scraping tools are becoming the workaround as major platforms lock down agentic browsing.
“if you use the Comet browser from Perplexity or any of the smart browsers or you try to use your browser from claude to like search for people to do anything, they try to kill it immediately.” ▶ 22:42
Apple v. OpenAI Trade-Secrets Lawsuit 6
Apple filed a 40-page lawsuit against OpenAI alleging a systematic effort to acquire confidential information, including encouraging Apple employees to study confidential materials ahead of OpenAI interviews and to bring hardware components and prototypes with them.
data_point🗣 Lon Harris (host)conviction: highAppleOpenAI
So what: Sets up potential injunctive relief against OpenAI's hardware ambitions and a high-profile trade-secrets precedent to track alongside the earlier Waymo v. Uber/Otto case.
“Apple last week filed a 40-page lawsuit against Open AI, alleging that the company made a systematic effort to acquire and use confidential information to help them replicate Apple's consumer electronics business.” ▶ 27:56
Jason Calacanis believes Apple would not have filed this trade-secrets suit unless it had strong evidence, comparing the situation to the earlier Waymo v. Uber case where the accused engineer was criminally convicted.
contrarian🗣 Jason Calacanis (host)conviction: highAppleOpenAIUberWaymo
So what: Investors should weight litigation risk to OpenAI's hardware plans (with Jony Ive) as elevated rather than treating the suit as a nuisance claim.
“you don't have this happen, these lawsuits, unless it is they have them dead to rights.” ▶ 30:03
Jason Calacanis argues OpenAI made a major strategic error by pursuing Apple hardware talent (including former Apple design executive Tang Tan) and damaging the ChatGPT-as-default-iPhone-assistant relationship, ceding that opportunity to Google, which he says is now making roughly $1 billion a year from its Gemini-Apple deal.
thesis🗣 Jason Calacanis (host)conviction: highOpenAIAppleGoogleGeminiSam Altman
So what: A concrete opportunity-cost thesis on OpenAI's platform strategy — the lost Apple default-assistant deal is now a recurring ~$1B/year revenue line for Google, worth tracking in Alphabet's disclosures as a proxy for what OpenAI forfeited.
“Google now is making a billion dollars a year off of the Apple relationship. They're doing Gemini. So, they lost the Chad GBT deal because of this.” ▶ 35:22
Jason Calacanis speculates that Jony Ive's OpenAI equity (from OpenAI's roughly $6.4 billion all-equity acquisition of his hardware startup) could now be worth more than his lifetime Apple equity, given OpenAI's rising valuation.
data_point🗣 Jason Calacanis (host)conviction: lowJony IveOpenAIApple
So what: A speculative but directional signal of how much paper wealth has shifted from Apple's design organization to OpenAI's hardware bet — worth checking against actual OpenAI cap-table disclosures if they surface in litigation discovery.
Jason Calacanis frames Sam Altman's pattern of major lawsuits (against Elon Musk, The New York Times, and now Apple) as 'enemies accumulating,' arguing these disputes could have been resolved as partnerships, and warns that once a hot company stumbles, accumulated ill will invites retaliation.
framework🗣 Jason Calacanis (host)conviction: mediumSam AltmanOpenAIElon MuskAppleNew York Times
So what: A reputational-risk framework for evaluating founder-led litigation exposure — watch for pressure on OpenAI to settle or restructure partnerships as a signal of weakening leverage if growth slows.
“Enemies accumulate. Be very very careful. And this is I think what Sam's learned.” ▶ 37:47
Jason Calacanis predicts Apple will pursue an injunction forcing OpenAI to halt or reverse elements of a competing hardware product and to stop poaching Apple employees, rather than settle quietly.
prediction🗣 Jason Calacanis (host)conviction: mediumAppleOpenAI
So what: Watch for a court-ordered delay to OpenAI's hardware device timeline as a near-term catalyst/risk for the AI-hardware race.
“I think they're going to go after the injunction which is you can't release a product and you got to like reverse this.” ▶ 42:37
Autonomous Vehicle Economics and Rollout Timeline 7
A newly proposed Washington DC bill would update the district's 2012 Autonomous Vehicle Act to allow fully driverless commercial robotaxi operation without a human safety operator; Waymo supports the bill while Uber opposes it in favor of a hybrid model mixing AV and human-driven rides.
data_point🗣 Lon Harris (host)conviction: highUberWaymo
So what: A concrete near-term regulatory catalyst to watch — the DC hearing outcome could signal how quickly other municipalities follow with driverless-commercial legislation.
China suspended new autonomous-vehicle licenses as of late April 2026, according to Reuters, after protests from roughly 10 million cab drivers facing about 10% downward pricing pressure from unrestricted robotaxi rollout across 20 providers in 10 cities.
data_point🗣 Jason Calacanis (host)conviction: highChina
So what: A leading indicator for US policy — labor-driven pushback tends to force AV licensing caps once rollout accelerates, even under a regulatory system with more central authority.
“China has suspended, this is from Reuters. What's the date on this one? April 29th, 2026.” ▶ 48:15
Jason Calacanis estimates ride-hailing is only about 1-2% of all US rides today, and of that, roughly 0.2-0.5% are currently autonomous (a figure he sourced from Gemini), meaning AVs are a small fraction of a small fraction of total US personal transportation.
data_point🗣 Jason Calacanis (host)conviction: mediumUberWaymoTesla
So what: Puts current AV hype in context — even under bullish doubling assumptions it takes years to meaningfully shift total ride volume, tempering near-term disruption timelines for rideshare/delivery labor and incumbent platform economics.
“1 to 2% of all rides in the US currently are ride shares and 0.2 to 5%, this is according to Gemini, 0.2 to 0.5% of those ride shares are in AVs currently.” ▶ 58:26
Assuming the AV share of US rides roughly doubles each year from today's sub-1% base, Jason Calacanis projects about 30% AV ride penetration by 2030 and roughly 50% by 2032-2033.
prediction🗣 Jason Calacanis (host)conviction: mediumUberWaymoTesla
So what: A concrete, falsifiable timeline investors can track against actual Waymo/Tesla/Zoox ride volume data to judge whether AV disruption is running ahead of or behind schedule.
“I am going to make two predictions. 50% of rides are um automated 2032.” ▶ 1:00:35
Jason Calacanis assigns greater than 50% odds that a viral video of an AV-caused fatality or serious injury triggers a one-to-two-year regulatory pause in autonomous-vehicle rollout, even though AVs are statistically safer than human drivers, who cause roughly 30,000 US deaths per year.
prediction🗣 Jason Calacanis (host)conviction: mediumWaymoTesla
So what: Identifies a tail-risk catalyst — a single viral incident could abruptly stall AV-exposed stocks (Tesla, Uber, Lyft) regardless of underlying safety statistics, a scenario worth monitoring via incident news flow.
“I think there is a very significant chance that all of this gets paused if just two people get killed” ▶ 59:42
Uber, Lyft, and DoorDash are managing what Jason Calacanis calls a 'civil war' transition — investing heavily in AV and robotics (Uber alone has put $10 billion into self-driving companies) while simultaneously supporting driver-protective legislation to slow the pace of full automation and retain their human workforce during the transition.
framework🗣 Jason Calacanis (host)conviction: highUberDoorDashLyft
So what: Explains an apparent contradiction in gig-economy platform behavior (pro-AV capex, anti-fast-rollout lobbying) as rational transition-risk management rather than hypocrisy — relevant to reading these platforms' AV capex and lobbying spend.
“Uber has put10 billion dollars into self-driving companies. So they're they know that this is happening.” ▶ 50:53
Tesla is positioned as the outlier pushing for federal preemption of state and local AV regulation, seeking a single national rulebook rather than the localized, city-by-city licensing approach Jason Calacanis expects to actually prevail in the US.
thesis🗣 Jason Calacanis (host)conviction: mediumTesla
So what: Suggests Tesla's regulatory strategy is structurally mismatched with how US transportation regulation actually works (state/local control), a potential headwind for its robotaxi ambitions relative to city-approved players like Waymo.
“that would be Tesla, right? Tesla wants federal legislation. They want to override every state's ability, every city's ability to make their own decisions.” ▶ 46:52
AI Job Displacement and Transition Policy 3
An open letter titled 'We Must Act Now,' signed by roughly 200 economists including 15 Nobel laureates plus the chief economists of OpenAI and Anthropic, Anthropic co-founder Jack Clark, former Google CEO Eric Schmidt, and venture capitalist Vinod Khosla, warns that AI will radically transform the US economy and calls for a plan to offset millions of potential white-collar job losses during the transition.
data_point🗣 Lon Harris (host)conviction: highOpenAIAnthropicJack ClarkEric SchmidtVinod Khosla
So what: A signal that AI-industry insiders now publicly acknowledge disruptive near-term labor impact — relevant to policy risk (automation taxes, transition funds) that could raise the cost of AI adoption for enterprises.
“200 economists including 15 Noble laureates published an open letter today. It's called we must act now.” ▶ 53:07
Jason Calacanis supports an 'AI unemployment fund' concept — an incremental tax on companies (he floats an extra 5%) when they replace a human job with AI, modeled on existing state unemployment-insurance contributions, to fund support for displaced workers.
thesis🗣 Jason Calacanis (host)conviction: mediumJason Calacanis
So what: A specific policy mechanism that, if adopted, would raise the effective cost of AI-driven headcount reduction for enterprises — worth modeling into automation ROI cases as regulatory risk.
“you put like an extra 5% in the in the tank for other people who are getting laid off like I like ideas like that.” ▶ 55:53
Jason Calacanis supports phased, state-level licensing caps on autonomous-vehicle rollout (roughly doubling the permitted share each year) funded by license fees earmarked for displaced-driver unemployment support, describing it as a workable transition mechanism.
thesis🗣 Jason Calacanis (host)conviction: mediumJason CalacanisUberDoorDash
So what: Another concrete policy lever (fee-funded phased licensing) that could shape the pace and cost of AV rollout for platform operators — worth tracking as state legislatures take up similar bills.
Crisis PR and Surveillance-Tech Backlash 4
Flock Safety CEO Garrett Langley calling opponents of his license-plate-camera network 'terrorists' (in response to the open-source Deflock camera-mapping project) backfired, and Jason Calacanis argues it mirrors past founder PR mistakes, such as Uber's Travis Kalanick dismissing surge-pricing critics, that inflame rather than defuse legitimate concerns.
contrarian🗣 Jason Calacanis (host)conviction: highFlock SafetyUber
So what: A reusable crisis-comms framework for evaluating portfolio companies facing backlash: dismissive, hyperbolic responses to legitimate criticism escalate controversy rather than containing it.
“Flock is doing good work, but their comms are suboptimal at best. I've seen this movie before with Robin Hood and Uber and it's an easy fix.” ▶ 1:03:05
Ring reportedly cut off its data-sharing collaboration with Flock Safety after backlash to a Super Bowl ad implying doorbell-camera footage would help 'spy on' neighbors, illustrating how partner platforms distance themselves from a surveillance company's PR failures.
data_point🗣 Lon Harris (host)conviction: mediumFlock SafetyRing
So what: A concrete example of reputational contagion risk in B2B surveillance/data partnerships — a partner's PR crisis can force other companies to sever integrations regardless of product legality.
Jason Calacanis argues Flock Safety could defuse privacy backlash by publicly committing to specific, auditable policies — no facial recognition, a 12-month data-retention cap, mandatory officer badge-ID logging for every license-plate lookup, and installation only in communities with roughly 75% or greater support — rather than dismissing critics.
framework🗣 Jason Calacanis (host)conviction: highFlock Safety
So what: A specific transparency playbook (retention caps, audit logs, community consent thresholds) that surveillance-adjacent companies could adopt to reduce regulatory and reputational risk — relevant to governance diligence on similar security-tech portfolio companies.
“We do not allow facial recognition. We do not take pictures of that and all data is gone in 12 months.” ▶ 1:07:29
Jason Calacanis notes he personally runs an extensive private surveillance setup (Unifi facial recognition and license-plate readers across multiple properties, retained indefinitely), illustrating that wealthy individuals already deploy the same surveillance capability that draws public backlash when done by companies like Flock Safety.
anecdote🗣 Jason Calacanis (host)conviction: mediumJason CalacanisUnifiFlock Safety
So what: Highlights an access/equity gap in surveillance tech — private, unregulated deployment by the wealthy versus regulated public deployment — a framing relevant to how surveillance-tech companies position themselves against privacy criticism.
“I have facial recognition on all my properties. I have license plate readers on all my properties.” ▶ 1:10:43
A Startup Is Trying to Buy PayPal… Craziest Deal of 2026! | E231273 min38 insights2026-07-15
▶ Watch on YouTube
On a This Week in Startups VC roundtable (host Alex with Eric Bahn of Hustle Fund and Jeff Morris Jr. of Chapter One), the panel dissects Stripe's reported $50B+ bid to take PayPal private and what it signals about public-vs-private market dynamics, then ranges across AI coding tools reaching 'AGI' levels, why startups avoid building custom model evals until forced to, why Chapter One is betting on LA/physical-AI deals to dodge fast-followers, the AI consumer-startup drought outside health hardware, AI regulation politics, crypto's pivot to stablecoins/RWAs over DAOs, and a Lloyd's-of-London-style space insurance thesis.
Stripe–PayPal Take-Private Deal & Corporate Ossification · 8Public–Private Market Blur & Secondary-Market Risk · 5AI Coding Tools Hitting an 'AGI' Ceiling · 5Model Lock-in, Custom Evals, and Competitive Moats · 4Chapter One's LA / Physical-AI Strategy to Avoid Fast Followers · 5Consumer AI Drought: Health Wins, Social Still Extraction-Driven · 5AI Regulation Politics, Youth Social-Media Limits, and Crypto's Institutional Pivot · 5Space Insurance as an Emerging Asset Class · 1
Stripe–PayPal Take-Private Deal & Corporate Ossification 8
Stripe, a private company, is reportedly trying to acquire PayPal, a public company, for more than $50 billion — an unusual direction for M&A that highlights how large private companies can now out-muscle public incumbents.
data_point🗣 Alex, hostconviction: highStripePayPal
So what: Watch for more well-capitalized private companies acquiring struggling public incumbents rather than the reverse; PayPal's minority shareholders face a steep markdown if the deal closes.
PayPal's share price has fallen from a peak above $300 around 2021 to a reported take-private offer near $16.50/share, illustrating how quickly a dot-com-era brand can lose the bulk of its market value.
data_point🗣 Alex, hostconviction: highPayPal
So what: A ~95% peak-to-trough decline in a former blue-chip fintech is a reminder that brand recognition alone doesn't protect valuation once growth and culture stall.
Eric Bahn argues large tech companies follow a predictable arc: they start scrappy and innovative, then professionalize as career-focused hires join and the culture shifts toward internal promotion politics rather than vision — a pattern he sees playing out at PayPal.
framework🗣 Eric Bahn, Hustle Fundconviction: highPayPal
So what: Investors can use this maturity-arc framework to flag aging tech incumbents at risk of stagnation or takeover before the market fully prices it in.
“oceans rise, empires fall” ▶ 3:33
PayPal's newly installed CEO (appointed around February 2026) told investors on the earnings call there are areas where the company can make large cuts, which Jeff Morris Jr. reads as confirmation of internal bloat and cultural calcification.
data_point🗣 Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter Oneconviction: mediumPayPal
So what: Signals that a cost-cutting/restructuring narrative at PayPal predates and likely factors into the Stripe acquisition talks.
“places where we can make large cuts in in the company”
Because it is private, Stripe can pursue a large, uncertain acquisition like PayPal without near-term public-market scrutiny of margins or EPS impact, giving it a longer time horizon than a public acquirer would have.
causal🗣 Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter Oneconviction: highStripePayPal
So what: Private-company balance-sheet flexibility is becoming a real competitive advantage in large-scale M&A, not just financing structure.
Eric Bahn can name fewer than 10 Silicon Valley tech companies (citing Microsoft and Oracle as examples) that have avoided the multi-decade ossification-and-decline trajectory seen at Yahoo and now PayPal.
framework🗣 Eric Bahn, Hustle Fundconviction: mediumMicrosoftOraclePayPal
So what: Durable multi-decade tech compounders are rare; investors should treat sustained reinvention as the exception, not the base case, when underwriting long-duration holds.
Webflow underwent major layoffs and a re-architecture around May 2026 to escape the same kind of cultural bloat that afflicted PayPal, after early investor Eric Bahn says the company's Web 2.0-era browser-design product got 'rug pulled' by the AI-native design paradigm.
anecdote🗣 Eric Bahn, Hustle Fundconviction: mediumWebflow
So what: A former category leader (Webflow) actively cutting staff to regain a 'start-uppy' pace is a live case study in whether cost discipline alone can restore competitiveness against AI-native challengers.
Fintech startup Flex closed a $70 million Series B1 at a $1.2 billion valuation around the same time as bank earnings came in strong, prompting Eric Bahn to float — as an untested hypothesis — that incumbent banks may start acquiring mid-tier, overlooked fintech startups.
exploratory🗣 Eric Bahn, Hustle Fundconviction: lowFlex
So what: A wave of bank-led fintech roll-up M&A, if it materializes, would be a departure from the current AI-dominated deal narrative and worth tracking via bank earnings commentary.
Public–Private Market Blur & Secondary-Market Risk 5
Jeff Morris Jr. argues the line between public and private companies is dissolving because private stock (e.g., Stripe shares via platforms like Solium) now trades with real liquidity, and companies like Anthropic and OpenAI issue informal quarterly performance updates that move public-market sentiment.
thesis🗣 Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter Oneconviction: highStripeAnthropicOpenAI
So what: If private companies effectively offer public-like liquidity and disclosure, the traditional rationale for going public (access to capital) weakens further, delaying IPO supply.
A recent dispute between USV and Andreessen Horowitz over Series H share terms — with conflicting claims about what was agreed — is cited as evidence that private markets lack the data transparency and price discovery of public markets.
anecdote🗣 Alex, hostconviction: mediumUSVAndreessen Horowitz
So what: Institutional-grade disputes over basic deal terms in late-stage private rounds signal governance and documentation risk that public-market investors don't face.
Jeff Morris Jr. expects more companies to go public over the next one to two years, pointing to SpaceX's IPO and anticipated Anthropic/OpenAI activity, but cautions that going public is no guarantee of upside since SpaceX is already trading below its IPO price.
prediction🗣 Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter Oneconviction: mediumSpaceXAnthropicOpenAI
So what: A rebound in IPO volume is likely near-term, but investors should expect a wide dispersion of post-IPO performance rather than assuming a rising tide.
Controlling who holds secondary shares has become a major operational burden for late-stage private CEOs, especially government contractors like Anduril, which must screen out foreign or unwanted investors (e.g., shell entities tied to sanctioned jurisdictions) to avoid jeopardizing government contracts.
causal🗣 Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter Oneconviction: highAnduril
So what: Defense/critical-industry startups increasingly need active cap-table governance infrastructure, not just fundraising — a service/tooling gap worth watching.
“we're trying not to. We're staying private and we're exercising pretty strict control over their their equity.” ▶ 12:00
Jeff Morris Jr. predicts tight, Anduril-style control over secondary-share trading will become the industry norm as more stories emerge of SPV managers disappearing with investor funds, likely prompting SEC-level intervention to clean up the secondary market.
prediction🗣 Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter Oneconviction: mediumAnduril
So what: Expect regulatory scrutiny of SPV structures and secondary-market intermediaries to increase, which could raise compliance costs for retail-facing secondary platforms.
AI Coding Tools Hitting an 'AGI' Ceiling 5
Hustle Fund runs with just 7 full-time staff (22 total including contractors) and only 4 investors managing roughly 700 portfolio companies, leaning heavily on internally built AI software for deal sourcing, outreach automation, and preliminary deal assessment.
data_point🗣 Eric Bahn, Hustle Fundconviction: highHustle Fund
So what: A 700-company portfolio run by 4 investors is a concrete data point on how far AI tooling can compress venture-firm headcount needs — a template smaller emerging managers may copy.
Eric Bahn believes current-generation coding AI tools (referencing Fable-based tooling and Opus) now give code-quality feedback at the level of a senior engineer, and calls this 'AGI' having effectively arrived within the coding domain specifically.
thesis🗣 Eric Bahn, Hustle Fundconviction: highFable
So what: If coding assistance has reached senior-engineer-level judgment, expect further compression of engineering headcount needs at startups and continued margin expansion for software companies.
“this is AGI. I think it's actually happened within coding” ▶ 35:32
Eric Bahn claims most venture capitalists have never actually used coding-AI tools like Claude Code in a real, hands-on way despite publicly urging founders to become AI-native — calling the industry's own AI adoption 'ass-backwards.'
contrarian🗣 Eric Bahn, Hustle Fundconviction: medium
So what: A gap between VCs' public AI advocacy and private AI usage suggests founders should discount generic 'be AI-native' advice from investors who haven't tested the tools themselves.
“the majority of VCs have not touched cloud code once” ▶ 23:37
Chapter One won an allocation in Better Up (invested in 2025) by building a working web app with user authentication using the founder's own infrastructure and demoing it at the second meeting — a task Jeff Morris Jr. says now takes roughly 5-10 minutes with current coding-AI tools.
anecdote🗣 Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter Oneconviction: highChapter OneBetter Up
So what: Demonstrating hands-on product-building capability during diligence is becoming a differentiated way for investors to win competitive deal allocations.
Jeff Morris Jr. predicts frontier AI labs' next major research push is 'physical AI' (robotics), interpreting labs' pivot toward robotics teams (e.g., OpenAI's) as an implicit signal that coding and other digital-native tasks are viewed as close to solved.
prediction🗣 Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter Oneconviction: mediumOpenAI
So what: Capital and talent flows into robotics/physical-AI teams at major labs are a leading indicator worth tracking for the next hardware-software investment wave.
“we conquered coding, and now we're going to um you know, focus on the physical world” ▶ 36:20
Model Lock-in, Custom Evals, and Competitive Moats 4
Jeff Morris Jr. estimates only a handful of pre-Series A companies in his portfolio build their own model evals in-house; most startups instead chase whichever model is cheapest until they reach product-market fit, at which point building defensibility against OpenAI and Anthropic becomes urgent.
data_point🗣 Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter Oneconviction: highOpenAIAnthropic
So what: Custom-eval tooling for post-PMF, pre-Series-A startups looks like an underserved market gap that Jeff Morris Jr. explicitly says needs to be built.
The real data-leakage risk to startups using major AI labs' models isn't training data (which labs contractually disclaim using) but usage metadata — tool-call frequency and patterns that Satya Nadella has said can itself be informative to the model provider.
framework🗣 Alex, hostconviction: mediumMicrosoft
So what: Startups building on frontier-model APIs should think about metadata exposure (call patterns, customer segmentation signals) as a competitive risk, not just raw data/prompt content.
At the pre-seed stage, Hustle Fund's ~700 portfolio companies will use whatever AI model is cheapest and available, with open-source models gaining traction purely on cost grounds; eval-building only becomes a real conversation once a company has a proven business post-PMF.
data_point🗣 Eric Bahn, Hustle Fundconviction: highHustle Fund
So what: Model-agnosticism at pre-seed means vendor-lock-in and eval-moat strategies aren't relevant filtering criteria for the earliest-stage investors yet.
Jeff Morris Jr. uses an F1 racing analogy: frontier models today are as close to each other in capability as F1 constructors' cars, so the real competitive edge for startups comes from company-specific fine-tuning and evals ('pit-stop strategy'), though he says most startups he sees are still at 'entry-level Miata spec.'
framework🗣 Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter Oneconviction: medium
So what: Investors should stop over-weighting which frontier model a startup uses and instead evaluate whether the team has built proprietary tuning/eval infrastructure around it.
“what the teams don't want to share is like their pit stop strategies for the next race, like the racing line they're going to take, like the tire management” ▶ 31:48
Chapter One's LA / Physical-AI Strategy to Avoid Fast Followers 5
Chapter One made 8 investments in the past year, 6 of them in Los Angeles and only 1 in Silicon Valley, deliberately avoiding the Bay Area software cluster where competitors can fast-follow a proven idea almost immediately.
data_point🗣 Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter Oneconviction: highChapter One
So what: Geographic and sector deconcentration away from Silicon Valley software is an explicit sourcing edge Chapter One is underwriting capital against.
Jeff Morris Jr. observes that physical-AI/hardware startup clusters form more slowly than software clusters because they require combined hardware-and-software execution and often sit inside regulated, legacy industries — a structural moat he views as an investment advantage.
framework🗣 Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter Oneconviction: high
So what: Regulatory and hardware complexity that slows competitor formation is being used explicitly as a moat-selection filter by at least one active venture fund.
“I find the clusters are slower to form within physical AI than software, which to me is an advantage” ▶ 39:22
Chapter One's LA-area bets include Airbase (a new bank that obtained the fastest US banking license on record, backed by Palmer Luckey), General Intuition (pivoted from gaming-clip app Medal TV, using that data plus ex-DeepMind UK talent to build physical-world/defense-drone AI), and Afero (an ex-Tesla team building software for auto manufacturers) — each chosen for unique data, regulatory positioning, or team rather than pure software speed.
thesis🗣 Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter Oneconviction: highAirbaseGeneral IntuitionMedal TVAfero
So what: A concrete checklist (proprietary data + regulatory moat + non-Silicon-Valley team) for sourcing defensible AI-adjacent startups outside the crowded software fast-follower zone.
Eric Bahn believes capital flooding into narrow, newly discovered AI-agent infrastructure niches (citing Oak, a company building a shared identity layer for agents that raised $60 million) is partly explained by mega venture funds needing to deploy large AUM into trendy categories, independent of proven total addressable market.
contrarian🗣 Eric Bahn, Hustle Fundconviction: lowOak
So what: Rapid multi-company funding sprees in narrow AI-infra niches (like agent identity) may be more AUM-deployment-driven than demand-driven, a caution flag for late entrants and follow-on investors.
Eric Bahn's investing mantra at Hustle Fund is that 'great hustlers look like anyone and come from anywhere,' and he argues that prolonged immersion in the Bay Area's insular tech culture erodes founders' and investors' originality of thought.
framework🗣 Eric Bahn, Hustle Fundconviction: mediumHustle Fund
So what: A geography-agnostic sourcing thesis (backed by Hustle Fund's actual portfolio) that investors overweighting Bay-Area-based founders may be missing comparable opportunity elsewhere.
“great hustlers look like anyone and come from anywhere” ▶ 45:41
Consumer AI Drought: Health Wins, Social Still Extraction-Driven 5
Jeff Morris Jr. says the biggest surprise of the AI wave so far is how few consumer companies have used AI in genuinely novel ways; early attempts to inject generative content into Instagram-style feeds largely flopped because users still want to connect with real people and real content.
contrarian🗣 Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter Oneconviction: high
So what: Consumer AI-content-feed startups betting on synthetic content substitution for social connection are working against a demonstrated user preference for authenticity.
“that to me was a sign that people actually do want to connect with real people and real content” ▶ 49:24
Consumer hardware/health startups such as Whoop and Oura have been the standout, under-recognized winners of the current consumer-startup era, while consumer social and marketplace startups have lagged behind — a gap Jeff Morris Jr. attributes to health being a large, under-served need incumbents ignored.
thesis🗣 Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter Oneconviction: highWhoopOura
So what: Consumer health/wearables remains a comparatively underappreciated category for new capital deployment relative to social/marketplace consumer bets.
“the wave of consumer hardware startups that have actually succeeded over the past several years is up is very understated” ▶ 50:02
Jeff Morris Jr. is bullish on 'AI-native fintech' products that give an AI agent its own bank account to act as an autonomous financial manager, predicting a breakout company will emerge in this category — but doubts it will be Robinhood, whose product surface he sees as already too crowded/busy.
prediction🗣 Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter Oneconviction: mediumRobinhood
So what: Agent-managed personal-finance products are a specific, actionable category to watch for new entrants rather than assuming incumbents like Robinhood default-win it.
Eric Bahn's fund backed Sage Haven, a safety-focused messaging app functioning as a guarded parallel to iMessage that lets parents and trusted community members message children, reflecting his broader conviction that consumer AI/safety products for families remain underbuilt.
anecdote🗣 Eric Bahn, Hustle Fundconviction: mediumSage Haven
So what: Child-safety-focused messaging/communication infrastructure is a specific consumer sub-category with active early-stage investment interest.
Both guests note that X's algorithm change (implemented the day of recording) sharply improved feed enjoyment, which Eric Bahn interprets as evidence that the 2005-2015-era 'joyful' style of social networking was displaced post-2015 by extraction-oriented, engagement-maximizing feed design — leaving room for a new startup to rebuild that experience without the extraction.
causal🗣 Eric Bahn, Hustle Fundconviction: mediumX
So what: A platform-level algorithm tweak visibly moving user sentiment suggests real appetite (and white space) for a chronological/relationship-first social product distinct from engagement-optimized incumbents.
AI Regulation Politics, Youth Social-Media Limits, and Crypto's Institutional Pivot 5
Jeff Morris Jr. argues Anthropic's (Dario Amodei's) early public messaging around AI-driven job loss was strategically damaging, undermining the public and political support the AI industry now needs to justify large infrastructure build-outs.
contrarian🗣 Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter Oneconviction: highAnthropic
So what: Lab communications strategy around job-displacement messaging is being blamed by an industry insider for eroding the political capital needed for data-center/infrastructure permitting and support.
“I was actually pretty shocked that um Dario and Anthropic, like a lot of their early comms, to me, even being in the in in the industry, were really scary”
Jeff Morris Jr. is broadly anti-regulation on which AI models can be released, arguing government oversight committees lack technical proximity to the technology and change unpredictably across administrations, and notes that Anthropic and OpenAI now maintain large Washington D.C. policy teams to manage this directly with both parties.
thesis🗣 Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter Oneconviction: highAnthropicOpenAI
So what: Regardless of which party controls Washington, expect continued heavy direct lab-to-government lobbying on model release policy rather than a stable, depoliticized regulatory framework.
Eric Bahn supports raising the effective minimum age for unrestricted social media access to 16-18 (citing UK/European precedent), comparing unrestricted youth social-media use to giving children cigarettes given that brain development, especially in males, continues to roughly age 25-26.
thesis🗣 Eric Bahn, Hustle Fundconviction: high
So what: Growing VC-class sentiment favoring youth social-media age restrictions could presage more startup/product opportunity in age-verification and safe-messaging tooling (paralleling his Sage Haven investment).
“brains are developing especially for men until we we don't stop developing until we're like 25 or 26” ▶ 56:54
Jeff Morris Jr. says stablecoins have dominated most large fintechs' product roadmaps for the past three years and will continue to, while the more novel current trend is bringing real-world assets (RWA) like equities on-chain to enable 24/7 trading and borrowing/lending — a direction Robinhood is already pursuing.
data_point🗣 Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter Oneconviction: highRobinhood
So what: Stablecoin infrastructure and on-chain RWA tokenization remain the highest-conviction, most durable crypto investment theses versus more speculative categories.
≈ “stablecoins have been i t for the past 3 years, a huge part of the of the focus” paraphrase
Jeff Morris Jr. says DAOs (decentralized autonomous organizations) have effectively died as a venture-backable governance model — a reversal from an era when VCs pitched entire 200-member DAOs for funding-round allocation — with venture and crypto both consolidating around founder-controlled 'founder mode' structures instead.
thesis🗣 Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter Oneconviction: high
So what: Governance-token/DAO-based fundraising structures are no longer a credible venture category; capital and talent have moved to founder-controlled institutional crypto infrastructure instead.
“you'd have like 200 members of the DAO, and the VCs would have to pitch the entire DAO on why they should take the VCs' money for the next funding round” ▶ 1:10:01
Space Insurance as an Emerging Asset Class 1
Eric Bahn frames Hustle Fund's portfolio company Charter Space, a launch-and-in-orbit insurance underwriter, as a modern Lloyd's of London — a company built on the thesis that rising launch volume and orbital payload value (satellites, habitats, potential data centers) will require growing risk-transfer infrastructure, similar to how 17th-century maritime insurance emerged for shipping trade.
thesis🗣 Eric Bahn, Hustle Fundconviction: mediumCharter Space
So what: As launch cadence and orbital asset value rise, dedicated space-insurance underwriting is a nascent but potentially large new insurance sub-category to track.
“I like to think of this company as Lloyd's of London” ▶ 1:04:59
The dawn of surgery bots + buy a home for $250 (w/ Andromeda & Mogul) | E231346 min24 insights2026-07-17
▶ Watch on YouTube
Andromeda Surgical CEO Nick Damiano describes building autonomous AI-driven surgical robots starting with a urology procedure (HoLEP), highlighting a 45-case data moat and $15M capital-efficient path to launch, while Mogul CEO Alex Blackwood details a fractional single-family rental investing platform with $250 minimums, 8-12% dividend yields, and plans for a secondary market.
Andromeda's Autonomous Surgery Roadmap · 5Surgical Data Moat & Scaling Playbook · 4Regulatory Strategy & Capital/Team Efficiency · 4AI/LLM Limits in Medical Robotics · 2Mogul's Fractional Real Estate Investor Economics · 5Mogul's Revenue Model, Liquidity Roadmap & Market Selection · 4
Andromeda's Autonomous Surgery Roadmap 5
Andromeda Surgical CEO Nick Damiano argues surgery today is 'almost untouched by AI' but expects AI and autonomy to become ubiquitous in surgical practice within roughly a decade, comparing the field's current state to the horse-and-buggy era of driving versus where autonomous cars like Tesla and Waymo are now.
thesis🗣 Nick Damiano, CEO of Andromeda Surgicalconviction: highAndromeda SurgicalNick DamianoTeslaWaymo
So what: Signals a long-run structural shift in surgical labor and medtech investment toward AI-driven robotics; investors should track early autonomous-surgery players before the category matures.
“surgery is currently almost untouched by AI, but in 10 years AI and autonomy will be ubiquitous” ▶ 1:43
Andromeda's current surgical robot is controlled via an iPad app running proprietary autonomy software paired with an off-the-shelf robotic arm, deliberately avoiding custom hardware so the company can focus engineering resources on the software/autonomy layer rather than building tools.
framework🗣 Nick Damianoconviction: highAndromeda SurgicalKuka
So what: Using off-the-shelf hardware versus vertically integrated robotics lowers capex and speeds iteration -- a capital-efficiency thesis investors can apply when evaluating other robotics startups.
“There's there's this arm from Cuka that we use right now. They're a German company that makes robot arms for different use cases” ▶ 11:40
Andromeda chose HoLEP (a laser prostate-reduction procedure for BPH) as its market-entry wedge because urologists were historically the earliest and most tech-forward adopters of surgical robots (pioneered by Intuitive Surgical's da Vinci), and because HoLEP has an unusually wide skill gap between novice surgeons (roughly 5 hours per case) and expert surgeons (as fast as 30 minutes), creating clear room for an AI-assisted skill-leveling product.
framework🗣 Nick Damianoconviction: highAndromeda SurgicalIntuitive Surgical
So what: Suggests a repeatable go-to-market playbook for surgical-AI startups: target sub-specialties with high surgeon variance and existing robot-adoption culture rather than broad platforms.
“you've got this huge gap between somebody who's just learning, who takes like five hours and struggles through a procedure, and then the best in the world who's doing it in 30 minutes easily” ▶ 4:26
Andromeda has already shipped early autonomy features including real-time surgical landmark tracking ('Google Maps for the body') and auto-pivoting control of the endoscope that automatically minimizes tissue-damaging force, with new software features released weekly based on live clinical cases.
data_point🗣 Nick Damianoconviction: highAndromeda Surgical
So what: A weekly ship cadence in a historically slow-moving medtech category is a differentiator investors can benchmark against incumbents' multi-year release cycles.
Damiano's long-term vision for surgery is a 'sue surgeon' model where a single surgeon oversees roughly ten simultaneous autonomous procedures from an air-traffic-control-style command position rather than performing one surgery at a time, expanding surgeon throughput rather than eliminating surgeons.
prediction🗣 Nick Damianoconviction: mediumAndromeda Surgical
So what: If realized, this multiplies effective surgeon supply many-fold, which could reshape healthcare labor economics and hospital staffing models -- a theme relevant to healthcare-labor and medtech investors.
“a surgeon sitting at kind of an air traffic control center where there's a lot of different procedures happening” ▶ 14:43
Surgical Data Moat & Scaling Playbook 4
Andromeda's biggest bottleneck is proprietary surgical data (robot kinematics, applied forces, tissue interaction) that cannot be scraped from the web or bought like typical enterprise AI training data; the company has performed only 45 total surgical cases to date, which Damiano says constitutes the entire world's dataset for this specific autonomous procedure.
data_point🗣 Nick Damianoconviction: highAndromeda Surgical
So what: Real-world procedure volume, not model architecture, is the moat in physical-AI/robotics categories -- investors should weight case-volume and clinical access as heavily as software talent when evaluating surgical-AI startups.
“it's the only data set of 45 cases that exist. Like that's 100% of the world's data” ▶ 7:37
Andromeda supplements its own clinical data by sourcing surgical videos from YouTube creators who are practicing surgeons, since kinematic and force data must be collected in-clinic but visual/procedural technique data can partly be gathered from existing public surgical video content.
data_point🗣 Nick Damianoconviction: mediumAndromeda Surgical
So what: Points to an underappreciated data source (creator-surgeon video libraries) that could become valuable IP or a licensing opportunity as surgical-AI demand grows.
Because the universe of distinct surgical tasks is finite and relatively small, Andromeda expects autonomy built for one procedure to generalize to anatomically similar procedures elsewhere in the body, letting the company scale from roughly one to two new procedures per year currently toward a stated target of ten new procedures per year.
framework🗣 Nick Damianoconviction: mediumAndromeda Surgical
So what: A task-transfer scaling model implies accelerating procedure coverage over time rather than linear cost per new procedure -- relevant to modeling the company's total addressable market expansion.
“because the set of surgical tasks people do now is finite and pretty small then yes you can you can generalize across procedures” ▶ 9:03
Damiano argues autonomous surgery is structurally easier to solve than autonomous driving despite having far less training data, because the surgical environment has no adversarial actors -- unconscious patients don't move or resist -- and fewer edge cases than the open road.
causal🗣 Nick Damianoconviction: mediumAndromeda SurgicalTeslaWaymo
So what: Reframes the assumption that surgery is harder than driving to automate -- investors comparing autonomous-vehicle timelines to autonomous-surgery timelines should adjust for lower environmental complexity, even though data scarcity remains a headwind.
“you don't have these other adversarial actors like other cars or pedestrians you have to model their behavior when you're in surgery. There's nobody else working against you” ▶ 11:01
Regulatory Strategy & Capital/Team Efficiency 4
Andromeda's FDA strategy is to submit frequent, small incremental updates rather than large infrequent submissions, since regulators evaluate the increment over the last-cleared version; keeping a surgeon in the loop, rather than shipping a fully autonomous unsupervised system, is viewed as easing regulatory acceptance.
framework🗣 Nick Damianoconviction: highAndromeda SurgicalFDA
So what: A frequent-small-submission regulatory cadence could let AI-medtech companies out-iterate incumbents who file large, infrequent submissions -- a structural speed advantage worth monitoring in FDA filings.
“what really matters is the increment over either what they've seen before or what you last submitted” ▶ 25:50
As of this interview, Andromeda is cleared to sell commercially only in New Zealand, is close to clearance in a few additional countries, and does not yet have U.S. regulatory clearance, despite already having signed LOIs from customers ready to pay once it can legally deploy.
data_point🗣 Nick Damianoconviction: highAndromeda Surgical
So what: Revenue is currently gated entirely by regulatory clearance rather than demand -- a binary catalyst investors should track (next clearance geography/date) before assuming commercial ramp.
“we're only clear to be on the market in New Zealand right now, though we're close in a few other countries” ▶ 24:55
Andromeda went from company founding to a launched robot in three years while spending only about $15 million total, which Damiano says makes it the fastest robotics company to reach launch and far more capital-efficient than the medtech-robotics industry norm of spending hundreds of millions of dollars pre-launch. The company is also about to announce a new funding round in the tens of millions of dollars.
data_point🗣 Nick Damianoconviction: highAndromeda Surgical
So what: Extreme capital efficiency versus incumbents (avoiding custom hardware, heavy automation of internal processes) suggests smaller check sizes can still produce category-competitive robotics products -- relevant comp for venture sizing in medtech robotics.
“we were the fastest robot from zero to launch in three years and we've only spent about 15 million total” ▶ 22:49
More than half of Andromeda's team has no prior healthcare industry experience; the co-founder previously built the first autonomous truck to drive on a U.S. freeway, and the company has hired several former autonomous-trucking colleagues as well as staff from Neuralink, making its talent base closer to an autonomous-vehicle/deep-tech company than a traditional medical device company like Intuitive Surgical.
data_point🗣 Nick Damianoconviction: highAndromeda SurgicalNeuralinkIntuitive Surgical
So what: Cross-pollination of autonomous-vehicle engineering talent into medtech is a hiring pattern investors can use to identify other 'AV talent diaspora' startups likely to move faster than legacy medtech incumbents.
“more than half of the team is not from has never worked in any healthcare at all” ▶ 23:33
AI/LLM Limits in Medical Robotics 2
Despite building an AI-autonomy company, Andromeda deliberately avoids using generative AI/LLMs within the actual surgical control product because current models are not yet reliable enough to rule out hallucination-style errors in a safety-critical setting; the current autonomy stack instead relies on the type of machine learning used in autonomous vehicles like Tesla and Waymo.
contrarian🗣 Nick Damianoconviction: highAndromeda SurgicalTeslaWaymo
So what: Counters the assumption that an 'AI company' automatically means LLM-based; investors should distinguish generative-AI hype from the perception/control AI actually being deployed in physical safety-critical systems.
“We have to be careful in the beginning to not have things that are going to hallucinate in weird ways” ▶ 21:35
Andromeda uses AI/LLMs extensively for internal operations such as documentation and parts of its coding workflow, but applies guardrails around AI-generated code because it can introduce nondeterministic bugs, and remains open to eventually using synthetic data or 'world model' approaches for surgical training data once those techniques mature, while acknowledging present drawbacks and risks of doing so today.
exploratory🗣 Nick Damianoconviction: lowAndromeda Surgical
So what: Signals synthetic-data/world-model techniques for physical-AI training are still pre-adoption even among aggressive AI-native robotics startups -- a leading indicator to watch for when these techniques cross into production use.
≈ “there are definitely drawbacks to using that and there's ways it could could go wrong” paraphrase
Mogul's Fractional Real Estate Investor Economics 5
Mogul, founded by ex-Goldman Sachs real estate private equity staff whose co-founder scaled Goldman's own single-family rental platform from zero to $1 billion in under 12 months with a team of three to four people, lets retail investors buy fractional equity shares in individual single-family rental homes for a $250 minimum, though the average check size is actually $15,000-$20,000 with 20-80 investors per property.
data_point🗣 Alex Blackwood, founder/CEO of Mogulconviction: highMogulAlex BlackwoodGoldman Sachs
So what: Despite marketing a low $250 entry point, the real investor base skews toward five-figure checks -- useful context for assessing Mogul's actual customer profile versus its retail-democratization pitch.
“my co-founder grew Goldman single family rental platform from zero to a billion in under 12 months with three to four individuals” ▶ 31:31
Mogul has completed 130 property acquisitions totaling over 600 rental units, with individual property values typically ranging from $500,000 to $2.5 million, and targets a blended investor return of roughly 15-20% annually (8-12% cash dividend yield plus 3-4% underlying appreciation amplified to 4-8% via mortgage leverage), while underwriting deals conservatively to a 12% IRR bare-case scenario.
data_point🗣 Alex Blackwoodconviction: highMogul
So what: A stated 15-20% blended target return with monthly cash distribution is a concrete benchmark investors can compare against public REITs or direct rental ownership.
“the assets typically yield between 8 to 12% per year for dividend payout” ▶ 0:31
Mogul structures each property purchase with an upfront maintenance reserve sized to the property's equipment lifecycle plus a full 12-month vacancy reserve, so that investors are never subject to a capital call even in a worst-case prolonged vacancy or major repair like a $67,000 HVAC replacement.
framework🗣 Alex Blackwoodconviction: highMogul
So what: Eliminating capital-call risk is a structural differentiator versus typical real estate syndications, reducing downside surprise risk for passive retail investors -- worth verifying against Mogul's actual reserve adequacy over a full market cycle.
“we actually capitalize a 12 month reserve. So that asset could sit completely vacant for up to 12 months and we would never call another dollar in the door” ▶ 36:00
Mogul investors receive a tax benefit where the 8-12% annual rental income yield is largely or entirely offset for tax-reporting purposes by real estate depreciation, which Blackwood calls a 'ghost expense' since it reduces taxable income without reducing the investor's actual cash flow.
framework🗣 Alex Blackwoodconviction: highMogul
So what: Depreciation-shielded rental yield materially changes after-tax return comparisons between Mogul and other yield-generating assets like bonds or REIT dividends, which are typically taxed as ordinary income.
“even though you're generating that 8 to 12% yield, the depreciation should cover it up and then some” ▶ 38:46
Mogul currently has no true liquidity mechanism: investors are told to expect a 5-7 year hold period per property with no way to exit early, comparable to the illiquidity of startup SPV investing that the host contrasts it with.
data_point🗣 Alex Blackwoodconviction: mediumMogul
So what: Illiquidity is a key risk factor for retail investors sold on the $250-minimum, 'headache-free' pitch -- worth flagging prominently against the return numbers when evaluating the product.
“today it's a 5 to sevenyear typical period” ▶ 42:27
Mogul's Revenue Model, Liquidity Roadmap & Market Selection 4
Mogul monetizes each deal through a 5% upfront platform fee on purchase price (amortized like roughly 1% per year of drag over a 5-year hold), plus a separate 1-1.5% fee paid by the property seller at closing when Mogul buys direct without a broker, plus roughly 2.75% effective interest earned on investors' maintenance and vacancy reserve cash.
data_point🗣 Alex Blackwoodconviction: highMogul
So what: The seller-paid fee and reserve interest are revenue streams that don't directly reduce investor returns, meaning Mogul's effective take-rate is higher than the visible 5% fee alone suggests -- important for modeling platform unit economics.
“we charge a platform fee up front. It's 5% charge as percentage of purchase price” ▶ 39:04
Mogul is launching new ancillary revenue lines including a title insurance joint venture (adding roughly $9,000-$12,000 per asset) by the end of the current quarter and a property insurance underwriting business, targeting roughly $4-5 million in recurring revenue in year one from these new verticals while also improving cash flow to investors via better underwriting data.
prediction🗣 Alex Blackwoodconviction: mediumMogul
So what: Vertical integration into title and insurance is a classic proptech margin-expansion playbook; the stated dollar figures give investors a near-term revenue catalyst to track for verification.
Mogul plans to launch a secondary market for its fractional property shares next year, with Mogul itself acting as the day-one market maker (taking a bid-ask spread and using rental income to cover potential debt service), aiming to create what Blackwood describes as a fully liquid secondary market that has never previously existed for this asset class.
prediction🗣 Alex Blackwoodconviction: mediumMogul
