So what: Offers a bullish scenario in which market-concentration risk resolves via broad earnings catch-up rather than a crash in mega-cap AI leaders; productivity and margin data are the key indicators to watch for this thesis playing out.
“If the adopters realize the expected benefit productivity because productivity is what drives margin, profits, GDP, the economy, everything.” ▶ 58:15
Demographics, Labor, and the AI Productivity Bet 2
Referencing a viral 'Centrini report' that got about 11 million views in a weekend and coincided with a market selloff around the start of the Iran war, Thomas argues the report's labor-market-disruption warnings ignored that the world may be at 'peak population,' with declining birth rates in Korea, China, and Japan and eventually the US, meaning AI-driven productivity gains could offset a shrinking workforce rather than simply displacing a growing one.
contrarian🗣 Jonathan Thomasconviction: mediumlabor marketdemographics
So what: A macro counter-narrative to AI-driven unemployment fears: declining global population growth could mean AI substitutes for a shrinking labor pool rather than displacing a growing one — worth tracking labor-force participation and immigration trends alongside AI capex data.
“if we're at peak population and it continues to go down and productivity goes up, you're threading a needle, but it could create the perfect outcome” ▶ 1:04:57
Despite his broadly non-negative long-run view on AI and labor, Thomas expects a possible 'air pocket' in white-collar employment over the next five years, even as he sees rising demand for robotics and AI-assisted caregiving to support roughly 68 million living baby boomers who will need physical care.
prediction🗣 Jonathan Thomasconviction: mediumlabor marketrobotics
So what: Distinguishes a near-term white-collar disruption risk (five-year horizon) from a long-run demand thesis for robotics/caregiving automation tied to an aging population — two separate AI-labor investment angles to track.
“we might have a little air pocket in the next 5 years for white collar employment” ▶ 1:05:55
Purpose-Driven Ownership: American Century and the Stowers Institute 3
American Century Investments directs over 40% of its annual dividends (roughly $2.2-2.3 billion cumulatively) to the Stowers Institute for Medical Research, a structure Thomas says he uses as a closing argument and frequent 'tiebreaker' when competing against larger rivals in institutional finals.
data_point🗣 Jonathan Thomasconviction: highAmerican Century InvestmentsStowers Institute for Medical Research
So what: A concrete example of how a non-financial differentiator (ownership/purpose structure) functions as a real institutional-sales edge for a mid-sized manager competing against BlackRock, Vanguard, and Fidelity on scale.
“know that 40% of the dividends that we generate will go to fund medical research to create a better world for you, your kids” ▶ 1:09:41
Thomas argues American Century's private, non-public ownership removes quarterly earnings pressure, letting the firm invest and reinvest for the long term in ways he believes publicly traded competitors cannot match.
thesis🗣 Jonathan Thomasconviction: highAmerican Century Investments
So what: An ownership-structure thesis: privately held asset managers may have a durable capital-allocation advantage over public peers in funding long-payoff bets such as new product lines or research, relevant when assessing competitive dynamics among asset managers.
A Stowers Institute discovery — studying a fish species' ability to regenerate bone — was licensed to Amgen, which now sells a drug that can reverse, not just slow, osteoporosis, one of the rare instances where the nonprofit research arm's foundational science was directly commercialized.
anecdote🗣 Jonathan Thomasconviction: highAmgenStowers Institute for Medical Research
So what: Illustrates a tangible pipeline from nonprofit foundational research to commercial pharma outcomes, reinforcing the credibility of American Century's purpose-driven differentiation story with a real monetization event.
“they have a drug that not only slows but can reverse osteoporosis” ▶ 1:16:38
Will the Fed Buy Stocks? | Animal Spirits 47372 min30 insights2026-07-15
▶ Watch on YouTube
Ben Carlson and Michael Batnick weigh the bull vs. bear case for the AI-driven market, concluding that cheapening valuations (especially Nvidia's forward P/E) and stable analyst EPS accuracy make the bull case stronger than the bear case for now. They lay out a bigger structural thesis that US households now hold more wealth in stocks than real estate, making equities systemically important enough that the Fed could someday step in and buy stocks directly during a severe downturn, and review data suggesting AI has so far been net job-creating rather than a labor-market killer.
Hyperscaler capex and market volatility · 6AI bubble debate versus valuation reality · 5Market breadth beyond mega-cap tech · 3Household wealth shift and the 'Fed buy stocks' thesis · 5AI and the labor market · 4Fixed income's improved margin of safety · 2K-shaped economy narrative cooling · 1Consumer behavior and media economics · 4
Hyperscaler capex and market volatility 6
IBM fell 23% pre-market after disappointing earnings despite being roughly a $270 billion company and one of the 50 largest in the world, showing that even mega-caps can still suffer sudden single-day 'flash crash'-style drops.
anecdote🗣 co-host, Animal Spiritsconviction: mediumIBM
So what: Size alone no longer protects a stock from violent single-day repricing on an earnings miss; a reminder that idiosyncratic risk persists even in mega-caps.
A Bank of America chart shows hyperscaler free cash flow crashing at almost the same rate semiconductor company free cash flow is rising, an inverse 'trade-off' the hosts called the chart of the year, and they argue this transfer of cash from hyperscalers to chipmakers cannot continue indefinitely.
data_point🗣 co-host, Animal Spiritsconviction: highhyperscalerssemiconductorsBank of AmericaNvidia
So what: Watch hyperscaler free cash flow trends as a leading indicator of AI capex fatigue; a reversal would pressure semiconductor earnings that are currently absorbing that cash.
“This chart cannot continue. It just can't.” ▶ 8:28
Meta released a low-priced agentic coding model (Muse Spark 11) through its own model API, raising the question of whether hyperscalers will eventually undercut each other on AI model pricing in a way that erodes their own profitability even as capex stays high.
contrarian🗣 co-host, Animal Spiritsconviction: exploratoryMetaMark Zuckerberghyperscalers
So what: A price war among AI model providers would be bearish for hyperscaler margins even if AI usage keeps growing, decoupling revenue growth from profitability.
The average absolute daily price change for the semiconductor index has been 3.36% over the last 50 days, a volatility level historically only seen during the COVID crash, the 2008 financial crisis, and the dot-com bubble unwind.
data_point🗣 co-host, Animal Spiritsconviction: mediumsemiconductorstechnology sector
So what: Elevated realized volatility in AI-linked chip stocks signals crowded, leveraged positioning that can amplify moves in either direction.
≈ “3.36% has been the average absolute daily change over the last 50 days, and you only see spikes like that during the COVID crash, during the financial crisis, and during the dot bubble unwind.” paraphrase
Goldman Sachs' momentum factor had one of its largest three-week sell-offs on record, down roughly 8% over three weeks, which the hosts read as a healthy unwind of crowded, leveraged momentum positioning rather than a broad market signal.
data_point🗣 co-host, Animal Spiritsconviction: mediumGoldman Sachsmomentum factor
So what: A momentum-factor unwind of this size can look like a market-wide selloff but is more about crowded positioning than deteriorating fundamentals.
Hyperscalers have barely begun borrowing to fund AI capex and still have substantial unused borrowing capacity, meaning the current capex cycle could extend well beyond what current spending levels suggest if they choose to lever up further.
causal🗣 co-host, Animal Spiritsconviction: mediumhyperscalersAI infrastructurecorporate debt
So what: Watch hyperscaler debt issuance as a signal of how much longer the AI capex supercycle can run; low current leverage means the cycle isn't obviously near its ceiling.
≈ “They haven't really even started borrowing that much money yet. They're just dipping their toe in the water. They still have so much capacity to borrow if they want to keep putting their foot on the gas pedal.” paraphrase
AI bubble debate versus valuation reality 5
One host compiled 10 reasons to be bearish (circular hyperscaler capex, Mag Seven underperformance, AI spending bleeding into GDP, retail investors 'all in,' inflation still around 3.5%, mortgage rates near 7%, complacency after three straight years of double-digit gains, AI 'checking every bubble box,' an overdue recession after 17 years, and stretched multi-year annualized returns) but concluded the bear case is weaker than the bull case because the bullish reasons are happening now while the bearish reasons are things that could happen but currently aren't.
framework🗣 co-host, Animal Spiritsconviction: mediumS&P 500hyperscalersMag SevenAI
So what: A structured bull/bear checklist for the AI trade; investors should track which of the 10 bearish conditions (especially a recession) actually starts materializing rather than assuming the bubble thesis by default.
≈ “The bearish arguments are way flimsier because the bullish reasons are happening now. The bearish reasons are things that could happen, but they're not happening.” paraphrase
Nvidia's valuation on a forward P/E basis is the cheapest it has been since early 2019, according to a Bloomberg chart, because earnings have grown faster than the stock price even after Nvidia's massive multi-year rally.
data_point🗣 co-host, Animal Spiritsconviction: highNvidia
So what: Falling forward P/E despite a huge run-up undercuts the simple 'Nvidia is a bubble stock' narrative and suggests the market may be pricing in more durable earnings than headline price gains imply.
≈ “Bloomberg had a chart that shows Nvidia's valuation is the cheapest since early 2019 on a PE ratio, which is kind of nuts to think about.” paraphrase
Forward P/E ratios for technology stocks are currently below both their 10-year and 5-year averages and near the lowest levels since the start of 2025, meaning tech valuations are improving even as many commentators call this a technology bubble.
contrarian🗣 co-host, Animal Spiritsconviction: mediumtechnology sectorDuality Research
So what: Cheapening forward valuations weaken the 'obviously overvalued' bubble argument and suggest the AI trade may be more earnings-driven than multiple-expansion-driven.
“Tech stock valuations are improving during what many think is a technology bubble.” ▶ 16:59
Analysts' forward S&P 500 EPS estimates have historically been accurate 67% of the time, with actual earnings landing within 5% of estimates, and estimates only diverge meaningfully from actuals during recessions.
framework🗣 co-host, Animal Spiritsconviction: highS&P 500earnings estimates
So what: A genuinely bearish call on current high forward-earnings expectations is effectively a recession call, not a valuation call, since estimates are historically reliable outside of downturns.
“They are quite accurate 67% of the time. Actual earnings are within 5% of estimates.” ▶ 21:00
Retail investors' net single-stock buying has fallen to a new post-COVID low, per Vanda Research data, which complicates the 'retail is all-in and driving the market' bear argument, though part of the decline may reflect retail shifting into single-stock ETFs instead.
data_point🗣 co-host, Animal Spiritsconviction: mediumretail investorsVanda Research
So what: If retail single-stock buying is genuinely fading rather than just migrating to ETF wrappers, one commonly cited late-cycle warning sign is weaker than assumed.
≈ “Retail investors' net single stock buying has fallen to a new post-COVID low.” paraphrase
Market breadth beyond mega-cap tech 3
The S&P 500 ex-Technology Index (ticker SPXT) closed at a new all-time high even while a meaningful share of technology names sit in a bear market, indicating the rest of the market is quietly strong.
data_point🗣 co-host, Animal Spiritsconviction: highS&P 500SPXT
So what: Broadening strength outside of tech reduces concentration risk narrative and suggests the rally isn't solely a mega-cap tech phenomenon.
≈ “The S&P 500 ex-Technology Index closed the week at a new all-time high.” paraphrase
Advance-decline lines for the S&P 500, S&P 400, and S&P 600 have all pushed to fresh all-time highs, and the equal-weight S&P 500 (RSP) is also at all-time highs, signaling broad market participation despite tech-sector turbulence.
data_point🗣 co-host, Animal Spiritsconviction: highS&P 500S&P 400S&P 600equal-weight index
So what: Broad advance-decline strength is a healthier technical signal than a narrow mega-cap-led rally and argues against an imminent top driven by internal weakness.
≈ “The advanced decline lines for the S&P 500, 400, and 600 have all pushed to fresh all-time highs.” paraphrase
Small-cap strength is itself partly an AI trade: about 24% of the Russell 2000 index is tied to AI in some form (tech is only ~18% of the index, industrials ~17% and also AI-exposed), and 52% of the Russell 2000's total first-half return came from AI-tied companies, according to Sonu of the Carson Group.
data_point🗣 co-host, Animal Spiritsconviction: mediumRussell 2000small capsAICarson Group
So what: Small-cap outperformance shouldn't be read as a pure 'AI trade rotation into value' story; much of the Russell 2000's gains are still effectively an AI-exposure bet, mainly via industrials.
“24% of the Russell 2000 index is tied to AI in some form. 52% of the total first half return comes from companies tied to AI.” ▶ 24:19
Household wealth shift and the 'Fed buy stocks' thesis 5
US household exposure to equities is at a record high, and the stock market is now a significantly greater component of total household net worth than real estate, a reversal that has never occurred before at this scale.
data_point🗣 co-host, Animal Spiritsconviction: highUS householdsequitiesreal estate
So what: If equities are now the dominant store of American household wealth, a severe bear market has larger macro (consumption, wealth-effect) consequences than in prior real-estate-dominated eras.
≈ “The stock market is a significantly greater component of total household net worth than real estate now, which blows my mind.” paraphrase
The shift toward equities dominating household net worth is structural, not cyclical, driven by the post-1980s rise of 401(k) and IRA-based stock ownership: only about 4% of Americans owned stocks in the 1950s, rising to about 19% by 1983, versus roughly 55-60% of Americans owning stocks today.
thesis🗣 co-host, Animal Spiritsconviction: highUS households401(k)equities
So what: Investors betting on a reversion to pre-1980s low equity ownership as a mean-reversion trade are likely fighting a permanent structural shift, not a temporary cyclical extreme.
“In the 1950s it was like 4% of people own stocks. By 1983 it was 19%.” ▶ 29:10
Because roughly 55-60% of Americans now own stocks (per Eric Balchunas' Bloomberg analysis), the stock market has become systemically important enough that the Fed could plausibly buy stocks directly during a severe future downturn, similar to what the Bank of Japan and China's central bank have already done.
contrarian🗣 co-host, Animal Spiritsconviction: mediumFederal ReserveBank of JapanChinaEric Balchunas
So what: A tail scenario worth pricing: direct central-bank equity purchases during the next crisis would put a much firmer floor under US stocks than markets currently assume.
≈ “I think he's absolutely right. I think it could happen. It would not shock me at all.” paraphrase
Because the stock market is now systemically important to household wealth, future bear markets are more likely to resemble fast 'flash crashes' that force policymakers into quick reversals than slow grinding declines, citing 2008's TARP reversal, COVID-era stimulus, and the 2025 tariff 'Liberation Day' 90-day pause as precedents.
framework🗣 co-host, Animal Spiritsconviction: mediumFederal Reservetariffsstock market
So what: Investors should expect sharper, shorter drawdowns followed by rapid policy reversals rather than prolonged bear markets, changing the calculus for buying dips versus waiting out declines.
“Bare markets in the future are going to be flash crashes.” ▶ 33:40
Michael Batnick argued that sending direct stimulus checks is the most effective way to fight a recession, and defended the policy despite the 2021 inflation surge by arguing the inflation was driven primarily by supply-chain disruption and a demand/supply imbalance rather than the checks themselves.
contrarian🗣 Michael Batnickconviction: mediumfiscal stimulusinflationFederal Reserve
So what: If the next recession occurs without a comparable supply-chain shock, direct stimulus checks could be deployed again with less inflation risk than the 2021 experience implies.
≈ “Part of the reason why we got the inflation was not just the checks, it was that the supply chain was turned off, and there was a huge imbalance of way too much demand and not enough of supply.” paraphrase
AI and the labor market 4
US software development job postings are up 15% since Claude Code's launch in late February 2025, even as overall job postings fell 7% over the same period, and 71% of the gains came from senior roles rather than junior hiring, per Indeed's chief economist.
data_point🗣 co-host, Animal Spiritsconviction: highClaude CodeAnthropicIndeedsoftware jobs
So what: Direct evidence against the thesis that AI coding tools are shrinking software engineering headcount; hiring for the roles AI is supposedly best at replacing is actually rising.
“US software development job postings are up 15% since the launch of Claude Code in late February 2025, while overall job postings fell by 7% over the same period.” ▶ 38:01
The unemployment rate for workers aged 20-24 has been essentially unchanged since ChatGPT's launch in late 2022, undercutting the widely repeated fear that AI is disproportionately displacing young, junior white-collar workers.
data_point🗣 co-host, Animal Spiritsconviction: highChatGPTlabor marketunemployment
So what: The most feared cohort for AI displacement (entry-level white-collar workers) shows no unusual deterioration in employment data nearly three years into widespread generative AI adoption.
The recent pattern of professional and business-services job growth rising and falling looks more like a pandemic-era hiring 'bullwhip' normalization (overhiring followed by correction) than an AI-driven layoff wave.
causal🗣 co-host, Animal Spiritsconviction: mediumprofessional services jobslabor market
So what: Attributing white-collar job softness to AI may be premature; pandemic-era hiring distortions offer a simpler, more consistent explanation for the recent data.
Despite continually more capable AI models, rising token consumption, and growing familiarity with AI tools, white-collar employment trends look better now than they did a year ago, meaning the repeated 'wait six months' AI-doomer predictions about mass white-collar job losses have not come true so far.
contrarian🗣 co-host, Animal Spiritsconviction: exploratoryAIwhite-collar employment
So what: The AI-driven-layoffs bear case for markets and the economy has repeatedly failed to show up in the data; investors should demand actual evidence rather than assume the disruption is imminent, while noting adoption may still be early.
“As of right now, they are [wrong]. Now, everyone keeps saying just wait. These models are going to get better. People are going to use them more. Companies haven't even started.” ▶ 39:58
Fixed income's improved margin of safety 2
The taxable-equivalent yield on high-yield municipal bonds is around 9%, roughly matching typical private-credit yields of about 9%, giving investors a liquid alternative that offers comparable income to illiquid private credit.
data_point🗣 co-host, Animal Spiritsconviction: mediummunicipal bondsprivate credit
So what: Investors chasing private-credit yield for income may be able to get comparable after-tax income in a liquid muni-bond wrapper instead, avoiding illiquidity risk.
“Look at the taxable equivalent yield on high yield munis. 9%. Compared to private credit, also around 9%.” ▶ 41:40
Current fixed-income yields (roughly 5% to 9% depending on credit quality) give investors a far bigger margin of safety than at any point in the last 15 years, reversing the 'rewardless risk' dynamic of the 2010s through early 2020s that pushed capital into private credit in search of yield.
thesis🗣 co-host, Animal Spiritsconviction: highbondsfixed incomeprivate credit
So what: Bonds are once again a genuinely attractive, lower-risk income option, reducing the need to reach into illiquid or riskier credit for yield.
“You have a way bigger margin of safety in bonds than you've had for any time in the last 15 years.” ▶ 43:08
K-shaped economy narrative cooling 1
In June, higher-income households' after-tax wage growth eased while lower-income cohorts' wage growth improved to a similar level, per Mike Zaccardi's data, suggesting the 'K-shaped economy' divergence narrative was partly a cyclical phase rather than a permanent structural break.
contrarian🗣 co-host, Animal Spiritsconviction: mediumK-shaped economywage growth
So what: Investment and consumer-spending theses built on a permanent, widening income divergence should be reassessed; the underlying data show the gap is narrowing, not widening.
Consumer behavior and media economics 4
Investors who started in Bitcoin ETFs at their January 2024 launch lost an average of 5.8% annually on a dollar-weighted basis even though Bitcoin itself roughly rose from about $46,000 to around $60,000+, because investors chased performance by buying and selling at the wrong times, per analysis referencing Jason Zweig and Jeffrey Ptak.
data_point🗣 co-host, Animal Spiritsconviction: highBitcoinBitcoin ETFs
So what: Even a rising underlying asset can produce negative investor returns due to behavioral timing mistakes; a cautionary data point for anyone chasing recent Bitcoin ETF performance.
“In aggregate, people who started out in the Bitcoin funds lost an average of 5.8% annually jumping in and out of these funds.” ▶ 25:52
Rising GLP-1 drug adoption (11% of US adults per this year's Gallup survey, likely undercounted) coincides with sweet-snack sales down 17% and broader US snack sales down 4% over the past four years, including at Smucker's Hostess snack division.
causal🗣 co-host, Animal Spiritsconviction: mediumGLP-1HostessSmucker'sGallup
So what: GLP-1 adoption is showing up as a measurable demand headwind for packaged-snack and confectionery companies, a trend worth tracking for consumer-staples exposure.
“In this year's Gallup survey, 11% of US adults currently take GLP-1.” ▶ 1:01:04
Comcast is splitting its cable and broadband businesses because each has become 'an anchor on the other,' and spun-off sister company Versant's Fandango/movie-ticketing platform segment is its only growing business line, generating $826 million in 2025 (up 3.9%) and accelerating to 9.5% revenue growth in Q1 2026.
data_point🗣 co-host, Animal Spiritsconviction: mediumComcastVersantFandangoCNBCMSNBC
So what: Legacy media conglomerates continue to break apart declining linear/cable assets from growing digital-adjacent businesses; Fandango's accelerating growth stands out as a bright spot within an otherwise shrinking media portfolio.
≈ “Fandango... did $826 million in 2025, up 3.9%. The only Versant segment that grew year-over-year, and it's accelerating. Q1 2026 platforms revenue rose 9.5% driven by Fandango movie ticketing.” paraphrase
Netflix's all-at-once release model causes shows to fall out of the cultural conversation within days as viewers binge and move on, while HBO's weekly-episode release model sustains audience engagement over weeks, helping explain why Netflix season-two shows often crash in viewership (e.g., White Lotus grew its audience 63% from season 1 to 2, House of the Dragon dropped only 8% between seasons) while comparable Netflix shows do not sustain audiences across seasons.
framework🗣 co-host, Animal Spiritsconviction: mediumNetflixHBOWhite LotusHouse of the Dragon
So what: Release-cadence strategy (weekly vs. binge) is a meaningful lever for subscriber retention and content ROI, a factor worth weighing for streaming-platform investment theses.
“Netflix gives you the whole season at once and most people finish it in four to six days. After that, the show falls out of the conversation.” ▶ 51:19
We Answer the Number One Question Facing Investors Right Now | WDWL38 min28 insights2026-07-13
▶ Watch on YouTube
DataTrek Research's Nick and Jessica argue the US has avoided recession for all but two months of the last 16 years because of structural changes (services economy, lower energy intensity, better management, faster policy response, gig-economy labor buffer, elevated deficit spending) and that this stability, not irrational exuberance, is what underwrites both the S&P's 20x multiple and the hyperscaler AI capex boom; they then flag that tech's June extreme overperformance versus the S&P is due for mean reversion, that semiconductor valuations have run far ahead of the rest of the Magnificent 8, and that history (both 2000-era dot-com and NASDAQ's four-year win-streak base rates) says the real risk to watch is a Fed-driven rate shock, not slowing demand.
Why the US Hasn't Had a Recession in 16 Years · 7Stable Macro Is the Real Source of the AI Capex Boom · 2Cracks in 'This Time Is Different' · 5Tech's June Extreme and the Case for Mean Reversion · 4Semiconductor Valuations Have Run Far Ahead of the Rest of the Mag 8 · 4Dot-Com Echo: Capex Spenders Losing the Market's Confidence · 2NASDAQ's Year-Four Seasonality Base Rates · 4
Why the US Hasn't Had a Recession in 16 Years 7
Over the last 15 years the US has been in recession for just over two months (1% of the time, all during the pandemic), versus 14% of the time in the prior 15 years and 13% in the 15 years before that, despite a string of shocks (2011 Greek debt crisis, 2015 global growth scare, 2018-19 Fed policy mistake and tariff uncertainty, 2021-22 inflation surge, 2022 Russia-Ukraine oil spike and 500bp of Fed hikes, 2023 regional bank failures, 2025-26 trade policy shock and Middle East war) that historically would each have been enough to trigger one.
data_point🗣 Nick, DataTrek Research co-founderconviction: highFederal ReserveUS economy
So what: If the market is correctly pricing a structurally lower recession probability, current valuations, tight credit spreads, and low volatility are rational rather than complacent — but it also means investors are unusually unpracticed at pricing an actual downturn.
“we have had literally no recession for the better part of 16 years now” ▶ 3:38
The shift from a manufacturing-heavy to a services-based US economy reduces recession risk because services demand (haircuts, healthcare, restaurants) is far less cyclical than durable-goods demand, and because services businesses don't build the inventory overhangs that historically cascade into production cuts and layoffs the way auto dealers (who carry ~60 days of inventory) do when demand drops.
framework🗣 Nick, DataTrek Research co-founderconviction: highUS economy
So what: Investors modeling recession risk from old industrial-cycle indicators (inventory builds, durable orders) may be using a framework that no longer fits a services-dominated economy.
“the typical dealer keeps 60 days inventory on the lot because they know the customers want to come in and buy a car right away” ▶ 6:35
The US economy has become significantly less energy-intensive than it was in the 1970s-90s, which reduces the frequency with which oil-price shocks (like the one that made Chrysler and its supplier base essentially bankrupt in the early 1990s) are able to trigger a recession.
causal🗣 Nick, DataTrek Research co-founderconviction: mediumChryslerUS economy
So what: Energy-shock-driven equity selloffs (like 2022's oil spike) may prove shallower and shorter-lived than historical analogs suggest.
US corporate management quality has structurally improved versus prior generations because today's executives have decades more accumulated management literature and case studies to learn from, making this an underappreciated driver of the economy's recession resistance even though high CEO turnover (average tenure ~4 years) creates the opposite impression.
contrarian🗣 Nick, DataTrek Research co-founderconviction: mediumUS economy
So what: Better-managed corporates should mean more resilient margins and fewer boom-bust capex mistakes across a cycle — a qualitative factor that's hard to price but supports paying up for stability.
“management is quite strong” ▶ 12:02
Monetary and fiscal policy have become much faster to correct course after shocks — Jerome Powell reversed his Q4 2018 rate-hike stance within days in January 2019 once the VIX spiked to 36 and stocks entered a bear market — which shortens the window in which a shock can snowball into a recession.
causal🗣 Nick, DataTrek Research co-founderconviction: highFederal ReserveJerome Powell
So what: A demonstrated, fast-acting policy put compresses the tail risk of equity drawdowns turning into prolonged bear markets, supporting the market's habit of buying dips.
The tech-enabled gig economy acts as a labor-market buffer, letting workers who lose a full-time job pick up gig work while searching for their next role, which smooths what would otherwise show up as recessionary spikes in unemployment.
framework🗣 Nick, DataTrek Research co-founderconviction: mediumUS economy
So what: Unemployment claims data may understate labor market stress and simultaneously make classic recession triggers (rapid layoff cascades) less likely to materialize as sharply as in past cycles.
US government deficit spending has run at roughly 6% of GDP recently versus about 3% from 1979-2010, providing incremental baseline demand that props up the economy — yet 10-year Treasury yields today are unchanged from 2002-2004 even though total federal debt has risen from about 60% of GDP then to 122% now, a disconnect Nick calls a 'deus ex machina' that the market hasn't punished with higher borrowing costs.
contrarian🗣 Nick, DataTrek Research co-founderconviction: mediumFederal ReserveUS economy
So what: This is a fragile support for the recession-resistance thesis: if the market ever repriced US borrowing costs for the higher debt/GDP level, the baseline demand cushion from deficit spending could reverse quickly and remove one leg of the 'no recession' case.
“it's sort of like a magic trick” ▶ 9:15
Stable Macro Is the Real Source of the AI Capex Boom 2
A steady economy producing steady earnings and cash-flow growth (current S&P earnings growth is running around +20% mid-cycle) is what supports the S&P 500 trading near 20x earnings and pushes investment-grade and high-yield credit spreads to multicycle (roughly 1st-percentile) lows and the VIX to a persistent sub-20 level — it is a rational repricing of stability, not irrational exuberance, and it is also what sustains the 'buy the dip' feedback loop among retail and institutional investors alike.
thesis🗣 Nick, DataTrek Research co-founderconviction: highS&P 500Federal Reserve
So what: If this stability narrative cracks, the same logic implies simultaneous multiple compression, credit-spread widening, and a vol spike — a coincident unwind across equities, credit, and vol rather than an isolated equity correction.
“You can buy the dip if you have confidence the economy is going to stay okay. You can't buy the dip if you don't.” ▶ 13:49
The current AI capex supercycle is only possible because hyperscalers, unlike industrial CEOs 25 years ago, no longer feel they must budget an incremental cash buffer for a cyclical downturn — many of today's capex decision-makers have never lived through a US recession, so the macro stability itself is the direct enabler of the scale of AI investment, not merely a coincidental backdrop.
causal🗣 Nick, DataTrek Research co-founderconviction: highAI capexhyperscalers
So what: A genuine change in the economy's recession odds (e.g., a Fed-driven shock) would be a direct threat to AI capex spending levels, not just to AI stock multiples, because the underlying confidence to spend without a downturn buffer would be undermined.
“We would not be investing this much in AI if the hyperscalers looked at their businesses which are all cyclical, right?” ▶ 14:15
Cracks in 'This Time Is Different' 5
Strong equity returns from this stability regime disproportionately benefit people who already have savings to invest (compounding at roughly 10%/year doubles wealth every seven years), while those without investable cash flow get none of the compounding benefit, meaning the recession-resistant economy is simultaneously widening the wealth gap.
thesis🗣 Nick, DataTrek Research co-founderconviction: highUS economy
So what: Policy risk (tax, redistribution, populist backlash) tied to wealth inequality is a slower-moving but real tail risk for equity holders benefiting from this same stability regime.
“strong equity returns obviously widen the wealth gap” ▶ 15:14
The US economy should be described as recession-resistant, not recession-proof — like a water-resistant watch that shouldn't be taken scuba diving — meaning an eventual exogenous, likely unpredictable shock could still tip it into recession even though the classic 'plain vanilla' recession triggers of the past no longer seem to apply.
framework🗣 Josh, host (summary endorsed by Nick)conviction: mediumUS economy
So what: Investors shouldn't treat the 16-year recession-free run as proof recessions are obsolete — tail-risk hedging still has a role even in a structurally more stable regime.
≈ “recession resistant, not recession proof” paraphrase
The Fed's 2% inflation target exists because a recession historically causes roughly a two-percentage-point decline in inflation, so the target is essentially built around the assumption that periodic recessions are the mechanism that resets inflation lower.
framework🗣 Nick, DataTrek Research co-founderconviction: highFederal Reserve
So what: This implies incoming Fed Chair Kevin Warsh faces a structural tension: durably hitting the 2% inflation target may require the very recession the current economic structure has become resistant to producing.
≈ “a recession causes a two-point decline in inflation. That's the 2% number.” paraphrase
In the 2023 regional bank crisis, US policymakers removed the effective $250,000 FDIC deposit insurance limit overnight without a congressional vote, guaranteeing all depositors at failed banks while letting the banks themselves go through resolution — a real-time demonstration that regulators will invent ad hoc solutions to problems previously thought impossible to solve quickly.
anecdote🗣 Nick, DataTrek Research co-founderconviction: highFDICFederal Reserve
So what: The proven willingness of policymakers to act aggressively and unconventionally within a single day is itself a reason markets price a lower probability of any shock cascading into a full-blown crisis — reinforcing the 'policy put' investors are implicitly relying on.
“we had an FDIC limit of $250,000 for a deposit account and then the next day it was unlimited” ▶ 18:35
Incoming Fed Chair Kevin Warsh inherits an economy that is unusually resilient to shocks but also prone to running inflation above the Fed's target because underlying demand stays persistently strong, so his two priorities will be not disrupting the resilient system and finding a way to bring inflation down without deliberately engineering a recession.
prediction🗣 Nick, DataTrek Research co-founderconviction: mediumKevin WarshFederal Reserve
So what: Watch Warsh's early rhetoric and any hawkish tilt closely — both DataTrek analysts flag Fed policy error as the single most likely trigger for ending the current stability regime.
“his job one has to be don't screw it up” ▶ 16:50
Tech's June Extreme and the Case for Mean Reversion 4
As of June 2, 2026, the XLK tech sector ETF had outperformed the S&P 500 by 29 percentage points over the trailing 50 trading days, a greater-than-six-standard-deviation event and the most extreme reading in DataTrek's dataset going back to 2015; since that peak, tech is down 6.3% versus a 0.6% S&P decline, an underperformance of 5.6 points.
data_point🗣 Jessica, DataTrek Research co-founderconviction: highXLKS&P 500
So what: Statistically extreme short-term outperformance readings like this have historically preceded a giveback period; the June 2 extreme functions as a concrete overbought signal for tech-heavy portfolios.
≈ “tech beat the S&P by 29 percentage points over the prior 50 trading days on June 2nd, which was over a six standard deviation event and the most extreme reading in this data set by a wide margin” paraphrase
On a trailing 100-day basis (data back to 1999), tech's 25-point outperformance of the S&P on June 2, 2026 was a greater-than-three-standard-deviation event versus a 27-year average of 1.2 points, occurring only about 0.7% of the time; the closest historical analog, 41 similar instances from December 1999-April 2000 averaging 30.9 points of outperformance, was followed every single time by tech underperforming over the next 100 days by an average of 10.8 points, while a smaller 2023 analog (right after the 2022 bear-market lows, as ChatGPT-driven AI excitement began) saw only a mild sub-1-point pullback.
data_point🗣 Jessica, DataTrek Research co-founderconviction: highS&P 500Nasdaq
So what: The dot-com-era analog argues for a meaningful tech giveback into autumn, while the 2023 analog argues the outcome could be much milder if the underlying secular AI growth story stays intact — the key swing factor DataTrek flags is the Fed, not fundamentals.
What actually ended tech's outperformance in 2000 was not valuation but the Federal Reserve's sequential rate hikes in February, March, and May of that year that pushed policy rates to new cycle highs; with new Fed Chair Warsh striking a notably hawkish tone and the 2022 hiking cycle having already shown how brutally rate increases can reprice high-multiple growth names, Fed policy — not stretched multiples — is the key risk to watch for tech in the second half of 2026, offset for now by a solid labor market.
causal🗣 Jessica, DataTrek Research co-founderconviction: highFederal ReserveKevin WarshNasdaq
So what: Tech investors should be watching Fed rate rhetoric and hikes far more closely than valuation multiples as the actual catalyst that historically breaks tech-led rallies.
≈ “what actually broke the back of tech in 2000 wasn't valuation, it was the Fed” paraphrase
DataTrek remains long-term bullish on US large-cap tech but expects its relative outperformance versus the S&P to pull back toward the 27-year average of 1.2 points by roughly late October 2026 (100 trading days from the June 2 extreme), which could show up as a broad 'tech bear market' in relative terms even without tech falling outright — for example via a multi-month rotation into healthcare and financials — and should be read as a healthy pause in a longer secular story rather than the end of it.
prediction🗣 Jessica, DataTrek Research co-founderconviction: mediumNasdaqS&P 500
So what: Positions sized for continued tech outperformance into year-end face an unfavorable statistical base rate; investors can hedge by rotating some exposure toward lower-multiple sectors like healthcare and financials without abandoning the long-term secular AI thesis.
≈ “believing in further strong near-term gains we think is betting against that 27 years of history” paraphrase
Semiconductor Valuations Have Run Far Ahead of the Rest of the Mag 8 4
Over the trailing 90 days, the Magnificent 8's current- and next-year EPS estimates rose an average of 7% and 4.8%, while the top five semiconductor names by weight saw estimates rise 36.5% and 33% — roughly five to seven times faster — with all five semi names posting double-digit upward revisions, not just one or two names carrying the group.
data_point🗣 Jessica, DataTrek Research co-founderconviction: highMagnificent 7semiconductors
So what: Earnings-estimate revisions, not narrative, have been the single variable separating winners from losers within tech in 2026; investors should track forward revision trends as the leading indicator for which subgroup keeps outperforming.
The valuation and price gap between semis and the rest of the Mag 8 has become extreme: excluding Tesla, the Magnificent 8 trades at 25.9x forward earnings versus 52.5x for the top five semiconductor names (a 27-point premium), and the five semi names are up an average of 168% year-to-date versus just 4.5% for the broader Mag 8.
data_point🗣 Jessica, DataTrek Research co-founderconviction: highMagnificent 7semiconductors
So what: The bar for semis to keep outperforming is now far higher than six months ago, since matching another 30%+ round of 90-day earnings revisions is statistically a tough ask — flags the group as more vulnerable to disappointment.
DataTrek expects the second half of 2026 to see a mostly mechanical (not fundamentals-driven) rotation from the extended, tripled-in-2026 semiconductor names into the lower-multiple, still-solidly-growing rest of the Magnificent 8, as portfolios that became overconcentrated in a handful of winning semi names need to spread exposure into names with lower valuations and decent fundamentals — though it's unclear whether the valuation gap alone triggers this or investors wait for hyperscaler Q2 earnings calls first.
prediction🗣 Jessica, DataTrek Research co-founderconviction: mediumMagnificent 7semiconductors
So what: A concrete, dated catalyst list (hyperscaler Q2 earnings calls) to watch for the semis-to-Mag-8 rotation thesis playing out.
Nick has more conviction in semiconductor capital-equipment stocks than in chip-sellers themselves, because hyperscalers are making a concerted effort to design their own chips in-house, which is fundamentally a capital-equipment demand story — the equipment makers benefit regardless of which company ends up 'winning' the chip-selling race.
contrarian🗣 Nick, DataTrek Research co-founderconviction: mediumsemiconductorshyperscalers
So what: A way to stay long the AI infrastructure buildout with less single-company earnings-revision risk than owning the chip designers/sellers directly.
Dot-Com Echo: Capex Spenders Losing the Market's Confidence 2
Investors are already showing less enthusiasm for 2026's dominant AI capex story: stock prices of big spenders like Meta and Oracle are not reacting positively to continued capex announcements, and in some cases companies are reportedly considering limiting further capex based on the negative stock-price reaction — a dynamic JPMorgan's Michael Cembalest compared to early 1999, when internet service provider stocks began falling (a referendum on confidence in the internet buildout) even as capex beneficiaries like Dell, Cisco, and Intel kept rising, which Cembalest views as the early warning sign that later hit the suppliers themselves.
contrarian🗣 Josh, host, citing Michael Cembalest (JPMorgan)conviction: mediumMetaOracleDellCiscoIntelJPMorgan
So what: A negative stock reaction from AI capex spenders (Meta, Oracle) despite continued high spending is flagged as a leading indicator that supplier/semi stocks could eventually 'run off the cliff' — worth monitoring capex-spender share-price reactions to earnings as an early-warning gauge.
“When the share prices of the spenders are no longer reacting positively, it's only a matter of time before the component suppliers realize that they've run off the cliff” ▶ 29:13
Nick, who traded internet stocks in 1999-2000, pushes back that the ISP-rollover-predicts-supplier-collapse analogy is incomplete: leadership at the very end of that cycle rotated to B2B commerce names (like Commerce One and i2 Technologies, tickers CMRC and ITWO) rather than the enthusiasm simply dying, and the actual cause of the 2000 crash (Nasdaq down 30% in a couple of weeks) was the Fed raising the cost of capital and cutting off access to capital, not demand exhaustion for the underlying technology.
contrarian🗣 Nick, DataTrek Research co-founderconviction: highCommerce OneNasdaqFederal Reserve
So what: If Nick's framing is right, a rollover in AI capex-spender stock prices doesn't necessarily mean the AI investment 'energy' disappears — it could rotate into a new leadership group (analogous to B2B in 2000-2002) — while the real risk to monitor remains Fed-driven cost-of-capital shocks rather than capex fatigue itself.
“it was the B2B companies that took over leadership at the very end of that cycle” ▶ 29:56
NASDAQ's Year-Four Seasonality Base Rates 4
The Nasdaq Composite is in year four of a bull run, having gained 43% in 2023, 29% in 2024, and 20% in 2025 following a 33% decline in 2022, and is currently up 13.1% year-to-date in 2026, running just below the historical average for a fourth consecutive up year.
data_point🗣 Jessica, DataTrek Research co-founderconviction: highNasdaq
So what: Sets the base case that 2026 is a normal, slightly-below-average year-four outcome rather than an anomaly requiring alarm.
Since 1972, the Nasdaq has strung together three consecutive years of 20%+ gains after a down year six times; in four of those six instances (67% of the time) the fourth year was also a gain, averaging 5.1% across all six year-fours (16.8% if the two losing year-fours — 1994 and 2022 — are excluded), and the index has never stopped rallying at exactly four straight up years since the early 1970s.
data_point🗣 Jessica, DataTrek Research co-founderconviction: highNasdaq
So what: History favors a fourth consecutive up year for the Nasdaq in 2026 (67% base rate), arguing against tactically exiting the AI-led rally purely because it has already run three strong years.
“the odds are 67% for a fourth year of gains” ▶ 34:18
Both of the Nasdaq's historical year-four losses within a three-plus-year 20%+ win streak — the 1994 3% pullback and the 2022 33% decline — shared the same root cause: a Federal Reserve rate-hiking shock, not deteriorating fundamentals or valuation exhaustion.
causal🗣 Jessica, DataTrek Research co-founderconviction: highNasdaqFederal Reserve
So what: Reinforces the episode's recurring theme: the single biggest risk to the current tech/AI rally continuing is a Fed-driven rate shock, and that's the variable to monitor rather than growth or multiple metrics.
DataTrek expects the Nasdaq to keep rallying beyond 2026 barring a Fed rate shock, drawing a direct parallel to the 1995-1997 and 2019-2021 three-year 20%+ streaks that both continued (into 1998's 40% gain and 2019-2021's run, respectively) before eventually ending in Fed-driven bear markets (2000, 2022); they frame ordinary pullbacks along the way as buying opportunities and cite how much money investors who sold the 1990s rally too early left on the table as the cautionary lesson.
prediction🗣 Jessica, DataTrek Research co-founderconviction: highNasdaqFederal Reserve
So what: A base-rate argument for staying invested through interim pullbacks in the AI-led rally, with the explicit caveat to exit or hedge only on evidence of a genuine Fed tightening shock, not on valuation or fatigue alone.

Hedgeye Real Conversations 2 episode(s) · 73 insights

Wall Street’s Biggest Week Starts With “The Call @ Hedgeye”69 min36 insights2026-07-15
▶ Watch on YouTube
Hedgeye's daily 'Call @ Hedgeye' morning meeting runs through analyst-by-analyst actionable long/short stock picks generated by combining Hedgeye's quantitative 'signal strength' list with fundamental research ('quantamental'). Headline items include PayPal's reported $53B take-private bid from Stripe and Advent (which Hedgeye's signal flagged two days early), a Nike-short/Adidas-long apparel pair trade, accelerating Las Vegas RevPAR alongside live MGM/Caesars takeout speculation, Eli Lilly's Medicare-subsidized GLP-1 opportunity, and a Conagra short predicated on a dividend cut exposing a 'fake' yield. The macro backdrop is Hedgeye's call for continued disinflation with rising odds of an August inflation re-acceleration ('Quad 3'), layered with AI-related threads on Google's Gemini 3.5 Pro launch, a cybersecurity-software rally, PJM power shortfalls, and growing enterprise adoption of cheap Chinese AI models.
Macro Regime, Rates & Rotation · 5Restaurants & Consumer Staples · 5Las Vegas Gaming & Casino M&A · 2Apparel & Retail · 4Healthcare & Biotech · 6Fintech, Real Estate & Auto M&A · 5Energy & Power · 2AI, Software, Cybersecurity & Policy · 7
Macro Regime, Rates & Rotation 5
Hedgeye has flagged CPI surprising to the downside for months, and Treasury yields made lower highs versus their May/June cycle peaks (10-year around 4.67%, 2-year peaking in June), signaling the market is positioning for broad disinflation.
thesis🗣 Keith (host)conviction: highCPI10-year Treasury2-year Treasury
So what: Apply a disinflation overlay to every stock and sector pick, since fighting the macro trend undermines security selection.
“we've been talking about CPI surprising on the downside for months now” ▶ 1:54
Hedgeye's proprietary inflation model publishes rate-of-change estimates about six weeks ahead of the official CPI release and correctly called May 2026 as inflation's cycle 'high water mark' at 4.25% before the print confirmed it.
framework🗣 Josh Steiner (financials analyst)conviction: highCPIHedgeye
So what: Front-run consensus inflation surprises by tracking rate-of-change rather than waiting for the official print.
“our numbers uh have a very good track record of being uh rate of change” ▶ 38:52
Hedgeye now assigns a 60% conditional probability that inflation accelerates in August while growth decelerates (a 'Quad 3' regime), up three percentage points from its prior estimate, after months of pure disinflation.
prediction🗣 Josh Steiner (financials analyst)conviction: mediumCPIQuad framework
So what: Watch for a regime shift into Quad 3 in August, which would argue for de-risking pure disinflation trades and rotating toward inflation-resilient positioning.
“we're now sitting at a uh 60% probability uh conditional probability that August uh will see inflation accelerate” ▶ 40:44
Hedgeye closed its long South Korean semiconductor/DRAM position into an overnight Kospi rally of more than 6% and moved the sector to neutral, rotating the freed-up capital into cybersecurity software names.
thesis🗣 Keith (host)conviction: highSouth KoreaDRAMsemiconductorscybersecurity
So what: Treat 'neutral' as an active decision to take profits after a big move rather than chase further upside; the rotation destination (cybersecurity) is itself a signal-confirmed trade.
“We were riding DRAM, the ticker DRAM, think semis, uh, fully loaded with South Korean semis on the long side. We got out and flipped into bug into cyber security.” ▶ 58:17
Hedgeye combines a quantitative price/volume 'signal' with fundamental analyst views (what they call 'quantamental') to time entries and exits, arguing that a good stock idea can go unrealized for weeks if bought at the wrong time.
framework🗣 Keith (host)conviction: highHedgeye
So what: Use a systematic timing signal alongside fundamental conviction rather than relying on fundamentals alone to decide when to buy or sell.
“our job is to time markets. Our job is to use AI and fractal signals to do so.” ▶ 7:53
Restaurants & Consumer Staples 5
Yum Brands was removed from Hedgeye's long 'signal strength' list after a brief stint, driven by a food-safety issue (contaminated lettuce, pico de gallo, and guacamole) that originated in Michigan and spread across several states.
data_point🗣 Bennett (restaurant analyst)conviction: mediumYum Brands
So what: A supply-chain food-safety scare can force an abrupt exit from an otherwise-strong same-store-sales story; watch for confirmation the issue is contained before re-entering.
“Bad lettuce, pico, guac” ▶ 3:59
Multinational quick-service restaurant chains such as McDonald's and Domino's face steepening same-store-sales comparisons as US consumers shift spending toward fast-casual, casual dining, and convenience-store food, a structural headwind independent of the broader macro cycle.
causal🗣 Bennett (restaurant analyst)conviction: mediumMcDonald'sDomino'sWingstop
So what: Avoid domestic-heavy legacy QSR names into tougher comps; the channel shift argues for owning fast-casual/convenience exposure instead.
Dutch Bros (the coffee chain, referenced on-air as 'Bros') is a buy at the low end of its trading range, supported by average unit volumes running above underwritten targets and management's plan to roughly double its store count — and EBITDA — by 2029.
data_point🗣 Bennett (restaurant analyst)conviction: highDutch Bros
So what: A unit-growth story trading near the low end of its range with volumes beating underwriting targets offers asymmetric upside if the doubling plan is executed.
“They're gonna double units by 2029 and effectively double EIDA on the way up there.” ▶ 6:37
Conagra (referenced on-air by ticker as 'Kagra'/CA) reported flat organic sales — a 240-basis-point sequential deceleration — with volumes down 1.6% versus a 0.5% increase the prior quarter, gross margins down 130 basis points, roughly $2 billion of impairment charges, and a dividend cut in half that took the yield down from about 10%.
data_point🗣 BC (staples analyst)conviction: highConagra
So what: The dividend cut confirms the short thesis that the reported yield was masking deteriorating earnings power; stay short into further guided declines.
A double-digit dividend yield paired with deteriorating earnings estimates is typically a value trap rather than a value opportunity — investors should recompute the 'real' yield after cutting earnings estimates rather than trusting the headline yield, especially when short interest is also elevated.
framework🗣 Keith (host)conviction: highConagra
So what: Screen for high dividend yield plus high short interest as a red flag before buying, not a buy signal.
“There's usually something wrong if it has high short interest or high dividend yield” ▶ 10:56
Las Vegas Gaming & Casino M&A 2
Las Vegas RevPAR accelerated sharply in recent weeks — up 33% for the week ended June 20, up 24% the following week, only up 3% around the July 4th holiday, and up 27% year-over-year in the most recent week — with the recovery entering its easiest comparison period and a strong event calendar (the McGregor fight, a hockey tournament) running through late July and August.
data_point🗣 gaming/leisure analyst (Jenkins)conviction: highLas VegasMGMCaesars
So what: Accelerating RevPAR into easier comps supports owning Vegas-exposed gaming names heading into earnings, independent of any M&A catalyst.
“RevPAR for the weekended 620 up 33% the week after that up 24.” ▶ 13:14
Caesars and MGM are both live M&A/takeout candidates, with Caesars viewed as closer to a deal than MGM, layered on top of an improving Las Vegas fundamental backdrop; MGM is flagged as the more actionable buy near the low end of its trading range.
thesis🗣 gaming/leisure analyst (Jenkins)conviction: highCaesarsMGM
So what: Own MGM for both the accelerating RevPAR fundamentals and optionality on a Caesars-style takeout premium.
Apparel & Retail 4
Nike (short position) is only in the early stages of what is expected to be a 3-to-5-year turnaround, with excess inventory clogging the retail channel; two small athletic retailers have already gone bankrupt, and further channel destocking could cause more retailer bankruptcies and pain for competitors like On Holdings and Deckers.
prediction🗣 McGoff (retail/apparel analyst)conviction: highNikeOn HoldingsDeckersDick's Sporting GoodsFoot Locker
So what: Stay short Nike and be cautious on retailers exposed to the athletic channel glut (Dick's/Foot Locker) until inventory normalizes, likely years away.
“I literally think it's going to be like a 3 to 5year process.” ▶ 16:35
Adidas — the only long name in Hedgeye's athletic-apparel coverage — is gaining market share via better incremental margins and by shifting product out of the messy wholesale channel into its own direct-to-consumer channel, and is out-executing Nike specifically around World Cup product despite Nike being the official USA team sponsor.
thesis🗣 McGoff (retail/apparel analyst)conviction: highAdidasNikeWorld Cup
So what: Pair long Adidas against short Nike as a relative-value trade on diverging revenue ('pod one') and cash-flow ('pod two') trends.
“Adidas is definitely out executing Nike and and and not by a little.”
Nike holds roughly 98% market share of basketball shoes priced above $180 but has no dominant presence in women's basketball specifically, a gap Adidas is exploiting by signing viral, highly marketable WNBA player Sophie Cunningham as an endorser.
data_point🗣 McGoff (retail/apparel analyst)conviction: mediumNikeAdidasSophie CunninghamCaitlin Clark
So what: Women's basketball footwear is a white-space category where Adidas can build share without directly displacing Nike's core dominant position.
“Nike's got 98% market share for basketball shoes over $180.” ▶ 20:10
Contour Brands (ticker KTB), after divesting its Lee jeans brand, is a buy on signal strength driven by strong demand for its Wrangler denim brand and the under-monetized Helly Hansen outdoor brand (only about $125 million in sales), which it acquired roughly six months ago from a Canadian retailer that had mismanaged it.
thesis🗣 McGoff (retail/apparel analyst)conviction: mediumContour BrandsWranglerHelly HansenCanadian Tire
So what: A steady 'base hit' long combining one growth brand (Helly Hansen) and one annuity brand in a favorable denim cycle (Wrangler), though not expected to be an explosive mover.
Healthcare & Biotech 6
10x Genomics flipped from a short — covered after riding it down from roughly $70 to $7 — to a new long around $11, based on a proprietary dataset showing the genomics tools/consumables cycle is inflecting upward in a pattern comparable to prior secular waves at Affymetrix, Applied Biosystems, and Illumina.
framework🗣 Tom (healthcare/biotech analyst)conviction: high10x GenomicsIlluminaBruker
So what: An AI-enabled scientific-discovery cycle should drive sustained consumable/razor-blade demand across genomics tools names (10x Genomics, Illumina, Bruker), not just a short-term bounce.
Eli Lilly is flagged as the day's top actionable buy because Medicare's new 'Bridge' program will make GLP-1 weight-loss drugs accessible to Medicare beneficiaries for about $50 a month — the first time Medicare has paid for weight-loss drugs — which should durably favor a small number of consumer-facing incumbents like Lilly and Novo Nordisk over follow-on competitors given how far pricing has already fallen.
thesis🗣 Tom (healthcare/biotech analyst)conviction: highEli LillyNovo NordiskMedicare
So what: Buy Lilly on weakness as mass-market, Medicare-subsidized GLP-1 access expands the addressable market while limiting room for new entrants to undercut on price.
“Medicare is rolling out a program called Bridge. It makes $50 a month glip one accessible to Medicare beneficiaries.” ▶ 29:51
Eli Lilly's newest weight-loss compound (described by the analysts, who struggled to name it precisely on air, as a triple-action drug) is said to reduce triglycerides and help treat sleep apnea, adding a second product leg beyond its original GLP-1 franchise.
data_point🗣 Tom (healthcare/biotech analyst)conviction: mediumEli Lilly
So what: A broadening drug pipeline beyond a single GLP-1 compound supports the durability case for staying long Lilly.
U.S. Physical Therapy (USPH) is flagged as a short candidate sitting in the 'splash zone' of the same managed-care cost pressures hitting HCA and Tenet, described as a high-multiple stock with both secular and cyclical/idiosyncratic downside risk.
thesis🗣 Tom (healthcare/biotech analyst)conviction: mediumUSPHHCATenet
So what: Extend the healthcare-services short thesis beyond the obvious hospital names to smaller high-multiple names exposed to the same reimbursement pressure.
“it's a high multiple silly little, you know, stock from my view.” ▶ 35:04
Elevance Health (ELV) reported its benefit margin rising 80 basis points to roughly 90% — described as a 'no-go zone' for managed care — while its core health-benefits operating margin (excluding the Carelon unit) fell 170 basis points; despite raising 2026 EPS guidance to at least $27, the implied second-half EPS of about $7 versus roughly $20 already booked signals a sharp deceleration.
data_point🗣 healthcare/managed-care analystconviction: highElevanceCarelonHCATenetMedicare AdvantageMedicaid
So what: The headline guidance raise masks deteriorating core managed-care economics — rising member risk profiles and states withholding Medicaid rate increases — that will keep flowing through to hospital operators like HCA and Tenet.
“staring at uh, 90% which is, you know, no-go zone in managed care.” ▶ 35:39
Managed-care cost pressure and states declining to raise Medicaid reimbursement reflect a broader post-pandemic 'disinflation/deflation' unwind after years of money-printing-driven healthcare cost inflation, and this unwind will keep pressuring hospital and services companies like HCA and Tenet.
causal🗣 healthcare/managed-care analystconviction: mediumElevanceHCATenet
So what: Treat healthcare services as a multi-quarter drag from the same disinflation dynamic driving the broader macro call, not an isolated sector issue.
Fintech, Real Estate & Auto M&A 5
PayPal is reportedly receiving a buyout offer from a group including Stripe and private-equity firm Advent for $53 billion, roughly a mid-20s percent premium to its prior-day $42 billion market capitalization.
data_point🗣 Josh Steiner (financials analyst)conviction: highPayPalStripeAdvent
So what: A confirmed $53B take-private bid at a roughly 25% premium sets a hard reference price for PayPal shares and signals renewed consolidation interest in payments.
≈ “a combined group which includes Stripe and a private equity firm uh called Advent for $53 billion.” paraphrase
PayPal fell from about $300 to about $40 a share over four years while Hedgeye held it short for most of that decline, then covered the short on June 29 and moved the name to a neutral 'bench' stance after its signal shifted from bearish to neutral just before the buyout news broke.
data_point🗣 Josh Steiner (financials analyst)conviction: highPayPal
So what: Illustrates the value of scaling out of a long-running short as a signal shifts, even without knowing the specific catalyst (an M&A announcement) in advance.
Hedgeye's quantitative signal moved PayPal onto its long 'signal strength' list two to three days before the Stripe/Advent buyout news broke, illustrating that price/volume-based signals can front-run fundamental or M&A news without any inside information.
framework🗣 Keith (host)conviction: highPayPalHedgeye
So what: Treat signal-list additions/removals as an early, legal proxy for positioning ahead of news, and act on the signal rather than waiting for analyst upgrades.
“The signal two days ago said this was coming.” ▶ 44:11
Compass (COMP) is a new highlighted long as a way to play falling mortgage rates tied to the broader CPI-rollover and disinflation call, with the stock's quantitative signal strength confirming the trade after a pullback tied to a recent interest-rate pop.
thesis🗣 Matt Cooper (real estate analyst)conviction: mediumCompass
So what: Own Compass as a levered, signal-confirmed proxy on the mortgage-rate-decline/disinflation thesis playing out elsewhere in the portfolio.
Lucid Group shares fell 50% and were halted after reports the company might go private or file for Chapter 11 (later denied by the company); Hedgeye has held it short since its 2021 SPAC deal, citing losses of over $300,000 per vehicle, perpetually negative gross margins, and roughly $1 billion per quarter in cash burn, with its Saudi PIF majority owner likely to keep funding it via further capital raises ahead of its lower-priced 'Cosmos' model rather than let it fail outright.
thesis🗣 auto analyst (Lucid coverage team)conviction: highLucidSaudi PIF
So what: Broken unit economics can persist for years as long as a deep-pocketed majority owner keeps recapitalizing the company; don't expect a near-term bankruptcy despite negative cash flow, but the equity remains structurally impaired.
“They lose over $300,000 per car uh and have like perpetually negative gross margins, burn one billion a quarter in cash burn.” ▶ 47:45
Energy & Power 2
US refining crack spreads are hitting record levels as diesel supply tightens — Ukrainian strikes are curtailing Russian refinery diesel exports while Russia shifts toward exporting crude instead — and US inventories continue to draw down, setting refiners up for very strong second- and third-quarter earnings, with much of the third-quarter margin already locked in given refining's production timing, and excess free cash flow expected to go toward buybacks.
causal🗣 Fernando (energy analyst)conviction: highUS refinersRussiaUkraine
So what: Own US refiners into a multi-quarter run of record crack spreads and buyback-driven capital returns, largely independent of near-term oil price moves.
“crack spreads are getting to record levels.” ▶ 50:27
PJM (the Pennsylvania-Jersey-Maryland power market) failed for a third consecutive auction to procure enough committed peak generation capacity, and Hedgeye prefers independent power producers Vistra and Talen over regulated utilities, with a further preference for Texas-region power exposure over PJM given faster buildout timelines amid AI-driven demand growth.
thesis🗣 Fernando (energy analyst)conviction: highPJMVistraTalenTexas
So what: Structural power-generation shortfalls from AI data-center demand favor merchant/independent power producers able to build quickly (Texas) over slower-moving regulated PJM utilities.
“PJM, Pennsylvania, Jersey, Maryland uh region had their uh third consecutive auction where they couldn't get enough power for peak sides uh committed.” ▶ 51:00
AI, Software, Cybersecurity & Policy 7
Google is rumored to release its Gemini 3.5 Pro model around July 17, after delaying the original June launch following the departure of AI researcher Noam Shazeer and other talent, and after running into agentic-use-case issues with the pre-trained version; Hedgeye's signal has already flipped Google back to a bullish trend, and a positive release is expected to support a Q2 revenue-growth acceleration narrative into earnings.
prediction🗣 software/internet analystconviction: mediumGoogleGemini
So what: Watch for the Gemini 3.5 Pro release as a binary near-term catalyst for Google shares heading into Q2 earnings; a delay or muted reception would be a warning sign for the cloud/AI growth narrative.
“we're getting a a potentially rumored release of the Gemini 3.5 Pro model on Friday.” ▶ 52:18
A wave of competing AI model releases — including OpenAI's GPT-5.6 and an expected DeepSeek stable release around July 24 — means Google has a lot to prove to maintain its position across the AI frontier, as models across the industry get both cheaper to run and more capable at agentic tasks.
data_point🗣 software/internet analystconviction: mediumGoogleOpenAIDeepSeek
So what: The AI model race is intensifying on both cost and capability fronts; Google's competitive position, not just its own execution, will determine whether it holds its AI leadership premium.
The February 'Mythos' model release initially crushed cybersecurity stocks on fears that AI would reduce future zero-day vulnerabilities, but instead forced enterprises to compress their security-spending timelines, which contributed to a cybersecurity-stock rally and played into IBM's guidance-driven stock plunge — its worst single day since 1987.
contrarian🗣 software/internet analystconviction: highPalo Alto NetworksCrowdStrikeDatadogIBM
So what: A feared AI-driven headwind for cybersecurity turned into a tailwind by accelerating enterprise security budgets; stay long the security-software complex into earnings season for confirmation.
AI agents are expanding the enterprise attack surface, which should keep driving durable demand for agent-identity and agent-observability security tooling, a theme Hedgeye has flagged alongside Datadog as an early beneficiary of AI/agent adoption across software.
thesis🗣 software/internet analystconviction: mediumDatadogAI agents
So what: Position in the 'agent layer' security and observability names as a second-derivative beneficiary of enterprise AI adoption, alongside the AI-infrastructure longs.
“AI agents means more attack services in the long term.” ▶ 56:25
Hedgeye's signal rotated CrowdStrike into the top third of its 93-name signal-strength list two days before it became one of the best-performing stocks in its coverage, at the same time the signal flagged the setup for IBM's guidance-driven plunge — its worst day since 1987 — illustrating the signal catching both a breakout and a blowup concurrently.
data_point🗣 Keith (host)conviction: highCrowdStrikeIBM
So what: Treat simultaneous signal moves across correlated names (e.g., security software up, a legacy IT vendor down) as a higher-confidence read on a sector rotation already underway.
“the signal had us move crowd strike CRWD, which is the best of all those stocks yesterday, I think.” ▶ 57:16
Enterprise adoption is increasingly migrating toward Chinese open-source AI models — 10 to 60 times cheaper than US equivalents — for workloads like coding agents, with DoorDash and Airbnb cited as companies already using them instead of OpenAI or Anthropic; Hedgeye has flagged this model-commoditization risk since January 2026, and it is now attracting mainstream media and political attention in both the US and China.
contrarian🗣 Felix (semiconductor analyst)conviction: highDoorDashAirbnbOpenAIAnthropicChina
So what: Model commoditization from cheap Chinese open-source alternatives is arriving faster than expected, pressuring the pricing power of premium US model providers and warranting a watch on which software/infrastructure names are most exposed.
“these Chinese models are 10 to 60 times cheaper than the US equivalents.” ▶ 59:09
New York's governor signed an executive order pausing (not banning) new data-center approvals, part of an emerging partisan split on AI infrastructure in which Republicans are generally more supportive of buildout and Democrats are increasingly divided due to reliance on big-tech campaign contributions; a likely compromise centers on regulating electricity and water use, with a more coordinated federal legislative effort not expected until after the midterms.
prediction🗣 Paul Glencher (policy analyst)conviction: mediumNew YorkAI data centers
So what: Growing state-level opposition to data-center buildout (starting with a major state like New York) is an early-stage but rising regulatory risk to AI infrastructure capex and the broader AI trade; watch for spread to other states ahead of the midterms.
“the governor signed an executive order. It's not a ban. It's just pausing them.” ▶ 1:00:28
The AI Trade Faces Its First Real Test | Protect the Pile Episode 2058 min37 insights2026-07-18
▶ Watch on YouTube
Hedgeye's Protect the Pile panel reviews an early Q2 earnings season in which small caps (Russell 2000 +21% YTD) are trouncing the Magnificent Seven (+1% YTD), flags a building healthcare 'consumption cliff' hitting hospitals and device makers, and dissects repeated 'great earnings, falling stock' reactions in AI/semis (ASML, TSMC, Samsung) alongside a Korea leverage/margin-call wave as early cracks in the AI trade. They close by debating AI business-model durability (Meta, OpenAI, Nvidia, enterprise cloud moats) through the lens of Netflix's historical capex-to-free-cash-flow inflection.
Small-Cap Rotation & Liquidity Backdrop · 4Earnings Season: Healthcare Consumption Cliff · 8AI Capex Cycle: Good Numbers, Falling Stocks · 6Korea Leverage and Margin-Call Mechanics · 4AI Business Models and Competitive Structure · 12Netflix as an AI Capex-to-Cash-Flow Analog · 3
Small-Cap Rotation & Liquidity Backdrop 4
The S&P 500 is up 10% year-to-date while the Magnificent Seven mega-cap tech basket is up only 1%, and the Russell 2000 small-cap index is up almost 21% YTD, showing market leadership has rotated sharply from mega-cap tech toward small caps.
data_point🗣 RPK (host)conviction: highS&P 500Magnificent SevenRussell 2000
So what: Confirms a broadening/rotation trade away from mega-cap AI leaders into small caps; watch whether Russell 2000 leadership persists.
≈ “the S&P is up right now still up 10% year-to date... the MAG 7's up only 1% year to date” paraphrase
The host's market-cycle heuristic holds that small-cap outperformance episodes like the current one tend to last roughly 6 to 18 months and recur about once every 15 years, suggesting today's small-cap rally could be a shorter-lived rotation rather than a durable multi-year regime change.
framework🗣 RPK (host)conviction: mediumRussell 2000small caps
So what: Sets a rough time horizon (roughly a year or so) for fading or riding the small-cap trade.
“it happens uh for it happens for about 6 months to uh 18 months every 15 years” ▶ 2:05
Global dollar liquidity's one-month annualized rate of change turned negative over the past three to four weeks amid renewed dollar strength, and the three-month rate has slowed to roughly 2% annualized, just above the 'yellow flag' threshold of nominal GDP growth below which asset prices historically become more at risk.
framework🗣 RPK (host)conviction: mediumUS dollarglobal liquidity
So what: A liquidity readout to monitor: if the 3-month rate falls below nominal GDP growth, risk assets historically become more vulnerable.
“I would say this is just sort of a yellow flag, not a not a red flag” ▶ 4:17
South Korea's KOSPI is the best-performing major equity market globally in 2026, up 58% year-to-date even after falling 23% over the past month, while Indonesia is the worst performer, down 33% YTD.
data_point🗣 RPK (host)conviction: highKOSPISouth KoreaIndonesia
So what: Highlights extreme dispersion and volatility in EM equities tied to the semiconductor/AI trade (Korea) versus laggards.
Earnings Season: Healthcare Consumption Cliff 8
With only about 10% of S&P 500 companies having reported, blended sales growth is tracking around 16% and earnings growth around 52%, though the panel believes the earnings figure is skewed by a few outlier names and likely overstates the true trend.
data_point🗣 RPK (host)conviction: mediumS&P 500
So what: Early-season growth rates look strong but should be discounted until more constituents report.
JPMorgan and Goldman Sachs posted very strong results driven by equity trading and investment banking, but because those businesses are cyclical, year-over-year comparisons become much tougher a year from now.
data_point🗣 Sam Ramen (PM)conviction: mediumJPMorganGoldman Sachs
So what: Bank earnings momentum is real now but sets up difficult comps in trading/IB revenue next year.
A 'consumption cliff' is emerging in U.S. healthcare utilization as fewer people re-up insurance coverage given higher pricing and costs, evidenced by Intuitive Surgical guiding down procedure growth, HCA missing and guiding down, and Elevance missing estimates, even as United Health beat and J&J was in line.
causal🗣 Sam Ramen (PM)conviction: highIntuitive SurgicalHCA HealthcareElevance HealthUnitedHealthJohnson & JohnsonTom Tobin
So what: Signals a potentially secular, not just seasonal, downturn in healthcare services/device utilization worth tracking through other hospital and device names with high correlation to admissions.
“we have a consumption cliff like happening right now in healthcare as people not as many people reupt on insurance this year given the pricing and the and the cost” ▶ 7:29
Because hospitals and other high-fixed-cost healthcare service businesses have very high incremental margins, even a small (roughly 1%) sequential slowdown in procedure volume like Intuitive Surgical's can disproportionately hurt earnings and could compound through the rest of the year rather than reverse as a one-quarter blip.
causal🗣 Sam Ramen (PM)conviction: highIntuitive Surgicalhospitals
So what: Investors in high-fixed-cost healthcare names should watch for cumulative deceleration rather than dismissing early misses as noise.
Within healthcare, biotech, large/mid pharma, and life-science tools have been performing well while services and devices (outside UnitedHealth) have been weak for over a year, splitting the sector between R&D-driven and utilization-driven subsegments.
data_point🗣 Sam Ramen (PM)conviction: mediumbiotechpharmalife science tools
So what: Suggests rotating within healthcare toward R&D/tools names and away from services/utilization-exposed names.
AI could accelerate pharma R&D productivity by mining decades of historical trial data to find subpopulations where previously failed drugs actually worked, potentially reviving 'orphaned' compounds at low incremental cost; the panel cites Eli Lilly as having worked on roughly 3 million compounds but brought only about 10,000 to market.
thesis🗣 RPK (host)conviction: mediumEli LillyAI
So what: A multi-year thesis for pharma/biotech productivity gains from applying AI to legacy R&D datasets — watch data/tools vendors that could benefit.
“somewhere it's like 10,000 drugs that they've released that were in service, but they had 3 million that they had worked on” ▶ 14:24
The host's rule of thumb is that after a sector gets massively overbuilt, it takes roughly five years from the peak before it becomes worth revisiting for a recovery trade, citing housing (buyable around 2010-2011) and internet-bubble-era data centers (buyable around 2005); he now applies this framework to the COVID-era life-science tools ordering boom that peaked more than five years ago.
framework🗣 RPK (host)conviction: mediumhousingdata centerslife science tools10x Genomics
So what: A timing framework for contrarian re-entry into previously overbuilt sectors — life-science tools may now be in the recovery window.
10x Genomics has posted roughly flat revenue growth for the past couple of years, down sharply from being a growth stock once valued near 70-100x, but easy year-over-year comparisons could support multiple expansion if sales reaccelerate.
data_point🗣 RPK (host)conviction: medium10x Genomics
So what: A potential contrarian re-rating candidate within life-science tools if the post-COVID overbuild hangover is ending.
AI Capex Cycle: Good Numbers, Falling Stocks 6
ASML, TSMC, Samsung Electronics, and Broadcom (Avago) all recently reported very strong results — TSMC and ASML posting roughly 70% year-over-year earnings growth — yet their stocks fell in the days after, which the panel calls a historically bad sign because it implies growth expectations are already priced in and the coming comparisons will be difficult.
