contrarian🗣 Sam Ramen (PM)conviction: highASMLTSMCSamsung ElectronicsBroadcom
So what: 'Good news, stock down' on four consecutive major semis prints is a classic late-cycle warning sign for the AI capex trade.
“it's never a good sign when you have great numbers and the stocks down” ▶ 18:39
The current momentum/systematic unwind in AI-related semiconductor and momentum baskets is, in speed and depth based on prime-brokerage positioning data, approaching the scale of the post-dot-com bubble selloff, despite occurring within just three to four weeks.
data_point🗣 Sam Ramen (PM)conviction: mediumsemiconductorsdot-com bubble
So what: A quantitative flag that current AI-trade deleveraging is unusually severe and fast, warranting close monitoring of positioning data.
“this momentum selloff now is approaching the size and depths of post the do bubble” ▶ 20:03
Google's recent equity raise and Amazon's bond offering a week or two earlier are read as opportunistic financing moves — taking capital while it's available — implying management may expect capital-markets access to get tougher or costlier later.
causal🗣 Sam Ramen (PM)conviction: mediumGoogleAmazon
So what: Hyperscaler capital-raising behavior can be read as a signal about management's own view of future funding conditions for the AI buildout.
“if they're going to give me money now, I'm going to take it now cuz later” ▶ 22:02
Announced 2026 hyperscaler capex likely won't all be spent in 2026 — some will slip into 2027 due to contractor backlogs and delays in local/federal permitting — which the panel views as a near-term positive for capex-linked 'picks and shovels' names since spending timelines are extending rather than being cut.
prediction🗣 Sam Ramen (PM)conviction: mediumhyperscaler capex
So what: Delays in capex execution push revenue recognition for suppliers into 2027, potentially supporting picks-and-shovels earnings longer than expected.
“it's not going to be spent in 26. Some of it's going to flow into 27” ▶ 22:29
Picks-and-shovels names tied to the AI buildout — semiconductors, memory, and industrials like Caterpillar (up roughly 300%) — have dramatically outperformed the Magnificent Seven hyperscalers (up only 1% YTD) on the market's assumption that hyperscalers will spend without discipline, a premise the speaker says he doesn't actually believe will hold.
contrarian🗣 RPK (host)conviction: mediumCaterpillarMagnificent Sevensemiconductors
So what: If hyperscalers eventually show capex discipline, picks-and-shovels stocks priced for unconstrained spending could be most at risk of a re-rating lower.
“these companies have thrown the the steering wheel out of the window and they will spend no matter what” ▶ 23:17
Hyperscaler capex guidance is now largely 'in the price,' so in-line spending announcements no longer carry positive surprise value for stocks, even though cloud growth at Amazon, Microsoft, and Google should still be strong this quarter due to compute capacity constraints among AI labs like Anthropic and OpenAI.
thesis🗣 RPK (host)conviction: mediumAmazonMicrosoftGoogleAnthropicOpenAI
So what: Expect strong cloud revenue prints this quarter, but without capex-guidance upside surprises, stock reactions may be muted or negative even on good numbers, echoing the semis pattern.
“the spend part of it is now in the price” ▶ 23:45
Korea Leverage and Margin-Call Mechanics 4
SK Hynix has completed a U.S. ADR listing and Samsung is reportedly considering one, coinciding with a sharp correction in Korean equities and heavy losses in semiconductor-related momentum trades.
data_point🗣 Brooks (PM)conviction: mediumSK HynixSamsung ElectronicsKorea
So what: Cross-listing activity plus deleveraging in Korea's chip-heavy market is a datapoint on how global capital has chased, and is now unwinding from, the AI/semis trade.
A large share of the recent leverage unwind in Korea's market came not from triple-leveraged ETFs but from ordinary single-stock margin lending, which the panelist argues is actually more dangerous and less contained than leveraged ETFs because margin calls can cascade into forced selling of unrelated holdings, whereas leveraged-ETF losses are capped at 100% of principal.
contrarian🗣 Brooks (PM)conviction: highKorealeveraged ETFsmargin lending
So what: Investors worried about reflexive blowups in hot markets should focus more on aggregate single-stock margin debt than on headline-grabbing leveraged ETF products.
“a lot of this leverage that seems to have been put in place in Korea was like good oldfashioned just single stock leverage” ▶ 25:28
Data circulating on social media reportedly indicates that roughly 1 in 30 Korean adults have been margin called amid the recent Korean market correction.
data_point🗣 Brooks (PM)conviction: lowKorea
So what: An extreme, if unverified, statistic illustrating how widespread retail margin leverage has become in Korea's equity market and the scale of forced-deleveraging risk.
“it's like one in 30 Korean adults have been margin called” ▶ 28:04
The panel suggests systemic risk from leveraged ETFs in Korea could be mitigated by capping issuance at 2x leverage or resetting exposure weekly/monthly instead of daily, allowing investors to take on risk without creating a cascading, runaway unwind.
framework🗣 Brooks (PM)conviction: mediumleveraged ETFsKorea
So what: A potential regulatory/product-design fix worth watching if regulators respond to the recent margin-call wave.
AI Business Models and Competitive Structure 12
OpenAI cutting the price of model access while simultaneously improving its ranking versus competitors could give it near-term commercial traction, but the panel views this pricing-versus-performance dynamic as a worrying precursor for the long-term sustainability of its business model.
contrarian🗣 Sam Ramen (PM)conviction: mediumOpenAI
So what: Signals that even the leading AI lab may be competing on price despite performance gains — a warning sign for long-term margin durability across the sector.
OpenAI's discussed plans to monetize through advertising integration on Instagram and by charging advertisers a premium to use its generative tools are viewed as smarter monetization paths than flooding social platforms with AI-generated content, which risks degrading the platform.
thesis🗣 Sam Ramen (PM)conviction: mediumOpenAIInstagramMeta
So what: Advertiser-tool monetization is viewed as a more durable, less platform-damaging AI revenue model worth tracking as it scales.
By leasing out excess data-center capacity to third parties instead of using it only internally, Meta converts previously zero-return internal capex into an immediate positive return, even if that return is much lower margin than its core advertising business — a structural critique of Meta's capital-allocation model.
causal🗣 RPK (host)conviction: mediumMeta
So what: Watch Meta's compute-leasing revenue disclosures as an indicator of capex ROI improving even while core-business incremental returns stay depressed.
Mark Zuckerberg and Meta AI chief Alexandr Wang appeared unusually synchronized in promoting Meta's AI narrative (e.g., around the 'Muse' model launch), which the speaker interprets as a coordinated effort to pump the stock ahead of an expected capital raise and continued aggressive spending, driven by Zuckerberg's belief that AI is existential to Meta's core business.
contrarian🗣 RPK (host)conviction: mediumMetaMark ZuckerbergAlexandr Wang
So what: A flag to watch for a near-term Meta capital raise and to treat aggressive social-media promotion of AI wins with skepticism.
Meta Compute and Grok can act as swing/spot suppliers of AI compute similar to Nebius and CoreWeave, but are unlikely to become real enterprise threats to AWS, Azure, and GCP in the near-to-medium term because enterprise customers require an application layer (Salesforce-like tooling, Kubernetes-based data management) that the big three have built over years, not just raw compute.
thesis🗣 RPK (host)conviction: highMetaGrokAWSAzureGoogle CloudNebiusCoreWeave
So what: Frames AWS/Azure/GCP's enterprise moat as durable against new AI-compute entrants, favoring incumbents over compute-only challengers for enterprise AI workloads.
The panel frames AI compute through Claude Shannon's information theory ('negentropy') as converting energy into structured information, explaining why AI infrastructure is inherently energy- and capital-intensive, and argues that as three to five large model providers all compete directly, the market shifts from an oligopoly/monopoly structure toward a competitive, commoditized, price-taking one.
framework🗣 RPK (host)conviction: mediumClaude ShannonAI models
So what: Supports a thesis that pure model-layer economics will commoditize, pushing durable value creation to the application layer rather than to underlying LLM providers.
The AI market is bifurcating into large frontier 'whale' model providers fighting each other for AGI-scale intelligence and smaller specialized 'minnow' models capturing narrower use cases, ending the prior era of 'co-opetition' among Big Tech (Amazon in retail/AWS, Microsoft in office/Azure, Google in search) that had allowed each to earn 30%+ ROIC in its own lane without directly competing.
framework🗣 RPK (host)conviction: mediumAWSMicrosoftGoogleAI models
So what: The end of non-overlapping co-opetition and rise of hyper-competition across AI could compress returns for large tech even as they keep gaining share versus smaller players — discount prior high-ROIC assumptions for these franchises.
“now just all the gloves are off, everyone's out, everyone's stabbing everybody in front” ▶ 41:21
Hyperscalers are now spending roughly 20% of revenue on capex, up sharply from a small fraction historically, which is mechanically driving down their return on invested capital even as bulls hope for a large eventual payoff.
data_point🗣 RPK (host)conviction: mediumhyperscalers
So what: Rising capex intensity is a quantifiable drag on ROIC that should be weighed against optimistic long-term AI monetization assumptions.
“they're spending 20% of revenue on capex like up from a fraction of that” ▶ 41:43
Unlike the hyperscalers, OpenAI and Anthropic are distinct businesses that are free-cash-flow negative and dependent on external bond and equity financing rather than self-funding cash generators, meaning investors should separate their risk profile from that of Amazon, Microsoft, and Google.
contrarian🗣 Sam Ramen (PM)conviction: highOpenAIAnthropichyperscalers
So what: Apply different risk/valuation frameworks to pure-play AI labs (financing-dependent, cash-burning) versus diversified hyperscalers (self-funded, profitable core businesses).
Nvidia owns Nemotron, an open-source model, and could give it away for free while leveraging its equity stakes in neoclouds like CoreWeave and Nebius to host it, potentially generating an ARR stream and repricing Nvidia from a pure chip company to a 'quasi chip software company.'
prediction🗣 RPK (host)conviction: lowNvidiaNemotronCoreWeaveNebius
So what: A speculative but potentially high-impact re-rating catalyst for Nvidia if it pivots part of its business toward software/ARR rather than pure hardware sales.
“What do you think is going to happen to the multiple of Nvidia?” ▶ 46:35
Drawing on AWS's and Azure's history of being margin-dilutive and criticized by analysts before maturing into standalone segments with 25-30%+ operating margins, the panel expects hyperscaler AI data-center/cloud businesses to follow a similar path toward roughly 30% operating margins because model providers have no choice but to pay for hosted compute.
framework🗣 Sam Ramen (PM)conviction: highAWSMicrosoft AzureGoogle Cloud
So what: Supports a bullish long-term margin thesis for hyperscaler AI infrastructure segments once they mature and get broken out, similar to the cloud-computing precedent.
Most everyday AI usage doesn't require frontier 'Lamborghini' models like ChatGPT or Claude — only bleeding-edge research and heavy token consumption truly need them — implying an enterprise replicating a large workforce needs only a small number of frontier-level agents alongside many cheaper niche, orchestrated models.
thesis🗣 Sam Ramen (PM)conviction: mediumChatGPTClaude
So what: Long-term compute demand may skew toward cheaper specialized models plus orchestration/context-management layers rather than universal frontier-model usage, favoring companies that solve orchestration over raw model scale.
“you don't need to tap the the Lamborghini uh to to to find out like, hey, what's uh what's the weather going to be like in Spain” ▶ 47:57
Netflix as an AI Capex-to-Cash-Flow Analog 3
Netflix's free cash flow margin has converged with its operating margin at roughly 27-28%, a sharp inflection from its early years of negative free cash flow funded through corporate bond issuance, which occurred once subscriber revenue growth began outpacing decelerating content-spend growth.
data_point🗣 Sam Ramen (PM)conviction: highNetflix
So what: Serves as a template for how AI infrastructure spenders could eventually inflect to strong free cash flow once capex growth decelerates relative to revenue — a pattern to watch in hyperscaler and AI-lab financials.
Netflix's strategic shift from licensing studio content to producing its own shows (e.g., Squid Game) drove an unprecedented ROI because near-zero incremental production cost could be spread across 60-100+ million subscribers paying roughly $15/month, a margin structure legacy studios never achieved.
causal🗣 RPK (host)conviction: highNetflix
So what: Illustrates how owning proprietary content/data at near-zero marginal cost creates outsized margin leverage — an analog for AI players that own proprietary datasets or compute versus those renting or licensing it.
“Your per cost per subscriber was in the cents for Squid Games” ▶ 55:18
Netflix's bid for Warner Bros/Time Warner content is viewed as a 'tell' that Netflix's organic growth story has matured, with engagement flattening and rising competition from short-form video, YouTube, and social media for viewers' limited time — an analog the panel extends to smaller, lighter AI models competing with frontier models for usage.
contrarian🗣 Sam Ramen (PM)conviction: mediumNetflixWarner BrosYouTube
So what: A cautionary analog: even category-defining tech/media leaders eventually need M&A to sustain growth once organic engagement plateaus — a pattern worth watching for AI incumbents.
≈ “the Time Warner bid was the was the tell of all tales that oh the the Netflix that I knew is no longer the Netflix that is today” paraphrase

Top Traders Unplugged 2 episode(s) · 69 insights

The Missing Barrels That Could Reprice Oil | Global Macro | Ep.10574 min32 insights2026-07-15
▶ Watch on YouTube
Natural-resource investor Adam Rosenwik argues the Strait of Hormuz closure caused a real ~1-1.5 billion barrel physical oil shock (unlike 2022's Russia scare), that the market is only halfway through a lagged inventory collapse that could reach a breaking point by late summer, and that the single biggest mispriced opportunity today is upstream natural gas to power AI data centers -- while his fund has rotated out of gold into energy.
The Capex Cycle Framework Behind Commodity Supercycles · 4Hormuz Closure: A Real Shock vs 2022's Phantom Disruption · 4The Missing Barrels: Inventory Math and the Summer Reckoning · 4Strategic Reserves Running Dry: US, OECD, and China · 3China's Refined-Product Export Ban as a Taiwan War Game · 4Positioning, the Ceasefire Myth, and Persistent Bearish Selling · 4Gold: Why Adam Rotated Out Near the Top and What Would Bring Him Back · 4Chinaification of US Strategic Capital and the Real AI Energy Bottleneck · 5
The Capex Cycle Framework Behind Commodity Supercycles 4
Commodity supercycles are driven by a multi-year capex cycle: prices rise while a deficit persists, eventually overshoot past the equilibrium level of new investment, then a wave of new supply comes online and prices collapse, wiping out investors until depletion eventually restores balance.
framework🗣 Adam Rosenwikconviction: highcapex cyclecommodity supercycle
So what: Investors can use this capex-cycle framework to judge where in the commodity cycle a given resource sits, rather than reacting to short-term price moves.
“I think in our industry, we have a sea star.” ▶ 5:43
Adam Rosenwik argues the current commodity upcycle actually began in 2020 at the COVID-era lows, not in 2022 with the Russia-Ukraine war or in 2025 with the Iran/Strait of Hormuz conflict; geopolitical shocks are accelerants that draw attention to a trend but are not the underlying cause of a genuine supercycle.
thesis🗣 Adam Rosenwikconviction: highRussiaUkraineIranStrait of Hormuz
So what: If true, investors should position for a multi-year structural commodity uptrend rather than trading each geopolitical headline as a one-off spike.
“I don't think that the straight of Hormuz is a catalyst for a super cycle.” ▶ 7:54
Outside of copper and lithium, meaningful new investment capital still has not flowed into oil, natural gas, or uranium projects despite years of price strength, which Adam Rosenwik sees as evidence the commodity upcycle is still in its early innings.
thesis🗣 Adam Rosenwikconviction: highoilnatural gasuraniumcopperlithium
So what: Sectors starved of new capex (oil, gas, uranium) may have more room to run than commodities like copper where investment has already resumed.
“this has a long long way to go and I think it's just getting started.” ▶ 9:29
Persistent underinvestment in commodity production has been extended beyond a typical cycle by two structural forces: years of ESG-driven capital avoidance of resource sectors, and a 'carry trade' that has pulled money into large-cap, high-multiple, low-volatility growth assets instead of commodities.
causal🗣 Adam Rosenwikconviction: mediumESGcarry trade
So what: This capital-allocation feedback loop implies commodity producers could stay capital-starved (and prices structurally higher) until the carry trade unwinds.
Hormuz Closure: A Real Shock vs 2022's Phantom Disruption 4
In the 2022 Russia-Ukraine conflict, Russian crude and refined product exports were never actually disrupted -- Russia rerouted sales to India, China and other buyers via shadow markets -- so the oil price spike to $120 was driven by fear rather than a real supply loss, unlike the 2025 Strait of Hormuz closure which caused an actual physical shut-in of production.
causal🗣 Adam Rosenwikconviction: highRussiaIndiaChinaStrait of Hormuz
So what: Distinguishing feared disruptions from real ones explains why 2022's oil spike reversed quickly while the 2025 shock could prove more durable.
Of the roughly 20 million barrels per day that normally transits the Strait of Hormuz, only about 5 million barrels per day could be rerouted through East-West bypass pipelines in Saudi Arabia, UAE and Iraq, leaving roughly 15 million barrels per day of upstream production shut in for about 100 days.
data_point🗣 Adam Rosenwikconviction: highStrait of HormuzSaudi ArabiaUAEIraq
So what: A 15 million b/d, 100-day shut-in is a genuinely massive physical supply loss that dwarfs the 2022 Russia scare, implying the oil market reaction should eventually be much larger.
“Of that 5 million or so is now getting out through East West bypass pipelines in Saudi Arabia, UAE and Iraq.” ▶ 12:44
The Hormuz closure withheld between roughly 1 billion and 1.5 billion barrels of oil from the global market over the ~100 days it lasted.
data_point🗣 Adam Rosenwikconviction: highStrait of Hormuzoil inventories
So what: This is the scale of supply loss that has to be absorbed by draining inventories, and it anchors the later math on how much further inventories must fall.
“you had between a billion and a billion and a half barrels impacted and that is just absolutely massive”
There is a roughly 60-90 day physical lag between an upstream oil supply shock and its appearance in inventory data, because oil must move through pumping, shipping, storage, refining and distribution before it shows up in reported stocks -- meaning today's inventory data reflects conditions from roughly two months earlier.
framework🗣 Adam Rosenwikconviction: highoil inventories
So what: Traders dismissing the bullish fundamental story because inventories 'haven't collapsed yet' may be using stale data that lags the real supply shock by 6-8 weeks.
The Missing Barrels: Inventory Math and the Summer Reckoning 4
US commercial plus strategic petroleum inventories were already down about 240 million barrels from their peak, and if the market is only halfway through working off the Hormuz-closure supply shock, the total US drawdown could reach roughly 500 million barrels, implying a global inventory draw near 1 billion barrels.
data_point🗣 Adam Rosenwikconviction: highUS inventoriesStrait of Hormuz
So what: A projected 500-million-barrel US drawdown gives investors a concrete inventory target to track for confirming or invalidating the bullish oil thesis.
“in the US right now, I think inventories from their peak are down about 240 million barrels or so” ▶ 16:41
Of the roughly 7-8 billion barrels of headline global oil inventory, only about 1 billion barrels is genuinely usable working capital -- after excluding roughly 2 billion barrels permanently trapped as pipeline line-fill, roughly 1.7 billion barrels always in transit on tankers, and the unreachable bottom 10% of every storage tank -- so a JPMorgan analysis pegs system stress starting around a 700-million-barrel draw and catastrophic failure around 1.2 billion barrels.
data_point🗣 Adam Rosenwikconviction: highoil inventoriesJPMorgan
So what: Headline inventory figures vastly overstate the oil market's real spare buffer; investors should track the roughly 1-billion-barrel usable pool, not the 8-billion-barrel gross figure, to gauge how close the system is to breaking.
“somewhere between 700 million and 1.2 billion is where you start to 700 million you start to develop pressure in the system and 1.2 you have a catastrophic failure of the system” ▶ 21:46
Because it took about 6-8 weeks for inventories to start visibly declining after the Strait of Hormuz closed, Adam Rosenwik expects a symmetric ~8-week lag before inventories start rebuilding even after the strait reopens, meaning the market is only about halfway through its inventory draw and could face a severe shortage around the end of summer.
prediction🗣 Adam Rosenwikconviction: highStrait of Hormuzoil inventories
So what: Investors should expect inventories to keep falling for weeks even after the strait reopens, which could catch bearish positioning offside into late summer.
“I don't think we have enough in the tanks to get us there. So, I think we're going to have a [music] huge, huge problem potentially at the end of the summer” ▶ 0:22
Adam Rosenwik expects clarity on the true market impact of the Hormuz closure within the next three to four months, and states that if no impact has materialized by then, the bullish oil thesis should be reassessed.
prediction🗣 Adam Rosenwikconviction: mediumStrait of Hormuzoil
So what: This gives investors a concrete falsifiability window (3-4 months) for testing the bullish oil thesis against actual inventory and price data.
“within 3 or 4 months, it will be. And I think we're going to have a very very different view of of of energy markets after that” ▶ 1:12:48
Strategic Reserves Running Dry: US, OECD, and China 3
The US Strategic Petroleum Reserve stands at about 319 million barrels, down roughly 100 million barrels over the last few months and at its lowest level since 1982, with Adam Rosenwik estimating only about 50-60 million more barrels can be drawn before the reserve's salt-dome storage hits operationally problematic pressure limits.
data_point🗣 Adam Rosenwikconviction: mediumUS Strategic Petroleum Reserve
So what: The US SPR is close to running out of usable spare capacity as a price-suppression tool, removing a lever policymakers have used to offset supply shocks.
“right now you're down at 319 million barrels” ▶ 25:14
OECD countries agreed to release about 400 million barrels of strategic reserves in total but are only about halfway through delivery and running behind schedule, and Adam Rosenwik doubts there is another large tranche of releases coming, partly because the largely US-funded OECD had an incentive to announce a maximal headline figure early to calm the market regardless of whether it would fully deliver.
contrarian🗣 Adam Rosenwikconviction: mediumOECDUS Strategic Petroleum Reserve
So what: If OECD SPR releases are capped near 400 million barrels and running behind, that removes an expected source of supply relief investors may be pricing in.
“there's a lot of reason to believe that 400 is on the upper end of what they can possibly do” ▶ 24:32
China is the only major non-OECD country with a materially sized strategic petroleum reserve, opaquely estimated at roughly 1 billion barrels, built specifically as insurance against a scenario where a US-led move to shut the Straits of Malacca and Hormuz during a Taiwan conflict leaves China energy-vulnerable.
data_point🗣 Adam Rosenwikconviction: mediumChinaTaiwanStrait of HormuzStrait of Malacca
So what: China's roughly 1-billion-barrel SPR gives it meaningfully more strategic buffer than markets may appreciate, complicating forecasts of how a prolonged energy shock would play out.
“they've built up kind of a billion barrels or so, a fairly large SPR uh figure” ▶ 28:06
China's Refined-Product Export Ban as a Taiwan War Game 4
The day the Strait of Hormuz closed, China banned refined-product exports (a policy still in effect) which paradoxically didn't reduce its crude import needs proportionally -- instead it pushed idled-refinery crude back into the spot market -- a move Adam Rosenwik interprets as a deliberate rehearsal of how China would manage energy security in a Taiwan conflict scenario, not an economically optimal decision.
contrarian🗣 Adam Rosenwikconviction: mediumChinaTaiwanrefined products
So what: If China's export ban is a geopolitical signal rather than a market response, investors should expect it to persist independent of price incentives, keeping global refined-product markets tight.
“I personally think if you look at what China has done since the start of the Hormuz crisis, it's a little bit of a war games or a trial run of what they would do in the event of going into Taiwan.” ▶ 29:01
Reported global oil demand-destruction figures of 5-6 million barrels per day (comparable in scale to COVID-19) are, in Adam Rosenwik's view, largely a modeling artifact of China's refinery shutdowns feeding into refinery-run-based demand estimates, not a genuine collapse in end-user consumption -- actual indicators like packed airports and rising vehicle miles show no COVID-like weakness.
contrarian🗣 Adam Rosenwikconviction: highChinaoil demand
So what: If demand is not actually falling, the market is under-drawing inventories relative to true consumption, making the bullish supply-shortage case stronger than headline demand data suggests.
“The airports are packed. I can't get a seat on half the flights” ▶ 35:54
Adam Rosenwik argues energy efficiency matters as much as diversification: Germany's shift away from nuclear toward renewables has coincided with a loss of manufacturing competitiveness, while the US's more hydrocarbon- and market-driven energy mix has delivered both the largest CO2 reduction and the biggest efficiency gains -- implying China's renewable and nuclear buildout will only reduce oil demand 'on the margin' for the next 15-20 years.
framework🗣 Adam Rosenwikconviction: mediumGermanyChinanuclear powerrenewables
So what: Investors shouldn't assume China's alternative-energy buildout will materially cut its oil demand in the medium term; hydrocarbons remain structurally necessary for efficiency.
“Germany has decided to go down a renewable path and get rid of its nukes and go towards renewables” ▶ 41:32
China is not capable of fundamentally restructuring its energy consumption within the next 12-18 months; Adam Rosenwik believes genuine structural change in China's oil demand would take five years or more, meaning its current refined-product export halt is a short/medium-term stockpile-management tactic, not evidence of a long-term demand shift.
thesis🗣 Adam Rosenwikconviction: mediumChinaoil demand
So what: Investors betting on a structural collapse in Chinese oil demand from its energy diversification push are likely mistiming the story by years.
“China is not in a position today to fundamentally change how it consumes energy” ▶ 57:09
Positioning, the Ceasefire Myth, and Persistent Bearish Selling 4
Investor and speculator positioning in energy was 'super super short' heading into the Hormuz crisis around March 2, to the point that several large multi-strategy hedge funds reportedly fired their entire energy trading desks in early March, just before the conflict escalated.
data_point🗣 Adam Rosenwikconviction: mediummulti-strat hedge fundsoil
So what: Extreme pre-crisis short positioning explains both the initial short-covering rally and the market's subsequent skepticism, suggesting sentiment (not fundamentals) has driven much of the price action.
“positioning of investors and speculators going into March 2nd was super short, super super short” ▶ 45:56
Adam Rosenwik disputes the market narrative of a full round-trip in oil sentiment, arguing investors were bearish throughout the entire episode -- covering shorts briefly for a couple of weeks (producing the initial rally) before bearish selling pressure resumed and accelerated once a perceived ceasefire took hold.
contrarian🗣 Adam Rosenwikconviction: mediumoilhedge fund positioning
So what: If the market never actually turned bullish, current bearish positioning may be more stretched and fragile than it appears, raising the risk of another sharp squeeze if inventories keep collapsing.
Adam Rosenwik believes there is no real ceasefire in the Iran conflict despite market pricing -- he argues neither side genuinely wants one, and the 'ceasefire' narrative is being sustained mainly because of the upcoming US midterm elections.
contrarian🗣 Adam Rosenwikconviction: mediumIranUS midterm elections
So what: If the ceasefire is more political theater than reality, renewed escalation risk is materially underpriced in oil markets.
“The only reason the narrative is even there is because of the midterms” ▶ 48:53
Adam Rosenwik's fund has held a longstanding view that global oil demand has been meaningfully stronger than consensus appreciated, which is why inventories stayed roughly stable last year rather than building the way they would have in the large surplus most forecasters assumed existed.
thesis🗣 Adam Rosenwikconviction: highoil demandoil inventories
So what: A demand base case stronger than consensus implies less cushion in the system, making today's inventory draws more structurally significant rather than a one-off shock.
“our long-term view has always been demand has been a little bit stronger, quite a bit stronger than most people appreciate” ▶ 47:48
Gold: Why Adam Rotated Out Near the Top and What Would Bring Him Back 4
Adam Rosenwik's fund exited nearly all of its gold mining equity positions in two tranches -- a small valuation-driven sale in October when the gold-to-oil ratio looked stretched, and the bulk in January, within about three days of gold's eventual price top -- rotating the proceeds into energy equities.
anecdote🗣 Adam Rosenwikconviction: highgold minersgoldenergy equities
So what: A well-timed rotation out of gold into energy by a natural-resources specialist fund signals a relative-value shift worth tracking for other commodity allocators.
“we we owe the timing gods one on that one because we we got that one pretty good within about 3 days at the top of of gold there”
Adam Rosenwik's January decision to exit gold was driven by several converging signals: a parabolic catch-up rally in silver (historically a marker that precious-metals rallies are ending), gold's own parabolic price action, a surge in 'fast money' gold ETF accumulation, and a market pricing in three rate cuts that he viewed as overly dovish given a hawkish Fed backdrop, creating 'buy the rumor, sell the news' risk.
framework🗣 Adam Rosenwikconviction: highsilvergold ETFsinterest rate cuts
So what: This gives investors a repeatable checklist (silver catch-up, ETF inflow surge, dovish rate positioning) for spotting precious-metals blow-off tops.
“silver staged this massive catch-up rally” ▶ 59:51
Adam Rosenwik frames the current gold drawdown as a normal bull-market consolidation rather than a trend change, noting historical precedents in 1973-74 and 2008-09 saw 35-40% pullbacks lasting 2-3 years, and the current pullback is only about halfway to that historical depth.
framework🗣 Adam Rosenwikconviction: mediumgold
So what: If gold is only mid-way through a historically typical consolidation, investors shouldn't expect a durable trend reversal for another 1-2+ years even if the current selloff feels severe.
“35%'s not uncommon. 40% is not unheard of” ▶ 1:02:15
Adam Rosenwik's contrarian signal for re-entering gold is sentiment-based: he says he'll get interested again 'when everyone stops asking' him to buy gold, since the current widespread desire among his audience for him to rotate back into gold is itself evidence the market hasn't bottomed.
contrarian🗣 Adam Rosenwikconviction: mediumgold
So what: Persistent bullish gold sentiment despite the drawdown suggests capitulation hasn't happened yet, arguing against a near-term bottom.
“when everyone stops asking me, that's when it'll be time to get back into gold” ▶ 1:03:11
Chinaification of US Strategic Capital and the Real AI Energy Bottleneck 5
The host argues the US is undertaking a 'Chinaification' of its economic model -- building direct government equity stakes in strategic companies via a sovereign wealth fund and 'Trump accounts' -- that could scale toward 5-10% government ownership of strategic US equities within 10 years, funded through dollar issuance and expected to accelerate after the midterms into next year.
thesis🗣 hostconviction: mediumUS sovereign wealth fundUS dollarTrump accounts
So what: If the US government becomes a large direct equity investor in strategic sectors, that changes capital allocation dynamics for commodities and infrastructure well beyond AI.
“I think of this as the Chinaification of of the American system.” ▶ 1:04:24
Adam Rosenwik expects US government strategic investment to concentrate in rare earths (via offtake agreements that set price floors to de-risk new capital), uranium mining and the fuel-enrichment/fabrication cycle, and copper -- though he's skeptical these will prove economically optimal long-term investments even though he thinks they're politically likely.
prediction🗣 Adam Rosenwikconviction: mediumrare earthsuraniumcopperUS government
So what: Government-backed price floors and offtake deals in rare earths and uranium could de-risk equity investment in those names even if the underlying economics remain questionable.
“I suspect that copper uh will be a fairly important strategic mineral for the government” ▶ 1:07:48
Adam Rosenwik describes copper as 'the antithesis of oil': despite prices near all-time highs and near-universal investor bullishness, mine supply grew quickly last year (contrary to consensus that supply can't grow), Chinese demand has stagnated for about 18 months, and inventories have swollen to 30-year highs -- making current valuations look completely disconnected from fundamentals.
contrarian🗣 Adam Rosenwikconviction: highcopperChina
So what: Despite government interest in copper as a strategic metal, Adam sees it as fundamentally overvalued today relative to oil, a potential caution signal for commodity investors.
“we look at the copper market and in many ways it's the antithesis of oil” ▶ 1:08:33
Adam Rosenwik's highest-conviction call is that the biggest opportunity in the market today is not AI chips or specialty metals but the upstream natural gas supply needed to power AI data centers -- an area almost nobody is investing in even though the incremental energy demand is 'astronomical.'
thesis🗣 Adam Rosenwikconviction: highnatural gasAI data centersspecialty metals
So what: Investors chasing AI exposure through chips or exotic metals may be overlooking the far larger and underinvested upstream energy supply chain that will physically constrain data center buildout.
“Nobody wants to invest in the molecule of natural gas, let's say, that will inevitably power this data center. That's the biggest opportunity that exists in the market today.” ▶ 0:00
Even market participants focused on AI's power needs are misdirecting capital toward building power plants -- which merely convert energy into electricity -- rather than toward the upstream energy source itself, which is the actual bottleneck.
causal🗣 Adam Rosenwikconviction: highAI data centersnatural gaspower plants
So what: Distinguishing 'energy' (the molecule) from 'power infrastructure' (the plant) points investors toward upstream gas supply and pipeline ownership as the underappreciated trade, not just power generation assets.
“what they really want is they they want to build the power plant but that's not energy” ▶ 1:10:14
Has Trend Following Changed Forever? | Systematic Investor | Ep.40865 min37 insights2026-07-12
▶ Watch on YouTube
Niels Kaastrup-Larsen and Alan Dunne review a dispersed first half of 2026 for CTAs, unpack how new Fed chair Kevin Warsh's pullback from forward guidance could add volatility and favor trend-following, then dig into three new papers: two arguing trend following is a structural portfolio diversifier for a post-globalization regime, and a CFM paper (with Jean-Philippe Bouchaud) contending that fast trend following's decline since 2010 is driven by volatility-adjusted tick size and the breakdown of the bank-warehousing execution mechanism, not AUM growth or electronification.
Fed regime change under Kevin Warsh · 9CTA and trend performance, first half of 2026 · 7Regime-change case for trend following (Aspect and Systematica papers) · 4Microstructure explanation for fast trend following's decline (CFM paper) · 11Retail access to professional derivatives markets · 3Jeremy Grantham's early quant investing history · 3
Fed regime change under Kevin Warsh 9
Kevin Warsh's first press conference as Fed chair was notably evasive on his economic views, featured a shorter post-meeting statement than under his predecessor, and he declined to submit a dot to the Fed's dot plot, signaling a deliberate retreat from detailed forward guidance.
data_point🗣 Alan Dunneconviction: mediumKevin WarshFederal Reserve
So what: Watch for reduced Fed transparency ahead, which could raise policy-driven volatility and shift markets toward relying on data over Fed rhetoric.
Warsh has commissioned an internal Fed task force on communications and is skeptical of forward guidance, arguing markets have become overly reliant on it and that the Fed should let markets draw their own inferences from economic data rather than try to anticipate the Fed's next move.
thesis🗣 Alan Dunneconviction: mediumKevin WarshFederal Reserveforward guidance
So what: A shift away from scripted Fed communication changes how bond markets should price the future rate path.
A study found that 68% of the variance in the 5-year Treasury yield around a Fed meeting was attributable to the Fed's statement, versus only 12% to the actual rate decision itself, evidence of how dependent markets became on Fed communication rather than the policy action.
data_point🗣 Alan Dunneconviction: highFederal ReserveTreasury yields
So what: Quantifies how much rates-market pricing is currently driven by Fed messaging rather than substance, a source of risk if that communication channel is scaled back.
≈ “68% of the variance in the 5-year Treasury yield after a Fed meeting was attributable to the statement, and only 12% to the actual rate decision itself.” paraphrase
Forward guidance as a policy tool was architected by Ben Bernanke via two 2004 speeches ('Fed Speak' and 'Central Bank Talk and Monetary Policy'), designed to influence long-term rates at a time before the Fed had tools like QE to do so directly.
data_point🗣 Alan Dunneconviction: mediumFederal Reserveforward guidance
So what: Understanding forward guidance's origin as a pre-QE workaround helps investors judge whether Warsh's rollback of it is a durable regime change or a cyclical stylistic shift.
A shift away from Fed forward guidance likely means higher market volatility and a larger bond term premium, and could specifically benefit trend-following strategies, since an incrementally revealed, data-dependent tightening cycle takes markets longer to fully price in than one pre-announced via a dot plot.
causal🗣 Alan Dunneconviction: mediumFederal Reservetrend followingbond market
So what: A structural Fed communication shift could become a tailwind for trend-following returns going forward, and for term premium in bonds.
The AI capex boom is creating an inflation dilemma for the Fed: memory chips, electricity, and consumer products from iPhones to gaming consoles are being pushed higher by AI-driven demand now, while the Fed expects the productivity gains from AI to eventually be disinflationary, without knowing when that relief will arrive.
thesis🗣 Alan Dunneconviction: mediumKevin WarshAI capexinflation
So what: An inflation overshoot risk exists near-term even if AI is disinflationary long-run, which could keep the Fed cautious about cutting rates.
≈ “We believe that we'll get a disinflationary impulse down the line, but we don't know when it is.” paraphrase
Despite markets pricing a hawkish read on Warsh after his first US press conference, the hosts believe consensus may be overestimating his hawkishness, since he separately said at a European conference that inflation risks are coming down.
contrarian🗣 Niels Kaastrup-Larsenconviction: lowKevin WarshFederal Reserveinflation
So what: If the market's hawkish read on Warsh is wrong, rate expectations could adjust dovishly, a two-sided risk for rates positioning.
Apple raised its prices roughly 20-25% in a single day, an increase notable enough that the hosts expect it to show up in official inflation data.
data_point🗣 Niels Kaastrup-Larsenconviction: mediumAppleinflation
So what: A concrete, trackable corporate pricing data point with a likely read-through into upcoming CPI prints.
≈ “Apple raising their prices 20-25% in a day” paraphrase
Beyond communication style, the more consequential change to watch under Warsh may be shifts to which economic data series the Fed collects and publishes and how they are calculated, since many market participants and models are built around the current data definitions.
prediction🗣 Niels Kaastrup-Larsenconviction: mediumFederal Reserveeconomic data
So what: Watch for methodology changes to key Fed-referenced data series, which could disrupt models and trading signals built on current definitions.
CTA and trend performance, first half of 2026 7
Niels' trend barometer stood at 50 as of early July 2026, a decent-to-strong reading, though performance had gone quiet-to-soft in the first week of July before reversing on renewed Middle East bombing campaigns, particularly in energy markets.
data_point🗣 Niels Kaastrup-Larsenconviction: mediumtrend barometerCTAoilMiddle East
So what: A live, weekly-updated gauge of how broadly CTAs are trending, useful for tracking near-term systematic-strategy positioning shifts.
As of July 7, 2026, systematic benchmarks were solidly positive year-to-date: the SG CTA Index +8.9%, SG Trend Index +8.5%, and Short-Term Traders Index +4.65%, versus MSCI World +9.65%, the US Aggregate Bond Index +0.25%, and S&P 500 total return near +10%.
data_point🗣 Niels Kaastrup-Larsenconviction: highSG CTA IndexSG Trend IndexMSCI WorldS&P 500
So what: Provides a concrete cross-asset performance scoreboard investors can use to benchmark CTA/trend allocations against equities and bonds for H1 2026.
First-half 2026 CTA performance showed notable dispersion: managers running non-trend strategies inside their books generally did somewhat better, while managers heavily exposed to previously popular 'alternative markets' mostly struggled, with few exceptions such as ISAM posting positive returns.
data_point🗣 Niels Kaastrup-Larsenconviction: mediumCTAISAMalternative markets
So what: Manager selection within trend/CTA allocations should weight strategy composition (trend vs non-trend sleeves, alt-market exposure), not just headline category returns.
From an allocator's vantage point, June 2026 was a weak month for many CTA managers versus a benchmark down under 2%, with commodity reversals in aluminum, copper, and soybean oil tied to a change in Middle East tone, followed by a rebound in corn heading into July.
data_point🗣 Alan Dunneconviction: mediumCTAcopperaluminumcornMiddle East
So what: Highlights which specific commodity reversals drove June's below-benchmark manager dispersion, useful for post-mortem attribution.
The recent bullish 'commodity supercycle' narrative around energy and precious metals closely echoes the setup seen in March-April 2022 after Russia's invasion of Ukraine, a period that in hindsight turned out to be an excellent year for CTAs, though the hosts stop short of forecasting a repeat.
thesis🗣 Niels Kaastrup-Larsenconviction: lowCTAcommodity supercycleoilprecious metals
So what: A narrative-driven commodity rally worth monitoring as a potential parallel to 2022's strong CTA year, without treating it as a forecast.
Fears in March 2026 that Middle East conflict would trigger a major Strait of Hormuz-driven oil price spike did not materialize, largely because China drew down its own reserves and rerouted oil flows via pipelines, letting the market absorb weeks of disruption.
causal🗣 Alan Dunneconviction: mediumoilMiddle EastChina
So what: Shows that 'this time it's different' crisis narratives around oil chokepoints can be overstated when China has spare reserve capacity to buffer supply shocks.
Copper is viewed as a key read on the AI infrastructure theme, with hyperscaler capex and data-center buildout plans continuing to escalate, a demand-side commodity driver that is structurally distinct from supply-shock narratives like Middle East oil disruptions.
thesis🗣 Alan Dunneconviction: mediumcopperAI capexdata centers
So what: Copper's AI-linked demand story is a durable secular trade thesis investors can track separately from geopolitical supply shocks.
Regime-change case for trend following (Aspect and Systematica papers) 4
Aspect Capital's paper argues the last 25 years of abundant, cheap energy and globalization's 'peace dividend' were a historical anomaly, and markets are now in a period of structural breaks, including bonds becoming less reliable equity diversifiers, the dollar's store-of-value status being reassessed, and central banks aggressively buying gold.
thesis🗣 Alan Dunneconviction: mediumAspectbondsgoldcentral banks
So what: Supports strategic, not just tactical, allocations to trend following and commodities as portfolio diversifiers in a regime-change environment.
Investors shouldn't assume future macro shocks will only come from oil as in the 1970s template; shocks could just as easily emerge from copper, food prices, a fiscal crisis hitting bond markets, or a large dollar rally or decline, each requiring different diversifying exposures.
framework🗣 Alan Dunneconviction: mediumcopperfiscal shockdollar
So what: Broadens the tail-risk-hedging playbook beyond energy-shock-focused diversifiers to fiscal and currency shock scenarios.
Systematica's paper makes the case for trend following through a 'total portfolio approach' lens, arguing that with stock-bond correlation shifting and other hedge fund strategies (and private equity) carrying embedded equity risk, trend is a valuable complement to equity market-neutral strategies because it offers convexity that market-neutral lacks.
framework🗣 Alan Dunneconviction: mediumSystematicaequity market neutraltrend following
So what: Allocators building genuine diversifier sleeves should pair trend with equity market-neutral rather than relying on bonds alone.
The broader industry conversation is shifting from 'which assets should I own' (e.g., the classic 60/40 stock-bond split) to 'which behavioral exposures or regimes should I own,' reflecting a total-portfolio mindset that evaluates a strategy by its marginal impact on the whole portfolio rather than its performance relative to similar-style peers.
framework🗣 Niels Kaastrup-Larsenconviction: medium60/40 portfoliototal portfolio approachtrend following
So what: A framework shift investors and allocators should adopt when evaluating whether to add trend following or other diversifying strategies.
Microstructure explanation for fast trend following's decline (CFM paper) 11
A new paper co-authored by Jean-Philippe Bouchaud (chairman of CFM) argues the primary driver of fast/short-term trend following's decline is volatility-adjusted tick size, dismissing two commonly cited explanations: industry AUM growth (inconsistent, since AUM stagnated for years while futures market liquidity grew) and market electronification (which shows no impact on interest-rate futures profitability).
contrarian🗣 Alan Dunneconviction: highJean-Philippe BouchaudCFMfast trend following
So what: Reframes the trend-following 'AUM got too big' narrative as empirically unsupported and points allocators toward a different, testable driver of returns.
In small-tick markets like currencies (e.g., EUR/USD with a tick around 0.00001), advertised bid/offer liquidity understates true depth, whereas large-tick markets such as bonds show liquidity more transparently at the touch, a structural feature the CFM paper ties directly to fast trend following's diverging performance across asset classes.
framework🗣 Alan Dunneconviction: mediumtick sizecurrenciesbonds
So what: Suggests fast-trend allocators should segment markets by tick-size regime rather than treating currencies and bonds as equally suited to short-term signals.
Before roughly 2010, large trend-following orders in FX (e.g., a $500 million to $1 billion dollar-yen trade) could be executed with big banks who would warehouse and pass on the risk to other banks, creating a cascading 'transmission mechanism' that itself generated further momentum in the market, an execution-driven effect the CFM paper argues was a major, underappreciated source of fast trend following's historical profitability.
causal🗣 Alan Dunneconviction: highforeign exchangebankstrend following
So what: Explains why fast trend outperformed pre-2010 independent of behavioral underreaction/overreaction theories, and why its edge eroded once that execution channel closed.
Since around 2010, banks have stepped back from warehousing large trade risk as high-frequency trading grew, breaking the FX and futures execution transmission mechanism that used to amplify trend-following orders into further momentum, a structural rather than cyclical change.
causal🗣 Alan Dunneconviction: highhigh-frequency tradingbankstrend following
So what: If the transmission mechanism is structurally gone, fast trend's historical edge in small-tick markets may not return even as volatility cycles turn.
Per the CFM paper, fast trend following's underperformance since 2010 is concentrated specifically in small-tick markets like equities and currencies; in large-tick markets it has kept working, even outperforming, across the full sample back to 1995, with no evidence of a 'CTA winter' in that segment.
data_point🗣 Alan Dunneconviction: highfast trend followingequitiescurrencies
So what: Allocators should differentiate fast-trend allocations by tick-size regime rather than treating short-term trend as uniformly broken.
The paper's Figure 10 shows fast trend following's underperformance was concentrated specifically between 2010 and 2020, with a partial recovery since 2020, leaving open whether this reflects a permanent structural break or simply a difficult decade, a question the paper itself does not resolve.
data_point🗣 Alan Dunneconviction: mediumfast trend followingCFM
So what: An open empirical question worth independent testing before assuming fast trend is permanently impaired versus merely cyclically out of favor.
The CFM paper finds that market participants have shifted toward more counter-trend, mean-reverting trading behavior recently, corroborating a similar observation made separately by Toby Crabel (author of Opening Range Breakout) that breakouts now see less follow-through and more frequent reversals at short time horizons.
data_point🗣 Alan Dunneconviction: mediumToby CrabelCFMmean reversion
So what: Independent corroboration from a veteran short-term trader strengthens the case that intraday/short-horizon market behavior has genuinely become more mean-reverting.
The paper finds trend following actually performs better in low-volatility environments, and that what has specifically been eliminated is trend followers' ability to profit from large one-day directional moves, a combination of liquidity and contract tick-size characteristics rather than liquidity alone, which did not explain the effect cross-sectionally.
data_point🗣 Alan Dunneconviction: mediumfast trend followingliquidityvolatility
So what: Suggests trend-following risk models should weight tick-size and one-day-move capture ability, not just realized volatility, when sizing fast signals.
Testing his own firm's models, Alan Dunne confirmed independently that short-term-timeframe classical trend-following signals have been unprofitable for roughly the past 15 years, with a break point around 2009 that matches the CFM paper's findings.
data_point🗣 Alan Dunneconviction: hightrend following
So what: Independent, real-world confirmation of the paper's core empirical claim from a practitioner who tested it against live systematic models.
Niels offers three alternative or complementary explanations for fast trend's decline beyond tick size: 2009-era banking reforms that restricted proprietary trading and pushed liquidity provision to be outsourced; the rise of multi-strategy 'pod shop' AUM, whose managers can reportedly be forced to cut risk by 50% within two hours of a Friday-afternoon risk call; and the fact that when exchanges shrink contract size (like CME's new 10-barrel oil contract), traders must buy far more contracts for the same notional exposure while per-contract fees likely stay flat, raising effective commission costs.
contrarian🗣 Niels Kaastrup-Larsenconviction: mediumbanking regulationCME Grouptransaction costs
So what: Multiple candidate drivers besides tick size should be independently tested before attributing fast trend's decline to a single cause; smaller futures contract sizes may quietly raise trading costs for CTAs.
≈ “If you're one of the pods and you get a call on a Friday afternoon that you need to cut your risk by 50%, they give you two hours to cut that risk.” paraphrase
A shift in execution style, from manually working paper order tickets during the day to algorithmic execution that goes straight from signal to exchange, is another likely factor behind changed short-term market dynamics, since execution algorithms behave differently than human traders by having no emotion and more patience.
causal🗣 Niels Kaastrup-Larsenconviction: mediumexecution algorithmstrend following
So what: Execution-technology evolution is an underappreciated, independently testable contributor to changed short-term market microstructure.
Retail access to professional derivatives markets 3
CME Group is launching a new oil futures contract sized at 10 barrels versus the standard 1,000-barrel contract, sharply cutting the contract size to draw more retail money into the market, a move Niels questions given that some financial instruments may not be well-suited for widespread retail trading.
data_point🗣 Niels Kaastrup-Larsenconviction: mediumCME Groupoil futures
So what: Signals continued exchange-level efforts to retailize professional derivatives markets, worth watching for participation and flow effects.
Niels cites last year's silver retail buying frenzy, where in his view it wasn't professionals who bought the highs, as a cautionary example of retail money entering markets late and near tops when access is broadened.
anecdote🗣 Niels Kaastrup-Larsenconviction: mediumsilverretail investors
So what: A live case study of retail-driven late-cycle buying risk relevant to any newly retail-accessible market, such as CME's smaller oil contract.
Alan Dunne counters that retail participation in commodities is largely unavoidable regardless of new contract design, since retail traders already access oil exposure via ETFs (which broke down when oil prices went negative) and spread betting, so CME offering smaller contracts is simply formalizing an existing retail segment.
contrarian🗣 Alan Dunneconviction: mediumCME Groupoil ETFspread betting
So what: Suggests new small-size contracts mainly channel existing retail demand into exchange-traded products rather than creating new speculative risk from scratch.
Jeremy Grantham's early quant investing history 3
A new biography of Jeremy Grantham, co-written with Edward Chancellor, reveals that Grantham's early firm (predating GMO) was an early pioneer of both passive investing and quantitative investing, and saw strong complementarity between value and momentum factors, a contrast to Grantham's public image as a purely fundamental, bottom-up value bear.
data_point🗣 Alan Dunneconviction: mediumJeremy GranthamGMO
So what: Reframes a famous 'perma-bear' fundamental investor as an early systematic and quant pioneer, relevant context for how long value-momentum complementarity has been recognized.
Grantham's team built an early quant equity factor model for a client, the IMF, that initially underperformed and was shelved, was later improved and worked well, but after roughly 10 years of further attempted enhancements the team couldn't meaningfully improve the model any further.
anecdote🗣 Alan Dunneconviction: mediumJeremy Grantham
So what: A cautionary data point for allocators evaluating managers who claim continuous alpha improvements to legacy quant models.
Grantham's team also identified a 'neglect factor,' stocks with low analyst coverage such as small caps, that tested well in combination with value and momentum but stopped working for roughly six years immediately after being implemented, a decades-old illustration that a well-researched factor can go dark for years once deployed.
anecdote🗣 Alan Dunneconviction: mediumJeremy Grantham
So what: A historical parallel to today's fast-trend-following drought, reinforcing that long dry spells in systematic factors are not new and shouldn't automatically be read as a strategy being permanently broken.

Risk Reversal · Dan Nathan 2 episode(s) · 64 insights

Jim Chanos: The AI Bubble Is “Much Worse” Than Dot-Com46 min37 insights2026-07-17
▶ Watch on YouTube
Short-seller Jim Chanos argues the AI infrastructure buildout is a bubble worse than the dot-com era in dollar scale and structure, driven by uneconomic data-center CapEx, deferred depreciation accounting, and deteriorating hyperscaler ROIC, while heavy issuance and speculative single-stock dispersion echo the setup of early 2021.
AI Bubble vs. Dot-Com: Scale and Verdict · 4Nvidia as the Valuation Ceiling · 5Data Center Economics: CapEx, Depreciation, and Duration Mismatch · 9Hyperscaler Capital Discipline and ROIC Deterioration · 5Neo-Cloud and Legacy Data Center Stress Signals · 4Circular Financing, Financialization, and Corporate Rivalries · 4Market Structure: Dispersion, Retail Speculation, and Positioning · 5Apple's AI Restraint as a Relative Winner · 1
AI Bubble vs. Dot-Com: Scale and Verdict 4
Jim Chanos argues the current AI infrastructure buildout dwarfs the dot-com bust's two failed telecom sectors (competitive local exchange carriers and fiber-light providers), which together spent only about $100 billion over five years from 1998-2002 (roughly $20 billion a year combined) — a sum individual companies now exceed in a single AI buildout cycle.
data_point🗣 Jim Chanosconviction: highdot-com bubbleCLECsfiber-lightAI buildout
So what: Investors comparing today's AI capex to the dot-com telecom overbuild are underestimating the scale gap — today's per-company spending dwarfs entire industries from 2000, implying a larger unwind if returns disappoint.
“those two industries as a whole, and we went back and looked last year in our files, those two industries spent a total of a hundred billion dollars over five years, from '98 to '02.” ▶ 14:08
In the dot-com era most infrastructure spending came from already-profitable enterprises (Bank of America, Merrill Lynch, GE, Coca-Cola) that stayed profitable and simply cut equipment orders when demand disappointed, whereas in the current AI cycle only the hyperscalers are reliably profitable while most of the rest of the ecosystem is unprofitable and dependent on continued funding to build data centers and AI models.
causal🗣 Jim Chanosconviction: highCiscoBank of AmericaCoca-ColaGEhyperscalers
So what: A demand shortfall today would hit a far less financially resilient base of spenders than in 2000, raising default/failure risk versus a simple order-cut cycle.
“if Coca-Cola needed 10,000 routers and it ordered a Cisco in 2000, by 2001 they cut it back to 2000 routers, right?” ▶ 15:21
Chanos describes the defining feature of the current market as pricing nearly every AI-adjacent stock as if its bullish narrative has already been proven true, directly comparing it to 1999-2000 when 'new economy' stocks were valued as if demand were infinite before order books and profits collapsed.
thesis🗣 Jim Chanosconviction: highdot-com bubbleAI stocks
So what: Signals investors should watch for a shift from narrative-based to fundamentals-based pricing as the key inflection point/regime-change trigger for AI stocks.
“In bull markets, you put a premium on promises, and bear markets, you put a discount on reality. And we're clearly in the former right now. Do we get to the latter? I don't know.” ▶ 0:00
Despite his bearish framing of AI infrastructure valuations, Chanos believes the AI buildout will still produce huge eventual winners and real wealth creation, comparable to how the internet buildout ultimately did despite the dot-com bust.
thesis🗣 Jim Chanosconviction: mediumAIinternet
So what: Tempers the bearish thesis — this is a call on bubble-stage mispricing and timing, not a permanent bet against AI's long-run value creation, useful for separating tactical shorts from a structural anti-AI stance.
“This is going to be like the internet. There's going to be huge winners that come out of this and wealth created on the backside of AI.” ▶ 44:17
Nvidia as the Valuation Ceiling 5
Chanos holds as an investment framework that no hardware company dependent on obtaining Nvidia chips to operate should trade at a higher valuation than Nvidia itself, yet he observes many AI-infrastructure and data-center-adjacent stocks currently violate this rule.
framework🗣 Jim Chanosconviction: highNvidia
So what: Gives investors a simple relative-value screen to flag overextended AI-adjacent stocks against the sector's price-setting bellwether.
“Not one company in the hardware space should trade at higher valuations than Nvidia. That's one of the things we hold dear.” ▶ 0:16
Chanos states plainly that no company dependent on Nvidia to exist should trade above Nvidia's own valuation, and yet many companies currently do trade at a premium to Nvidia.
contrarian🗣 Jim Chanosconviction: highNvidia
So what: Explicit mispricing across the Nvidia supply chain is a candidate short list for investors who agree with the valuation-ceiling framework.
“No one that is dependent upon Nvidia to exist should trade at a higher valuation than Nvidia itself. And and and yet many many companies do.” ▶ 20:54
Nvidia's roughly 75% gross margins are likely to hold for the next couple of years absent real competition, but visibility beyond that is limited because the physical-plant cycle for new chip and memory capacity is itself only about two years, meaning multi-year margin assumptions embedded in valuations are largely guesswork.
framework🗣 Jim Chanosconviction: mediumNvidia
So what: Investors modeling Nvidia or its supply chain on flat long-term margins are extrapolating well past the horizon the underlying technology cycle actually supports.
Micron's stock ran from around $100 eighteen months ago to a peak of about $1,250 before pulling back to roughly $950, with bulls citing long-term supply agreements and reported order backlog implying sustained ~80% gross margins, despite Micron having posted negative gross margins as recently as three years ago.
data_point🗣 host (Dan Nathan/Guy Adami)conviction: mediumMicronDRAMhigh bandwidth memory
So what: Highlights how quickly the market re-rated a historically cyclical, thin-margin memory maker on the assumption that this cycle's pricing power is durable — a key test case for the CapEx/long-term-agreement bull thesis.
“Micron's a company that had negative gross margins 3 years ago, right?” ▶ 19:08
Chanos is skeptical that Micron's contracted order-book and long-term agreement backlog will fully materialize as booked, arguing history counsels caution whenever a company is awarded a premium market cap for future contracted revenue that has not yet been earned.
contrarian🗣 Jim Chanosconviction: mediumMicron
So what: Signals a potential fade/short candidate if long-term-agreement-based revenue projections fail to convert, a pattern Chanos has seen recur across cycles.
“History says you should be very cautious about that view.” ▶ 20:17
Data Center Economics: CapEx, Depreciation, and Duration Mismatch 9
Chanos argues today's AI data-center buildout differs fundamentally from the 2005-2006 U.S. housing bubble because even after defaults, home prices largely held their value, whereas hundreds of billions of dollars of AI data-center CapEx may be completely uneconomic as physical structures from day one, independent of whether the underlying AI models ultimately succeed.
contrarian🗣 Jim Chanosconviction: highAI data centershousing bubble
So what: Implies downside in AI infrastructure assets could be structurally worse than a housing-style correction since the physical collateral itself may never recoup its cost.
“We are embarking on hundreds of billions of dollars in CapEx and projects that may be completely uneconomic from day one.” ▶ 8:51
Many data-center, storage, and office-building projects are being underwritten at pre-tax cap rates of 5-7% while the 10-year Treasury yields about 4.5%, with sponsors layering on leverage and mezzanine financing to promise equity investors roughly 15% returns; Chanos warns rates rising to 6-7% would blow up asset class after asset class.
causal🗣 Jim Chanosconviction: highdata centers10-year Treasurycap rates
So what: Gives a concrete rate level (6-7%) to watch as a tripping point for real-asset and data-center valuations financed on thin cap-rate spreads.
≈ “Rates going to six or or seven blows that all up. Um, blows up asset class after asset class.” paraphrase
Chanos expects credit spreads to begin meaningfully widening if the 10-year Treasury yield accelerates toward 5% with some momentum, noting that triple-C junk spreads are already widening while BB/BBB spreads have not yet moved.
prediction🗣 Jim Chanosconviction: mediumcredit spreads10-year Treasurytriple-C bonds
So what: Gives a specific, monitorable macro trigger (yields approaching 5%, CCC spread behavior) for when AI-adjacent credit risk could start repricing broadly.
SL Green, a major New York City office landlord, trades at roughly a 5% cap rate and its stock has gone nowhere in 25 years, which Chanos cites as a real-world bellwether for how persistently low cap rates can still produce a poor equity outcome, despite Chanos & Co. holding no position in the stock.
anecdote🗣 Jim Chanosconviction: mediumSL Green
So what: A cautionary real-asset case study for investors assuming low cap rates guarantee good long-term equity returns.
“SL Green's stock's gone nowhere in 25 years. It's been a terrible investment.” ▶ 10:52
Investors are committing to 20-year data-center assets based on current spot lease pricing that may only be guaranteed for a year or two, a duration mismatch Chanos compares to 19th-century railroads that overbuilt capacity on high freight rates and went bankrupt when rates fell, and to the repo-market funding mismatch that broke down in the 2008 financial crisis.
framework🗣 Jim Chanosconviction: highrailroads2008 financial crisisdata centers
So what: Frames the core structural risk in AI infrastructure investing as a duration/pricing mismatch rather than a demand question — worth tracking lease renewal terms, not just headline deal announcements.
“People are making decisions on long-term projects based on spot prices. And that that's a terrifying thing.” ▶ 17:06
GPUs sitting unplugged in warehouses are booked as 'construction in progress' on balance sheets, meaning depreciation on them doesn't start — and the deployment clock itself may not begin for up to 18 months after a chip is received — which understates true economic depreciation for hyperscalers and neo-clouds.
data_point🗣 Jim Chanosconviction: highNvidiahyperscalersconstruction in progress accounting
So what: A specific balance-sheet line item (construction in progress) investors can track to gauge how much true depreciation/obsolescence risk is being deferred off the income statement.
“this this subaccount of property, plant, equipment called construction in progress keeps growing. Um and that's basically stuff that isn't plugged in yet or or capitalized interest or capitalized labor costs.” ▶ 24:08
Because one company's AI CapEx dollar becomes another company's (e.g., Nvidia's) revenue and profit rather than an expense, S&P 500 earnings growth is running roughly 4-5 times its long-run ~6%-a-year trend (about 28% this year, another 20%-plus next year), a dynamic Chanos says also occurred at the peak of the 1999-2000 dot-com boom.
causal🗣 Jim Chanosconviction: highS&P 500Nvidia
So what: A macro tell for index-level earnings quality — outsized aggregate EPS growth driven by circular CapEx spending is a warning sign rather than evidence of broad-based economic strength.
“you don't get above trend 5x above trend or 4x above trend unless something like that is happening where $1 is a profit is not a cost for another company.” ▶ 25:17
Hyperscalers and neo-clouds are depreciating GPUs over roughly 5-6 years even though Chanos's own research indicates chips run continuously have a physical life of 10-12 years; Chanos's firm conservatively models a 10-year life in its analysis and still cannot make most data-center economics pencil out.
data_point🗣 Jim Chanosconviction: highGPUshyperscalersneo-clouds
So what: Suggests depreciation-schedule assumptions used by bulls are not even the binding constraint — the economics fail even under generous useful-life assumptions.
“if you run these chips 365 24/7 what have you they they burn out after 10 to 12 years. I mean you have a physical life of 10 to 12 years on all these chips.” ▶ 26:51
Power is not a real bottleneck for AI data centers: electricity costs are only about 5-6% of a data center's revenue, the smallest cost component, and the U.S. has ample cheap power; the actual risks are transmission and regulatory bottlenecks plus potential political backlash against new data-center construction.
contrarian🗣 Jim Chanosconviction: highpower gridTexas griddata centers
So what: Pushes back on a popular bull narrative (power scarcity as a moat/bottleneck) — investors overweighting 'grid access' deals as a durable advantage may be mispricing that catalyst.
“It power costs for a data center are about 5 to 6% of revenues. They are the the smallest cost component is power.” ▶ 32:50
Hyperscaler Capital Discipline and ROIC Deterioration 5
The return on incremental invested capital for the major hyperscalers as a group has fallen from about 40% a year and a half ago to about 20% today, and Chanos's firm is tracking this metric closely for clients because continued spending at the current pace could push it toward 10%.
data_point🗣 Jim Chanosconviction: highhyperscalersAmazonGoogleMetaMicrosoft
So what: A concrete, trackable metric (incremental ROIC) that signals when AI capex is crossing from value-accretive to value-destructive for the biggest spenders.
“It's gone from as a group 40% a year and a half ago to about 20% today. And and if the spend keeps up at this kind of rate, it's going to be moving toward 10%.” ▶ 35:09
If the major hyperscalers (Google, Meta, Amazon, Microsoft, Oracle) are collectively earning only about 10% pre-tax on incremental invested capital, the less-capitalized neo-clouds financing their buildouts with debt would be in far worse shape.
causal🗣 Jim Chanosconviction: highneo-cloudsOraclehyperscalers
So what: Frames neo-cloud credit and equity risk as a leveraged, second-order bet on hyperscaler capital returns — worth monitoring before neo-cloud debt matures.
“if if Google and Meta and Amazon and Microsoft and Oracle as a group are incrementally only earning 10% on their invested capital pre-tax, then the neo clouds are in a whole world of hurt.” ▶ 35:38
Oracle has the worst return-on-invested-capital metrics among the major cloud players, and its stock is down roughly 50-70% from levels reached after its OpenAI/AMD-related backlog announcements last September, which Chanos believes rival hyperscaler CEOs (Andy Jassy, Sundar Pichai, Satya Nadella) are watching as a cautionary signal for their own capital discipline.
thesis🗣 Jim Chanosconviction: highOracleOpenAIAMDAndy JassySundar PichaiSatya Nadella
So what: Oracle's re-rating functions as a live case study and leading indicator for how the market will eventually price other hyperscalers if their ROIC keeps deteriorating.
“Oracle has the worst of those ROIC metrics I just talked about.” ▶ 36:41
Chanos predicts that sometime within roughly the next 12 months, investors will begin seriously questioning whether the next trillion dollars of AI CapEx makes sense given hyperscalers' declining incremental operating income, when comparable returns could be earned risk-free in Treasuries.
prediction🗣 Jim Chanosconviction: highhyperscalersTreasuries
So what: Gives a rough timeline (within about 12 months) for a potential inflection point in market sentiment toward AI capex discipline — an event to position around.
≈ “Does this next trillion dollars make sense if you're only going to earn 50 billion on it? Uh we could earn that that on treasuries.” paraphrase
Despite rising AI CapEx guidance across the industry — for example Amazon raising its capex guidance from roughly $120 billion to about $200 billion — Satya Nadella indicated around October/November of last year that Microsoft/Azure had shifted from being compute-constrained to energy-constrained, a narrative in tension with continued aggressive spending increases.
data_point🗣 host (Dan Nathan/Guy Adami)conviction: mediumMicrosoftAzureAmazonSatya Nadella
So what: A potential inconsistency to watch — if energy, not compute, were truly the binding constraint, continued massive compute capex raises would be harder to justify.
Neo-Cloud and Legacy Data Center Stress Signals 4
Nebius, one of the most capital-intensive neo-clouds — spending $4.40 in capital to generate $1 of revenue — announced a new asset-light business model in which it provides the software stack while clients fund the data center, GPUs, and maintenance CapEx, which Chanos calls a tacit admission that owning the physical assets is not actually the moat neo-clouds have claimed for the past two years.
contrarian🗣 Jim Chanosconviction: highNebius
So what: A pivot away from asset ownership by a leading capital-intensive neo-cloud undercuts the bull case that owning GPUs/data centers is a durable moat — worth watching for similar shifts at other neo-clouds.
“They spent $4.40 in capital to to generate $1 in revenue.” ▶ 28:08
CoreWeave has reportedly been exploring derivatives to hedge downside risk in GPU and other asset prices, which Chanos views as rational but notes it cuts against the prevailing bullish narrative that these assets only appreciate in value.
contrarian🗣 Jim Chanosconviction: mediumCoreWeave
So what: A tell that even insiders at asset-heavy neo-clouds may not believe the 'assets can only go up' narrative they're selling to investors.
“allegedly Coreweave has been looking a way to to to hedge downside in in asset prices which I thought was interesting”
Legacy data-center operators are increasingly putting their portfolios up for sale and losing executives (one, the former Cyxtera, which went bankrupt in 2023, is attempting to come public again as 'C-Squared'); Chanos believes this reflects operators' realization that maintenance CapEx costs — driven up by labor and equipment shortages — will be higher than previously disclosed to investors, further depressing already-low returns.
thesis🗣 Jim Chanosconviction: highCyxteralegacy data centers
So what: Insider asset sales and executive departures at legacy data-center operators are a concrete, observable signal of deteriorating economics ahead of it showing up in reported numbers.
“they're realizing their maintenance CapEx numbers are going to be higher in the future than they've let their investors know.” ▶ 30:21
xAI signed short-term (roughly three-month) compute deals worth about $1 billion each with Anthropic and Google, not coming online until fall and cancellable by either counterparty, shortly before SpaceX's IPO valued xAI's implied equity stake at $250 billion in February — deals Chanos characterizes as highly promotional rather than durable commercial commitments.
contrarian🗣 Jim Chanosconviction: mediumxAIAnthropicGoogleSpaceXElon Musk
So what: Illustrates how short-duration, easily cancellable compute deals get marketed as long-term demand validation ahead of financing events — a pattern to scrutinize in other AI infrastructure 'wins.'
“I think they were highly promotional deals.” ▶ 31:40
Circular Financing, Financialization, and Corporate Rivalries 4
Nvidia's equity investments in AI companies that are also its customers create a leverage effect because those investees use the resulting equity stakes as collateral to raise additional equity or debt; while nominally small relative to Nvidia's balance sheet (similar to how Lucent's and Nortel's customer financing looked small in the dot-com era), Chanos flags it as reflecting an incestuous web of AI insiders who are now becoming competitors.
contrarian🗣 Jim Chanosconviction: mediumNvidiaLucentNortel
So what: Circular vendor financing inflates reported demand and creates hidden leverage; the Lucent/Nortel parallel is a specific historical precedent that ended badly.
“it also reflects a little bit of the incestuous nature of the personalities who were there at at at creation, if you will, all all helping each other out and investing each other that are now basically competitors.” ▶ 40:59
