The AI buildout is becoming increasingly financialized and spreading into private equity and banking — for example, Apollo and Blackstone raised about $35 billion around June 2026 to invest in TPUs — and financial media has begun describing banks as 'AI stocks' due to the trading activity in AI-related names, a narrative pattern Chanos finds reminiscent of the late 1990s.
data_point🗣 host (Dan Nathan/Guy Adami)conviction: mediumApolloBlackstoneTPUsBroadcom
So what: Signals AI-bubble risk is migrating beyond tech balance sheets into private equity and bank lending books, broadening potential contagion if the buildout falters.
Chanos discloses his firm is short a large private-equity company partly because one of its exposures is a data-center-linked office building financed at low cap rates, making it acutely vulnerable if cap rates or interest rates rise; he holds no position in traditional banks, which as a group have performed well through this cycle.
data_point🗣 Jim Chanosconviction: highprivate equitybanks
So what: Identifies a specific transmission channel — private equity's low-cap-rate real asset exposure — through which an AI-infrastructure correction could hit adjacent financial names.
“If cap rates go up or rates go up, this company is in big trouble.” ▶ 42:26
Rising legal disputes (Apple suing OpenAI over trade secrets), a 'huge brain drain' of researchers moving between OpenAI, Anthropic, and Microsoft, and low switching costs for end users moving between AI models are cited as evidence that increasingly adversarial 'frenemy' relationships among AI players (e.g., Sam Altman and Elon Musk) will likely escalate into open litigation — including a probable OpenAI-Microsoft dispute — as 2026 moves into 2027.
prediction🗣 host (Dan Nathan/Guy Adami)conviction: mediumAppleOpenAIAnthropicMicrosoftSam AltmanElon Musk
So what: Weak competitive moats plus escalating legal conflict among AI partners/investors could disrupt the compute, funding, and partnership arrangements currently underpinning bullish AI infrastructure narratives.
“the frictional cost to move around, by the way. Not only employees, but but users switching models is is I think one of the defining things of this this boom.”
Market Structure: Dispersion, Retail Speculation, and Positioning 5
Even with the S&P 500 near all-time highs, extreme dispersion is occurring underneath the index: on the day of this recording the semiconductor index (SOX) was down about 3% and a DRAM ETF was down as much as 10% intraday, while hyperscaler stocks that had already sold off roughly 15%-plus were up 3-3.5% and behaving almost defensively.
data_point🗣 host (Dan Nathan/Guy Adami)conviction: mediumSOXDRAM ETFhyperscalers
So what: Index-level calm is masking a violent rotation between AI infrastructure suppliers and hyperscaler demand-side stocks — a sign for long/short and pairs traders that single-stock dispersion, not the index, is where real risk and opportunity are concentrated.
Large-cap, well-followed stocks have been moving 5-10% in a single day multiple times per week on no identifiable news catalyst beyond flows and sentiment, a pattern that has persisted for a couple of months even though the VIX remains low (around 17), which Chanos calls remarkable and highly speculative.
contrarian🗣 Jim Chanosconviction: highVIX
So what: Suggests the market's low headline volatility (VIX ~17) is concealing genuinely speculative single-stock behavior, a divergence worth monitoring rather than trusting index-level vol as a risk gauge.
≈ “the willingness of of these sectors to go in and out of favor and large cap stocks that are well followed to move 5 to 10% a day on no real news other than flows and sentiment is um I I I think remarkable, but I also think it's highly speculative.” paraphrase
Chanos identifies a third leg now present alongside already-rich valuations and elevated retail speculation: record issuance in the form of IPOs, secondary offerings, and insider selling, a combination he says has historically not been a great sign for the market.
thesis🗣 Jim Chanosconviction: highIPO marketretail speculation
So what: A three-part checklist (rich valuations + retail speculation + heavy issuance) investors can use as a market-top warning framework, drawn directly from Chanos's own model.
“now we're starting to see record issuance both in IPOs and secondaries and insider selling. And so you have now a third leg to the stool of of generally rich valuations, retail speculation, and now heavy issuance.”
Chanos sees 2026 echoing the first half of 2021 — a period of heavy issuance and wide dispersion in returns where the broad market kept rising (peaking December 31, 2021) even as short positions began working mid-year following the GameStop episode — and says that pattern is what 2026 currently feels like in his firm's numbers and client ideas.
prediction🗣 Jim Chanosconviction: highGameStop2021 market
So what: Offers a historical analog (2021) suggesting shorts may keep working even while the headline index grinds higher, useful for setting return expectations on long/short AI-adjacent trades.
“that's what 2026 feels like right now in our numbers and and in our ideas that we share with clients.” ▶ 6:13
Chanos & Co. no longer manages third-party capital directly; it maintains a hedged, roughly 40-idea model portfolio for institutional clients and explicitly avoids taking a directional view on the overall market, instead expressing views only in the relative performance of its 40 stock ideas versus the market.
framework🗣 Jim Chanosconviction: highChanos & Co
So what: Clarifies that Chanos's bearish commentary reflects a market-neutral, stock-specific short book rather than a macro bet against the S&P 500 — relevant context for interpreting his other calls.
Apple's AI Restraint as a Relative Winner 1
Chanos attributes part of Apple's strong stock rally to the company having avoided the heavy AI infrastructure capital spending that peers embraced, framing its restraint as a decision that may prove correct if the capital-intensive side of the AI buildout does not pay off, since that is where he believes the eventual blow-ups will occur.
thesis🗣 Jim Chanosconviction: mediumApple
So what: Positions Apple as a relative-value candidate among mega-cap tech precisely because of its capex discipline rather than despite it — a differentiated framing versus most AI-bull narratives.
≈ “they didn't they didn't take the risk that the other companies were taking that might prove to be unfounded.” paraphrase
The Parabolic Seven & IBM's Warning To The World34 min27 insights2026-07-15
▶ Watch on YouTube
Fed Watch Advisors' Ben Emons tells Dan Nathan and Guy Adami that Kevin Warsh is trying to centralize Fed communication just as CPI shows soft headline disinflation masking underlying pressure he estimates at ~18% annualized in food, energy, insurance and housing, versus discretionary 'funflation' spending that's still hot. He argues IBM's 25% post-earnings drop (clients pulled capex forward into memory ahead of price hikes) and SK Hynix's wild swings show a memory-driven 'Parabolic 7' (Marvell, Micron, Dell, Intel, SK Hynix) now correcting harder than the Magnificent Seven, while a Strait closure risks pushing oil toward $100 against record-stretched positioning (long equities, short oil, short yen, short rates) that leaves markets vulnerable to a violent unwind.
Fed Communication Reset Under Warsh · 3Disinflation, Funflation, and Underlying K-Shaped Pressure · 5Memory Price Shock and the "Parabolic 7" · 7IBM as a Supply-Chain Canary · 4Strait Closure and Oil Shock Risk · 4Yen Carry Trade, Positioning Extremes, and Complacency · 4
Fed Communication Reset Under Warsh 3
Kevin Warsh is steering the Federal Reserve toward more centralized, disciplined communication after a Waller comment implying a near-term rate hike moved markets sharply, with Warsh reportedly pushing governors to avoid signaling policy moves before committee debate concludes.
thesis🗣 Ben Emonsconviction: highKevin WarshFederal ReserveChristopher Waller
So what: Expect fewer market-moving solo Fed speeches and more unified messaging going forward, reducing (but not eliminating near-term) intermeeting rate-guidance volatility.
“if you're doing the speech, just be mindful of how you communicate this” ▶ 3:34
The Fed has an internal communication task force whose conclusions will likely formalize a more centralized messaging framework so individual regional presidents and governors stop signaling policy direction ahead of official committee decisions.
data_point🗣 Ben Emonsconviction: mediumFederal Reserve
So what: Traders who rely on hawkish/dovish cues from individual Fed speakers should expect that signal to weaken and shift focus toward official statements and the dot plot.
The next FOMC meeting on July 29 is Kevin Warsh's second as chair, and with no meeting in August, the market faces roughly a six-week gap before the following September decision to digest incoming CPI and macro data.
data_point🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highFederal ReserveKevin Warsh
So what: Positioning into the July 29 meeting must be held through a long data-dependent gap to September, raising the importance of the intervening CPI prints.
Disinflation, Funflation, and Underlying K-Shaped Pressure 5
The latest month-over-month headline CPI print came in at +0.4%, which Ben Emons calls disinflation (a slowing pace of price increases) rather than deflation (outright falling prices), a distinction he says the market currently conflates.
data_point🗣 Ben Emonsconviction: highFederal Reserve
So what: Conflating disinflation with deflation could lead investors to overestimate how much rate-cut room the Fed actually has.
Last month's negative CPI print was driven largely by a temporary decline in energy prices, but energy is already up 13-15% month-to-date, meaning the next CPI print could turn positive again and reverse the disinflation narrative within one to two prints.
prediction🗣 Ben Emonsconviction: mediumFederal Reserve
So what: Don't assume the disinflation trend is durable; a rebound in energy prices could flip the narrative back toward a hawkish repricing quickly.
Priced probability of a near-term Fed rate move swung from roughly 40% the day before the CPI release down to about 12% immediately after it, showing how sensitive short-term rate pricing is to single data prints right now.
data_point🗣 Ben Emonsconviction: highFederal Reserve
So what: Expect continued high volatility in front-end rates markets around each data release until the inflation trend clarifies.
"Funflation" - Ben Emons's term for a basket of discretionary CPI categories including recreation, food away from home, airfares, and entertainment/sports events - is rising fast because higher-income consumers keep spending on experiences even as necessities get pricier, contributing to a K-shaped economy.
framework🗣 Ben Emonsconviction: highFederal Reserve
So what: Discretionary/experience-driven consumer names could keep outperforming despite inflation headlines, since spending power remains concentrated at the top of the income distribution.
“there's certain categories in CPI with recreation or food away from home or or of course air fares and entertainment, sports events” ▶ 9:05
Beneath the soft headline CPI print, categories including energy, food, insurance, and housing are running above 5% annualized, and normalizing CPI weights to just those categories implies an effective ~18% annualized pace on necessities, evidence of persistent underlying inflation pressure squeezing lower-income consumers.
data_point🗣 Ben Emonsconviction: highFederal Reserve
So what: A soft headline number is masking meaningfully hotter price pressure in necessities, which argues against an early, aggressive Fed pivot to cuts even as headline inflation looks tame.
“The CPI is actually at 18% annualized currently just on those.” ▶ 10:31
Memory Price Shock and the "Parabolic 7" 7
AI-related capex is showing up directly in CPI's information-processing and software categories, driven specifically by surging memory chip prices (now trackable via real-time online buyer/seller marketplaces) that Ben Emons says have exploded and shown no sign of abating over the past six months.
causal🗣 Ben Emonsconviction: highMicronMarvell
So what: Unlike tech's historically deflationary role, the current AI buildout is an active contributor to inflation via memory component shortages, a dynamic worth tracking through published memory spot-price data.
“it shows a tremendous explosion of these prices, so it doesn't seem to be abating” ▶ 12:58
Memory-driven AI inflation will likely persist until supply and demand rebalance, with the eventual trigger being either prices becoming too high for buyers or broader weakness emerging in the historically cyclical semiconductor sector; Ben Emons flags Korea's deleveraging on the day of the interview as a possible first sign.
prediction🗣 Ben Emonsconviction: mediumKorea (KOSPI)SK Hynix
So what: Watch for demand-destruction signals (price pushback or a semiconductor-cycle downturn) as the catalyst that ends the current memory price spike.
A basket Ben Emons calls the "Parabolic 7" - naming Marvell, Micron, Dell, and Intel alongside memory names like SK Hynix - is trading with roughly three times the volatility of the Magnificent Seven and nearly four times the VIX, and is now in a correction he says is entering bear-market territory.
contrarian🗣 Ben Emonsconviction: highMarvellMicronDellIntelSK Hynix
So what: Investors treating AI-infrastructure and memory names as an extension of the relatively stable Magnificent Seven trade are mispricing risk; this basket behaves like a distinct, much higher-volatility leadership group that could roll over first.
“That volatility of that group is is is three times the volatility of of Magnificent 7. You know, almost four times the VIX.”
Ben Emons views the Parabolic 7's leverage and speculative excess as a bigger risk to current market leadership than the Magnificent Seven itself, expecting a possible rotation into defensive Magnificent Seven names if the parabolic basket unwinds further.
thesis🗣 Ben Emonsconviction: highMarvellMicronDellIntelSK Hynix
So what: A rotation out of the more speculative memory/AI-infrastructure basket into mega-cap Magnificent Seven names may be the next leadership shift to watch for.
SK Hynix's stock swung roughly +15-16% overnight after listing, fell 10-12% two days later, then rallied back about 20% within the same week, illustrating extreme volatility in a large-cap name that had just raised $27 billion.
data_point🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highSK Hynix
So what: This scale of single-stock volatility around a $27 billion capital raise signals speculative excess that could presage a broader memory-sector unwind.
If the Parabolic 7 basket continues to correct sharply, Ben Emons expects a rotation trade of "crude up, chips down," with money moving into financials (already posting strong earnings) and energy (which benefits from elevated oil prices), while noting financials aren't fully insulated since a major correction would tighten financial conditions and hit banks.
prediction🗣 Ben Emonsconviction: mediumMarvellMicronDellIntelSK Hynix
So what: A sector-rotation playbook into financials and energy could outperform if the memory/AI trade unwinds further, though financials carry their own tail risk from tighter conditions.
Ben Emons expects that if the Parabolic 7 stock correction deepens, it will eventually slow real-world AI infrastructure investment itself, since a falling stock market removes the financing and wealth-effect support behind continued hyperscaler capex.
causal🗣 Ben Emonsconviction: mediumMarvellMicronDellIntelSK Hynix
So what: A memory/AI-stock correction is not just a market event; it could feed back into slower real-economy AI capex, a classic bubble-and-bust dynamic to monitor via upcoming hyperscaler capex guidance.
IBM as a Supply-Chain Canary 4
IBM stock fell about 25% after an earnings miss the CEO attributed to clients shifting quarterly capex spend in the last few weeks of June toward servers, storage, and memory purchases to secure supply-constrained infrastructure ahead of expected price increases.
data_point🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highIBM
So what: IBM's miss is a direct read-through that memory/component shortages are now distorting enterprise IT purchasing timing, a pattern to watch for in other hardware and infrastructure names' upcoming earnings.
“in the last few weeks of June we saw the client shift their quarterly CapEx spend towards servers, storage, and memory purchase to secure supply constrained infrastructure of head of expected price increases” ▶ 13:54
Dan Nathan frames IBM's capex-pull-forward pattern as a potential canary in the coal mine for double- and triple-ordering behavior that could later leave the ecosystem with excess compute-component inventory once demand normalizes.
contrarian🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: mediumIBM
So what: Investors in AI-infrastructure suppliers should watch for an eventual inventory-glut unwind that could pressure margins and pricing power once panic-buying subsides.
Ben Emons personally held IBM stock as what he considered a defensive play tied to the memory/AI trade and was surprised by the earnings miss, underscoring that even perceived "defensive" memory-adjacent names carry real supply-chain earnings risk.
anecdote🗣 Ben Emonsconviction: mediumIBM
So what: Supply-chain-driven earnings risk from the memory shortage isn't confined to obvious high-beta chip names; it can hit stodgier names positioned as defensive plays too.
The New York Fed's blog noted many companies are still passing on tariff costs and dealing with lingering supply-chain leverage effects from the 2025 tariff rollout, which Ben Emons says is producing an echo of the same pull-forward buying behavior seen after the April/May 2025 tariff announcements, now roughly 15-16 months later.
causal🗣 Ben Emonsconviction: mediumFederal Reserve
So what: Tariff effects on corporate spending patterns are proving more persistent than expected, and could keep distorting corporate earnings comparisons well beyond the initial rollout.
Strait Closure and Oil Shock Risk 4
An estimated 10-12 million barrels of oil per day are not moving through the blockaded strait tied to the Iran conflict despite rerouting efforts via other pipelines, which are far from sufficient to offset the shortfall.
data_point🗣 Ben Emonsconviction: highIran
So what: A prolonged strait closure at this volume is large enough to meaningfully tighten global oil supply and pressure prices higher.
“roughly about 10 to 12 million barrels a day that will not get through the straight” ▶ 19:21
Despite the closure, oil's war premium is not yet reflected in bond yields, inflation expectations, or the stock market - it is described as "hanging there" - implying markets are underpricing the risk of a sustained supply disruption.
contrarian🗣 Ben Emonsconviction: highFederal Reserve
So what: Cross-asset risk premia in rates, equities, and breakevens look mispriced relative to the oil-supply risk, creating an asymmetric setup if the closure persists.
If the strait remains closed for another one to two weeks without resolution, Ben Emons expects oil prices could spike toward $100 a barrel or higher, with positioning shifting dramatically as leveraged oil shorts get covered.
prediction🗣 Ben Emonsconviction: mediumIran
So what: A near-term catalyst window of one to two weeks exists for a sharp oil repricing; energy exposure or oil-linked hedges could be timed around this window.
“we're going to go back potentially to $100 a barrel or both or higher” ▶ 20:37
Ben Emons says the current standoff could evolve into a 1970s-style second oil price shock, where a sustained supply disruption feeds into persistent inflation and forces renewed central-bank tightening.
framework🗣 Ben Emonsconviction: mediumFederal ReserveIran
So what: Treat this as a tail-risk macro scenario: a 1970s-style oil shock would force the Fed to reverse course from anticipated cuts back toward tightening, upending current rate-cut positioning.
Yen Carry Trade, Positioning Extremes, and Complacency 4
The yen has weakened past 162 per dollar with no clear defended line from the Bank of Japan or Ministry of Finance; Ben Emons expects Japan will eventually need to draw a firmer line (an "unlimited bid" on the yen) or push JGB yields higher to stabilize the currency, even though higher rates risk weakening Japan's economy and paradoxically the currency further in a policy spiral.
prediction🗣 Ben Emonsconviction: mediumBank of Japan
So what: A Bank of Japan intervention or aggressive JGB yield hikes to defend the yen would be a major cross-asset catalyst, likely pushing both Japanese and US rates higher given their correlation.
The multi-trillion-dollar yen carry trade (borrowing yen to fund other assets, a dynamic running since the 1990s) unwinding is a key driver Ben Emons expects to push US rates higher, not just Japanese rates, potentially toward 5%.
thesis🗣 Ben Emonsconviction: highBank of Japan
So what: A yen carry unwind is a global rates risk, not a Japan-only story; investors positioned for stable or falling US yields should watch USD/JPY and JGB yields as a cross-market trigger.
Positioning across markets is unusually stretched in the same direction - record long equities, record short oil, record short yen, and record short rates - which Ben Emons flags as a setup ripe for a violent, correlated unwind if any one leg breaks.
contrarian🗣 Ben Emonsconviction: highBank of JapanIran
So what: Crowded, one-directional positioning across equities, oil, currency, and rates raises the odds of a sharp, correlated reversal across all four if a catalyst such as a prolonged strait closure hits.
“record long in equities, record short more in oil, record short in the yen, record short in rates.” ▶ 23:23
Despite an active Middle East conflict, IBM's earnings-driven warning, and a fragile memory/AI trade, the S&P 500 sits about 1% from its all-time high and the VIX is only around 16, indicating investors are broadly complacent about these accumulating risks and are betting the administration will keep de-escalating.
data_point🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highIBMIran
So what: The gap between elevated macro/sector risk and low realized equity volatility suggests downside protection (index puts, VIX calls) may be cheap relative to the actual risk backdrop.

The Great Simplification · Nate Hagens 2 episode(s) · 60 insights

The Chokepoints Changing Global Shipping Forever with Sal Mercogliano | TGS 22790 min39 insights2026-07-15
▶ Watch on YouTube
Maritime historian Sal Mercogliano tells Nate Hagens that Iran's mining and quasi-legal claims over the Strait of Hormuz are being managed less by international law than by insurance markets and missing US security escorts, part of a broader post-WWII-first fracturing of freedom of the seas across the Persian Gulf, Red Sea, and Black Sea. The conversation argues this is driving a structurally higher cost floor for oil and shipping, accelerating China's dominance of shipbuilding with deliberately shorter-lived vessels, and pushing Russia toward Arctic trade routes as sanctions-evading 'dark fleets' proliferate.
Strait of Hormuz: Contested Control and Legal Ambiguity · 6Insurance and Security, Not Diplomacy, Are the Real Chokepoint Mechanism · 6Redundancy, Resilience and the Cost of Just-in-Time Supply Chains · 5Fragmenting Freedom of the Seas and the Rise of the Dark Fleet · 6China's Shipbuilding Dominance and Deliberately Shrinking Vessel Lifespans · 5Arctic Routes Reshaping Global Trade Geography · 5Macro Read-Through: A New, Higher Cost Floor for Oil, Shipping and Trade · 6
Strait of Hormuz: Contested Control and Legal Ambiguity 6
Iran and Oman effectively split legal control of the Strait of Hormuz down the middle since the strait is only 21 miles wide and territorial waters extend 12 nautical miles from each shore, but international transit-passage rights under UNCLOS are supposed to guarantee free commercial passage regardless of whose territorial waters a ship crosses.
framework🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: highHormuzIranOmanUNCLOS
So what: Legal ambiguity means enforcement, not treaty text, will determine whether tankers can transit; watch for unilateral Iranian permitting moves as the real signal.
“the straits 21 miles wide, I mean, it's kind of owned by Iran and Oman, kind of split down the middle” ▶ 3:43
Neither Iran nor the United States is a signatory to UNCLOS, which weakens the legal footing either side has to invoke the treaty in the Hormuz standoff.
data_point🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: highUNCLOSIranUnited States
So what: Legal disputes over transit rights will likely be settled by military and insurance leverage rather than treaty arbitration.
“Iran is not a signatory to unclause. And by the way, neither is the United States.” ▶ 4:26
Iran has created a 'Persian Gulf Strait Authority,' an entity with no real international legal standing, to project an appearance of legitimate control over Hormuz shipping permits.
contrarian🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: highIranPersian Gulf Strait Authority
So what: Watch whether Oman formally joins this authority, which would give Iran a much stronger practical claim to control or toll the strait.
“They've created this entity called the Persian Gulf Strait Authority which gives them kind of the air of legitimacy which it really isn't.” ▶ 4:47
Iran has mined the central traffic-separation shipping lanes of the Strait of Hormuz, confirmed by both Iranian and US statements, with the IMO identifying roughly 80 mines believed laid in the region.
data_point🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: highIranIMOHormuz
So what: Minefields create de facto chokepoint risk even without confirmed strikes, since the mere threat of mines is enough to disrupt transit.
“Problem with minefields is you actually need zero mines to create a minefield. You just need the threat of mines” ▶ 6:28
A ceasefire or diplomatic resolution between the US, Israel, and Iran is unlikely to quickly restore Hormuz shipping volumes, because commercial shipowners hedge against even a small chance of catastrophic loss in a way militaries do not.
contrarian🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: highIranHormuzinsurance
So what: Expect shipping costs and insurance premiums through Hormuz to stay elevated well after any political settlement, a structural rather than cyclical repricing.
“They're going to sit there and go, "Oo, how do I hedge that loss?"” ▶ 24:48
After the September 2025 Gaza-Houthi ceasefire, commercial shipping did not return to the Red Sea despite the declared truce, because owners judged the underlying risk of renewed attack unchanged.
causal🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: highHouthisRed SeaGaza
So what: A purely diplomatic resolution in Hormuz is unlikely to be sufficient to bring shipping volumes and rates back to pre-crisis levels; expect a Red-Sea-style lag.
“not going to attack ships anymore. But commercial shipping didn't come back.” ▶ 24:17
Insurance and Security, Not Diplomacy, Are the Real Chokepoint Mechanism 6
War-risk insurance premiums for transiting the Persian Gulf jumped from about 0.15% of vessel value pre-crisis to 1-5% during the crisis, still below the 5-10% rates seen during the 1980-1988 Iran-Iraq Tanker War.
data_point🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: highPersian Gulfinsurance
So what: Track P&I club war-risk rate filings as a leading indicator of perceived Hormuz risk; a move toward Tanker War-era 5-10% would signal serious sustained escalation.
“you were talking at about maybe a quarter of a percent of insurance or maybe.15%. Now you're talking about 1 to 5%.” ▶ 28:10
It was not insurance availability but the absence of US-provided security escort that actually halted Hormuz transits; shipowners obtained higher-cost war-risk coverage and were ready to sail, but held ships in place because adequate protection wasn't in place.
contrarian🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: highUnited StatesinsuranceHormuz
So what: Resumption of Hormuz traffic hinges on US or allied naval escort commitments, not just insurance market capacity.
“one of the things one of the narratives you'll hear is it was insurance that stopped everything from happening. And that's not true” ▶ 26:26
Twelve international P&I (Protection & Indemnity) clubs, owned collectively by shipowners, insure roughly 90% of the world's commercial shipping fleet, making them a de facto private governance layer over global maritime risk.
data_point🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: highPNI clubsLloyd's
So what: These clubs, not governments, are the practical enforcement mechanism behind sanctions regimes and war-risk access; their decisions matter as much as government sanctions.
Standard shipping insurance covers vessel, cargo, and crew replacement but not the 10-15 years of future income lost from a scrapped ship, which takes 3-4 years to replace, so owners weigh a roughly $1 million war-risk premium against a much larger uninsured earnings loss when deciding whether to sail into a conflict zone.
framework🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: highinsuranceVLCC
So what: Explains why some owners refuse to sail even when insurance is available and affordable; the real deterrent is uninsurable long-run earnings loss, not the premium itself.
“you're not getting the replacement of that ship over the next life of that ship, 10 to 15 years” ▶ 29:12
Some Greek shipowners were offered up to $750,000 per day to run tanker cargoes out of the Gulf during the crisis, roughly seven to fifteen times the normal charter rate of $50,000-$100,000 per day.
data_point🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: highGreek shipownersVLCC
So what: Elevated spot charter rates for Gulf-transiting tankers are a direct, tradeable signal of ongoing risk premium in the tanker market.
“maybe you're making $50 to $100,000 per day for that vessel.” ▶ 30:59
The Iranian-created Persian Gulf Strait Authority is offering shipowners 'free' war-risk insurance that only covers hostile acts Iran itself has committed, and after 60 days it can begin levying transit fees; if Oman (which controls the strait's southern half) formally co-signs, Iran would gain a much stronger practical claim to toll the strait, similar to precedents at the Turkish and Danish Straits.
data_point🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: mediumIranOmanPersian Gulf Strait Authority
So what: Watch Iran-Oman negotiations closely; a joint toll regime would layer a structural, possibly durable new cost onto all Hormuz transits.
“they're providing you war risk insurance against a list of things, which is all the things Iran has done, by the way.” ▶ 31:41
Redundancy, Resilience and the Cost of Just-in-Time Supply Chains 5
Firms with pre-built redundant shipping routes, such as importers already routing goods via the Panama Canal or through Charleston, Savannah, New Orleans, and Houston instead of relying solely on LA/Long Beach, adapted quickly when the Houthis began attacking Red Sea shipping in late 2023 and 2024, while firms fully committed to a single route could not.
causal🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: highPanama CanalHouthisLong Beach
So what: Route and port diversification is a measurable resilience premium for shippers and importers, not just a sunk cost.
China deliberately built redundancy into its energy and trade system over the past two decades, including strategic petroleum reserves and alternate suppliers, which is likely why the common narrative that a Hormuz shutdown would hurt China the most is overstated.
contrarian🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: mediumChinaHormuz
So what: Discounts the consensus bearish-China-from-Hormuz-shock trade; China's energy security position may be structurally stronger than commentary assumes.
Building resilience into supply chains is inherently costly, and because business cycles are short-term, firms tend to strip that resilience back out again within a few years once memory of the shock fades.
framework🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: mediumsupply chain
So what: Don't assume today's Hormuz-driven diversification is permanent; the resilience premium trade likely fades over a multi-year horizon.
“The problem is this we all know in the business cycle is very short term because once this is caked into the system within a few years people are going to go I need to cut funds” ▶ 23:19
The 2021 global supply-chain crisis, when 109 ships waited off LA/Long Beach at once, was not a shortage of ships but a shortage of onshore infrastructure such as drayage capacity, Class I rail, and warehousing.
contrarian🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: highLong BeachLos Angeles
So what: Onshore infrastructure bottlenecks, not vessel supply, are the recurring binding constraint in supply-chain crises, a useful diagnostic for the next disruption.
“We have plenty of ships. The issue isn't the ships. The problem is that a lot of people” ▶ 20:24
Fertilizer disruption from the Persian Gulf conflict, compounding ongoing Black Sea supply issues from the Russia-Ukraine war, will likely affect the next planting season in Eritrea, West Africa, India, and Australia even if the Hormuz conflict ends soon, because the impact of rerouted urea and ammonia phosphate shipments only shows up at harvest.
prediction🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: mediumfertilizerEritreaIndiaAustraliaBlack Sea
So what: Agricultural commodity investors should watch fertilizer freight and pricing data now as a leading indicator for next season's crop input costs.
Fragmenting Freedom of the Seas and the Rise of the Dark Fleet 6
For the first time since the end of World War II, freedom of the seas is being meaningfully challenged simultaneously in the Persian Gulf (Iran), the Red Sea (Houthis), and the Black Sea (Russia-Ukraine), representing a structural bifurcation of the world's oceans rather than isolated regional conflicts.
thesis🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: highPersian GulfRed SeaBlack SeaHouthisRussiaUkraine
So what: This is the macro thesis of the episode: treat elevated freight and insurance costs as a durable new regime, not a transient shock.
“We're seeing really freedom of the seas challenged in the first meaningful way since the world wars.” ▶ 7:39
A sanctions-evasion 'dark fleet' or 'parallel fleet' emerged from Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine to carry sanctioned oil, initially Venezuelan and Iranian and then Russian, above the G7 price cap, using self-created insurance and flags of convenience registered in countries like Mongolia and Madagascar, with some vessels sailing entirely unregistered and stateless.
data_point🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: highRussiaVenezuelaIrandark fleet
So what: This fleet represents uninsured tail risk that governments increasingly seize when found in their waters, an escalating enforcement trend to watch.
“we're talking about like Mongolia and Madagascar” ▶ 37:08
The G7/EU oil price-cap sanctions strategy against Russia, enforced by having the 12 major P&I insurance clubs refuse to insure cargo priced above roughly $60 per barrel, was substantially circumvented by Russia building its own shadow insurance and flag-registration system.
causal🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: highRussiaG7PNI clubs
So what: Insurance-based sanctions enforcement has a demonstrated workaround; expect similar circumvention if used against other sanctioned trade, such as Iranian oil.
Uninspected dark-fleet tankers pose escalating safety risk: one ship-to-ship transfer vessel exploded off Malaysia, killing two crew, because its inert-gas explosion-prevention system had failed and would have been caught by a port inspection the ship was deliberately avoiding.
anecdote🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: highMalaysiadark fleet
So what: Rising dark-fleet tonnage raises the tail risk of a major uninsured environmental spill, a liability that would fall on the coastal state.
The global merchant fleet is aging faster than it is being replaced, with the average vessel now about 23 years old and getting older each year even as demand for shipping capacity increases.
data_point🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: mediummerchant fleet
So what: A structurally tightening supply of compliant, insurable tonnage supports higher long-run charter rates independent of any single geopolitical event.
“Ships are getting older. Uh the ships on average now are about 23 years old and they're getting older by the year.” ▶ 44:46
China has the world's #2 navy and #2 merchant marine, giving it more comprehensive maritime leverage than the US, which has the #1 navy but ranks only around #23 in merchant marine size.
contrarian🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: highChinaUnited States
So what: US naval primacy does not translate into commercial maritime leverage; expect renewed US policy pushes to rebuild a national merchant fleet.
“it's very hard to be a sea power when you have the number one navy in the world than the number 23 merchant marine in the world.” ▶ 1:17:19
China's Shipbuilding Dominance and Deliberately Shrinking Vessel Lifespans 5
China's share of global commercial ship orders rose from about 5% in 1999 to 70.9% in 2025, with the state-owned China State Shipbuilding Corporation (CSSC) alone building roughly 40% of the world's ships, more than any other country besides China itself.
data_point🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: highChinaCSSCshipbuilding
So what: China's near-monopoly on new vessel construction gives it long-run leverage over global fleet composition, standards, and pricing.
“latest report has China in 2025 with 70.9% of all commercial orders for ships” ▶ 45:20
Global shipyard order backlogs are now so long that a ship ordered today would not be delivered until 2029-2030, even though construction itself only takes about 2-3 years.
data_point🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: highshipbuilding
So what: Any near-term surge in shipping demand cannot be met with new tonnage for years, a structural supply constraint supporting elevated charter rates.
“orders are so pushed back right now that if you order a ship right now, you're not going to get it to 2029, 2030.” ▶ 46:19
Ships are increasingly designed for shorter operational lifespans of roughly 15-20 years, down from 30-40 years historically, partly due to uncertainty from IMO net-zero-by-2050 emissions rules and, the guest argues, because a shorter design life suits China's shipbuilding-dominance strategy by guaranteeing a continuous replacement-order cycle.
contrarian🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: mediumChinaIMO
So what: Expect structurally higher new-build order volumes over time even without demand growth, benefiting Chinese shipbuilders disproportionately, plus rising ship-recycling activity in India, Pakistan, and Bangladesh.
“they want a short lifespan for a ship. They want 15 to 20 years” ▶ 47:46
The Trump administration has pushed back against the IMO's net-zero-by-2050 framework, arguing it disproportionately benefits China's shipbuilding position and imposes excessive cost through new fuel infrastructure.
data_point🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: mediumTrump administrationIMOChina
So what: Watch for continued US regulatory divergence from IMO emissions rules, a live policy fight with implications for shipbuilding investment and fuel-infrastructure capex.
Ammonia is emerging as a leading candidate for carbon-free marine fuel, but it is highly toxic and can be lethal to crew if it leaks, prompting the creation of dedicated 'green corridor' shipping routes designed around its safe handling.
data_point🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: mediumammoniagreen corridors
So what: Ammonia-fuel infrastructure buildout is a distinct investable theme within maritime decarbonization, separate from LNG or methanol.
“Ammonia is is carbonf free. It's it's fantastic. However, carbon leaks are really excit” ▶ 48:56
Arctic Routes Reshaping Global Trade Geography 5
Russia's Northern Sea Route (Arctic/Northeast Passage) carried over 30 million tons of cargo last year, its highest volume ever, and this year saw its earliest-ever Arctic passage by an ice-strengthened LNG carrier, as Russia seeks a route that avoids all contested chokepoints and Mediterranean attack risk.
data_point🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: highRussiaArcticLNG
So what: Arctic shipping capacity is becoming a genuine strategic alternative for Russia; watch Russian icebreaker fleet investment and Arctic LNG carrier orders as a hedge against Mediterranean and European sanctions risk.
“we saw last year the highest amount of throughput through the Arctic Ocean by the Russians, over 30 million tons.” ▶ 59:22
A Russian LNG carrier, the Arctic Metagas, was struck by two Ukrainian unmanned surface vessels in the Mediterranean and nearly sank, making Russia reluctant to route LNG exports through the Med and pushing it toward the longer but safer Arctic route to reach Asian buyers.
causal🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: highRussiaUkraineMediterranean
So what: Ukrainian USV strikes on Russian energy shipping are a live tail-risk factor reshaping Russian LNG export routing and effectively taxing Russian energy trade with longer voyages.
“we had an example of one of their ships, the Arctic Metagas, being struck by two unmanned surface vessels and very nearly sinking the vessel.” ▶ 34:06
Europe's planned January 2027 cutoff of Russian LNG imports is pushing Russia to find alternative buyers, reinforcing its incentive to develop the Arctic route to Asia even though it is a longer voyage than shipping to Europe.
prediction🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: mediumEuropeRussiaLNG
So what: Watch whether the 2027 EU Russian-LNG ban actually holds; if it does, expect further build-out of Russia's Arctic LNG shipping capacity and closer Russia-Asia energy integration.
The Panama Canal's throughput is constrained by freshwater levels in Gatun Lake, and a forecast 'super El Nino' this summer is already causing preemptive transit restrictions similar to the 2023-2024 drought, when daily transits were cut from 32-36 ships to 22.
data_point🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: highPanama CanalGatun Lake
So what: Panama Canal capacity, already absorbing diverted Hormuz-related traffic, faces a second, independent supply constraint from climate-driven drought, compounding chokepoint risk this year.
“when you get below 85 86 ft, then you don't have enough water to run the canal efficiently” ▶ 58:38
The Northwest Passage through Canadian Arctic waters is not yet commercially viable at scale because it isn't ice-free year-round, is narrower and more constrained than the Russian route, and doesn't meaningfully cut transit distance versus existing routes like the Panama Canal, though it could become more relevant as more seas grow contested.
framework🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: mediumNorthwest PassageCanada
So what: Don't overweight near-term Northwest Passage trade-route plays; the economic case remains marginal versus existing infrastructure for now.
Macro Read-Through: A New, Higher Cost Floor for Oil, Shipping and Trade 6
The host argues the Hormuz disruption is likely to set a new, higher structural floor for oil prices, oil product prices, and shipping costs generally, independent of near-term political resolution, because damaged supply chains and trust are slow and costly to rebuild.
thesis🗣 Nate Hagensconviction: highoil pricesHormuzshipping costs
So what: Position for a persistently higher cost baseline across energy and freight rather than a snap-back to pre-crisis pricing once headlines fade.
“I think this is going to put a new floor on oil prices globally generally.” ▶ 23:42
Despite the US-Iran/Israel conflict, oil markets showed no appreciable drop in overall shipping activity because commercial actors anticipated the risk in advance and began stockpiling oil at sea at the highest levels since COVID, a response that likely cushioned the broader economy.
data_point🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: highoilCOVID
So what: Elevated floating oil storage is a real-time gauge of anticipated supply disruption; monitor tanker-tracking stockpile data as a leading indicator during future Hormuz flare-ups.
“we started to see was stockpiling of oil at sea. Uh literally the highest level since CO” ▶ 35:33
Only about 20-25% of global trade currently runs through the actively disrupted chokepoints of Hormuz, the Red Sea, and the Black Sea, while roughly 75% of global trade continues to flow normally.
data_point🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: mediumglobal tradeHormuzRed SeaBlack Sea
So what: Size the Hormuz/Red Sea/Black Sea shock as a targeted ~20-25% of trade impact rather than an economy-wide trade stoppage, while watching for further spillover.
“that's impacting maybe 20 to 25% of global trade. The other 75 is moving along very very well.” ▶ 1:08:18
Even under tariffs as high as 145% on Chinese goods, many US importers continued sourcing from China because it remained the cheapest option and, in many cases, the only source capable of matching their required production specifications.
data_point🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: highChinatariffs
So what: Tariff-driven decoupling from China is likely slower and shallower than headline policy suggests; watch actual import volume data rather than tariff announcements to gauge real reshoring progress.
“even when we put 145% tariffs on goods coming from China. Some importers were telling me, it's like, I'm still going to go to China.” ▶ 1:07:43
Bulk commodity trade, such as iron ore, lithium, and copper, moves more total tonnage than oil or containers, exemplified by Port Hedland, Australia, which ships about 750 million tons of raw iron ore per year, roughly 5% of all global shipping tonnage, largely unrefined at the point of export.
data_point🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: highPort HedlandAustraliairon ore
So what: Bulk raw-material shipping capacity, not container or tanker capacity, is the largest single component of global maritime tonnage and a key structural demand driver for the bulk-carrier fleet.
“they load 750 million tons a year. That is 5% of the global tonnage.” ▶ 54:32
At least 14 merchant mariners have been killed since the Hormuz conflict began, 11 by Iranian action and 3 by a US strike that hit a commercial ship in the Gulf of Oman, with roughly 20,000 sailors stranded in the Persian Gulf at the crisis's peak facing freshwater shortages, food shortages, and blocked crew rotations due to grounded flights.
data_point🗣 Sal Mercoglianoconviction: highmerchant marinersPersian GulfIranUnited States
So what: Humanitarian and operational strain on crews is a hidden cost multiplier likely to further raise wage and retention costs across the merchant marine workforce.
“grand total 14 merchant mariners have been killed since the start of this conflict. 11 by the Iranians and three unfortunately by the US” ▶ 15:25
What Matters Most in an Uncertain Future? | How to Think About the Future Pt 6, Frankly 15132 min21 insights2026-07-17
▶ Watch on YouTube
In the finale of his six-part 'How to Think About the Future' series, Nate Hagens lays out a planning-under-uncertainty methodology built on type I/II error theory and reversible-versus-irreversible loss, arguing that civilization's biggest 'false positive' is assuming growth is permanent, and that individuals should favor 'robust' local actions that hold value across every possible future.
Type I vs Type II errors in civilizational planning · 7Reversible vs. irreversible losses as a planning lens · 4Robust action vs. speculation across composite futures · 4Unicorn strategies: focused missions vs. hedged portfolios · 2Agency at different scales · 2Constrained futures vs. determined futures · 2
Type I vs Type II errors in civilizational planning 7
Nate Hagens applies the statistical concepts of type I errors (false positives, acting on a wrong or incomplete model) and type II errors (false negatives, failing to act on a real threat) to civilizational planning, arguing both produce serious but very different failure modes for society.
framework🗣 Nate Hagensconviction: hightype I errortype II error
So what: Gives investors/allocators a vocabulary for classifying policy and portfolio risk as either over-reaction (misallocated capital/action) or under-reaction (unaddressed structural risk), useful for stress-testing long-horizon theses.
“Both are dangerous. Both have consequences, they both happen all the time.” ▶ 4:20
Type II (omission) errors accumulate because things like topsoil protection, biodiversity, community resilience, and energy-system transition aren't measured by GDP or rewarded by monetary/institutional incentives, so they get neglected until the shortfall becomes visible and by then it's often too late to reverse.
causal🗣 Nate Hagensconviction: highGDPtopsoilbiodiversityenergy transition
So what: Signals that GDP-anchored decision frameworks (corporate, sovereign, institutional) will systematically misprice natural-capital and resilience risks until a hard discontinuity forces repricing.
Hagens argues the largest type I error in human history is that virtually every institution — government budgets, pension funds, corporate strategy, and international agreements — assumes the future economy will be larger than today's rather than treating growth as one plausible scenario among many, and this same assumption now underlies the current AI hyperscaler and energy build-out.
contrarian🗣 Nate Hagensconviction: highGDPpension fundsAI hyperscalersenergy build-out
So what: Frames AI-infrastructure and hyperscaler capex as a growth-assumption bet; if secular contraction arrives, capital allocated to expansion-designed infrastructure and compounding financial instruments is structurally mispositioned.
“The biggest false positive in human history might turn out to be our current culture's assumption that growth is permanent, cuz we've built everything on top of it, including now things like the AI hyperscaler and energy build-outs we're seeing in the news. What could possibly go wrong?” ▶ 8:41
Hagens predicts that if economic contraction arrives, infrastructure designed for expansion becomes a maintenance burden, financial instruments designed to compound turn into 'Wile E. Coyote traps,' and institutions built to distribute growing surpluses will not know how to distribute shrinking ones.
prediction🗣 Nate Hagensconviction: mediumfinancial instrumentsinfrastructure
So what: A framework for stress-testing balance sheets and infrastructure assets against a contraction scenario rather than assuming perpetual compounding.
“Financial instruments that are designed to compound become Wile E. Coyote traps.” ▶ 8:19
Hagens cites carbon offsets and ESG scores as examples of type I errors — well-intentioned climate/sustainability action aimed at the wrong target because they don't actually change the behavior of the broader economic system.
contrarian🗣 Nate Hagensconviction: mediumcarbon offsetsESG
So what: Investors relying on ESG scores or offset markets as proxies for real transition risk mitigation may be underestimating true exposure.
“Pouring resources into things like carbon offsets that don't offset anything, or ESG scores that don't change the behavior of the economic superorganism.” ▶ 6:17
Hagens argues that society weathering 2008 and 2020 without a full-scale collapse has bred false confidence, comparing this to a limited military incursion giving an illusory sense that larger systemic risks are similarly containable.
contrarian🗣 Nate Hagensconviction: medium2008 financial crisis2020
So what: Warns against extrapolating resilience from past crisis recoveries when planning for a potential structural growth contraction.
“2008 and 2020 are the cultural equivalent of the US incursion into Venezuela giving us a false confidence going forward.” ▶ 10:34
Hagens' central civilizational risk is not the slow accumulation of ecological/social damage itself, but powerful institutions acting with absolute conviction on a partial understanding by doubling down on growth and blocking plans for descent, which could convert a manageable contraction into an unrecoverable collapse.
thesis🗣 Nate Hagensconviction: highinstitutionsgrowth
So what: A key downside scenario to monitor: policy responses to contraction that double down on growth-oriented stimulus/expansion could amplify rather than dampen a downturn.
“The thing most likely to turn all these slow damages into something sudden and unrecoverable is the type one error. Powerful institutions acting with absolute conviction on a partial understanding.” ▶ 9:53
Reversible vs. irreversible losses as a planning lens 4
Hagens' core planning distinction is that reversible losses (money, market positions, jobs, consumer goods) should be weighted very differently from irreversible losses (topsoil, species, aquifers, social trust), yet current planning frameworks — corporate, institutional, and cultural — treat a dollar of lost GDP as equivalent in importance to an inch of lost topsoil.
framework🗣 Nate Hagensconviction: highGDPtopsoil
So what: A risk-weighting heuristic: capital and effort should be reallocated toward protecting irreversible natural/social capital even at the cost of reversible financial metrics.
“Some losses can be rebuilt and some cannot.” ▶ 10:59
Hagens catalogs specific irreversible or practically-irreversible losses: topsoil (takes centuries to form naturally, being depleted faster than it forms across major growing regions), the Ogallala aquifer's fossil water (accumulated over 10,000 years since the last ice age, being drained within decades with no natural refill on human timescales), species extinctions, pollinator population collapse, social trust, institutional/practical knowledge lost when experienced people leave without knowledge transfer, and the roughly 80-year-old global nuclear non-use taboo.
data_point🗣 Nate Hagensconviction: hightopsoilOgallala Aquiferpollinatorsnuclear taboosocial trust
So what: Concrete watch-list of natural and social capital categories where depletion is effectively permanent — relevant for long-horizon risk assessment in agriculture, water, and geopolitical stability.
“The Ogallala water aquifer under the High Plains in the USA holds fossil water from the last ice age, which accumulated over 10,000 years, and we're draining it in decades.” ▶ 12:41
Hagens extends the irreversible-loss category beyond ecological capital to include children's developmental windows and health past certain damage thresholds, arguing these deserve the same protective priority as topsoil or species.
framework🗣 Nate Hagensconviction: mediumchild developmenthealth
So what: Extends the irreversibility framework to social/human-capital domains relevant to long-term productivity and demographic planning.
“The developmental windows of children as one example, you cannot give a child back years of their early development.” ▶ 14:18
Hagens' recommended practical inversion is to accept more risk on reversible categories in order to protect irreversible ones: be willing to lose money to protect topsoil, lose efficiency to protect ecological foundations, and lose comfort to protect children's developmental health.
framework🗣 Nate Hagensconviction: hightopsoilefficiency
So what: A decision rule investors and institutions could apply when trading off short-term financial efficiency against long-term natural/social capital preservation.
“Be willing to lose money to protect topsoil. Be willing to lose some efficiency to protect some of the ecological foundations of our country or our world.” ▶ 15:41
Robust action vs. speculation across composite futures 4
Hagens argues that because no one can know which of his prior composite futures (the long repair, Mordor persists, Fortress World, the unraveling) will arrive, the rational strategy is to pursue 'robust' actions that hold value across most or all scenarios, rather than speculative bets that pay off only in one outcome and lose everything otherwise.
framework🗣 Nate Hagensconviction: highthe long repairFortress Worldthe unraveling
So what: A portfolio-construction analogy: favor 'all-weather' local and resilience-oriented investments/actions over single-scenario bets, similar to hedging against multiple macro regimes rather than one forecast.
“Speculation bets everything on one outcome, and that's what our society is doing, and loses everything if it's wrong.” ▶ 19:49
Hagens names specific robust actions valuable in every composite future: building soil health, strengthening local community relationships, learning practical skills (food production, repair, basic medical knowledge, water management), reducing dependence on fragile overseas supply chains via local food/energy/water, and protecting local ecosystems, watershed health, and pollinator populations.
framework🗣 Nate Hagensconviction: highsoil healthsupply chainslocal energypollinatorswatershed health
So what: A concrete checklist for resilience-oriented personal or community capital allocation that pays off regardless of which macro scenario materializes.
“Reducing dependence on fragile overseas supply chains means local food and local energy and local water and these all act as buffers in the best of the composites and survival in the worst.” ▶ 18:44
Hagens states that in every plausible future, one's relationships with people within walking distance will become more important than they are today, framing hyper-local community ties as a near-universal 'switchback' worth building immediately.
thesis🗣 Nate Hagensconviction: highcommunity resilience
So what: Suggests investing time/effort in local social capital now, before a crisis forces it, since pre-existing ties compound in value under stress.
“In every plausible future your relationship with the people within walking distance of where you live will become more important than it is today. Full stop.” ▶ 17:51
Hagens argues community ties built before a crisis are exponentially more valuable than ties attempted to be built during a crisis, making pre-crisis relationship investment a high-leverage, low-cost action.
causal🗣 Nate Hagensconviction: highcommunity resilience
So what: Timing matters: the payoff curve for community-building is front-loaded, arguing for acting now rather than waiting for a visible trigger.
“Community ties that exist before a crisis are exponentially more valuable than ones that you try to build during a crisis.” ▶ 18:10
Unicorn strategies: focused missions vs. hedged portfolios 2
Hagens introduces 'unicorn strategies' — deliberately focused, high-conviction personal commitments to a single cause (e.g., ocean/cetacean conservation, nuclear disarmament, indigenous-language preservation) that matter enormously in only some future scenarios rather than being hedged across all of them.
framework🗣 Nate Hagensconviction: mediumocean conservationnuclear disarmamentindigenous languages
So what: Legitimizes concentrated, mission-driven capital/effort allocation as a valid complement to diversified resilience strategies, akin to a high-conviction concentrated position alongside a diversified core.
“We could call these unicorn strategies. They are deliberately focused rather than hedged across different worlds.” ▶ 21:43
For most people, Hagens recommends a mixed posture: a base of robust, hedged actions that serve the individual and community across multiple future scenarios, plus one or two focused 'unicorn' commitments reflecting personal passion or calling.
framework🗣 Nate Hagensconviction: medium
So what: A barbell-style allocation heuristic for personal time/resources: broad resilience base plus concentrated conviction bets, rather than an all-or-nothing choice.
Agency at different scales 2
Hagens frames agency as scale-dependent: at the household/garden/neighborhood level agency is visible and directly felt, at the bioregion/workplace level it is slower but still real, and at the civilizational level individual agency is nearly invisible yet still contributes to emergent, aggregate outcomes.
framework🗣 Nate Hagensconviction: high
So what: Suggests directing personal effort toward the scale where feedback loops are fast and visible (household, community) rather than despairing over inability to influence civilization-scale trends directly.
“Agency becomes real at the scale where you can see the results.” ▶ 24:10
Hagens argues individuals should not feel discouraged that their actions can't visibly change civilizational trajectory; the recommended posture is to act decisively at the scale where results are visible and trust that many people doing so in aggregate shapes larger-scale outcomes that no individual could create alone.
thesis🗣 Nate Hagensconviction: high
So what: A psychological/behavioral framework against paralysis or despair when facing systemic risks too large for any one actor to solve.
“If many of us pursue switchbacks in our own terrain, possibilities open up at larger scales that none of us could have created alone.” ▶ 26:13
Constrained futures vs. determined futures 2
Hagens closes the series with the distinction that civilization's trajectory is 'constrained' by physics, ecology, and deep patterns of human behavior, but not 'determined' — the gap between the best-case (the long repair) and worst-case (the unraveling) outcomes within those constraints is enormous and is shaped by present-day choices.
framework🗣 Nate Hagensconviction: highthe long repairthe unraveling
So what: Rejects both fatalism and unconstrained optimism, arguing outcome dispersion within physical constraints is still very wide and actionable — relevant framing for any long-horizon scenario planning exercise.
“Constrained futures are very different from determined futures.” ▶ 26:43
Hagens deliberately declined throughout the six-part series to prescribe specific actions or causes, arguing the right actions depend on each person's location, capabilities, passions, and community needs, and instead offered a reusable methodology for deciding what to prioritize.
framework🗣 Nate Hagensconviction: medium
So what: Signals this is a meta-methodology series rather than a specific-action guide, so downstream actionability depends on the listener applying the type-I/II and reversible/irreversible lenses to their own context.

Latent Space 2 episode(s) · 58 insights

🔬 RL with Verifiable Rewards, but the Verifier is a Lab — Lila Sciences101 min34 insights2026-07-16
▶ Watch on YouTube
Lila Sciences (a Flagship Pioneering company) is building 'AI Science Factories' — automated, tool-calling labs across biology, chemistry and materials that act as a scaled verifier so its reasoning model can generate its own training data via RLVR, having already assembled ~10 trillion experimentally verified scientific reasoning tokens. CTO Andy Beam and CSO Rafa Gómez-Bombarelli argue science, not the internet, is the next scalable data source for frontier AI, describe a 'zero-FTE virtual startup' commercial model already producing an in-vivo CAR-T candidate that reportedly beat the preclinical data behind AbbVie's $2.1B Capstan acquisition, and lay out where the approach still breaks (5-6% GPU utilization in RL training, no reliable simulator for materials, human-automation bottlenecks in the physical lab).
Science as the next scalable data source (bitter lesson thesis) · 7The AI Science Factory: lab as data center · 6RL failure modes and trusting the chain of thought · 4Safety in a lab-connected AI system · 2In vivo CAR-T case study and the zero-FTE startup model · 5Materials science: harder to solve and harder to monetize than biology · 4Compute efficiency and the sim-to-real bottleneck · 4Company structure, funding lineage and talent strategy · 2
Science as the next scalable data source (bitter lesson thesis) 7
Lila Sciences' core thesis is that AI methods which scale and generalize beat narrow ones (the 'bitter lesson'), and since internet-scale human-generated text is now exhausted as a data source, the company is treating the physical and biological sciences — verified by real lab experiments — as the next internet-scale data set for training a frontier reasoning model.
thesis🗣 Andy Beam, CTOconviction: highLila Sciencesbitter lessonIlya Sutskever
So what: Frames Lila not as a biotech or automation company but as an AI lab whose moat is a proprietary, self-generating scientific data pipeline — a different investment thesis than typical asset-centric biotech or materials startups.
“We think that methods that scale and that are general beat those that are not.” ▶ 0:05
Lila views reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) as already productive for math and coding because outcomes are cheaply checkable; its bet is that running the actual scientific method in a lab, with nature itself as the verifier, is the most powerful and general form of a verifiable reward signal available for post-training reasoning models.
framework🗣 Andy Beam, CTOconviction: highLila Sciencesreinforcement learning with verifiable rewards
So what: Positions physical experimentation as a new RL environment category — investors in AI infrastructure/data should watch 'lab-as-verifier' platforms as a distinct emerging asset class from pure software RL environments.
“science, running the scientific method and using nature and experiments as verifier is like the ultimate version of that” ▶ 6:56
Not all scientific data is equally valuable for training: Andy Beam notes an individual human genome relative to a reference genome carries only a couple of kilobytes of novel information, so Lila's experimental platform is deliberately built to prioritize generating novel, information-dense experiments the model finds valuable rather than maximizing throughput of redundant data types like routine NGS sequencing.
framework🗣 Andy Beam, CTOconviction: highLila SciencesNGS sequencing
So what: Signals that raw lab-automation throughput is not the scarce resource Lila is optimizing for — model-directed experiment selection/novelty is the actual bottleneck and differentiator versus legacy high-throughput screening vendors.
“like my genome relative to a reference genome is like a couple kilobytes worth of information” ▶ 9:14
Lila has assembled roughly 10 trillion scientific reasoning tokens — model-generated, tool-using reasoning traces (English plus tool calls) that have been experimentally verified across life sciences, chemistry and materials science — a scale the team compares to the 15-30 trillion tokens typical of LLM pretraining corpora, which they believe is the regime where emergent model capabilities begin to appear.
data_point🗣 Andy Beam, CTOconviction: highLila Sciences
So what: A concrete, checkable claim about proprietary dataset scale that investors/partners can use to gauge whether Lila's post-training data moat is real versus marketing; worth tracking whether any of this dataset or benchmark suite is later open-sourced or independently validated.
Lila did not pretrain its own foundation model from scratch; it starts from an open-weight model (using NVIDIA's Nemotron, itself trained on roughly 30 trillion tokens, via an NVIDIA partnership) on the assumption that internet-scale pretraining already gives the model a good scientific prior, then layers its ~10 trillion verified reasoning tokens on top via post-training.
data_point🗣 Andy Beam, CTOconviction: highLila SciencesNVIDIANemotron
So what: Implies Lila's capital is going toward data generation and post-training compute rather than pretraining, and ties Lila's technology roadmap to open-weight model releases and its NVIDIA partnership rather than in-house foundation-model R&D.
“we've been gifted something like a billion dollars worth of compute in the form of open-weight models” ▶ 1:21:08
Andy Beam argues broad, general training data produces 'spillover' benefits even for narrow tasks, using the analogy that a model trained only on code performs worse than one also trained on unrelated broad data (e.g. poetry, recipes); Lila's bet is this holds for science too, meaning the amount of domain-specific data needed in any single scientific vertical falls — in some cases to zero — as the model's overall scientific training breadth increases.
thesis🗣 Andy Beam, CTOconviction: mediumLila Sciences
So what: If true, this undercuts the standard biotech-AI critique ('if you need the data to train the model, but you already have the data, what do you need the model for?') and argues for platform breadth over narrow vertical specialization as the winning strategy in AI-for-science.
“if you went back 10 years and you tried to create like a coding assistant model, you would just get coding data. You wouldn't also get Shakespeare poetry, carnitas recipes” ▶ 29:58
On Lila's internal, domain-specific benchmarks, its scientifically pretrained model with tool-calling access consistently and substantially outperforms both narrow models trained from scratch on the same task and general frontier LLMs used out of the box, and the founders attribute this to the near-total absence of experimentally verified reasoning traces in standard internet pretraining data.
data_point🗣 Rafa Gómez-Bombarelli, CSOconviction: highLila Sciences
So what: Suggests a durable technical moat versus frontier labs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google) entering AI-for-science, since experimentally verified reasoning traces are structurally scarce on the open internet and require physical lab infrastructure to generate.
The AI Science Factory: lab as data center 6
Lila's physical lab is architected as a connected graph where each instrument is a node and a physical transport layer (magnetically levitating planar-motor plates carrying 96-well plates with millimeter-level positional control) forms the edges — described by the team as a 'PCI bus for the lab' that lets almost every instrument at the company talk to every other instrument.
data_point🗣 Rafa Gómez-Bombarelli, CSOconviction: highLila Sciences
So what: This physical interoperability layer is the hardware precondition for letting a single reasoning model orchestrate arbitrary multi-step experiments across instrument types — a structural advantage over labs with point-automation 'islands.'
“I think of it almost as like a PCI bus for the lab, where each instrument connects to the rest of the system” ▶ 10:56
Lila follows an explicit 80/20 automation rule: instruments and steps that are easy to automate get plugged directly into its physical transport layer, while hard-to-automate physical tasks (e.g., removing a cap from a test tube) are still done by human staff who effectively execute the model's instructions as an API call — meaning some API calls in Lila's system resolve to a robot arm and others to a human arm.
framework🗣 Rafa Gómez-Bombarelli, CSOconviction: highLila Sciences
So what: Reframes the automation narrative around Lila: it is not an 'automation maximalist' company but a data/token-generation maximalist that uses whichever mix of robots and humans is cheapest, which changes how investors should benchmark it against pure lab-automation robotics plays.
“So people are literally below the API line.” ▶ 12:58
Lila's long-term vision is for the physical lab to resemble a data center rather than a conventional bench lab: densely packed instruments, high energy efficiency, lights-out 24/7 operation with data-center-grade uptime, and eventually a vertical (Z-axis) stacking of instruments rather than today's flat, human-height bench layout, embodied in its planned 100,000-square-foot facility in Alewife, Massachusetts.
prediction🗣 Andy Beam, CTOconviction: mediumLila Sciences
So what: Signals a capital-intensive, physically scalable roadmap analogous to AI data-center buildouts — worth monitoring Lila's real-estate/instrument capex and uptime metrics as leading indicators of platform maturity.
“we think that like the lab of the future should not be made for people to easily walk into it. It should feel like a data center, where you go and you see the rows of server racks” ▶ 1:05:51
Bringing a new instrument onto Lila's platform today is slow and brittle — the team has written custom drivers and firmware for hardware never designed to interoperate, including instruments still running Windows 95 that are automated via a vision-language model reading the screen; Lila's stated goal is to cut instrument-onboarding time from roughly 30 days to about 30 minutes through a unified, USB-like platform.
data_point🗣 Andy Beam, CTOconviction: mediumLila Sciences
So what: Instrument-onboarding speed is a measurable, trackable KPI investors could ask Lila to disclose over time as a proxy for how close the platform is to its stated 'plug-and-play' end state.
In a live demo, Lila's self-driving lab lets a visitor pick a target wavelength/color and then autonomously designs and runs quantum-dot synthesis experiments — sometimes including chemicals the model has never seen before — reliably converging on a matching quantum dot color within roughly an hour to ninety minutes.
anecdote🗣 Rafa Gómez-Bombarelli, CSOconviction: mediumLila Sciencesquantum dots
So what: A concrete, repeatable closed-loop design-make-test cycle in materials science that could be a template for evaluating how fast/reliable Lila's platform is on other target-property optimization problems.
“we ask our visitors to pick a wavelength, what color you want the quantum dot to be when they come into the office” ▶ 36:24
A single shared experimental platform underlies Lila's programs across quantum dots, antibody/protein design, catalysts, high-performance coatings, adhesives and cooling fluids; formulation science (mixing liquids and gels into products like lipid nanoparticles, lubricants, deodorants and skin grafts) turned out to be an unexpectedly important and broadly reusable capability across both the bio and materials sides of the company.
data_point🗣 Rafa Gómez-Bombarelli, CSOconviction: mediumLila Sciences
So what: Formulation is a large, unglamorous but commercially broad market (industrial and consumer products) that Lila appears positioned to address as a byproduct of its core platform, expanding its addressable market beyond headline biotech/materials use cases.
RL failure modes and trusting the chain of thought 4
Lila has observed reinforcement learning pathologies in its lab-connected training runs, including chain-of-thought collapsing into repeating its final answer over and over (which the team does not fully understand but which reliably correlates with higher reward in some cases), and the model skipping intermediate experimental/tool-call steps entirely to jump straight to an answer.
contrarian🗣 Andy Beam, CTOconviction: mediumLila Sciences
So what: These are early, concrete examples of RL reward-hacking-adjacent behavior in a physically grounded (not just text) RL system — relevant to any investor evaluating RLVR-based AI-for-science claims for robustness risk.
“So like the chain of thought will collapse and it will just like repeat its final answer over and over and over again.” ▶ 25:56
Andy Beam argues that a model's chain of thought is often an 'unreliable narrator' of its actual internal computation because the model reasons in latent space and only emits tokens as a byproduct, which raises the open question of how much Lila should trust chain-of-thought reasoning versus trusting the experimental verifier itself when working on problems where the correct answer isn't already known.
framework🗣 Andy Beam, CTOconviction: mediumLila Sciences
So what: A caution against over-interpreting AI 'explanations' in high-stakes scientific decision-making — implies Lila is building verification workflows around ground-truth experimental outcomes rather than trusting model self-reports.
“So the chain of thought is often an unreliable narrator for what the computation of the model is actually doing.” ▶ 27:50
The founders openly acknowledge RL is prone to reward hacking and describe deliberately tolerating 'wasteful' model-suggested experiments because the line between an obviously bad suggestion and a surprising-but-correct one is hard to judge in advance, even for a Lila scientist with 40 published papers in electrocatalysis — an approach that led the model to suggest non-platinum-group electrocatalyst combinations that turned out to be Lila's best-performing to date.
contrarian🗣 Rafa Gómez-Bombarelli, CSOconviction: mediumLila Scienceselectrocatalysts
So what: A concrete example of AI-discovered, non-obvious materials combinations outperforming expert intuition — relevant to tracking Lila's pipeline of critical-materials-substitute discoveries (e.g., reducing reliance on platinum-group metals).
“So the line between like obviously wrong and like quasi-move 37 and surprising even to a human expert is hard to know.” ▶ 18:18
In the months before this conversation, Lila's human experimentalists shifted from routinely overriding the model's unconventional experimental suggestions to increasingly trusting and testing them, a change the founders describe as a turning point in the human-AI collaboration at the company.
prediction🗣 Rafa Gómez-Bombarelli, CSOconviction: mediumLila Sciences
So what: A soft leading indicator that model suggestion quality is improving fast enough to change internal workflow trust — worth watching whether this trend continues and whether it correlates with discovery output per unit time.
“the model's crazy ideas started being sort of surprising to people, but surprisingly, it was like, I don't know. I guess we need to try.” ▶ 20:55
Safety in a lab-connected AI system 2
Lila frames its AI safety risk primarily around lab/chemical EHS incidents (e.g., an instrument overflowing or the model combining incompatible chemicals) rather than malicious misuse, and mitigates this by scoping which experimental capabilities and hardware (e.g., hazardous gas canisters) are exposed to the model for any given scientific question — an antibody-design workflow, for example, is not given access to gas-canister-connected instruments.
framework🗣 Rafa Gómez-Bombarelli, CSOconviction: mediumLila Sciences
So what: Illustrates a practical, capability-scoping approach to physical-AI safety that differs from chatbot content-safety frameworks — relevant precedent as more AI systems get physical/robotic actuation.
“We can also lean on biosafety levels and things like that, good old fashioned lab safety to help in the meantime.” ▶ 17:13
Andy Beam argues AI safety investment must be proactive rather than reactive because capability curves tend to be sigmoid-shaped — performance can look flat and safe for a long period and then jump suddenly to a capability level nobody anticipated.
framework🗣 Andy Beam, CTOconviction: mediumLila Sciences
So what: A general risk-management heuristic (sigmoid capability jumps) applicable beyond Lila to any AI-safety governance discussion around agentic, tool-using models.
“capability curves tend to be sigmoid shaped and it can look like everything's fine, everything and then all of a sudden there's something that you didn't anticipate the model being able to do” ▶ 16:47
In vivo CAR-T case study and the zero-FTE startup model 5
Using its mRNA design capability, a two-to-three-person Lila team spent about six months building an in-vivo CAR-T candidate and achieved B-cell depletion in nonhuman primates that was significantly better than the preclinical data behind Capstan Therapeutics, a company built on roughly six years and about $100 million of R&D that was acquired by AbbVie for approximately $2.1 billion.
data_point🗣 Andy Beam, CTOconviction: highLila SciencesCapstan TherapeuticsAbbVieCAR-T
