A Fed research finding that Cayman Islands-domiciled (largely US-run) hedge funds now absorb roughly 34-37% of net US Treasury note and bond issuance raises concern that traditional foreign buyers are drying up and marginal demand now comes from levered funds that could be forced sellers in a risk-off shock.
data_point🗣 host (Natalie Brunell)conviction: mediumCayman IslandsTreasury issuancehedge funds
So what: A Treasury market increasingly reliant on levered offshore funds as marginal buyer is a structural fragility to watch for a forced-selling shock during risk-off episodes.
“the findings suggest that Cayman Islands hedge funds are the marginal foreign foreign buyers” ▶ 41:05
Hunter offers an alternative read on the Cayman Islands Treasury-buying data: rather than a systemic risk, it may reflect wealthy investors seeking safety in bonds, consistent with his view that the bond market is bottoming and reflects bearishness about stocks.
contrarian🗣 David Hunterconviction: lowTreasury bondsCayman Islands
So what: Reframes a data point others see as a red flag into evidence supporting his bullish bond-bottom thesis, illustrating how the same data can support opposite market narratives.
Hunter is bullish on Kevin Warsh as incoming Fed chair, describing him as a natural monetarist who understands that money printing eventually causes inflation and who will prioritize inflation control over premature rate cuts, in contrast to what Hunter calls the 'rock star' Fed communication style under Powell, Bernanke, and Yellen.
thesis🗣 David Hunterconviction: highKevin WarshJerome PowellFederal Reserve
So what: A Warsh-led Fed that resists premature easing and reduces forward-guidance noise changes the policy backdrop investors should expect going into the next cycle.
“I think he's great. I mean, um, I think he's almost made for that job.” ▶ 19:13
Housing Market Peaking 2
Hunter believes housing prices are peaking this cycle and may not be exceeded in the next cycle, because a weaker economy, job insecurity, and stricter bank lending standards will offset any demand boost from falling mortgage rates.
prediction🗣 David Hunterconviction: mediumhousing marketmortgage rates
So what: Suggests housing is a poor place to expect a cyclical rebound even as rates fall, since credit access and buyer psychology will remain impaired through the downturn.
“I think we're peaking in housing.” ▶ 15:46
A 70-80% equity bear market combined with a projected 30-40% hit to housing prices would produce a severe wealth-effect shock that is very hard to recover from quickly, even if mortgage rates eventually fall back to 2.5-3% during the bust's aftermath.
causal🗣 David Hunterconviction: mediumhousing marketwealth effect
So what: The combined equity and housing wealth destruction implies a much longer, deeper economic recovery than prior cycles, relevant for consumer-discretionary and housing-linked positioning.
“If you get the combination of that and say a 30 to 40% hit to housing prices, you know, the whole wealth effect is huge and you don't come back out of that quickly.” ▶ 16:46
Bitcoin: A Bearish Technical Read 3
Hunter, who admits he doesn't closely follow crypto, has turned technically bearish on Bitcoin, with a chart-based target near $75,000 and now potentially $50,000, and says this is strictly a technical read rather than a fundamental view.
prediction🗣 David Hunterconviction: lowBitcoin
So what: A well-known bull on equities and metals turning technically bearish on Bitcoin is a notable outside signal, though Hunter himself flags it should be weighted lightly given his admitted unfamiliarity with the asset.
“I'm bearish Bitcoin, but I'm the first one to tell you I don't follow it.” ▶ 47:26
Hunter attributes Bitcoin's underperformance more to negative price momentum ('the tape') than to a breakdown in the liquidity backdrop, drawing a parallel to how late entrants in silver's rally between $100-120 got trapped and are now bailing out.
causal🗣 David Hunterconviction: mediumBitcoinsilver
So what: If the selloff is momentum-driven rather than fundamentals-driven, a reversal in price action itself (not a change in liquidity conditions) could be the catalyst to watch for a bottom.
“momentum be gets momentum.” ▶ 47:39
Hunter expects leveraged Bitcoin holders such as MicroStrategy's Michael Saylor to get squeezed as momentum-driven selling compounds during the downturn.
prediction🗣 David Hunterconviction: mediumMichael SaylorMicroStrategyBitcoin
So what: Highly leveraged corporate Bitcoin treasuries are flagged as a specific stress point to monitor if Bitcoin's downtrend continues.
Liquidity, Japan Risk, and Investor Positioning 5
Hunter is skeptical that there is currently abundant traditional liquidity driving markets, noting the Fed's balance sheet has shrunk from about $9 trillion to roughly $6.4-6.7 trillion, which undercuts the popular narrative that expanding liquidity explains the rally.
contrarian🗣 David Hunterconviction: mediumFederal Reserve balance sheet
So what: If the rally isn't being driven by expanding Fed liquidity, it's more reliant on sentiment and a 'wall of worry' unwind, which could reverse faster once psychology shifts.
Hunter flags Japan, with government debt over roughly 200% of GDP, as a likely trouble spot in the global bust: decades of money-printing to suppress rates have kept the economy going, but if inflation finally breaks out there, rates will follow and the government may not be able to afford higher debt service.
causal🗣 David Hunterconviction: mediumJapan
So what: Japan's debt-to-GDP load makes it a key macro tripwire to watch; a Japanese rate breakout could be an early signal that the global bust thesis is playing out.
“I think Japan is a potential trouble spot in the bust.” ▶ 43:21
Hunter downplays the risk of a repeat yen carry-trade blowup like August 2024, arguing leveraged players already got burned once and are unlikely to have rebuilt that trade at the same scale.
contrarian🗣 David Hunterconviction: mediumJapanyen carry trade
So what: Tempers the popular 'yen carry trade unwind' tail-risk narrative, suggesting the bigger Japan risk is a structural rate breakout rather than a repeat leverage unwind.
“I struggle with this idea that there's some big carry trade blow up over there” ▶ 44:33
Hunter notes institutional investors have kept 'one foot out the door' of the stock market since around October 2022, remaining skeptical and underinvested despite market highs -- a persistent 'wall of worry' he cites as a reason the rally still has room to run.
thesis🗣 David Hunterconviction: highinstitutional investors
So what: Widespread institutional underweight positioning represents latent buying power that could keep fueling the melt-up as skeptics capitulate and chase performance.
“the institutions have have had one foot out the door to the stock market, you know, since October 2022” ▶ 42:39
Hunter warns that in an 80% S&P decline scenario, nearly everything falls together, so stock selection offers little protection; he also argues the decades-old 'time in the market, not timing the market' buy-and-hold mantra could fail badly this cycle, since recovering from an 80% drawdown could leave the market well below current highs even after a 3-4x cyclical bull rally off the bottom -- his advice is to neither be greedy nor too quick to exit given the market could still rally 30-40% first.
framework🗣 David Hunterconviction: highS&P 500buy and hold
So what: Challenges the default buy-and-hold playbook for this specific cycle, suggesting investors weigh tactical de-risking plans rather than assuming decades-long compounding will bail out a severe drawdown.
“Don't be greedy, but don't be too anxious to get out either.” ▶ 55:49
The Surprising Truth About Who's Buying Bitcoin in Europe32 min20 insights2026-07-14
▶ Watch on YouTube
Julian Liniger, founder/CEO of the self-custodial Bitcoin exchange Relay, describes Europe's Bitcoin buyers as older and more affluent than expected (not young digital natives), details how the EU's new MiCA license reshaped market access, and argues the current bear market's retail apathy stems from European cost-of-living pressure and capital chasing AI/big-tech hype rather than Bitcoin disbelief, with a bottom already in and a new bull market expected in 2027.
Relay's Self-Custodial Business Model · 3Who's Actually Buying Bitcoin in Europe · 4EU Regulation (MiCA) and Political Economy · 2Bear Market Psychology and Retail Behavior · 6Macro Bitcoin Outlook and Capital Rotation · 5
Relay's Self-Custodial Business Model 3
Relay, a Bitcoin-only self-custodial exchange founded in Switzerland in 2020, has onboarded more than 100,000 Europeans who have collectively bought around 20,000 Bitcoin through the platform over six years.
data_point🗣 Julian Liniger, CEO of Relayconviction: highRelayBitcoin
So what: Signals meaningful organic European retail on-ramp adoption for Bitcoin outside the major custodial exchanges, a proxy data point for regional retail demand.
“we've onboarded more than 100,000 Europeans to Bitcoin, and they bought around 20,000 Bitcoin with us in the last 6 years” ▶ 0:54
Unlike custodial exchanges such as Coinbase or Bitpanda, Relay never holds a penny of user funds, instead integrating a self-custodial wallet directly into its exchange so users always control their own keys.
thesis🗣 Julian Liniger, CEO of Relayconviction: highRelayCoinbaseBitpanda
So what: Differentiates Relay's counterparty-risk profile from custodial competitors, relevant when assessing exchange/platform risk in the European Bitcoin on-ramp market.
Liniger believes self-custodial Bitcoin will become easier to use than custodial financial services within 5 to 10 years, as tools like cloud backup and face-ID recovery eliminate the need to manage seed phrases while custodial providers face growing regulatory compliance burdens.
contrarian🗣 Julian Liniger, CEO of Relayconviction: mediumBitcoinRelay
So what: If self-custody UX converges with or beats custodial convenience, it could accelerate disintermediation of custodial exchanges and ETFs as the default retail access point for Bitcoin.
“it will be actually in the self-custodial way it's going to be easier to interact with Bitcoin than in custodial way” ▶ 5:19
Who's Actually Buying Bitcoin in Europe 4
Relay's actual core customer base skews toward people aged 40 to 80 who are not tech- or finance-savvy (e.g., taxi drivers, hairdressers, shop workers), contradicting the company's original assumption that its users would be young, digital-native, anti-establishment millennials in their 20s and early 30s.
contrarian🗣 Julian Liniger, CEO of Relayconviction: highRelayBitcoin
So what: Suggests the addressable market for Bitcoin retail on-ramps is broader and older than typically assumed, which matters for sizing future adoption curves and marketing strategy in the space.
“He lives on the countryside in Germany. He's 43 years old.” ▶ 6:32
Some of Relay's more affluent, older private clients started investing a few hundred dollars and have scaled up to allocations of 10,000, 100,000, or even millions of dollars in Bitcoin.
data_point🗣 Julian Liniger, CEO of Relayconviction: mediumRelayBitcoin
So what: Indicates a wealth-accumulation pathway among retail Bitcoin buyers in Europe, useful signal for private banks and wealth platforms assessing client demand for Bitcoin exposure.
Germany makes up more than 50% of Relay's user base, with Switzerland, Austria, Italy, and France following as the next largest markets, while Eastern and Northern Europe remain comparatively small despite no active marketing being needed to drive the initial German-speaking growth.
data_point🗣 Julian Liniger, CEO of Relayconviction: highRelayGermanySwitzerlandAustriaItalyFrance
So what: Maps the concentration of European Bitcoin retail demand geographically, relevant for competitors or investors evaluating regional expansion priorities.
Bitcoin adoption rate is roughly 20% in Switzerland versus about 10-15% in Germany and under 10% in most other European countries, which Liniger attributes partly to cultural trust in local/Swiss brands rather than purely to financial-system stability.
data_point🗣 Julian Liniger, CEO of Relayconviction: mediumSwitzerlandGermanyBitcoin
So what: Provides a rare country-level adoption-rate benchmark for Bitcoin in Europe that can anchor forecasts of total addressable market by country.
“the adoption rate in Switzerland is like 20% which is ahead of the average 10% 10 15% for example in Germany and 10 or less percent in other European countries” ▶ 13:42
EU Regulation (MiCA) and Political Economy 2
The EU's MiCA (Markets in Crypto-Assets) license, which Relay obtained by the end of 2025, is described as nearly equivalent to a banking license and was mandatory for any firm to keep offering crypto brokerage services to EU clients past the compliance deadline this month, forcing unlicensed competitors to halt EU operations entirely.
data_point🗣 Julian Liniger, CEO of Relayconviction: highMiCARelay
So what: MiCA functions as a high barrier to entry that could consolidate the European crypto exchange market around a small number of licensed players like Relay, a competitive-dynamics signal for the sector.
Liniger believes EU politicians and traditional media push negative Bitcoin narratives (volatility, crime, energy use, quantum risk) partly to keep citizens holding euros, which are subject to high inflation and taxation of up to 50% income tax plus unrealized gains, capital gains, and wealth taxes.
contrarian🗣 Julian Liniger, CEO of Relayconviction: mediumECBBitcoinGermany
So what: Frames EU regulatory and media hostility toward Bitcoin as a structural, incentive-driven headwind rather than a purely technical concern, relevant to expectations for how fast policy friction eases.
“most EU countries take like up to 50% income tax and then there's unrealized gain tax and capital gain tax” ▶ 14:58
Bear Market Psychology and Retail Behavior 6
Despite the fear and greed index sitting at absolute fear during this bear market, Relay retains almost all of its recurring Bitcoin savings-plan (DCA) subscribers, showing that educated long-term holders are not capitulating even as one-time discretionary buying dries up.
data_point🗣 Julian Liniger, CEO of Relayconviction: highRelayBitcoin
So what: Sticky DCA behavior among educated holders suggests a resilient floor of demand independent of sentiment swings, useful for gauging structural versus speculative Bitcoin demand.
Relay consistently sees roughly an 80/20 buy-to-sell ratio among users, with buy-the-dip volume spiking three to five times the average on days Bitcoin drops 4-5%, indicating dip-buying dominates over panic-selling on its platform.
data_point🗣 Julian Liniger, CEO of Relayconviction: highRelayBitcoin
So what: A concrete, platform-level buy/sell ratio investors can use as a sentiment proxy for retail dip-buying conviction during drawdowns.
According to Liniger, no Relay user who has run a Bitcoin savings plan for at least four years has ever lost money, reinforcing his framing of DCA as a reliably profitable long-term strategy on the platform's data.
data_point🗣 Julian Liniger, CEO of Relayconviction: highRelayBitcoin
So what: A specific, testable claim about DCA outcomes that investors could use to evaluate the historical robustness of a multi-year Bitcoin dollar-cost-averaging strategy.
“No one has ever lost money that has uh done a savings plan for 4 years” ▶ 25:07
In contrast to the two prior bear markets Relay has been through, this one is marked by unusual retail apathy rather than active trading or panic, which Liniger attributes to European cost-of-living pressure (rising rent, food, insurance, taxes) leaving people with less money and attention for Bitcoin.
causal🗣 Julian Liniger, CEO of Relayconviction: mediumBitcoinGermanyFranceItalyPortugal
So what: Ties depressed retail engagement to macro household-budget stress rather than lost conviction in Bitcoin, implying pent-up demand could return once real incomes stabilize.
European retail capital that might otherwise go to Bitcoin is instead chasing the AI/big-tech hype (Google, Amazon, Meta, Nvidia) and high-profile IPOs like SpaceX, reflecting a short-term, gambling-driven search for fast returns rather than long-term dollar-cost-averaging discipline.
causal🗣 Julian Liniger, CEO of Relayconviction: mediumBitcoinSpaceXNvidiaGoogleAmazonMeta
So what: Identifies AI-stock and mega-IPO mania as a direct competitor for the same pool of speculative retail capital that would otherwise flow into Bitcoin, a rotation dynamic to watch as the AI trade matures.
“They invest rather in in the big tech, Google, Amazon, you know, Meta, etc., Nvidia.” ▶ 22:50
Citing Michael Saylor, Liniger argues most people who believe Bitcoin is 'too late' after its prior 100x simply haven't invested the roughly 100 hours needed to genuinely understand it, whereas those who do usually end up starting a long-term savings plan.
framework🗣 Julian Liniger, CEO of Relayconviction: mediumBitcoinMike Saylor
So what: Frames retail non-participation as an education/time-investment gap rather than a valid valuation objection, a lens for evaluating why adoption lags despite bullish fundamentals.
“You need to put in at least 100 hours.” ▶ 24:53
Macro Bitcoin Outlook and Capital Rotation 5
Liniger states Relay saw a roughly 56% drawdown in Bitcoin price and a clear bottom in user activity and sentiment in April, with improvement in May and a strong rebound in user activity starting in June.
data_point🗣 Julian Liniger, CEO of Relayconviction: highBitcoinRelay
So what: Provides a platform-level, activity-based timestamp for the cycle bottom that can be cross-checked against price and on-chain data for cycle-timing purposes.
“We have though seen a bottom in April, clearly.” ▶ 29:27
Liniger expects Bitcoin to stay relatively stable to modestly higher through the rest of this year without reaching $100k, because AI buildout and big-tech IPOs are absorbing hundreds of billions of dollars in liquidity and geopolitical war uncertainty continues to weigh on risk appetite.
prediction🗣 Julian Liniger, CEO of Relayconviction: mediumBitcoinSpaceXNvidia
So what: Sets a near-term price ceiling expectation ($100k unreached this year) tied explicitly to AI-driven liquidity absorption as the mechanism, a thesis investors can monitor via AI capex and IPO flow data.
Liniger predicts that once the AI investment cycle matures ('bubbles out') and investors realize gains next year, freed-up capital will rotate into hard assets including Bitcoin, driving a new bull market with all-time highs well above $100k in the following year.
prediction🗣 Julian Liniger, CEO of Relayconviction: mediumBitcoin
So what: Articulates a specific capital-rotation catalyst (AI trade maturing → profit-taking → reallocation to hard assets) that investors could track as a leading indicator for the next Bitcoin bull leg.
“there will be a lot of free capital that will need to flow somewhere” ▶ 30:53
Liniger's core long-term thesis is that Bitcoin's fixed supply makes it structurally unique versus every other asset class, since stocks, gold, and oil can all be created or produced in greater quantity, positioning Bitcoin as the ultimate long-term store of value as global asset supply expands.
thesis🗣 Julian Liniger, CEO of Relayconviction: highBitcoin
So what: Restates the core scarcity thesis underpinning Liniger's (and Relay's) long-term bullishness, the anchor assumption behind his multi-year price and adoption forecasts.
“It's the only thing that it's just fixed supply.” ▶ 26:16
Relay's stated long-term goal is to eventually go public, betting that Bitcoin's brand recognition (comparable to Coca-Cola or Apple) combined with the company's own Bitcoin treasury holdings creates a flywheel where rising Bitcoin prices simultaneously boost company valuation, client asset values, and trading volume/revenue.
thesis🗣 Julian Liniger, CEO of Relayconviction: mediumRelayBitcoin
So what: Signals a future European Bitcoin-exchange IPO candidate whose valuation is explicitly levered to Bitcoin price appreciation, worth tracking as a potential public listing tied to Bitcoin's cycle.

Macro Voices · Erik Townsend 1 episode(s) · 47 insights

MacroVoices #541 Dr. Anas Alhajji: Bab el-Mandeb: The Next Oil Chokepoint Nobody's Watching116 min47 insights2026-07-16
▶ Watch on YouTube
Dr. Anas Alhajji argues the Strait of Hormuz was deliberately closed by the US (via an EU insurance-law mechanism, not military action) to demonstrate energy/AI dominance over China, that rogue IRGC factions rather than Iran's negotiators are now blocking reopening, and that Bab el-Mandeb, refining capacity, and SPR policy are the next fronts investors should watch, with LNG and coal as the clearest long-term winners.
Bab el-Mandeb: The Next Chokepoint · 6How Hormuz Was Really Closed · 7IRGC Rogue Factions as the Wildcard · 6China's Demand Management Playbook · 5Refining Capacity Is the Real Bottleneck · 5SPR Mechanics: Loans, Limits, and Leverage · 6Post-Hormuz World: OPEC+ and the Permanent Risk Premium · 5Investment Positioning: LNG, Coal, and the Market Wrap · 7
Bab el-Mandeb: The Next Chokepoint 6
A Houthi attack on the Saudi airport in Abha effectively ended the informal Saudi-Houthi agreement that had kept Bab el-Mandeb calm, making it the next flashpoint to watch after Hormuz.
causal🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: highBab el-MandebHouthisSaudi Arabia
So what: Watch for renewed Houthi-Saudi hostilities as a leading indicator of a second insurance-driven chokepoint shock.
Roughly six million barrels per day of oil, mostly Russian and Saudi crude, transits Bab el-Mandeb, the entrance to the Red Sea.
data_point🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: highBab el-MandebRussiaSaudi Arabia
So what: Sizes the volume at risk if the strait is disrupted, a scale large enough to move global benchmarks.
“about six million barrels of oil basically passes through Babel Mandib mostly Russian and Saudi oil” ▶ 9:08
The real risk to Bab el-Mandeb isn't the Houthis physically blockading it, but a single tanker attack causing European (mostly UK) insurers to cancel war-risk coverage, replicating the mechanism that shut Hormuz.
framework🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: highBab el-MandebHouthisIRGC
So what: Investors should track marine war-risk insurance pricing/cancellations as the real transmission mechanism for chokepoint shocks, not just headline attack counts.
“the fear is not the Houthis blocking Babel Mandi. The fear is that few guys basically launching an attack on couple of oil tankers and as a result the insurance companies...will literally cancel the insurance” ▶ 9:48
If Bab el-Mandeb closes, Saudi Arabia would lose more than four million barrels a day of crude flow, since that is the same oil already being diverted from the Gulf through east-west pipelines because of the Hormuz crisis.
data_point🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: mediumSaudi ArabiaBab el-MandebStrait of Hormuz
So what: A Bab el-Mandeb closure would compound rather than diversify the existing Hormuz supply risk, since it's the same barrels' only remaining export route.
Russia would be a relative winner if Bab el-Mandeb insurance seized up, because its exports to India and China already move on sanctioned tankers insured outside the Western market.
contrarian🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: mediumRussiaIndiaChinaBab el-Mandeb
So what: A shadow-fleet/insurance bifurcation means Western sanctions-linked disruptions can inadvertently strengthen Russia's competitive position in Asian crude markets.
Alhajji expects any Bab el-Mandeb disruption to be short-lived given a severe Arab-coalition and US response, but insurance costs will stay structurally higher even after the strait reopens, just as happened with Hormuz.
prediction🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: mediumBab el-MandebSaudi ArabiaUnited States
So what: Expect a persistent war-risk insurance premium embedded in shipping costs even after any acute event resolves, a structural tailwind for freight/insurance-linked plays and a floor under landed oil costs.
How Hormuz Was Really Closed 7
Alhajji publicly predicted roughly a year before the Iran conflict began that the Strait of Hormuz would be closed, but by the United States rather than Iran, as a message to China about US energy and AI dominance.
prediction🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: highStrait of HormuzChinaUnited States
So what: A track record of correctly calling the closure mechanism a year in advance lends weight to Alhajji's broader US-China dominance thesis for positioning going forward.
The Strait of Hormuz was effectively closed without any direct military action: an EU solvency law forced European war-risk insurers to cancel coverage across the whole Indian Ocean within seven days after a US Navy strike near Sirri Island killed about 85 Iranian sailors.
causal🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: highStrait of HormuzUnited StatesIranEuropean Union
So what: Reveals an insurance-regulation transmission channel for chokepoint shocks that can shut shipping lanes faster than military blockades, and that could recur at any strait if a similar trigger event occurs.
“I said that Iran cannot close the straight but the United States will close the straight and I strongly believe that is exactly what happened” ▶ 19:46
Iran did not actually close Hormuz; it began claiming credit for the closure only after Western insurers had already halted coverage, similar to a gangster who shows up to guard a mall that's already locked for the night.
framework🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: highIranStrait of Hormuz
So what: Separates the narrative (Iran 'closing' the strait) from the actual mechanism (insurance cancellation), which matters for correctly forecasting when and how the strait could reopen or close again.
“just like a gangster goes to the mall after it closed after 10 p.m. with his gun and he stood in front of the door” ▶ 18:33
China anticipated the Hormuz closure over a year in advance by monitoring Arabic-language energy commentary (including Alhajji's own Arabic-language writing) and began pre-building oil, natural gas, and coal inventories well before the conflict started.
anecdote🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: mediumChina
So what: China's information-gathering and stockpiling advantage suggests it will continue to front-run Western-facing geopolitical energy shocks, a structural edge worth tracking in Chinese import/inventory data.
Alhajji's central thesis is that the Hormuz closure was a 'deep state' strategy, consistent with the Trump administration's November 2025 National Security Strategy, to project US dominance in energy and AI over China rather than primarily a response to Iran's nuclear program.
thesis🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: highUnited StatesChinaIran
So what: If correct, the conflict's resolution will be driven by US-China strategic signaling dynamics, not purely by Iran-nuclear negotiations, changing how investors should read de-escalation headlines.
A helium/LNG plant in Qatar, the source of roughly 75% of the helium used by South Korea and Taiwan for semiconductor manufacturing, was destroyed early in the war, cutting off a critical chip-industry input for Asia.
data_point🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: highQatarTaiwanSouth Koreasemiconductors
So what: A helium supply shock adds a second, less-watched constraint on Asian chip production alongside energy costs, reinforcing the reshoring case for US/Arizona semiconductor capacity.
Regional oil benchmarks diverged sharply from headline Brent/WTI: Dubai/Oman medium sour crude spiked above $170 with some spot cargoes near $200, meaning the real price shock to Asian buyers was much larger than Brent and WTI suggested.
contrarian🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: highDubai crudeOman crudeBrentWTIAsia
So what: Analysts anchored only to Brent/WTI missed the actual magnitude of demand destruction in Asia; regional sour-crude benchmarks are the more accurate gauge of stress during chokepoint events.
IRGC Rogue Factions as the Wildcard 6
The Iranian regime is not a unified actor; IRGC hardliners operate outside the elected government, have grown wealthy from sanctions-era smuggling, and have a direct financial incentive to prevent normalization.
thesis🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: highIRGCIran
So what: Negotiation headlines with Tehran's official negotiators may not translate into actual de-escalation as long as IRGC hardliners retain independent operational control and financial motive to keep tensions elevated.
The extremist IRGC faction driving current attacks functions like an international cartel with reach into Yemen (via Houthi factions), Lebanon, and Iraq, and even a presence in Mexico, Venezuela, and Argentina.
data_point🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: mediumIRGCHouthisLebanonIraq
So what: The network's geographic reach means chokepoint risk isn't confined to Hormuz or Bab el-Mandeb; the same actors have latent capacity to project disruption more broadly.
US and Iranian negotiators may both be negotiating in good faith, but a resolution is blocked because rogue IRGC elements, who don't represent the elected government, actually control the leverage over the strait.
thesis🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: highIRGCIranUnited States
So what: A resolution requires the US to militarily degrade IRGC extremist elements, not just reach a diplomatic deal with Tehran's official team, implying a longer and more violent path to reopening than headline diplomacy suggests.
Fiery public statements from both the Trump administration and Iranian officials are largely theater for domestic audiences and should not be read as signals that a full-scale war is resuming.
contrarian🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: mediumUnited StatesIran
So what: Traders should discount rhetoric-driven price spikes on official statements unless corroborated by actual attacks, since much of the messaging is aimed at domestic politics rather than signaling escalation intent.
A full collapse of the Iranian regime, not just a change of government, would be the worst-case outcome for the region and Western Europe, since it would trigger a refugee exodus toward Europe and stoke Kurdish and Baloch separatism in Turkey and Pakistan.
thesis🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: mediumIranTurkeyPakistanEurope
So what: Because no regional or Western power wants regime collapse, expect US policy to continue targeting IRGC extremist elements specifically rather than pursuing broader regime change, limiting tail-risk of state failure.
An attack on an LNG carrier was the actual 'red line' that triggered a rapid US retaliation, more so than other attacks, indicating the US is prioritizing protection of LNG shipping specifically.
data_point🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: highLNGIRGCUnited States
So what: LNG carrier safety is functioning as a tripwire for US military response, making LNG shipping routes a useful leading indicator for de-escalation or re-escalation.
China's Demand Management Playbook 5
China cut oil imports by roughly six million barrels a day when Dubai/Oman crude spiked above $170, a deliberate 'buy low, sell high' response consistent with its demand behavior over the past decade.
data_point🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: highChina
So what: China's import elasticity at high prices is a repeatable, trackable signal; large import cuts should be read as price discipline, not necessarily distress.
China's six-million-barrel import cut was not mainly drawn from visible onshore inventories (only about 50 million barrels were used); it broke down into roughly 0.8-1.0 million b/d of halted inventory-building, about 1.5 million b/d absorbed into non-satellite-visible floating storage, higher domestic production, and a ban on refined-product exports.
data_point🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: highChina
So what: Popular narratives that tracked only onshore/satellite-visible Chinese inventories overstated the real drawdown and understated China's resilience; floating storage should be added to any China demand model.
China's official Q2 2026 GDP growth print was the lowest since the 1990s, partly reflecting genuine demand destruction from high oil prices layered on top of the ongoing shift to electric vehicles.
data_point🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: mediumChina
So what: Confirms that even China's demand-management playbook has a real economic growth cost, which caps how long the strategy can be sustained.
China's import-cutting strategy is only sustainable in the short-to-medium term; if the Hormuz situation dragged on for another year, Alhajji believes China would become the biggest economic loser of the crisis.
prediction🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: mediumChina
So what: A prolonged chokepoint crisis would eventually force China to either resume higher-priced imports or accept deeper economic damage, a scenario worth stress-testing in China-linked positions.
China has been preparing since around 2019 for a potential closure of the Malacca Strait amid expectations of war or sanctions, which explains its heavy build-out of solar, wind, hydro, and nuclear power as an energy-security hedge rather than primarily a climate policy.
contrarian🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: mediumChina
So what: Reframes China's renewable buildout as strategic energy-security insurance rather than climate virtue, implying continued state-backed investment in domestic power generation regardless of climate-policy shifts elsewhere.
“they were convinced that Malika strait will be closed. So China basically is preparing for the Malika strait to be closed” ▶ 54:17
Refining Capacity Is the Real Bottleneck 5
US refineries are running near 96-97% of capacity on average (some individual refineries above 100%), so even a surge in gasoline and diesel demand cannot pull through significantly more crude, undermining the bullish 'tank-bottom' narrative for crude prices.
contrarian🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: highUnited Statesrefining
So what: Crude-price bulls betting on a demand-driven price spike from low product inventories are likely mispricing the constraint; the binding limit is refining throughput, not crude availability, capping WTI/Brent upside even amid a product squeeze.
“when refineries are running at almost full capacity, even if there is massive demand for gasoline and diesel, if you cannot process the crude, then you are not going to demand the crude” ▶ 1:09:48
The Trump administration's 'energy dominance' policy focused on upstream production and neglected downstream refining capacity, which Alhajji calls the biggest structural criticism of the policy.
thesis🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: highUnited Statesrefining
So what: Any durable US energy-dominance strategy requires new refinery capacity or expansions; until that happens, US refined-product exports and domestic prices remain structurally constrained.
The global diesel crisis was driven by the shutdown of exports from three major Gulf refineries (in Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, and the UAE), compounded by panic-driven export restrictions in China, India, and South Korea and by tankers being resold and re-routed multiple times mid-voyage.
causal🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: highKuwaitSaudi ArabiaUAEChinaIndiaSouth Korea
So what: Diesel scarcity was amplified by policy panic and hoarding, not just physical supply loss, meaning the crisis could recur even without a fresh chokepoint event if similar export-restriction panic sets in.
Without US SPR releases of medium sour crude, domestic diesel prices in the United States could have reached roughly $12 a gallon.
data_point🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: mediumUnited StatesStrategic Petroleum Reserve
So what: Quantifies the real economic value of SPR releases beyond headline barrel counts, arguing for their strategic (not just fiscal) justification.
“without the US strategic petroleum reserve releases, diesel prices in the United States probably would be in in the range of $12 a gallon” ▶ 1:19:45
US diesel exports reached record highs because SPR releases of medium sour crude let domestic refiners cut their own medium-sour import needs, freeing more medium sour crude for the rest of the world while the US exported more refined diesel.
causal🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: mediumUnited StatesStrategic Petroleum Reserverefining
So what: US refiners and diesel exporters are direct beneficiaries of SPR release policy, a dynamic likely to continue as long as releases persist.
SPR Mechanics: Loans, Limits, and Leverage 6
The widely cited '250 million barrel floor' for the US Strategic Petroleum Reserve is a legal limit set by Congress, not a technical one, and the law allows the president to authorize releases below that floor by declaring an emergency.
contrarian🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: highStrategic Petroleum ReserveUnited States
So what: The SPR has more usable capacity than headlines suggest, meaning further large releases remain legally available if the administration wants to suppress prices further, a bearish tail risk for crude bulls.
The US could technically draw the SPR down by roughly another 100 million barrels beyond its current level without hitting real geological constraints, since the underground salt caverns are filled with brine, not air, when oil is withdrawn.
data_point🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: mediumStrategic Petroleum Reserve
So what: Technical cavern-integrity concerns cited by bulls as a hard floor on SPR drawdowns are overstated, removing one of the bullish arguments for a near-term SPR-driven price floor.
As a net oil exporter, the United States is exempt from the International Energy Agency's rule requiring members to hold 90 days of net oil imports in strategic reserves, giving it more legal room to draw the SPR down than import-dependent IEA peers.
framework🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: highStrategic Petroleum ReserveUnited States
So what: Removes another commonly cited institutional constraint on further SPR drawdowns, reinforcing that policy choice, not rules, will determine future release pace.
SPR releases (about 99 million barrels released so far, with roughly 73 million barrels of authorized releases remaining) are structured as loans, not sales: companies must return the borrowed oil plus an in-kind interest premium, and because they borrowed near $120/barrel and are repaying when oil trades near $60-80, oil companies and traders are profiting substantially at taxpayer expense.
contrarian🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: highStrategic Petroleum ReserveUnited States
So what: Companies holding SPR loan obligations have an embedded profit motive to refill at low prices, which argues against the bullish thesis that SPR refilling itself will drive prices materially higher.
“they borrowed the oil at 120 and later on when they return it, they will return it when oil is 60 or 70 or 80” ▶ 1:06:49
Because SPR releases peaked at up to 1.9 million barrels a day while technical injection (refill) capacity is limited to roughly 400,000 barrels a day, the SPR refill process will only create a soft price floor rather than driving crude prices meaningfully higher.
prediction🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: highStrategic Petroleum Reserve
So what: Investors betting on an SPR-refill-driven crude rally should temper expectations; the asymmetry between release and injection rates limits the bullish impact to a price floor, not a rally.
Japan's SPR drawdown was driven primarily by yen weakness, which pushed oil priced in yen to an all-time high in April, rather than by physical shortage; Japan will not refill its reserves until the yen strengthens, unlike India, which rebuilt its inventories to their highest level since COVID.
causal🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: mediumJapanIndiaStrategic Petroleum Reserve
So what: Japan's SPR refill timeline is a currency call, not an oil-market call; JPY strength (or weakness) is now a direct input into future Japanese crude demand.
Post-Hormuz World: OPEC+ and the Permanent Risk Premium 5
Alhajji proposes OPEC+ reform bylaws should shift from measuring member production to measuring exports, should track all liquids rather than just crude, and should partner with consuming nations to jointly build strategic reserves in locations free of maritime choke points.
framework🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: highOPEC+
So what: An OPEC+ pivot toward export-based rules and jointly-funded strategic reserves would be a structural shift in how the cartel manages supply signaling, worth monitoring in future OPEC+ communiques.
Alhajji proposes OPEC+ fund strategic petroleum reserves in importing countries like India, which lack the capital to build them alone, in exchange for those countries later exporting refined oil during a Hormuz-type closure, effectively converting large importers into quasi-OPEC+ exporters.
thesis🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: mediumOPEC+India
So what: Watch for OPEC+ capital deployment into third-country storage infrastructure as a new form of producer-consumer strategic alliance that could reshape crude flows during future chokepoint crises.
New cross-border oil pipeline and canal megaprojects (such as routes through Syria or Israel) no longer make security sense given cheap drone-strike capability demonstrated in the Ukraine war; only projects with independent economic logic, like the UAE's Fujairah pipeline, are worth building.
contrarian🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: highUAEFujairahSyriaIsrael
So what: Investors should be skeptical of chokepoint-bypass pipeline proposals pitched primarily on security grounds; only projects with standalone economic returns (like UAE's Fujairah route) are likely to actually get built.
The UAE's departure from OPEC does not threaten the cartel's cohesion, because no OPEC+ member wants to trigger a price war, so OPEC+ will remain functionally intact.
thesis🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: mediumOPEC+UAE
So what: Headlines about OPEC+ fragmentation from the UAE exit are likely overstated as a bearish catalyst for cartel discipline.
Even after a formal resolution to the Hormuz crisis, an elevated 'ghost of Hormuz' risk premium will persist indefinitely because any militia, disgruntled group, or even a trader with a large social-media following can now trigger price spikes with unverified attack claims.
thesis🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: highStrait of Hormuz
So what: Structurally higher volatility and headline-driven whipsaws in oil prices should be expected as a permanent feature of the market, favoring options/volatility strategies over simple directional crude bets.
“we created a monster that no one can slain period” ▶ 1:33:20
Investment Positioning: LNG, Coal, and the Market Wrap 7
Alhajji's top investment recommendation is US LNG names, calling natural gas and especially US LNG infrastructure the biggest structural winner from the combination of Hormuz-driven energy-security concerns and rising AI/data-center power demand.
thesis🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: highLNGnatural gas
So what: Positions US LNG producers/exporters as a direct long-term beneficiary of both geopolitical risk premia and structural AI power demand growth.
“I am very big on LNG names.” ▶ 1:36:35
Coal is positioned as a secondary winner alongside LNG because it is a cheap, domestic energy source in many affected consuming countries, and governments are increasingly framing all domestic energy sources, including coal, under a national-security umbrella.
thesis🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: mediumcoal
So what: Coal names in consuming countries could see policy tailwinds previously reserved for renewables, as 'national security' framing overrides prior climate-policy resistance to coal investment.
Countries are broadly adopting a 'Chinese model' of linking domestic energy sources to national security, which lets governments push through subsidies and policies for domestic energy (across natural gas, coal, and renewables) that previously stalled due to cost or climate-policy opposition.
framework🗣 guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)conviction: mediumChinanational security
So what: Expect a broad wave of national-security-justified energy subsidies across multiple energy sources and countries, benefiting domestic energy producers regardless of political party in power.
The MacroVoices trading desk's trade of the week is long Cheniere Energy (LNG) shares near $255.83 on a technical breakout above its descending trendline from the March peak, paired with a $240-strike protective put (36 days to expiry, costing about 1.8% of the stock price) to guard against a failed breakout.
data_point🗣 Patrick (trading desk co-host)conviction: mediumCheniere Energy (LNG)
So what: A concrete, hedged way to express the LNG structural-winner thesis discussed in the interview, with a defined technical stop level at $240.
“buying the $240 strike put for $4.55, giving us 36 days of protection” ▶ 1:41:39
Large speculator positioning in S&P 500 futures surged from a one-year score of 16 to 94 in a single month, indicating the rally is being driven by systematic, momentum-chasing money rather than conviction built up over time.
data_point🗣 trading desk co-host (COT Signal analyst)conviction: mediumS&P 500
So what: A rapid, one-month positioning surge to the top decile is fragile; a market rollover could trigger fast CTA-driven unwinding, so equity longs should watch for a break below roughly 7,400 on the S&P 500 as a systematic sell-trigger zone.
≈ “large speculators positioning in the S&P futures has surged from 16 to 94 and that just happened in a single month” paraphrase
Large speculator positioning in WTI crude has fallen back to a one-year score of just 19, near pre-war levels, meaning nearly all of the war-risk premium that speculators added during the Iran conflict has already been unwound even as the conflict re-escalates.
data_point🗣 trading desk co-host (COT Signal analyst)conviction: mediumWTI
So what: Futures positioning has essentially 'unpriced' the ongoing conflict, creating room for speculative long-building (and a price move higher) if renewed Bab el-Mandeb or Hormuz escalation is confirmed.
“the market spent the last month unpricing a war that never actually ended” ▶ 1:48:36
Coffee futures saw large speculator positioning jump from a one-year score of 0 to 51 in a single month, the largest such increase across all tracked assets, after an eight-month, 44% bear market bottomed on Brazilian weather damage, El Nino concerns, and thin global inventories, yet positioning remains far below the historical ~40% net-long peak.
data_point🗣 trading desk co-host (COT Signal analyst)conviction: mediumcoffee
So what: Coffee's repricing looks driven by short-covering rather than crowded new-long positioning, leaving room for the rally to extend if price, open interest, and large-speculator buying continue to rise together.
“Large specs are net long only about 15% of their total open interest compared to their historical peak, which is usually around 40%.” ▶ 1:52:41

80,000 Hours Podcast 1 episode(s) · 44 insights

Can $500 Billion Win the AI Race? | Anton Leicht94 min44 insights2026-07-14
▶ Watch on YouTube
Carnegie Endowment fellow Anton Leicht argues that middle powers (Europe, Japan, South Korea, etc.) face a default future of capturing all of frontier AI's risk and none of its benefit unless they pursue a compute-for-access strategy with US labs; he separately sketches, and largely dismisses as unlikely, an allied $500 billion four-year coalition project to build an independent frontier AGI model, while also covering AI taxation, labor-market policy, soft nationalization of US labs, and the pause/slowdown debate.
Middle Powers' Default AI Dependency · 7$500B Coalition Frontier-Model Gambit · 12Taxing and Cushioning the AI Transition · 9Manufacturing as the New AGI Moat · 4Soft Nationalization of US AI Labs · 3Pause, Slowdown, and the China Question · 7Country Winners and Losers in the AGI Economy · 2
Middle Powers' Default AI Dependency 7
Countries that do not build their own frontier AI models default into capturing all the societal risk that comes from these models spreading through society while capturing almost none of the economic benefit, according to Anton Leicht.
framework🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: highmiddle powersfrontier AI models
So what: Frames the baseline policy problem for every non-US, non-China government: passive non-participation in the AI supply chain is itself a strategic loss, not a neutral position.
Leicht's recommended strategy for middle powers to secure reliable AI access is compute-for-access: build data centres that absorb US labs' inference-capacity crunch, and in exchange negotiate guaranteed frontier-model access at parity with the US commercial market.
framework🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: highmiddle powersUS AI labsdata centres
So what: A concrete, testable framework for evaluating national AI deals: watch whether new data-centre buildouts in allied countries come bundled with explicit model-access-parity clauses.
Leicht estimates the realistic benchmark labs need to consider diversifying inference compute outside the US is a data centre built in about 18 to 24 months, ideally offering something like a gigawatt of capacity within one to two years.
data_point🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: mediumdata centresUS AI labs
So what: Gives investors a concrete timeline/scale benchmark for judging whether a given national data-centre project is fast enough to actually earn access-parity leverage with frontier labs.
Direct government-to-government AI deals are fragile: the UK-US Technology Prosperity Deal, which included a Stargate data-centre buildout pillar, unravelled after the US inserted unrelated food-safety demands into negotiations and OpenAI partly reneged on the Stargate component citing copyright and energy-price concerns.
causal🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: highUnited KingdomUnited StatesOpenAIStargate
So what: Warns investors and policymakers that headline US bilateral tech-cooperation deals can be renegotiated or partly abandoned via unrelated political leverage, making them a weak basis for long-term AI-access planning.
Leicht argues deals struck directly with AI labs are more durable than deals struck with the US government, because labs that have sunk billions into a country's data centre gain a commercial incentive to lobby the US government against export restrictions that would strand that investment.
thesis🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: mediumAI labsNvidiaAnthropicUS government
So what: Suggests investors should track lab-to-country commercial commitments (data-centre buildouts, access-parity contracts) as a more reliable predictor of durable AI market access than government treaties.
≈ “if the US government decides to come up with some access restriction, then the American firms that are involved in these deals... now have an incentive to tell the US government... can you at least give a carveout to this country” paraphrase
The UAE's chip-access deal and Norway's original Stargate arrangement (now effectively a Microsoft buildout after OpenAI partly walked back its commitment) both illustrate that early compute deals were struck from weak bargaining positions before frontier-access anxiety became acute, and neither secured genuine access-parity guarantees.
data_point🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: mediumUAENorwayOpenAIMicrosoftStargate
So what: Signals that early-mover AI infrastructure deals in the Gulf and Nordics should not be read as templates for future access-parity negotiations; later entrants may need to negotiate harder terms.
The Fable export-control episode, reported by Wired as tied to Fable's access being extended to South Korea's SK Telecom over alleged China ties, has accelerated Washington's policy conversation toward export-controlling AI models (weights and/or outputs) much faster than Leicht expected, compressing the window middle powers have to set up compute-for-access arrangements.
causal🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: highFableSK TelecomSouth KoreaWired
So what: A concrete near-term catalyst: watch for accelerated US export-control rulemaking on model weights/outputs, which would tighten the timeline for any middle power still negotiating access-parity deals.
≈ “The Fable thing — even if it is a red herring — has shaken up a lot of people, and has made a lot of people more worried about this.” paraphrase
$500B Coalition Frontier-Model Gambit 12
Leicht's own back-of-envelope estimate is that a coalition of middle powers pooling talent, compute, funding, and supply-chain leverage could build a competitive frontier AI model for roughly $500 billion over a four-year project.
data_point🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: mediummiddle powersfrontier AI model
So what: Sets a concrete, named price tag for an independent non-US/non-China frontier-AI effort that investors and policymakers can use as a reference point when new sovereign AI initiatives are announced.
Leicht argues $500 billion is not an absurd sum if the best version of an allied liberal-democracy coalition — the EU plus Canada, the UK, Australia and Japan and South Korea — pooled resources, since it would be affordable for that combined economic bloc.
thesis🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: mediumEuropean UnionCanadaUnited KingdomAustraliaJapanSouth Korea
So what: Frames the feasibility question as political willingness rather than raw affordability, which is the key variable for investors tracking whether a rival non-US frontier lab could actually get funded.
Leicht's proposed structure for a coalition frontier lab is to replicate the standard frontier-lab organizational model exactly (pay top engineers heavily, give them massive compute, minimal outside interference) rather than reinvent it, funded by irrevocable upfront capital commitments from every member government routed through a small number of designated national liaisons.
framework🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: mediumfrontier AI labscoalition governments
So what: A concrete organizational blueprint that would signal a serious (vs. symbolic) state-backed AI effort if it appeared in real policy proposals.
≈ “You just need to reinvent the wheel... In fact, you mustn’t reinvent the wheel in any of this.” paraphrase
Leicht's preferred coalition membership is the EU, UK, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, South Korea, and Japan (Taiwan if achievable), but he expects the UK, South Korea, and Japan to opt out initially because their current bilateral relationships with the US look like a better bet than joining an EU-led coalition with a poor track record of delivering announced joint projects.
contrarian🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: mediumEuropean UnionUnited KingdomSouth KoreaJapanCanadaAustralia
So what: Predicts a narrower, EU-Canada-Australia-anchored coalition as the realistic starting membership, useful for tracking which countries' policy moves would actually signal this project advancing.
Leicht frames the coalition frontier-model project economically like a warplane or aircraft carrier — a strategic asset governments buy without expecting it to pay for itself — rather than a commercial venture, distinguishing it from US labs which must justify returns to private capital markets that do not exist at scale outside the US.
framework🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: mediumcoalition governmentsUS AI labs
So what: Reframes how to value a state-backed AI project: judge it against defense-budget logic, not venture-style ROI, when assessing whether treasuries will actually fund one.
Leicht is pessimistic that a successful European frontier model would translate into strong international commercial market share, noting Europe has historically found it harder to build and sell a winning software product than to build large infrastructure, even though Mistral retains a decent US market share today.
contrarian🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: mediumMistralEuropean Union
So what: Tempers expectations that a state-funded European frontier model would become commercially self-sustaining or a genuine Nvidia/OpenAI/Anthropic-style competitor even if technically successful.
Leicht's base case is that the $500 billion coalition project does not happen, primarily because political awareness in national treasuries lags the required urgency by months, causing the effective price tag to keep rising — from $500 billion today to an estimated $600 billion in six months, and toward roughly $1 trillion after further delay.
prediction🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: highmiddle powerscoalition governments
So what: A high-conviction call that a state-backed rival to US frontier labs is unlikely near-term; if unexpected coalition funding news emerges, it would materially update this base case.
≈ “So $500 billion is a price tag now. If we convince people to do it, we’re going to have to tell them in six months that now it costs $600 billion.” paraphrase
Leicht's rough estimate for the compute a coalition frontier project would need is on the order of 3 million Blackwell-class chips — more than are currently available to purchase given Nvidia's limited supply — making chip acquisition, not just funding, a binding constraint.
data_point🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: mediumNvidiaBlackwell
So what: Quantifies the hardware bottleneck for any non-US frontier effort; a useful reference point for judging whether announced sovereign AI compute deals are anywhere close to frontier scale.
≈ “something like 3 million Blackwell-class chips for building this thing” paraphrase
Leicht notes much of a frontier lab's effective talent base today consists of coding agents rather than only human engineers, so a coalition project without access to leading tools like Codex or Claude Code would be materially slower to build a competitive model, and the gap would widen the longer that access is denied.
framework🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: mediumcoding agentsCodexClaude Code
So what: Identifies coding-agent access as an underappreciated AI-competitiveness bottleneck alongside chips — worth tracking as a leading indicator of whether any non-US lab can realistically catch up.
≈ “much of the talent base of frontier labs today are already coding agents” paraphrase
Leicht expects Nvidia to be commercially motivated to sell chips broadly to diversify its buyer base, but expects the US government to resist Nvidia selling at scale to a coalition explicitly aiming for frontier parity, since that carries different security optics than routine commercial sales or even sales to China's diffusion-stage buyers.
causal🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: mediumNvidiaUnited StatesChina
So what: Suggests chip-export policy risk for any coalition AI project is asymmetric: Nvidia's commercial incentives favor the deal, but US political/security incentives cut against it, making export-license politics a key catalyst to watch.
Leicht argues the main leverage point for a coalition to secure chips and coding-agent access is the semiconductor supply chain itself — particularly ASML in the Netherlands, plus Japanese and South Korean high-bandwidth-memory and semiconductor-equipment suppliers — since Nvidia and the US depend on these upstream inputs to build chips at all.
framework🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: mediumASMLNetherlandsJapanSouth KoreaNvidia
So what: Identifies ASML and Japanese/Korean semiconductor-equipment suppliers as the practical geopolitical leverage points in any allied AI-access negotiation, relevant for assessing export-control risk around these firms.
Leicht believes US policymakers currently do not take a middle-power government-led AGI project seriously, expecting it to fail like past catch-up projects, which paradoxically means the US may not act early to block chip exports — creating a limited window for a coalition to get chips online before Washington reassesses the threat.
contrarian🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: mediumUnited Statesmiddle powers
So what: Implies a narrow, closing arbitrage window: a credible coalition project might acquire chips precisely because it is currently underestimated in DC, before political attention catches up.
Taxing and Cushioning the AI Transition 9
Leicht argues a US government equity stake in AI companies — an idea associated with Bernie Sanders and reportedly under White House consideration — is a bad policy because even a 10x-100x rise in AI-lab valuations would not come close to funding large-scale social disruption relief, since the broader economy dwarfs the labs even at that scale.
contrarian🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: highBernie SandersUS governmentAI labs
So what: A direct, high-conviction pushback against a live US policy proposal; investors should weight equity-stake-in-AI-labs proposals as economically insufficient even if politically popular.
Leicht warns that a US equity stake tied to AI-lab valuations (e.g., a Trump account funded with AI equity) would create a strong government incentive to under-regulate those labs, since tougher AI regulation would directly reduce the value of the government's own holdings.
causal🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: highUS governmentAI labs
So what: Flags a specific conflict-of-interest mechanism worth monitoring: any formal US government equity stake in frontier AI firms should raise flags about weakened future safety regulation.
For non-US allies, Leicht argues the closest workable analogue to an AI-lab equity stake is a genuinely diversified sovereign wealth fund that includes AI labs as one hedge among many, rather than a narrow fund that only buys stakes in a single AI developer.
framework🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: mediumAnthropicsovereign wealth funds
So what: A concrete design principle for any allied sovereign-AI-investment vehicle: diversification across the AI stack (or broader markets), not concentrated single-company exposure.
≈ “the stupid “I only buy Anthropic stock” version of the sovereign wealth fund” paraphrase
Leicht prefers raising the corporate income tax over a narrow AI-usage 'token tax' (which would blunt AI adoption and competitiveness) or a broad consumption tax (which is politically toxic because it appears as a visible line-item, e.g. on a food-delivery order, even though most of its burden actually falls on companies).
thesis🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: mediumcorporate income taxconsumption tax
So what: Gives a concrete policy preference to watch for: corporate-tax-rate proposals are the more likely funding mechanism for AI-transition safety nets than usage-based AI taxes.
Leicht expects a massive revenue crisis for treasuries broadly as economic activity shifts from heavily taxed human labour toward lightly taxed capital and AI agents, estimating that naively moving one unit of revenue from labour to capital could lose half or more of the associated tax revenue given current corporate tax rates (~25%) and capital investment tax breaks.
data_point🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: mediumcorporate taxlabour markets
So what: A quantified warning about a structural fiscal shock as AI displaces labour income; relevant for anticipating future corporate tax hikes or new capital-taxation regimes across developed economies.
Leicht argues capital-flight risk from AI-heavy firms is more limited than feared because compute-intensive firms remain physically anchored to data centres, meaning a coordinated 10-15% minimum corporate tax floor across allied countries could still capture meaningful revenue despite firms' nominal mobility.
framework🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: mediumdata centrescorporate tax
So what: Suggests physical data-centre location will remain a durable tax-revenue anchor even as firms become more digitally 'mobile,' informing expectations around future minimum-tax coordination among allies.
Leicht makes the counterintuitive recommendation that middle powers should make labour markets more flexible, not more rigid, as AI disruption arrives, because protecting current jobs risks a much worse later outcome (mass layoffs and higher structural unemployment) once protected firms get outcompeted by more AI-adaptive domestic or foreign rivals.
contrarian🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: highlabour marketsmiddle powers
So what: A specific, actionable labor-policy call that runs against the intuitive protectionist response to AI job displacement; useful for evaluating which countries' labor reforms are AI-transition-compatible.
≈ “you have to bite the bullet on the counterintuitive conclusion of making your labour market more flexible as the AI disruption comes in” paraphrase
Leicht proposes 'junior job subsidies' — paying firms to keep early-career workers employed alongside AI agents — as a bridge measure, distinguishing this cohort from older displaced industrial workers because young workers want career progression rather than just wage replacement, making simple wage or job guarantees insufficient to prevent political backlash.
framework🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: mediumlabour marketsjunior job subsidy
So what: A concrete, named policy mechanism to watch for as governments design AI-transition social safety nets targeted at early-career workers specifically.
Leicht expects AI-driven job disruption to be politically blamed on AI regardless of how well-designed the actual safety net is, especially when it coincides with weak macro sentiment, favoring the political appeal of restrictive policies (token taxes, human-in-the-loop mandates) that would slow AI adoption and further erode competitiveness.
prediction🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: mediumlabour marketsAI adoption
So what: Anticipates a political feedback loop where economic anxiety produces anti-AI-adoption policy that then worsens the competitiveness problem it was meant to solve — a risk factor for firms operating in jurisdictions prone to this dynamic.
Manufacturing as the New AGI Moat 4
Leicht's core near-term industrial policy recommendation is aggressive foreign direct investment screening, citing Bezos's new company Prometheus buying up manufacturing capacity as an example of outside actors moving to acquire undervalued industrial assets before their owners realize how valuable those assets will become once integrated with AI-driven robotics.
thesis🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: highPrometheusJeff Bezosforeign direct investment
So what: A concrete, actionable policy call (FDI screening on manufacturing assets) and a specific company (Prometheus/Bezos) to watch as a bellwether of outside capital moving early on the AI-manufacturing thesis.
Leicht argues that owning AI-compatible manufacturing capacity — factories with sensors and feedback loops that can report performance data back to a directing AI model — may become as scarce and valuable an asset as the frontier AI model itself, because building such a factory is nearly as hard as building the model.
framework🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: highmanufacturingroboticsAI models
So what: Identifies AI-integrable manufacturing capacity as an underpriced strategic asset class, relevant for investors evaluating industrial/robotics companies as potential AGI-economy bottleneck plays.
≈ “it’s almost as hard to build a factory that can do this as it is to build an AI model that can do that” paraphrase
Leicht warns that selling industrial assets today at a price that looks like a windfall risks giving away decades of future AGI-economy revenue, since buyers with better foresight about AI's trajectory can offer prices that look generous today but represent a steep discount to future value.
thesis🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: highmanufacturingforeign direct investment
So what: A direct warning to owners of industrial/manufacturing assets in middle-power countries against selling to well-capitalized foreign buyers without pricing in AI-driven future value.
≈ “this would have been your slice of the light cone: this is a super important piece of manufacturing; this would have secured your stake in the AGI economy for decades to come” paraphrase
Leicht lists specific steps to make manufacturing 'AI compatible' and thus attractive to frontier labs for cooperation: modularize production to react quickly to AI-directed changes, keep a workforce skilled enough to implement ad hoc modifications, avoid regulating away data-sharing with AI developers, and instrument plants with sensors that feed performance data back to the model.
framework🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: mediummanufacturingroboticsAI labs
So what: A practical checklist for industrial policymakers or manufacturers seeking to position their facilities as attractive AI-lab cooperation partners rather than acquisition targets.
Soft Nationalization of US AI Labs 3
Leicht's mainline scenario is that the US does not formally nationalize AI labs via executive order or legislation; instead it exerts control informally through government-linked observers embedded in labs, feedback loops to the executive branch, and implicit threats to use tools like export controls or the Defense Production Act.
prediction🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: highUnited StatesAI labsDefense Production Act
So what: Sets an expectation that US government control over frontier labs will show up as informal pressure and negotiated concessions rather than a single visible regulatory event — useful for interpreting future lab policy shifts.
Leicht expects ongoing, ad hoc 'messy negotiations' between the US administration and AI labs (citing the Fable episode as an example of this pattern) rather than a comprehensive nationalization framework, eventually producing only minimal executive orders that standardize parts of an otherwise informal back-and-forth.
prediction🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: mediumFableUnited StatesAI labs
So what: Predicts continued regulatory unpredictability for AI labs operating in the US, with policy emerging through case-by-case negotiation rather than clear statutory rules — a relevant uncertainty factor for AI-lab investors.
Leicht observes that the open policy debate ecosystem (what Dean Ball called the 'republic of letters' during the SB-1047 discussion) is being starved as more people with the best information move from public writing into inside-government or inside-lab roles, making external, credible analysis both scarcer and more necessary.
framework🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: mediumAI policy discourse
So what: Flags a growing information asymmetry: outside observers should expect less public visibility into frontier AI developments over time as expert discussion migrates behind closed doors inside labs and governments.
Pause, Slowdown, and the China Question 7
Leicht is skeptical of an AI 'pause,' arguing that the politically realistic coalition assembled to push for one (groups opposed to AI for unrelated reasons like jobs or environmental concerns) is unlikely to stay together long enough to negotiate the sophisticated international treaty a real pause would require, while a naive unilateral US pause would crash markets tied to AI infrastructure investment.
contrarian🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: highAI pauseUnited States
So what: A high-conviction case against a naive domestic AI pause as both politically fragile and financially destabilizing, relevant for assessing tail risk around pause-movement legislative proposals.
≈ “the first thing you do is you crash the stock market, because that is just super dependent on the infrastructure investments” paraphrase
Leicht distinguishes a 'pause' from a 'slowdown' that reallocates compute away from frontier R&D and toward inference, deployment, and societal hardening, viewing the slowdown version as more coordinated and potentially workable in principle.
framework🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: mediumAI slowdowncompute allocation
So what: Offers a more tractable near-term policy alternative to a hard pause that investors and policymakers could realistically see implemented, shifting compute demand from training toward inference infrastructure.
Leicht argues a symmetric global frontier-capability slowdown would be geopolitically asymmetric in practice, because China is not currently doing much frontier development and would disproportionately benefit from a slowdown while it catches up on semiconductor manufacturing and AI diffusion.
causal🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: mediumChinasemiconductor manufacturingfrontier AI
So what: Explains why the US is unlikely to accept a frontier-training slowdown on its own terms, a key structural obstacle for any international AI-slowdown treaty proposal.
Leicht argues any credible US-China AI slowdown deal would need matched Chinese concessions on semiconductor indigenization and AI diffusion, not just frontier training limits, and considers verifying Chinese compliance on those broader industrial-policy fronts far harder than verifying a narrow training-compute cap.
contrarian🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: mediumChinaUnited Statessemiconductor manufacturing
So what: Highlights verification of Chinese industrial policy (not just compute caps) as the binding constraint on any credible bilateral AI-slowdown treaty, a key risk factor for such negotiations succeeding.
Leicht considers a blanket 'ban on superintelligence' operationally meaningless since narrow AI systems are already superhuman at specific strategically relevant tasks like large-scale data and image analysis; he views banning closed recursive self-improvement (RSI) loops within labs as the more operationalizable and defensible policy target.
framework🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: mediumrecursive self-improvementsuperintelligence
So what: Reframes the AI-safety policy debate away from vague 'superintelligence bans' toward a specific, monitorable target (closed RSI loops), useful for evaluating the seriousness of future AI-safety legislative proposals.
≈ “this is like the lazy version of this “Do you want to ban calculators?” thing” paraphrase
Leicht says he would feel much more comfortable with a US 'decisive strategic advantage' in AI if it were constrained by both classic constitutional separation of powers and broader Western-alliance-level distribution of control, versus a scenario of tight amalgamation between labs and the US security apparatus with few checks, which he explicitly does not want to see.
thesis🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: mediumUnited StatesAI labsdecisive strategic advantage
So what: Articulates a specific governance condition (alliance-level and constitutional checks) that would distinguish an acceptable vs. dangerous concentration of AI-driven geopolitical power — a lens for evaluating future US AI-security policy moves.
≈ “I’d feel a lot better about a Western alliance with a strategic advantage, with the DSA, than just an unconstrained US.” paraphrase
Leicht argues that broadening the set of actors with genuine leverage over powerful-AI deployment decisions (more middle powers able to withdraw compute or supply-chain inputs) narrows the range of possible outcomes and reduces loss-of-control risk driven by a single volatile actor making unconstrained decisions, even though it also constrains upside from bold unilateral moves.
framework🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: mediummiddle powersloss of control
So what: Provides a structural argument for why distributing AI-related leverage across allies (not just the US) could be a net safety positive, relevant to debates about centralizing vs. distributing control over frontier AI deployment.
Country Winners and Losers in the AGI Economy 2
Leicht is bullish on Germany's long-run AI position specifically because its skilled workforce, large industrial (rather than service-heavy) economy, and existing bilateral relationships are the right assets to extract counter-leverage in a US-led AGI economy, contingent on Germany fixing generic, non-AI-specific problems like labour-market flexibility and returning to 3-4% growth.
thesis🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: mediumGermany
So what: A specific country call: Germany's AI-era competitiveness hinges on generic domestic reforms (labor flexibility, growth) rather than AI-specific policy, useful for evaluating German economic-policy news through an AI lens.
Leicht is comparatively bearish on Singapore, Hong Kong, and even the UK for the AGI era specifically, because their heavily service-based economies are the segment most exposed to disruption by AI agents, meaning strong current economic performance does not predict resilience once AGI-level automation arrives.
contrarian🗣 Anton Leichtconviction: mediumSingaporeHong KongUnited Kingdom
So what: Flags service-economy hubs (Singapore, Hong Kong, UK) as structurally more exposed to AI-driven disruption than their current strong growth numbers would suggest, a relevant risk factor for country/sector allocation.

Hidden Forces · Demetri Kofinas 2 episode(s) · 43 insights

The Strong Do What They Can and Suﬀer What They Must | Jonathan Kirshner74 min26 insights2026-07-13
▶ Watch on YouTube
Political scientist Jonathan Kirshner argues the real lesson of Thucydides's Peloponnesian War is that great powers are undone by their own hubris and restlessness, not by shifts in the balance of power, and applies that lens directly to the U.S., framing Iraq, Afghanistan, the 2008 financial crisis, and the current Iran strikes as expressions of unconstrained imperial overreach. He contends Trump-era foreign policy defies standard IR theory and realism and is better explained as a 'personalist kleptocracy,' warning that depleted Middle East munitions stockpiles and eroding alliance credibility are directly weakening the U.S. position against China in East Asia.
Reinterpreting Thucydides: Hubris, Not Power Transition · 4The Political Logic of Restraint and Atrocity · 3American Hubris: Iraq, Afghanistan, and the Financial Crisis · 4Iran Strikes and the Cost to America's China Pivot · 3Trump Foreign Policy as Personalist Kleptocracy · 4Alliance Credibility Erosion and the Stakes in East Asia · 4Domestic Institutional Erosion and Executive Power · 2Wealth as a Gateway to Political Power · 2
Reinterpreting Thucydides: Hubris, Not Power Transition 4
Graham Allison's 'Thucydides Trap' framework, which argues rising Athenian power provoked Spartan fear and caused the Peloponnesian War, rests on a shallow, decontextualized reading of Thucydides and misrepresents the historian's actual argument.
contrarian🗣 Jonathan Kirshnerconviction: highGraham AllisonThucydidesAthensSparta
So what: Investors and policymakers using the 'Thucydides Trap' as a framework for US-China conflict risk may be relying on a flawed historical analogy that overstates the inevitability of great-power war.
