One in five Americans will be over age 65 by 2030, up from about one in six in 2020 and roughly one in twenty in 1920, implying the US economy is shifting from a growth-oriented profile toward one increasingly focused on maintenance and eldercare, with healthcare employment becoming more important.
prediction🗣 Kyla Scanlon, economist/authorconviction: highdemographicshealthcareaging population
So what: An accelerating shift toward an older population profile supports a longer-term overweight thesis on healthcare/eldercare services and labor, while implying slower trend GDP growth and different equity-market leadership than in a younger, growth-heavy economy.

What Bitcoin Did · Peter McCormack 1 episode(s) · 30 insights

Britain Was Taken Over By The Anywheres | David Goodhart100 min30 insights2026-07-14
▶ Watch on YouTube
In a wide-ranging What Bitcoin Did interview, author David Goodhart argues AI could finally erode the status and job security of Britain's overproduced graduate class the way globalization hollowed out blue-collar manufacturing workers, and connects that shift to his 'anywhere vs somewhere' framework for explaining Brexit, populism, and the UK's unresolved 2029 election-realignment risk. Host Peter McCormack presses him on UK tax burdens, small-business regulation, and the money-supply/asset-inflation dynamic, while Goodhart lays out concrete policy proposals on land tax, social care wages, and pension-fund UK equity allocation.
AI and the Future of Graduate Labor · 5UK Fiscal Drag and Tax Policy · 6UK Startup Ecosystem and Capital Allocation Gaps · 3Populism and the 2029 Election Risk · 8Small Business and Regulatory Burden · 3Social Care as an Underinvested Public Service · 3Demographic and Class Realignment · 2
AI and the Future of Graduate Labor 5
David Goodhart argues AI could do to Britain's 'graduate elite' what globalization did to factory workers, ending the automatic path to high-status, well-paid jobs for generalist university graduates who aren't in the top 5-10% of their field.
thesis🗣 David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)conviction: highAIuniversity graduatesUK labor market
So what: Signals a structural repricing of white-collar/graduate labor risk from AI, shifting the disruption narrative from the globalization-era blue-collar story to upper-white-collar workers.
“This could be the end of the line for people with generalist academic skills.” ▶ 0:38
Goodhart expects AI's disruption of white-collar work to unfold more slowly than commonly feared, drawing an analogy to historical fears that cars replacing horses would cause mass unemployment.
framework🗣 David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)conviction: mediumAIautomationlabor market
So what: Tempers aggressive near-term AI-unemployment shock narratives that some market commentary is pricing in.
“everybody feared that there that there'd be mass unemployment of all the young lads who looked after horses.” ▶ 2:17
Goodhart singles out junior clerical, legal and accountancy work as already largely automated or semi-automated and set to shrink further under AI, and explicitly flags coding as a vulnerable career choice because AI can now do it.
prediction🗣 David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)conviction: highAIlegal servicesaccountancycoding jobs
So what: Points to specific white-collar job categories facing near-term AI displacement risk, relevant to wage/employment forecasts in legal, accounting and junior software roles.
“perhaps I should train as a plumber or an electrician or um not so much a coder obviously cuz cuz AI can do coding” ▶ 6:26
Goodhart predicts skilled trades will see rising status and pay as AI erodes graduate-job premiums, citing the UK's national shortage of welders who can earn £60,000-£70,000 within three to four years of training.
data_point🗣 David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)conviction: mediumskilled tradesweldersUK labor market
So what: A concrete wage data point supporting a thesis of rising trade compensation relative to entry-level graduate salaries, relevant to vocational-training and trade-labor investment themes.
“we have we have a national shortage of welders. You know, within three or four years of training as a welder you can be earning 60 or 70 grand.” ▶ 7:02
Goodhart suggests that as AI erodes the graduate class's automatic claim to high status and income, it could make that class more politically sympathetic to blue-collar workers already displaced by globalization, potentially softening the UK's 'anywhere vs somewhere' political divide.
thesis🗣 David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)conviction: mediumAIpolitical realignmentgraduate class
So what: A second-order political-economy implication: AI-driven status compression among elites could reduce rather than deepen UK political polarization, relevant to forecasting future policy coalitions.
UK Fiscal Drag and Tax Policy 6
Goodhart says UK earners between roughly £60,000-£70,000 and £150,000 face effective marginal tax rates of about 70-80% once lost personal allowances and child benefit tapering stack up, which he says is pushing skilled people to relocate to Dubai and the US.
data_point🗣 David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)conviction: highUK tax policymarginal tax ratesDubaibrain drain
So what: A quantifiable UK tax-cliff problem that can be tracked as a leading indicator of high-earner emigration and talent flight from Britain.
“if you're earning anything between sort of 60 70 and a kind of 150 grand, you you have all of these points where your marginal tax rate uh is sort of 70 or 80%.” ▶ 35:09
Goodhart states the UK now has the most progressive tax system in Europe when comparing the tax burden on the top 10% of earners to the middle 10%, exceeding even the Scandinavian countries.
data_point🗣 David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)conviction: highUK tax policyincome inequality
So what: Challenges the 'UK under-taxes the rich' narrative sometimes used to justify further wealth-tax proposals, relevant context for UK fiscal/tax-policy debates.
“we now have the most um progressive tax system in the whole of Europe.” ▶ 34:44
Goodhart argues the UK's fiscal deterioration is mainly attributable to COVID-era furlough spending rather than lingering scarring from the 2008 financial crisis, which he says turned out to be comparatively small.
causal🗣 David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)conviction: mediumUK fiscal policyCOVID furlough scheme2008 financial crisis
So what: Reframes the source of UK structural deficit pressure toward pandemic-era stimulus design rather than the GFC, relevant to assessing UK gilt/fiscal-risk narratives.
“The real scarring was was the was COVID and the furlough scheme.” ▶ 54:48
Peter McCormack and David Goodhart agree that continual expansion of the money supply, such as COVID-era furlough financing, transfers value upward to existing asset owners rather than to wage earners.
causal🗣 Peter McCormack (host)conviction: mediummonetary policyinflationasset ownerswealth inequality
So what: A Cantillon-effect framing directly relevant to hard-asset/bitcoin investors: inflationary financing benefits existing asset holders, a rationale often used for scarce-asset allocation.
“it is a way it does extract value upwards rather than downwards.” ▶ 55:34
Goodhart proposes a land value tax on unproductive wealth (using 'the Duke of Westminster' as his example) as an alternative revenue source that wouldn't penalize entrepreneurs, suggesting it could replace stamp duty and possibly council tax.
framework🗣 David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)conviction: mediumland taxstamp dutycouncil taxUK tax reform
So what: A specific tax-reform proposal that would reshape UK property/land taxation, relevant for real estate and land-holding investors watching for future UK tax-reform risk.
“it's a way of trying to trying to get a few more billion out of the Duke of Westminster without without crushing the next sort of tech uni- unicorn creator.”
Goodhart argues the UK's relatively low capital gains tax is a genuine economic asset worth protecting rather than raising.
contrarian🗣 David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)conviction: mediumcapital gains taxUK tax policy
So what: Pushes back against current UK political pressure to raise capital gains tax, framing it as competitiveness-preserving policy risk to watch.
“the fact that we have quite low capital gains tax is an is an asset.” ▶ 56:44
UK Startup Ecosystem and Capital Allocation Gaps 3
Goodhart cites economist Andy Haldane's claim that the UK has more tech unicorns than any country outside the US and China, but says most get sold to US acquirers rather than scaling independently, due to smaller UK market size and a shallower pool of risk capital than in America.
data_point🗣 David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)conviction: mediumUK tech unicornsAndy Haldaneventure capitalUS acquirers
So what: Explains a persistent structural leakage in UK tech value creation where exit value accrues to US acquirers, relevant to UK tech-sector investment and IPO-pipeline analysis.
“we have the highest number of tech unicorns, I think, anywhere outside China and America.” ▶ 57:55
Goodhart states UK pension funds allocate only about 4% of assets to UK equities despite successive reform efforts by Jeremy Hunt and Rachel Reeves aimed at raising that share.
data_point🗣 David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)conviction: highUK pension fundsJeremy HuntRachel ReevesUK equities
So what: A concrete, trackable data point on UK equity home-bias underinvestment, relevant to UK equity market liquidity/valuation debates and whether pension reform will actually move flows into UK-listed companies.
“they invest like 4% in UK equities. I mean, it's ridiculous.” ▶ 58:43
Goodhart highlights SearchLand, a UK PropTech startup founded by his son and university friends that scrapes local-authority planning data nationwide, as having become the largest company in its niche with strong venture backing despite the broader difficult UK business climate.
anecdote🗣 David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)conviction: mediumSearchLandPropTechUK venture capital
So what: A named, concrete example of a scaling UK startup in the property-data/PropTech niche, useful as a specific data point for UK proptech deal-sourcing.
“they're now I think the biggest the biggest company in the sector and they and they they got good people and they got they got a bunch of capital backing and they're doing very well.”
Populism and the 2029 Election Risk 8
Goodhart frames UK politics through an 'anywhere' (mobile, university-educated, roughly 25-30% of the population) versus 'somewhere' (rooted, place-attached, roughly 40-50% of the population) divide, arguing this cultural/education-based split, not the EU itself, was the real driver of the Brexit vote.
framework🗣 David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)conviction: highBrexitanywhere/somewhere divideUK electorate
So what: A durable framework for reading UK populist voting patterns as an elite-overrepresentation revolt rather than pure economic protest, useful for forecasting future UK election outcomes and policy swings.
“the European Union was kind of collateral damage in that vote.” ▶ 13:44
Goodhart argues the 2019 Boris Johnson-led Conservative 'realignment' that could have permanently reoriented UK politics toward 'somewhere' voters failed because of COVID, abandonment of the 'leveling up' agenda, and a post-Brexit 'quasi open door' immigration policy adopted to fill post-pandemic labor shortages.
causal🗣 David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)conviction: highBoris JohnsonBrexitleveling upUK immigration policy
So what: Explains why post-Brexit UK politics remains unsettled rather than realigned, implying continued policy unpredictability risk through the current parliament.
“they didn't have time to to to make Brexit work.” ▶ 25:24
Goodhart's central 2029 UK election scenario is that only a Conservative-Reform coalition can reverse what he calls the 'overshoots' of recent decades (economic overregulation, high immigration, cultural marginalization).
prediction🗣 David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)conviction: mediumReform UKConservative Party2029 UK election
So what: Lays out a specific electoral-coalition scenario for 2029 that would determine UK policy direction on tax, immigration and regulation, relevant for UK political-risk positioning.
“the only way you do that is by electing a Tory reform coalition government. I mean, that's the only way I can see in 2029.” ▶ 1:29:33
Goodhart warns that if Rupert Lowe's breakaway 'Restore' party (associated with figures he names as Harrison Pitt, Connor Tomlinson and Charlie Downs) splits 2-3% of the vote away from the Tories or Reform in key constituencies, it could let a Labour/Green/Lib Dem government back into power in 2029, which he calls a serious risk.
prediction🗣 David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)conviction: highRupert LoweRestoreHarrison PittConnor TomlinsonCharlie DownsReform UK
So what: Names the specific new political entity and personnel to track as a wildcard that could fragment the UK populist-right vote ahead of 2029.
“Restore taking 2 or 3% off either the Tories or Reform in different constituencies” ▶ 1:30:43
Goodhart calls Kemi Badenoch's Conservative Party politically 'under-priced,' expecting economically frustrated but socially liberal higher-professional-class voters who won't back Reform to eventually swing to the Tories under her.
prediction🗣 David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)conviction: mediumKemi BadenochConservative Partyhigher professional class
So what: Identifies a specific voter-migration thesis relevant for gauging UK political sentiment shifts and Conservative Party recovery odds.
“the Tories are kind of under-priced.” ▶ 1:12:49
Goodhart names Danny Kruger (Reform UK) as the most impressive politician he's seen for his ability to discuss non-economic issues like family life and social cohesion, contrasting him with an otherwise heavily economics-focused political class.
anecdote🗣 David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)conviction: mediumDanny KrugerReform UK
So what: Flags a specific rising political figure worth tracking within Reform UK's leadership bench.
“Danny's Danny's the best he's got.” ▶ 1:15:14
Goodhart is skeptical Andy Burnham can bridge the anywhere-somewhere divide, arguing Manchester itself functions as 'the London of the north' (about 25% students) and keeps pulling the brightest people away from struggling nearby towns like Rochdale, Bury and Bolton.
contrarian🗣 David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)conviction: mediumAndy BurnhamManchesterUK regional inequality
So what: Challenges the popular narrative of Burnham/Manchester as a model for UK regional revival, useful for assessing 'levelling up' and UK regional-investment narratives.
“Manchester is essentially the kind of London of the north.” ▶ 1:32:00
Goodhart predicts that if Andy Burnham becomes prime minister and fails to deliver visible change like Keir Starmer, voters who defected to Zack Polanski's Greens will likely return there rather than stay with Labour.
prediction🗣 David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)conviction: mediumAndy BurnhamZack PolanskiGreen PartyLabour Party
So what: A specific voter-flow prediction relevant to modeling UK left-of-center vote fragmentation risk ahead of 2029.
“those voters who went off to Zack Polanski come back to Burnham will probably go back to Zack Polanski.” ▶ 1:31:42
Small Business and Regulatory Burden 3
Peter McCormack says UK small businesses like his coffee shop face such heavy combined business rates, energy costs and VAT that only a thin margin remains for profit, making it hard to run a business without being personally hands-on full-time.
anecdote🗣 Peter McCormack (host)conviction: highUK small businessVATbusiness ratesenergy costs
So what: A first-hand, specific account of UK small-business cost-structure squeeze, relevant to UK SME/hospitality-sector margin and investment-risk assessment.
“if you take a cup of coffee, it's like this small sliver at the top you might have a chance to make a profit.” ▶ 36:51
McCormack argues UK employment rights and minimum-wage law have shifted work 'from a privilege to an entitlement,' which he links to difficulty finding and retaining reliable managers and staff for small businesses.
causal🗣 Peter McCormack (host)conviction: mediumUK employment lawminimum wagestaffing
So what: A labor-market friction claim from an active UK employer, relevant to hiring-cost and staffing-availability assessments for UK SMEs.
“we've turned work from a privilege to an entitlement through employment rights and minimum wage.” ▶ 36:36
McCormack recounts UK expats in the US telling him lower US taxes leave people with more money to spend, creating a more 'bustling economy,' while the UK by contrast is 'designed as an economy to take' rather than one that moves.
anecdote🗣 Peter McCormack (host)conviction: mediumUS tax policyUK tax policyeconomic growth
So what: An anecdotal but pointed comparative framing of US vs UK tax/growth dynamics shaping sentiment among UK entrepreneurs considering relocation.
“I could I could not go back to the UK cuz it's designed as an economy to take.” ▶ 32:53
Social Care as an Underinvested Public Service 3
Goodhart proposes creating a formal 'enhanced care practitioner' role with basic medical skills (taking vital signs, giving injections, drawing blood) to bridge social care and the NHS, arguing better preventative social care could reduce costly NHS hospital-bed occupancy.
framework🗣 David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)conviction: mediumsocial careNHSenhanced care practitioner
So what: A specific, actionable policy proposal for UK health/social-care workforce reform to track in future government announcements, relevant to healthcare-services investors.
“can take uh can take vital signs, can give injections, can take blood, can do” ▶ 43:50
Goodhart cites a data point that elderly falls cost the NHS roughly £4 billion a year, and argues social-care programs focused on balance-improving exercise could meaningfully cut that cost by reducing hospital admissions.
data_point🗣 David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)conviction: mediumNHSsocial careelderly falls
So what: A quantified NHS cost-saving lever (preventative social care/exercise programs) relevant to healthcare-cost and social-care-investment analysis.
“people falling over cost the NHS like sort of 4 billion pounds a year or something.” ▶ 46:32
Goodhart argues the UK should raise pay for social-care workers by roughly £1-£1.50 an hour above the national minimum wage via a dedicated 'social care minimum wage' to address a shortage of care workers as more women who once filled those roles now pursue higher-education professional careers instead.
framework🗣 David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)conviction: mediumsocial careminimum wageUK labor supply
So what: A specific, costed policy lever for addressing UK social-care staffing shortages, relevant for tracking future wage-policy changes affecting the care sector's cost base.
“we just need to uh we need to have a a special social care minimum wage that's, you know, perhaps a pound, a pound 50 an hour higher than the than the national um minimum wage.”
Demographic and Class Realignment 2
Goodhart says the UK's 'higher professional class' has grown from roughly 3-4% of the population in the 1960s-70s to about 12% today (35-40% including lower professionals), and this expansion has driven its Conservative vote share down from about 70% in 1974 to roughly 15% now.
data_point🗣 David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)conviction: highhigher professional classUK voting patternsConservative Party
So what: A hard, quantified data point on the scale and voting-behavior shift of the UK's professional-managerial class, useful for modeling UK electoral demographics and policy implications on tax and regulation.
“back in '74 election, 2/3, 70% of high professional people voted Tory. Now, it's probably 15% or something.” ▶ 1:37:45
Goodhart confirms UK demographic projections point to the country no longer being majority-white by around 2060, framing this as a topic requiring open political discussion about downstream effects on voting blocs and social cohesion rather than being dismissed as inherently racist to raise.
data_point🗣 David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)conviction: mediumUK demographicsimmigration policy
So what: A concrete demographic data point shaping UK immigration-policy debate and long-horizon political-risk modeling.
“Oh, absolutely. 2060, yeah.” ▶ 1:28:51

0xResearch 1 episode(s) · 30 insights

Token Vs Equity, Venice AI, Robinhood Chain & Strategy's Recovery60 min30 insights2026-07-12
▶ Watch on YouTube
Blockworks research analysts Carlos and Kunal join host Luke to argue that Strategy's record $216M Bitcoin sale is paradoxically bullish, dig into the Venice AI equity-raise controversy as a case study in crypto's broken token-vs-equity disclosure norms (with MetaDAO cited as a fix), and trade the first week of Robinhood chain, favoring Syrup/Maple over Uniswap and Arbitrum.
Strategy's Bitcoin Sale and the Sentiment Inflection · 5Venice AI: Product Metrics and Tokenomics · 6Venice's Token-vs-Equity Rift and the Industry Disclosure Gap · 10Robinhood Chain Launch: Winners, Losers, and Pair Trades · 7Prediction Markets as a Real-Time Information Edge · 2
Strategy's Bitcoin Sale and the Sentiment Inflection 5
Strategy (MicroStrategy) sold about $216 million of Bitcoin, its largest single sale to date, executed under its new 'digital credit capital framework' which had guided the market toward the company being open to selling BTC.
data_point🗣 Luke, hostconviction: highStrategyMicroStrategyBitcoin
So what: Confirms Strategy has operationally shifted from pure accumulation to active balance-sheet management, a structural change watchers should price into future BTC supply/demand models.
“They sold about $216 million worth of Bitcoin, which uh I believe has been their their largest sale.” ▶ 1:13
The market's reaction to Strategy's Bitcoin sale flipped historical logic: previously, Strategy buying BTC was read as bullish and financing purchases via Stretch issuance became bad news as it drove new lows, but now Strategy selling BTC is being read as good news, with Strategy, Stretch, and Bitcoin all recovering and outperforming the Nasdaq since the sale.
contrarian🗣 Luke, hostconviction: mediumStrategyStretchBitcoinNasdaq
So what: A reversal in how the market interprets Saylor-driven flows is an early technical/sentiment signal that the prior bear-market narrative around Strategy-as-risk may be turning.
“what might have been bad news is treated as good news by the market” ▶ 2:51
Bitcoin ETFs saw combined inflows of over $500 million on the Monday and Tuesday following Strategy's sale, the highest inflow figure since early May, after record outflows in both May and June.
data_point🗣 guest, Blockworks research teamconviction: mediumBitcoin ETFsBitcoin
So what: Fresh ETF inflow strength after two months of record outflows is the most credible read on actual institutional demand returning, worth tracking as a recovery confirmation signal.
“over $500 million in inflows for the ETFs, which to me is the most credible signal for actual appetite uh coming back for Bitcoin.” ▶ 3:08
The market now has clarity on how Michael Saylor plans to bring Strategy's Stretch preferred stock back to par (enough cash to fund preferred dividends without further BTC sales for now), but the situation remains fundamentally Saylor-driven and fragile — a renewed macro sell-off or a roughly 10% Bitcoin drawdown could put Stretch back under pressure and reopen uncertainty about further forced BTC sales.
framework🗣 guest, Blockworks research teamconviction: mediumStrategyStretchBitcoinMichael Saylor
So what: Investors should treat the current calm as conditional, not resolved — a fresh BTC drawdown is the trigger to watch for a repeat of the Stretch-discount/forced-selling dynamic.
Strategy's balance sheet carries a hurdle rate and leveraged assumptions tied to Bitcoin's price, so continued BTC underperformance over a long horizon would resurface the same issues: Stretch trading at a discount and likely further Bitcoin sales.
causal🗣 Luke, hostconviction: mediumStrategyStretchBitcoin
So what: Frames Strategy/Stretch as a leveraged, path-dependent proxy on Bitcoin's price trend rather than a simple buy-and-hold vehicle — watch BTC's trend, not just spot price, to gauge Stretch risk.
“there's there's a hurdle rate on his balance sheet, and there're levered assumptions there” ▶ 5:03
Venice AI: Product Metrics and Tokenomics 6
Venice AI's dual-token model requires users to stake VVV and then lock it to mint DM tokens, where one DM entitles the holder to $1 of compute in perpetuity; despite ongoing token emissions and rising circulating supply, the share of holders staking VVV has stayed constant and the share locking VVV to mint DM has been steadily rising, indicating genuine demand for compute rather than pure speculation.
data_point🗣 Kunal, Blockworks researchconviction: highVenice AIVVVDM token
So what: Stable/rising staking and locking ratios despite dilution is a fundamentals-based signal that Venice's token demand is usage-driven, supporting a bullish read on the protocol independent of speculative flows.
“one DM gives you $1 of compute tokens in perpetuity.” ▶ 7:24
Venice is adding roughly 1,000-1,100 new paying subscribers per day (with free-user growth even more exponential), and subscription revenue is used to buy and burn VVV — though only new subscriptions currently count toward burns, not recurring ones.
data_point🗣 Kunal, Blockworks researchconviction: highVenice AIVVV
So what: Rapid, consistent subscriber growth funding a buy-and-burn mechanism is a directly trackable, fundamentals-linked catalyst for VVV token demand.
“almost every day they're adding like 1,000 to 1,100 new subscribers.” ▶ 8:47
Beyond subscription-funded burns, Venice has a discretionary burn mechanism: users who lock staked VVV to mint DM forgo about 20% of the emissions they would otherwise receive, and the team can choose to burn those forgone emissions.
framework🗣 Kunal, Blockworks researchconviction: mediumVenice AIVVVDM token
So what: A second, discretionary burn lever gives the Venice team additional control over VVV supply dynamics beyond organic subscription growth, relevant to modeling long-term token scarcity.
“they basically forgo like 20% of the emissions they're supposed to receive.” ▶ 9:25
Venice differentiates on privacy tiers ranging from anonymous access to frontier models (identity hidden but prompts still processed/retained), to a default private tier, up to TEE and end-to-end-encrypted tiers with stronger guarantees but reduced model access; its moat is being the easiest consumer front-end for private inference as mainstream providers like Claude begin adding KYC requirements to certain models.
framework🗣 Kunal, Blockworks researchconviction: mediumVenice AIClaudeChatGPT
So what: If large model providers keep tightening identity requirements, consumer demand for an easy private-inference alternative like Venice could structurally grow, a thesis worth monitoring via Venice's subscriber trend.
A significant share of early Venice Discord activity centers on building NSFW/AI-companion applications, a use case largely blocked on mainstream model providers, illustrating a concrete pocket of demand created precisely by competitors' content restrictions.
anecdote🗣 Kunal, Blockworks researchconviction: lowVenice AI
So what: NSFW/companion use cases are an early, if unglamorous, proof point of real willingness-to-pay for uncensored private inference — a niche but monetizable demand driver.
“these uh what do you call like AI companions or like NSFW AI companions” ▶ 13:32
Venice processes roughly 100 billion tokens per day with thin margins because a large base of free users consumes heavily subsidized compute; this cost structure — not simply a desire to extend runway — was the real rationale for the $65 million equity raise, aimed at expanding GPU capacity and reducing reliance on external infrastructure partners like Fal and Near Cloud to improve margins.
causal🗣 Kunal, Blockworks researchconviction: highVenice AIFalNear Cloud
So what: Explains the equity raise as a margin-improvement and capacity investment rather than distress financing, relevant context for judging whether the raise dilutes or strengthens the long-term VVV thesis.
“they consume at least like 100 billion tokens daily, and just some rough maths would tell you that the margins on this product is likely very thin” ▶ 18:53
Venice's Token-vs-Equity Rift and the Industry Disclosure Gap 10
Venice raised $65 million in equity at roughly a $1 billion valuation, and in doing so the team clarified that VVV is a compute-utility claim rather than an equity claim, with equity holders being the 'true owners' of Venice — a distinction that triggered a VVV sell-off from about $14-15 down to about $11.
data_point🗣 Kunal, Blockworks researchconviction: highVenice AIVVV
So what: Confirms VVV holders do not have equity upside in Venice the company; investors treating VVV as an equity proxy were repriced sharply once this was clarified.
“Venice raised if I'm not wrong 65 million at like a 1B valuation and that was basically an equity raise, right?” ▶ 10:19
The broader market reaction to the Venice equity raise signals investor fatigue with crypto's token-vs-equity dichotomy itself: investors don't inherently care whether they get exposure via a token or an equity, they just want one clear, well-defined instrument.
contrarian🗣 Carlos, Blockworks researchconviction: highVenice AIVVV
So what: Projects that keep both an ambiguous token and a separate equity instrument risk a structural valuation discount versus projects with one clean claim on value — a pattern to screen for across the sector.
“I don't think people would care if if you just had an equity or if you just had a token. Like they just want a clear way to to get exposure to something.” ▶ 0:09
Because it is unclear exactly how much of Venice's future revenue flows to equity holders versus token holders, the market applies a heavier discount to VVV's future growth than it would to an instrument with a clean, disclosed claim.
framework🗣 Kunal, Blockworks researchconviction: mediumVenice AIVVV
So what: Undisclosed revenue-split structures are a quantifiable valuation drag; clearer disclosure on the equity/token split would be a plausible re-rating catalyst for VVV.
“the market applies like a more hefty discount to like VVV's future growth.” ▶ 23:27
Circle is cited as the clean template for separating token and equity: Circle's publicly traded equity, USDC (a liability on Circle's balance sheet), and the underlying reserve assets are all distinctly delineated, whereas Venice's problem was that the market didn't know whether VVV sat as a liability (compute claim) or functioned as equity — a gap traditional finance would close via S-1-style disclosure and registration requirements that crypto issuers generally lack.
framework🗣 Luke, hostconviction: highCircleUSDCVenice AIVVV
So what: Provides a concrete disclosure template (clear balance-sheet placement of token vs. equity claims) that investors can use to screen which token/equity hybrids are lower-risk than others.
“the most extreme example it's so stupid it's obvious is like Circle” ▶ 25:36
At the time of taping, the market was valuing Venice's equity at roughly $1 billion and its VVV token at roughly $1.2 billion combined.
data_point🗣 Luke, hostconviction: mediumVenice AIVVV
So what: Gives a concrete relative-value anchor for anyone evaluating whether VVV or Venice equity is the better risk/reward at current pricing.
“so now we know the equity is valued like 1 billion, right? And the tokens at like, you know, 1.2 B.” ▶ 34:03
Given a choice between the two, Kunal would rather hold Venice's equity than VVV right now, because near-term growth is concentrated in the equity-funded GPU/data-center buildout rather than in the compute-utility token.
thesis🗣 Kunal, Blockworks researchconviction: mediumVenice AIVVV
So what: A near-term relative-value call favoring Venice's private equity over its liquid token, useful for anyone weighing exposure between the two instruments.
“I would rather own the equity at this point than the token.” ▶ 35:48
Despite preferring the token on ideological grounds, Carlos would long VVV over Venice equity, but flags a structural flaw: equity holders can capture upside that VVV holders cannot participate in, so a scenario where VVV underperforms while equity value keeps compounding leaves token holders locked out of the better outcome.
contrarian🗣 Carlos, Blockworks researchconviction: lowVenice AIVVV
So what: Highlights an asymmetric-risk problem in hybrid token/equity structures: token holders bear downside correlation to the business without guaranteed access to the equity's upside.
“I hate that there is an an equity um component that can gain value without mine getting value”
Pump.fun's 'ace round' equity raise let token holders convert VVV-style meme-coin tokens into equity at a fixed 2.5-to-1 ratio, giving token holders an option to gain equity exposure and letting the team route converted tokens to team members to align incentives — a structure other fully-circulating-supply tokens could replicate when they need to raise equity without diluting via new token issuance.
framework🗣 guest, Blockworks research teamconviction: mediumPump.fun
So what: A repeatable mechanism for projects with fully circulating token supply to raise equity capital while keeping token and equity holders aligned — a template other teams facing the Venice-style dilemma could adopt.
“I think 2.5 tokens is equal to one equity.” ▶ 38:28
MetaDAO's ownership-coin/ICO model is framed as the most credible structural fix to the token-vs-equity ambiguity problem, since the token itself is the equity claim, letting investors underwrite the company's fundamentals directly without separately diligencing what the token confers; MetaDAO's recent Lasso Finance raise attracted $25 million in commitments and it is now running a Credible Finance raise.
thesis🗣 guest, Blockworks research teamconviction: highMetaDAOLasso FinanceCredible Finance
So what: MetaDAO-style ICOs remove the token/equity ambiguity risk premium entirely, making them a structurally favored venue for early-stage crypto capital formation going forward.
“MetaDAO ICOs are consistently the easiest and fastest deals for us to underwrite.” ▶ 40:52
Across a report covering six liquid tokens (Ether.fi, Gnosis, Plasma, Avalanche, Jupiter, and Aave), roughly a third of the research time on each protocol — except one — went into determining what value the token itself confers and what the team has disclosed about value accrual, rather than assessing the underlying business, illustrating an industry-wide disclosure gap that keeps many tokens trading at a discount to their fundamentals.
data_point🗣 guest, Blockworks research teamconviction: mediumEther.fiGnosisPlasmaAvalancheJupiterAave
So what: Tokens whose value-accrual mechanics are clearly disclosed shouldn't need to trade at a discount to an equivalent equity claim; clarified disclosure is a plausible re-rating catalyst across most of the sector.
“at least a third of my time researching each protocol was looking at their token, what value it confers to holders” ▶ 43:30
Robinhood Chain Launch: Winners, Losers, and Pair Trades 7
One week after launch, Robinhood chain has around $260 million in TVL and roughly $500 million in daily DEX volume, with strong bridge inflows across multiple assets, marking a notably strong initial launch.
data_point🗣 Luke, hostconviction: highRobinhood Chain
So what: Fast accumulation of TVL and volume in week one is an early signal that Robinhood chain could sustain meaningful DeFi activity rather than fading like typical incentivized chain launches.
“TVL is up to 260 mil.” ▶ 45:30
CashCat, a meme coin referencing the name Robinhood was reportedly originally going to use for its company, became an early breakout runner on Robinhood chain and reached a multi-million-dollar market cap, illustrating meme-driven speculative flow accompanying the chain's launch.
anecdote🗣 Luke, hostconviction: lowRobinhood ChainCashCat
So what: Meme-coin runners are a recurring pattern on new chain launches and a short-term speculative trade, though not indicative of durable chain fundamentals.
“this was what Robin Hood originally wanted its company name to be.” ▶ 46:00
A proposed pair trade is long Syrup (Maple) versus short Uniswap and Arbitrum: Robinhood chain's Morpho vault targets up to 7% yield by allocating between Syrup-USDG and Ethena's USDe, and since Syrup offers a higher standalone stablecoin yield (~5%) than comparable options, a large share of vault deposits should flow through Syrup, while Uniswap's fee capture stays minimal despite volume and Arbitrum's revenue take is only about 1-2% of FDV once bot-driven meme-coin transaction fees normalize.
contrarian🗣 guest, Blockworks research teamconviction: mediumSyrupMapleUniswapArbitrumEthenaRobinhood Chain
So what: Directly actionable pair trade: Robinhood chain's yield mechanics disproportionately benefit Syrup/Maple, while Uniswap and Arbitrum's volume gains are seen as unlikely to convert into proportional token value.
“Syrup stands to benefit a fair bit from this uh this integration. So, I think a long Syrup trade makes sense.” ▶ 49:06
Maple's total outstanding loans are at all-time highs, with almost no visible impact from Bitcoin's roughly 50% drawdown on the chart, indicating resilient product-market fit that is likely to accelerate further with Robinhood chain's integration.
data_point🗣 Luke, hostconviction: highMapleBitcoin
So what: Maple's loan growth decoupling from Bitcoin's drawdown supports the thesis that its lending business has durable demand independent of broader crypto market sentiment.
“this is total outstanding loans for for Maple. Uh it's at all-time highs.” ▶ 51:20
USDG, Robinhood chain's base stablecoin, has roughly $3 billion in circulating supply and is currently the fastest-growing top-10 stablecoin by percentage over the trailing 30 days, helped by DeFi protocols on Robinhood chain (e.g. Maple's new USDG pool, already around $220 million) having strong incentives to deepen USDG liquidity.
data_point🗣 guest, Blockworks research teamconviction: mediumUSDGRobinhood ChainMaple
So what: USDG's growth is structurally tied to Robinhood chain's DeFi ecosystem expanding, making USDG supply growth a proxy indicator to track for the chain's broader adoption.
“Of the top 10 stable coins, USDG is actually the fastest growing in percentage terms of the past 30 days.” ▶ 52:35
Uniswap is currently the only DEX live on Robinhood chain, and Robinhood chain has already become Uniswap's second-largest chain by volume at $528 million, trailing only Ethereum mainnet's $770 million — a meaningful, non-trivial volume share that could make Robinhood chain Uniswap's top chain within weeks.
data_point🗣 Carlos, Blockworks researchconviction: mediumUniswapRobinhood ChainEthereum
So what: Contrary to the view that Robinhood chain's meme-driven volume is insignificant for Uniswap, its rapid rise to second-largest-chain status is a concrete bullish data point for Uniswap volume share, though durability beyond the meme-coin phase remains uncertain.
“Second largest chain by volume for Uniswap is already Robinhood chain, 528 million dollars.” ▶ 55:09
As Robinhood chain matures beyond its initial meme-coin-driven trading phase toward tokenized equities and real-world assets, Uniswap is expected to face competition from other emerging RWA-focused trading venues such as Rialto and Lighter, which could cap how much of that future volume accrues to Uniswap.
framework🗣 guest, Blockworks research teamconviction: lowUniswapRialtoLighterRobinhood Chain
So what: Uniswap's current dominance on Robinhood chain shouldn't be assumed durable once tokenized equity/RWA trading scales, since specialized competitors are already positioning for that flow.
Prediction Markets as a Real-Time Information Edge 2
Watching Hyperliquid's HIP-4 sports prediction market during a live World Cup match, odds moved (e.g., falling for Egypt) ahead of the actual goal appearing on a delayed TV/stream broadcast, showing the prediction market reacts faster than typical broadcast delay allows.
anecdote🗣 Kunal, Blockworks researchconviction: mediumHyperliquidPolymarket
So what: Live sports prediction markets can function as a faster real-time information feed than broadcast video, a mechanism traders could exploit for latency edge in in-play betting or hedging.
“it is pretty cool to see like the market react way fast like way faster than than you have it on your stream.” ▶ 58:52
The order book on live sports prediction markets tends to visibly thin out right before high-stakes moments like a shot on goal or a penalty kick, since liquidity providers pull back rather than take on the risk of an imminent outcome-defining event.
framework🗣 guest, Blockworks research teamconviction: lowHyperliquidPolymarket
So what: A thinning order book on a live prediction market can itself serve as a leading indicator that a decisive in-game moment is about to occur, before the event is visible on a delayed broadcast.
“you'll suddenly see the order book thin out cuz no one wants to provide liquidity.” ▶ 59:25

Perun 1 episode(s) · 28 insights

North Korea's Weird New Navy - New Destroyers & A North Korean Blue Water Navy?56 min28 insights2026-07-12
▶ Watch on YouTube
North Korea has begun commissioning 5,000-ton missile-heavy destroyers (hulls 51/52) and is aiming for two large surface combatants a year plus a future 10,000-ton design, but the host argues this blue-water buildup is a poor investment relative to North Korea's cheaper, more survivable existing options (ballistic-missile submarines, road-mobile missile launchers) given its weak economy and the overwhelming regional anti-ship/air-power threat from South Korea, Japan and the US.
Russia-North Korea Wartime Alliance Enables Naval Modernization · 4The KPA Navy's Dismal Pre-Buildup Baseline · 3Parallel Track: Ballistic-Missile Submarines and SLBMs · 3The New 5,000-Ton Destroyers: Specs and Production Targets · 5Distinctly North Korean Design Quirks · 3The Investment-Logic Critique: Why Build These At All? · 5North Korea's Anemic Economy Underscores the Resource Misallocation · 3Regional Strategic Implications · 2
Russia-North Korea Wartime Alliance Enables Naval Modernization 4
Since the war in Ukraine began, North Korea has supplied Russia with artillery shells, tactical ballistic missiles, and tens of thousands of troops (including combat in the Kursk region) in exchange for billions of dollars in hard currency and technical assistance, funding much of its current military modernization.
causal🗣 hostconviction: highNorth KoreaRussia
So what: This Russia-DPRK exchange is a key funding and technology source behind North Korea's new naval buildup, making the partnership's durability a leading indicator for further KPA modernization.
Because new UN sanctions on North Korea require Russian assent and existing sanctions enforcement partly relies on Russia policing shipments to itself, the Russia-DPRK alliance has both blocked new sanctions and undermined enforcement of the existing regime.
causal🗣 hostconviction: highNorth KoreaRussia
So what: Effective sanctions relief via Russia's veto power is a structural, durable channel for North Korean rearmament that isn't contingent on any single shipment or deal.
“kind of like putting Alapone in charge of prohibition enforcement” ▶ 3:08
The North Korean short-range air defense system mounted on the new destroyers looks visually near-identical to Russia's Pantsir-M naval air defense system, suggesting direct Russian technical assistance despite Pyongyang's claim the ship was built with no outside help.
contrarian🗣 hostconviction: mediumNorth KoreaRussiaPantsir-M
So what: If confirmed, direct Russian systems transfer would materially accelerate North Korean naval capability beyond what indigenous industry alone could deliver, and is a signal worth tracking in future ship reveals.
Rather than directing its Russia-driven financial and technological windfall toward improving living standards after a difficult COVID period, Kim Jong-un's government channeled most of it into a broad military buildup that now explicitly includes the navy.
thesis🗣 hostconviction: highNorth KoreaKim Jong-un
So what: Confirms the regime's revealed spending priority is military hardware over civilian welfare, a durable pattern for forecasting future resource allocation.
The KPA Navy's Dismal Pre-Buildup Baseline 3
Kim Jong-un himself has publicly described the Korean People's Army Navy as having gone through 'over 70 years of stagnation' and called it 'the weakest of all the services of our armed forces,' an unusually blunt admission from the regime's own leadership.
data_point🗣 Kim Jong-un (quoted by host)conviction: highNorth KoreaKim Jong-un
So what: The regime's own harsh self-assessment explains the political urgency behind the current naval investment push and signals it is a top-down, leader-driven priority.
“over 70 years of stagnation” ▶ 5:02
As of the 2021 Military Balance, the Korean People's Army Navy numbered only about 60,000 personnel versus more than 1 million in the ground forces, and its most common vessel type was roughly 142 aging torpedo boats requiring close-range attacks with 1960s-era torpedoes.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: highNorth Korea
So what: Quantifies just how far behind the KPA Navy started, making the leap to 5,000-ton guided-missile destroyers a genuinely large capability jump rather than incremental modernization.
Prior to this buildup, North Korea's only submarine fleet of scale was 20 Romeo-class boats based on a Soviet design discontinued in 1961 and first delivered via China in 1973, armed with SET-60 torpedoes that entered service in 1961, and its only two frigates dated to the 1970s with manually operated weapons.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: highNorth Korea
So what: Establishes the extremely low technological starting point against which claims about a modern blue-water fleet must be judged, and implies heavy reliance on external assistance to close the gap this fast.
Parallel Track: Ballistic-Missile Submarines and SLBMs 3
North Korea is developing at least two ballistic-missile submarines and a future nuclear-powered SSBN of roughly 8,000 tons potentially carrying up to 10 submarine-launched ballistic missiles, giving it a naval leg to its nuclear deterrent.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: mediumNorth KoreaHero Kim Gun-ok
So what: A maturing SSBN program is the more credible long-term nuclear-deterrence investment for North Korea and should be watched more closely than the surface destroyer program for actual strategic significance.
As of a mid-2025 report, nearly two years after being launched and unveiled in September 2023, submarine 841 (Hero Kim Gun-ok) showed no evidence of having entered extended operational service, according to IISS assessment, despite North Korean claims about the program.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: mediumNorth KoreaHero Kim Gun-ok
So what: Indicates a real gap between North Korean propaganda claims and operational readiness that likely also applies to the destroyer program, warranting skepticism about stated commissioning timelines.
North Korea's first apparent test of a submarine-launched ballistic missile dates back to late 2021, meaning the program has had roughly five years to mature toward mid-Cold-War-level capability, though warhead integration, range and reliability remain unconfirmed.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: mediumNorth Korea
So what: A multi-year head start on SLBM testing suggests the submarine-based nuclear deterrent could mature faster than outside observers assume, independent of how the surface fleet program performs.
The New 5,000-Ton Destroyers: Specs and Production Targets 5
Kim Jong-un has set a goal of commissioning at least two large surface combatants of 5,000 tons or more per year for the next five years, plus a future roughly 10,000-ton 'strategic' destroyer design, a cadence that would approach the US Navy's own rate of about two Arleigh Burke-class destroyers per year despite North Korea's GDP being less than a third the size of New York City's municipal budget.
prediction🗣 Kim Jong-un (quoted by host)conviction: highNorth KoreaKim Jong-unArleigh Burke class
So what: If sustained, this stated production cadence would be a major and closely trackable indicator of North Korean industrial capacity and resource prioritization worth monitoring via satellite imagery over the next several years.
The new 5,000-ton destroyers (hull 51 and hull 52) carry roughly 88 vertical launch cells (64 aft, 24 forward) plus additional anti-ship missile box launchers and short-range air-defense systems for a total of over 100 missiles carried, giving them a higher missile-cell density per ton than a US Arleigh Burke, which mounts 96 Mark 41 cells on roughly double the displacement.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: highNorth KoreaArleigh Burke class
So what: By raw missile-per-ton metrics the design is genuinely competitive, meaning the ships pose a real point threat even though their broader survivability is likely far weaker than peer designs.
Hull 52 partially capsized during its 2025 launch and was later recovered, with North Korean state media describing the ship as having 'safely entered the water vertically,' and individuals reportedly involved in the incident were arrested.
anecdote🗣 hostconviction: mediumNorth Korea
So what: Signals both real construction/quality-control immaturity in North Korea's large-hull shipbuilding and an intense, punitive political pressure on shipyards to hit Kim Jong-un's production targets.
“safely entered the water vertically” ▶ 14:43
Between April and October 2025, hull 51's design was substantially reworked: its mixed 32-cell forward VLS array was replaced with a uniform 24-cell arrangement, its Russian AK-630-style close-in weapon system was swapped for one resembling the Chinese Type 730, and its sensor/electronic-warfare suite was changed, indicating the ship's earlier public reveal likely used placeholder or unfinished systems rather than a finalized design.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: highNorth KoreaChina
So what: Suggests North Korea's destroyer program is still in active design iteration rather than mature production, so specifications shown at commissioning may not reflect the eventual production standard for the follow-on hulls.
Before the destroyers, North Korea had already fielded modernized Amnok-class corvettes with upgraded sensors and a Russian-style Sea Whiz CIWS, but these still lack vertical launch cells and use a manually operated main gun, showing the naval buildup has been incremental rather than an instant leap to top-tier capability.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: mediumNorth Korea
So what: Establishes a stepping-stone pattern (corvettes then destroyers) useful for anticipating what North Korea's next surface combatant class might look like.
Distinctly North Korean Design Quirks 3
The new destroyer mounts manually operated heavy-machine-gun 'gunports' cut directly into the hull and superstructure, a design abandoned by most navies since the pre-dreadnought era because such mounts have poor fields of fire, no protection, and force crew to walk past a loaded, unstabilized weapon inside the ship's passageways.
contrarian🗣 hostconviction: highNorth Korea
So what: A tangible, low-cost tell that some North Korean design choices prioritize visual firepower and prestige-signaling over genuine combat effectiveness or crew safety.
“it is literally just a heavy machine gun on a manually controlled mount in the hallway of a ship” ▶ 24:52
Interior footage of the destroyer shows wood-grain paneling, smooth finished ceilings, and decorative elements including an apparently unsecured bust, in contrast to the exposed piping, wiring and visible firefighting equipment typical of Western warship interiors seen on the US Zumwalt class, a European frigate, and Japan's Mogami-class frigate.
contrarian🗣 hostconviction: mediumNorth Korea
So what: Interior finishing choices that hide access panels and system routing can compromise damage control and maintenance in real combat, a warning sign about the ship's true survivability regardless of its exterior armament.
The apparent use of civilian-style construction standards, materials and possibly civilian dockyard capacity, rather than stricter military survivability standards, could explain how North Korea claims it can sustain a two-destroyer-per-year build cadence, similar to how France's Mistral-class amphibious ships leveraged civilian shipbuilding techniques to cut cost and time.
framework🗣 hostconviction: mediumNorth Korea
So what: If true, this would mean the ships trade survivability for speed and cost, reinforcing the case that they are poorly optimized for actual high-intensity combat despite an impressive missile loadout.
The Investment-Logic Critique: Why Build These At All? 5
If the goal is naval-based nuclear deterrence, a nuclear-tipped cruise missile carried by a surface destroyer is a strictly worse platform than North Korea's own developing ballistic-missile submarines, since a submerged submarine is harder to detect, track and kill, and an SLBM has more range and is harder to intercept than a cruise missile.
contrarian🗣 hostconviction: highNorth Korea
So what: Explains why every other major nuclear power bases its sea-based deterrent on SSBNs rather than surface ships, implying North Korea's destroyer-based nuclear option is a genuine strategic anomaly worth questioning rather than taking at face value.
If deterrence rather than a symbolic naval presence is the true goal, North Korea's existing road-mobile ICBM and heavy MRL/TBM launchers are cheaper, more easily concealed via bunkers and decoys, and more survivable than a destroyer-based nuclear-cruise-missile capability, as illustrated by Iran's buried missile and launcher infrastructure reportedly surviving US strikes even as Iran's surface fleet was rapidly destroyed.
causal🗣 hostconviction: highNorth KoreaIran
So what: Provides a concrete comparative benchmark (Iran's ground-based vs. naval assets) for judging which North Korean delivery systems are likely to survive a first strike and remain a credible deterrent.
Given the extensive anti-ship missile, ISR and air-power capabilities of South Korea, Japan and the United States in the immediate region, the host argues North Korea's new destroyers are unlikely to survive a real conflict, drawing a direct parallel to Iran's larger surface combatants being 'picked apart' by US air power even while docked and defended by ground-based air defenses during the 2025 US-Iran war.
contrarian🗣 hostconviction: highNorth KoreaSouth KoreaJapanUnited StatesIran
So what: Suggests the destroyers' primary value would be pre-conflict prestige and deterrence signaling rather than any credible combat role in an actual regional war scenario.
“picked apart by air power like it was Toranto all over again” ▶ 40:29
For peacetime power-projection missions such as escorting sanctions-evading shipments (the role Russian warships play for shadow-fleet tankers), a cheaper, longer-range corvette or offshore patrol vessel would be more cost-effective than a short-legged, heavily armed 5,000-ton destroyer, an irony highlighted by Germany cancelling a longer-ranged, more lightly armed frigate design around the same time North Korea commissioned its missile-heavy destroyer.
contrarian🗣 hostconviction: mediumNorth KoreaRussia
So what: Indicates a mismatch between the ship's design (short range, heavy armament) and its most plausible peacetime use case, further undermining the stated investment rationale.
The host concludes North Korea's destroyer program has 'vaguely Plan Z vibes' -- an extremely ambitious effort likely to produce a handful of camera-friendly ships while representing a doomed, poor use of North Korea's scarce resources compared to cheaper, more survivable alternatives like ballistic-missile submarines and road-mobile missile launchers.
thesis🗣 hostconviction: highNorth Korea
So what: Frames the entire program as a resource-misallocation story rather than a genuine military capability story, the key takeaway for anyone assessing North Korea's overall strategic trajectory.
“the whole thing has vaguely plan Zed vibes about it” ▶ 54:34
North Korea's Anemic Economy Underscores the Resource Misallocation 3
Bank of Korea estimates put North Korean real GDP growth at 3.1% in 2023 and 3.7% in 2024, but these gains follow contractions in 2022, 2021, 2018, 2017 and 2015, meaning recent 'growth' headlines describe a rebound off a depressed base rather than a broad economic transformation.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: highNorth Korea
So what: Tempers any narrative of a North Korean economic renaissance and reinforces how strained the resource base is that's being redirected into destroyer construction.
Despite recent growth headlines such as taxis reappearing in Pyongyang, North Korea's total GDP remains measured only in the tens of billions of US dollars, and its wealth gap with the comparatively resource-poorer South Korea has continued to widen rather than narrow, undercutting any 'economic miracle' framing.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: highNorth KoreaSouth Korea
So what: Puts the scale of the naval investment in stark relief: North Korea is committing a meaningful share of an extremely small economy to a fleet whose military value the host disputes.
North Korea's key military-industrial advantage over market economies is coercive labor mobilization -- Kim Jong-un can simply direct workers and engineers into shipbuilding without competing for scarce technical talent the way Western defense industrial bases must -- which helps explain how it can sustain an ambitious build cadence despite minimal GDP.
framework🗣 hostconviction: mediumNorth KoreaKim Jong-un
So what: Explains a structural reason North Korea's stated production targets shouldn't be dismissed purely on GDP grounds, even though the ships' military value remains questionable.
Regional Strategic Implications 2
Because the People's Liberation Army Navy continues to expand rapidly, forcing the United States, Japan and South Korea to continuously right-size their own fleets against a growing Chinese naval threat, North Korea's navy has essentially no realistic path to grow its surface warfare capability faster than the anti-ship capabilities being built up around it in the region.
prediction🗣 hostconviction: mediumNorth KoreaChinaUnited StatesJapanSouth Korea
So what: Implies the relative threat posed by North Korea's surface fleet is likely to shrink over time even as absolute ship numbers grow, limiting the program's long-run strategic payoff.
Regional military planners may actually prefer that North Korea keeps investing in surface destroyers rather than in additional nuclear-armed ground-based launchers or ballistic-missile submarines, since destroyers are comparatively easy to detect, track and destroy and represent a less survivable, less threatening nuclear-delivery platform.
contrarian🗣 hostconviction: mediumNorth KoreaSouth KoreaJapanUnited States
So what: Reframes the destroyer program as a potential silver lining for regional defense planners: it diverts North Korean investment away from harder-to-counter and more genuinely threatening survivable delivery systems.

Mindscape · Sean Carroll 1 episode(s) · 28 insights

Mindscape Ask Me Anything, Sean Carroll | July 2026245 min28 insights2026-07-13
▶ Watch on YouTube
Physicist Sean Carroll's July 2026 Mindscape AMA works through the Boltzmann brain problem as a genuine internal-consistency crisis for the standard Lambda-CDM cosmology, defends Hilbert-space realism against QBism and instrumentalism, and relays Peter Thiel's stated strategy (from a lecture on Roger Scruton) of attacking physics/string theory to delegitimize academia broadly, which Carroll ties to his own public debate with Eric Weinstein amid deep US science-funding cuts. He also lays out his framework for emergence as compression-not-addition-of-information, his compatibilist take on free will, and his skepticism that current LLMs can have anything genuinely 'matter' to them.
Boltzmann Brains, Dark Energy, and the Meaning of Energy · 7Quantum Foundations: Wave Function Realism vs QBism · 5Physics as a Political Target: Thiel and Weinstein · 3Academic Meritocracy, Diversity, and Research Incentives · 4AI, LLMs, and the Limits of Machine Consciousness · 3Emergence, Explanation, and the Limits of Induction · 4Democracy, Local News, and Voter Participation · 2
Boltzmann Brains, Dark Energy, and the Meaning of Energy 7
The Boltzmann brain problem is a genuine internal-consistency crisis for Lambda-CDM, the default cosmological model: if the universe's positive cosmological constant persists forever, random thermal fluctuations in eternal de Sitter space will eventually produce vastly more randomly-fluctuated 'Boltzmann observers' (including full copies of a person's current brain state) than genuine observers reaching the present via ordinary low-entropy history, which is self-undermining because it destroys any reason to trust one's own memories or reasoning.
thesis🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: highBoltzmann brainsLambda-CDMcosmological constantde Sitter space
So what: Frames a widely-used 'default' cosmological model as potentially logically incoherent unless resolved, which matters for anyone tracking how seriously the physics establishment treats foundational challenges to consensus cosmology.
“the prospect of Boltzman brains dominating the universe is an absolute killer for models that predict that” ▶ 3:04
Carroll argues the theoretical physics community suffers from 'Boltzmann brain blindness' — a collective avoidance of a problem that would be taken far more seriously if it weren't associated with a silly-sounding name and speculative far-future scenarios, despite it threatening the internal coherence of the field's favorite cosmological model.
contrarian🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: mediumBoltzmann brainstheoretical cosmology community
So what: A named cognitive bias in how an entire research field triages which foundational problems get funded attention versus dismissed as 'not serious' — relevant to anyone assessing how efficiently academic physics allocates effort to real anomalies.
“there is a certain thing that I would like to label Boltzman brain blindness” ▶ 4:11
Carroll's own proposed resolution (a paper with Kimberly Boddy and Jason Pollack) argues that a true quantum thermal (de Sitter) state produces no real fluctuations when unobserved — unlike the classical case — which would eliminate Boltzmann brains entirely, but this only works if Hilbert space is infinite-dimensional; in his separate finite-dimensional Hilbert space work (with other collaborators), the universe would eventually recur and the problem would return.
framework🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: mediumKimberly BoddyJason PollackHilbert spacequantum mechanics
So what: Shows that a major cosmological paradox may hinge on an unresolved foundational question in quantum mechanics (what 'happens' to an unobserved quantum state) rather than new astrophysical data — a reminder that some 'physics' problems are really interpretation problems.
“In quantum mechanics, if you just have a stationary thermal state, then nothing ever happens really. There's no real fluctuations.” ▶ 21:46
There is tentative observational evidence, including from the Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument (DESI), that dark energy may be dynamical ('quintessence') rather than a true constant, which would let the cosmological constant fade away over time and sidestep the eternal-fluctuation setup that produces Boltzmann brains — but Carroll calls this evidence still quite weak and urges patience.
data_point🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: lowDESI (Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument)dark energyquintessence
So what: An early, unconfirmed signal that the standard cosmological-constant picture could be wrong — worth tracking as DESI and successor surveys accumulate more data on dark energy's time-dependence.
“there's a little bit of evidence from the dark energy spectroscopic instrument and other other places that maybe the dark energy is changing, but I would I would call that still quite tentative” ▶ 19:31
The string-theory 'swampland' program (associated with Cumrun Vafa) suggests that low-energy effective theories with a positive, everlasting cosmological constant may be theoretically unreachable ('in the swampland') from any consistent UV-complete theory like string theory, offering another possible escape from the Boltzmann brain problem, though Carroll stresses this is unproven speculation.
framework🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: lowswampland programstring theoryCumrun Vafacosmological constant
So what: One of the live theoretical bets in fundamental physics about whether accelerating universes with a permanent positive cosmological constant are even mathematically consistent with quantum gravity.
“there's an absolute feeling out there that perhaps theories with a positive cosmological constant are all in the swamp land” ▶ 19:05
Unlike the historical ultraviolet catastrophe (resolved by quantizing radiation) and quantum field theory's infinities (resolved by renormalization/effective-field-theory cutoffs), the cosmological constant problem cannot be fixed by invoking unknown high-energy ('ultraviolet') physics, because the energy scale needed to match the observed cosmological constant is below a single electron-volt — far beneath the proton mass — making it fundamentally an infrared (large-distance) puzzle that standard effective-field-theory tricks can't hide.
causal🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: highcosmological constant problemeffective field theoryquantum field theoryultraviolet catastrophe
So what: Explains structurally why the cosmological constant problem has resisted 40+ years of theoretical effort in a way earlier 'catastrophe' problems in physics didn't — a key reason it remains one of the field's deepest open puzzles.
“you can't solve the problem of the cosmological constant just by choosing a really really low ultraviolet cutoff and hiding everything in ultraviolet physics. The cosmological constant is by itself an infrared phenomenon.” ▶ 1:09:19
Energy is not a globally conserved quantity in general relativity for an expanding universe: Noether's theorem ties energy conservation to time-translation symmetry, and an expanding universe's laws are not time-translation invariant, so quantities like photon and vacuum energy density genuinely fail to conserve in the naive sense — there is no 'source' the added energy comes from.
data_point🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: highgeneral relativityNoether's theoremcosmological constantenergy conservation
So what: A foundational physics point that corrects a common misconception (that expansion secretly 'creates' energy from somewhere) — useful context whenever dark-energy or cosmic-expansion claims get simplified for lay audiences.
“in general relativity, the energy is just not conserved” ▶ 2:18:01
Quantum Foundations: Wave Function Realism vs QBism 5
Density operators (mixed states) may be more fundamental to quantum mechanics than pure state vectors — in the algebraic formulation of QM, states are naturally mixed and pure states are a special case, and this could offer a route (via Carlo Rovelli's 'thermal time hypothesis') to solving the 'problem of time' in quantum gravity, where the Wheeler-DeWitt equation has no time evolution.
framework🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: lowdensity operatorsCarlo Rovellithermal time hypothesisWheeler-DeWitt equationquantum gravity
So what: A speculative but structurally important idea for resolving one of quantum gravity's deepest open problems (why time seems to disappear from the fundamental equations).
“maybe there is just a fundamental ontological oomph to the idea of mixed states in quantum mechanics that there isn't in classical mechanics” ▶ 54:53
Carroll rejects QBism (quantum Bayesianism), which treats the wave function as merely an observer's subjective credence rather than a real physical entity; he holds that the wave function represents something physically real (Hilbert-space realism) and sees no evidence supporting the QBist claim that only measurement outcomes, not the quantum state itself, exist.
contrarian🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: highQBismquantum Bayesianismwave functionHilbert space
So what: Stakes out a clear side in one of the most consequential live disputes in physics/philosophy of science — whether quantum theory describes objective reality or merely our beliefs about it.
“there's zero evidence for that. I mean I think there's plenty of evidence that what exists is represented very very very well by the quantum wave function” ▶ 1:45:10
In the Many-Worlds interpretation, the universal wave function evolves fully deterministically, but individual observers within the universe experience genuine indeterminism because no one has access to the 'God's-eye' global view of the full wave function — a distinction Carroll says even sophisticated listeners often conflate.
framework🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: highMany-Worlds interpretationdeterminismwave function
So what: Clarifies a frequently-muddled point that determines how Many-Worlds bears on free-will and prediction debates: determinism at the universal level does not imply predictability or determinism for any observer inside it.
“the universe that observers experience is not deterministic” ▶ 2:47:07
Carroll argues quantum mechanics implies position is not a fundamental feature of reality — position is not a privileged observable any more than momentum is — reinforcing a broader trend, alongside the holographic principle and quantum gravity research, toward treating spacetime locality as emergent rather than basic.
thesis🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: mediumpositionquantum mechanicsholographic principleemergent spacetime
So what: A specific, falsifiable-in-principle claim underpinning the broader 'spacetime is emergent' research program that motivates much of modern quantum gravity work.
“I think that the strong implication of quantum mechanics is that position isn't fundamental because it's not in quantum mechanics” ▶ 2:39:38
In practice, essentially everyone working on quantum gravity today is either an Everettian (Many-Worlds) or holds an unexamined, hazy Copenhagen-style view; almost no one in that literature engages with hidden-variable (pilot-wave) or objective-collapse interpretations, despite these being live options in the broader foundations-of-QM debate.
data_point🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: highquantum gravityMany-Worlds interpretationhidden variablesobjective collapse modelsCopenhagen interpretation
So what: A sociological data point about which interpretive camp actually dominates cutting-edge theoretical-physics research, versus which gets debated in popular philosophy-of-physics discourse.
“In practice, everyone who does quantum gravity is either um a many worlds, an everian or someone who has some hazy commitment to something like the Copenhagen interpretation without thinking about it very carefully” ▶ 3:26:20
Physics as a Political Target: Thiel and Weinstein 3
Carroll relays that Peter Thiel, in a lecture on conservative philosopher Roger Scruton, explicitly argued that to undermine trust in universities and academia broadly (seen as an obstacle to spreading his preferred values), the effective strategy is to attack academia's most respected, highest-status field rather than its weakest points — and that Thiel identified physics, specifically string theory, as that target field.
data_point🗣 Sean Carroll (describing Peter Thiel's stated argument)conviction: highPeter ThielRoger Scrutonstring theoryacademia
So what: Documents an explicit, named strategic rationale from a major tech financier for delegitimizing elite academic institutions via their most prestigious science — relevant to understanding the funding and rhetorical pressure campaign against university research.
“what is the most respectable field within academia? It's physics. And what is the highest status subfield within physics? And again, this is Peter Teal saying these things.” ▶ 35:04
Carroll says he debated Eric Weinstein (a former Thiel Capital employee whose 'Geometric Unity' theory challenges mainstream physics) publicly, including on Piers Morgan's show, not expecting to change Weinstein's mind, but specifically to signal to the public that Weinstein's challenge does not represent a legitimate scientific critique of string theory or physics as practiced.
thesis🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: highEric WeinsteinPiers MorganThiel CapitalGeometric Unitystring theory
So what: Explains the strategic calculus behind a high-profile public physics debate as reputational defense rather than genuine scientific exchange — a pattern relevant to any public figure weighing whether to debate fringe challengers.
“I just wanted to get across to people the impression that this particular person did not represent an actual legitimate respectable challenge to physics as it's traditionally done, whether it's string theory or anything else” ▶ 30:47
Carroll characterizes the current environment for US science funding as 'historically terrible,' citing the current administration's cuts to scientific support and redirection of resources, and frames his willingness to publicly push back against figures like Weinstein as part of a broader defense of science's public legitimacy amid this funding assault.
data_point🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: highUS science fundingTrump administrationacademic research funding
So what: Contextualizes public physics disputes within a live, material threat to US research funding levels — relevant to tracking political risk to the academic science sector.
“We're in the middle of a really historically terrible time for science in the United States and maybe worldwide because the government is terrible in all sorts of reasons and all sorts of ways and it's cutting scientific uh support” ▶ 31:41
Academic Meritocracy, Diversity, and Research Incentives 4
Carroll cites research showing that diversity — including personal-background diversity, not just intellectual diversity — measurably improves problem-solving specifically on novel, ill-defined, or fluid problems (as opposed to well-understood, narrowly-defined ones), because people's differing life experiences shape which theoretical intuitions and solution strategies they bring to genuinely open questions.
data_point🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: highdiversity researchacademiaproblem-solving
So what: A specific, falsifiable empirical claim (diversity's benefit is concentrated on novel/undefined problems, not routine ones) that sharpens the usual generic case for diversity in research teams and institutions.
“if you have a situation where things are fluid or things are not very well defined or you're coming up with a new problem that you've never faced before, diversity is helpful in solving those problems” ▶ 39:12
Citing sociologist John Skrentny's early work and a study by former Harvard president Derek Bok, Carroll notes that US affirmative action succeeded both in maintaining university academic standards and in producing a cohort of elite-educated people who otherwise would not have had access — despite originally being adopted as a bureaucratically 'cheap,' low-effort mechanism rather than a carefully individualized fix for discrimination.
data_point🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: mediumaffirmative actionJohn SkrentnyDerek BokHarvardPrinceton
So what: A specific empirical rebuttal to the claim that affirmative action failed or eroded standards, relevant to ongoing US policy and legal battles over the practice in higher education and hiring.
Carroll argues current academic grant-application processes are so burdensome that they amount to nearly doing the research project in advance, wasting the time of researchers with strong track records; he also argues that because PhD advisor choice in one's twenties effectively locks in a career trajectory, academia should make it structurally easier for researchers to pivot fields later in their careers.
framework🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: highacademic grant fundingPhD programsresearch career incentives
So what: A concrete institutional-reform critique of how science funding and career structures discourage risk-taking and mid-career pivots, relevant to anyone assessing the efficiency of the academic research pipeline.
“picking your PhD adviser is the second most important person you will intentionally choose in your life after your spouse because it affects the rest of your career in a very dramatic way” ▶ 1:34:24
Carroll defends keeping peer reviewers anonymous, despite frequently receiving what he considers 'inane' referee comments and once having a physics paper rejected by two referees who gave mutually contradictory reasons, because unmasking reviewers would create far worse conflicts of interest — e.g., junior researchers unwilling to critique senior physicists who might later control their hiring.
framework🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: highpeer reviewacademic publishingPhysical Review Letters
So what: Weighs in on an active debate about reforming academic peer review, arguing the anonymity trade-off still favors anonymity despite its real costs to creative/unconventional papers.
“the incredible damage that can be done by referees not being anonymous would be much worse” ▶ 3:41:41
AI, LLMs, and the Limits of Machine Consciousness 3
Carroll says AI-plagiarism detection via keystroke analysis and digital forensics is not realistically scalable or enforceable, and advocates instead for shifting grading weight toward in-class, no-computer assessments and requiring students to discuss and revise papers directly with instructors, since this makes it far harder to submit AI-generated work the student doesn't understand.
framework🗣 Sean Carrollconviction: mediumAI plagiarismhigher educationacademic integritylarge language models
So what: A practical, adopted policy response to AI-driven academic dishonesty that other educators could apply, reflecting how higher education is adapting grading structures around LLM availability.
“using AI to do your academic work for you among students is absolutely a huge growing problem” ▶ 3:04:14
Carroll is skeptical that current large language models can have anything genuinely 'matter' to them or be conscious, because LLMs lack any biological, metabolic, or homeostatic process with real stakes (no body, no free-energy-driven non-equilibrium state to sustain) — though he leaves open that a future AI architecture with something analogous to a body could someday cross that threshold.
