So what: A near 3-4% single-session drop in mainland indices tied to geopolitical risk-off flows is a signal to watch for further spillover from US-Iran tensions into China-exposed portfolios.
“Shanghai Composite falling around 2.06% and Shenzhen component dropping 3.48% partially attributed to riskoff sentiment surrounding escalating US Iran tensions.” ▶ 0:58
China's Ministry of Human Resources and Social Security's newly released 15th five-year plan omitted a numerical target for urban job creation for the first time since at least the 1990s, breaking with a prior target of just over 55 million new urban jobs over the last five years.
data_point🗣 Alice Hanconviction: highChina
So what: Beijing dropping a headline metric it has always used as forward guidance signals real internal uncertainty about the labor outlook, making official Chinese economic targets a less reliable anchor for growth and consumption forecasts going forward.
“for the first time in decades, it didn't set a numerical target for urban job creation over the next 5 years.” ▶ 1:28
James King argues employment is the area most likely to destabilize China's economic model, and that China's simultaneous, rapid adoption of AI and factory automation makes it a real-world test case the rest of the world should watch for how hyper-fast automation affects jobs and social stability.
thesis🗣 James Kingconviction: highChina
So what: China's labor market response to AI adoption over the next few years is an early, high-scale signal investors elsewhere can use to gauge how quickly AI-driven job displacement could hit developed-market labor markets.
“I think this makes China a guinea pig for the rest of the world to watch.” ▶ 4:10
Xiaomi's car factory in Beijing runs a 100%-automated production line with no humans directly assembling vehicles (only quality-control staff on site), using about 700 robots to produce a new car every 76 seconds, illustrating how far advanced Chinese manufacturing automation has already progressed.
data_point🗣 James Kingconviction: highXiaomiChina
So what: A 76-second, fully robotic production cadence at Xiaomi is a concrete benchmark for how quickly Chinese manufacturers can scale automated capacity, with implications for labor costs and blue-collar employment in the sector.
≈ “those production lines produce a new car every 76 seconds. 700 robots are employed.” paraphrase
China's flexible/gig-economy workforce reached about 320 million workers this year (44% of the total workforce), up from roughly 280 million last year, a scale close to the entire US population of about 350 million.
data_point🗣 James Kingconviction: highChina
So what: A jump of roughly 40 million gig workers in a single year signals accelerating labor-market informalization that could pressure consumer spending stability and raise social-stability risk premia on China exposure.
“The number of people now in China in flexible employment is about 320 million workers this year.” ▶ 0:00
Demographic Strain and China's Buffers Against Labor Shock 3
At 44% of the workforce in flexible/gig employment, China's exposure to labor informality is far larger than in Europe, the UK, or the US, according to Alice Han.
data_point🗣 Alice Hanconviction: highChina
So what: Comparatively higher gig-economy exposure means China's consumer and labor data may be more volatile and less policy-responsive than Western peers during an AI-driven employment shock.
“that's a huge amount that's you know much larger than Europe the UK and the US” ▶ 9:44
Alice Han argues China has real buffers against labor-market shock: low household debt levels, a lower cost of living, and rents that have fallen substantially since the property bust began in 2021, which could let the government cushion the blow via social security support or a universal-basic-income-style program.
framework🗣 Alice Hanconviction: mediumChina
So what: If China leans on rent deflation and low household leverage as social stabilizers, watch for expanded social-security or basic-income policy rather than debt-fueled stimulus as the government's preferred response to gig-economy stress.
China's fertility replacement rate has fallen to about 1, among the lowest in the world and behind only South Korea's roughly 0.75-0.8, adding demographic uncertainty on top of AI-driven labor disruption that Alice Han says explains why Beijing chose not to set a jobs target this cycle.
data_point🗣 Alice Hanconviction: highChinaSouth Korea
So what: Combined demographic and AI-labor uncertainty undermines the reliability of China's medium-term workforce and growth projections, a factor for anyone modeling long-run Chinese consumption or labor supply.
≈ “China has one of the lowest replacement rates now about one in the world.” paraphrase
The 1990s SOE Layoffs as a Template for AI-Era Disruption 3
Around the turn of the millennium, China laid off roughly 35 million state-owned-enterprise (SOE) workers in a program known as "smashing the iron rice bowl," in which factories were sold off cheaply and workers lost the pensions, healthcare, and housing subsidies they had been promised for life.
data_point🗣 Alice Hanconviction: highChina
So what: This is the closest historical precedent for how Beijing might handle mass AI-driven job displacement: harsh short-term restructuring justified by long-run efficiency gains.
Alice Han frames the open question as whether AI can deliver enough of a productivity and growth boost to re-absorb China's displaced workforce the way WTO accession in 2001 and the subsequent property-sector boom did after the SOE layoffs of the early 2000s -- an outcome she says is genuinely uncertain.
contrarian🗣 Alice Hanconviction: mediumChina
So what: If AI adoption fails to replicate the growth-and-reabsorption effect that WTO entry and the property boom provided last time, expect persistent Chinese labor slack and greater pressure for fiscal/social stimulus rather than organic recovery.
Having reported on the ground in China during the SOE layoffs, James King witnessed demonstrations by laid-off workers in northern China that lasted weeks to months, and argues this history shows China has never been a paternalistic communist state that protects its workers -- ordinary Chinese people have repeatedly borne heavy sacrifice to build the country's economic miracle, a pattern he says the 320-million-strong gig workforce now continues.
anecdote🗣 James Kingconviction: highChina
So what: Investors should not assume Beijing will proactively protect displaced gig/AI-era workers just because of the state's political rhetoric; the historical pattern favors efficiency over worker protection.
“the outside world has of China as a sort of managed communist state has always been vastly wrong.” ▶ 16:50
Tencent, Manus and Beijing's AI Techno-Nationalism 7
Tencent is in discussions to become the largest shareholder in Manus, the Chinese-founded agentic-AI startup, after Beijing blocked Meta's roughly $2 billion bid to acquire Manus on regulatory/national-security grounds and ordered the deal unwound, leaving Manus's two founders effectively stuck in mainland China.
data_point🗣 Alice Hanconviction: highTencentManusMeta
So what: Beijing's willingness to unwind a major US acquisition of a Chinese AI asset before Washington acted signals rising regulatory risk for any foreign investor or acquirer targeting Chinese AI IP.
James King argues this episode shows the narrative that the US is the primary aggressor restricting cross-border AI/tech deals is incomplete -- Beijing moved to block a foreign (Meta) acquisition of Chinese AI IP (Manus) before Washington's own objections materialized, evidence China is just as active in walling off its AI technology.
contrarian🗣 James Kingconviction: highChinaMetaManus
So what: Expect a bilateral, not one-sided, tightening of cross-border AI M&A and technology-transfer restrictions, raising deal risk for any foreign investor eyeing stakes in Chinese AI startups.
“the idea that the US is the leading party in the tech war between China and the US... is not... certainly not quite right.” ▶ 23:06
Tencent is China's largest listed company by market capitalization, at roughly $533 billion (Hong Kong-listed), and its WeChat super-app has about 1.4 billion users; James King says embedding Manus's agentic-AI engine into WeChat could be a major upgrade to an app that has changed little in recent years.
data_point🗣 James Kingconviction: highTencentWeChatManus
So what: A Manus-powered agentic AI layer inside WeChat's 1.4-billion-user base would be one of the largest consumer AI-agent rollouts globally, worth tracking for Tencent's monetization and engagement metrics.
“Market capitalization of 10 cent at the moment is about 533 billion US” ▶ 24:23
Manus reached roughly $500 million in annualized recurring revenue earlier this year, versus Tencent's approximately $100 billion in revenue last year, underscoring that Manus remains an early-stage asset relative to its prospective new majority backer.
data_point🗣 Alice Hanconviction: highManusTencent
So what: The revenue gap suggests Tencent's motivation is strategic (agentic-AI capability and WeChat integration) rather than a near-term financial contributor, a useful lens for valuing the deal.
≈ “Manis reached a recurring annual revenue of $500 million earlier this year.” paraphrase
Manus's co-founders were summoned to Beijing by the National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC) -- rather than the Cyberspace Administration of China, which has historically handled similar tech-company discipline (e.g., Didi) -- and questioned over alleged foreign-direct-investment rule violations tied to the Meta deal, which James King and Alice Han read as a sign the central government/State Council is asserting direct, high-level control over AI/tech policy.
causal🗣 James Kingconviction: mediumManusChina
So what: NDRC (rather than CAC) involvement signals Beijing is elevating AI-technology-transfer control to top-level industrial policy, a precedent likely to apply to future foreign deals involving Chinese AI startups.
Chinese officials reportedly described Meta's attempted acquisition of Manus as a "conspiratorial attempt to hollow out China's technology base," language James King says reveals the degree of institutional alarm in Beijing about foreign control of Chinese AI IP.
data_point🗣 James Kingconviction: highChinaMetaManus
So what: Such charged official rhetoric signals Beijing is likely to treat foreign acquisitions of frontier Chinese AI assets as a national-security red line going forward, not a one-off.
“a conspiratorial attempt to hollow out China's technology base.” ▶ 27:53
Alice Han frames an open strategic question: whether Manus can now globalize from within China via Tencent/WeChat rather than through its earlier Singapore-relocation strategy, arguing this will set a precedent for whether other Chinese AI startups (she names Moonshot alongside Manus) can shed a "China association" the way ByteDance did successfully with TikTok, given Tencent itself has stayed largely domestic while ByteDance globalized.
framework🗣 Alice Hanconviction: mediumManusByteDanceTikTokTencentMoonshot
So what: Whether Manus follows the Tencent (domestic-first) or ByteDance (globalized) path will indicate how investable Chinese AI startups can be for international users/customers going forward, distinct from their access to foreign capital.
Gen Z's Exodus to Lower-Tier Cities and the 'Lying Flat' Backlash 5
About 20% of Chinese university graduates now relocate to lower-tier cities for employment after graduating, driven primarily by high living costs in tier-1 hubs like Shanghai and Beijing -- a trend Alice Han says has moved from niche to mainstream.
data_point🗣 Alice Hanconviction: highChina
So what: A structural shift of graduate labor toward lower-tier cities could redistribute consumption and property demand away from China's largest metros over time, a factor for regional real-estate and retail exposure.
“about 20% of graduates relocate to lower cities for employment.” ▶ 43:04
James King frames China's youth migration to tier-3/4 cities as a more extreme version of the US "soft life" trend or UK "downshifting," driven by the intensity of China's "996" work culture (9am-9pm, six days a week), and draws an explicit parallel to the burnout culture of 1970s-80s Japanese salarymen, calling it "China in the passing lane."
framework🗣 James Kingconviction: highChinaJapan
So what: If the burnout-driven downshifting trend intensifies as it did in Japan, it could presage a longer-term shift toward lower labor-force participation intensity and slower productivity growth in China's largest cities.
“This is China in the passing lane.” ▶ 36:11
Goldman Sachs estimates China's shadow/overbuilt housing supply -- the stock of 'ghost city' apartments -- at roughly 100 million units, and James King argues the youth migration to lower-tier cities, where home prices can run about 3x cheaper than Shanghai and rents as low as $200/month in some areas, is helping absorb some of that oversupply.
data_point🗣 James Kingconviction: mediumChinaGoldman Sachs
So what: Migration-driven absorption of the roughly 100-million-unit shadow housing supply is a slow-moving but genuine tailwind for stabilizing lower-tier Chinese property markets, worth tracking against official destocking policy.
“the shadow supply so the overbuild of apartments uh it's apparently around 100 million units” ▶ 33:46
China's Ministry of State Security has publicly accused overseas groups of funding influencers to promote 'lying flat' as a way to sabotage China's development, without citing evidence, while President Xi Jinping has separately urged citizens to 'eat bitterness' rather than embrace the trend -- signs the youth-downshifting movement has become politicized.
data_point🗣 James Kingconviction: highChinaXi Jinping
So what: Politicization of the lying-flat trend suggests Beijing sees youth labor-force disengagement as a genuine governance risk, raising the odds of policy intervention (positive or coercive) targeting youth employment attitudes.
“the Ministry of State Security has accused overseas groups uh funding influencers to constrain China's development by brainwashing people to lie flat.” ▶ 39:09
James King notes a policy contradiction: Beijing is simultaneously offering loans and tax incentives to encourage young people to start businesses in rural, lower-tier parts of China to boost regional growth, while politically condemning the broader 'lying flat' mindset as anti-productive -- reflecting what he calls conflicting signals during China's economic transition.
contrarian🗣 James Kingconviction: mediumChina
So what: The tension between incentivizing rural relocation and discouraging reduced work intensity suggests Beijing's youth-employment policy is still unsettled, creating uncertainty for anyone modeling regional Chinese growth or consumption patterns.
2026 Predictions: Half the Workforce in Gig Work, Chinese AI Models Gaining Share 2
James King predicts that by next year roughly half of China's workforce -- about 362 million workers -- will be in gig/flexible employment, up from the current 44% (320 million), a number he says he is using to dramatize how much Chinese society is changing.
prediction🗣 James Kingconviction: mediumChina
So what: If gig employment approaches 50% of China's workforce within a year, it would mark a historic shift toward labor informality with implications for consumer credit, benefits policy, and social-stability risk premia on China assets.
“I'm going to predict that about half of China's workers next year will be gig economy workers. That will be about 362 million workers.” ▶ 46:18
Alice Han cites data showing Chinese open-source AI models have outpaced American models in token usage since the start of 2026, by some metrics using roughly six times more tokens, and predicts Chinese open-source models will keep gaining share due to lower cost, easier hosting, and fine-tuning, noting at least one Chinese model is now nearly as competitive as Claude for coding tasks.
prediction🗣 Alice Hanconviction: mediumChinaClaude
So what: Rising real-world token-usage share for cheaper Chinese open-source models is a leading indicator for where global enterprise AI infrastructure spend and developer mindshare could shift, relevant to competitive positioning of US foundation-model providers.
“by some metrics now six times more uh more used in terms of token usage metrics.” ▶ 47:17

In Good Company · Nicolai Tangen 1 episode(s) · 24 insights

Eliot Higgins: How Bellingcat Hunts Down the Truth | Podcast | In Good Company40 min24 insights2026-07-15
▶ Watch on YouTube
Bellingcat founder Elliot Higgins explains how open-source investigation (geolocation, satellite imagery, Russia's for-sale government data) cracked the MH17, Skripal, and Navalny cases, and argues AI's biggest current effect on truth is giving people a 'permission structure' to dismiss real evidence as fake, inside an engagement-driven information ecosystem that structurally rewards disinformation over verification.
Bellingcat Origins and Open-Source Methodology · 4Landmark Investigations: MH17, Skripal, Navalny · 4Retaliation and Personal Security Risks · 3AI and the 'Permission Structure' for Denying Reality · 3Disinformation Economics and Democratic Decay · 4Funding Squeeze in Investigative Journalism · 2Signal Value of Verified Information for Decision-Makers · 4
Bellingcat Origins and Open-Source Methodology 4
Bellingcat's entire investigative model rests on 'open source investigation' -- using publicly available material like social media posts, satellite imagery, and marine-traffic or flight-tracking data -- a data surface founder Elliot Higgins says has 'mushroomed' over the last 15 years, expanding what a well-organized group of amateurs can independently verify.
framework🗣 Elliot Higginsconviction: highBellingcatElliot Higgins
So what: The growing volume of investigable public data is a structural tailwind for any organization that builds systematic OSINT capability rather than relying solely on official or media sources.
“So, that's using publicly available material to investigate a range of different topics.” ▶ 0:51
Bellingcat's signature 'geolocation' technique -- matching video content to satellite imagery to identify exactly where footage was filmed -- originated in 2012 when Elliot Higgins, then an untrained blogger, began cataloguing YouTube videos from the Syrian war.
anecdote🗣 Elliot Higginsconviction: highElliot HigginsBellingcatSyria
So what: A now-standard verification technique in conflict and disinformation analysis traces to one amateur's persistence, showing how low the barrier is to building high-value OSINT capability from scratch.
“I realized you could use satellite imagery to figure out where stuff was filmed. And we call this geoloccation.” ▶ 4:50
Bellingcat runs a three-tier structure: about 35 core staff (mostly researchers and trainers), roughly 200 trained volunteer investigators it can direct, and a self-organizing Discord community of about 43,000 people that surfaces leads the core team isn't even looking at.
data_point🗣 Elliot Higginsconviction: highBellingcatDiscord
So what: A case study in how a lean paid core team can generate outsized investigative output by training and coordinating a much larger unpaid network.
“There's also a Discord server where we have lots of people. It's kind of our community server that has about 43,000 people on it.” ▶ 2:58
Bellingcat's core operating principle is to never prejudge a conclusion -- evidence is gathered and verified first, opinions form only afterward -- which Higgins frames as the key differentiator from partisan online communities that pick a side and then hunt for confirming 'evidence.'
framework🗣 Elliot Higginsconviction: highBellingcatElliot Higgins
So what: An evidence-first discipline that is a transferable analytical framework for any research process, including investment diligence, trying to avoid confirmation bias.
“It's you never prejudge a situation. Just because you feel something should be the truth doesn't mean it actually is the truth.” ▶ 3:31
Landmark Investigations: MH17, Skripal, Navalny 4
Bellingcat's crowdsourced volunteer research after the July 2014 downing of Malaysia Airlines flight MH17 uncovered details -- including the origin point from which the missile launcher had launched -- that no other organization, official or otherwise, had found.
data_point🗣 Elliot Higginsconviction: highBellingcatMH17Russia
So what: Shows OSINT can out-perform state intelligence services on hard factual questions when applied at volunteer scale and speed.
“that led us to finding all kinds of stuff that no one else anywhere had found like the origin of the missile launcher where it had launched from.” ▶ 6:40
Bellingcat identified the two Russian officers behind the 2018 Skripal poisoning in Salisbury by exploiting Russia's 'gray data market,' where nearly all government records (passport files, car and house registrations) are purchasable if you know who to pay, after noticing the suspects' passport numbers were only a few digits apart.
causal🗣 Elliot Higginsconviction: highSkripal poisoningRussiaBellingcat
So what: A durable structural weakness -- corrupt, for-sale Russian government data -- has repeatedly exposed state operations and is worth monitoring as a source of future disclosures.
“their passport numbers were just a few digits apart, which is very suspicious.” ▶ 7:44
In investigating the 2020 poisoning of Alexei Navalny, Bellingcat purchased Russian phone records showing the FSB chemical-weapons team's cell-tower pings, letting investigators reconstruct the agents' movements across Europe and Russia minute by minute.
data_point🗣 Elliot Higginsconviction: highAlexei NavalnyFSBRussiaBellingcat
So what: Demonstrates a repeatable forensic method -- cell-tower triangulation via purchased records -- for tracking state actors that other researchers could apply to similar cases.
“the phone records aren't just who you've called, it's every single cell phone tower you connect to, which can then be mapped out.” ▶ 9:23
A Bellingcat colleague, posing as an FSB officer over a spoofed phone number, got one of Navalny's actual poisoners to describe on tape exactly how the operation was carried out -- poison applied to the seam of his underpants that activated when he dressed for the airport -- directly contradicting Putin's public denial days earlier.
anecdote🗣 Elliot Higginsconviction: highAlexei NavalnyFSBRussiaBellingcat
So what: A vivid example of open-source and social-engineering tactics combining to produce confession-grade evidence that overturned an official state denial within days.
“you put um poison on the seam of his underpants, and then when he put them on and went to the airport, it activated” ▶ 10:32
Retaliation and Personal Security Risks 3
Higgins says Russian retaliation against Bellingcat has escalated since 2015 from state-media smear campaigns (RT, Sputnik) to hacking attempts and, most recently, the jailing of six Bulgarian nationals convicted of spying on a Bellingcat colleague for two years on behalf of the FSB.
data_point🗣 Elliot Higginsconviction: highRussiaFSBBellingcat
So what: Signals that Russian state security services treat open-source investigators as high-priority targets, relevant to assessing operational and personnel risk for any organization publishing sensitive Russia-related research.
“most recently there's been six Bulgarians who were jailed because they were spying on one of my colleagues for 2 years and that was linked to the uh Russian FSB.” ▶ 7:10
As a result of his work, Higgins is treated as a high-value security target -- UK counterterrorism police from the West and East Midlands periodically visit him to brief him on relevant threats -- requiring him to constantly distinguish ordinary 'weirdness' from targeted surveillance.
anecdote🗣 Elliot Higginsconviction: mediumElliot HigginsBellingcat
So what: A reminder that high-impact investigative work carries persistent personal-security costs that scale with the target's public profile.
“at the moment I have West Midlands or East Midland's counterterrorism police uh pay me the occasional visit to let me know what's going on in their world, which is always a bit disconcerting.” ▶ 11:26
Higgins says he can no longer safely travel to the United States because Bellingcat has been swept into the 'censorship industrial complex' conspiracy theory alleging collusion between the Biden administration, academics and journalists, and because Elon Musk has publicly accused Bellingcat of being a government asset, raising his perceived risk of detention at a US airport.
anecdote🗣 Elliot Higginsconviction: highElliot HigginsElon MuskBellingcat
So what: Shows how US political conspiracy narratives and prominent tech and political figures can materially constrain the operations of an independent research organization with no documented ties to the alleged conspiracy.
“Elon Musk called us a scop.” ▶ 34:31
AI and the 'Permission Structure' for Denying Reality 3
Higgins argues AI's biggest current effect on the information environment isn't generating convincing fakes but giving people a 'permission structure' to dismiss real, verified evidence they dislike by simply claiming -- often without proof -- that it's AI-generated.
contrarian🗣 Elliot Higginsconviction: highElliot Higgins
So what: For any market participant relying on visual or video evidence to inform decisions, this predicts rising 'reflexive denial' friction even when the underlying evidence is genuine and verified.
“I think AI gives people a permission structure to deny my reality.” ▶ 29:22
A fully AI-generated far-right UK rapper -- fake music, face, and persona -- still achieved real audience engagement, because people engaged with content they wanted to be true rather than content that was factual, illustrating that 'truthiness' now outcompetes factual accuracy for attention.
data_point🗣 Elliot Higginsconviction: mediumElliot Higgins
So what: Evidence that synthetic-media engagement is decoupled from authenticity, relevant to modeling platform engagement economics or reputational risk from AI-generated content.
“there was a far-right UK rapper who was entirely AI generated. The music, the face, everything was fake, but lots of people engaged with it” ▶ 29:55
Despite experimenting with AI extensively, Higgins says it hasn't yet materially changed Bellingcat's core investigative process -- its clearest practical use so far is coding and automation (Claude wrote a scraping script that finished an 8-year-delayed task in 10 minutes), while AI-assisted geolocation produces only 'mixed' results and still needs manual verification.
data_point🗣 Elliot Higginsconviction: mediumElliot HigginsBellingcatClaude
So what: A grounded, first-hand counterpoint to AI-transforms-everything narratives: for OSINT-adjacent workflows, current-generation AI is a productivity tool for coding and automation, not yet a reliable analytical replacement.
“It wrote me a Python script and within 10 minutes it had done a job that I'd been putting off for eight years.” ▶ 30:57
Disinformation Economics and Democratic Decay 4
A large-scale Russian disinformation campaign targeting Armenia -- 'hundreds of videos, fakes, all sorts' -- generated almost no engagement because English-speaking social media audiences had no pre-existing interest in Armenian politics, showing that disinformation requires an audience already motivated to engage, not just volume of fake content.
causal🗣 Elliot Higginsconviction: highRussia
So what: Disinformation risk should be assessed by audience receptivity to a narrative, not sheer campaign volume, useful for gauging which fake narratives are actually likely to move opinion or markets.
“for disinformation to work, you need people who want to engage with it.” ▶ 19:15
Higgins frames functioning democracy as resting on three pillars -- verification (establishing what's true), deliberation (deciding what to do about it), and accountability (holding power to account) -- and argues all three are degrading into performative, loyalty-signaling substitutes where truth is secondary to demonstrating in-group allegiance.
framework🗣 Elliot Higginsconviction: high
So what: A reusable framework for assessing institutional and governance quality in any country -- screen for which of the three pillars (verification, deliberation, accountability) is failing when evaluating political risk.
“verification. How do we know what's true?” ▶ 25:57
Higgins says the information system has shifted from a top-down, institutionally-verified model to a peer-to-peer, many-to-many system where platforms like X monetize engagement over accuracy, structurally favoring populist, authoritarian, and conspiratorial content because it doesn't require procedural verification to spread.
causal🗣 Elliot Higginsconviction: highX
So what: A structural, platform-economics explanation for why misinformation persistently outcompetes verified reporting, relevant to reputational and narrative risk around any engagement-optimized platform.
“That's now collapsed into a peer-to-peer manyto many information system where institutions are competing for attention with the public in systems that rewards engagement over the truth.” ▶ 24:45
Higgins calls the post-2016 consensus that disinformation is primarily a 'foreign interference' supply-side problem 'complete nonsense' -- he argues the real driver is public distrust of institutions pushing people into engagement-optimized online spaces that were never designed for truth-seeking.
contrarian🗣 Elliot Higginsconviction: highRussia
So what: Implies policy responses focused solely on removing foreign disinformation supply will underperform; institutional distrust on the demand side is the more durable variable to watch.
“Problem is that's complete nonsense.” ▶ 27:23
Funding Squeeze in Investigative Journalism 2
US foreign-aid funding cuts eliminated an estimated 25-40% of global funding for investigative-journalism NGOs virtually overnight, according to Higgins, even though Bellingcat itself took no US aid money -- a sector-wide funding shock that hit outlets in the Global South hardest.
data_point🗣 Elliot Higginsconviction: highBellingcat
So what: A quantifiable, sudden supply-side shock to the independent-journalism and OSINT ecosystem -- fewer investigative outlets globally means less scrutiny of state and corporate actors going forward.
“about 25 to 40% of the global funding for the kind of you investigative journalism NOS's was lost overnight because of those US aid funding cuts.” ▶ 35:25
Bellingcat's funding evolved from 2014 Kickstarter crowdfunding to a mix of foundational funders, workshops (about 30% of income), and small donations, and is now shifting further toward high-net-worth individual donors as foundations redirect money to keep other collapsing NGOs afloat after the US aid cuts.
data_point🗣 Elliot Higginsconviction: mediumBellingcat
So what: Shows how philanthropic capital reallocation after a funding shock creates knock-on effects even for organizations not directly dependent on the cut funding source.
“we raise about 30% of our income from workshops” ▶ 35:12
Signal Value of Verified Information for Decision-Makers 4
Higgins argues that when 'reality becomes contested' -- citing the fast-shifting, hard-to-verify narrative around the Iran conflict as an example -- it becomes very difficult to make tactical or strategic decisions, which is why Bellingcat has trained investment firms, alongside journalists and NGOs, in open-source investigation techniques.
thesis🗣 Elliot Higginsconviction: highBellingcatIran
So what: Direct signal that institutional investors are already building internal OSINT capability to compensate for unreliable official narratives on geopolitical flashpoints, a capability edge worth benchmarking against.
“it's very hard to make any real you know tactical strategic decisions about things and this is where open source investigation is so valuable.” ▶ 28:33
On the Iran conflict, Higgins says there is abundant information but it is mostly 'very poor quality' and untrustworthy, forcing Bellingcat to fall back on techniques like infrared satellite-imagery differencing to build damage heat maps when standard visual satellite and social-media data were unavailable due to Iran's internet shutdown.
data_point🗣 Elliot Higginsconviction: highIranBellingcat
So what: A concrete alternative-data method -- infrared or thermal satellite differencing -- for assessing conflict-zone damage and activity when conventional visual and social feeds go dark, relevant to anyone tracking Iran-related tail risk.
“there's lots of information it's just very poor quality information that you can't trust.” ▶ 29:02
Bellingcat found and published video of Tomahawk missiles striking near, not on, an Iranian school building within about 48 hours of the strike, which let journalists press President Trump on the discrepancy; he responded by claiming 'the Iranians have Tomahawk missiles as well,' an answer Higgins calls 'completely ridiculous' but says was forced by Bellingcat's verifiable evidence.
anecdote🗣 Elliot Higginsconviction: highIranDonald TrumpBellingcat
So what: A concrete, recent example of open-source evidence directly constraining a sitting US president's public statements on an active Iran conflict, a template for how OSINT can move accountability in real time.
“we found a video of Tomahawk missiles striking close to that building but not on it.” ▶ 22:28
Higgins says investigators have 'at best two or three hours' to get verified evidence into public discourse during a fast-breaking event, because once people settle on a belief about what happened they rarely revise it, and official or government narratives will otherwise fill the vacuum first.
framework🗣 Elliot Higginsconviction: high
So what: A concrete timing benchmark for how fast credible counter-narratives must be published to compete with first-mover, often state-driven, narratives after breaking events.
“I think you've got at best two or three hours in many cases.” ▶ 22:10

Real Vision · Raoul Pal 1 episode(s) · 23 insights

Why Did the Iran War Restart? | Macro Mondays w/ Andreas Steno & Mikkel Rosenvold33 min23 insights2026-07-13
▶ Watch on YouTube
On Real Vision's Macro Mondays, Andreas Steno tells host Mikkel Rosenvold that renewed Iran-US tension over the Strait of Hormuz is disrupting refined-product flows (not crude) more than headlines suggest, that US CPI should print well below consensus, and that both the memory/semiconductor trade and the US-China critical-metals decoupling trade are far from over despite vocal bearish calls.
Strait of Hormuz oil conflict · 7Inflation and Fed rate path · 5Memory and semiconductor cycle · 7US-China critical metals decoupling · 4
Strait of Hormuz oil conflict 7
Following renewed IRGC attacks and a stalled ceasefire, Donald Trump signaled the US wants to control or 'run' the Strait of Hormuz, an operationally undefined move (patrolling coastlines, seizing islands, or a naval blockade) that Andreas Steno says signals further disruption risk to oil flow even though its exact form is unclear.
data_point🗣 Andreas Stenoconviction: mediumDonald TrumpStrait of HormuzIRGCIran
So what: An ambiguous US military posture around Hormuz is a live tail-risk for oil and shipping insurance markets even without a formal closure.
Despite the escalation, spot oil prices have not even broken above the prior Thursday's peak, and tanker-tracking data (credited to analyst Rory Johnston) shows persistent 'dark transit' flows out of the Strait of Hormuz, much of it quietly routed through Abu Dhabi/the UAE, that Trump has not yet publicly acknowledged.
data_point🗣 Andreas Stenoconviction: highStrait of HormuzRory JohnstonAbu Dhabioil price
So what: Real physical oil flow is materially better than the geopolitical headlines imply, arguing against chasing oil-price spikes on Hormuz fear alone.
“it's been clear to me for a while that the flow was better than reported everywhere basically” ▶ 5:23
The alternate oil transit route through Oman that had been used to bypass a closed Strait of Hormuz has now effectively been shut down after IRGC strikes over the weekend, removing one of the workarounds that had kept oil moving.
causal🗣 Andreas Stenoconviction: mediumOmanIRGCStrait of Hormuz
So what: Loss of the Oman workaround narrows the remaining logistics options for oil exporters and raises the odds of further price volatility if other routes are targeted next.
Steno estimates the region is currently short about 4 million barrels/day versus a normalized oil flow, down from a peak shortfall of roughly 8 million b/d in April-May, with the US and China jointly bridging the gap via strategic petroleum reserve drawdowns that have only 3-5 months of remaining runway.
data_point🗣 Andreas Stenoconviction: mediumUnited StatesChinastrategic petroleum reserveoil price
So what: The reserve-drawdown buffer is finite; watch for renewed oil-price pressure in roughly a quarter if the underlying shortfall isn't resolved by then.
“my best assessment is that we lack four million barrels a day” ▶ 7:38
Steno's base case is that oil settles around an $80 equilibrium and does not retest $100, arguing that once oil nears $100 the world 'gets very creative' (demand destruction, substitution, alternate routes) in ways not currently priced by the market.
prediction🗣 Andreas Stenoconviction: mediumoil price
So what: A capped ~$80 oil view argues against positioning for a runaway energy-driven inflation spike from the Iran conflict.
“I think the equilibrium right now is probably 80” ▶ 9:37
The real bottleneck from the Strait of Hormuz disruption is refined products, not crude — refiners have shifted marginal capacity toward jet fuel at the expense of gasoline and diesel, producing record-high US crack spreads and preventing the drop in crude from fully passing through to consumer fuel prices.
causal🗣 Andreas Stenoconviction: highStrait of Hormuzcrack spreadsjet fueldiesel
So what: Crack-spread strength (rather than crude itself) is the metric to watch for when pump prices and near-term consumer inflation will actually ease.
“we're talking record high crack spreads in the US because refiners have moved marginal capacity to jet fuel instead of um gasoline and diesel, etc.” ▶ 10:08
China retains real leverage over oil prices heading into the US midterms because it could deliberately ramp oil purchases to move prices higher around the election, a risk Steno says the White House does not appear to be publicly addressing.
contrarian🗣 Andreas Stenoconviction: mediumChinaoil priceUS midterms
So what: Election-cycle politics gives China an underappreciated lever over US energy costs, a geopolitical risk factor for oil positioning into next year's midterms.
“the Chinese, they hold a pretty decent card if they start buying a lot of oil just before the midterms” ▶ 28:54
Inflation and Fed rate path 5
Steno's proprietary now-casting model, run across every subcategory of the US CPI basket, points to a soft June US CPI print landing about 0.2 percentage points below consensus, driven by broad-based disinflation rather than primarily an energy-price story.
prediction🗣 Andreas Stenoconviction: highUS CPIFederal Reserve
So what: A broadly soft CPI print (not just energy-driven) would be a stronger signal for the Fed to ease than an energy-only beat, supporting front-end rate repricing.
“my best guess is that we get an a report that prints 0.2 percentage points below the consensus tomorrow” ▶ 11:56
Steno's specific numeric call for the upcoming US CPI print is 3.7% headline (versus roughly 3.9% consensus and a 4.2% prior print) and 2.7% core inflation.
prediction🗣 Andreas Stenoconviction: highUS CPI
So what: A concrete, checkable inflation call investors can mark against the actual print to gauge Steno's now-casting track record and reposition the front end of the curve accordingly.
“Three 3.7 for for headline and and 2.7 for for core.” ▶ 13:20
The prior week's FOMC minutes were the most hawkish Federal Reserve communication since the 2022 hiking cycle, but Steno frames this as a forecasting error rather than a genuine change in the Fed's reaction function — meaning a soft CPI print should quickly cause the committee to reverse its hawkish tone.
framework🗣 Andreas Stenoconviction: highFederal ReserveFOMC
So what: If the Fed's hawkishness reflects a bad inflation forecast rather than a fixed policy stance, a soft CPI print becomes a high-probability catalyst for rapid front-end rate repricing.
“I see it more as a as a forecasting error than a policy error” ▶ 12:59
The renewed Iran/Hormuz oil disruption may hand incoming Fed voice Kevin Warsh a convenient excuse to delay the start of a rate-cutting cycle, even though Steno expects political pressure on Warsh to cut eventually.
thesis🗣 Andreas Stenoconviction: mediumKevin WarshFederal Reserve
So what: Geopolitical noise from Iran could delay a Fed pivot that the underlying inflation data would otherwise justify, a timing risk for rate-sensitive trades.
European June inflation data already came in benign outside of the energy component, reinforcing the view that the broader disinflation trend is not simply an oil-price story.
data_point🗣 Andreas Stenoconviction: mediumEuropeinflation
So what: Confirms the disinflation narrative is broad-based across regions, supporting a lower-for-longer inflation view independent of oil headlines.
Memory and semiconductor cycle 7
Steno dismisses the popular 'mean reversion' call on semiconductor and memory stocks associated with a vocal bearish crowd (including Michael Burry), arguing the trivial point that cyclicals shouldn't be bought at peak earnings misses the real question — whether the sector has actually peaked — and he sees no evidence of a peak in underlying spot prices.
contrarian🗣 Andreas Stenoconviction: highMichael Burrysemiconductorsmemory stocks
So what: Steno explicitly frames the bear case as lazy and unsupported by spot-price data, arguing traders shouldn't exit memory/semis on mean-reversion narratives alone.
“I consider that analysis lazy to be honest.” ▶ 22:02
Underlying spot prices for semiconductors/memory chips are still accelerating, up roughly another 20% since Micron and SK Hynix's latest updates, and a smaller Taiwanese chip supplier said last week it expects major players to hike prices 30-40% in Q3 — a move not yet reflected in forward earnings estimates.
data_point🗣 Andreas Stenoconviction: highMicronSK HynixTaiwan
So what: Forward consensus earnings estimates likely understate the memory cycle's strength if a further 30-40% price hike lands in Q3, implying room for earnings beats.
“we're probably up another 20% or so” ▶ 18:06
There is a large divergence between bearish portfolio managers, who expect the semiconductor cycle to peak around Q1 2027, and the chipmakers themselves — SK Hynix says its order book is solid through 2031 and expects 2027 to be even wilder than 2026 — leading Steno to guess the cycle peaks closer to late 2027 or early 2028.
prediction🗣 Andreas Stenoconviction: mediumSK Hynixhyperscalers
So what: Company order-book guidance implies the memory upcycle extends roughly a year beyond current sell-side peak-earnings assumptions, favoring holding through near-term volatility.
“they say we we basically have a solid orders book until 2031” ▶ 18:55
Hyperscalers are directing effectively all of their free cash flow into semiconductor purchases, a capex transfer from hyperscalers to chipmakers that Steno calls the defining market story of 2026 (following 2025) and expects to continue into 2027.
thesis🗣 Andreas Stenoconviction: highhyperscalerssemiconductors
So what: Frames AI-infrastructure capex as a multi-year structural flow into chipmakers rather than a peaking trend, a lens for evaluating hyperscaler and semiconductor earnings through 2027.
South Korean export data, a high-frequency proxy for the memory trade, softened slightly in the first 10 days of July versus an 'extraordinary' June, but Steno attributes this to seasonal vacation effects rather than a genuine rollover, and flags it as the single most important chart to watch for the trade's end.
framework🗣 Andreas Stenoconviction: mediumSouth Korea exportsTSMC
So what: South Korean export growth is the trigger to watch — a genuine rollover in this series (not just a seasonal dip) would be the signal to exit the memory trade.
“when this chart rolls over um the memory trade is done” ▶ 21:19
Steno discloses he personally holds about 9-10% of his portfolio in memory stocks and over 25% including broader semiconductor names, despite acknowledging his momentum-driven book has lost money over the prior two weeks.
anecdote🗣 Andreas Stenoconviction: highmemory stockssemiconductors
So what: A concrete, transparent position size signals genuine high conviction in the memory trade despite recent short-term drawdown, useful context for weighing his bullish calls.
“I have 9 10% of my portfolio in memory stocks” ▶ 23:35
Key near-term catalysts for the memory/AI trade are bank earnings (Citigroup, Goldman Sachs) alongside US CPI on Tuesday, and ASML's earnings on Wednesday, which Steno calls the only real European gauge of AI capex momentum and an important global bellwether given the scrutiny over its China sales exposure.
framework🗣 Andreas Stenoconviction: mediumASMLTSMCCitigroupGoldman SachsChina
So what: ASML's earnings and order book on Wednesday are a near-term binary catalyst for the AI capex narrative that investors in the memory/semis trade should watch closely.
“ASML is yeah probably the only gauge you have in in Europe on on the AI momentum” ▶ 24:37
US-China critical metals decoupling 4
Despite a recent Trump-Xi meeting in Beijing and talk of a trade 'ceasefire,' Chinese export data on critical/strategic metals (through June 1) shows no reversal of the underlying decoupling trend, even though some of Steno's decoupling-themed portfolio positions suffered short-term price hits from the ceasefire narrative.
contrarian🗣 Andreas Stenoconviction: highChinaUnited StatesDonald TrumpXi Jinping
So what: The ceasefire narrative that pressured decoupling-themed metals positions is not supported by actual trade-flow data, arguing for staying with the decoupling trade rather than exiting on headline risk.
“the trade relationship is as frosty as it's ever been” ▶ 26:43
Steno expects any critical-metals scarcity risk to be mitigated the way Russia sanctions were circumvented — China rerouting sales through third countries such as Malaysia that maintain good relations with the US — meaning an outright supply cutoff is unlikely even if tensions escalate further.
framework🗣 Andreas Stenoconviction: mediumChinaMalaysiaRussia
So what: Third-party rerouting caps the tail risk of an actual critical-metals supply shock, tempering how aggressively to price in scarcity premiums.
The long-term US-China critical-metals decoupling trade remains intact and is likely to see major investment regardless of the outcome of upcoming US and EU elections, with the EU now adopting what Steno calls a version of 'the Trump playbook' to secure its own supply chain independence from China.
thesis🗣 Andreas Stenoconviction: mediumEuropean UnionChinaUnited States
So what: Supply-chain reshoring/derisking from China is a durable multi-year theme in both the US and EU regardless of election outcomes, supporting a long-horizon allocation to critical-metals and reshoring plays.
Steno argues the metals-decoupling trade won't gain price momentum until the US dollar weakens and energy costs fall, which in turn requires a softer inflation print and a shift in Federal Reserve rhetoric; he has begun building exposure but remains deliberately cautious until the Fed acknowledges the disinflation his data already shows.
framework🗣 Andreas Stenoconviction: mediumUS dollarFederal Reservecritical metals
So what: The critical-metals trade's timing is gated by the dollar and Fed rhetoric, not fundamentals alone — a softer CPI print and dovish Fed pivot are the triggers to watch before adding size.
“our data is screaming it in one direction right inflation down um softer dollar everything related to that but the those in charge they haven't really acknowledged that” ▶ 31:15

Conversations with Tyler · Cowen 1 episode(s) · 21 insights

Chase Koch on Principles, Music, and Overcoming Entropy | Conversations with Tyler62 min21 insights2026-07-15
▶ Watch on YouTube
Chase Koch (Koch Industries board leader and founder of Koch Disruptive Technologies) explains that Koch has quietly become a ~$50B tech-invested private company where refining is now just 4% of capital, describes its contribution-motivated hiring and bottom-up management model, and details building a personal AI 'operating system' on Claude Code he says makes him 100X-1000X more effective. He also gives a candid take on Spotify's value-capture problem for artists and points to a fast-shifting alternative-education market that Stand Together and the VELA Fund are capitalizing on.
Koch's Pivot From Energy Conglomerate to Tech Company · 5Talent and Bottom-Up Culture as Koch's Competitive Moat · 4Enterprise AI Adoption Inside a Major Private Conglomerate · 3Streaming Economics and Artist Value Capture · 2Alternative Education as a Fast-Growing Market · 3Migration From Coastal Metros to Low-Cost, Low-Regulation Markets · 2A Framework for Selecting Long-Term Partnerships · 2
Koch's Pivot From Energy Conglomerate to Tech Company 5
Koch Industries now allocates only 4% of its overall capital to refining, the original business its founder started, contradicting the common perception that Koch is primarily an energy company.
data_point🗣 Chase Kochconviction: highKoch Industries
So what: Investors and counterparties who still model Koch as an energy/industrial conglomerate are working from a stale picture; its capital allocation and risk profile are now tech- and diversified-industrial-driven.
≈ “4 percent of the overall capital consumed at Koch is in refining, which is basically where my grandfather started the company.” paraphrase
Koch Industries has invested close to $50 billion in technology over roughly the last 10 years, including acquiring ERP software company Infor, which brought over 30,000 software engineers into the organization.
data_point🗣 Chase Kochconviction: highKoch IndustriesInfor
So what: A ~$50B decade-long private tech buildout inside a company most people still tag as 'industrial' signals how much capital is moving from legacy energy/industrial balance sheets into software and data capability, largely invisible to public markets since Koch is private.
≈ “We've invested, over the last, I would say, close to 10 years, $50 billion in tech.” paraphrase
Koch's 2013 acquisition of connector maker Molex was a strategic pivot into IoT, giving Koch the hardware needed to pull data off machines, products, and plants across its industrial businesses.
causal🗣 Chase Kochconviction: highKoch IndustriesMolex
So what: Molex functioned as an industrial-IoT beachhead acquisition — a template for how legacy manufacturers can buy their way into data capability rather than build it organically.
Chase Koch argues that old industrial companies that fail to transform themselves with technology will become obsolete quickly, framing this explicitly around Schumpeter's creative destruction as an operating principle Koch tries to apply internally.
thesis🗣 Chase Kochconviction: highKoch Industries
So what: A direct warning from an operator inside a 130,000-employee private conglomerate: legacy industrials that don't self-disrupt are a fade/short-the-laggards thesis, not just management-consultant rhetoric.
≈ “For those, even old industrial companies that aren't transforming themselves with technology, they're going to be dinosaurs really quick.” paraphrase
Koch Industries has always been privately held and intends to remain private indefinitely, which Chase Koch says is part of why the market misunderstands how much of its business has shifted away from energy.
data_point🗣 Chase Kochconviction: highKoch Industries
So what: No IPO optionality to watch for; Koch's scale ($50B tech investment, 130,000 employees, 60 countries) makes it a large but permanently opaque private competitor across software, connectors, fertilizer, and consumer/forest products.
≈ “We've always been private, and we plan to stay private.” paraphrase
Talent and Bottom-Up Culture as Koch's Competitive Moat 4
Koch Industries deliberately recruits 'contribution-motivated' talent over credential-motivated candidates, prioritizing grit and work ethic over pedigree such as elite-school degrees or GPA.
framework🗣 Chase Kochconviction: highKoch Industries
So what: A hiring filter that explicitly discounts credentialism is a structural people-cost/retention advantage worth benchmarking against for anyone assessing private-company operating efficiency or competing for the same labor pool.
≈ “I call it access to the farm team.” paraphrase
Koch's current CIO, Jarrod Benson, has no college degree and started at the company striping parking-lot lines before working up through data science and building Koch's cybersecurity function as CISO.
anecdote🗣 Chase Kochconviction: highKoch IndustriesJarrod Benson
So what: A concrete internal case study of Koch's non-credentialist promotion path reaching the C-suite, evidence the hiring philosophy isn't just messaging.
≈ “His first access to Koch was stripping lines in the parking lot. He just wanted to get in the building.” paraphrase
Koch's management model is explicitly bottom-up empowerment rather than top-down control, aiming for a culture where employees know what to do without being told rather than depending on directives from the smartest person in the room.
framework🗣 Chase Kochconviction: highKoch Industries
So what: This is Koch's stated organizational-design thesis (market-based management) for how a 130,000-person private company scales decision-making without centralized bottlenecks — a reference model for large decentralized-conglomerate governance.
≈ “What if you had a culture where everyone knew what to do without being told?” paraphrase
Koch Industries operates with roughly 130,000 employees across 60 countries and all 50 US states, and deliberately kept its headquarters in Wichita, Kansas partly to retain access to a Midwest talent pool it views as culturally aligned.
data_point🗣 Chase Kochconviction: highKoch IndustriesWichita Kansas
So what: Geographic HQ decisions tied to labor-market values-fit, not just cost, is a variable worth tracking for other large private employers competing for talent outside coastal hubs.
“I would say talent and values.” ▶ 41:22
Enterprise AI Adoption Inside a Major Private Conglomerate 3
Chase Koch built a personal AI 'operating system' using Claude Code by feeding it his roles across Koch (board leader, investment-committee member, origination/partnerships) and Stand Together, then having it design five discrete 'engines' covering origination, quarterly business review analysis, and strategy recommendations.
anecdote🗣 Chase Kochconviction: highClaude CodeKoch Industries
So what: A senior operator at a ~$125B-plus private conglomerate is using an agentic coding tool to design his own decision-support workflow rather than commissioning custom enterprise software — a signal for how fast agentic AI tools are penetrating executive-level (not just engineering) workflows.
≈ “How can Claude help me become 100X more effective and efficient? What engines do I need to build to make me a better decision maker across all these disparate roles?” paraphrase
Chase Koch describes the productivity gain from his Claude Code-built workflow tools as '100X to 1,000X' improvement in effectiveness, and says this applies regardless of role — sales, admin, or executive.
data_point🗣 Chase Kochconviction: highClaude Code
So what: An extreme multiplier claim from an actual enterprise user (not a vendor) — useful anecdotal data point for gauging real-world ROI narratives around agentic AI coding tools inside large private-sector organizations, though it is a subjective, unverified self-report.
≈ “It literally is 100X to 1,000X in terms of what it can do, and that's anyone.” paraphrase
Koch Industries is actively encouraging employees across the company and its affiliates to adopt tools like Claude and ChatGPT as part of its bottom-up empowerment culture, rather than mandating specific top-down AI initiatives.
framework🗣 Chase Kochconviction: mediumKoch IndustriesClaude Code
So what: Large private employers pushing grassroots, opt-in AI-tool adoption (versus centralized IT rollouts) is a distribution channel worth watching for enterprise AI vendors like Anthropic and OpenAI.
Streaming Economics and Artist Value Capture 2
Chase Koch argues Spotify's platform economics let the consumer capture nearly all the value created, while artists capture very little — citing his own band's roughly 5 million streams as insufficient income to live on if it were a band's sole income source.
thesis🗣 Chase Kochconviction: highSpotify
So what: A musician-operator's direct testimony on streaming unit economics reinforces the bear case on DSP (digital service provider) royalty models as a durable artist revenue source, relevant to anyone evaluating music-royalty or streaming-platform investments.
≈ “With Spotify, I think the consumer captures all the value.” paraphrase
Because streaming royalties don't sustain artists financially, the music industry and artists are being pushed toward alternative monetization: touring, merchandise sales, and brand partnerships.
causal🗣 Chase Kochconviction: highSpotify
So what: Confirms that live events, merch, and brand-sponsorship revenue lines (not recorded-music royalties) are the growth areas to watch in the music economy as streaming compresses per-play artist economics.
≈ “But the artist doesn't capture any value.” paraphrase
Alternative Education as a Fast-Growing Market 3
Stand Together's own research found that family openness to sending children to nontraditional schools jumped from about 20% pre-COVID to roughly 70-80% in the most recent measurement.
data_point🗣 Chase Kochconviction: highStand Together
So what: A 3-4x swing in demand-side openness to alternative schooling models (measured by an organization actively funding that shift) is a strong tailwind data point for microschool, ed-tech, and alternative-education investment theses.
≈ “If you look at over the last year, we've measured that same question, and it's north of 70 percent, I think closer to 80 percent.” paraphrase
The VELA Fund, run with the Walton family, is deploying fast capital ('very fast checks') to parents, teachers, and entrepreneurs building alternative schools, and this model has helped build over 5,000 schools in the last five years.
data_point🗣 Chase Kochconviction: highVELA FundWalton family
So what: 5,000+ new schools in five years via a rapid-deployment micro-grant model is a concrete, scaling supply-side data point for the alternative-education sector that investors in ed-tech or school-services could track.
≈ “we're writing very fast checks to any parent, any teacher, any entrepreneur that wants to transform education so they can go build it.” paraphrase
Chase Koch cites Alpha School and Khan Academy as proof-of-concept examples for individualized, project-based, technology-enabled learning replacing the traditional one-size-fits-all classroom model.
framework🗣 Chase Kochconviction: mediumAlpha SchoolKhan Academy
So what: These two names are the reference brands Stand Together points to when describing where it is directing education capital — a useful shortlist for tracking the individualized-learning/ed-tech category.
Migration From Coastal Metros to Low-Cost, Low-Regulation Markets 2
Chase Koch observes a trend of people and businesses leaving coastal markets for places like Wichita, Kansas due to high taxes, heavy regulation, and elevated cost of living, noting the Wall Street Journal profiled Wichita's cost-of-living appeal three weeks before this conversation.
thesis🗣 Chase Kochconviction: mediumWichita Kansas
So what: First-hand observation (backed by a WSJ writeup) of continued coastal-to-Midwest/Sunbelt migration driven by tax and regulatory arbitrage — relevant to regional real estate, relocation, and state-tax-policy investment theses.
≈ “You see people leaving the coast wanting something different, whether it's because they're taxed too much, or the regulations are too high, the cost of living is too.” paraphrase
Chase Koch is personally building long-term assets in downtown Wichita, including a music venue and mixed-use housing, betting that the city will keep attracting people and businesses drawn by its talent pool and lower costs.
anecdote🗣 Chase Kochconviction: mediumWichita Kansas
So what: A wealthy insider putting personal capital into Wichita real estate/entertainment infrastructure is a revealed-preference signal reinforcing his stated migration thesis.
A Framework for Selecting Long-Term Partnerships 2
Chase Koch's framework for a durable 'preferred partnership' requires three elements: an aligned vision (a clear shared North Star), aligned values (how problems are solved and opportunities captured), and complementary capabilities that create real synergy between parties.
framework🗣 Chase Kochconviction: highKoch Industries
So what: A reusable three-factor screen (vision, values, capabilities) that Koch applies to evaluating any long-term partner, supplier, or customer relationship — a due-diligence checklist transferable to evaluating corporate partnerships or JV structures.
≈ “We believe that a preferred partnership, three things have to be present, for a long-term mutually beneficial partnership.” paraphrase
Koch's Stand Together platform formed a values-aligned partnership with MrBeast (Jimmy Donaldson), who Chase Koch says has over half a billion YouTube subscribers and more than a billion followers across all platforms combined.
data_point🗣 Chase Kochconviction: highMrBeast
So what: A concrete, current audience-scale data point (500M+ YouTube, 1B+ cross-platform) on the single largest individual content creator, relevant to anyone valuing creator-economy reach or brand-partnership deals tied to him.
≈ “I think he just hit half a billion subscribers. That was on YouTube, but if you look at it across everything, he's more than a billion now.” paraphrase

Invest Like the Best · Patrick O'Shaughnessy 1 episode(s) · 21 insights

How to Raise a Few Billion Dollars52 min21 insights2026-07-14
▶ Watch on YouTube
A veteran fundraiser (author of 'The Dao of Fundraising', formerly at General Catalyst) breaks down capital-raising as a psychology problem, not a logic problem, laying out frameworks — desire minus fear, the law of differentiation, trade-offs, and pipeline math — for how individuals, funds, and institutions actually move money, with a pointed aside on VC's reversal on defense-tech investing.
Trust vs. Belief: The Core Fundraising Mechanic · 4Friends, Family, and the Hard Reelect Ceiling · 2Institutional LPs: Misalignment and Momentum · 3Building Consensus at Institutional Scale · 3The Law of Differentiation · 3Trade-offs, Pipeline Math, and Sales Psychology · 4Trust Archetypes and Choosing Who Represents You · 2
Trust vs. Belief: The Core Fundraising Mechanic 4
Belief (intellectual agreement that a pitch makes sense) and trust (confidence the person will actually deliver) are distinct, and a prospect can fully believe a pitch's logic yet still say no because they don't trust the pitcher to execute — explaining why logically airtight pitches often fail to close.
framework🗣 guest (fundraising advisor, author of 'The Dao of Fundraising')conviction: highfundraising
So what: When a well-reasoned pitch stalls, the fix is building trust (track record, consistency, proof of execution) rather than adding more logical arguments.
“Belief is like I believe you. Yes, what you're saying makes sense. I believe you... But I don't have faith in it. I don't trust it.” ▶ 2:34
The guest's persuasion formula is 'desire minus fear': raising desire helps, but the higher-leverage lever is eliminating fear through trust — if a decision feels completely riskless to the counterparty, they will act on it.
framework🗣 guest (fundraising advisor, author of 'The Dao of Fundraising')conviction: highfundraising
So what: Investor-relations and fundraising teams should audit pitches for fear-reduction (references, consistency, scarcity-driven trust signals) rather than piling on more return projections.
“If they have no fear, in other words, it's riskless, they will do it.” ▶ 9:29
There is no such thing as genuine 'risk-loving' versus 'risk-averse' investor behavior; people who take large risks have simply convinced themselves the risk is smaller than it really is in order to justify the reward, the same psychological mechanism that drives gambling at a casino.
contrarian🗣 guest (fundraising advisor, author of 'The Dao of Fundraising')conviction: highrisk psychologygambling
So what: Investors should treat self-described 'high risk tolerance' skeptically in themselves and others — it's often risk-rationalization, a known source of cognitive mistakes in sizing positions.
≈ “I think that no one actually really invests with a lot of risk. I think that people actually convince themselves that the risk is far less than than what it really is in order to justify the risk they're taking so they can receive the reward.” paraphrase
The single most common fundraising mistake is over-indexing on logic (returns, credentials) while failing to address the counterparty's underlying fear; logic is the output that justifies a decision already made emotionally, not the input that creates the decision.
framework🗣 guest (fundraising advisor, author of 'The Dao of Fundraising')conviction: highfundraising
So what: Pitch decks and IR narratives that lead with logic/returns and never target fear are structurally mispitched, regardless of how strong the numbers are.
“The first mistake that everybody makes is that they think that the logic is where you're winning it. The logic actually is an output of a successful sales pitch, not the input.” ▶ 35:41
Friends, Family, and the Hard Reelect Ceiling 2
The 'friends and family round' is commonly misunderstood as charity money from people who can afford to lose it; in practice these investors trust the founder and want to see them succeed, with that desire outweighing their fear of loss — and treating it as charity (e.g. not repaying) is the most expensive capital a founder can raise because it damages the relationship.
contrarian🗣 guest (fundraising advisor, author of 'The Dao of Fundraising')conviction: highfundraisingearly-stage capital
So what: Founders should treat friends-and-family capital with the same repayment/return discipline as institutional capital, since the real cost is relationship damage, not just financial loss.
≈ “I think it's kind of an urban myth that oh, go to friends and family cuz they'll give you the money as charity. I think the most expensive money is borrowing money from your friends cuz you don't give the money back, your friendship is not the same anymore.” paraphrase
A fundraiser's first capital close functions as a ceiling on the ultimate raise (the 'hard reelect number'): if friends and family will give roughly $1 million, a realistic initial campaign target is $2-3 million, and this ratio holds at scale — a first close of $1 billion tends to top out near $2 billion.
framework🗣 guest (fundraising advisor, author of 'The Dao of Fundraising')conviction: highfundraising
So what: Fund managers and founders can forecast a realistic total raise by roughly doubling their known committed base ('hard reelect number') rather than setting arbitrary targets.
≈ “If your first close is a billion dollars, you tend to tap out at two billion. Because your first close almost always is your hard real life number.” paraphrase
Institutional LPs: Misalignment and Momentum 3
Very few limited partners (state pension plans, consultants, sovereign wealth funds) are actually personally compensated based on the returns the general partner generates for the fund, creating a structural misalignment where a 'this will make you money' pitch often fails because the individual LP contact's own compensation isn't tied to fund performance.
causal🗣 guest (fundraising advisor, author of 'The Dao of Fundraising')conviction: highlimited partnersstate pension planssovereign wealth funds
So what: GPs raising institutional capital should identify each LP contact's actual personal motivation (career risk, co-investment access, fee optics) rather than assuming return-maximization drives their decision.
“There are a lot of people who just simply don't make money if you make money.” ▶ 8:49
Institutional allocators like state pension plans and sovereign wealth funds respond to specific, idiosyncratic asks — co-investment rights, fee structure, access, transparency, or intellectual-property/thought leadership — more than to generic return pitches; identifying and delivering on that specific want builds the relationship that eventually snowballs into referrals from one institution to the next.
framework🗣 guest (fundraising advisor, author of 'The Dao of Fundraising')conviction: mediumlimited partnersstate pension planssovereign wealth funds
So what: IR teams should map each LP's specific non-return priorities individually rather than running one generic pitch across all allocators.
“Do they care about co-investment? Do they care about fees? Do they care about access, transparency? Do they care about intellectual property? Do they care about just being entertained? Find it. Give it to them.” ▶ 13:06
Institutional capital follows a 'path of least resistance' toward assets already in motion — money preferentially flows to funds and founders that are already succeeding, reinforcing winner-take-most dynamics rather than flowing to the objectively best unproven opportunity.
causal🗣 guest (fundraising advisor, author of 'The Dao of Fundraising')conviction: mediumventure capital
So what: Emerging managers and founders should expect capital formation to compound for incumbents disproportionately to performance differences, making early momentum-building disproportionately valuable.
“The path of least resistance is often the money goes to stuff already in motion.” ▶ 10:59
Building Consensus at Institutional Scale 3
Consensus decision-making structures (committees) almost never produce contrarian bets unless the group is deliberately designed to make them, which is precisely why differentiated, contrarian venture firms are structurally rare and hard to find.
causal🗣 guest (fundraising advisor, author of 'The Dao of Fundraising')conviction: highventure capitallimited partners
So what: Allocators seeking genuine contrarian exposure need to look past committee-governed vehicles toward managers explicitly structured to override consensus.
“I have never seen a committee, a consensus decision-making process, make a contrarian bet. Unless the group is designed to make contrarian bets.” ▶ 12:37
General Catalyst built LP consensus fund-by-fund by intentionally serving early state pension and sovereign wealth investors' specific needs, which generated referrals ('you realize this state pension fund invested') that roughly doubled each subsequent fund's size — a deliberate process that required accepting the loss of early smaller backers (family offices, endowments) who no longer fit once the fund scaled.
anecdote🗣 guest (fundraising advisor, author of 'The Dao of Fundraising')conviction: highGeneral Catalyststate pension planssovereign wealth funds
So what: A case study in deliberately engineering LP-base consensus as a repeatable, fund-over-fund scaling mechanism rather than relying on returns alone.
≈ “You have to have the courage to lose the people who actually were the people who invest with you cuz you were contrarian... you have to have the courage to get past the innovator's dilemma.” paraphrase
Applying the technology-adoption chasm model to capital formation: once a fund or company crosses from early adopters to the early majority, consensus dynamics take over and the category tends to become winner-take-all for whoever crossed first.
framework🗣 guest (fundraising advisor, author of 'The Dao of Fundraising')conviction: mediumventure capital
So what: Being first to cross the early-majority threshold in a capital-raising category can be worth disproportionate effort because the payoff is winner-take-all, not proportional.
“We know that the winner on the other side of that gap is actually winner take all.” ▶ 12:13
The Law of Differentiation 3
The guest's core fundraising equation is (Track Record + Differentiation) divided by Complexity of Story — track record includes behavioral consistency (not just returns), differentiation can be contrarian bets, operational intensity, unique market access, or an outsized GP commit, and complexity of story actively erodes trust regardless of the other two variables.
framework🗣 guest (fundraising advisor, author of 'The Dao of Fundraising')conviction: highfundraisingventure capital
So what: A reusable diagnostic for evaluating why a fundraise is stalling: improve track record, sharpen differentiation, or — most commonly overlooked — simplify the story.
≈ “Track record plus differentiation divided by the complexity of your story.” paraphrase
Complications in a pitch damage trust in two ways: they dilute the prospect's own confidence, and more importantly they prevent the prospect from repeating a simple, defensible rationale to the other decision-makers they must convince — illustrated by the OJ Simpson trial's 'if the glove doesn't fit, you must acquit,' which gave jurors a repeatable phrase to justify their verdict to the public.
framework🗣 guest (fundraising advisor, author of 'The Dao of Fundraising')conviction: highfundraising
So what: Fundraisers should hand every internal champion a single repeatable, simplified phrase they can use to defend the decision to their own committee or boss.
“If you have complications, make sure you give them that phrase that allows you to cut through those complications even if you have complications.” ▶ 20:18
Great differentiation requires real sacrifice, and firms lose credibility when they abandon a stated principle for a hot opportunity — as of this conversation, the vast majority of venture firms that publicly pledged in 2019 to never invest in weapons are now leading investment into the defense/weapons sector, which has become the hottest area in venture capital.
contrarian🗣 guest (fundraising advisor, author of 'The Dao of Fundraising')conviction: highventure capitaldefense techweapons investing
So what: Defense/weapons tech is currently the hottest VC investment category, a notable sector rotation; LPs should also weigh reputational/consistency risk when firms reverse publicly stated investment principles.
“You can go back to 2019 and the vast majority of venture capital firms say, 'We will never invest in weapons.' It's the hottest area right now. The same people who said they would never invest in weapons are actually now leading the weapons charge.” ▶ 21:41
Trade-offs, Pipeline Math, and Sales Psychology 4
Fundraising trade-offs work like the 'quality, cost, speed — pick two' rule: size and terms trade off against each other, but speed is actually a function of trust and genuine scarcity, not of lowering terms; institutional diligence timelines (e.g. months of process for a state pension plan) don't shorten just because terms improve.
framework🗣 guest (fundraising advisor, author of 'The Dao of Fundraising')conviction: highfundraisingventure capital
So what: Fund managers trying to close faster should engineer genuine scarcity rather than cutting fees/terms, since terms don't materially compress institutional process timelines.
“Speed actually is trust... you get to pick two.” ▶ 23:09
Faking scarcity in a raise (implying limited capacity when there isn't any) is immediately detected by sophisticated investors and destroys credibility and trust velocity — only firms with genuine scarcity (e.g. Benchmark) can use it as a real lever.
framework🗣 guest (fundraising advisor, author of 'The Dao of Fundraising')conviction: highBenchmarkventure capital
So what: Investors should treat manufactured urgency/scarcity claims from fundraisers as a red flag; genuine scarcity is rare and typically reserved for top-decile, oversubscribed managers.
“The investor 100% knows that they're lying and you immediately lose credibility.” ▶ 24:37
The largest asset managers treat fundraising as pure funnel math: total raised equals pipeline size multiplied by conversion ratio multiplied by average check ('bite') size, and once you know your conversion ratio (e.g. 20%, or roughly 1 in 10 after clearing the hard reelect number), success becomes a matter of volume of meetings rather than persuasion skill per meeting.
framework🗣 guest (fundraising advisor, author of 'The Dao of Fundraising')conviction: highfundraisingasset managers
So what: Fundraising teams should track and optimize conversion ratio as the single KPI, since total capital raised is mechanically pipeline x conversion x bite size.
“It's literally just pipeline times conversion ratio times bite size. That literally is the only math that is important for a fund raise.” ▶ 26:10
The Karpman drama triangle applies to sales/LP meetings: prospects often feel like a 'victim' of some problem (e.g. fees, poor service elsewhere); the fundraiser's job is to identify that drama and position as the 'hero'/solution, or, if they cannot solve it, to show genuine empathy for the underlying problem, which itself builds enough trust to be heard.
framework🗣 guest (fundraising advisor, author of 'The Dao of Fundraising')conviction: mediumfundraisingKarpman drama triangle
So what: A concrete meeting-prep checklist: identify the prospect's felt 'victimhood,' decide whether you can be the hero or must be the empathetic ally, and structure the pitch accordingly.
“Is there drama in this person or these people? Do I have the ability to be a solution to that drama? If I cannot be a solution to that drama, can I empathize with that drama?” ▶ 29:06
Trust Archetypes and Choosing Who Represents You 2
Oprah Winfrey is held up as a master trust-builder via a six-factor combination: 'goodness' (kindness plus conviction), reciprocity (gifting the audience), manufactured consensus (Oprah's Book Club, which became more influential than bestseller lists), borrowed authority (guests with credibility on issues), likability, and self-imposed scarcity (never appearing on other shows or in ads).
framework🗣 guest (fundraising advisor, author of 'The Dao of Fundraising')conviction: mediumOprah Winfrey
So what: A transferable checklist (goodness, reciprocity, consensus, authority, liking, scarcity) for building durable public/investor trust in a personal or fund brand.
“She had a scarcity about her in that she really didn't show up anywhere else but Oprah... if you want to see Oprah, you had to tune in to that. And that creates trust.” ▶ 32:20
