The right head of investor relations ('Secretary of State') should be chosen to match the specific image the principal wants LPs to infer when the principal isn't in the room, and — contrary to common assumption — an ex-investment-banker/deal-partner is often the wrong hire, since understanding 'the system' is less valuable than a hire whose actual domain language and experience matches what the investors care about.
contrarian🗣 guest (fundraising advisor, author of 'The Dao of Fundraising')conviction: highinvestor relationsfundraising
So what: Funds building out IR/fundraising teams should hire for domain-language fit with their target LP base over generic dealmaking pedigree.
≈ “When I meet somebody who says, 'Well, the reason why I could be such a great fundraiser as an investment banker as an ex-deal partner is because I understand the system,' I look and go, 'That's exactly the wrong person.'” paraphrase

Johnny Harris 1 episode(s) · 20 insights

The REAL reason the US can’t beat Iran40 min20 insights2026-07-16
▶ Watch on YouTube
A historical explainer arguing that Iran's 200-year experience of foreign powers (Russia, Britain, the US) extracting its resources and repeatedly crushing its democratic movements — climaxing in the 1953 CIA-backed coup against Mosaddegh — explains why the 1979 revolution produced a deliberately coup-proof, multi-layered theocratic security state that pursues nuclear weapons and proxy militias as deterrence, and why it is unlikely to collapse from external strikes despite roughly 80% of Iranians reportedly opposing it.
19th-Century Great Game Set the Template for Foreign Control · 4Oil Turned Iran from Buffer State into a Strategic Prize · 3The 1953 CIA-Backed Coup Created a Durable Grievance · 3Oil Wealth Fueled a Western-Aligned Military Buildup and Elite Backlash · 2Khomeini's Revolution Engineered a Coup-Proof Regime · 4Nuclear Weapons and Proxy Militias as the Regime's Deterrence Strategy · 2The Regime-Population Gap and Fragility of the Ceasefire · 2
19th-Century Great Game Set the Template for Foreign Control 4
In the 1800s, Russia and Britain competed for influence over Iran (then called Persia) using military coercion and 'economic concessions' — monopoly grants over industries like tobacco and banking — sold by the Qajar shahs for cash, embedding a durable pattern of external powers extracting Iranian resources without formal occupation.
framework🗣 Johnny Harris, narratorconviction: highIranRussiaBritainQajar dynasty
So what: This concession-based extraction model explains why Iran's regime today is hypersensitive to any deal perceived as ceding sovereign control over resources or nuclear infrastructure — a lens for reading Iran's negotiating posture.
Britain's official 19th-century doctrine toward Iran was to avoid formal occupation while ensuring no rival power, namely Russia, could dominate it — a strategy of indirect control exercised through economic leverage rather than colonization.
data_point🗣 British official, quoted by narratorconviction: highBritainRussiaIran
So what: Shows the origin of the 'control without occupying' playbook that great powers (and arguably Israel/the US today) still apply toward Iran.
“We do not want to occupy Iran, but we cannot see it occupied by our foes” ▶ 4:05
In 1890 the Shah granted Britain a 50-year monopoly on Iran's tobacco trade, and the resulting public boycott and protest forced its cancellation, proving early that mass mobilization could reverse foreign economic concessions in Iran, a precedent repeated in 1979.
causal🗣 Johnny Harris, narratorconviction: highBritainIran
So what: Establishes a recurring pattern: broad-based Iranian street mobilization has historically been the mechanism that reverses unpopular deals with foreign powers, not elite negotiation.
Iran's 1905-1906 constitutional revolution created a parliament to limit the Shah's power and curb secret foreign deals, but it was violently crushed when Russia sent 12,000 troops who shelled the parliament building and killed hundreds of lawmakers, showing outside powers historically intervened militarily whenever Iranian democratic institutions threatened foreign access.
causal🗣 Johnny Harris, narratorconviction: highRussiaIran
So what: Frames a recurring risk pattern: Iranian democratic openings have historically triggered foreign military suppression rather than support, informing skepticism about externally-driven 'regime change to democracy' scenarios.
Oil Turned Iran from Buffer State into a Strategic Prize 3
The 1908 discovery of oil in Iran coincided with the British Navy's shift from coal to oil power, converting Iran from a mere buffer protecting British trade routes into the linchpin of British naval dominance, and giving rise to the company that became British Petroleum (BP).
causal🗣 Johnny Harris, narratorconviction: highBritainBPIranRoyal Navy
So what: Illustrates how a single resource discovery locked in decades of great-power military presence in Iran — a structural template for reading today's contest over Iran's nuclear and energy assets.
Prior to the 1951 nationalization push led by Mohammad Mosaddegh, Britain was taking roughly 84% of the profits from Iran's oil operations, with Iran receiving only about 16%, which fueled the political momentum for full nationalization.
data_point🗣 Johnny Harris, narratorconviction: highBritainMohammad MosaddeghIran
So what: A concrete economic grievance (an 84/16 profit split) that drove nationalization — useful baseline for understanding why resource-sovereignty rhetoric remains potent in Iranian politics.
“Iran is only getting 16% of the company's profits.” ▶ 17:50
When Mosaddegh's parliament nationalized the Anglo-Iranian oil company in 1951, Britain retaliated with a naval blockade at the Persian Gulf and an economic boycott of Iranian oil rather than concede full national control, an early version of the sanctions-and-blockade playbook still used against Iran today.
causal🗣 Johnny Harris, narratorconviction: highBritainIranPersian Gulf
So what: The 1951-53 blockade is a direct historical precedent for modern sanctions/naval-pressure strategy against Iran, relevant to gauging effectiveness of current economic pressure campaigns.
The 1953 CIA-Backed Coup Created a Durable Grievance 3
The CIA spent roughly $12 million in today's money on a 1953 covert campaign — bribing politicians, paying crowds to protest, and funding newspapers — that helped remove democratically-elected Prime Minister Mohammad Mosaddegh after British intelligence convinced Washington he could let the Soviets access Iranian oil.
data_point🗣 Johnny Harris, narratorconviction: highCIAMohammad MosaddeghBritainEisenhowerRussia
So what: This coup remains a foundational grievance the regime cites to justify anti-Western policy; any future US-Iran negotiation carries this historical weight and helps explain persistent distrust.
After reinstalling the Shah in 1953, Britain retained control of Iran's oil business but agreed to a 50% profit split and diluted its own share by bringing in other nations, including a large stake for the United States, which had helped fund the coup — structurally tying US oil interests to Iran from that point forward.
data_point🗣 Johnny Harris, narratorconviction: mediumBritainUnited StatesIran
So what: Marks the start of direct US commercial and strategic entanglement in Iranian oil, a precursor to decades of subsequent US involvement in Iranian affairs.
Following the coup, the Shah's CIA-assisted secret police captured, tortured, and surveilled political opponents, while the Shah simultaneously granted Americans in Iran legal immunity — a policy the narrator notes directly echoed the extraterritorial privileges Russia had forced on Iran over a century earlier.
causal🗣 Johnny Harris, narratorconviction: highShahCIAUnited StatesIran
So what: Highlights a repeating historical structure (foreign legal immunity, domestic repression) that Iran's later revolutionary rhetoric explicitly invoked, useful for understanding regime messaging today.
Oil Wealth Fueled a Western-Aligned Military Buildup and Elite Backlash 2
Fueled by 1970s oil price surges, the Shah became the world's largest buyer of military equipment, making Iran completely reliant on US weapons and a major regional military power, even as the US simultaneously encouraged Iran to start a nuclear energy program.
data_point🗣 Johnny Harris, narratorconviction: highShahUnited StatesIran
So what: The US itself seeded Iran's nuclear program in the 1970s, important context for the 'who started this' framing in the current nuclear standoff.
Despite building infrastructure, expanding education, and growing a middle class, the Shah's rule paired Western-style modernization with authoritarian repression — censorship, eliminating opposition — and visible extravagance such as helicopter-borne desert parties with French chefs, widening the gap between elite oil wealth and public resentment.
data_point🗣 Johnny Harris, narratorconviction: highShahIran
So what: The elite/public wealth gap under a Western-aligned autocrat is a recurring precondition for revolution that recurs in other resource-rich, externally-backed regimes.
Khomeini's Revolution Engineered a Coup-Proof Regime 4
Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini's revolutionary rhetoric fused anti-imperial grievance with Shia doctrines of martyrdom and divine justice, framing over a century of British, Soviet, and American intervention as the root cause of Iran's suffering.
contrarian🗣 Ruhollah Khomeini, quoted by narratorconviction: highRuhollah KhomeiniBritainRussiaUnited States
So what: Explains why the regime's ideology cannot be separated from anti-imperial grievance — purely religious or purely nationalist framings of the regime each miss half the picture.
“They have reduced the Iranian people to the level lower than an American dog.” ▶ 25:03
The Islamic Republic's constitution created a 'filtered democracy': citizens elect a parliament, a president, and an 88-member assembly that can appoint or remove the Supreme Leader, but two religious councils appointed by the Supreme Leader control who is allowed to run for office and which laws pass — so elections operate without altering ultimate religious control.
framework🗣 Johnny Harris, narratorconviction: highIranSupreme Leader
So what: Clarifies that electoral reform pressure inside Iran cannot dislodge the regime's core power structure, which is a key assumption for anyone modeling internal-reform-driven scenarios.
Iran's regime maintains overlapping security layers beyond its conventional military — the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps with its own ground, air, and naval forces, plus a volunteer militia network embedded in every city and neighborhood — giving Iran over half a million active personnel and well over a million including militias.
data_point🗣 Johnny Harris, narratorconviction: highIranIslamic Revolutionary Guard Corps
So what: This multi-layered, redundant security architecture is a key reason targeted strikes or leadership decapitation are unlikely to collapse the regime — relevant to sizing military or regime-change scenarios.
An unnamed expert consulted by the narrator argued that Iran's regime was deliberately designed after 1979 to survive decapitation strikes, noting that killing a hundred top officials would simply be met by a hundred more waiting to take their place.
prediction🗣 unnamed expert, quoted by narratorconviction: mediumIranIsrael
So what: Suggests airstrike-based decapitation campaigns against Iranian leadership are unlikely to produce regime collapse on their own, a key input for assessing Israeli/US strategy outcomes.
“if Israel takes out a hundred top officials in an airstrike, there'll be a hundred more waiting to take their place” ▶ 36:45
Nuclear Weapons and Proxy Militias as the Regime's Deterrence Strategy 2
The narrator frames Iran's decades-long pursuit of nuclear weapons as a response to watching regional rivals acquire the 'ultimate deterrent' against foreign intervention, paired with funding proxy militias across the Middle East to project power and threaten enemies.
thesis🗣 Johnny Harris, narratorconviction: highIranMiddle East
So what: Frames Iran's nuclear pursuit as defensive/deterrence-driven rather than purely ideological, implying negotiated security guarantees — not just sanctions or strikes — may be needed to change the regime's calculus.
Israel's stated strategy in its recent wars with Iran has aimed not just at halting Iran's nuclear capability but at weakening its regional proxy militia network, with Israeli leadership having sought regime collapse for roughly two decades and having now secured direct US involvement in that campaign.
thesis🗣 Johnny Harris, narratorconviction: highIsraelIranUnited States
So what: Signals that the current conflict's objective, per the narrator's reading of Israeli leadership, extends beyond nuclear containment to full regime change — a materially different (and riskier) endpoint than deterrence.
The Regime-Population Gap and Fragility of the Ceasefire 2
According to polls cited by the narrator, roughly 80% of Iranians do not support the current regime, yet the population remains caught between an oppressive domestic government and outside powers seeking to control the country, a dynamic the narrator argues has repeated for over two centuries.
data_point🗣 Johnny Harris, narratorconviction: mediumIran
So what: A large gap between regime and popular sentiment means outcomes hinge heavily on whether external pressure empowers or further entrenches the regime — a key variable for any Iran-risk scenario analysis.
“80% according to some polls, do not support this regime” ▶ 37:30
A US-Iran ceasefire was signed roughly a month before this video's release, but the narrator argues a ceasefire does not equate to lasting certainty, given Iran's 200-year history of external powers using diplomacy as cover for continued coercion or reneging on agreements.
thesis🗣 Johnny Harris, narratorconviction: mediumUnited StatesIran
So what: Investors or analysts tracking Iran-related risk (oil supply routes, Strait of Hormuz, sanctions-relief trades) should treat the ceasefire as fragile and reversible rather than a durable resolution.

Asianometry · Jon Y 1 episode(s) · 15 insights

The Miraculous Cochlear Ear Implant34 min15 insights2026-07-16
▶ Watch on YouTube
This Asianometry documentary traces the roughly 175-year path from Volta's 1800 battery-shock ear experiment to the modern cochlear implant, showing how Cochlear Limited's 1985 FDA-approved 22-electrode Nucleus 22 -- built on Graeme Clark's biomimetic electrode design and staged Australian government grants -- overtook 3M's earlier-approved single-electrode device by solving speech recognition, a problem a long line of underfunded, patent-averse, or overly complex rivals (Djourno, Chouard, House, the Hochmairs) could not crack. The field consolidated into a Cochlear/MED-EL duopoly and is now moving toward totally implantable, robotically-inserted next-generation devices.
Technical bottleneck: why multi-electrode won · 4Regulatory threshold and the FDA race · 3Corporate winners and losers · 3IP strategy and public funding as moats · 3Next frontier and risks · 2
Technical bottleneck: why multi-electrode won 4
Auditory nerves have a hard pulse-rate ceiling of roughly 200-300 (up to about 500) pulses per second, but decoding human speech requires around 3,000 pulses per second of information -- this physiological mismatch is the core constraint that forced cochlear implant designers toward multi-electrode architectures rather than a single stimulation point.
framework🗣 narrator (Jon Y)conviction: highGraeme Clark
So what: Explains why single-electrode devices (3M/House) could only convey sound awareness while multi-electrode devices (Cochlear's Nucleus 22) could convey actual speech -- the technical gap that decided which architecture captured FDA approval for speech recognition and long-run market leadership.
“about 200 to 300 pulses per second, maybe 500 for a bit” ▶ 20:10
Firing multiple cochlear implant electrodes simultaneously inside the fluid-filled cochlea caused electrical crosstalk that garbled sound, a problem that repeatedly undermined multi-electrode attempts from the Hochmairs' 8-channel device (1977) through the late 1980s.
causal🗣 narrator (Jon Y)conviction: highHochmairMED-EL
So what: Crosstalk was the key unsolved engineering problem blocking speech recognition until Blake Wilson's CIS algorithm solved it -- investors evaluating neurostimulation/bioelectronic devices should watch for analogous channel-interference bottlenecks as a marker of where the next technical unlock (and value creation) will occur.
Blake Wilson's continuous interleaved sampling (CIS) algorithm, developed in the late 1980s at the Research Triangle Institute, solved the crosstalk problem by breaking sound into non-overlapping electrical frames fired on a staggered schedule across electrodes instead of continuously, and this approach became foundational to modern cochlear implant speech processing.
framework🗣 narrator (Jon Y)conviction: highBlake WilsonMED-EL
So what: A single algorithmic breakthrough, not new hardware, unlocked commercial-grade speech recognition industry-wide; MED-EL's 1994 Combi 40 (8-channel, 12,120 pulses/sec) was built directly on it -- a reminder that in medtech, signal-processing IP can be as valuable as implant hardware.
Graeme Clark solved the mechanical problem of inserting a flexible multi-electrode array deep into the tightly curved, 1-2mm-wide scala tympani of the cochlea by copying the flexible-tip, stiffer-base structure of grass blades entering a curled turban shell -- a design insight from beachcombing in 1976.
anecdote🗣 narrator (Jon Y)conviction: highGraeme ClarkCochlear
So what: A biomimicry insight, not more funding or better algorithms, unblocked the last engineering barrier to Cochlear's 20-plus-electrode array -- a reminder that in hard-tech medtech the deciding edge can come from an unconventional cross-domain analogy rather than additional R&D spend.
Regulatory threshold and the FDA race 3
The NIH's 1975-1977 Bilger study of 13 cochlear implant recipients concluded the devices were safe and could aid lip-reading and environmental-sound identification, but explicitly could not yet support open-set speech recognition -- this became the formal bar the entire industry then raced to clear.
data_point🗣 narrator (Jon Y)conviction: highNIH
So what: Regulatory/scientific bodies often define the next competitive frontier explicitly; whichever company clears a named unmet threshold first captures outsized share -- a pattern worth watching in other emerging medtech categories.
3M's single-electrode cochlear implant, based on William House's design, received FDA approval for adults in 1984, a full year before Cochlear's Nucleus 22, but because it could only convey sound awareness and not speech, 3M lost its early-mover advantage and exited the cochlear implant market entirely by the late 1980s.
causal🗣 narrator (Jon Y)conviction: high3MWilliam HouseCochlear
So what: Being first to regulatory approval did not translate into lasting market leadership once a technically superior, speech-capable competitor was cleared a year later -- a caution against overweighting 'first FDA approval' as a moat signal without assessing the underlying performance gap.
In October 1985 the FDA approved Cochlear's Nucleus 22 based on data from 87 patients, explicitly noting that some patients could understand speech without lip-reading aid -- a capability single-electrode devices could not match -- cementing multi-electrode implants as the dominant paradigm going forward.
data_point🗣 narrator (Jon Y)conviction: highFDACochlear
So what: The 87-patient dataset and specific speech-recognition claim set the durable product-differentiation bar in the category, and remains relevant context for evaluating clinical trial design and claims in current neurostimulation device approvals.
Corporate winners and losers 3
3M passed on partnering with Graeme Clark's cochlear implant program despite being approached, choosing instead to focus on the single-electrode device it had already licensed from William House -- a decision that left the technically superior multi-electrode market to the Australian Nucleus/Telectronics partnership that became Cochlear Limited.
causal🗣 narrator (Jon Y)conviction: high3MGraeme ClarkNucleus LimitedTelectronicsCochlear
So what: An incumbent backing an already-licensed, familiar technology over a harder but ultimately superior approach lost the category to a smaller, government-backed challenger -- a recurring incumbent-bias pattern worth flagging when assessing which players in an emerging medtech field are betting on defensible versus inferior technology.
The company that became Cochlear Limited originated as an internal project inside Nucleus Limited (owner of pacemaker maker Telectronics), formed after Australian government grants (1 million AUD in 1979, a further 2 million in 1981) funded commercialization of Graeme Clark's research; the team, initially led by David Money, was later spun into the standalone company Cochlear.
data_point🗣 narrator (Jon Y)conviction: highCochlearNucleus LimitedTelectronicsGraeme Clark
So what: Cochlear Limited, today the dominant global player in cochlear implants, has an origin rooted in public R&D grants plus a strategic parent's adjacent device-manufacturing expertise (pacemakers) -- a template for how capital-intensive, safety-critical medtech often gets bootstrapped via government funding plus corporate incubation rather than pure venture capital.
The Hochmairs, after earlier multi-electrode and analog-broadband cochlear implant research at the Technical University of Vienna and consulting work for 3M, founded MED-EL in 1989, which grew into the second-largest cochlear implant manufacturer globally after Cochlear Limited.
data_point🗣 narrator (Jon Y)conviction: highMED-ELHochmairCochlear
So what: The cochlear implant market consolidated into a duopoly dominated by teams with decade-plus technical R&D histories predating their commercial entity -- the barrier to entry in this category is technical and regulatory depth, not just capital.
IP strategy and public funding as moats 3
Andre Djourno, the French co-inventor of the first cochlear implant prosthesis in 1957, refused on principle to patent his devices or procedures because he believed science should be a public good; this made it difficult to raise funding for a higher-quality device, and his refusal to let his surgical partner approach companies for better manufacturing led to the collaboration's collapse by 1959.
causal🗣 narrator (Jon Y)conviction: highAndre DjournoCharles Eyries
So what: A pioneering, working prototype from 1957 failed to become a commercial product because its inventor deliberately forewent IP protection -- illustrating that in capital-intensive medtech, patentability is often a prerequisite for the follow-on funding needed to reach commercialization, regardless of technical priority.
“he never patented any of the devices or procedures associated with his work” ▶ 9:19
Claude-Henri Chouard patented his multi-electrode cochlear implant device in France and implanted six patients between 1976 and 1977, but the device's large size and complicated, time-consuming surgical insertion procedure hampered its clinical adoption despite being a serious technical contender.
causal🗣 narrator (Jon Y)conviction: highClaude-Henri Chouard
So what: Even with IP protection secured, a device can still lose the market if it is impractical for surgeons to implant -- surgical and procedural ergonomics can be as decisive a commercial factor as the underlying technology or patent position.
Australian government funding was decisive at each stage of Cochlear's commercialization: initial grants of only a few thousand dollars in 1971-72 were withheld until viability was proven, followed by a two-year, 1 million AUD commercialization grant in January 1979, and a further 2 million AUD in 1981 that let Nucleus hire its first dedicated cochlear implant team.
data_point🗣 narrator (Jon Y)conviction: highCochlearNucleus LimitedGraeme Clark
So what: Public R&D funding, gated on proof of concept rather than given upfront, was the capital source that took a category-defining medtech company from lab prototype to commercial product -- relevant precedent when evaluating government-grant-dependent medtech and deep-tech startups today.
Next frontier and risks 2
Current cochlear implant R&D is focused on totally implantable devices with no external microphone or processor component, and on robotic-assisted surgical insertion, both aimed at giving patients more freedom and improving surgical consistency.
prediction🗣 narrator (Jon Y)conviction: exploratoryCochlearMED-EL
So what: Totally implantable devices would represent the next major product upgrade cycle for incumbents Cochlear and MED-EL, and a potential new entry point for competitors -- worth tracking as a catalyst for replacement-cycle revenue or share shifts in the space.
Graeme Clark began implanting cochlear devices in children starting in 1990, a decision that drew significant criticism from parts of the deaf community, and the ethics of pediatric cochlear implantation remains a hotly contested issue today.
contrarian🗣 narrator (Jon Y)conviction: mediumGraeme ClarkCochlear
So what: Pediatric implantation is both a major addressable-market expansion and an ongoing social and ethical controversy -- a non-technical risk factor (advocacy pushback, potential regulatory friction) worth factoring in alongside clinical and technical considerations for the hearing-device space.
